## Page 6
Foreword
By Stephen M. Shore Ed.D.
Autism and Learning Differences: An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
is a natural extension of Michael McManmon’s first book Made for
Good Purpose: What Every Parent Needs to Know to Help Their
Adolescent with Asperger’s, High Functioning Autism, or a Learning
Difference Become an Independent Adult. This treasure chest of
strategies goes into the nitty gritty of how to support individuals ages
16–26 who reside on the autism spectrum or learning disabilities
spectrum to lead independent, fulfilling, and productive lives.
As an individual on the autism spectrum who could have used many of
the strategies outlined in this book, it is my honor to have been
associated with the College Internship Program (CIP) as an advisory
board member from the time when the program was but a few buildings
in Lee, Massachusetts. CIP now encompasses six locations throughout
the United States.
Ten years ago, when I was invited to their site to give a short talk on
being on the autism spectrum, I was impressed by the dedication of all
those involved as they set the stage for those with Autism and other
Learning Differences to achieve success in higher education,
independent living, and employment. However, what was then, and
continues to be, the most striking part of CIP is the positive, active, and
participatory focus on helping them understand what it means to have
autism, and to employ those very characteristics to lead fulfilling and

---
## Page 7
productive lives. In brief, this is the first program to successfully
transition young adults from a deficit-based to an abilities-based model
of autism and other conditions.
The book begins with an overview leading to the understanding of what
an Autism Spectrum Disorder or Learning Difference diagnosis means
to clinicians, teachers, parents, and students. Combined with
methodology in active learning, the Autism and Learning Differences
Toolkit builds a firm foundation for supporting individuals with these
differences as they begin to recognize, and then move towards,
mastery in the skills needed for life, school, work, and independent
living.
Recognizing that there’s more to life than school and work, the book
then turns towards expanding the world of people with ASD and
Learning Differences by examining how we understand ourselves as
threads in the larger fabric of society leading to developing a personal
code of ethics and values in a self-exploratory, rather than a
prescriptive manner. This turning outwards to society is further
exemplified in a curriculum focused on recognizing, addressing, and
finding peaceful resolutions to differences in opinion and conflict.
The foundation of self-understanding and one’s effect on others so
expertly presented in this book would have made my initial employment
after college much easier as I would have been able to parse out my
strengths and challenges as well as to more effectively communicate
with coworkers and supervisors.
The greatest ideas, teachings, and supports are only as good as their
delivery. The Autism and Learning Differences Toolkit masterfully
describes how the concepts in this book can be taught as dedicated
units or integrated into related, pre-existing curricula.
Continuing, the question of how we know our efforts are successful is
presented via an assessments chapter that supports those working
with individuals having ASD and Learning Differences as well as the
students themselves, which in turn results in greater self-awareness.
Finally, all involved in the College Internship Program – from directors

---
## Page 8
to staff members, to students, to alumni – share their experiences and
insights gained from participation in the program.
Autism and Learning Differences: An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
joins other state of the art curricula. While this book focuses on
individuals with ASD and Learning Differences, it will be helpful to all
seeking to learn to employ strengths, interact with others, and become
contributing members of society in pursuit of leading fulfilling and
productive lives.
Chock-full of activities, ideas, examples, exercises, teaching materials,
and strategies, I give this resource my highest recommendation for
anyone wanting to learn more about supporting individuals on the
Autism Spectrum or with other Learning Differences as they gain the
skills needed to lead meaningful, fulfilling, and productive lives.
Stephen Mark Shore, Ed.D.
Internationally renowned educator,
consultant, presenter, and author on
issues related to the autism spectrum
Clinical Assistant Professor, Adelphi University
Individual on the Autism Spectrum

---
## Page 9
Author’s Note
“You were made for good purpose
and are inherently valuable.”
Michael P. McManmon
Parents and professionals have commented about the inspiration,
hope, and knowledge they have garnered from reading my first book,
Made for Good Purpose: What Every Parent Needs to Know to Help
Their Adolescent with Asperger’s, High Functioning Autism, or a
Learning Difference Become an Independent Adult. They identify with
the stories and appreciate the understanding it has given them
regarding an Autism Spectrum diagnosis. Made for Good Purpose
went into great detail about the important variables that need to be
addressed then eventually mastered by this population as they leave

---
## Page 10
home and then transition to college, vocational training, careers, and
independent living.
As I speak and present at professional conferences in the United
States and around the world, I have been asked repeatedly to take my
first book further. Specifically, I’ve been asked to share the detailed
fund of knowledge, expertise, and research that CIP (College Internship
Program) has compiled over the past 30+ years in teaching, training,
and preparing young adults on the Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)
and Learning Differences (LD) Spectrum with the skills necessary to
lead meaningful and productive lives.
In working with young adults at CIP’s six centers across the United
States, it has become clear to me that CIP’s staff, as well as other
educators, clinicians, and professionals who work with this population,
would find a Teaching Toolkit outlining the unique approach,
techniques, methodology, and pedagogy of CIP useful. This book
details clearly and concisely how to work with, teach, inspire, and
impart skills for life, school, work, and independent living to
adolescents and young adults on the vast and variable ASD/LD
Spectrum. It also stresses the importance seeing each person as an
individual with unique strengths, limitations, and differences in context
to their official diagnosis. Using a person-centered approach to obtain
life skills, reach goals, and maintain emotional and physical well-being
has helped thousands of CIP students on the Autism and Learning
Differences Spectrum achieve success.
The goal of this book, Autism and Learning Differences (An Active
Learning Teaching Toolkit) is to help develop and then build a firm
foundation of knowledge that will give educators, clinicians, and
professionals the ability to impart essential life skills to the young men
and women they work with each and every day. This book will also be
useful to those who are in support roles (providing transitional services,
tutoring, mentoring, and health and wellness services) to this
population. Autism and Learning Differences (An Active Learning
Teaching Toolkit) is a treasure trove of information that contains

---
## Page 11
practical, user-friendly materials backed by solid research during
decades of implementation at CIP’s six locations nationwide.
I am delighted to share CIP’s experience in this field as I ease into my
retirement years. It is my way of saying thank you to all the parents,
educators, and those in the mental health arena who have trusted me
and my staff with their children, students, and clients. It is an honor for
me to be able to share the ideas, methodology, curriculum materials,
assessment strategies, and resources that were developed and
created at CIP during the past three decades.
Autism and Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit) also includes contributions from leading experts in the field,
and from CIP’s staff, students, and alumni. These articles, stories, case
studies, and vignettes provide the reader with anecdotal evidence,
teachable moments, and a further fund of knowledge that helps tie the
content of the book and actually using the content of the book together.
This Teaching Toolkit contains task analyses, tips, recommended
structures, and an abundance of advice that has worked over the
decades that CIP has been in existence. It gives educators and those
who work with ASD or LD adolescents and young adults, ideas,
examples, handouts, and practical activities for use in the classroom,
workplace, and community.
Autism and Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit) can also be used to train aspiring teachers, as well as
clinicians and professionals as they prepare (academically) to work
with high school and post-secondary students on the wide-ranging
ASD/LD Spectrum. This book will also be useful to further the
professional development of those already working in this field.
This Teaching Toolkit is meant to show those who work with – or are
planning to work with – this population, how to empower each individual
young person to gain the skills needed to build and create a happy life
that has purpose and meaning. Using an active, participatory learning
model each individual will grow in the four competency areas that make
up the core of CIP’s work: skills for life, school, work, and independent

---
## Page 12
living. As specific strategies to promote skill acquisition in academic,
social, cognitive, and sensory areas emerge in the text, ongoing
support and encouragement is given – at every step along the way – to
the teachers, mentors, and role models who believe that the purpose of
education is to impart knowledge in the best way possible.
This book also tells many remarkable, poignant, and meaningful stories
that include the inspirational writing, artwork, and poetry of young
people who are on the ASD/LD Spectrum. The importance of hearing
these stories from students and alumni was brought home to me
recently when I attended an awards dinner and ceremony honoring
CIP’s 30th year anniversary and the 10th year anniversary of our
Brevard, Florida Center. A young man named Moultrie, a former CIP
Brevard Center student (who is now a staff member teaching
“Reframing,” acting as Student Council Advisor, and an Alumni
Admissions Ambassador) spoke about his experiences at CIP.
Moultrie told the audience what it felt like to have all his belongings
packed into large white trash bags, of being driven to Florida, and of
walking through the front door of CIP with no idea what was in store for
him. He talked about how hard those first few weeks were. As he
continued to speak, Moultrie elaborated further by telling us that early
on in his first semester at CIP, he decided that things could go one of
two ways: he could move forward and make the best of it, or stay stuck,
confused, and become even more isolated. He decided right then and
there that CIP did not stand for the “College Internship Program” but,
was instead, an acronym for “Constantly in Progress.” Looking at
things from this perspective allowed Moultrie to begin a new journey
and then to thrive, succeed, and become the confident and articulate
young adult that he is today. It was a long, hard – but worthwhile
journey.
As a member of CIP’s Brevard Center staff, Moultrie is a contributing,
highly functional successful young man who makes a difference daily in
the lives of others who are on the ASD/LD Spectrum. His eloquent
speech that night brought tears to my eyes. Moultrie ended his

---
## Page 13
presentation by telling the audience that he is now and always will be
“Constantly in Progress.”
This book tells many stories like Moultrie’s and gives a template with
step-by-step guidance on the best ways to impart knowledge to
adolescents and young adults with an ASD/LD diagnosis.
It is my hope that this book will provide practical advice and
encouragement – and serve as a blueprint – for those who work with,
guide, and mentor the adolescents and young adults who carry an ASD
or LD diagnosis. I also hope that it reinforces the fact that your role as
an educator, clinician, or professional is to equip these young people to
meet the social, intellectual, and personal challenges of the future. But
most of all, I sincerely hope that it allows each person who reads,
implements, and benefits from the materials in this book the opportunity
to emulate Moultrie and be “Constantly in Progress.”
Michael P. McManmon, CIP Founder
Lanesborough, Massachusetts

---
## Page 14
How to Use This Book
“Begin at the beginning and go on until you
come to the end: then stop.”
Lewis Carroll, Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland
Again and again experience has shown that young people on the
Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) or Learning Differences (LD)
Spectrum who have been enabled to take a responsible role in their
own learning in partnership with adults, deal with life, school, work, and
independent living more effectively. Educators, clinicians, support
professionals, and others using this Teaching Toolkit will be able to
offer adolescents and young adults with an ASD/LD diagnosis a
research driven, evidence-based, logical, and structured learning
experience that develops an individual’s concepts, knowledge, and
outcomes needed for success.
The Toolkit will focus on four key competency areas: skills for life,
school, work, and independent living. In addition to this skill set, the
Teaching Toolkit devotes a chapter to understanding the ASD/LD
diagnosis from the perspective of the educator, parent, and student.
Further chapters focus on how to impart and teach ethical values to
this population and how to incorporate social inclusion, peer mediation,
and peer mentoring into an already crowded curriculum. There is also a
chapter on assessment that gives a variety of strategies to measure
outcomes.
Of special interest are the pages devoted to hearing from experts in the
field, as well as CIP’s (College Internship Program’s) own Program

---
## Page 15
Directors, staff, students, and alumni. By providing the reader with
advice from experts and input from CIP staff, students, alumni, this
Teaching Toolkit imparts hope, inspiration, and further evidence that
the methodology presented in this book is sound and that it works.
The “Active Learning” model presented throughout this book can be
used in its entirety to develop a cohesive curriculum at the high school
or post-secondary level. The methodology and underlying pedagogy
can also be used by taking specific chapters or topics within chapters
and using these separately to address issues relevant to the needs of
the educator, clinician, or support professional working with this
population. Either way, Autism and Learning Differences (An Active
Learning Teaching Toolkit) will help adolescents and young adults
obtain the skills and competencies needed to transition from
dependence to independence.
Takeaway
Use the quotations, fast facts, helpful hints for teachers and
students, teachable moments, and takeaways in this book to
generate group discussion, activities, role plays, participation,
and action. At CIP’s (College Internship Program’s) six centers
and at our National Office we take inspirational quotes, tips, and
fast facts and have students use artistic expression to illustrate
them. We then hang these up in classrooms, offices, hallways,
and even in the bathrooms.
Throughout the book there are a number of pages that can be
photocopied for use by the students. These are indicated by the
symbol:

---
## Page 16
ICONS identify the handouts and sidebars that are featured in the
margins throughout the book. These handouts and sidebars provide an
at-a-glance reference to give those using the Teaching Toolkit
inspiration, encouragement, fast facts, or classroom management tips
and techniques that relate to each chapter’s content.
Inspirational Quotes
These quotes are meaningful to the content of the chapter and are
meant to inspire students and those who work with them.
Helpful Hints for Students
Things like using dual alarm clocks, electronic-based note-taking
systems, “Executive Function” techniques, or strategies like “The
Donkey Rule,” are presented in a quick and easy-to-integrate way.
Fast Facts about ASD and LD
These tips are well-known and documented facts about Autism
Spectrum Disorders (ASD) and Learning Differences (LD). Fast Facts
are useful for reviewing or adding to an already established fund of
knowledge about an on the Spectrum diagnosis.
Triage for Teachers
A treasure trove of on the spot, quickly achieved, tried and true,
behavioral strategies, teaching techniques, or classroom management
suggestions.

---
## Page 17
Takeaways
A user-friendly snapshot and summary of the concept being taught or
portrayed in a chapter or a specific part of a chapter.
Teachable Moments
These are those gems of wisdom that firmly ingrain the content being
taught by a real-life example or experience from CIP’s staff,
contributing experts, and others.

---
## Page 18
Chapter Overview
Reading this overview is important as it will give educators, clinicians,
and professionals increased clarity about the content of the book and a
first glance summary of each chapter. Taking the time to preview the
chapters before implementing the content will allow those using the
toolkit – or those who plan to use it – to see the logical progression,
comprehensive nature, and structure of the book as one chapter leads
to the next.
Being able to utilize each chapter’s content in the most effective way
possible, to ensure retention and measurable outcomes, will lead to a
progressive, orderly plan for successfully implementing the step-by-
step methodology and the underlying pedagogy of Autism and Learning
Differences (An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit).
As stated in the Author’s Note at the beginning of this book, this
teaching and training manual is based on 30+ years of in-depth
research, curriculum development, assessment strategies, and the
solid measurable outcomes achieved at the College Internship
Program’s (CIP’s) six centers throughout the USA. Over the years, CIP
has worked successfully with thousands of young adults who reside on
the ASD/LD Spectrum. Readers will see, after reading the chapter
overview that this book is comprehensive, user friendly, and practical.
This teaching Toolkit gives those using it a flexible, well-tested
framework for using the content of each chapter and for creating a
sustained commitment to an active, participatory learning environment.

---
## Page 19
Overview of Chapter 1
Getting Started: Understanding the Clinical
Diagnosis
The focus of this chapter is looking at and understanding the wide
range of characteristics and behaviors that adolescents and young
adults display that result in an Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) or
Learning Differences (LD) diagnosis. Taking the diagnostic criteria
apart and approaching it from a clinical, educational, parental, and
student standpoint regarding the day-to-day school experience of
students and teachers in the classroom setting is vital to being able to
help each individual adolescent or young adult succeed in various
learning environments.
Chapter 1 also takes a look at the dynamics and role that parents play
in their adolescent’s or young adult’s diagnosis in relation to the school
setting and then looks at the diagnosis from the perspective of the
student who has received an ASD or LD classification.
This important first chapter starts to lay the ground work that will help
construct the foundation that is necessary in order to build upon and
impart knowledge to ASD/LD adolescents and young adults. This
foundation can then be built upon effectively, so that each young
person being taught obtains the necessary skills for life, school, work,
and independent living.
This chapter gives the user of the book a basic understanding of the
best ways to impart knowledge and then looks at incorporating an
active, participatory learning model into any learning environment.
Additional relevant theories that work well for the majority of
adolescents and young adults diagnosed on the wide and varied ASD
or LD Spectrum are also discussed. Chapter 1 becomes the blueprint
for utilizing the ensuing chapters in a logical, tried and true fashion.

---
## Page 20
Overview of Chapter 2
The Methodology of Active Learning
“Active Learning” is the methodology used throughout Autism and
Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit) book.
“Active Learning” is a process whereby students engage in activities,
such as reading, writing, discussion, or problem solving, that promote
analysis, synthesis, and evaluation of class content. This participatory
learning model gives teachers, clinicians, and professionals working
with adolescents or young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum (ages 16–
26) an experiential way of imparting knowledge. “Active Learning”
incorporates the needs of each individual student as he or she
participates in a group or classroom learning environment.
The experts tell us that using an “Active Learning” model that enables
students to learn and develop through participation at each step in their
learning, builds confidence, self-esteem, and retention of knowledge.
This methodology also helps to develop interpersonal skills, critical
thinking skills, and a curiosity about learning. These strategies
transform teachers from dispensers of information to translators of
information. It allows those who impart knowledge to become the
facilitators or vehicles for this process.
“Active Learning” underpins each chapter in this Teaching Toolkit and
comes into especially clear focus in Chapters 3 to 6 that focus on the
four competency areas: skills for life, school, work, and independent
living.
Overview of Chapter 3
Competency 1: Skills for Life
The focus of Chapter 3 is all about helping students acquire the basic
skills for life that each adolescent or young adult on the ASD (Autism
Spectrum Disorder) or LD (Learning Differences) Spectrum will need
to develop in order to function in and be part of the wider community

---
## Page 21
that surrounds them. Chapter 3 – Competency 1: Skills for Life
presents a structured framework that anyone working with ASD or LD
adolescents and young adults can use. Teaching skills for life – using a
variety of tools, techniques, tasks, and analyses – to those on the
diverse ASD or LD Spectrum will provide measurable outcomes and
become the fund of knowledge that each young person needs to
develop in order to work through the next three skill competencies:
skills for school, work, and independent living.
Skills for life help young people on the ASD or LD Spectrum navigate
the challenges of everyday life. Acquiring these skills through “ADL”
(Activities for Daily Living) allows each individual to deal effectively with
these demands and develop into a healthy, responsible, and productive
adult. Skills for life are not a given and have to be explicitly taught,
modeled, and practiced over and over again.
In general, skills for life are considered to be those abilities that help
promote well-being, positive health outcomes, and productive
development. Developing skills for life results in the acquisition of social
and interpersonal skills (including communication, refusal skills,
assertiveness, and empathy), cognitive skills (including decision-
making, critical thinking, and self-evaluation), and emotional coping
skills (including stress management). This chapter also includes how to
implement and use “Executive Function” strategies in connection with
learning everyday life skills so that organization, prioritization, and
logical thinking can also occur.
These abilities comprise a set of core skills that empower young people
to take steps to engage in positive social relationships, choose a
healthy lifestyle, and make contributions to society. Skills for life are
important in helping each ASD or LD individual shape his or her world,
not just cope or try to cope with it.
Overview of Chapter 4
Competency 2: Skills for School

---
## Page 22
Nationally, 35% of students identified with LD (Learning Differences)
drop out of high (secondary) school. Less than 2% of those who do
graduate high (secondary) school attend a four-year college. The high
school drop-out rate for students with an ASD (Autism Spectrum
Disorder) diagnosis is approximately twice that of general education
students. Only 40% of adolescents or young adults diagnosed with
ASD (Autism Spectrum Disorder) ever attend college. (Statistics from
the National Longitudinal Transition Study)
Comprehensive academic support that builds success in school and
moves towards high (secondary) school graduation, a degree, or
career and vocational training is the focus of Chapter 4 – Competency
2: Skills for School. This chapter will assist those working with ASD or
LD adolescents and young adults to acquire the core elements needed
for successful academic achievement. As students create a “Person
Centered Plan” (PCP) that takes into consideration their special
interests, hopes, dreams, wishes, and potential career paths, they
develop individual and academically appropriate strategies to succeed
in school with measurable outcomes and realistic goals.
This chapter also helps students achieve additional “Executive
Function” abilities. Building on the “Executive Function” aptitudes
mastered in Chapter 3, students learn further techniques and strategies
that are needed for effective learning and skill development. Sections
showing students how to manage time, how to advocate for themselves
(disclosure), and how to request accommodations in the classroom
setting are also covered.
Those imparting knowledge to ASD/LD adolescents and young adults
can incorporate programs like CIP’s “Bookends”™ – a strategy for
starting and ending the school week – and a variety of tips, techniques,
and ideas to help these important skills for school emerge in each
individual. Chapter 4 helps students start to build on their academic
success by creating a portfolio of their work and a record of
achievements.
This chapter also talks about effective classroom management
techniques and how to recognize the warning signs of bullying and

---
## Page 23
peer pressure. The latest statistics tell us that 65% of parents report
that their children on the ASD Spectrum have been victimized by peers
in some way within the past year. (National Autism Association)
This chapter ends with a section entitled: “Ready or Not, Here I
Come!” and illustrates various transitioning strategies that can be used
to assist each individual as he or she prepares for college and life after
high school. Especially helpful is a student handout that shows students
the realistic academic differences and expectations that exist as they
prepare to transition from high to post-secondary education.
Educators, clinicians, and professionals will continue to help these
young men and women build on the skills learned from this chapter as
the next competency area comes into focus: Chapter 5 – Competency
3: Skills for Work.
Overview of Chapter 5
Competency 3: Skills for Work
With unemployment and underemployment among young adults with
an ASD (Autism Spectrum Disorder) hovering around 90%, the
importance of imparting skills for work cannot be overstated. Only 25–
50% of young adults with Learning Differences (LD) will have any paid
employment. Having and keeping a job helps in the development of an
adult identity, promotes self-esteem, supports a growing social circle,
and provides financial opportunities.
This chapter will focus on further development of the academic portfolio
begun in Chapter 4 – Competency 2: Skills for School, and also look at
the role that “special interests” play in relationship to career paths and
choices. Readers will be given resources and materials to help
students explore likes, dislikes, as well as strengths and challenges.
Those working with these young men and women (especially career
and transition counselors or mentors) will be able to help students
utilize their assets and gifts to develop the many skills necessary to
achieve success in any working environment.

---
## Page 24
Learning and developing employment tactics such as resume creation,
and preparing for the interview process, as well as showing students
the importance of career counseling (so that each individual can
understand their real abilities vs. grandiose beliefs) are included.
Strategies for showing students how to see the big picture: social
competencies on the job, then doing a skills match for jobs and career
path are also given.
Looking at the value of an entry level job, internships, and community
service will allow those working with ASD or LD students to show each
individual how vital it is to understand not only the workplace culture
(with its own “Hidden Curriculum”) and its many challenges but how to
integrate into this world via entry level or volunteer opportunities.
Encouraging ASD or LD students to choose volunteer work, part-time
work, and internships as alternatives to a full-time job, will allow these
young men and women to master the core competencies they have
developed in the classroom and work toward gaining meaningful, paid
full-time employment later on.
Adolescents and young adults will learn how to use “Executive
Functions” on the job so that they can learn to do the multiple tasks
associated with their career choice. Learning about perseverance and
to keep questioning until an answer is found – known as “Dogged
Persistence” – and developing an ability to think outside the box are
also included in this chapter.
Chapter 5 provides a framework that will allow adolescents and young
adults with an ASD or LD diagnosis to make good choices about
careers or further job training opportunities. It also gives many
examples and resources that can be modified or integrated into any
learning environment and provides opportunities for individuals with an
ASD or LD diagnosis to thoroughly explore career choices and learn
about the world of work.
Overview of Chapter 6
Competency 4: Skills for Independent Living

---
## Page 25
The national average of US college graduates diagnosed with ASD
(Autism Spectrum Disorder) or LD (Learning Differences) who are
planning to live independently and separate from their parents is (as of
this publication date) 15%. As adolescents and young adults gain
“Executive Function” ability and the core skills needed for success in
the skills for life, school, and work competency sections of this book,
developing goals and practicing independent living skills is the next
logical step. Chapter 6 – Competency 4: Skills for Independent Living
helps students prepare for this next step in their development.
Transitioning from dependence to independence is necessary for those
leaving high school, attending college, or starting careers.
Chapter 6 examines the skills that are needed to be successful when
living independently whether alone or with others (roommates, friends,
or in a relationship), and also looks at living within or part of a larger
group or community – such as a college or university dormitory or an
apartment complex. The Skills for Independent Living chapter looks at
the need for concrete residential strategies, and the value of peer and
social mentoring. Looking at various forms of mediation, handling
finances and budgeting, and looking at “Executive Function” abilities in
relation to living independently are also included. The “Hidden
Curriculum” that comes into play with this newly found independence,
interdependence, and other strategies for being successful in a
“neurotypically wired” world are also part of this chapter.
In the Feeling Fit, Healthy, and Ready to Go sub-section, wellness,
nutrition (including how to eat in a healthy way), and how to deal with
stress when feeling overwhelmed with a situation (or about to lose
emotional control), are also outlined.
Chapter 6 looks at using “The Donkey Rule” in this context with
practical examples for helping the ASD/LD adolescents and young
adults make good decisions.
This chapter allows those working with ASD and LD individuals to have
a good plan, an array of materials, tools, ideas, and strategies for
helping each emerging adult succeed at living independently. Chapter
6 – Competency 4: Skills for Independent Living concludes the skills

---
## Page 26
chapters (3–6) of the book and ends with concepts for learning
openness, tolerance, and diversity that will be expanded upon in
Chapter 7.
Overview of Chapter 7
Imparting Ethical Values and Other Kinds of
Values
The focus of Chapter 7: Imparting Ethical Values and Other Kinds of
Values is being able to identify the values and ethics of each ASD
(Autism Spectrum Disorder) or LD (Learning Differences) adolescent
or young adult and look at those of their peers and others in their
communities. Values in life, in a classroom setting, in the workplace,
and that reside in each individual will only work if they are implemented
through development rather than change. Developing ethical values
involves asking each adolescent or young adult to take ownership of
the process for creating a set of standards to live by.
Being able to put an individual ego aside and develop a strong moral
fiber builds character and allows each young person to focus on the
wider world around him or her. Each skill set acquired in the preceding
chapters is more meaningful when structured around a base of values
and ethics.
Working with those on the ASD/LD Spectrum to develop and then look
at shared values is key to understanding how people treat each other,
how they perceive each other’s needs, and how they behave
sympathetically towards their parents, peers, those they are in a
relationship with, and those in the broader community. Shared values
allow people to communicate effectively with each other, to exercise
objectivity and judgment, and to respect truth.
Obtaining and then mastering the abilities that allow those on the
ASD/LD Spectrum to become self-reliant, self-respecting, self-
confident, and self-disciplined reinforces and puts into perspective, by
giving reasons for learning the valuable life skills presented in the first

---
## Page 27
few chapters of this book. The purpose of Chapter 7 is to have the
young men and women you teach, counsel, and mentor explore the
values they feel are important. This in turn leads to each one becoming
a productive and contributing member of society.
Other topics covered in Chapter 7 are looking at two definitions of
ethics and why sometimes there isn’t a rule. Young people will also
explore what makes up an ideal citizen and learn how to trust their
instincts and gut feelings. Looking at and exploring ethical and moral
dilemmas – as well as the vital elements of each person and their role in
the community – leads to talking about how each person or a group of
individuals can effect positive change, especially through “Community
Service Learning.” Through role playing, outreach activities, and the
instructional materials and handouts in this chapter, students will
develop a personal code of ethics and a code of conduct for their
classroom.
Overview of Chapter 8
Awareness, Peer Support, and Conflict
Resolution
Chapter 8 gives educators, clinicians, and the support professionals
who work with adolescents and young adults on the ASD or LD
Spectrum information, teaching tools, techniques, and materials to
raise awareness and then address conflict such as: bullying,
harassment, teasing, discrimination, and verbal or physical assault.
It has been proven throughout the United States and the world, that
oftentimes, the best way to raise awareness, provide assistance, and
then resolution to conflict at school and in the workplace in general, is
through peer listening, peer support groups, and an actual peer
mediation process.
Having young adults address these issues directly with peers (who are
trained and supported by their teachers, support professionals, and

---
## Page 28
school or workplace administrators) often turns out to be the most
effective way to lessen conflict at school or at work.
It does not matter whether the conflict originates in school, workplace,
at home, or in local communities. Providing a structure (in the learning
and working environment) where young adults, especially those on the
Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and Learning Differences Spectrum
(LD), can (a) know the signs of conflict; (b) be able to tell someone; (c)
have an action taken to stop the conflict; and (d) find a peaceful
resolution to the conflict is extremely important.
Encouraging young people to be part of the awareness campaign,
solution, and resolution is what Chapter 8: Awareness, Peer Support,
and Conflict Resolution is all about.
Overview of Chapter 9
Curriculum Integration
Chapter 9 will focus on how to integrate and merge teaching the skills
for life, school, work, and independent living, as well as the content of
the additional chapters, into an already established curriculum or into
specific subject areas.
Various models are possible. From 100% use of dedicated time in a
specialized school or a transitional services program that is devoted to
assisting adolescents and young adults with an ASD (Autism
Spectrum) or LD (Learning Differences) diagnosis, to a fully dispersed
model in which the content of this book or chapters from this book are
integrated into classes or lessons that take place during a regular high
school or college day. Many high schools, colleges, universities, and
ASD/LD support programs will, in practice, opt for a mixed model
which will reduce the pressure to find 100% dedicated time.
This chapter supports using the materials contained in this teaching
and training manual in whatever way best suits the educator or
professional that is using it and show various models for
implementation that encompass all models for using this book.

---
## Page 29
Overview of Chapter 10
Assessment, Evaluation, and Measuring
Outcomes
Assessment in connection with Autism and Learning Differences (An
Active Learning Teaching Toolkit) does not, in any way imply, that
adolescents or young adults are failing a competency level or have
failed to achieve or obtain a skill set.
This chapter is not about judgment of the worth, personality, or value of
an individual person. It is about finding areas of strength and building
upon those strengths while developing strategies to address limitations.
This is particularly important to keep in mind when assessing progress
of adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum. It is also
equally important to remember when using the assessment strategies
and models in this book that progress can come from:
•
The adolescents and young adults themselves through self-
assessment and peer-aided assessment that provides evidence
of progress towards preset goals and objectives.
•
Teachers, clinicians, professionals, and parents assessment of
progress and results.
This chapter promotes active participation by encouraging students to
review and record their own progress, and develop other skills such as
managing their own learning through various record keeping methods
such as a personal progress file, journal, or record of achievement.
Examples of assessments from College Internship Program (CIP) are
included in this chapter, as well as examples of the measurement tools
being used at CIP’s six centers throughout the United States.
The focus of the chapter is to create assessment strategies to assess
the four competency areas fairly and effectively.

---
## Page 30
Overview of Chapter 11
In Their Own Words: Stories from CIP
The College Internship Program (CIP) has developed cutting edge
curriculum strategies, teaching, and assessment materials that have –
over time – grown into a well-rounded program to help those with an
ASD (Autism Spectrum Disorder) or Learning Differences (LD)
diagnosis obtain skills for life, school, work, and independent living.
The Autism and Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit) model of working with young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum
has been successfully implemented in CIP’s six centers with thousands
of students. Each CIP Center has a Program Director, as well as staff
members and support personnel who come from a diverse background
of psychology, counseling, teaching, administration, mentoring, and
other human service fields. Many of CIP’s staff have had or now have
personal connections with those on the Autism and/or Learning
Differences Spectrum.
CIP’s Program Directors and their staff members are passionate about
working with ASD and LD students. These incredibly talented
individuals understand the unique strengths and challenges that each
student possesses and together they prepare CIP’s students for
meaningful lives, positive school experiences, workplace readiness,
and successful independent living.
As readers experience the pedagogy and methodology of Autism and
Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit) through
the eyes of Program Directors, staff, students, and alumni by reading
their personal stories and essays, the first ten chapters of this book
come to life and become real for the readers.
The creative writing, poetry, photographs, and artwork that accompany
these stories tell the reader about individual students and their
participatory experiences at CIP and those of the staff who work with
them every day. As these individual experiences are relayed to readers,
implementing the ideas of the Toolkit on a daily basis becomes more

---
## Page 31
applicable for those who will integrate the ideas contained in this book
into their own work with those on the ASD/LD Spectrum.
The goal of this very personal chapter is to inspire educators,
clinicians, and professionals to venture forward with confidence and a
realistic viewpoint of how and why this methodology works. It is also
meant to be a celebration of the individual journey that each person
who has contributed to this chapter has taken. By sharing their stories,
the voices of the CIP Program Directors, their staff members, and the
young men and women who have benefited from using the core
principles outlined in this book are validated, heard, and honored.
Overview of Chapter 12
The Experts Speak!
This chapter is devoted to hearing from the experts across the US and
around the world that focus their work, their expertise, and their
professional writing on a particular aspect of the Autism or Learning
Differences Spectrum. By including the expertise and life’s work of
these distinguished educators, clinicians, and professionals in Autism
and Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit), the
reader is offered an additional evidence-based fund of knowledge on
the best ways to work with and teach adolescents and young adults on
the ASD/LD Spectrum. Readers will find these contributions
encouraging, heartening, hopeful, and extremely valuable.
The knowledge portrayed in these individually written essays, articles,
and stories adds up to decades of clinical research, ongoing
implementation, documented assessment, and measurable outcomes.
This in turn has led to acceptance in today’s clinical world of the
ASD/LD research and writings of these contributors. These experts are
the professional men and women who have set standards, provided
evidence-based outcomes, and offered their expertise to Dr. Michael
McManmon and to CIP (College Internship Program) over the past 30
+ years.

---
## Page 32
Hearing about the lives, workplace experiences, clinical research,
development, and conclusions of others in the ASD/LD field is relevant
and adds tangible backing and support to the content of this Teaching
Toolkit.

---
## Page 33
Preface
The Center for Disease Control estimates that one in 68 children will be
diagnosed with an Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and that this
population is aging fast. Roughly 50,000 of these children will turn 18
each year. Therefore, a book dedicated to preparing educators,
clinicians, and support professionals to work with these emerging
adults is not only needed, wanted, and requested – but warranted.
Autism and Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit) encompasses a broad range of teaching and learning
opportunities that focus on the importance of participation by each
young person with an ASD or LD diagnosis, in his or her own learning.
Central to this involvement are curriculum links, self-assessment
strategies, and an understanding of the four competency areas that will
give each individual skills for life, school, work, and independent living.
Reading and utilizing the How to Use This Book and the Chapter
Overview sections that precede this preface will allow those using this
book to build a solid foundation for imparting the knowledge contained
in Chapters 1 through 12. This foundational work is necessary for using
the methodology in the most comprehensive and effective way possible.
This Teaching Toolkit goes a long way toward helping those who work
with adolescents and young adults on the ASD or LD Spectrum
embrace the vision of inclusion by preparing these young men and
women for independent, successful, fulfilling, and worthwhile lives. This
book gives activities, ideas, examples, exercises, teaching materials,
and strategies – all designed to enhance the skills that educators,
clinicians, and support professionals need to develop.

---
## Page 34
Integrating the Toolkit’s methodology into existing or specifically created
curricula, programs, support services, or vocational training programs,
allows for individual student successes using this tried and true,
research-based way of working with adolescents and young adults with
an ASD or LD Spectrum diagnosis. As young people obtain skills for
life, school, work, and independent living they become valued
members of society in a way that is meaningful and appropriate.
This book gives schools, colleges, and universities, as well as
educators, clinicians, and support professionals a starting point for
working with adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum in
a specific and advantageous manner. It also allows for the coordination
of teaching, learning, and collaboration with each each individual
student through person-centered planning and self-directed learning.
Autism and Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit) is filled with the shared vision, shared methodology, and
shared values of the College Internship Program (CIP). This book is
based on the research, implementation, assessment, results, and
outcomes methodology that is in use at CIP Centers nationwide. This
tried and true approach allows those working with adolescents and
young adults on the wide-ranging ASD or LD Spectrum to help each
individual develop the habit of reflection on his or her learning
experiences thus enabling each young person to absorb more
throughout the process. While individual needs will differ, the
commonalities that run through an ASD or LD diagnosis – such as
difficulty with social interactions, “Executive Function” abilities,
academic challenges, or in obtaining basic life skills – enable this
Teaching Toolkit to be all encompassing and useful within the context
of each person’s unique diagnostic challenges.
As the chapters, or selected materials, ideas, and concepts from the
chapters, are incorporated into an existing or newly created curriculum,
students become more curious and more motivated to learn. Using
these ideas, materials, and standards in the learning environment will
allow adolescents and young adults to strengthen their life skills, gain

---
## Page 35
understanding and acceptance of their diagnosis, and build the
foundation that will lead to social and civic responsibility.
As each person begins to understand his or her individual challenges in
a more complex way, these young men and women learn to imagine
and then create alternative solutions. Giving ASD and LD adolescents
and young adults more responsibility and significant inclusion in the
learning process shows each learner how decisions are made and how
these decisions affect the lives of those around them. As adolescents
and young adults learn how to work collaboratively with others – on
their challenges and the challenges of others with whom they are
forging relationships – they not only achieve personal rewards, they
learn that they, as individuals, can make a difference and that success
breeds success.
The materials and advice from this book can be easily integrated into
an already crowded curriculum. Autism and Learning Differences (An
Active Learning Teaching Toolkit) details this process and explains the
wider benefits that will be achieved by this integration. Use of this
methodology then becomes part of a wider agenda for improvement of
an educator’s, clinician’s, or support professional’s ability to deliver
knowledge to this special needs population.
Using this book will create a community of ASD/LD adolescent and
young adult supporters with a shared vision and shared goals. This
Teaching Toolkit is meant to inspire a sustained commitment to a
learning environment that builds mastery of the skills for life, school,
work, and independent living with an emphasis on the ASD/LD
learner’s right to accommodations, quality education, peer and
mentoring support, as well as transitional services.
High schools and institutes of higher learning using this Teaching
Toolkit will be able to create a unified curriculum that allows for
enhanced and mature relationships between staff and students. This
book will assist schools as they develop a teaching philosophy based
on mutual respect, dignity, and awareness. This assistance is
invaluable to those who are employed in high schools, universities, or
vocational schools as the numbers of enrolled ASD/LD students

---
## Page 36
continues to grow. This Teaching Toolkit will also be an important
resource to the clinician, psychologist, or teacher who is working with
individual students in an out-of-school setting or in one-on-one tutoring.
This book will also work well in post-secondary programs that are
similar to the CIP (College Internship Program) model, or in transitional
programs that assist young adults as they move to their next level of
learning and prepare for careers and independent living. Autism and
Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit) contains a
flexible, well-tested, framework that combines a sequence of teaching
and learning activities with a firm understanding of an ASD or LD
diagnosis in an adolescent or young adult.
The purpose of creating this Teaching Toolkit is to form a bond and a
level of respect, inclusion, and commitment between those imparting
knowledge and those receiving knowledge. Providing coherence in the
way those on the ASD and LD Spectrum are educated will help each
emerging young adult deal with their difficulties and develop the
knowledge, skills, and understanding of themselves that they need to
live confident, healthy, independent lives.
As each young person attains the essential and necessary life skills
detailed in this book – educators, clinicians, and support professionals
then help each individual learn to play a larger role in society by
acquiring self-confidence and a sense of responsibility for his or her
own well-being. This, in turn, leads to each adolescent or young adult
becoming a valued and important participant at school, at work, and as
they integrate into their neighborhoods and surrounding communities.
Having educators, clinicians, and professionals who work with these
young men and women commit to creating an inclusive learning
environment with this specific purpose in mind, is the reason Autism
and Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit) was
created.
Francine Britton, Content Editor
for Autism and Learning Differences (An
Active Learning Teaching Toolkit)

diagnosis perform below their enrolled grade, 45% in

The number of students on the ASD/LD Spectrum grows daily. As high
schools, colleges, and universities try to cope with the growing number
of adolescents and young adults diagnosed with Autism Spectrum
Disorder (ASD) and Learning Differences (LD), integrating, merging,
and/or adapting a well-rounded, research-based pedagogy that gives
students unparalleled support and guidance into existing curricula is
essential.
The focus of Chapter 1: Getting Started is on understanding the criteria
used to make an ASD (DSM–5 Autism Spectrum Disorder) or LD
(Learning Difference) diagnosis from a clinical perspective and how
this diagnosis impacts teachers, parents, and most importantly, the
adolescents and young adults who carry the diagnosis. Emphasis on
the criteria for Sensory Processing Disorders and Asperger’s Disorder
(AD) is also included, as the rise in adolescents and young adults on
the ASD/LD Spectrum with these classifications is much more
prevalent today than in previous years and decades.
Chapter 1 takes the criteria used to determine individual learning styles
and discusses the best ways to impart knowledge to the ASD or LD
population via brain-theory-based teaching strategies. Looking at the
diagnosis of ASD or LD from a clinical and teaching standpoint, as well
as a student and parent perspective – as related to the day-to-day
school experience of adolescents and young adult students in the

---
## Page 41
classroom setting – gives the reader a solid, easy-to-understand
starting point for comprehending and using the chapters that follow in a
logical and organized way.
In order to utilize the information in the ensuing chapters effectively,
reading this first chapter is necessary. It is the blueprint that allows the
rest of the book to be constructed. Chapter 1 gives those who plan to
use Autism and Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit) a frame of reference for measuring success, as well as the
strategies and teaching techniques for imparting the knowledge that
will lead ASD and LD students to proficiency in the four competency
areas: skills for life, school, work, and independent living.
Understanding the wide range of criteria for an ASD or LD diagnosis
(and the commonalities that both carry) from various viewpoints will
allow practitioners to understand that skills for life, school, work, and
independent living are vital and will allow these young men and women
to become successful, valued, contributing, independent, and
productive members of society.
So let’s get started!
Understanding the ASD Diagnosis
from a Clinical Standpoint
We are witnessing a transformation in the way that adolescents and
young adults diagnosed with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) are
educated. The experts tell us that the years between 18 and 26 can be
extremely challenging for these young men and women. The struggle
for independence is a difficult, arduous, and long process and emotions
run very high. Instead of transitioning from dependence to
independence, these adolescents and young adults withdraw and
isolate. Feelings of anxiety, depression, and inadequacy, that have
always been there, intensify. Their social skills are poor and structure
(consistency, routines, rules, and predictability) is lacking. Rigid

---
## Page 42
thinking and inflexibility are present, and most likely, healthy routines
are not. Obsessions, fixations, and “special interests” take up an
extraordinary amount of the adolescent’s or young adult’s time, leading
to further isolation and reliance on these practices.
The 2013 DSM–5 (an internationally used medical manual) currently
bases its description of those diagnosed with ASD (Autism Spectrum
Disorder) on two sets of criteria that involve interpersonal impairments
(American Psychiatric Association 2013). The first is impairment in
social interactions, and the second is impairment in communications.
Included in the diagnostic criteria for impairment in social interactions
are problems with nonverbal behaviors.
Problematic nonverbal behaviors encompass:
•
Not making eye contact.
•
Inability to read facial expressions.
•
Inability to decipher and exhibit body postures.
•
Inability to adapt to or regulate gestures.
•
Inability to make friends among one’s peers.
•
Difficulties with spontaneity.
•
Difficulties sharing with others.
•
Difficulties with emotional reciprocity.

---
## Page 43
Included in the diagnostic criteria for impairment in social
communications are problems with basic communication.
Problematic basic communication encompasses:
•
A delay in spoken language.
•
The inability to start and/or maintain a conversation with others.
•
Use of memorized language or scripts or unusual language.
•
Difficulty with spontaneous or imaginative interactions.
•
Preoccupation with restricted interests.
•
Insistence on particular routines or rituals.
•
Unusual motor mannerisms.
•
Preoccupation with objects or details.
Most of us have some of these traits. The problem arises when the
scope of the challenges encountered by people on the ASD Spectrum
exceeds those of people who are not on the Autism Spectrum
(“neurotypicals”).

---
## Page 44
The DSM–5 also states that abnormal functioning must be present in at
least one area involving social interaction, social communicative
language, or peer-related interactions that are symbolic or imaginative.
SOCIAL DEFICITS
Although some of the criteria used to determine an ASD (Autism
Spectrum Disorder) diagnosis are different than the criteria used for an
Autism diagnosis, the bottom line is the same: social deficits. As such,
ASD is further defined by problems with:
•
Receptive or expressive social communication.
•
Selective social attachments.
•
Social interaction.
•
Eye contact, facial expression, posture and gestures intended to
regulate social interactions.
•
Developing peer relations.
•
Mutual sharing of interests, activities, and emotions.
•
Socio-emotional reciprocity.
•
Modulating behavior according to social context.
•
Integrating social, emotional, and communicative behaviors.
•
Responsiveness to another person’s communications.
•
Stereotypical, repetitive, or idiosyncratic speech.
•
Pitch, rhythm, and intonation of speech.
In order to meet the criteria for an ASD diagnosis, adolescents and
young adults must have social impairments, deficits, and significant
communication challenges in a number of areas. This can also be
perceived as differences in style and communication that can be quite
functional in certain settings and situations and not so functional in
others.

---
## Page 45
Understanding a Learning
Differences (LD) Diagnosis from a
Clinical Standpoint
Adolescents and young adults diagnosed with Learning Differences
(LD) often face challenges for which they are not prepared. These
young men and women often experience a decline in school
performance and struggle with emotional, behavioral, and mental health
challenges.
As stated in the “Fast Facts” at the beginning of this chapter, the
statistics are startling. As of this writing LD is diagnosed in 15% of the
adolescent and young adult population. This diagnosis can have a
profound impact on individuals and their families. Currently 2.4 million
students with an LD diagnosis receive special education services,
representing 41% of all students receiving these services. Additionally,
67% of students with LD graduate from high school vs. 74% of students
in the general population.
Learning Differences (LD) may affect the ability to speak, listen, think,
read, write, spell, or compute and impacts an adolescent’s or young
adult’s ability to comprehend, absorb, or retain what has been learned.
The most prevalent LD is in the area of reading, known as Dyslexia.
There are many types of Learning Differences (LD); it is a broad term.
Most often they fall into three broad categories:
•
Reading disabilities (Dyslexia).
•
Written language disabilities.
•
Math disabilities.
Learning Differences (LD) can also encompass: ADHD, processing
difficulties, and/or other learning challenges. Some individuals – despite
having an average or above average level of intelligence – have real
difficulty acquiring basic academic skills. These skills include those

---
## Page 46
needed for successful reading, writing, listening, speaking, and/or
math.
Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) is associated with poor
grades, poor reading and math standardized test scores, and
increased grade retention. ADHD is also associated with increased use
of school-based services, increased rates of detention and expulsion,
and ultimately with relatively low rates of high school graduation and
post-secondary education.
Other related categories include differences or disabilities that affect
attention, social skills, and “Executive Function” abilities (such as
deciding when to begin a task or how to finish the task within a time
frame), remembering instructions and directions, or even forgetting how
to act appropriately in a classroom setting or during day-to-day
activities with peers and family members.
The most common form of a Learning Difference (LD) diagnosis is
Dyslexia. Many adolescents and young adults with LD struggle with
reading. This makes it difficult to decode words accurately, which can
lead to trouble with fluency and comprehension. As students progress
through middle school, high school, and higher education, more and
more of the information they need to learn is presented in written
(through textbooks) or oral (through lecture) form. This multiplies the
difficulties they have in school and is usually the turning point for not
completing high school or continuing on any further with their
education.
DYSLEXIA
Dyslexia is characterized by difficulties with accurate and/or fluent word
recognition and by poor spelling and decoding abilities. Reading
disabilities affect 2% to 8% of adolescents and young adults. To read
successfully, students must:
•
Focus attention on the printed symbols.
•
Recognize the sounds associated with letters.

---
## Page 47
•
Understand words and grammar.
•
Build ideas and images.
•
Compare new ideas to what is already known.
•
Store ideas in memory.
A person with Dyslexia can have problems in any of the tasks needed
to be able to read. Other types of reading disabilities can appear in the
upper grades when the focus of reading shifts from word identification
to comprehension.
DYSGRAPHIA
Writing, too, involves several brain areas and functions. The brain
networks for vocabulary, grammar, hand movement, and memory must
all be working in a coordinated and simultaneous way in order to write.
A writing disorder can result from problems in any of these areas. For
example an adolescent or young adult with a writing disability, or an
expressive language disorder, might be unable to compose complete
and grammatically correct sentences no matter how hard they try.
Students often have great ideas in their mind but are unable to get it out
into a well-written form.
Here’s an example of Dysgraphia:
    DEAR BARBARA BIANCO
      THANKS SO MUCH FOR THE BEAUTIFUL
        BLUE FRISBEE YOU SENT ME FROM NORTH
CAROLINA ILOVEIT ITS BEAUTIFUL PLEASE
ALAWYS ONCE IN AWHILE LOOK FOR FRISBEES
TO SEND TO ME AT STORES SCHOOLS COLLIGES
WORK BUSINESSES WHERE EVER YOU GO ON
VACATION THRIFTSHOPS FLEAMARKETS
AND CHURCH BAZARS.

## Page 48
THANKYOU YOUR FRIEND JCN
DYSCALCULIA (DIFFICULTY WITH
MATHEMATICS)
Math involves recognizing numbers and symbols, memorizing facts,
aligning numbers, and understanding abstract concepts like place,
value, and fractions. Any of these segments of mathematics may cause
difficulty for adolescents, or young adults with developmental disorders
in this area. This difficulty is also called Dyscalculia. Problems with
numbers or in understanding basic arithmetic (adding, subtracting, etc.)
usually show up during the primary school years and exacerbate in
middle and high school. Disabilities that appear in the later grades are
more often tied to problems in reasoning rather than problems with
mathematics.
Students with Learning Differences are just as smart (and sometimes
smarter) than their peers, but experience difficulty learning in
conventional school settings. These young people have attention
difficulties, lack social skills, need to learn “Executive Function”
techniques, and address other developmental issues as well. It is not
uncommon for adolescents and young adults with a LD or AD (Attention
Difficulties) diagnosis to struggle with routine learning activities, to
experience social problems, or to be labeled negatively by their peers.
Similarly, those with social and other developmental problems may
struggle with peer relationships so significant that they lose interest in
school. When working with adolescents and young adults who have a
Learning Difference (LD) diagnosis, the emphasis in Autism and
Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit) is on
understanding an individual student’s strengths and assessing those
areas that need further development.
Assisting LD adolescents and young adults with skills for life, school,
work, and independent living presents many ongoing challenges. For
any variety of reasons, many of these young people may have

---
## Page 49
interrupted their education and/or are unable to take the steps
necessary to move forward toward finishing school, working, or job
training opportunities. They need help with the completion of a high
school diploma and then transitioning to college or toward specific
technical skills or vocational training. This Teaching Toolkit can provide
this much-needed assistance to those working with the LD population.
As attainment of the various components in each of the chapters
addressing each life skill occurs, this can – in turn – give these young
men and women the motivation necessary to assume adult roles and
responsibilities.
ALTERNATIVE WIRING ACROSS THE ASD/LD
SPECTRUM
The word “neurotypical” means being typically wired. For most of us
this means that in social and interpersonal situations when a signal
(message) comes in from point “A” (such as someone saying “good
morning, how are you?”), that signal is picked up by our ears which
sends the greeting to point “B.” Point “B” decodes the message and
initiates a socially appropriate “neurotypical” response (usually eye
contact with the person and the return greeting of (“I’m fine; how are
you?”).
An adolescent or young adult on the ASD or LD Spectrum who has a
fascination or “special interest” in bicycles relates differently to
someone who does not have this “special interest.” This person takes
point “A,” someone saying a simple “good morning, how are you?” and
most probably will not go to point “B” by saying “I’m fine; how are you?”
in a return greeting.
To this young person, this simple greeting may bounce around in his or
her brain from point “A to F to B to Z to J” (instead of the “neurotypical”
“A, B, C, and D”) eventually landing firmly on the word bicycle. In the
ASD or LD mind it immediately releases a wealth of information that

---
## Page 50
usually stuns the greeter who just happened to be passing by, saying
the innocuous “good morning, how are you?”
Rather than responding with a “neurotypical” response, the
alternatively wired response may come out as: “Well, it’s not a good
morning to me at all, since I have a broken bicycle, and it is especially
horrible since the part I need to fix it costs 50 dollars!” Seizing the
opportunity to share the wealth, an exhaustive, lengthy lecture on
bicycles, bicycle repair, and the exorbitant cost of replacement parts
may follow, leaving the casual greeter confused, but much better
educated about bicycles in general and specifically the repair of
bicycles.
The job of the educator, clinician, or professional working with these
young men and women is not to have them completely eliminate their
adherence to routines, “special interests,” or isolationist tendencies, but
to assist them in modifying maladaptive or non-functional patterns so
they can become more socially functional and productive.
The more stress an adolescent or young adult on the ASD or LD
Spectrum experiences, the greater and narrower their need for
structure will become. In a world where social and communication cues
are blurred, sensory systems lack a regulator, and feeling confused is
the norm, the need for consistency and predictability can quickly
degrade into compulsions. In order to help a student reduce a growing
sense of chaos and gain some kind of control or intervention is
necessary.
From a clinical standpoint, educators, clinicians, and professionals can
assist adolescents and young adults with an ASD/LD diagnosis by
helping them recognize the normal, functional, and practical aspects
that having a routine or structure gives them. The second step is
helping each person differentiate these routines and structures from
dysfunctional rigidity and obsessions. Once structure is in place,
adaptive routines can be put in motion to help minimize everyday
demands. A third step is to introduce the notion of improvisation by
encouraging these adolescents and young adults to add spontaneity as

---
## Page 51
a new routine and make flexibility part of their structure when things
don’t go as planned.
To the adolescent or young adult with an ASD or LD diagnosis – rituals,
routines, and structure provide safe havens. Transitions, flexibility, and
spontaneity are exciting but also very scary. Your job is to help them
understand their diagnosis from a clinical perspective.
Using the fund of strategies and techniques from this Teaching Toolkit
will allow these young men and women to head out into the unknown
and turn stressful situations into positive ones. As each individual gains
the life skills necessary to transition from dependence to independence
and turns a lack of understanding of their ASD/LD diagnosis to an
understanding of it, acceptance occurs and rapid and positive change
happens.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Adolescents and young adults with an ASD or
LD diagnosis are “alternatively” wired. One analogy I use when talking
with students about the difference between being “neurotypically vs.
alternatively wired” is that people on the ASD or LD Spectrum are like
the Androids of the world. I tell them that the single greatest
advantage to owning an Android mobile device is the ability to

---
## Page 52
customize it. It’s unique, a bit harder to use, but capable of many great
things. On the other hand, people who are not on the ASD or LD
Spectrum are like the iOSs of the world. These devices are
everywhere and very easy to use and just about anyone can operate
one. Neither one of these options for a mobile device is more or less
acceptable than the other. They are simply different from each other.
In the end, each device produces the same result but uses a different
format, analogy, and logic to get there. I, who reside firmly on the
ASD/LD Spectrum, proudly tell people “I am an Android.”
SENSORY PROCESSING DISORDERS (SPD)
Sensory processing (sometimes called sensory integration or SI) is a
term that refers to the way the nervous system receives messages from
the senses and turns these messages into appropriate motor or
behavioral responses. When someone bites into a hot dog, rides a
bicycle, or listens to music, completion of the activity requires
processing the sensation or “sensory integration.”
Sensory Processing Disorder (formerly known as sensory integration
dysfunction) is a condition that exists when sensory signals don’t get
organized into appropriate responses. Pioneering occupational
therapist and neuroscientist A. Jean Ayres, PhD, likened SPD to a
neurological traffic jam that prevents certain parts of the brain from
receiving the information needed to interpret sensory information
correctly. A person with SPD (Sensory Processing Disorder) and a
diagnosis of ASD (Autism Spectrum Disorder or LD (Learning
Differences) will find it difficult to process and act upon information
received through the senses. This creates challenges in performing
countless everyday tasks as well as motor clumsiness, behavioral
problems, anxiety, depression, and school failure.
Symptoms of Sensory Processing Disorder (SPD), like those of most
disorders, occur within a broad spectrum of severity. While most of us
have occasional difficulties processing sensory information those with
SPD have chronic difficulties that disrupt everyday life.

---
## Page 53
SPD can affect the adolescents and young people you work with in only
one sense – for example, just touch or just sight or just movement – or
in multiple senses. A young person with SPD may over-respond to
sensation and find clothing, physical contact, light, sound, food, or other
sensory input to be unbearable. Another might under-respond with little
or no reaction to stimulation, even pain or extreme hot and cold. Still
others exhibit an appetite for sensation that is in perpetual overdrive.
These young people are often misdiagnosed with Attention Deficit
Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD).
Sadly, misdiagnosis is common because many health care
professionals are not trained to recognize sensory issues. In general,
individuals with an ASD or LD diagnosis share a number of over and/or
under reactivities that can occur throughout the Spectrum of sensory
modalities. These include:
BRAIN WITHOUT SPD
•
Tactile (kinesthetic or touch).
•
Visual (sight).
•
Auditory (sound).
•
Olfactory (smell).

---
## Page 54
•
Gustatory (taste or texture).
•
Vestibular (balance and movement).
•
Proprioceptive (body position).
•
Body perception (body awareness).
•
Bilateral coordination (parallel use of both sides of body).
•
Praxis (motor planning).
•
Lateralization (hand preference).
•
Rhythmic synchrony (ability to achieve a rhythmic flow with
ourselves and others).
The three primary challenges brought about through sensory triggers
include: hypersensitivity; hyposensitivity; mixed hyper/hyposensitivity.
Here is a list of the most common sensory issues that adolescents and
young adults experience.
SENSORY PROCESSING CHALLENGES
•
Extra sensitive to touch – does not like to be touched or can’t be
touched enough.
•
Sensitivity to sounds – may cover ears when the same noises
don’t bother others.
•
Picky eaters – will only eat one or two familiar foods.
•
Avoidance of sensory stimulation – won’t put hands in anything
messy or only wears certain clothes that feel right.
•
Uneasiness with movement – fear of amusement park rides,
playground equipment, and being turned upside down.
•
Hyperactivity – can’t be still during the day or get to sleep at
night.
•
Hypoactivity – inability to respond in any way, when needed, i.e.,
zoning out.

---
## Page 55
•
Fear of crowds – crowded areas bother him or her to the point of
public melt-downs.
BRAIN WITH SPD
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Most people who know me know that I am on
the ASD/LD Spectrum. Specifically, I have Asperger’s Disorder (AD)
with sensory processing challenges. I grew up in a really chaotic
home and had to find some semblance of calm and quiet to help me,
help myself. The vignette regarding my own sensory issues is adapted
from a story that appeared in my first book, “Made for Good Purpose
…”
Many of the traits and symptoms that I portray in the story appear on
the sensory processing checklist. What is even more interesting (or
stunning) for readers to know is that I did not receive an actual
diagnosis until I was well into my fifties and I had been this way for
most of my life.
We all live in a world that is saturated with sensory overloads. Sensory
overloads and distortions are inescapable. However, most people
(myself excluded) are wired typically. For the most part “neurotypicals”
are capable of blocking out these annoying stimulations, distractions,

---
## Page 56
and annoyances. Adolescents and young adults who are wired
differently cannot. Further, if “neurotypical” people are experiencing
sensory overload – they tell someone – who may assist them and
make things better. However for someone whose sensory systems are
tuned to a 50 or 60 out of 100 (or even higher) vs. a 10 or 20 out of
100, their options can quickly revert to inappropriate behaviors.
“Young Michael”
As a child, I was afraid of loud noises and would cry when I
heard a siren. I ate only peanut butter or butter sandwiches for
lunch throughout elementary school. I would not eat fruits or
vegetables (a texture thing). I hated holidays because my entire
family would be with relatives, friends, and neighbors and the
house would be full of people, noise, and a changed schedule
for the day.
I was always a strict rule follower. In a family where everyone
smoked and stayed in bed until noon on weekends, I was up
early. On Sundays, my family always went to church but
because of all the late sleepers we would always arrive late.
This meant we had to stand at the back as all seats were taken.
These were major rule violations to my way of thinking and
caused me much distress. Then, to make matters worse, I
would be squished into our big old car where everyone lit up
cigarettes (smell violations) and talked (noise violations).
Needless to say, I was a bundle of nerves each Sunday. But
somehow, at this very young age, I figured out a way to cope
and lessen my stress. I decided I would get up early and walk a
few miles each Sunday so that I could attend church all by
myself.

---
## Page 57
My being able to take care of young Michael surfaced as I
devised my own accommodations (a term that has only come
into being in the last decade or so) to cope. The walk provided
me with peace and quiet; and as an added bonus, I could have
silence and calm as I dressed and prepared for this weekly
ritual. When I returned home, I had the whole house to myself –
bliss!
“Neurotypicals” take for granted the acquiring of socially appropriate
responses and tools that we began collecting as infants. For
adolescents and young adults with an ASD or an LD diagnosis these
options are not always clear cut, available, or taught to them.
Understanding just how wide-ranging and diverse the ASD/LD
Spectrum can be by using a specific clinical diagnosis to pinpoint what
each student – as an individual – needs is essential to using this
Teaching Toolkit. Doing so will allow educators, clinicians, and
professionals to help each person obtain the skills necessary for
success not only in life, but in school, work, and independent living.
Looking at an Asperger’s Disorder
(AD) Diagnosis from a Clinical
Perspective
Adolescents diagnosed with Asperger’s Disorder (AD) are typically:
•
Intuitive.
•
Holistic.
•
Subjective.
•
Random.
•
Synthesizing.

---
## Page 58
•
Prone to look at wholes.
Adolescents and young adults who carry an AD diagnosis show the
following diagnostic features and these symptoms often started before
age three:
•
Impairment in social interaction.
•
Impairment in communication .
•
Repetitive and restricted Interests.
•
Cognitive ability in average to above average range.
•
Cognitive characteristics.
•
Sensory differences.
•
Motor skills differences.
Those diagnosed with Asperger’s Disorder (AD) have qualitative
impairment in social interactions resulting in:
•
Decreased understanding of nonverbal behaviors.
•
Peer relationship problems.
•
Difficulty with spontaneous sharing .
•
Lack of social or emotional reciprocity.
AN ASPERGER’S DIAGNOSIS WITHIN AN
ASD DIAGNOSIS
Those diagnosed with Asperger’s Disorder with the ASD classification
