have difficulty in varying degrees with:
Pragmatic language
•
The ability to make judgments about the listener’s needs and
capabilities in order to regulate speech style and content
according to situational needs of listener.

---
## Page 59
•
The ability to apply the rules of discourse in order to engage in
cooperative conversational exchanges.
•
Sharing interests in common with peers.
•
Interpreting nonverbal indications of social norms such as turn-
taking.
•
Flexibility in topic choice.
•
Coordination of verbal and nonverbal expression.
•
Appropriate prosody (speed, volume, tone).
•
Maintaining a focus on a topic for a sustained period of time.
•
Stereotypical language.
ADDITIONAL BARRIERS TO
UNDERSTANDING COMMUNICATION
Adolescents and young adults with Asperger’s Disorder (AD) have
trouble with paralinguistic features of language. These features are the
inflection, emotional vocal tone, facial expression, gesture, posture, and
syntax. They also have trouble understanding meaning, such as:
abstract content, poor inferential thinking, and egocentric definitions of
language. They often interpret language very literally. Here are some
examples of taking things literally:
•
“You should know that I no longer belong to the club.”
•
“I’d like to wish you good luck.”
•
“My feet are killing me.”
•
“Is this written in stone?”2
Twachtman-Cullen 1998, p.206
Examples of restricted, repetitive behavior, interests, and activities:
•
All-encompassing preoccupation.

---
## Page 60
•
Inflexible routines.
•
Stereotypical motor mannerisms.
•
Preoccupation with parts of objects.
Characteristics of thinking and learning with an Asperger’s Disorder
(AD) diagnosis:
•
Excessive focus on details.
•
Difficulty with meaning of concept and knowing what’s relevant,
often referred to as “Central Coherence Theory.”
•
Distractibility.
•
Concrete thinking.
•
Difficulty combining or integrating ideas.
•
Difficulty with organization and sequencing.
Here is an example from a student that portrays the characteristics of
thinking and learning associated with an Asperger’s Disorder
diagnosis.
Novel of Love
“I love you,” she said.
“I love you more than I can say.”
“I love you, too,” he answered.
“My love for you is higher than the highest mountain which is Mount Everest which is over
29,000 feet high.
My love for you is deeper than the deepest ocean which is the Marianas trench which is
over 36,000 feet deep!!!!
If you were to drop a stone the size of your head into water 36,000 feet (10,900 meters)
deep, it would take the stone about an hour to reach the bottom.”
- Anonymous student
And this is an example of a sequencing problem.

---
## Page 61
Understanding the ASD/LD
Diagnosis from a Teaching
Standpoint
The statistics tell us that:
•
35% of students identified with Learning Differences (LD) drop
out of high school.
•
Less than 2% of students with an LD diagnosis who do
graduate from high school attend a four-year college.
•
40% is the national average of youth with an ASD diagnosis who
attend college or post-secondary career preparation/training.
•
Only 15% of college graduates on the ASD/LD Spectrum will
live independently.
•
Adolescents and young adults with Learning Differences (LD)
receive lower earnings and have higher high school dropout

---
## Page 62
rates. If an LD student graduates from high school… attendance
beyond this achievement it is usually not viable.
•
Young adults with an ASD or LD diagnosis who do not seek
careers or college will in all probability not live independently.
•
25–50% is the national average of any paid employment for
young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum.
Regardless of the educator’s professional training, background, and
motivation, it is futile to try and meet all the needs presented by the
growing numbers of adolescents and young adults with an ASD or LD
diagnosis. However, using a variety of tried and true strategies,
materials, and ideas that will help high school and post-secondary
students learn to function effectively and achieve rich and varied
personal gains is realistic.
The methodology that underpins Autism and Learning Differences (An
Active Learning Teaching Toolkit) is based on active, participatory
learning. In other words, learning that involves adolescents and young
adults in the process of obtaining and retaining knowledge, at each
step along the way. By developing the unique and considerable talents
and gifts that each young person on the ASD/LD Spectrum possesses
– with their input – educators can mentor each student in learning how
to function in and contribute to society using these attributes and
knowledge.
Understanding the ASD or LD diagnosis from a teaching standpoint
allows the educator to assist on the Spectrum students by providing a
scripted, uniform way to impart knowledge, and measure progress.
Teachers also glean a tangible way of tracking progress and advice on
what to do when changes are needed or new strategies have to be
developed or devised.
When changes are required, teachers who understand the importance
of the clinical diagnosis from a teaching standpoint are able to share
this knowledge with colleagues. This in turn helps others become more
effective educators of students who are on the ASD/LD Spectrum.
Comprehending the special needs, strengths, and unique perspectives

---
## Page 63
of each ASD or LD adolescent or young adult being taught becomes a
cornerstone for maximizing success. This is the value of integrating an
understanding of the clinical diagnosis into the teaching perspective.
Teachers working with secondary and post-secondary students on the
ASD or LD Spectrum need to be able to support each student’s
individual diagnosis in the following areas:
•
Educational.
•
Vocational.
•
Physical development.
•
Wellness.
•
Integrating creative arts.
•
Medical.
•
Therapeutic.
•
Counseling and guidance.
•
Social mentoring and coaching.
From a teaching standpoint we know that ASD/LD students at the high
school and college/university level are thinking and retaining knowledge
in unique ways that are very different from their “neurotypical” peers.
Adolescents and young adults on the ASD Spectrum and often those
on the LD Spectrum are at a maturity level that is approximately three
to five years younger than their chronological ages.
LEARNING STYLES
Thoroughly understanding the criteria for various learning styles and
then learning about brain-based teaching strategies will allow
educators to assess students in the classroom setting. Teachers can
then create strength-based approaches that integrate the maturity level
of the ASD/LD student and his or her preferred learning style into an
overall teaching plan.

---
## Page 64
There are three types of learning styles that are generally accepted as
primary. 
These 
include: 
(1) 
visual, 
(2) 
auditory, 
and 
(3)
tactile/kinesthetic.
See the learning styles handout on page 14.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: I am a visual learner. In my case, the best way
for me to retain knowledge is to take notes using drawings. This
technique was developed by Mark Twain in 1899 (Twain 1917).
Mark Twain’s Explanation of Visual
Drawings
•
The first one is a haystack - below it a rattlesnake - and it told
me where to begin to talk about ranch-life in Carson Valley.
•
The second one told me where to begin the talk about a
strange and violent wind that used to burst upon Carson City
from the Sierra Nevada every afternoon at two o’clock.
•
The third picture, as you can easily perceive, is lightning; its
duty was to remind me when it was time to begin talk about
San Francisco weather, where there is no lightning - nor
thunder, either.
The visual notes I created (see the illustration below) are based on a
presentation I was about to give at a conference. To prepare for my
presentation, the first thing I did was to take a piece of paper and fold

---
## Page 65
it until I had 16 boxes. Then, I visually wrote down what was important
for me to know and remember in order to relay information about CIP’s
work with young people. Here’s what I was thinking when I drew some
of the boxes.
As the visual notes progress you’ll read about box 2 – and how
important it is for students to separate (a process called
individualization) from home and parents and take steps toward
independence. In box 3 – I talk to the audience about how hard this is.
Then I go on to tell them it is like cutting what I like to call “a steel
umbilical cord” (see box 4).
As my lecture continued I got to talking about the “Donkey Rule,” in
box 10, the “Hidden Curriculum” (see the bathroom door marked W in
box 12), and then on to PCPs (Person Centered Plans) in box 13
(these three things will be detailed later in the book). I then went on
with my analogy about students and tulips in box 14 (i.e., some bloom
early … some bloom late … but all are beautiful). Box 15 reminded
me to thank everyone for coming and the last box was my cue to end
my presentation (closure) by handing out my Think Positive stones
(little talismans that I create) to remind everyone to shift their thinking
and turn negatives into positives.

---
## Page 66
Adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum might cross
over all modalities with no particular preference or may learn best from
a combination of two or three learning styles. The key is to ensure that
preferred learning styles of on the Spectrum students in various
classroom settings and learning environs are identified.
Learning Styles
There are three types of learning styles that are generally
accepted as primary.
Visual Learners:

---
## Page 67
•
Tend to think in pictures (they create vivid mental
images to gather and retain knowledge) .
•
Are skilled at reading and understanding visual
information (they excel at absorbing information in
technical manuals, charts, maps, graphs).
•
Enjoy art, photographs, movies, and videos.
•
Enjoy 
assembling 
puzzles, 
painting, 
sketching,
reading, and writing.
•
Are good at noticing visual details, understanding
metaphors and analogies.
•
Often excel in graphic arts, and in finding solutions to
problems by examining visual details.
•
Learn by watching others perform activities.
Auditory Learners:
•
Process information verbally and perform best when
using words and language.
•
Typically develop better than average vocabularies
and often have superior speaking abilities.
•
Think in words rather than pictures.
•
Tend to be good listeners.
•
Are good public speakers and/or conversationalists
who are skilled at verbal humor and are good story
tellers.
•
Have a good memory for auditory information.
•
Do well when someone explains things verbally.
Tactile/Kinesthetic Learners:

---
## Page 68
•
Learn by doing.
•
Benefit most from action-oriented activities.
•
Are good at performing mechanical tasks.
•
Have good coordination and are skilled at performing
physical activities.
•
Learn by interacting with the environment and
handling or touching things.
•
Often express their feelings through body movements.
•
Are good at repairing, fixing, and assembling things.
•
Are good at sports, working with their hands, and
getting mechanical things to work.
•
They do well when allowed to perform activities on
their own.
Other learning styles include:
•
Spatial.
•
Logical.
•
Mathematical.
•
Musical.
•
Interpersonal or social.
•
Intrapersonal or solitary.
Adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum
might cross over all modalities with no particular preference
or may learn best from a combination of two or three learning
styles.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 69
CREATIVE THINKING ASSESSMENTS AND
THE LEARNING PYRAMID
Buffalo State College has done a lot of research in helping educators
determine styles for “Creative Thinking.” The Bethel Institute in Bethel,
Maine created the “Learning Pyramid” almost 40 years ago – proving
via brain theory, the best way to ensure the retention of information is
through a participatory model.
New work on “Creative Thinking” that has come out of FourSight™,
based in Evanston, Illinois, is illustrated by the example on page 16 that
was published in the New York Times, February 9, 2014, Learning to
Think Differently, by Laura Pappano. The article states:
“Understanding the creative thinking patterns of students allows
educators to group like-minded thinkers together and to add an element
in their teaching practice that encompasses these differences.”
“Creative Thinking” tools help individuals and teams solve problems.
Page 16 has an abridged version of the FourSight™ assessment to
identify thinking styles. Asking students to participate in taking it will
allow educators to look at where students are coming from. The
answers can be used to start designing plans to impart knowledge in a
way that that takes advantage of an individual’s “Creative Thinking”
styles.
CREATIVITY STUDIES
Once thinking and learning styles are determined, integrating the
concept of creativity studies to further understand the impact of an
ASD/LD diagnosis in the classroom setting is a natural next step.
“Creative Study” is the ability to spot problems and devise smart
solutions. Gerard J. Puccio, chairman of the internationally known
Center for Studies in Creativity at Buffalo State College (which has the
oldest Creative Studies program in existence (1967)), tells us: “The
reality is that to survive in a fast changing world you need to be

---
## Page 70
creative. That is why you are seeing more attention to creativity in
universities; the marketplace is demanding it. Critical thinking has long
been regarded as the essential skill for success but that is not enough.
Creativity moves beyond mere synthesis and evaluation and is the
higher order.”
“The Bathroom Bodyguard”
This is Mathew Lahue’s story (Mathew is a student at Buffalo
State College). You enter a public restroom and the stall lock is
broken. To Mathew, this problem was troublesome and
stressful. After dealing with this uncomfortable situation for
years, Mathew came up with a solution. It’s called the Bathroom
Bodyguard. Standing before his Buffalo State College
classmates and Professor Cyndi Burnett, Mr. Lahue displayed a
device he concocted from a large washer, metal ring, wall hook,
rubber-bands, and a Lincoln Log. Slide the ring in the crack and
twist. The door stays shut. The device fits into a purse or pocket.
The point: The world is full of problems that are very
troublesome to some people and not to others. Allowing
students (especially ASD/LD adolescents and young adults) to
think about, present, and find solutions to things that bother
them, while being acknowledged, listened to, and understood,
uncovers a whole lot of potential for problem solving and
solutions. And sometimes the bonus is finding solutions not only
to things others may not have thought about – but to things that
may actually need solutions in order to master the skills needed
for life, school, work, and independent living.

---
## Page 71
FourSight™ Thinking Assessment
(abridged version)
I’m most motivated by…
(a) a need for clear understanding of the
facts
(b) a great idea
(c) an opportunity to perfect an existing
solution
(d) a job to do
I learn best through…
(a) facts, research, data
(b) stories, ideas, concepts
(c) evaluating options

---
## Page 72
(d) trying things out
I like to spend time…
(a) working with information
(b) thinking about new possibilities
(c) making things “just right”
(d) testing, prototyping, doing
Score: How many times did your answer correspond
to: (a) ____ (b) ____ (c) ____ (d) ____ ?
Profiles: A high number may suggest a preference for
one of these thinking styles.
(a) Clarifier: cautious, structured, thinker who likes to
gather data to understand the reality and to identify
problems, gaps, and opportunities
(b) Ideator: playful, original thinker, who likes to see
the “big picture” and make new connections that
may break the paradigm
(c) Developer: detailed planner who likes to evaluate
and perfect the best version of a solution and
anticipate how it will move forward with the most
success
(d) Implementer: confident, action-oriented risk taker
who likes to learn by doing
No preference? Many people are not limited to one
thinking style. More than 20% of people who take the
assessment select a different letter for each question.
Their strength is in moving evenly through the process
and harmonizing with the group.

---
## Page 73
You can help students further understand their
learning styles by relating and looking at the links and
conclusions that can be drawn as they start the
process of accepting and working with their diagnosis
in the school setting.
Permission granted to reproduce this abridged version of the
FourSight™ Thinking Assessment from: Puccio, G.J. (1999)
Creative Problem-Solving Preferences: Their Identification
and Implications. Creativity and Innovation Management 8,
171–178. Current information on FourSight is available at
www.FourSightonline.com.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
In 2010, creativity was the most crucial factor for success found by IBM
in a survey of 1500 chief executives in 33 industries. It is the most used
buzzword on LinkedIn, and we know that some students on the
ASD/LD Spectrum thrive when allowed to explore their own creativity.
That’s what is so great about incorporating this strategy into everyday
teaching. Traditional academic disciplines still matter, but as content
knowledge evolves, process skills (see the “Learning Pyramid” below)
become essential building blocks to learning.
Getting back to Laura Pappano’s New York Times, February 9, 2014,
article Learning to Think Differently, “The Bathroom Bodyguard”
illustrates how creativity plays out in a real-time and place example
from the Center for Studies in Creativity.
Laura Pappano’s article ends with some things she observed in the
classroom that day. Students engaged in Creative Thinking Studies
posted their thoughts about thinking creatively on a white board at the
end of the class that day. Their reflection included the following words:
•
Intrinsicly (sic) motivated.
•
Implementer.

---
## Page 74
•
Seek quantity.
•
Cognitive thinker.
•
Divergent.
•
Scamper.
•
Clarifier.
•
Divergent and convergent thinking.
•
Habits and blocks.
•
Wild and unusual.
•
Creative preferences.
•
Brain writing.
•
You don’t shoot down my ideas.
•
Seek the unusual and accept that maybe not so unusual after
all.

---
## Page 75
Used with permission from NTL Institute for Applied Behavioral Science, 300 N. Lee Street,
#300, Alexandria, VA 22314.
THE LEARNING PYRAMID
The “Learning Pyramid” was created and developed in the 1970s in
Bethel, Maine. It is a tool that gives the educator, clinician or
professional an overview of how brain-theory-based learning works.
Experience has shown that young people who have been enabled to
take a responsible role in partnership with the adults are able to retain,
learn, and implement knowledge more effectively. The concepts that
underpin the “Learning Pyramid” are referred to as “Active Learning”
throughout this book and augment much of the pedagogy in Autism and
Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit).
ASD or LD students who are offered learning experiences that develop
their concepts, skills, and dispositions through active participation
achieve academic and life skills with a 90% comprehension rate. The
“Learning Pyramid” is a powerful teaching tool and is especially
effective when ASD/LD students are encouraged to be part of the
learning process that leads to the retention of knowledge.
PREVENTIVE/PRO-SOCIAL TEACHING
Teaching, in a preventive and pro-social manner, builds a foundation of
knowledge that establishes the basis for a productive relationship and a
respectful learning environment between teachers and students. Time
spent incorporating preventive and pro-social techniques can produce
great results and allow those working with adolescents and young
adults diagnosed with ASD or LD to move forward at a quicker pace.
These concepts support teaching in this manner.

---
## Page 76
Following Instructions
It is important for students to accept the premise that they are
going to be asked to complete certain tasks and to report back
regarding the completion of tasks. Start off with simple requests
about what you want the student to do:
•
Ask the adolescent or young adult (or group, class) to focus
their attention and look at you.
•
Give an instruction.
•
Ask to have the instruction acknowledged by replying ok or
yes, I’ll do that.
•
Have the person, group, or class do the task.
•
Ask for the participants in the learning activity to report back
to you when the task is completed or the instructions are
followed.
•
Give only positive feedback regarding the interaction.
Accepting Criticism
It is vital that adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD
Spectrum learn how to accept feedback (either positive or
negative) from those who teach, mentor, or work with them.
Practicing accepting feedback prepares these young people to

---
## Page 77
be good students, good employees, good friends, and ultimately
good partners in a relationship.
•
Ask the adolescent or young adult to focus their attention
and look at you.
•
Tell the individual that you are going to give them some
feedback and ask to have the feedback acknowledged by
saying ok or I understand.
•
Reinforce that all questions are intelligent.
•
Explain why it is important to be able to accept feedback.
•
Give feedback by using a calm voice and sure manner.
•
Give only positive feedback regarding the interaction.
TEACHING TIPS
PERSONAL INSIGHT: I know first-hand how hard following
instructions can be for adolescents and young adults. Based on my
own experience as an adult with an ASD/LD diagnosis, I always say to
educators: Do not assume that those with ASD or LD understand
something just because they parrot back what they have heard; offer
added explanation then simplify directions, expectations, and
outcomes, especially when concepts are abstract. Using multiple
modalities will help ensure that instructions are understood.
Oriane Robison, a staff member from CIP Bloomington, offers this
advice: “Keep in mind that adolescents and young adults on the
ASD/LD Spectrum have difficulty breaking down large or complex
tasks and the instructions that go with these tasks. They often find the
request to follow instructions overwhelming. Show students how to
break tasks into smaller components then try giving simplified
instructions in multiple ways (verbally, visually, and if possible, by
demonstration). This really helps.”

---
## Page 78
Joan Williamson, the Program Director at CIP Brevard, says: “When a
student is facing a daunting ‘to -do’ list accompanied by many
instructions on how to do the tasks at hand, he or she can easily tune
out and shut down. When you see this happening, have the student
knock out the task he or she dreads most … first. Once this hurdle is
cleared – and the task is completed – stress lessens and the student
is able to go on to the next task (which is not as formidable), more
easily.”
When problems do arise it is wonderful to have pre-taught the
adolescent or young adult how to solve the problem in a rational
manner. By teaching this skill up front, those working with the ASD/LD
student are being proactive and not reactive. Future problems are
prevented and teachers can impart a skill that the young person will
have for a lifetime.
•
Explain that you are going to teach a problem solving technique
that can be used when having problems with another person.
•
Explain why it is important to learn this skill.
•
Assist student in coming up with solutions.
Explain the importance of each of the following components:
•
Sitting down.
•
Looking straight at the person.
•
Using a calm regular voice tone.
•
Using no abusive language.
•
Explaining the problem clearly.
•
Staying on topic.
•
Trying to find an acceptable solution.
•
Compromising if possible.
•
Leaving off on a positive note.

---
## Page 79
THE TEACH-ABILITY QUOTIENT
Teaching insight: The question we hear all the time at our Centers,
especially from parents, is: How do you get my (son or daughter) to
do ______? (Fill in the blank!) The answer is we don’t.
Our problem is that we spend too much time being human do-ers
rather than being human beings. I often tell parents and staff that it
always comes down to the same solution: setting the example and
then being patient enough to wait for each individual person you are
working with to respond.
At CIP, we can help our students acquire skills, and get further faster,
by remaining teachable and open to change ourselves. You may ask:
Why does it always require me (the teacher or professional imparting
knowledge) to do the work? I always reply: “Because it actually is
good for you, and in the long run your personal misery quotient will be
lower if you just start being open to change, and remaining teachable
now.”
The paraphrased saying might be: “Do unto yourself what you are
asking your students to do unto themselves.” Ask yourself the following
questions and see if your individual “Teach-ability Quotient” is high
enough to set good examples for the adolescents and young adults you
work with daily:
•
Do I feel comfortable asking for advice?
•
Do I easily admit I am wrong?
•
Am I able to receive criticism without being hurt?
•
Do I enjoy listening to other people’s thoughts and opinions
without feeling the need to express my own?
•
Am I able to disagree with someone without feeling like I have to
debate the issue?
•
Am I willing to look at all sides of a situation before I form an
opinion?

---
## Page 80
•
Do I have to justify my opinions all of the time?
•
Am I able to let people make their own mistakes so they can
learn from them?
•
Am I willing to try new things?
•
Do I accept my own mistakes without having to be perfect?
•
Am I willing and flexible enough to change plans at the last
minute?
•
Can I be a team player?
•
Can I listen and not judge?
Teaching insight: Anthony Milla, a Lead Life Skills coordinator from
CIP Brevard, submitted the story “Overcoming My Own Rigidity”, that
illustrates the “Teach-ability Quotient” and its 13 points perfectly. As
the story unfolds it becomes a significant teachable moment for
Anthony and his CIP Social Skills Team.
USING THE RULES OF ENGAGEMENT
As educators, clinicians, and professionals working with adolescents
and young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum, the “Rules of
Engagement” on pages 22 and 23, are especially important for each
teacher, mentor, role model, or advisor to follow. These basic tenets for
helping ASD/LD adolescents and young adults achieve success and
master the skills for life, school, work, and independent living in
subsequent sections of this book will provide yet another link on the
chain that joins the ASD/LD clinical diagnosis to the teaching
environment in a healthy and positive way.

---
## Page 81
“Overcoming My Own Rigidity”
Our students arrive at CIP with many different areas to improve
upon. One of the more common problems I see is an issue with
rigidity, specifically – the inability to be flexible with scheduling,
social skill classes, or changing routines. A less common and
more difficult characteristic that CIP students arrive with is
intolerance for others. Sometimes this presents as a student
having a hard time seeing eye-to-eye with others. In more
extreme cases, it presents as a student having a hard time even
being around others because the thought of listening to their
opinions is extremely adverse. In these cases, our “Social Skills
Team” has to be creative with CIP’s programming, so that our
students buy into the program and get motivated to learn things
that will help them succeed when they leave.
This was the case with Matt, a student who had a hard time
attending high school because he couldn’t bring himself to deal
with his peers. The CIP Social Skills Team did not look back
and ask why he felt this way; instead we looked forward and
asked: “How can we help him overcome these obstacles?”
My first few meetings with Matt were “Executive Function” (EF)
appointments to work on skills like organization, prioritization,
and task initiation. In this case, I would stand outside of his
bedroom trying to get him to participate and he would stare at
me through a small crack in the door. As you can imagine, Matt
and I didn’t get a lot done.
Most appointments consisted of me asking Matt if his room was
clean, if his homework was done, and if he’d taken a shower
that day. These queries were generally met with disdain and a
grunt for yes or a closing of the door in my face for no. He lived
in a two-bedroom apartment with a quiet roommate who kept to
himself and this suited Matt just fine; he had no desire to make

---
## Page 82
friends or allow others to get to know him. It seemed to me that
the thought of dealing with others was almost physically painful
for him. At that point I could see that there was no value for Matt
in scheduling evening appointments and he certainly wasn’t
going to let me into his room, because there were no issues to
work on there, as far as he was concerned.
As much as I’d like to take credit for Matt’s initial improvements,
I cannot. Matt was a very career-and-academic focused
student, and it wasn’t until he saw the value of life skills as a way
to improve in these areas himself that he began to buy into my
piece of the program.
Eventually Matt was willing to let me into his room for a limited
amount of time. Aside from the basic cleaning issues, I
discovered that he was spending 40–60 hours a week playing a
favorite video game. This was affecting the amount of time he
had to work on his studies and the amount of effort he was
willing to put into his life skills tasks. This became the target of
my EF program for Matt.
I started by attempting to limit the amount of time he was
spending on the computer. Matt saw this as my attempt to
control his personal time and didn’t understand the correlation
between the time he spent playing his game and his lack of
overall success in the program. He quickly reverted back to not
participating in his EF appointments. I approached the Social
Skills Team about the problem I was having with Matt and we
spent a significant amount of time brainstorming ways not only to
change his behavior but also to help him understand why
change was necessary. Eventually the “team” came up with a
plan to help him schedule his responsibilities around his gaming
instead of forcing him to change his gaming schedule.
I came up with a form that Matt filled out chronicling his game
play each day. From there we scheduled tasks in a way that

---
## Page 83
didn’t interfere with his computer time. Matt began to see
improvement in his school work as he (by his own volition)
increased the amount of time he was spending on homework.
He also started to spend more time out of his room, which
helped increase his social interaction with his roommate. By
doing this, he found out he actually got along with him. As he
started to participate in a simplified EF program with me, we
began to gain momentum. Matt started to participate in other
areas of life skills like cooking instruction and occasionally
weekend activities.
By the end of Matt’s time at CIP, he was living in a house with
three other students. He had decreased his game play to three
to four hours per day and we continued to schedule his
appointments around his computer time.
In the end it was me and my team of co-workers who needed to
overcome “our cumulative rigidity,” in order to find a way to work
with Matt. In this way we did what was best for Matt and did not
try to make him conform to our idea of how a student should act.
By working together as “a team” we were able to come up with a
program that allowed Matt to buy into the program without giving
anything up. This was a valuable teachable moment … for all of
us.
Anthony Milla
Lead Life Skills Coordinator, CIP Brevard

---
## Page 84

---
## Page 85
The Rules of Engagement
The ultimate goal in looking at an ASD or LD diagnosis from a teaching
perspective is to equip those who teach, counsel, or mentor
adolescents and young adults with a variety of tools and strategies
designed to facilitate learning across the criteria that make up an
individual’s diagnosis. Implementing the suggestions and advice on

---
## Page 86
pages 22 and 23 will allow those who work with ASD or LD students to
impart knowledge in an appropriate and specific way … while students
are more teachable and willing to listen and learn. Using this
information will allow for a more productive relationship with your ASD
and/or LD students and develop a respectful learning environment.
Time spent on implementing and using this information can produce
great results and allow educators, clinicians, and professionals
imparting knowledge to move forward at a quicker pace while ensuring
that the specific accommodations each ASD or LD learner needs is
provided.
Understanding the ASD/LD
Diagnosis from a Parenting
Standpoint
It is valuable and insightful for educators, clinicians, professionals, and
the high schools, colleges, and universities they work in to look at the
impact of an ASD or LD diagnosis from a parenting perspective.
Parents of adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum are
unsure about what the future holds for their children and they wonder if
their son or daughter will ever be able to graduate from high school;
start or finish college; get or keep a job; begin or maintain relationships;
or live without their emotional or financial assistance. As loving parents,
they see their sensitive and intelligent children trapped by anger and
resentment. They see adolescents and young adults who are inflexible
and often feel hopeless and isolated. As these young men and women
withdraw deeper and deeper into a shell of low self-esteem and
insecurity, parents feel powerless to change this.
Knowing that these young people often blame their parents for their
failures and frustrations – having compassion and understanding for
what a parent has gone through, must be going through, and will
continue to go through by looking at the diagnosis from this standpoint

---
## Page 87
helps those working with ASD/LD students to bring a sense of empathy
and compassion into the education equation.
As teachers, clinicians, and professionals working with these young
men and women, we know from experience that parents of adolescents
or young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum have consciously or even
subconsciously taken on a more involved role in their son’s or
daughter’s life. They have had to advocate endlessly with teachers and
administrators and, at times, to assume the role of a social worker or
an activity director as well.
These parents often feel compelled and driven to hold on to and
manage their son’s or daughter’s life on a daily basis, especially in the
education arena. Parents certainly understand how vital it is for their
children to have more autonomy and more self-direction; it is just really
hard to let this happen, especially in a school setting. Therefore, they
easily fall into what is referred to as the “Parent Trap.”
THE “PARENT TRAP”
The focus on helping dependent children to be successful with the
many facets of adolescence and young adulthood (especially during
the high school and college or career training years) has a hidden trap.
These are the parents whose work and dedication to the success of
their children’s lives, education, and/or career choices is all time-
consuming and exhausting.
Making things run smoothly has become ingrained in the relationship
with their adolescent or young adult child and this is especially true
concerning every facet of their son’s or daughter’s education.
However, at times, the chronological age of the parents outdistances
their ability to keep up with all that is going on in their adolescent’s or
young adult’s life, causing parents to become more focused and intent
in assisting in the education process. Parents are doing things to
assure success for their children. However, this encourages the

---
## Page 88
emerging young adult to become dependent upon his or her parents for
his or her achievements. This is the conflict, the “Parent Trap.”
Parents need to learn to do less for their high school and college age
children and this is where those working with them – in an educational
setting – can help. Adolescents and young adults will need to make
decisions and judgments on their own, and parents need to be assured
by educators, clinicians, and professionals working with their child, that
they are able to do so – albeit one step at a time.
The most important thing those working with students on the ASD/LD
Spectrum can do is to frame things positively for parents. Realizing that
these parents have had more to say about their child’s life than parents
whose children are not on the Spectrum helps those imparting and
teaching life skills understand that this dependency was helpful before
high school and benefited the child during his or her formative years.
What worked in elementary and middle school now has a detrimental
effect and can hinder progress in high school, college, in the
workplace, or at job training programs.
Talking about the process of separation and individuation while
understanding each individual parent’s symbiotic relationship with their
child will allow each parent, in his or her own fashion, to break this
dependency and build a more interdependent relationship with his or
her child.
The role of the educator, clinician, or professional, in collaborating with
parents, is to understand that a parent’s role – up to this point in his or
her adolescent’s or young adult’s life – has been that of total immersion.
When adolescence and young adulthood begin, parents need to
transition out of this role and allow educators, clinicians, and
professionals who are trained to work with the ASD/LD population –
and who understand the ASD/LD diagnosis – to assist in the transition
between dependence and independence.
Helping parents understand that adolescents and young adults on the
ASD/LD Spectrum need other people whom they can rely on for advice
when questions about what to do arise, or problems need solutions, is

---
## Page 89
an excellent place to start. As choices and options are presented to
parents by those trying to assist their emerging adult children in
obtaining life skills and an education, they will realize that it is
absolutely fine and appropriate to have their son or daughter ask for
advice and decision-making help from teachers, mentors, advisors, and
others.
Here are some things educators, clinicians, and professionals can do to
frame this process positively for parents:
•
Parents of adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD
Spectrum need to be involved in each step of their son or
daughter’s education within reason and within a set of
standards and norms. Fostering student-run Individualized
Education Plans (IEPs), taking advantage of scheduled parent-
teacher conferences, and one-on-one meetings provides an
opportunity to give parents advice, encouragement, and
guidance so that they can take steps to help them understand
their child’s diagnosis in relationship to school, counseling, and
advising. Reiterate that parent involvement is needed but that it
is important that the pattern of hyper-vigilance be much more

---
## Page 90
balanced so that their son or daughter can transition to
independence successfully.
•
Supporting parents and assuring them that their child can be
successful in high school, college, career training, and beyond
by allowing their adolescent or young adult to ask questions on
his or her own – or go to a teacher or advisor or tutor to help him
or her figure out the answers to problems – is necessary and
appropriate at this stage in each young person’s development.
Allowing this not only helps the parent with separation issues,
but helps each student on the ASD/LD Spectrum to feel like he
or she (not their parent) is in control of his or her life.
Encouraging each parent to back off enough to allow this to
happen will ensure that each young person will gain the ability
he or she needs to ask questions and use his or her own
determination and resources to find answers that lead to
success in school and beyond.
•
Explain to parents that there are people whom their son or
daughter can rely on when questions about what to do arise.
Talk about how these people will show their emerging adult child
how to ask for what is needed. Explain that this process is called
individuation and that it is vital. It will allow their son or daughter
to feel in control of his or her life and become a capable young
adult who will then make appropriate decisions by having a
sense of self-determination.
Parents’ Checklist
Here is a checklist that can be used when meeting and working
with parents in the school setting.
•
Don’t refer to a student in the third person when he or she is
in attendance.

---
## Page 91
•
Allow students the opportunity to direct the meeting.
•
Make sure that you know and understand the parents’ goals,
as well as their son or daughter’s goals. (These may be
radically different.)
•
Always address the agreed upon (parent and student) goals
in all communications and interactions with parents.
•
Share the plan as to how the student’s goals are being
addressed in the classroom and school setting.
•
Be positive and assuring and make sure you mention some
positive/small accomplishments no matter how poorly the
student is doing.
•
Inform parents of the issues and struggles that the student is
facing in a positive manner while keeping the goals for the
student as the main focus.
•
Be realistic, but not negative.
•
Parents need the information presented to be clear and
concrete.
•
Don’t share the minutia … focus on the bigger picture.
•
Areas of need should be based on assessments presented.
•
Build into the plan any of the crucial things that the parent or
student have requested to show working on those areas.
•
Address issues that surround student needs and realities vs.
having all the attention and goals focus totally on academics.
Be aware that oftentimes the parents and/or siblings of the
student also reside somewhere on the ASD or LD Spectrum
themselves.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 92
Parents know that their child is facing overwhelming challenges and
they often feel that they are the ones who are left to pick up the pieces
when their son or daughter (through no fault of his or her own) fails in
the education process. Empowering parents by understanding what
they are going through and how the ASD/LD diagnosis their son or
daughter carries affects the adolescent or the young adult in school and
throughout the learning process is essential.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: In my first book, “Made for Good Purpose …” I
related the story of Jeff to readers. This vignette has been adapted
here as a real-time and place example of the “Parent Trap.” Readers
will see how clinicians and the professionals working with Jeff helped
his mom escape from the “Parent Trap” and learn to trust the people
working with her son.
“Jeff”
Jeff, originally from California, enrolled in CIP Amherst when he was 19
years old. Having graduated from a boarding school for students with
learning differences, his IQ was in the superior range. He was
articulate, well-mannered, and easygoing.
During his orientation week, Jeff’s mom attended the session where
staff take parents through the process of “letting go.” They showed
Jeff’s mom the best and most effective ways for her to communicate
with her son, his teachers, his advisors, and other staff members so
that Jeff could learn to stand on his own two feet and start taking those
critical first steps towards independence.
Two weeks later, Jeff’s advisor discovered that Jeff’s mom was calling
him from California to get him up in the morning (4 a.m., her time) and
then calling him many times throughout the day to see how he was
doing. She would give him instructions and advice (almost hourly) as he
progressed from one class to another. Jeff (in turn) was waiting for his
mom to call and supervise not only his entire school day, but his whole
life.

---
## Page 93
Staff intervened. With awareness, support, and a lot of coaching and
talking about the “Parent Trap” from Jeff’s advisor and teachers, Jeff’s
mom was able to back off and start letting go one small step at a time.
Jeff could then start taking his own steps for participation in the learning
process and start working towards self-reliance.
Knowing that it takes time and a conscious effort to change symbiotic
relationships, understanding the ASD/LD diagnosis from a parent
perspective allows those who work with these adolescents and young
adults to help parents transition through the separation process
smoothly and professionally. Professionals in the education arena can
work in harmony and understanding to assure parents that their
adolescents or young adult will know how to ask for guidance from
those who are qualified to give it. As these young men and women
develop a self-advocacy plan and achieve individuation, parents will
begin to understand that the role of the educator in this process is to
assist each individual in clarifying and reaching goals that will lead to
success in life, school, work, and independent living.
That being said – high schools and colleges are trying to cope with
large numbers of students who are on the ASD/LD Spectrum.
Mainstreaming, accommodations, and transitional services – in many
states – are rights by law. Professionals who forge good interpersonal
relationships with parents and understand their point of view can help
them become part of a much healthier and successful process.
Together they can advocate team-based solutions built on mutual
understanding for helping these young men and women accept their
diagnosis, set goals, master the skills needed to graduate high school,
attend college, get and keep a job, and eventually live independently.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Sharona Sommer, a former CIP Head Student
Advisor wrote the concluding case study for this section of Chapter 1,
about Janie and her parents. I feel her story is a good way to conclude
this section of Chapter 1 and begin the next section. This story
illustrates how educators, clinicians, advisors, and mentors can help
parents face the reality of an ASD or LD diagnosis as they look at the
diagnosis from their child’s point of view. In Sharona’s story, this family

---
## Page 94
begins to form a realistic plan for going forward with Janie’s education,
independent living arrangements, and a plan for her future with Janie’s
full cooperation and input.
“Janie and Her Parents”
When I first met Janie, she was living independently with
financial support from her parents. She was able to pay her bills
and keep her apartment somewhat clean. Prior to meeting Janie
(during the previous year), I learned that she had graduated high
school, been accepted to and had gone away to a four-year
college. As her story emerged, Janie had a very difficult time
staying on top of all the demands that were expected of her in
this new academic environment. There were too many
assignments, 
too 
many 
people, 
terrible 
anxiety, 
and
unfortunately, Janie failed the semester. It was a huge setback
for her family, but Janie decided that she wanted to stay in
college and she decided to enroll at the local community college.
Janie was doing better but there were still trust issues in dealing
with her family. Janie had a history of lying to her parents when
she was having a difficult time coping. Instead of asking for help,
Janie would make impulsive decisions that would land her in
trouble. It was a vicious cycle but it was important for Janie and
her family to realize why this pattern was happening. Janie
needed to accept the reality of her strengths and challenges and
her parents needed to allow her to do this and not come to her
“rescue” when problems or difficulties surfaced.
I was hired to work with Janie on her self-esteem, life skills, and
improving her level of motivation. In the process, I was also very
involved with her parents during family meetings designed to

---
## Page 95
bring us all together to discuss the reality of the situation. With
sensitivity, compassion, and honesty, we discussed all the
positive steps Janie has taken to get her life on track. We went
over realistic expectations considering Janie’s level of anxiety
and her past experiences. Then we had the difficult talk of
preparing for the future.
Despite Janie being gifted, the reality was that no one could
predict how much support (financial and otherwise) Janie would
need in the future. Knowing that Janie’s parents could have the
support they needed from a third party (me in this case)
provided them with peace of mind and acceptance of their
daughter and what the future would hold. I could also be there
for Janie as she worked on her skills for life, school, and
independent living. Positive communication between Janie and
her parents was essential in helping the family reframe the
situation and be much more hopeful for the future.
As educators, I know that you are faced with difficult situations
and how this creates the need for uncomfortable conversations
with the families of your ASD/LD students. I urge you to keep in
mind that the family is doing the best they can with what they
know. They are also looking to you for support, guidance, and
reassurance that things will be ok. The future will look a lot
brighter for the parents whom you work with if you consistently
reframe situations to see the positive, set realistic expectations
for the family, and teach patience by your example.
Understanding the diagnosis from the parent perspective
(above) and student perspective (below), as well as the criteria
of an ASD/LD diagnosis that this chapter contains, will go a long
way in helping you achieve success in working with these
emerging young adults who have entered your classroom.
Sharona Sommer is a Certified Professional Coach with over
20 years’ experience in the Special Education field. She
focuses her coaching practice on improving the quality of life

---
## Page 96
for parents of children of all ages with Asperger’s Disorder and
other Learning Differences. Before opening her own coaching
practice, Sharona worked for six years with The College
Internship Program in the role of Head Student Advisor. At CIP,
Sharona coached young adults with various learning
disabilities as they transitioned to adulthood. That experience
led her to her current passion of helping families gain peace of
mind and a harmonious family environment.
A Reflection on the Story: “Janie and Her
Parents”
Further insight by Sharona Sommer
Having worked with parents of young adults with Asperger’s Disorder
(AD) and Learning Differences (LD) for many years, I have deep
respect and empathy for the challenges that families face every day. It
is scary not knowing what tomorrow will hold for their son or daughter,
much less the distant future. In my experience, I have found that
parents found comfort in being acknowledged and validated. I know
that their experience is indeed frightening, confusing, and frustrating
and how important it is for me to acknowledge this. In communicating
with parents, I’ve found that it is beneficial to focus on three main areas:
•
Help parents reframe their thoughts and perceptions so that they
can focus on the positive advances that their son or daughter is
making and not on any recent setbacks that may have
happened.
•
Discuss realistic expectations vs. expectations that are too high
or low for their adolescent or young adult child. This includes
setting healthy boundaries and being clear (from a teaching
perspective) about the reality of what their son’s or daughter’s
future will look like. That means being able to help them accept

---
## Page 97
that it may be different from what they had envisioned for their
child.
•
Help parents “be patient” with the process. Explain that the
timeline may take longer than they would like and that they will
need support along the way.
Understanding the ASD/LD
Diagnosis from a Student
Standpoint
Receiving an ASD or LD diagnosis is truly a beginning … although it will
take a while for each adolescent or young adult to accept not only his or
her diagnosis, but to conclude that this diagnosis is indeed a starting
point. Most young people have little self-understanding of their
diagnosis. They do not know that they truly were made for good
purpose and are inherently valuable.
These amazing young people do not know what their individual profile
of strengths looks like and they do not know how to go about becoming
self-sufficient, independent, and productive. They are often unaware of
the unique, positive attributes they bring to the world. If the adolescents
and young adults you are working with are serious about getting on
with their lives, getting an education, doing meaningful work, and living
independently, accepting an ASD or LD diagnosis is paramount.
For those who work with these young people in schools or other
settings, understanding the ASD/LD diagnosis from a student
perspective is about helping ASD/LD adolescents and young adults
embrace their authentic self. This in turn allows for progress in their
lives at school, at work, in establishing relationships, and, when they
are ready, to live independently. Most ASD/LD students run from their
diagnosis. They feel threatened and defensive; they do not want to be
different. They think “If I don’t fit in, then I don’t belong.” And sometimes

---
## Page 98
… they look at their diagnosis from this alternative perspective: When
adolescent and young adult students look at their friends and peers,
they often convince themselves (in spite of an official diagnosis) that
everyone has minor differences and that they are not different from their
classmates. Thinking in these broad strokes about what it means to
have a disability often leads the adolescent or young adult to conclude
that their diagnosis was probably a mistake, and does not apply to
them. The result is that they try to blame others (often parents or
teachers) and no one can blame them.
Educators, clinicians, and professionals who work with adolescents and
young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum can do many things to help
these young men and women understand that true self-knowledge is
the key to empowerment. By enabling the ASD/LD adolescent or young
adult to accept and deal with challenges they face, the sense of failure
and despair that has plagued each individual for most of his or her life
is lessened and eventually released.
Helping students gain self-knowledge about individual differences is the
first step towards acceptance of an ASD or LD diagnosis. This
acceptance then becomes the foundation for developing the unique
and considerable talents each young person possesses and then
teaching these young men and women how to function in and
contribute to society using these attributes. Looking at the diagnosis of
ASD/LD from an individual student’s point of view can help educators,
clinicians, and professionals open the door so that each adolescent or
young adult can master the basic social, academic, and life skills that
will lead each one to success in life, school, work, and independent
living.
Some basics for implementing this process are:
•
Teaching students how to self-advocate.
•
Allowing students to be actively involved in the direction of their
lives.
•
Creating “Person Centered Plans” (this is covered in great detail
in further chapters) that focus on short- and long-term goal

---
## Page 99
setting.
•
Outlining the skills necessary for success.
•
Helping the ASD/LD adolescent or young adult gain a high level
of motivation to meet individual goals.
Along with self-knowledge – learning self-regulation, decision-making,
and problem solving are areas that are essential for success. For
individuals with an ASD or LD diagnosis, being aware of themselves,
the environment around them, and how the two affect each other does
not come naturally. Implementing the “Continuum of Growth” model will
help students focus on individual acceptance of their diagnosis by
learning to understand it and then to accept it. As teachers, clinicians,
and the professionals who work with these young men and women
think about a diagnosis from a student perspective, they can work to
further increase awareness that will lead to each student participating,
being present, and advocating for themselves during their school years.
By introducing the “Continuum of Growth” model, each student will
begin to accept his or her individual diagnosis.
THE “CONTINUUM OF GROWTH”
Self-awareness: Young people need to be reminded that they are
separate individuals with unique personalities. This is a good thing.
They need to comprehend that they are living among individuals with
many types of personalities, some positive, some not. As the ASD/LD
adolescent or young adult learns that people have outgoing,
introverted, exuberant, joyful, or sullen affects to their personalities,
they begin to understand self-awareness. Slowly, but surely, they let go
of their self-absorption and start learning to relate to people who have
personalities that are similar or vastly different from their own. As these
young men and women become self-aware, the seeds of self-
understanding and self-acceptance are planted and they begin to
accept who they really are.

---
## Page 100
Self-knowledge: Adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD
Spectrum tend to embrace their authentic self no matter how many
years of therapy they may or may not have had. In spite of teachers, or
peers, or even their parents who may have said they were stupid, or
bullied them, or tried to make them fit in, these young people are still
firmly who they were meant to be. This is a plus! Knowing that they can
be authentic allows for progress. Once students begin to see that the
same goofy characteristics they ran from in middle school and thought

---
## Page 101
were silly – are now a source of strength and ability – their self-
knowledge increases and further acceptance of their diagnosis occurs.
Understanding that they process information differently and that they
are not lacking in intelligence or an ability to learn, but learn in non-
traditional ways, allows progress to happen rapidly.
Self-understanding and self-acceptance: Students can begin to
learn self-acceptance by working in the learning environment with
caring teachers and professionals who value the people they are and
are becoming. During classroom teaching, role playing, role modeling,
and the practice of necessary social competencies, students begin to
recognize who they really are – unique differences and all – and start
appreciating all they have to offer. This self-acceptance will allow these
young men and women to gain the self-understanding and confidence
needed to make significant changes in their behavior.
Using what they are learning or have learned, instead of comparing
themselves with others or trying to be people they are not, leads to self-
acceptance through self-understanding. In learning to value and love
who they truly are, depression and lack of motivation for making
changes starts subsiding. This leads to being able to self-regulate. It’s
all about becoming right-sized in what often appears to the ASD or LD
learner to be a one-size fits-all world.
Self-advocacy: This plays a role in nearly every aspect of life for
individuals on the ASD or LD Spectrum – in school, at home, in the
community, and at work. The more self-awareness and self-determined
each person with an ASD or LD diagnosis possesses, the more they
can individually advocate for their own comfort, happiness, and well-
being. This might mean approaching a teacher if they are having
trouble keeping up with the lectures because they just can’t write fast
enough or being able to express to a friend that they don’t always get
his or her jokes because they take them literally. Being able to speak
up, be heard, and be part of a learning, working, or living environment
that allows this, can help the ASD/LD adolescent or young adult gain
the confidence needed to self-disclose and ask for accommodations,

---
## Page 102
further instructions, or additional help to meet goals and achieve
success.
ASD/LD students often lack an understanding of where they fit into the
scheme of things and they have trouble with the overall structure of a
classroom setting. To help each individual become more self-aware:
•
Encourage students to work in smaller groups in the classroom
setting and socialize in smaller groups.
•
Introduce the concept of expanding horizons perhaps through
volunteer work or a small-group service-learning project.
•
Allow students to experience inclusion – giving their time to
important causes and events that encompass a wider
community and world.
•
Model self-acceptance, self-assurance, and self-esteem.
•
Engage and introduce peers who have the same diagnosis and
who model self-awareness to the student having trouble with the
concept.
PERSONAL INSIGHT – self-disclosure: There is a sign over my
desk that I encourage students to embrace. “The genuine evokes the
genuine.” I know that students must be courageously honest and
willing to accept themselves – diagnosis and all. A diagnosis that
leads to true self-knowledge and understanding opens the adolescent
or young adult “up” to their new lives. Being able to self-accept and
then self-disclose to others allows these young men and women to
create wonderful things in their lives and grow without fear. From this
comes self-confidence and self-esteem.
Self-determination: If the adolescents and the young adults being
taught display a true desire to learn about themselves, their diagnosis,
and how to get on with their lives, helping them understand the role
self-determination plays is the last logical step in the “Continuum of
Growth.” Once students start changing their perceptions of their
diagnosis and start putting as much time and energy into learning about
nonverbal language and social competencies as they do their “special

---
## Page 103
interests,” things start changing in a positive way, especially at school.
Self-determination allows the ASD or LD adolescent or young adult to
start co-existing with comfort in the surrounding “neurotypical” world.
Viewing the diagnosis from the student standpoint helps the educator,
clinician, or professional understand how the ASD or LD student feels.
Looking at a student’s point of view allows those who work with these
adolescents and young adults in a learning environment to show
compassion, empathy, and provide the tools and foundation needed not
only to help each person individually, but to foster inclusion. As
students lessen their frustration and isolation by understanding their
clinical diagnosis, they will feel better and do better in school. This
understanding can then carry over to work, relationships, and success
in living independently.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Chapter 1 ends with a story written by one of
our Alumni students who attended CIP’s Bloomington Center. Adria
Nassim’s story illustrates how self-acceptance allowed her to start and
then continue on a path that allows her to be the best she can be. As
Adria acknowledged areas that she needed to work on, and then put
in the time and effort to understand her diagnosis, and set goals, she
became very motivated to forge her own unique path in life. Adria’s
honest portrayal of herself allows those using this Teaching Toolkit to
see the content of not only this chapter, but the ensuing chapters of
this book, in action. Adria exemplifies the concepts and advice in this
book and shows all of us that obtaining skills for life, school, work, and
independent living is worth the effort and actually works.
“Living and Loving Life Outside the Walls”
My name is Adria Nassim. I am from Floyds Knobs, Indiana and
participated in CIP Bloomington’s program for three years. I
started my involvement with the program in the summer of 2008.

---
## Page 104
I was getting ready to begin my senior year of college at Brescia
University, a small, Catholic campus in Owensboro, Kentucky
where I was getting a BA in English and Spanish.
While I was doing quite well academically, I became
unintentionally involved in an incident of severe bullying which
later resulted in my parents and I deciding that a temporary
leave of absence and a more supportive environment better
suited to young adults with my specific needs and my dual
diagnosis – Asperger’s Disorder (AD) and nonverbal learning
disability – would be the best course of action rather than
remaining on a traditional college campus.
During my first few semesters at CIP I had weekly one-on-one
therapy sessions with the center’s on-site psychologist. He and I
worked together on stress and anxiety reduction through talk
therapy and techniques like meditation and working to change
some of my fearful thought process by exchanging negative
thoughts for positive ones. I was also receiving most of CIP’s
other services including advising, where I was learning to keep a
monthly budget and pay bills, and residential services where I
was learning daily living skills like meal planning and buying my
own groceries, as well as how to maintain a living space. I also
held three volunteer positions within the community: one at a
local children’s science museum, one at a preschool program for
children with disabilities, and one teaching swim lessons to
children and teens with disabilities.
As I continued to make steady progress over the next year, the
need for weekly therapy sessions lessened. I began to realize
that although what happened back at school felt awful, I was in
a new place, and this was a new chance to start over. What
happened there was not going to follow me here, so why not
start living again?

---
## Page 105
I sometimes tell families at CIP open house events, “A kid like
me may do well at a traditional college. They may have plenty of
academic drive and discipline and achieve top grades, but after
graduation, they will still struggle to find their place in the real
world due to issues with social interaction both in a peer setting
and in relation to the workplace.”
Young adults like me may also lag behind developmentally in
terms of acquisition of age appropriate life skills. A college or
university expects that its students will be self-sufficient, able to
cook a meal, manage personal expenses, and dress
appropriately for a job interview simply because, in the United
States, colleges and universities take this for granted. They
focus on an applicant’s intelligence inside the classroom. They
figure, if she can get this far, she must be intelligent therefore
she must be independent and self-sufficient, right?
I think many college admissions departments would be very
right to say something like my words instead. “Young people in
my shoes are very bright, but our brains are often missing the
grey between the black and white that ties together both the
academic world – the world of fact and logic to the real world
filled with daily tasks like housekeeping, dinners out with
classmates, part-time jobs, church retreats, academic advisor
appointments, and how to go about making it all work.” I would
then urge staff in these departments to try to understand us as
we have come to understand ourselves and our individual
diagnoses.
I eventually returned to Brescia a year and a half after starting at
CIP to complete my degree. It was a great college year. I
graduated in May of 2010 with a Bachelor’s degree and later,
came back to CIP’s Bloomington Center to focus less on
academics and more on social skills building and learning skills
that would allow me to succeed when seeking employment.

---
## Page 106
I moved into an apartment a few blocks from the center with
another CIP student and took a paid position working as a
reporter with the Indiana Daily Student (IDS), IU Bloomington’s
student newspaper and writing voluntarily for Bloomington’s
Ryder magazine.
The IDS was a great way to meet friends and gain confidence. I
loved the fact that I was able to be part of a bigger purpose and
that I was wanted for something I was good at. I still stay in
touch with some of the students I met there today. I also joined a
local church and a college and young adult ministry group that I
am also still a part of today. Most of my friends are part of this
group and everyone has been so welcoming and supportive. We
do social outings throughout the week as well as small groups
and volunteer projects.
I have since moved out of the apartment complex and now live
in a house a few blocks away with Lucy, a four-year-old Yellow
Lab named for The Beatles’ “Lucy in the Sky with Diamonds”
song. She is trained as a service dog for mild autism, learning
disability, and mild cerebral palsy. She’s the highlight of my day
and an absolute people magnet – just about everybody inside
the county line knows us!
I look back on my experience at CIP now, and I think it was, as
my parents will tell you, one of the riskiest but best decisions
they have ever made for me. They let me go – bit by bit
throughout life. First to summer camp, then to college, and finally
to CIP (College Internship Program). And now I’m not stuck on
my laptop playing video games all day and confining myself to
my bedroom. I’m living outside the walls and loving it! It was a
risk and an investment we all agreed to make, one that I saw
value in as much as they did, so the effort was mutual: they put
forth the financial commitment, and I put forth the physical effort.

---
## Page 107
Today when my parents ask me if I’m happy, I say, “Yes. You
can stop asking now. I really am. Oh, and by the way, I can’t
come home this weekend. I have plans with friends …”
Adria Nassim
CIP Alumna and Autism/Special Needs Advocate and Speaker
With Thanks: I wish to personally thank Adria Nassim for this
honest, heartfelt, and inspiring story. It is a wonderful
contribution to this book. Well done Adria!
Making It Real
WHAT?
Looking at the wide range of characteristics and behaviors
that adolescents and young adults (ages 16–26) display that
result in an Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) or Learning
Differences (LD) diagnosis.
Taking the diagnostic criteria apart and approaching it from
a clinical, educational, parental, and student standpoint
regarding 
the 
day-to-day 
school 
experiences 
and
interactions of students and teachers in the classroom
setting.
Looking at the dynamics and role that parents play in their
adolescent’s or young adult’s diagnosis in relation to the
school setting and then looking at the diagnosis from the
perspective of the student who has received an ASD or LD
classification.
HOW?

---
## Page 108
By giving educators, clinicians, and professionals a basic
understanding of the best ways to impart knowledge and
then showing readers how to incorporate an active,
participatory learning model into any learning environment.
By illustrating other relevant theories for learning, as well as
tips, techniques, and ideas that work well for the majority of
adolescents and young adults diagnosed on the wide and
varied ASD or LD Spectrum.
BENEFITS?
Chapter 1 becomes the blueprint for utilizing the ensuing
chapters in Autism and Learning Differences (An Active
Learning Teaching Toolkit) in a logical, tried and true
fashion.
“With diagnosis, the journey to self-awareness and self-
acceptance begins. In my case, my diagnosis was an
enormous relief. I could finally put a name to my way of being
in the world and start understanding the impact that being on
the ASD Spectrum has had on my life and work.”
Michael P. McManmon
NOTES:
 
 
 

---
## Page 109
 
 
 
 
 

---
## Page 110
The Methodology of Active
Learning
CHAPTER 2 OVERVIEW:
CREATING AN “ACTIVE LEARNING” ENVIRONMENT

ACTIVITIES
