---
## Page 111
The Methodology of Active
Learning
“Out of clutter, find simplicity;
from discord, find harmony.
In the middle of difficulty, find
opportunity.”
Albert Einstein
“Anything one person can imagine,
other people can make real.”
Jules Verne
“Believe you can and you’re halfway
there.”
Theodore Roosevelt
“For most students on the ASD and LD
Spectrum academic learning is too
abstract.
Adolescents and young adults need to
see,
touch, and live what they are learning
about.”
Michael P. McManmon

---
## Page 112
“What I hear, I forget;
What I see, I remember;
What I do, I understand.”
Old Chinese proverb, sometimes attributed to Confucius
“Active Learning” is a process whereby students engage in activities,
such as reading, writing, discussion, or problem solving that promote
analysis, synthesis, and evaluation of class content.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: The College Internship Program (CIP) has
always believed in education by empowerment and has developed a
curriculum that equips young adults with the skills needed to figure out
who they are, what they are capable of achieving, and what they can
bring to the world. We know from our work in this field that “Active
Learning” helps our students obtain key life skills while building self-
esteem, arousing their curiosity about learning, and developing their
interpersonal skills. This in turn stirs leadership development in each
individual and brings the world of higher education and careers closer
to home for those who receive an Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) or
Learning Differences (LD) diagnosis. This chapter will show those
working with this population how to implement “Active Learning” into
any learning environment.
We are witnessing a transformation in the way adolescents and young
adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum are prepared by high schools,
colleges, universities, transitional programs, and career training centers
for life, school, work, and independent living.
The latest research, evidence, and statistics tell us that using an “Active
Learning” model to enable students to learn and develop through
participation at each step in the learning process builds confidence,
self-esteem, and retention of knowledge. It enables those on the
ASD/LD Spectrum to obtain interpersonal and critical thinking skills, as
well as a curiosity about learning. The “Active Learning” methodology
(that is present in each chapter in this book) comes into especially

---
## Page 113
clear focus in the chapters that detail the four competency areas: skills
for life, school, work, and independent living (Chapters 3 to 6).
“Active Learning” is a term that refers to several models of instruction
that focus the responsibility for learning on learners. Imparting
knowledge in this manner to adolescents and young adults who receive
an ASD/LD Spectrum diagnosis helps those who work with these
young men and women understand that in order to learn, an ASD or LD
learner must do more than just listen. ASD and LD students need to
discuss and be engaged in solving problems in order to fully
understand each subject being taught.
“Active Learning” relates to the three learning domains referred to as
knowledge, skills, and attitudes (KSA). In particular, students need to
be engaged in higher-order thinking tasks such as analysis, synthesis,
and evaluation. “Active Learning” engages students in these three
aspects; thinking, doing things, and reflection.
(Bloom 1956)
Autism and Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit) gives readers a variety of classroom activities and materials
that show educators, clinicians, and professionals how to deliver the
various teaching concepts and models of learning in ways that will
appeal to the adolescent and young adult age range. The stories, case
studies, anecdotal evidence, and vignettes that appear throughout this
book also serve this purpose and relate the text of this chapter to the
real-time 
and 
place 
experiences 
of 
educators 
and 
support
professionals who have been using an “Active Learning” model for
teaching.
By combining curriculum content with opportunities educators can
provide ASD/LD students with a clear sense of what is to be
accomplished during each and every learning activity. Understanding
and thinking about the task, information, skill, or knowledge to be
imparted, then taking action that is challenging, engaging, and
meaningful, results in “Active Learning.”

---
## Page 114
Reflecting upon what has been learned during the experience takes
this one step further. Reflection helps students retain what they have
learned – it makes knowledge stick. Reflection helps students and
teachers understand the meaning and impact of their efforts and links
what they have learned with what they have done. Without reflection,
students may just go through the motions of learning. “Active Learning”
is enhanced when students play a significant role in designing the
learning experience, then reflecting upon it.
Practicing reflection helps the ASD/LD adolescent or young adult gain a
greater sense of self. For example, when learners are asked to think
about their goals and their individual progress in an area of study, they
have the opportunity to master self-assessment skills that help them
become more independent. These young men and women then acquire
insights that allow each person to build on their strengths and set goals
in areas where they need further development. Reflection also offers
the instructor an opportunity to identify and assess the knowledge that
students gain through “Active Learning.” Teachers can then assist
students in developing a portfolio, or record of achievement to
acknowledge progress, or to help each individual set and reach
additional or higher goals through a “Person Centered Plan” (PCP).
“Active Learning” and the strategies used to support it can transform
teachers and the school support professionals who work with these
young people from dispensers of information to translators of
information. Those who impart knowledge become the facilitators or
vehicle for this process.
The information, tasks, analyses, strategies, and examples given in the
ensuing chapters possess two qualities:
•
The materials and activities are challenging, engaging, and
meaningful to adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD
Spectrum.
•
The content of the knowledge being imparted addresses a real
need, so that the adolescents and young adults participating in

---
## Page 115
the learning environment perceive the learning activities as
relevant and important.
Comprehension is enhanced when students play a significant role in
designing the learning experience. Each tool, technique, suggestion,
and classroom activity in this book allows for input from students. As
evidenced by brain-based thinking research, students who are involved
in the process of obtaining and gaining knowledge have a greater
sense of ownership for what they are learning and invest more of their
time and efforts in their academics and career studies.
The methodology throughout this Teaching Toolkit is not only active,
but persistent and cognizant of the behavior standards, practices,
learning outcomes, and accomplishments that can be achieved when
students are involved in each step of an “Active Learning” based
curriculum. As they mediate, muse, contemplate, ponder, deliberate,
cogitate, reason, and speculate about their learning experiences they
ask: What am I doing and why? What am I learning?
For example: When learners are asked to think about their goals and
chart their progress as they work through each life skill competency
area they have the opportunity to learn self-assessment skills that help
them become more self-assured and independent. As individuals
acquire insights that allow them to build on their strengths and set goals
in areas where they need further development, they achieve success.
“Active Learning” allows for collaboration between teachers and others
who work with the ASD/LD adolescents and young adults and the
young men and women themselves. Together they identify clear
objectives, opportunities, and areas for improvement in a collaborative
environment.
Creating an “Active Learning”
Environment

---
## Page 116
It is useful – at this point – to take one more look at the “Learning
Pyramid” before going further, as the strategies for imparting
knowledge in this chapter and throughout the rest of this Teaching
Toolkit are reflected in the lower half of the pyramid.
Used with permission from NTL Institute for Applied Behavioral Science, 300 N. Lee Street,
#300, Alexandria, VA 22314.
The statistics for retention of knowledge are astonishing when students
teach and mentor each other. When adolescents and young adults with
an ASD/LD diagnosis are taught by demonstration, discussion,
reflection, and then encouraged to tell and demonstrate for others what
they have learned – a 90% retention rate of a subject, concept, life skill,
or even a new idea is achieved.
The first element in setting up an “Active Learning” environment in the
classroom is for the instructor or facilitator to have a clear sense of
what needs to be accomplished. The second element of “Active
Learning” is an action performed by students on their own behalf. The
third element (as mentioned in the beginning of this chapter) is

---
## Page 117
reflection; processing or reconstructing the experience and then
making the connection to what they have learned.
Here are the best and most effective teaching modalities to use when
working with ASD/LD adolescents and young adults that will support
positive learning outcomes and the creation of an active, participatory
learning experience.
CLASS OR SMALL GROUP DISCUSSIONS
Discussions can be conducted with any class size, although it is
typically more effective in smaller numbers. Discussion requires
students to think critically on the subject matter and use logic to
evaluate the position of each participant.
Some of the benefits of using discussion as a method of learning are:
•
It helps students explore a diversity of perspectives.
•
It develops habits of collaborative learning.
•
It helps students develop skills of synthesis and integration, and
it leads to transformation.
(Brookfield 2005)
A group discussion is not just people chatting. Therefore, it is useful to
set up some basic ground rules for group discussions. Here are some
suggestions for ground rules:
•
Participants listen to each other.
•
Participants respect other people’s ideas.
•
Participants do not hurt each others’ feelings.
•
Participants speak briefly, clearly, and precisely.
Before starting a group discussion, ask your students what kind of
ground rules might apply to make the discussion more effective. Ask
them to choose a moderator or chairperson for the discussion. The
main role of the moderator or chairperson is to maintain the rules

---
## Page 118
agreed upon by participants. If needed, remind the chairperson not to
talk too much, but to listen and facilitate communication among the
participants. If a board or flip chart is used, everybody needs to be able
to see the writing clearly. Concentric circles work well for group
discussions or having the students sit in a horseshoe formation. A
group discussion often leads to brainstorming once a problem, topic, or
issue has been brought into focus.
Steps for a Group Discussion
•
State the reasons for and the goals of the discussion
beforehand.
•
Talk in an informal way and ask about any concerns
students might have so that they can help choose a topic
within a learning objective.
•
Select a moderator to start the discussion.
•
Encourage group members to present the pros and cons of
the topic. Everyone should have a chance to speak and
share ideas.
•
In order to encourage participation and draw on the wisdom
of all the students in the classroom, consider the creation of
small groups.
•
Gather information and analyze. Have someone take notes
on the blackboard.

---
## Page 119
Steps for a Brainstorming Session
•
Identify the issue(s).
•
Write the issue(s) on the board and explain. (Ask for a
volunteer to do this.)
•
Ask the group to think about the issue(s) for a few minutes.
•
Invite quick ideas on the issue(s) without elaboration.
•
Ask learners not to interrupt or argue.
•
Stop the brainstorming at some point and ask learners to
clarify each idea.
•
Put ideas into categories and ask students to prioritize.
•
Discuss and underline the ideas agreed upon and next
steps to be taken.
THINK-PAIR-SHARE
Think-pair-share is an activity where students take a minute to ponder
the previous lesson and discuss it with a peer. The outcomes of this
dialogue are then shared with the rest of the group working on the
same lesson in a meaningful and reflective way. Think-pair-share
exercises are useful in situations where students can identify and relate
what they learned previously to others.
Prepare participants with sound instruction before expecting them to
discuss a topic on their own. If properly implemented, think-pair-share
saves the facilitator of the class time and keeps students motivated,
involved, and provides a cumulative assessment of progress.

---
## Page 120
Designing Engaging Group Instruction
When planning group instruction, consider the following:
•
How can I make the idea more concrete?
•
How can I arrange for participatory learning?
•
How can I make the lesson more visual?
•
How can I offer an auditory learning experience?
•
How can I use community-based learning opportunities?
Tie in available resources:
•
Videos, music, iPad/iPod games, Smart Boards, pictures,
PowerPoint presentations, books, stories from magazines
and newspapers, etc.
•
School, neighborhood, and community libraries.
•
Look for photographs, illustrations, and find videos to
support 
your 
instruction: 
www.youtube.com; 
and
www.teachertube.com.
•
Use 
website 
resources 
such 
as: 
www.n2y.com;
www.edhelper.com; www.abcteach.com.
LEARNING CELLS
Learning cells are an effective way for a pair of students to study and
learn together. The learning cell concept was developed by Marcel
Goldschmid of the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology in Lausanne,
Switzerland in the late 1960s. A learning cell is a process of learning

---
## Page 121
where two students alternate asking and answering questions on
commonly read materials.
To prepare for this task, students should read the assignment
individually, then write down any questions that they might have about
what they just read. At the next class meeting, the teacher or facilitator
will randomly put the students in pairs.
The process begins by designating one student from each group to
begin by asking one of their questions to the other. Once the two
students discuss this chosen question, the other student will ask a
question they have prepared and they will alternate accordingly. During
this time, the teacher or facilitator is going around the class from pair to
pair giving feedback and answering any questions students may have
about what they are doing. This system is also referred to as a student
dyad.
COLLABORATIVE LEARNING GROUPS
A successful way to learn different material for different classes. This is
where an instructor gathers students in groups of three to six people
and gives them an assignment or task to work on together. The
assignment might be answering a question or presenting knowledge to
the entire class collaboratively. The students in the group should
choose a leader and a note-taker to keep them on track with the
process.
This is a good example of “Active Learning” because it causes the
students to review the work that is being required of them before it has
actually been taught by a teacher.
(Adapted from McKinney 2010)
STUDENT DEBATES

---
## Page 122
Student debates are an “Active Learning” way for students to learn
because they allow ASD/LD adolescents and young adults the chance
to take a position and gather information to support their point of view
and then explain it to others. Debates help break up cognitive rigidity by
allowing students to look at two sides of an issue. Participating in a
debate gives each student a chance to be part of a fun and challenging
activity while gaining some experience with giving a verbal
presentation.
VIDEOS
A reaction to a video is also an example of “Active Learning.” We know
for sure that most ASD or LD students love to watch movies. Videos
help students understand what they are learning in an “alternative
presentation” mode. Educator and author Kathleen McKinney tells us
to:
•
Make sure that the video relates to the topic that they are
studying at the moment.
•
Include a few questions before you start the video so students
will pay more attention and notice where to focus during the
video.
•
Use reflection: After the video has finished, divide students
either into small groups or pairs so that they can discuss what
they learned, and write, visually illustrate, or orally present a
review or reaction to the movie.
(Adapted from McKinney 2010)
ROLE PLAYING/MODELING
One of the best methods of “Active Learning” to use when working with
the ASD/LD population is role playing or having someone role model a
skill, concept, idea, or activity. Role playing and role modeling provide

---
## Page 123
an atmosphere where things can be done over and over again – and
practiced 
until 
perfect 
or 
completely 
understood. 
Role
playing/modeling are excellent ways for ASD/LD adolescents or young
adults to practice job interviewing skills, public speaking, or preparing
for social interactions during parties, events, or when conversing and
getting to know people. Role playing/modeling is also useful for
practicing scenarios about grocery shopping, banking, visiting a doctor
or dentist, making conversation with classmates, friends, and teachers,
or having an interaction with someone new.
Triage for Teachers
“Help your students to use their active listening and learning
skills by keeping classes enjoyable, positive, and most of all
interactive! Keep the segments that you are teaching short and
dynamic to retain each person’s attention.”
Lance Haskins, CIP Brevard
“Establish a tone with the class by working with students to help
them understand what is expected and to show mutual respect
for each other. Allow students opportunities to correct
themselves by giving them a reminder to try again.”
Joan Williamson, Program Director, CIP Brevard
“Reflection is processing or reconstructing the learning
experience and connecting it to retention. Reflection helps
students and teachers understand the meaning and impact of
their efforts and links what they have learned with what they
have done. Without reflection, students just go through the
motions of learning.

---
## Page 124
Practicing reflection helps the ASD/LD adolescent or young
adult gain a greater sense of self. When learners are asked to
think about their goals and their individual progress they have
the opportunity to master self-assessment skills that help them
become more independent. Reflection offers the instructor an
opportunity to identify and assess the knowledge that students
gain through ‘Active Learning.’”
Michael P. McManmon
Organizing Workshops
The process for doing this is:
•
Have students choose a topic of interest that they know well
and are excited about sharing with others.
•
Organize the workshop in a suitable place. Students and
any guests they invite (another class or group of like-minded
people) need to be able to hear each other and feel
comfortable.
•
Have a workshop facilitator or ask two or three people to
share this role. (Note: Those running the workshop need to
keep the discussion going and keep it fair.)
•
Discuss the topic and have the “experts” (those who are
passionate about the subject) share their expertise.
•
Have the participants of the workshop break out and work in
smaller groups. Instruct the participants to bring information
they have learned during the break out, or what they would
like to contribute on the topic, back to share with the larger
group.

---
## Page 125
•
Record impressions, decisions, conclusions, or additional
information gleaned on large pieces of paper so that all can
see.
•
If possible, come up with a plan for future actions and next
steps forward. This might be a community- based service
learning project that uses the skills obtained at the workshop
session.
Here are the steps to follow for a role play.
•
Choose the subject matter and outline a basic plot.
•
Select the participants.
•
Do a small role play to begin with. Later, it can evolve into a
longer scenario in the form of a series, in which the same
characters reappear, or more students join in as more situations
are covered.
•
Prepare flash cards of sayings or colloquial language that the
participants can use to help them during the role play.
•
Encourage the students to make up their own spontaneous
dialogue to suit the scenario.
Role modeling: Adolescents and young adults who have already
obtained the skills, knowledge, and concepts being taught, or who
actively engage in showing others their skill set(s) or knowledge, gain
even more self-confidence when modeling these behaviors and skills
for others. Students who model life skills, concepts, and ideas for
others – by being real-life examples – create a win-win situation for all
involved in the experience. Role modeling leads us to learning by
teaching others.
Organizing workshops on topics that relate to learning social skills,
“Executive Function” concepts, resume writing, job interviewing, etc. is
an excellent “Active Learning” project for an entire class to take on.
This allows the members organizing the event to obtain a skill or new

---
## Page 126
area of knowledge and then to teach each other in a learning
environment that mimics what happens in the real world of continuing
education and work.
LEARNING BY TEACHING OTHERS
Learning by teaching others (the 90% level on the “Learning Pyramid”)
is a prime example of “Active Learning” because students actively
research a topic and prepare the information so that they can teach it to
others either in a class setting, workshop setting, peer mentoring
setting, recreational setting, or social setting. This helps those on the
ASD or LD Spectrum retain knowledge because adolescents and
young adults:
•
Often learn and communicate better with their peers than their
teachers.
•
Are more likely to access their prior knowledge, which is
essential to learning.
•
Are more likely to find personally meaningful problem solutions
or interpretations.
•
Receive more frequent and more immediate feedback.
•
Will increase their self-confidence and self-reliance.
•
Know that a task that they have done themselves or as part of a
group is more highly valued.
•
Know their conceptions of learning can change, which in turn
has implications for cognitive development.
•
Know that students who work together on tasks learn to work
with other people of different backgrounds and attitudes.
•
Will learn strategies for their own learning by observing others.

---
## Page 127
“Good World/Bad World”
This is a great activity to get a discussion going
and practice a variety of inductive learning
methods. Divide students into small groups.
Begin with a premise (feel free to make up your
own) 
that 
affects 
the 
whole 
school, 
the
community, or the world, such as:
1. What if you win the lottery with a ticket you
found on the street? It’s a large amount of
money – enough to assure that you could live
comfortably for the rest of your life without
having to work. But … not enough to live
extravagantly for your whole life. What is your
responsibility to your immediate family, your
extended family, your friends, the community,
or people you know who are struggling

---
## Page 128
financially? What is your responsibility if
someone asks you to lend them money?
2. What might happen (good or bad) if someone
with a scientific background who “knew how
to,” could perform cloning and the process was
basically unregulated? What are the good
things that might be done with this technology?
What are the negative outcomes that might
occur through cloning technology? Should
government develop regulations proactively to
control how cloning is used in the future?
3. Someone you know posts a very derogatory
comment about one of your friends on their
Facebook page that is not true. Your friend is
very upset and is very concerned about the
impact it will have on her reputation. She is
already seeing comments posted to the
Facebook page from others who, in turn, are
making further derogatory remarks on this
“page” about her and discussing “all of this” at
school. What is the responsibility of social
media companies to monitor, regulate or edit
what is posted on the pages of their Internet
service? What positive and negative outcomes
might result from such actions by these
companies?
Some groups should explore the favorable
outcomes of the premise and other groups
should 
investigate 
the 
unfavorable
consequences. Bring the groups together to
contrast the various opinions. Remind the group
that there are no “right or wrong” answers, only
approximations that might lead to possible
solutions or outcomes. The purpose of the

---
## Page 129
discussion is to generate dialogue and discuss
many options. Consensus or a recommended
course of action is not needed.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Student-Centered Teaching and
Learning
In a traditional teaching approach most class time is spent with the
teacher lecturing (the 5% retention percentage level on the “Learning
Pyramid”) as students watch and listen. Students then work individually
on assignments, and discussion or active participation is discouraged
as time is usually a factor.
In the reality of today’s traditional classroom setting, most educators
need to move quickly through materials in a pre-determined manner
and have very little flexibility in order to meet preset deadlines.
However, what most educators and the schools, universities, and
vocational centers where they work, don’t realize, is that teaching in a
student-centered, active, and participatory way will actually save them
time and be much more effective. The knowledge that students will
absorb and then retain will provide the measurable outcomes their
principals, deans, and academic supervisors are looking for in the first
place.

---
## Page 130
“Adrift at Sea”
(Adapted from a US Navy Seals training exercise)
This exercise develops the skills needed to work
as a group or team and is a prime example of
using “structured inquiry.” Divide students into
small groups.
Scenario: You and “your group” are “adrift at sea” in
the Pacific Ocean. Much of your sailing vessel and its
contents have been destroyed by a fire. Your boat is
now slowly sinking. You have a rubber life raft, just
large enough to carry you and your crewmates. There
are only six items intact and not damaged by the fire.
Your primary task, as a group that wants to
become “survivors” of this incident, is to put the
items in the order of importance to maximize
your chances of rescue.
List these items on a white or black board:
a large can/jug of water;
fishing tackle;
mosquito net;
basket of food;
shaving mirror;
and a large sheet of plastic.

---
## Page 131
Have each group generate a discussion and then
present their conclusions (in any format they
like) to the other groups.
Once all groups have stated what they chose and
why, tell the group that the #1 item for survival
determined by the Navy Seals is the shaving
mirror! If you can’t signal for help and actually be
rescued the food, water, mosquito net, sheet of
plastic, and fishing tackle will help initially, but
your crew will not (unfortunately) survive the
catastrophe.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Student-centered teaching methods shift the focus of activity from the
teacher to the learners. Examples of this include scenarios in which
students solve problems, answer questions, formulate questions of their
own, discuss, explain, debate, or brainstorm during class. This is also
known as cooperative learning. In cooperative learning students work
in teams on problems and projects under conditions that assure both
positive interdependence and individual accountability. Active,
participatory learning is also part of the inductive teaching and learning
model where students are presented with challenges (questions or
problems) and learn the course material in the context of addressing
these challenges.

---
## Page 132
Inductive methods include inquiry-based learning, case-based
instruction, problem-based learning, project-based learning, and
discovery learning. These pedagogical models are also the basis for
what is known today as the “Service-Learning” or “Community-Based
Learning” models that are in use at all levels of learning (kindergarten
through post-secondary) around the world.
Student-centered methods have repeatedly been shown to be superior
to the traditional teacher-centered approach to instruction, a conclusion
that applies whether the assessed outcome is short-term mastery,
long-term retention, or the acquisition of critical thinking and creative
problem solving skills. Using these models with adolescents and young
adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum is especially effective and an ideal
approach for imparting skills for life, school, work, and independent
living.
INQUIRY-BASED LEARNING

---
## Page 133
Inquiry-based learning starts by posing questions, problems, or
scenarios rather than simply presenting established facts or portraying
a smooth path to acquiring knowledge. When using an inquiry-based
methodology, educators and professionals should help students identify
and research issues, questions, and problems then look for ways
outside the classroom setting to develop knowledge and/or solutions.
Inquiry-based learning is generally used in small-scale investigations,
projects, or research, and usually takes place outside of the traditional
classroom setting. Inquiry-based learning covers a range of
approaches to teaching and learning that include:
•
Field-work.
•
Case studies.
•
Investigations.
•
Individual and group projects.
•
Research projects.
Specific learning processes that people engage in during inquiry-based
learning include:
•
Creating questions of their own.
•
Obtaining supporting evidence to answer the question(s).
•
Explaining the evidence collected.
•
Connecting the explanation to the knowledge obtained from the
investigative process.
•
Creating an argument and justification for the explanation.
In confirmation inquiry, students are provided with the question and
procedure (method) where the results are known in advance, and
confirmation of the results is the object of the inquiry. Confirmation
inquiry is useful to reinforce a previously learned idea; to experience
investigative processes; or to practice a specific inquiry skill, such as
collecting and recording data.

---
## Page 134
“Marooned”
(Adapted from a US Navy Seals training exercise)
This activity (unlike “Good World/Bad World”) is
all about “reaching consensus” and is used to
emphasize 
the 
idea 
that 
we 
can 
all 
be
resourceful when the need arises. It also
highlights that thinking calmly and creatively with
others often leads not only to consensus, but
oftentimes, to a solution to the dilemma. Many
“Active Learning” modalities come into play in
this exercise. Brainstorming is the most effective
process for reaching consensus and is an
effective strategy for finding solutions to “the
marooned” problem.
1. Divide students into groups of five or six.
2. Present the scenario that involves the group
being marooned somewhere. (This might be in
a jungle; in a damaged submarine on the sea
floor; in the middle of New York City without
any money; physically lost in a new or strange
city, etc.)
3. Give each group some discussion prompts
such as: “What is the first thing you might do?”
“How do you think you might make this
happen?” “What is the second thing you might
do?” “How do you think you might make this
happen?” Then the third thing, etc., until a
logical plan is in place.

---
## Page 135
Each group must work out a realistic survival
strategy. Participants should talk about the
specific details of the situation and make a
realistic assessment of the dangers, dilemmas,
and problems to be resolved.
Groups 
should 
present 
their 
ideas 
and
conclusions in a variety of informative and
creative ways to the other groups. Consensus
should be reached on the presentation format
but it might end up being a short story
(realistically portrayed with emotion and simple
props) or a fictional diary entry, a letter home, a
poem, pieces of scripted comedy/drama, or non
verbally through pantomime or artwork.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Triage for Teachers
•
Build teamwork! It is the key to high morale and
encourages participants to share information, discuss
issues, and make decisions for the good of the whole team. It
also helps students see that decisions made by one person
don’t foster a culture of cooperation, sharing, and trust.
•
Value the ideas of these emerging adults who are dealing
with an ASD/LD diagnosis, school, and trying to plan for a
future. Discuss ideas for improvement as a group in a way
that is open and without judgment. Let each person know
that what they think is important and counts in this
discussion.

---
## Page 136
•
Encourage group feedback so that everyone can analyze
the outcomes and can recommend solutions, come up with a
plan B, or take the next steps to follow-up on what they have
learned.
The more that students are involved, the greater their sense of
ownership over the learning experience and the more of
themselves they will invest in their academics.
“Never doubt that a small group of
people can change the world; indeed it
is the only thing that ever has.”
Margaret Mead
In structured inquiry, students are provided with the question and
procedure (method), however, the task is to generate an explanation
that is supported by the evidence collected in the procedure.
In guided inquiry, students are provided with the research question
only, and the task is to design the procedure (method) and to test the
resulting explanations. Because this kind of inquiry is more open than a
confirmation or structured inquiry, it is most successful when students
have had numerous opportunities to learn and practice different ways to
plan experiments and record data.
In open inquiry, students form questions, design procedures for
carrying out an inquiry, and communicate their results.
PROBLEM-BASED LEARNING

---
## Page 137
Problem-based learning is a student-centered pedagogy where
students learn about a subject through the experience of problem
solving. Students learn thinking strategies to acquire knowledge. The
goals of problem-based learning are to help the students develop
effective problem solving skills, self-directed learning, effective
collaboration skills, and a high level of personal motivation to acquire
knowledge.
Working in groups, students identify what they already know, what they
need to know, and how and where to access new information that will
lead to solving the problem. Educators then facilitate learning by
supporting, guiding, and monitoring the learning process. This process
builds confidence in learners so that they feel they can meet the
challenge of the problem being presented to them.
Educator and brain-based learning specialist, Harold Barrows defines
the “Problem-Based Learning Model” as follows:
•
Student-centered.
•
Learning that is done in small student groups, ideally six to ten
people.
•
Facilitators or tutors guide the students rather than teach.
•
A problem forms the basis for the organized focus of the group,
and stimulates learning.
•
The problem is a vehicle for the development of problem solving
skills – it stimulates the cognitive process.
(Barrows 1996, p. 199)
Problem-based learning promotes the development of critical
thinking skills. As the ASD or LD adolescent or young adult learns how
to analyze a problem, identify relevant facts, generate hypotheses,
identify necessary information for solving the problem, and make
reasonable judgments about solving the problem, critical thinking skills
emerge.

---
## Page 138
Employers value the positive attributes of communication, teamwork,
respect, and collaboration that problem-based learners develop. These
skills better prepare students to function in the ever changing
information explosion that we see in the 21st century world around us.
Incorporating problem-based learning into a curriculum for those on the
ASD/LD Spectrum includes building these attributes through skill
building, interpersonal interactions, and through the problem solving
process.
Discovery learning takes place in problem solving situations where
the learner draws on his or her own experience and prior knowledge. It
is a method of instruction through which students interact with their
environment by exploring and manipulating objects, wrestling with
questions and controversies, or performing experiments.
Here are some examples of “inquiry-based learning activities” that
encompass many of the examples of inductive learning listed in the
above section. Try these out with groups of students to introduce the
various “Active Learning” concepts or modify them with your own
ideas.
GROUP ROLES
Explain that being a “contributing” member of a group or team in a
“positive way” is something that anyone who participates in life in
general will need to develop in order to be successful at school, when
working, and as each person becomes a valued and responsible
member of his or her community.
This activity is designed to look at the five roles that usually emerge
(some positive, some negative) when any group of five or more people
forms. Being aware of these roles can help individuals make better
choices about how they interact with others.
Explain that learning to work as part of a group or team is essential.
However, each person has the power to decide whether the role they
assume is positive or negative. Once each person in the group is

---
## Page 139
aware of the various group roles, individuals can make better choices
about how to interact with peers, teachers, counselors, authority
figures, co-workers, and their own families.
“What’s My Role?”
Tell students that the five roles that most often emerge in groups that
work together are:
•
The Leader: Keeps the group on task! Tries to get everyone to
participate.
•
The Encourager: Acknowledges the contributions of others.
Asks questions to clarify ideas.
•
The Passive Player: Does not lead, follow, or ask questions.
Acts bored, as if they couldn’t care less about being part of the
group.
•
The Team Player: The cheerleader of the group. Goes along
with what the rest of the group wants and pitches in, helps out,
cleans up, and is there for everyone.
•
The Hinderer: Says and does things that have nothing to do
with the group. Is disruptive and tries to distract others. This
person also tends to engage in “blurting!”
Activity: Divide students into groups of five or more. Pose one or more
of the scenarios listed below or substitute your own ideas instead of the
ones suggested. Five volunteers in each group choose one of the five
roles (drawn from slips of paper) to portray. Each group practices their
scenario for 15 or 20 minutes (perhaps in a hallway, another room, or
outside) and then acts out their scenario (with as little drama as
possible), so that the rest of the class has a chance to determine who
played what role in a realistic manner. Hinderers and passive players
should be true to form but not overly dramatic. In the end, all roles
should be obvious, not just the negative ones.

---
## Page 140
Here are some scenarios you might use:
Group A: A small group of students at a small liberal arts college get
together to play ping pong several times a week. They get along well
and enjoy each others’ company as well as the sport. Their skill levels
range from excellent to beginner. The more advanced players want to
formalize the group into a campus “club,” invite new members from
throughout the student body, and work towards competing with similar
clubs from other college campuses. Several of the students in the
current group oppose the idea. Each student should play their role as
the group tries to come to consensus about whether they should keep
the group “as is” or become an official club that would have more
members and work towards getting involved in official competitions.
Group B: A group of friends wants to get together for dinner. Some
suggest going out; some suggest ordering in; some suggest a potluck
as not everyone has the money to eat out; some suggest having a
potluck and going for ice cream afterward. Discuss where to go
(people should offer several ideas); how you will get there; and how
each person will pay if this option is chosen. Talk about alternatives –
as not everyone has the money to go out to eat. Decide what to do
and present your scenario from beginning to end with group roles
becoming obvious as the scenario plays out.

---
## Page 141
Group C: A group is talking about having an ice festival in town during
the February school break. In addition to the events already planned
(ice 
carving, 
snowman 
building, 
showing 
movies, 
having
entertainment, and serving lots of food), half the group wants to have
a Polar Bear Run (a race where people run down Main Street in their
bathing suits in freezing weather). This group is very vocal. However,
half the group thinks this is too risky (people getting sick; people
dressing inappropriately; the risk of falling on ice or snow; etc.) and,
while not quite as vocal, they are firm in their beliefs. The entire group
has to make a decision and illustrate the process they used to do so –
all group roles should be played out.
Group D: This group works at the local McDonald’s after school.
Each week there is a team meeting of co-workers with the manager of
the McDonald’s. This week the manager asks the group of workers to
think of five things that would make their jobs better and end up
increasing sales at the restaurant, plus boost the morale of the team.
Since business has been slow, if the group cannot come up with
solutions that might work, people will have to be laid off. Of course the
group Hinderer couldn’t care less, and the Passive Player has no
comment. The group should show how they worked together to
overcome the challenges of the Hinderer and Passive Players in the
group and come up with some ideas for the manager so that everyone
might stay employed.
Group E: The Student Council has been charged with organizing the
homecoming event for their high school. Traditionally, it has involved:
decorating the school hallways; getting each class of students
(freshmen, sophomores, juniors, and seniors) to build a float;
organizing a parade of students, sports teams, and school clubs;
running a fund raiser to cover the cost of the weekend; and, finally,
organizing two school-wide events; a football game and a dance.
Some of the students on the council think they should just do it the
way it was done “last year” while others think they should do
something new and different. Have each group member play his or her
“group role” as they discuss the project and try to bring the Student

---
## Page 142
Council to consensus on how they will plan and execute the
Homecoming Weekend.
Reflection:
After each group presents a scenario, ask:
•
Who were the leaders of this group?
•
Who were the encouragers?
•
Were there people who hindered the activity?
•
Was there a passive player?
•
Who were the team players?
Ask:
•
What could this group have done differently to work as a better
team?
•
Did everyone get a chance to have his or her voice heard?
•
Were ideas accepted without judgment?
•
Did everyone remember to listen to the person who was talking
or making suggestions?
•
How did the group decide on an outcome for the activity or
scenario they portrayed?
Making It Real
WHAT?
Active Learning is an effective experiential, participatory way
to impart knowledge to adolescents and young adults on the
ASD/LD Spectrum (ages 16–26.)
HOW?

---
## Page 143
Active Learning enables students to learn and develop
through participation at each step in their learning.
Active Learning develops interpersonal skills, critical thinking
skills, and a curiosity about learning.
Active Learning allows each student to build confidence and
gain self-esteem, while obtaining and retaining knowledge.
Active Learning engages students in thinking, doing things,
and reflection.
BENEFITS?
Active 
Learning 
strategies 
transform 
teachers 
from
dispensers of information to translators of information.
Educators become the facilitators or vehicles for this process
as they create classrooms that embrace learning!
Active Learners acquire a thirst for knowledge and obtain
skills for life, school, work, and independent living! This in
turn stirs leadership development in each individual and
brings the world of higher education and careers closer to
home for those who receive an ASD or LD diagnosis.
NOTES:
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

---
## Page 144
 
 
 

---

RELATIONSHIPS

EVALUATION
THE EMOTIONAL THERMOMETER
CHECKLIST AND NOTES
Competency 1: Skills for Life

---
## Page 146
“If young people are serious about
learning social skills they will put their
time and energy into learning about
nonverbal language and social
competencies and ‘shift’ will happen.”
Michael P. McManmon
“Everyone makes social mistakes. The
only way to avoid social mistakes is to
live in a cave.”
Jennifer Halloran, CIP Bloomington
“A person, who never made a mistake,
never tried anything new.”
Albert Einstein
“Challenges in social interactions are
often the biggest barrier between a
person on the ASD or LD Spectrum
and success.”
Michael P. McManmon
“One must give value to their existence
by behaving as if one’s very existence
were a work of art.”
Friedrich Nietzsche

---
## Page 147
“Social competencies are the biggest area of deficit for ASD
adolescents and young adults.”
Jennifer Kupiec, CIP Amherst
TEACHING INSIGHT: Chapter 3 contains a treasure trove of
information, ideas, and activities for imparting basic skills for life. The
social competencies detailed in this chapter are the proficiencies that
all ASD or LD adolescents and young adults you work with will need
to master. These competencies can be taught during classes or
sessions that are specifically geared to teaching skills for life.
Alternatively, many of these competencies can be modeled and
integrated into an appropriate part of an overall teaching day when
opportunities arise for introducing a specific life skill or a combination
of skills. They can also become the subject for teachable moments
when spontaneous situations occur.
Life skills are the communicative, social, and emotional coping skills
and strategies that each adolescent or young adult with an Autism
Spectrum Disorder (ASD) or Learning Differences (LD) diagnosis will
need to obtain in order to function in and be part of the wider
community that surrounds them. This chapter presents a structured
framework for imparting this knowledge using a variety of materials,
tools, techniques, tasks, and analyses developed by CIP (College
Internship Program) to teach social, cognitive, and emotional well-being
life skills to high school and post-secondary students on the diverse
ASD or LD Spectrum.
Building on the clinical diagnosis blueprint that emerged in Chapter 1,
and the foundation of an “Active Learning” methodology outlined in
Chapter 2, Chapter 3 – Competency 1: Skills for Life begins to build
and develop the basic and essential communicative and coping skills
that each adolescent or young adult will need to obtain. The information
in this chapter can be used in part or parcel to teach, instruct,

---
## Page 148
challenge, and engage students in acquiring these specific skills,
regardless of where they reside on the ASD or LD Spectrum.
Skills for life allow young people to navigate the challenges of everyday
living. They show each individual how to deal effectively with the
demands of day-to-day life and develop into a healthy, responsible,
productive adult. Acquiring skills for life is necessary in order for each
adolescent or young adult to become part of the wider world of school,
work, and independent living.
Skills for life are not a given and have to be specifically taught to those
on the ASD or LD Spectrum. Using the role playing and role modeling
scenarios provided in this chapter, as well as creating cohesive groups
that work as a team, will result in retention of the skills for life being
taught.
In general, skills for life are considered to be those abilities that help
promote well-being, positive health outcomes, and productive
development. Learning basic skills for life will allow each adolescent or
young adult to obtain (I) social and interpersonal skills (including
communication, refusal skills, assertiveness, and empathy), (II)
cognitive skills (including decision-making, critical thinking, and self-
evaluation), and (III) emotional coping skills (including stress
management and emotion regulation). These abilities comprise a set of
core skills identified in this chapter as Sections I, II, and III that will
empower the young people you work with to take steps to engage in
positive social relationships, make contributions to society, and
promote their own health and well-being. Skills for life are important in
helping each ASD or LD individual shape his or her world, not just cope
with it.
Section I of this chapter will focus on developing the social and
interpersonal skills that help adolescents and young adults make
informed decisions, communicate effectively, and become aware of
social nuances as they learn how to communicate clearly with others.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: The following story, adapted from “Made for
Good Purpose” … sets the scene for this first section. As readers will

---
## Page 149
remember, I was not diagnosed with Asperger’s Disorder until I was in
my mid-fifties. Being able to communicate outside my own ego, my
somewhat skewed sense of self, and my social awkwardness, by
learning basic communication skills was something that came to me
late in life – but thankfully these skills did come. As I listened to and
observed others, engaged in role playing, and became willing and
receptive to doing things differently, I learned valuable skills for life
that to this day, serve me well.
Making changes and adopting new behaviors is extremely difficult for
young people on the ASD/LD Spectrum. It often takes years of role
playing and instruction in social nuances before these adolescents and
young adults are willing to change anything or do anything new. The
information in this chapter was developed and designed to break this
cycle much earlier. By appealing to a young person’s sense of logic and
approaching this logic from many different directions such as learning
social competencies, using “Reframing” techniques, incorporating
wellness strategies, as well as sensory integration and cognitive
behavior therapy, social nuances become more of a norm for these
young men and women. In order to master social competencies, those
imparting this knowledge to adolescents and young adults on the
ASD/LD Spectrum need a wide range of tools that interact
constructively with a variety of social contexts. These tools can be
either physical (i.e., application of information technology) or socio-
cultural (i.e., language).
“Jim and Michael”
In 1979, my brother Jim moved to Las Vegas with his wife and
children to run a children’s group home that I owned and
operated. When he arrived, he asked where he should do his
banking and I referred him to the same bank that I had been
going to for the past four or five years. At that time (the good old

---
## Page 150
days), all banking was done manually and by written check.
There were no computers and people interfaced with a teller
(usually the same one) week after week.
The next time I went to the bank and gave the teller my check to
cash, she looked at it and said to me “Oh, you’re Jim’s brother!”
and then told me everything “under the sun,” about my brother,
my family, and most disturbingly … my very own life. As I walked
away from the teller, I remember thinking: “How did this woman
know everything about me and my life when my brother had only
gone to the bank once and I have been coming here for five
years?” I let it pass and filed it away in my personal
“discrepancy file” (located specifically, in my mind, on the right
side of my brain, in a little square box) along with all the other
discrepancies that had accumulated throughout my childhood
and adulthood. I explained it away, as simply “Jim talks too
much and you can’t shut him up.”
It was not until years after my diagnosis and a lot of running into
walls, isolation, and unhappiness, that I had finally experienced
enough discrepancies to get that “ah ha” moment of clarity.
Having become an astute student after learning about nonverbal
and social behavior by reading Stephen Shore’s book “Beyond
the Wall,” I finally understood that this experience with the teller
was crucial to understanding my Asperger’s Disorder diagnosis.
It slowly dawned on me that I had never once said anything
friendly or personal to the bank teller for the five years that I saw
her each week at the bank. I had never asked, “How’s your
day?” or said “Crazy weather, we’re having!” or anything a non-
Asperger’s person would say. I never learned her name or
anything about her life. While thinking about this past event 27
years ago – and how Jim interacted with the teller in the bank
that day – I decided to try to practice what I was learning from
Stephen Shore’s book. I guess the saying “better late than

---
## Page 151
never” applies here and that this was a teachable “aha” moment
for me.
I found myself heading for the Thrifty Car Rental in Indianapolis,
Indiana. As we were heading from the airport to the car rental
center, all of a sudden, the thought occurred to me that I could
say something to the young man who was driving me. Perhaps
we could even have a conversation. This was truly a novel
thought and something that in the past (remember the bank?) I
would have considered a total waste of my time. Even though I
still thought that I had better things to think about and do at this
particular time and place, I was determined to give casual
conversation with a stranger a shot.
The first thing I thought of to say was “how about those Colts?”
(I knew the Indianapolis Colts had a great football team that
year) and even though I was not interested in them, and in fact
hoped they would lose (I was a Patriots fan), I thought this young
man might be interested in them. Well, we were off and running
and then a problem occurred. How would I keep the
conversation going? I changed the subject to the weather, and
then to bicycling (one of my “special interests”) and then to art
museums and famous works of art (which was another “special
interest”). While continuing on our drive, I found out from this
young man that there were bike paths in Indianapolis I could ride
on, and that there were some great museums I could go to. After
this encounter, I felt an amazing surge in my self-esteem and a
little more competent about navigating the social world around
me.
Why did it take so many years and so many discrepancies for
me to understand this? Why did it take me 27 years to take
action? This answer was “brought home” to me recently during
a conversation I was having with my friends, Jack and Malcolm.
They have both known me for more than 20 years – before and
after – my diagnosis. Jack said to Malcolm, “Michael has gone

---
## Page 152
from being one of the biggest ‘asses’ in town to being one of its
biggest ‘assets.’” Malcolm totally agreed. This might sound like
severe or harsh criticism to many people … but not to me. It was
the biggest compliment they could have paid me. A real tribute
to how far I have come in understanding and then learning to
modify my behavior. In mastering this basic “skill for life”
(becoming socially competent) and having the ability to self-
regulate I started to feel “a part of” instead of “apart from”
people around me.
Flash forward … to last summer – July, 2014. I was headed for
the bank and after concluding my transaction with the teller, I
took a moment to call a colleague on my way home (who knew
this story well) to tell her I had asked the teller her name and that
we had engaged in “chit chat” and that all these years later, I am
still amazed that I can do this!
Section I: Developing Social and
Interpersonal Skills for Positive
Social Relationships
Recognizing social skill differences and addressing these when
working with adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum is
paramount. Depending on the situation and the individual diagnosis,
these deficits will range from mild to severe. On the mild end of this
scale, these young men and women may appear shy, quiet, or
reserved. On the more socially challenged end of things, they can be
disruptive, monopolize conversations, and seem to know everything
about all things. ASD/LD students on this end of the scale are often
extremely argumentative and prone to engage in inappropriate
behaviors.

---
## Page 153
As you work with students on the ASD/LD Spectrum you will encounter
a great deal of rigid thinking that is tied to the erroneous premises each
individual has adopted in his or her mind regarding the rules of life.
Many of these erroneous premises seem logical to the ASD or LD
thinker. As an educator, clinician, or professional working to develop
social and interpersonal skills, you will need to invite each individual to
examine new perspectives. Sometimes simply saying “Did you ever
look at it this way?” or “Might I make a suggestion?” or even “Can we
try an experiment for a week?” might be the key to having individuals
try something new or different. Urge students to be fearless and
courageous during role playing exercises and when trying out new
strategies.
Problem solving: Teach that there are multiple techniques to help
understand a social or interpersonal problem such as brainstorming or
asking for help from others. Challenge students to think outside the
box whenever an opportunity presents itself. Take a look at the black
and white thinking model on page 58 and see if your students can
comprehend that in real life the answers or solutions to a problem are
not always clear-cut.
Triage for Teachers
“Encourage the use of ‘video self-modeling.’ It gives students
the opportunity to practice skills repeatedly and then have
something to reference in the future.”
Jodi Pierce, CIP Brevard
“If behavior issues arise and someone is losing or seems to be
losing control, ask: ‘Have you taken your medications? Have you
eaten breakfast or any other food today? Did you take a shower
and put on clean clothes?’ If not … start by seeing if doing or

---
## Page 154
providing these things will turn the situation around. An energy
bar, something to drink, medications, or a warm, relaxing
shower may be what is needed rather than disciplinary or
medical attention.
Reflection is processing or reconstructing the learning
experience and connecting it to retention. Reflection helps
students and teachers understand the meaning and impact of
their efforts and links what they have learned with what they
have done. Without reflection, students just go through the
motions of learning.
Practicing reflection helps the ASD/LD adolescent or young
adult gain a greater sense of self. When learners are asked to
think about their goals and their individual progress they have
the opportunity to master self-assessment skills that help them
become more independent. Reflection offers the instructor an
opportunity to identify and assess the knowledge that students
gain through ‘Active Learning.’”
Michael P. McManmon, CIP Founder

---
## Page 155
Black and White Thinking
I am right.
You are right.
We are both right.
We are both wrong.
You can be right and I can be wrong.
I can be partially right and you can be partially right.
I can be partially right and you can be partially wrong.
You can be partially right and I can be partially wrong.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
The student handout on the left illustrates just how wide and varied the
perception or understanding of a situation, a solution, or a problem can
be.
Discuss alternative definitions as to what it means to fail. Is the glass
half-full or half-empty? For instance, Thomas Edison did not succeed at
making the light bulb the first time around. It took him 999 attempts until
he discovered the right combination of metals that would work
(tungsten). He had to ask and then find the answers to many questions
until he could figure out how to make his light bulb work. We could say

---
## Page 156
all those attempts were mistakes or failures or we could reframe our
perception and say that he had 999 approximations to the right
answer. This same process and procedure applies to most inventions
in the modern world. Everything we learn brings us closer to doing
things better and having our lives work better for us. At CIP (College
Internship Program) we often tell students that we have never had a
student fail at anything as long as he or she was willing to ask for help
and was willing to approach things from a new or untried angle.
TEACHING INSIGHT: There are no mistakes . . . just approximations
to the right answer. We also tell our students that each question they
ask will get them closer to finding the solution to a problem and each
newly acquired answer will do the same thing. Helping students
understand that everything in life is a question, an answer, a
conversation, a technique, a game, is important, and in order for them
to move forward in life, essential.
Social communication is literally a set of rules for life! It’s about
modeling and teaching behaviors that help adolescents and young
adults on the ASD or LD Spectrum understand that they need to
develop a vast array of social competencies in order to master these
rules. It’s also important to build relationships with students which
become the foundation for better communication, improved decision-
making, and increased performance. The most direct way to build
relationships is by getting to know each individual you work with,
counsel, or mentor personally. Through confidential conversations you
can then integrate the information you obtain into an overall person-
centered teaching and learning plan – referred to as a PCP throughout
this book.
As you work with adolescents and young adults to help them master
each social competency area, ask each one to: “Use their brain to think
about others. Use their eyes to make connections (looking another
person in the eye). Use their body to make connections (i.e., shaking
hands, or touching someone lightly on the shoulder). Use their words to
make connections (how are you doing today?).”

---
## Page 157
Life skill competencies can also be learned by having an individual or a
group of individuals work with a social mentor or a clinician. Role
modeling and role playing can also be done outside the classroom
setting with teachers, peer mentors, or advisors.
This chapter offers readers a flexible, user-friendly way of imparting
social communication skills. Using the suggestions, activities, and
handouts provided to address areas of need as they arise, or as a
complete unit of study, will be beneficial to any ASD or LD adolescent
or young adult whom you are working with.
MAKING EYE CONTACT
Eye contact is defined as looking directly into a person’s eyes when
that person is talking. Because the visual sense is the dominant sense
for most people, eye contact is an important nonverbal communication
skill that individuals diagnosed with ASD or LD need to acquire. The
way people look at others communicates many things including
interest, affection, hostility, or attraction. Eye contact is needed in order
to maintain the flow of conversation and for gauging the other person’s
response.
Practice making eye contact: Set up a role play with students paired
off in groups of two. Have one person maintain a glance towards a
person’s face without glancing away for more than three seconds at a
time then switch. Then play the game Blink to illustrate the concept of
staring. Have one person stare at the other person until one person
blinks. Then switch.
Ask: How does it make people feel if no one looks at them directly (by
using eye contact) as they speak? What is the difference between eye
contact and staring?

---
## Page 158
LEARNING TO LISTEN
Listening to someone who is talking tells the speaker whether or not
someone cares about what he or she has to say. It also tells the person
speaking that the listener is interested in the topic and is paying
attention to what is being said. When the listener’s actions match up
with the words the speaker is saying, both the listener and the speaker
increase trust, clarity, and rapport. When they don’t match up, they
generate tension, mistrust, and confusion.
Practice listening: Role play this scenario in small groups of two to six
people. Have participants choose one person to be the speaker. While
the speaker is talking he or she maintains eye contact with the
listener(s) and vice versa. Listeners keep their body language and
posture oriented towards the speaker (leaning forward slightly, keeping
head up). Participants engage in reflective statements regarding the
speaker’s topic of conversation; this can include: paraphrasing what
was said, reflecting on what was said, or asking questions about what
was said.

---
## Page 159
Discuss: How might the listener change the subject appropriately if he
or she senses you are not interested in the conversation? How does it
make the speaker feel when it is clear he or she is not being listened
to?
The activity entitled Talking Down is a humorous and sometimes
startling way to practice listening and making eye contact. As the
activity progresses students can see (and hear) what happens when no
one listens to what is being said. This activity illustrates that without
mastering the ability to listen to others, things quickly fall apart. As the
activity progresses students will see it’s impossible to establish a
relationship when both people talk at once and that without being able
to listen to others no direction or bond can form. Chaos reigns.
GREETING SKILLS

---
## Page 160
Greeting skills are all about being comfortable in introducing yourself
to others in a friendly and confident way. Greeting someone means
displaying good posture, making eye contact, and giving them a
friendly smile. Brief verbal greetings and a firm handshake are usually
also part of this process. Exchanging greetings does not come naturally
for most adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum.
Sometimes it’s shyness, self-confidence, or a self-esteem issue.
Sometimes it’s a sensory issue – not liking the feel of shaking hands or
being physically that close to another person. Therefore, it is a skill that
needs to be learned and developed.
The ability to interact confidently with others promotes open and honest
dealings or discussions with friends, teachers, co-workers, and
acquaintances. However, adolescents and young adults with an ASD
or LD diagnosis often have social phobias such as an anxiety disorder
involving a fear of public scrutiny, fear of speaking in public, and they
worry excessively about being embarrassed or humiliated. This may
make skill development in this area difficult. Time spent on role playing,
or having a social mentor join in and help a student who is having
difficulty with greeting skills will build the confidence needed for this
basic life skill.
Demonstrate examples of friendly verbal greetings such as, “Hi, how
are you?” or “Nice weather out there today!” Have participants role
play this in groups of two or three using a normal tone of voice. Each
person in the role play maintains eye contact and smiles. Each one
introduces him or herself and shakes hands whenever a hand is
extended to them.
Ask: What is a normal tone of voice? When should your greetings be
formal? When should they be informal?
INITIATING AND MAINTAINING A
CONVERSATION

---
## Page 161
Initiation means learning to start or join in and become an active
participant in conversations. Keeping the conversation going takes
practice and role play so that the adolescents and young adults you
work with feel at ease when conversing with others.
Instructions: A good way to get this going is to put participants into
pairs or small groups. Make a comment or ask a question that
introduces a topic that pairs or small groups of students can talk about.
Ask one person to start the conversation and then allow their partner or
others in the group to have the chance to participate as well. As each
person contributes to the conversation the person who started it keeps
it going and makes an effort to include themselves in the dialogue. This
can be done by asking follow-up questions and making appropriate or
relevant comments or by having each person ask open-ended
questions that don’t require one-word answers. Keep in mind the
abilities and interests of the adolescents and young adults working on
this skill when suggesting or choosing a topic of conversation.
Prompts:
•
Bring in a newspaper and have students choose a headline to
discuss with each other.
•
Have the dialogue start off with latest movie seen, video
watched, event attended, or even what happened the night
before.
•
Discuss the weather (especially if there was a record event of
hurricane, flood, or major snowstorm recently).
•
Talk about someone both parties know (in a positive way).
“Talking Down”

---
## Page 162
Everyone has a partner and they sit in pairs close
together and face to face. Holding eye contact the
whole time, both people talk at the same time about a
topic they have chosen beforehand. (See the prompt
list below for ideas or let students come up with their
own.) Set a timer for 30 seconds and see what
happens then try it for a minute.
The aim is for each pair to keep talking at all costs
while trying to make the other person give up and stop
talking so that one of the two can be heard. No
physical contact is made; only expressive talk is used.
Players will soon find out that they cannot hear a word
that the other person is saying because they are
concentrating on talking and telling their own story.
Students will soon see that talking louder, faster, and
with emotion and gestures does nothing to get the
other person’s attention if he or she will not listen to
you.
Since neither player hears the other person over the
racket they can’t use their listening skills; there is no
positive interaction, no affirmation, no dialogue, and
therefore no relationship. The point of it all: that
without listening to each other and allowing some
degree of give and take, affirmation, or response to
what one person is saying, there can be no direction
or useful conversation.
Prompts:
•
Talk about everything that happened to you from
time you got up this morning until now.
•
Tell your favorite fairytale, with as much expression
and animation as possible.

---
## Page 163
•
Be a salesperson in a store at the mall trying to
convince someone to buy a particular product. (Be
aggressive as your job depends on selling a
certain amount of this item each day or you’re out
of a job.)
•
Persuade the other person of a belief you hold
near and dear: soapbox style – stand on a chair if
you’d like – so that you make your point.
Discuss: How frustrating this exercise was!
Ask: What did it feel like to be in a position where no
matter how loud or hard you tried to be heard – you
were not. Talk about what each person participating
learned from it. Talk about give and take and
reciprocal conversation.
After the discussion, do the exercise again with the
same story line and this time have one person
patiently listen to the other until the story is told, and
the speaker feels listened to, then switch. During this
version of Talking Down, all listeners will be
supportive, ask questions at the end of the story, or
during it if appropriate.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 164
Ask: Have you started a conversation with anyone new lately? How
might you start a conversation differently with someone new compared
to someone you know well? Here are some good conversation
starters?
•
Share plans for upcoming holidays or seasons (summer
vacation, skiing trip in winter, etc.)
•
Books read or things seen during the week (a traffic accident, a
funny photograph, a local event).
•
Tell about something heard that was funny, stupid, or intelligent.

---
## Page 165
ENDING A CONVERSATION
The goal of this role play is to have adolescents and young adults learn
to terminate conversations in a clear and courteous manner.
Have a group of students start a conversation. As the conversation
slows ask them to role play one or more of them needing to leave
(phone call, another appointment, need to get home, etc.). Have the
person who is ending the conversation include a courteous statement
such as, “It was great talking to you!” or “It was great meeting you!”
Then have this person make a departing statement such as, “I’ve got to
get going, hope to talk to you soon.” You can stretch this further by
having another role player need to leave, and then another so that a
variety of scenarios for terminating a conversation are played out.
Have the departing students demonstrate how to initiate a handshake
when appropriate (such as a formal situation or when first meeting
someone, or saying goodbye to an authority figure or someone in
charge), or a more informal ending (hug, air kiss, or light tap on
shoulder or arm if a good friend or relative and participant feels
comfortable in doing so). Have each person who is ending the
conversation make eye contact while saying goodbye.
Tip for initiating an appropriate handshake: Use this example from the
TSA Air Marshall training guide to help your students:
“Handshaking: Reach out and take the hand of the other person.
Grasp firmly and at the same time maintain eye contact to the ‘count
of three’ then let go of the other person’s hand.”
Encourage your students to talk about the fact that even TSA Air
Marshalls need lessons in social skills – proving how important mastery
of these skills is even for those who are not on the Spectrum.
Ask: How can you properly end a conversation if the other person does
not slow down or stop talking?

---
## Page 166
INTERRUPTING OR JOINING A
CONVERSATION
Instructions: During this role play students start a conversation and
the pre-designated person who wants to join in waits for a break in the
conversation (for example, when speaking slows down or when the
subject of the conversation is changed). Then the designated listener
excuses him or herself for interrupting the conversation, with: “Excuse
me for interrupting.” This role player asks if he or she may participate in
the conversation, “Would you mind if I join you?” He or she then makes
a relevant statement or asks a question to engage in the conversation.
Something like: “I heard you talking about the storm last night,” or “I
just saw that movie and I really liked it too;” anything that contributes to
the conversation in a positive way and that allows the interruption to be
smooth and appropriate is fine.
Ask: What are some clues to look for that others may give you if they
do not want you to join the conversation?
ASKING PERMISSION
Instructions: Have adolescents and young adults practice verbally and
politely the request or privilege of engaging in an action. Have
participants avoid arguing if told no. Have each person end the role
play by saying thank you if permission is granted or politely ask for an
explanation if permission is not granted.
ACCEPTING CONSTRUCTIVE CRITICISM
Instructions: Have participant or participants in this role play maintain
eye contact. The person accepting constructive criticism needs to say
“thank you for bringing that to my attention.” This person defers from
arguing and remains calm. He or she then asks for advice on how to
better the behavior being discussed. Have each person who engages

---
## Page 167
in this role play give a verbal response that indicates understanding of
the constructive criticism and that he or she will work on the area or
areas of need.
MAINTAINING SELF-CONTROL
Instructions: Put students in pairs. Have each person in the role play
choose a role: one is the instigator and the other one is the person who
is trying to maintain control. The participant who is trying to keep it all
together needs to maintain a relaxed posture and facial expression, use
a calm voice, and appropriate voice volume. This person also needs to
defer from arguing and engage in relaxation techniques that are
appropriate to the environment (example, count to ten silently, engage
in deep breathing) to do so.
Ask: How do you decide or figure out what is an appropriate voice
volume for the situation is? What are some environments or situations
where it is particularly important to maintain self-control?
“BLURTING”
Adolescents and young adults may “blurt out” the answer, an idea, or
thought at an inappropriate moment. This often happens at school, in
social settings, or at home. Mostly it’s because the individual has not
developed patience and/or turn-taking skills. It might also be because
he or she has poor impulse control and just can’t hold the answer in –
but – it may be because of another person’s eyes! ASD or LD
adolescents and young adults who have communication issues may not
have a very well developed understanding of eye gaze.

---
## Page 168
Many educators use their eyes to silently communicate which table or
person they are talking to when they ask questions. In a normal
classroom setting the expectation is that an individual(s) will follow the
eye gaze and do what the silent communication is asking. Adolescents
and young adults who struggle to attend class or those who find it
difficult to pick up on social cues and gather information from people’s
eyes will not necessarily see the teacher doing this or understand it and
so may blurt out an answer.
If this is a problem, the easiest thing to do is to use the name of the
person you are talking to. When correcting a student who is “blurting” it
is important to make it clear that you were using your eyes to indicate
who should answer, so that they learn to ‘‘read’’ your eye gaze. For

---
## Page 169
example, “I am looking at (student’s name)” and “I am talking to
(student’s name),” or “I am not talking to you right now.” You may also
want to remind the individuals you work with to think and to listen with
their eyes, as well as their brains and ears.
What Is Blurting?
“Blurting” is anything you do with your words or your body that
interrupts the thoughts of another person in an unexpected way.
“Blurting” is considered a rude behavior because it doesn’t
show others that you were being considerate (by just listening),
when you added your words or body movement to the
conversation.
Think about … what type of “impression” it makes if you “blurt”
with your words in class or shove your way into a group?
When you “blurt” with your words it means:
1. You aren’t really paying attention to what the other person is
saying. Others will interpret this to mean that you think what
