you have to say is more important than what someone else
has to say.
2. You aren’t paying attention to who the speaker is thinking
about when he or she is talking; you weren’t thinking with
your eyes.
3. You make comments out loud while the teacher is talking
even though the other kids are keeping those same thoughts
inside their heads.

---
## Page 170
When you “blurt” with your body it means:
You make big body movements that distract other people.
You unexpectedly walk into a group or away from the group,
distracting other people’s thoughts. For example, if you enter a
group that is working together in a way that all the attention is
drawn to you so people can’t concentrate on what they were
doing or saying. This is barging into the group. This is a
physical form of “blurting.”
Or: You touch another person in an unexpected way, for
example, a slap on the back, bumping into them, shoving,
cutting in line, etc.
To avoid “blurting:”
1. Think with your eyes. If someone is looking at another
person (that is, thinking about this other person) while they
are talking, it is probably not expected that you start talking
at that moment.
2. Enter slowly, subtly, and quietly into a group so that as you
enter, you tend to blend into the group rather than call
attention to yourself.
Blurting handout adapted from Michelle Garcia Winner’s “Social
Thinking” curriculum: www.socialthinking.com.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
HOW TO ACCEPT “NO” FOR AN ANSWER

---
## Page 171
Instructions: Create a scenario where the answer is an un-negotiable
no! Have the participants in the role play maintain eye contact. Have
one person deny the request and the other one say, “ok.” Each person
in the role play has to defer from arguing, whining, or pouting. If the
participant being told no does not understand why the answer is no, he
or she needs to practice calmly asking for a reason. If participants
disagree or have a complaint, role play how to bring this up with an
appropriate person who is in charge or a mediator.
RATIONAL PROBLEM SOLVING
Instructions: Define the problem for those taking part in the role play.
Have participants list all possible options and solutions to the problem.
Have them list advantages and disadvantages for each option. They
then choose the best possible option and follow through with it. This is a
good role play to do in small groups of three or four participants so that
the skill set can be mastered by hearing how others come up with
solutions.
The activity on the next page (p.66) is to get the concept of rational
problem solving across to students on the ASD/LD Spectrum.
DISAGREEING APPROPRIATELY
Instructions: Have one person in this role-play state a position with a
good or definite rationale. They must defer from arguing. Have the
student who stated the position listen, while the other participants give
their opinion and take a different stance. If a solution is needed, use
rational problem solving techniques learned in the role play above. Ask
the students to end the disagreement by verbally agreeing to disagree.
DISCUSSIONS

---
## Page 172
Instructions: Have one of the two participants in a group of role
players (six to eight people) wait his or her turn to speak. When it is
that person’s turn to speak ask the person to state his or her
opinions/statements in complete sentences. Tell all participants in the
discussion that they need to stay on topic and allow time for each
person to have his or her say. Have each one comment appropriately
on the other person’s opinions/statements. Stress how important it is to
maintain listening skills throughout the discussion.
Here are some ground rules for participating in a discussion:
•
Remember you will get a chance to have your say, in just a
minute. Exercise patience and wait your turn.
•
Don’t interrupt another person because when you interrupt
you’re not giving the other person time to talk.
•
Listen, even though you secretly think the other person does not
have anything worthwhile to say or contribute to the
conversation. If you can stick this part out – you may very well
change your mind as your listening skills kick in.
•
Express your opinions, thoughts, and feelings using respectful
language.

---
## Page 173
Takeaway
“Attending activities with students and observing their behavior is
the best way to help students gain better social skills. Some
students I have observed have trouble with large crowds of people.
If you are aware of this student’s issue, you can teach the student to
arrive early before the crowd, or later when the crowd has
dispersed. This can relieve social anxiety. Students may not know
the source of their apprehension, but by experiencing social
situations that cause discomfort we are provided with a teaching
opportunity.’’
Jan Petrides, CIP Berkshire Center
“The Skateboarding Dilemma”
Divide students into groups of three to six people. The
scenario is that “you all like skateboarding.” Your group
got together and created a skateboarding rink in part of
the school playground – so that you can enjoy
skateboarding and do it safely. Your teachers and school
authorities have warned your group that everyone who
uses the skateboarding rink has to be responsible. Your
role play group has already pre-determined that everyone
in the group will take turns monitoring this area during
recess and lunchtime to avoid any problems.

---
## Page 174
One day, a classmate arrives at the rink with his
skateboard but no protective gear (helmet, knee pads,
etc.). He begins to skateboard and does a few dangerous
moves that put other “boarders” at risk. He continues to
act irresponsibly and ignores you and your friends when
you try to intervene.
What do the participants in your role play group think the
person who is monitoring the rink that day should do?
Keep in mind that everyone in your group promised the
school authorities that skateboarding would be done
safely and that rules would be put in place to make sure
this happens. You need to resolve this problem rationally
and quickly. What do you do?
Discuss:
•
Options for handling this situation such as what does
the group do first to make sure others are safe around
this risk-taking “boarder?”
•
If you need to tell someone about this – who might you
tell and what might be the outcome?
•
Who might be affected if you and your friends are not
responsible about everyone’s safety at the rink?
•
How can you ensure that everyone uses safety gear
and acts responsibly?
•
What would be the effect on future skateboarding if
someone is hurt?
Each group presents their solutions and the rationale
used to make these decisions to the other groups and
discusses the various ways people looked at and solved
the “Skateboarding Dilemma.”
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 175
MAKING A GOOD IMPRESSION
Instructions: Tell participants they need to maintain eye contact, smile
appropriately, and engage in listening skills. In most cultures people shake
hands with each other when first meeting someone or in a formal setting.
They also speak in language appropriate to the situation. For example,
speak assertively when you want to be persuasive; formally when in a
formal situation; and informally when among friends. Students also need to
maintain an appropriate body posture and most of all stay on topic while in
conversation.
Ask: How do you know if a good impression was made?
INFORMAL LANGUAGE
Instructions: Tell the adolescents and young adults you are working with
that we use informal language in informal situations, such as with friends,
family, or familiar settings. We do not use informal language in very formal
situations such as job interviews. Informal language includes slang,
colloquial expressions, and any other conversational terms that express
relaxed language.
Ask: Who do you prefer to use informal language with you?
GIVING A COMPLIMENT
Instructions: Engage in attentive behaviors with students divided into
small groups of two people for this role play situation. Have one person in
the role play make a positive statement regarding the other person based
on their behaviors or appearance (for example, their dress, hairstyle,
offering the correct answer in class, etc). Have the person who is giving a
compliment use sincerity, kindness, and discretion (so that boundaries are
not crossed).
Point out what inappropriate statements would sound like – such as
discussing or making comments about an individual’s body, shape, size,
mannerisms, or any form of harassment. Have participants take turns role

---
## Page 176
playing an apology if something was said that makes the other person feel
uncomfortable.
Ask: What should you do if someone makes you uncomfortable with a
compliment? What is the difference between compliments and
harassment? What should you do if you say something that makes another
person uncomfortable and how can you tell that they are uncomfortable?
ACCEPTING A COMPLIMENT

---
## Page 177
Instructions: Have the participant accepting the compliment maintain eye
contact and smile at the person giving the compliment. Have the receiver
of the compliment make an appreciative statement such as “thank you.”
Explain that it is usually hard for people regardless of learning differences
to accept compliments.
ENTERTAINING COMPANY
Instructions: Have small groups of students role play a specific situation –
such as having friends in for pizza and a movie. Have the student who is
hosting the event invite guests in and make introductions if necessary. As
the host makes guests comfortable, for example, with comfortable seating,
offering refreshments, etc., he or she also points out where the bathroom is
located. Using a pleasant voice tone and facial expressions, the host starts
a conversation and makes complimentary statements or comes up with a
topic for conversation. As the event continues he or she circulates to make
sure everyone has refreshments and is engaging in conversation with
others.
“The Interview Game” (Allow 15 minutes)
Here’s a classroom or small group session activity about
learning conversational flow and asking open ended
questions. It’s a great icebreaker called “The Interview
Game.” This activity is useful for reinforcing how
important it is to ask questions. It also shows participants
that questions are the best way to keep a conversation
going.
For this icebreaker, have one participant interview the
other for three minutes. The interviewer is to find three

---
## Page 178
interesting facts about the other person they are
interviewing by asking them a lot of questions (Where do
you go to school? Where do you live? Where were you
born? How many people in your family? and so forth).
After the interview, students should share with the class
the three interesting things they found about the other
person.
Early Conversations – Putting People at Ease
•
For this role play tell the individuals you are working
with that making simple observations is one of the
best ways to get a conversation going. Ideally it is best
to avoid asking too many questions in an early or first
conversation. Let others talk or make statements and
ask questions so they feel as though they have some
control. Divide students into pairs and have each
other play the role of being the person who makes the
receiver comfortable.
•
Once an individual has made the receiver in the
conversation comfortable, he or she can start asking
questions. Talk with students about how questions
can be turned into statements to come off as less
threatening. For instance, “What do you think of this
restaurant?” can be turned into “I’ve never been to
this restaurant before; I wonder what it is like?”
Discuss: The importance of being alert and monitoring
how the receiver is feeling as a conversation progresses.
Let each person know the importance of “small talk,” and
how it puts people at ease. Here is a quick list of common
small-talk subjects: weather, sports, hobbies, latest
comics read and movies seen, home town, holidays.
Ask: 
What 
topics 
did 
participants 
bring 
up 
in
conversation?

---
## Page 179
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
PLANNING WHAT TO SAY
Instructions: This is a good scenario for ASD/LD adolescents and young
adults to practice and is a great tool for them to use when planning a
phone conversation, meeting someone for the first time, going to an
interview, or making an appointment with a doctor or dentist. Make sure
participants plan statements and areas of conversation that are relevant to
the person and setting, for example, student having to call to request a job
interview, dispute a telephone bill, or make arrangements for a meeting
with a school principal, or someone who is in authority. Have participants
think of relevant questions to ask regarding the possible topic matter.
Have each one participating in the exercise think of how they might depart
or terminate the pre-planned conversation. He or she might say something
like: “I’ll look forward to talking to you again sometime soon,” or “I’m
expected at a meeting in about five minutes, but I’m glad we got to talk to
each other,” or “Thank you for helping me with my problem or making an
appointment for me.”
Ask: How many details should you plan on sharing? When is it good to
have a planned response?
STATING AN OPINION
Instructions: During the role play a participant makes a clear statement
that is a personal opinion, not a fact. Participants in the exercise need to
make sure they are in an appropriate situation to state an opinion, such as
in a class, group discussion, brainstorming session, etc. (Remember: If
stating an opinion does not add, or contribute, to the situation then there is
likely no need to express it.) The opinion that is shared needs to be
phrased appropriately and arguing is not an option.
Ask: How do you inform others of your opinions? Is it ok to disagree with
someone? What is the difference between a fact and an opinion? How do

---
## Page 180
you feel when others force their beliefs on you?
ASKING PERMISSION
Instructions: Work in pairs. Have the person asking permission maintain
eye contact with the listener and ask in a calm and direct manner what he
or she would like permission to do. The listener receives the request,
reviews it, and grants or denies the permission. If denied the listener
remains polite in the refusal.
Discussion questions: What are some situations that are appropriate to
ask permission? Examples of this are: Using the phone, going out,
entering another person’s room, borrowing a possession, bringing items
into the home, decorating a room, using privileges, or bringing guests into
the home, etc.
CALLING A FRIEND ON THE PHONE
Instructions: Students should alternate roles for this role play. Use skills
for planning what to say, if necessary. Be sure the role players discuss
and set up a scenario where the caller is calling during an appropriate time
(people typically don’t call each other before 10 a.m. or after 9 p.m. unless
someone has specifically said this is ok). Have each person practice using
phone etiquette such as avoiding making calls when spending time with
someone else. Start off by simply saying “Hello.” Have one person role
play making the call and identifying his or herself (using first and last
name), and state where he or she is calling from (for example, from home
or from a place of employment). Remind participants to speak in an
audible voice tone and when the conversation is over say “Goodbye,” and
gently place the receiver down or … if role playing using a cell phone close
it quietly. Remind everyone that when leaving or taking a message to make
sure it is complete and that all information obtained is correct.
REINFORCING SOCIAL COMPETENCIES

---
## Page 181
Invitations vs. Inspirations
If we think back to the most enjoyable conversations we’ve had with
people, there usually was no sense of “What do I say now?” or “Do I talk
next?” If we want the young people we work with to learn how to have
conversational flow, we need to help them understand the social principles
of invitations and inspirations.
An invitation is when you (the initiator of the conversation) say something
that explicitly lets your partner know it is his or her turn to speak.
An inspiration is when you (the initiator of the conversation) say
something that makes your partner want to speak out spontaneously.
Here’s a great metaphor to give students that demonstrates invitation vs.
inspiration.
“The Deli Metaphor”
Scenario: Imagine that you and your conversation partner are working in
a (poorly designed) deli. Half of the ingredients are at one end of the deli
counter, and half of the ingredients are at the opposite end. The two of
you need to make a sandwich, so you decide to stand at opposite ends
of the counter and slide the sandwich back and forth. Your partner adds
some lettuce, and then slides it down for you. You add some mayo, and
then you slide it back so he can add some turkey. It’s a bit of a strange
image, but stay with me. This is an extremely useful metaphor.
Now, let’s make the image a little stranger. Let’s say that you and your
partner are chatting as you work (normal enough), but that the sandwich
you are making represents your conversation (not so normal). You ask
“How was your weekend?” and slide the sandwich down the counter.
Your partner replies “Oh, it was great. How was yours?” and slides the
sandwich back. You reply “It was fine,” and try to return the sandwich.
The sandwich travels six inches and stops dead.
What happened? Well, you didn’t give your partner a clear invitation or a
strong inspiration. Without either of those things, your partner didn’t
know what to say next (and perhaps was unsure if it was his turn to

---
## Page 182
speak). So he didn’t respond. The conversation lapsed, and the
sandwich stopped sliding.
Remember, an invitation is when you say something that explicitly lets
your partner know it is his or her turn to speak. And an inspiration is when
you say something that makes your partner want to speak out
spontaneously. Without an invitation or an inspiration, your partner
might not know what to say or whether to respond. That’s why you want
to be deliberate by offering invitations and inspirations to your partner.
Using Invitations
Discuss: Why invitations such as “What did you do today?” makes
conversation partners realize the initiator of the conversation is:
•
Communicating that it is the other person’s turn to talk.
•
Giving the other person a cue of what to talk about.
Tell students that: Invitations act as our secret weapons in
conversations. In the beginning, it is going to be common for individuals
initiating a conversation not to know what to say next. Even the most
experienced conversationalists experience this. Tell these young men and
women that this is where you can insert an invitation into the conversation
and get things rolling again. The best invitation is an open-ended
question. There is an art to asking open-ended questions. If you ask a
superficial question (“What do you think of the weather outside?”), you’re
not going to get to really know the person you are talking to. But if you are
too intimate (“What is your biggest regret in life so far?”), people are going
to get uncomfortable. The trick is to start superficial and then get more
intimate as you observe someone’s body language becoming more
comfortable. It’s important for students to know that this transition from
superficial to intimate occurs over the course of a relationship, and can
sometimes take months before the other person is ready to start really
opening up about themselves. Once someone talks intimately with you,
you can start sharing intimate things about yourself.
Practicing invitations: After you feel students have a firm grasp on
invitations, they should practice issuing invitations with each other. Have

---
## Page 183
students pair up and practice a conversation by asking open-ended
questions with one person the initiator of the conversation and the other on
the receiving end of the conversation. End the role play by reminding
students that you cannot build an entire conversation on invitations. A
conversation will end up sounding like an interview and it will be awkward.
A positive conversation will have a natural feeling, with occasional
invitations from both speakers.
NONVERBAL COMMUNICATION HANDOUTS
The student handouts (pp.71, 72, and 74) talk about improving nonverbal
communication skills and reading body language. Encourage the young
people you teach, mentor, and advise to start discussions or do creative
role plays with these handouts. Students at our centers have illustrated
these concepts through skits, stories, and artwork around the concepts in
these handouts. Allow students to use pantomime, mime, and humor to
develop nonverbal communication skills and being able to read body
language, in each other.
Evaluating Nonverbal Signals
Eye contact
Is the person looking you in the eye or is he or she glanci
downward?
Facial expression
What is his/her face showing? Is it mask-like and
unexpressive, or emotionally present and filled with
interest?
Tone of voice
Does his or her voice project warmth, confidence, and
interest, or is it strained and blocked?
Posture and gesture
Is his or her body relaxed or stiff and immobile? Are
shoulders tense and raised, or slightly sloped?
Touch
Is there any physical contact? Is it appropriate to the
situation? Does it make you feel uncomfortable?

---
## Page 184
Intensity
Does he or she seem flat, cool, and uninterested, or over-
the-top and melodramatic?
Timing and pace
Is there an easy flow of information back and forth? Do
nonverbal responses come too quickly or too slowly?
Sounds
Do you hear sounds that indicate caring or concern?
Types of Nonverbal Communication and Body
Language
Facial expressions: The human face is extremely expressive and
able to express countless emotions without saying a word. And
unlike some forms of nonverbal communication, facial expressions
are universal. The facial expressions for happiness, sadness,
anger, surprise, fear, and disgust are the same across cultures.
Body movements and posture: Consider how your perceptions
of people are affected by the way they sit, walk, stand up, or hold
their head. The way you move and carry yourself communicates a
wealth of information to the world. This type of nonverbal
communication includes your posture, bearing, stance, and subtle
movements.
Gestures: Gestures are woven into the fabric of our daily lives. We
wave, point, beckon, and use our hands when we’re arguing or
speaking animatedly – expressing ourselves with gestures often
without thinking. However, the meaning of gestures can be very
different across cultures and regions, so it’s important to be careful
to avoid misinterpretation.
Touch: We communicate a great deal through touch. Think about
the messages given by the following: a firm handshake, a timid tap

---
## Page 185
on the shoulder, a warm bear hug, a reassuring pat on the back, a
patronizing pat on the head, or a controlling grip on your arm.
Space: Have you ever felt uncomfortable during a conversation
because the other person was standing too close and invading your
space? We all have a need for physical space, although that need
differs depending on the culture, the situation, and the closeness of
the relationship. You can use physical space to communicate many
different nonverbal messages, including signals of intimacy,
aggression, dominance, or affection.
Voice: It’s not just what you say; it’s how you say it. When we
speak, other people read our voices in addition to listening to our
words. Things they pay attention to include your timing and pace,
how loud you speak, your tone and inflection, and sounds that
convey understanding, such as “ahh” and “uh-huh.” Think about
how tone of voice, for example, can indicate sarcasm, anger,
affection, or confidence.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 186
NONVERBAL COMMUNICATION AND BODY
LANGUAGE IN RELATIONSHIPS
Here are some good examples to discuss with your students:
Ted, Arlene, and Jack are all articulate speakers who say one thing while
communicating something else nonverbally, with disastrous results in
their relationships:
Jack gets along with his friends at school, but not with the people who
matter most to him. If you were to ask his good friends and relatives why
this is, they would say that Jack is “too intense.” Rather than look at you,
he devours you with his eyes. And if he takes your hand, he lunges to
get it and then squeezes so hard it hurts. Jack is a caring guy, but has a
terrible time being in sync with people. This awkwardness limits his
ability to advance at work. He just isn’t seen as being good with others.
Arlene is attractive and has no problem meeting eligible men. Keeping
them is the problem! Arlene is funny and interesting, but even though she
constantly laughs and smiles, she radiates tension. Arlene’s shoulders
and eyebrows are noticeably raised, her voice is shrill, and her body is
stiff. Being around Arlene makes many people feel uncomfortable.
Arlene has a lot going for her that is undercut by the discomfort she
evokes in others.
Ted thought he had found the perfect match when he met Sharon, but
Sharon isn’t so sure. Ted is very eligible. He is good looking,
hardworking, and a smooth talker. The trouble is that Ted seems to talk
more to “himself” than to Sharon. When Sharon has something to say,
Ted is ready with a reply before she finishes her thought. This makes
Sharon feel ignored, and she has started to date other men. Ted loses
out at school and at his part-time job for the same reason. His inability
to listen to others makes him unpopular with many of the people he
most admires.
These smart, well-intentioned people struggle in their attempt to connect
with others. None of them are aware of the nonverbal messages they
communicate. 
If 
you 
want 
to 
communicate 
effectively, 
avoid
misunderstandings, and enjoy solid, trusting relationships both socially and

---
## Page 187
professionally, it’s important to understand how to use and interpret
nonverbal signals.
Once your students develop their abilities to manage stress and recognize
emotions, they will naturally become better at reading the nonverbal
signals sent by others.
As students continue to pay attention to the nonverbal cues and signals
they send and receive, their ability to communicate will improve!
THE VALUE OF PEER MENTORING FOR
IMPARTING SOCIAL SKILLS
Research shows that modeling behavior by peer mentors in real-life
situations carries the highest degree of learning success. At our six CIP
centers students receive a carefully selected social mentor who is within
the age range of the student by about five to ten years. These peer
mentors serve as a generational, social translator. Mentors accompany
students on outings to museums, computer stores, and other sites,
connecting a student’s “special interests” with opportunities to practice
social competencies in a variety of relaxed and real-world settings.
Because individuals encounter people from diverse backgrounds, the
ability to interact in heterogeneous groups is of particular importance. The
need to think and act reflectively is central to understanding this interplay
of both intrapersonal (within the person) and interpersonal (between
persons) interactions. This can mean the difference between success and
risk in dealing with the challenges of everyday life. Having a peer mentor
experiencing and advising during real-life situations cements not only the
social skills that are being taught and reinforced, but the context of when
and how to use them.
TEACHING INSIGHT: Jan Petrides wrote an article for CIP’s blog on
teaching social skills through recreational activities (which happens to be
the next social skills topic in this section of Chapter 3). I’ve reprinted it
here to give those using Autism and Learning Differences (An Active
Learning Teaching Toolkit) a further fund of knowledge to draw upon
about how to teach social skills in a real-time and place setting. Jan’s

---
## Page 188
words conclude Section I: Social and Interpersonal Skills for Positive
Social Relationships. Her story forms a bridge as readers end Section 1
and start to delve into Section II: Developing Cognitive Skills: Decision-
Making, Critical Thinking, and Self-Evaluation.
Improving Nonverbal Communication Skills and
Reading Body Language
Good 
communication 
is 
the 
foundation 
of 
successful
relationships, both personal and professional. But we communicate
with much more than words. Most of the messages we send other
people are nonverbal. Nonverbal communication includes our facial
expressions, gestures, eye contact, posture, and tone of voice. The
ability to understand and use nonverbal communication, or body
language, is a powerful tool that can help you connect with others,
express what you really mean, navigate challenging situations, and
build better relationships.
What is nonverbal communication and body language?
Nonverbal communication – or body language – is a vital form of
communication. It is a natural, unconscious language that
broadcasts our true feelings and intentions in any given moment
and clues us in to the feelings and intentions of those around us.
When we interact with others, we continuously give and receive
wordless signals. All of our nonverbal behaviors–the gestures we
make, the way we sit, how fast or how loud we talk, how close we
stand, how much eye contact we make–send strong messages.
These messages don’t stop when you stop speaking either. Even
when you’re silent, you’re still communicating nonverbally.
Oftentimes, what we say and what we communicate through body
language are two totally different things. When faced with these
mixed signals, the listener has to choose whether to believe your

---
## Page 189
verbal or nonverbal message, and, in most cases, they’re going to
choose nonverbal.
Why nonverbal communication matters! If you want to
communicate better in all areas of your life, it’s important to become
more sensitive to body language and other nonverbal cues, so you
can be more in tune with the thoughts and feelings of others. You
also need to be aware of the signals you’re sending off, so you can
be sure that the messages you’re sending are what you really want
to communicate.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Teaching Social Skills through Recreational
Activities
Social skills are difficult for most of our students with Autism
Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and Learning Differences (LD). Many
students are uncomfortable making eye contact and may not
understand subtle social cues or facial expressions. Some students
may be confused or distressed when new or unfamiliar activities are
presented and may also struggle with peer interactions.
Recreational activities can help in many ways by making social
interactions more comfortable. Students should be required to
attend some sort of social activity each week. Staff can mentor
students by allowing them to experience new and different things
and supporting them as they do.
Self-care and grooming are essential. It is “key” to how one is
perceived and accepted by the community. Self-care basics can be
taught by teachers, social mentors, clinicians, and others for any
community experience. The first perception that people get can

---
## Page 190
“make or break” a social interaction. Recreational activities offer an
opportunity to teach grooming, and what dress and manner is
appropriate to the event. Teach that expectations vary depending
upon the activity. For example: What is appropriate for a local ball
game would not be acceptable at a Broadway play.
Any trip that involves interacting with the community can be a
positive experience for those on the ASD or LD Spectrum. Pre-
teaching allows staff to reduce any anxiety students may have. For
instance, asking a clerk for help finding something can be
challenging for some students. Knowing student needs, and
rehearsing strategies prior to the interaction can help. Role playing
can also be a useful strategy.
Attending activities with students and observing their behavior is
the best way to help students gain better social skills. Some
students I have observed have trouble with large crowds of people.
If you are aware of this student’s issue, you can teach the student to
arrive early before the crowd, or later when the crowd has
dispersed. This can relieve social anxiety. Students may not know
the source of their apprehension, but, by experiencing social
situations that cause discomfort, they are provided with a learning
opportunity.
Staff can work together with students to identify the source of
discomfort, and together, develop techniques to improve a
student’s comfort level. Once students learn techniques that work
for them, they will be able to experience things that they would not
have been able to do before. These strategies are important for the
student to learn so they can make adjustments independently and
live a life not limited by their social inadequacies.
At CIP, residential staff members are present in many facets of the
students’ lives. Social skills are integral to every aspect of the
residential experience for our students. “Recreational activities act
as a medium for social interaction. It allows staff to observe
students in social situations that are not available in the school

---
## Page 191
milieu. One example of this was a teachable moment with a student
I worked with on a shopping trip to the mall.
I looked over at him as he sat on a bench in the middle of the mall
stuffing popcorn in his mouth handful after handful. Most of the corn
was falling in his lap. This behavior would go unnoticed watching
movies in the student lounge. Yet, in the greater community, this
behavior drew negative attention to him. Taking trips with students
and observing their behavior is the best way to know where they
may need help.
One must be careful not to overstep your relationship with students.
Students that you have known only a short time are not likely to
accept your feedback. Once you have developed a positive
relationship with a student, then you can be an effective role model
and mentor. Having a positive relationship with your students is
important in order for them to accept your help.”
Jan Petrides, CIP Berkshire Life Skills Coordinator
Section II: Teaching Cognitive Skills:
Decision-Making, Critical Thinking,
and Self-Evaluation
The second section of Chapter 3 examines the need to impart cognitive
skills to adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum in a
structured, logical manner. This section gives educators, clinicians, and
professionals the ideas, materials, and resources that will develop these
skills in each individual they work with. Looking at “The Donkey Rule,”
“Discrepancy Theory,” the “Hidden Curriculum,” “Reframing,” and self-
evaluation techniques such as the “Emotional Thermometer” will allow
those working with this population to have a plan, strategies, and

---
## Page 192
classroom materials for teaching these cognitive skills through active,
participatory learning.
Young people, regardless of where they reside on the ASD/LD Spectrum
often lack workability and have a difficult time being a member of a team.
You also know for sure that these emerging young adults are usually very
gifted in their areas of “special interest,” but because of their inability to
interact with others when working in groups or as part of a team, they can
become rigid, inflexible, and stuck.
The first skills for life strategy and tool you will impart to those you teach,
counsel, and mentor is “The Donkey Rule.” The two key components that
make up “The Donkey Rule” are cognitive flexibility and “Discrepancy
Theory.” As stated throughout the text of this book, you know students on
the ASD/LD Spectrum can be extremely rigid in their thinking, and lack the
ability to compromise, negotiate, or even entertain an opinion that is
outside of their own belief system. Because of this rigidity, students often
try to go it alone in decision-making. When they encounter problems with
this approach, the results and outcomes are usually negative.
Cognitive rigidity or inflexibility makes it difficult for those on the ASD/LD
Spectrum to build any kind of coping system. Teaching adolescents and
young adults about “The Donkey Rule,” and how to use it to handle the
many areas of cognitive rigidity, where they simply cannot accept change,
use basic logic, or think outside the box, will be well worth your classroom,
clinical, or professional time. Practicing “The Donkey Rule” through role
playing, role modeling, and concrete examples from your own lives and the
lives of others will reinforce that “The Donkey Rule” is a powerful tool for
anyone to use – regardless of whether or not they are on the ASD/LD
Spectrum. Teaching the concept of “The Donkey Rule” and using the story
that accompanies this rule is a good place to start imparting the knowledge
and coping techniques necessary to continue building and reinforcing the
social and interpersonal skills mastered in Section I of this chapter.
THE DONKEY RULE
If five people that an adolescent or young adult trusts say it’s a donkey and
the person who is asking the opinion thinks it’s a horse – it’s a donkey! By

---
## Page 193
using logic the adolescent or young adult takes the majority opinion of the
five trusted people he or she has asked (teachers, advisors, mentors,
friends, or parents) to heart because these five people are in all probability
– right. The adolescent or young adult can believe that what these trusted
individuals say is factual and true and that it is based on the perceptions
and observations these caring people have made in getting to know and
understand the person asking the question or stating the dilemma. Tell the
person asking for advice (quite bluntly, if you must) that by not taking this
advice to heart, he or she becomes a jackass – this is “The Donkey Rule.”
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Feel free to share this story adapted from my first
book, “Made for Good Purpose …” about a student named Mark with the
young people you work with (see p.80). Mark’s story should generate a
poignant discussion in your classroom or during an informal social
mentoring small group session about the value of using “The Donkey
Rule.” Listeners can then give examples or personal stories from their
own lives when using “The Donkey Rule” might have come in handy, or
when “The Donkey Rule” helped someone make a good decision or
change an opinion that was erroneous.

---
## Page 194
“The Donkey Rule”
•
Listening to advice and using mentors.
•
If five people you trust (mentors) call it a horse and you still call it
a donkey, then don’t be a jackass.
The key element is picking five people (mentors, advisors,
teachers, family members, or mature friends) to ask an important
question to before you make a decision (especially in critical
situations).
Five mentors I can rely on for good advice:
1. __________________________________________
2. __________________________________________
3. __________________________________________
4. __________________________________________
5. __________________________________________
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 195
Adolescents and young adults on the ASD or LD Spectrum are often
socially disconnected and are overly sensitive to the point that they are
consumed with their own feelings and their own version of events. At this
same time, they are often oblivious to the effects of their own behavior and
ignore how other people think and feel. This creates a skewed vision of the
world and impairs their ability to make sound decisions. This is why
learning to use “The Donkey Rule” is so important.
“The Donkey Rule” will help adolescents and young adults make good
decisions. Once a network of trusted individuals is established, these
young men and women can poll four or five of them, and know that the
advice given will provide guidance in the right direction.
“Donkey Rule” Activity: Divide students into groups of eight people. One
person states a dilemma and the others offer advice. Role play the options,
opinions, and advice given. The majority opinion of others will be offered
as the solution. The person with the dilemma will then make a decision.
Tell participants that they can make up a scenario, or use a real-life
example (as Mark did) where asking the opinion of others would have
been useful.
Discuss: Have each group talk with other groups that participated in the
activity about the dilemma presented, the advice given, and discuss why
the initial advice was accepted or rejected.
Ask: Why do you think Mark was so resistant to taking the advice of
people he trusted? What factors led to Mark taking the advice of others?
Does “The Donkey Rule” make sense to you?
TEACHING INSIGHT: “The Donkey Rule” is one of the first things we
teach students at CIP. This simple rule helps students make appropriate
decisions by showing students that they need to ask for input and advice
from several trusted people when making decisions. Doing this helps
them build their own sense of social judgment.
The handout on page 78 is for the young people you work with to keep
handy to remind them of “The Donkey Rule,” and who they might turn to
for advice.

---
## Page 196
DISCREPANCY THEORY
“Discrepancy Theory” helps adolescents and young adults see the effects
of their behavior (cognitive rigidity) on themselves and on others.
“Discrepancy Theory” is a structure that helps those on the ASD/LD
Spectrum begin to understand some of the negative emotions they feel
toward a person, situation, or event. It explains the reasons why they might
hold conflicting beliefs about themselves, or a discrepancy concerning an
action or something they have seen or heard.
This theory explains two different types of self-images that young people
on the Spectrum often have about themselves. The first image is the
actual self and represents reality and who they actually are. This is the
what you see is what you get (WYSIWYG) person. The second image
represents the person most ASD/LD adolescents and young adults think
they are. This image is usually very different from the actual self, or the
reality-based person.
Many adolescents and young adults on the ASD or LD Spectrum do not
take notice of the normal discrepancies that occur in their day-to-day lives
even though they may have occurred repeatedly year after year.
“Neurotypical” individuals tend to notice these discrepancies easily and
take action quickly. For example, if it has warmed up in the spring, a
“neurotypical” person will realize it is time to stop wearing a winter coat
and start wearing a lightweight jacket. Someone with an ASD or LD
diagnosis, on the other hand, may very well be wearing a winter coat, hat,
and gloves because he or she simply has not noticed the difference in
weather, or even that the season has changed. Friends and teachers have
probably asked, “Why are you wearing your winter coat, when it is so
warm out?” The answer will most probably be rigid and defensive, and the
person will continue to wear the winter coat until he or she decides to
change it.
Accepting the need for change and adopting new behaviors are extremely
difficult for people on the ASD or LD Spectrum and it often takes years of
role playing and instruction before any changes or the willingness to try
something new will occur. Most, if not all, of the curriculum materials in
Autism and Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit)
have been developed and designed to break this cycle much earlier in an

---
## Page 197
adolescent’s or young adult’s life. By appealing to a young person’s sense
of logic and approaching this logic from many different angles, such as
social thinking, wellness, sensory integration, and cognitive behavior
therapy, dealing with change and becoming more cognitively flexible can
be learned and becomes more of a norm. At CIP we teach this process in
“Reframing” classes.
“Mark”
Mark was a student from Montana and an only child. He had
received his high school diploma and was an outstanding piano
player. Mark had a pleasant disposition and demeanor and was
easy to know and like. It was clear to the staff that he was socially
awkward, and had been very sheltered by his parents at home. He
lacked social judgment and was in complete denial about needing
help in this arena.
Mark was easily led into situations he did not know how to handle.
For instance, during his first few months at CIP, he met another
student named Josh, and the two became instant friends. At first,
this seemed all right with staff because Mark was very happy
having a friend who wanted to spend time with him. Mark asked to
share an apartment with Josh. Mark talked with his advisor about
the pros and cons of living with Josh. After talking it through, Mark’s
request to room with Josh received approval.
Unfortunately for Mark and his concerned advisor, Josh turned out
to be a manipulative person and a new set of problems developed.
Within a month or so, it became apparent to CIP staff that Mark was
doing all the cleaning, cooking, and upkeep of the shared
apartment. After further investigation, they also realized that Mark
was cleaning Josh’s room for him, doing his laundry, and lending or
giving him money.

---
## Page 198
Mark requested and received a lot of counseling and advice on how
to handle the disparity in this relationship, and staff continued to
monitor the situation. Unfortunately, Josh continued to manipulate
Mark. Staff thought it best to have them switch roommates at this
point.
Mark was getting advice about the situation from his advisor,
therapist, other staff members, and from me daily (all of us, people
he trusted). He did not seem to be able or want to listen to us, or our
advice. Eventually, Josh left the program, and we thought the
problem was resolved. Mark made a lot of progress after this and
began to develop healthier friendships, took an internship position,
and began to socialize more with others.
Mark continued working with the staff, gaining confidence in his
ability to make the right social judgments. It was at this point, that
Josh invited Mark to visit him for a weekend at his new apartment.
Mark asked staff, his parents, and his peers what they thought.
Each person he asked told him not to go. He ended up in my office
at the end of the day and we discussed the pros and cons of visiting
Josh. I asked Mark to tell me what he thought “The Donkey Rule”
was. He recited it word for word.
I then asked him if he thought that his parents, his advisor, his
therapist, his tutor, the residential staff, his friends, and I all had his
best interests at heart. He said that he thought we did. We talked
about “The Donkey Rule” together: if five, six, or seven people he
trusted and asked advice from said “Don’t go to Josh’s this
weekend!” should he follow that advice or go down there? Mark
knew the right answer but decided not to take the advice. He visited
Josh and did not have a nice time or a good experience.
When Mark returned to campus, he decided he would work hard to
learn what to look for in friends and to distinguish the good and not
so good in people. He began to utilize the advice of others on how
to make appropriate social judgments. From that point on, he made
tremendous progress, becoming a role model for others by telling
his story and stressing the importance of “The Donkey Rule” to his

---
## Page 199
peers and new students and using his own experiences as an
example.
A couple of years later Mark gave a speech at CIP’s annual
parent’s weekend about using good social judgment and learning to
trust in “The Donkey Rule.” His presentation was impressive,
heartfelt, and compassionate. Mark has gone on to do really well
and part of this is due to his ability to learn from his mistakes. He
now has a job, his own apartment, and most importantly, the ability
to make good choices for himself.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
REFRAMING
Teaching “Reframing” in a specific learning module or class will help
adolescents and young adults break out of their habitual ways of looking at
things. “Reframing” shows each individual – or a group or class of
adolescents and young adults who carry an ASD or LD diagnosis – how to
look at their lives from a fresh perspective.
A frame can refer to a belief or to a limiting view of the world. Similar to a
window frame, the smaller the view, the less an adolescent or young adult
will see outside it. “Reframing” seeks to convert thoughts and feelings
around a negative situation into a positive pathway for change.
“Reframing” identifies the good intention in a negative behavior and
readjusts the thought or revaluation process so that behavior will be more
successful. Understanding this theory helps those on the ASD or LD
Spectrum redefine experiences and relationships in new positive
directions.
By learning and mastering “Reframing” techniques and understanding that
negative preconceived notions and expected outcomes can significantly
skew the outcome of any situation, adolescents and young adults on the
ASD/LD Spectrum can learn to create a foundation for change and growth.
They will learn that they can take an active role in contributing to their own

---
## Page 200
happiness by regulating emotions and learning new behaviors through this
process.
“Reframing” involves teaching individuals (as early in the school day as
possible) in a classroom setting or other distraction-free environment, such
as a quiet room. In this safe, secure setting instructors guide students
through:
•
A discussion of monthly, weekly, or daily “Reframing” themes they
will work on.
•
Emotional Thermometers (see page 83) and discussion.
•
Practice situations (if applicable).
•
Several “Hidden Curriculum” items (see page 85).
•
An “Executive Function” check-in to see if everyone is ready for his
or her day.
Students are then ready to face their day, having learned valuable insight
about themselves by being aware of their individual wellness issues from
the previous evening, and getting organized for their upcoming day.
In classes or lessons designed to teach “Reframing” skills, adolescents
and young adults gain self-awareness by learning how to monitor eating,
sleeping, wellness, and interactions using an assessment scale called
“The Emotional Thermometer.” Young adults learn to assess and look for
patterns from the day and night before. For instance, they (and those who
interact with them) will clearly see the effect eating food with too much
sugar or junk food has on a person’s body. By using “The Emotional
Thermometer,” ASD/LD students are able to chart how they feel after 24
hours with no daylight, or isolating on a computer all night long, or going for
hours at a time without talking to another human being. By tracking
emotions via “The Emotional Thermometer” system, adolescents and
young adults can see that how they are feeling is a direct result of what
they have been eating, how long they have been sleeping or not sleeping,
and the effects that self-isolation is having on their daily well-being.
Young adults with an ASD or LD diagnosis have spent most of their lives
struggling to operate within the framework of society that simply does not
understand their differences. As stated before in Chapter 1, these

---
## Page 201
differences are wide and far ranging – but have commonalities. The young
men and women who receive an ASD or LD diagnosis have significant
difficulties in social interactions, along with restricted and repetitive
patterns of behavior and interests.
Responses to emotions are monitored daily by each individual for the
purpose of self-reflection and for gaining insight into the understanding of
each person’s emotional and sensory issues. Through daily self-appraisal,
adolescents and young adults can learn to identify the relationship
between behaviors and habits, and track social and emotional functioning.
Awareness is the first step in the process of change. Once the adolescent
or young adult understands his or her individual patterns and behaviors –
he or she can choose to change them.
Showing students how to use “The Emotional Thermometer” will allow
each individual to make adjustments to his or her personal “Emotional
Thermometer” as needed. This in turn, helps the ASD/LD student not only
to stay under control, but to improve interactions with teachers, peers,
friends, and parents. The first steps to making things better might be going
for a walk with friends, taking a swim, or going out with a roommate for a
meal. By using “The Emotional Thermometer” system, adolescents and
young adults will learn to self-regulate and make good choices.
TEACHING INSIGHT: Read and discuss Emily’s story during a
“Reframing” class. Emily’s story is something most ASD/LD adolescents
and young adults will relate to and sets the scene for beginning to use
“The Emotional Thermometer” and other “Reframing” techniques to
achieve cognitive flexibility.

---
## Page 202
“Emily”
Emily, in typical college freshman fashion, was always skipping
breakfast and heading right to her 9 a.m. class. By staying up
too late every night trying to get some extra studying time in,
followed by gaming on her computer, Emily often overslept and
was always running late. Unfortunately, this scenario happened
on the night before an important biology exam, and she barely
made it to her class on time the next morning. In spite of a
rushed start, Emily was confident that she knew the material that
she was going to be tested on better than anyone else did. She
had been fascinated by biology since she was a young girl, and
this was a subject area she always did well in. So why would
Emily then fail this midterm? An even better question is why
would Emily not even complete the exam that morning?
Emily was very embarrassed about being “labeled” with a
nonverbal learning disorder. She saw the diagnosis as
derogatory and inaccurate. Emily never wanted special help or
an accommodation. She did not ask or advocate for more
testing time on her exams even though her processing speed
was slower than that of a “neurotypical” college student. Emily
knew that she was entitled to this type of assistance because of
her sensory issues, but her own sense of pride kept her from
asking for it. Distractions during the exam, like the ticking of a
clock, the shuffling of papers, or the coughs and slight
movements of her classmates in their seats, were major
distractions that would break her concentration and delay her
processing speed.

---
## Page 203
After this biology exam failure, Emily acknowledged that she
“might” need some help and asked her social mentor to help her
cope with this failure and learn to self-advocate. She started by
attending “Reframing” classes at CIP (instead of sleeping until
the last minute and rushing off to her college classes) and slowly
learned how to regulate, listen, and read her emotions using
“The Emotional Thermometer” system. Slowly but surely, Emily
gained some much-needed insight into what her nonverbal
learning differences were all about. As she started to understand
and accept her differences and her diagnosis and see the
positive aspects of her personality, she started to deal with her
issues in spite of her reluctance.
More importantly, Emily started to achieve the level of success
that her native intelligence could produce. By using solutions,
like self-advocacy, to solve her problems in class and at test
taking time, she learned to literally reframe her narrow
perspective into new beliefs. Emily then started to look at her
learning disability simply as a difference, not a disability – akin to
speaking a language different than her peers. She then set
about learning the predominant culture’s language (or
“neurotypical” language) in order to be successful. She also
gained control over her core wellness issues such as hunger,
fatigue, and anxiety in order to be ready to face the challenges
of each day.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
As the young men and women you work with learn to regulate their
emotions by altering their behavior each one will realize that they can
create the positive feelings they want by altering the factors that
contribute to their wellness. Young adults can get a new perspective on
how their own regulation (or lack thereof) affects others around them.
As students learn to control some of the variables that cause
depression and anxiety, they become emotionally more astute, and in

---
## Page 204
many cases (by learning these skills and coping mechanisms), they
can reduce their need for medications.
TEACHING INSIGHT: Used as a daily process, “The Emotional
Thermometer” and “Reframing” classes help connect the dots
between a student’s behavior and their emotions. With practice,
individuals can develop self-efficiency and self-control by associating
new behaviors with past emotions. Since individuals with an ASD or
LD diagnosis crave consistency, an everyday (8 a.m. or as early as
possible) “Reframing” session using “The Emotional Thermometer” is
an important factor in rewiring the complex patterns of the brain. This
breakthrough will not be rapid but working with weekly themes
incorporated and coordinated with his or her daily lesson plans will
produce tangible and behavior-changing results. Time and practice
lead toward wellness and independence.
“The Emotional Thermometer” handout below can also be made into a
poster for classrooms.
THE EMOTIONAL THERMOMETER
“The Emotional Thermometer” provides a visual tool for self-regulation
in areas such as anger, hunger, loneliness, fatigue, anxiety, fear,
disorientation, flexibility, and consideration of others. Emotional
regulation is a developmental skill that is used in interactions between
the brain mechanism and daily experiences with others. Teaching that
the body regulates emotions helps each young adult or adolescent
react in appropriate ways. “The Emotional Thermometer” exercise
helps to identify internalized emotions, a first step towards self-
regulation.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Try the activity that Teresa Colby did with her
class (on p.84) with the adolescents and young adults you teach,
counsel, and mentor! Theresa talks about the “Reframing” process
with real-time examples from her classroom. Duplicate and use her
sound ideas for implementation through student artwork and

---
## Page 205
descriptive writing. It will allow you to really know and then help your
students as they work on skills for life and setting goals.
“The Emotional Thermometer”
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
“Reframing Class”
“Reframing” Class is the first class of the morning for most CIP
students. “Reframing” is a term that therapists use to describe
the process in which a person converts the thoughts and
feelings around a negative situation into a positive pathway for
change. “Reframing” identifies the strengths and values of a
behavior and readjusts the thinking process, so that the person
is able to recognize the positive and successful outcomes of the
behavior.
CIP’s yearly theme for “Reframing” is designed to help students
with an ASD or LD diagnosis understand that they are

---
## Page 206
“inherently valuable and made for good purpose.” “Reframing”
allows students to learn how to reframe their perspective, and
take an active role in creating and defining their own happiness;
our students have just as many strengths and values as any
other college student.
One of the first steps in “Reframing” is to know yourself: to
recognize your strengths and limitations, and how to create your
values based on those strengths and limitations. These are
difficult concepts for students with ASD and LD to grasp
because the concepts are often abstract and require
metacognition and self-awareness. To help understand and
identify these concepts, visual representation of the self serves
as a springboard for discussion and communication.
In “Reframing” Class, the students made framed panels that
visually represent how they perceive themselves. Students were
given free choice in media and encouraged to think abstractly
and symbolically about themselves, rather than produce a
simple mirror representation of the self. The students also wrote
explanations of their panels. Below are the words that CIP
students Kiersten and Dustin wrote to accompany their self-
imagery.

---
## Page 207
Kiersten:
I chose a horse to represent myself because horses are kind,
strong, they work hard and they help people with their lives. I
think I am all of these things as well. These are what I most
value.
Dustin:
This is a self-portrait of me sleeping on the floor. I put myself
relaxed and stretched out, but in reality, I have all these things
going on in my head: Thoughts and interests like science,
medicine, math, music, nature, cars, guitars, drawing, writing,
swords, shields, and honor. The world outside my thoughts
contains random things pertaining to my life, like animals,
friends that I study with, water bottles, my computer, and my
family.
Teresa Colby, CIP Bloomington, Reframing Instructor

---
## Page 208
THE HIDDEN CURRICULUM
Teaching about the “Hidden Curriculum” during a “Reframing” class, or
in any situation where it is seems appropriate, will demonstrate the
social rules that most young people on the ASD/LD Spectrum just do
not get, or even know about. The term “Hidden Curriculum” describes
the unwritten social rules and sets of expectations for behavior in
society that most of us adhere to. Richard Lavoie (cited in Bieber 1994,
p.84), leading expert in the field of the “Hidden Curriculum,” described
it as “the important social skills that everyone knows, but no one is
taught. This includes assumed rules, adult and student expectations,
idioms, and metaphors.”
Understanding the “Hidden Curriculum” is difficult for everyone, but it is
especially so for individuals with a deficit in social interactions. It is
almost as though there is a secret language “neurotypicals” use that is
not apparent to individuals on the ASD or LD Spectrum. A scenario that
Brenda Smith Myles, author of “The “Hidden Curriculum”: Practical
Solutions for Understanding Unstated Rules in Social Situations” talks
about when lecturing is her example of a young man with Asperger’s
Disorder using a public men’s bathroom. No one ever specifically tells
young boys or young men the rules of operation so to speak … for using
the men’s room. Boys and men (not on the ASD/LD Spectrum)
instinctively know the answer to the following question: If there are
seven urinals and there is a man using the far left urinal, where would
the man coming into the bathroom go? The unwritten answer is the far
right urinal. If a third man comes in to the bathroom, which urinal would
he use? He would go to the middle urinal. The unwritten rule is that
each person keeps a respectful distance for privacy.
Because of the way an ASD or LD mind works, these types of
situations are not always apparent to young men on the Spectrum, nor
are the many other unwritten rules, such as not taking the urinal right
next to another man when the bathroom is empty. Other behaviors for
using the men’s room, such as not talking to other men or boys, or
looking at them or their body parts, is behavior that needs to be taught,
practiced, and reinforced. These things are obvious to their

---
## Page 209
“neurotypical” peers, who seem to pick up these cues by osmosis,
while young men with an ASD or LD diagnosis have to learn these rules
one by one. And, if that is not enough of a challenge, they need to learn
that there are variations of the rules to be used in different situations
and under varying conditions. To add even more confusion to the
subject, bathrooms in various cultures operate differently. This is why
instruction and mastery of these nuances is so very, very important.
Providing detailed and specific “Hidden Curriculum” instruction and
practice serves as an effective method of pre-teaching for students with
ASD and LD. For example: the instructor uses topics that are apparent
and easily understood by a “neurotypical” student, but difficult for
students with learning differences to understand, such as the bathroom
example above, or examples where interpreting rules and instructions
can have a double meaning. The following scenario, while humorous,
shows those imparting knowledge how important this teaching is. Think
of what might happen when an adolescent or young adult on the ASD
or LD Spectrum takes the Laundromat instructions literally.
“The Laundromat”
In the Laundromat, the sign instructing patrons to “immediately remove
clothes when red light goes out” refers to the clothes inside the
washing machine or dryer, not to the clothes you are wearing. This is
so the machine will be available for another patron’s use when you are
done.
“Hidden Curriculum on the Go” (a 2010 AAPC iPod and iPad
Application)
“Hidden Curriculum” instruction spells out the unwritten rules and
allows students to practice specific situations beforehand. All
adolescents and young adults with an ASD or LD diagnosis need to be
taught about societal expectations that include: Idioms or metaphors;
behaviors and actions; assumed roles; nonverbal actions – such as

---
## Page 210
staring or inappropriate touching, and any other topic that may seem
like common sense to a “neurotypical” person.
As an educator, clinician, or professional utilizing this book, you are
already employing role play to impart social skills. Role playing is
equally valuable for imparting the “Hidden Curriculum” agenda. The
valuable social skills taught by using role playing to rehearse everyday
situations, such as taking a book from the library, asking a stranger for
directions, or asking another student on a study date work really well for
teaching about the “Hidden Curriculum.” “Hidden Curriculum”
instruction is a process that allows for rapid and new growth.
Understanding that context can be viewed in a variety of ways is an
important skill for young adults on the Spectrum to master. If the
adolescents and young adults you work with don’t know the social
nuances in life and why they need them they cannot go forward with
confidence and success in day-to-day interactions necessary for
independent living.
When you are about to make the following statements to the ASD or LD
adolescents or young adults you work with daily please keep in mind
the answer is almost always a negative one and that you will indeed
have to teach, role model, and role play correct behaviors for them over
and over again until it starts to sink in.
Everyone knows that …
No … ASD or LD adolescents or young adults do not
know.
Common sense tells us …
Young people on the ASD or LD Spectrum really do
have a hard time using common sense or even
understanding the common sense concept.
It should be obvious …

---
## Page 211
And it is … to a “neurotypical” person, but not to
someone with an ASD or LD diagnosis.
I shouldn’t have to tell you …
Not only will you have to tell the ASD or LD student
what it is, you will have to repeat the rule, task, or
concept over and over again.
Use videos or movies to analyze social situations – sitcoms generally
work well.
Try this in a class: Stop and start a video the group or class is watching
together then see if your students can predict what will happen next.
See if they can notice sarcasm or idioms, recognize facial expressions,
and recognize nonverbal communication. At CIP, we have used “I Love
Lucy” segments and staff will play a minute or two of the episode and
then hit the pause button on the DVD and ask, “What is Fred’s face
saying to you? What do you think Ricky will do next? What emotion is
Ethel feeling?” Most of this would be crystal-clear and apparent to an
eight-year-old but can mystify a 20-year-old on the Spectrum.
Personal Thanks: I’d like to thank Mary Lawler, former Program
Director at CIP Amherst for creating the role playing scenarios for this
activity. She based the following scenarios on actual situations and
events that occurred at her center and provided her students with real-
time-and-place opportunities to develop their “Hidden Curriculum”
radar.
“HIDDEN CURRICULUM” ROLE PLAYING
SCENARIOS
Divide students into small groups. One person takes the lead in
modeling an inappropriate behavior. The rest of the group has to use
logic and discussion to figure out what is the “Hidden Curriculum,” in

---
## Page 212
the scenario, what is wrong with the action being portrayed, or what
has been misinterpreted. Ask groups to think about what might be the
right or correct thing to do, and how others might assist or help the
person who does not understand the “Hidden Curriculum” agenda.
Scenario 1: It was a beautiful day when a group of staff and students
left their school campus to spend the day on a river in a state park.
The anticipation about this school trip had been building for days. The
plan was to spend the morning white water rafting and then have a
picnic lunch.
Upon arrival, the group is told by the park personnel that they will
have to kayak rather than use the rafts because the river was too low.
Drew, one of the students, blurts out loudly, “I’m not going, I hate
kayaking!” The staff notices that the other students are being affected
by Drew’s adamant and loud refusal to participate because things are
not going as planned. They are becoming nervous that the kayaking
will be dangerous or unpleasant. One of the staff takes Drew aside
and tries to calm him down but he persists in his loud and public
refusal behaviors and increasingly rigid manner.
Is there a better way for Drew to deal with his concerns? How is the
“Hidden Curriculum” (Drew’s behavior making him the center of
attention; threatening to ruin the trip for others, and his blurting) at work
here? Would separating Drew from the group work? What else might
the leaders do?
Scenario 2: Julie loves to participate in discussions in class. She is
very smart and thinks quickly. She is also very articulate and has
strong opinions. Her classroom teacher uses discussion a lot in class
as a means to keeping the students engaged and thinking. The
teacher tends to ask questions to stimulate thought and keep the
discussion moving. Julie answers every question the teacher asks
very quickly and talks at length on the topic at hand. She also makes
a comment and states her opinion after other students contribute to
the discussion.

---
## Page 213
Julie is oblivious to the fact that her behavior is frustrating the other
students in the class and causing them to stop participating. What are
the “social norms” in this situation? What is a more appropriate way for
Julie to participate in class discussions?
Scenario 3: Jason has significant sensory issues that he does not
understand well and that cause him to want to have something in his
mouth to chew on almost all the time. He has developed the habit of
pulling at his socks until they unravel at the top and then chewing on
the strings he pulls out. As a result of this, Jason always has a piece
of string that he is chewing on and his socks are always unraveled
and hanging down over his shoes. Additionally, Jason leaves a trail of
chewed string behind him wherever he goes or an array of saliva-
drenched string around his chair. Most importantly, there are almost
always bits of chewed string in his beard and on his shirt. Jason is
oblivious to how this makes him look; how it affects other people; or
how others perceive or react to him. Neither subtle nor sarcastic
comments from others have worked in getting him to stop doing this.
What aspects of the “Hidden Curriculum” are in play here? What is
Jason not understanding? How might a teacher, mentor, or social skills
advisor help Jason?
Scenario 4: Kate often tells the adults she confides in that she is
lonely and wants to have more friends. She can identify a number of
students in her school that she likes and shares some common
interests with such as video gaming, animation, drawing, and cooking.
Kate spends all of her out-of-school time in her bedroom on her
computer or playing video games. She has a notebook of sketches
that she has drawn but refuses to ever let anyone see them. She
cooks and bakes but will often allow her “creations” to spoil or
become stale rather than share them with others.
When Kate is in school she tries to make friends by telling people that
they “should” spend more time with her. She will try to have
conversations with others that revolve around her feelings of
loneliness and her demands that others should be willing to be her

---
## Page 214
friend. She doesn’t understand why people don’t seem to like her and
this causes her to become depressed and defensive.
What is Kate not understanding? What might she do to find and keep
some friends? How might her teacher or social skills instructor help
her?
Scenario 5: Paul is extremely intelligent but struggles with time
management and getting his school work done. He is currently
working on his senior research project and has a great topic in mind
about which he knows a great deal. He has read numerous books on
the subject and has stored an incredible amount of knowledge about it
in his head. He can talk for a long time about his topic in both an
articulate and insightful way. But, he is having trouble getting his
knowledge down on paper.
Whenever a deadline is approaching, he assures his parents and
professors that he is doing well and will have the required piece of his
paper in by the required date. People tend to believe him because he
can speak about his topic so eloquently and intelligently. However,
when the due date arrives, he does not have anything to hand in. He
then makes up an excuse as to why and this is almost always
something quite outlandish. After a while, his parents and teachers
stop believing him and start to pressure him to get the paper written.
This causes Paul to grow angry and defensive. Unfortunately, his
behaviors are causing him to fail in school.
What is going on with Paul? What is he doing that is causing him to
fail? What might he do differently?
Discuss: Talk about the fact that all these scenarios have a “Hidden
Curriculum.” And talk about why learning to look for the “Hidden
Curriculum” in life is important and how mastering these largely hidden
skills will help each person develop an awareness of these unwritten
rules. Talk about the importance of watching, observing, and doing
what peers and others who are not on the ASD/LD Spectrum are doing.
Discuss: Why do we behave differently at school, church,
synagogues, mosques, funerals, and in public places than we do at

---
## Page 215
home, or when goofing around with friends? Why do words like “fat,
shut-up, and take your time,” have double meanings and when used in
an incorrect context – can have disastrous results?
Ask the young men and women you are working with if they can
pinpoint specific examples in their own lives (now that they are aware of
the “Hidden Curriculum”) where knowing about these unwritten social
rules would have helped them out of a tricky situation.
Some Basic “Hidden Curriculum” Rules
Treat all authority figures with respect (e.g., teachers, school
principals, police, and firefighters). For instance: “You would not
address a police officer like you would your brother!”
Not all people you are unfamiliar with are strangers you can’t
trust. You may not know your bus driver or your police officer,
but these are people who help you.
What may be acceptable at your house may not be acceptable
at a friend’s house. For example, although it is acceptable to put
your feet up on the table at your home, your friend’s mom may
be upset if you do that in their home. In this case, do what your
friends do. And also abide by requests such as “At our house we
…” or “At our house we don’t … ”
People do not always want to know the honest truth when they
ask you a question. Your best friend does not want to hear that
she looks fat in a new dress she just bought for the high school
dance.
Teachers do not all have the same rules. One teacher may allow
gum in the classroom, while the other gives out fines for chewing

---
## Page 216
gum. Ask: what the rules are and even go so far as to suggest
the rules be posted, explaining that you sometimes forget what
class has which rules.
Teachers have certain assumed expectations for their students.
Each student is expected to greet the teachers, sit down when
the bell rings, and listen quietly to announcements.
When a teacher gives you a warning, it means that she wants
the behavior to stop and that most likely there will be a
consequence if the behavior occurs again.
It is absolutely (100%) impolite to interrupt someone when he
or she is talking, unless it is an emergency.
Acceptable slang that may be used with your peers (e.g., dawg,
phat, yo, whuzup?) may not be acceptable when interacting
with adults.
When the teacher is scolding another student, it is not the best
time to ask the teacher a question.
When a teacher tells another student to stop talking, it is not an
appropriate time for you to start talking to your neighbor.
People are not always supposed to say what they are thinking.
(Adapted from Smith Myles et al. 2004)
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
HIDDEN CURRICULUM SOCIAL SKILLS
There is no one comprehensive list of all “Hidden Curriculum” areas
that relate to skills for life. The previous page is a brief listing (Smith
Myles et al. 2004),in handout form of things that will help adolescents

---
## Page 217
and young adults on the ASD or LD Spectrum. Discuss each item on
this list with students and then role play some of the scenarios such as
being pulled over by the police for speeding; being invited to a friend’s
house and having that friend explain about the new white wall to wall
carpeting; or picking up a friend who obviously just got an unflattering
haircut, etc. Review “blurting” and reinforce the rules about not always
saying what you are thinking.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: To augment this section and other chapters of
Autism and Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit) that address the “Hidden Curriculum” in various contexts, Dr.
Brenda Smith Myles, whose work is cited above, has contributed:
“The Hidden Curriculum in College” that she co-authored with Jill
Hudson, MS from the Ohio Center for Autism and Low Incidence in
Columbus, Ohio and Dr. Hyo Jung Lee from Dongguk University in
Seoul, South Korea to Chapter 12 – The Experts Speak! Dr. Brenda
Smith Myles is one of the leading experts and foremost researchers in
the subject area of the “Hidden Curriculum.” Please turn to Chapter 12
to read more about her work in this arena.
“EXECUTIVE FUNCTION” ABILITY
ASD/LD adolescents and young adults often lack an internal filing
system to help them cope with not only the day to day, but life in
general. You can help these young people help their brains to create
systems, strategies, and develop techniques that will give each
individual the “Executive Function” ability he or she needs to succeed in
life, school, work, and independent living.
The young people you have been working with are learning a great deal
as you impart the Skills for Life chapter content. The next logical step in
this teaching process is learning about and then being able to instill
