“Executive Function” abilities in each individual. “Executive Function”
describes a set of cognitive abilities that control and regulate behavior.
“Executive Function” ability is necessary for goal-directed behavior and
includes being able to initiate and stop actions, to monitor and change

---
## Page 218
behaviors as needed, and to plan future behaviors when faced with
novel tasks and situations. “Executive Function” allows adolescents
and young adults to anticipate outcomes and adapt to changing
situations.
“My filing system is all messed up!”
From your brain’s point of view, life is a staggering blast of
completely disorganized stuff. Impressions, feelings, images,
smells, etc., are being fire-hosed into your brain through your
eyes, ears, nose, and skin every waking hour. It’s confusing,
never-ending, and hopelessly chaotic. At least it would be, if
your brain didn’t create an internal filing system to organize it.
The ability to conceptualize and think abstractly is where most ASD or
LD students need extra instruction, practice, and training. Nearly half of
CIP’s (College Internship Program’s) students who have attended
college before enrolling in our program have had to leave post-
secondary education due to difficulty in this key area. Therefore,
“Executive Function” strategies, techniques, and materials will be given
throughout the Teaching Toolkit in context to each competency skill
chapter, starting with this one: Skills for Life.
The basic “Executive Function” processes that adolescents and young
adults on the Spectrum need to develop center around:
•
Planning.
•
Organizing.
•
Prioritizing.
•
Shifting (cognitive flexibility).
•
Memorizing.

---
## Page 219
•
Checking (facts, feelings, emotions, ideas, school work,
homework, and work-related things).
The “Executive Function” techniques in this chapter and within these
parameters will look at:
•
Time management, prioritization, and follow through (this is
critical with academics and studies).
•
Socialization (with peers, co-workers, and others).
•
Organization (of all personal spaces, work, and school areas).
•
Shifting strategies to acquire flexibility.
•
Planning and organizing information and ideas.
•
Being able to focus on relevant themes rather than irrelevant
details.
•
Imitating and sustaining activities.
•
Self-monitoring and regulating behavior.
Ami Klin from the Yale University School of Medicine’s Child Study
Center was having a discussion with some people shortly after a
presentation he gave at the Asperger’s Association of New England
Conference and he said, “Reading the encyclopedia about how to
drive in New York City is not the same as doing it.” (Klin 2010)
While many students know the rudiments of how to study, how to take
quizzes, and how to write papers, they have been propped-up in a very
structured environment (usually with a lot of parental input and help)

---
## Page 220
and without the distractions and freedom that a normal post high
school, college, or independent living situation presents. The little
things that are often done to help students by parents, teachers,
clinicians, professionals, and others are often the glue that holds these
young adults together: providing a quiet study space, giving a lot of
one-on-one support, time keeping, and being regimented taskmasters.
Students preparing for college, careers, vocational training, or
independent living need “Executive Function” ability in order to create a
structure for themselves that works in an environment full of peers who
may be loud, distracting, and very active. Ideally this process needs to
be taught in high school or during a first year college course. Young
adults on the Spectrum who have not learned or are not in the process
of learning “Executive Function” abilities will more than likely fail in
school and at trying to live independently.
You know from working with adolescents and young adults on the
ASD/LD Spectrum that they experience inhibition, poor planning, time
perception limitations, and a lack of internal ordering. “Executive
Function” ability can change this by allowing students to organize and
plan, to set and achieve goals, to understand complex and abstract
concepts, and to prioritize and manage time.
Learning to use logic and create strategies and plans to solve
problems, while processing information and controlling behaviors, leads
to accomplishing a task. This is not always easy for teenagers and
young adults who have many competing interests. However, like most
things in life it creates another avenue for growth.
“Executive Function” abilities need to be part of each adolescent’s or
young adult’s life. These skills become front and center in each
person’s academics, as well as how he or she manages money, health
and fitness, the workplace, and living independently. Someone on the
ASD/LD Spectrum cannot operate a household, a life, or a relationship
without learning these basic tenets. As the young men and women you
work with acquire the ability to create personal strategies and then use
these strategies with peers and others, they acquire “Executive
Function” ability.

---
## Page 221
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Being on the ASD/LD Spectrum myself, I know
from my own experience the problems that develop because of poor
planning or the inability to be on time and meet deadlines. In my case,
I had a great deal of difficulty getting started or initiating a task that I
did not want to do and I would do anything to avoid it. I would clean
my room or help others clean theirs, until the last critical minute when I
had to do my project, assignment, or task and then I would do it all at
once, and right before it was due. I was messy and disorganized in
the way I kept my class notes and important papers. I had no system
to take care of this. I also spent a lot of time trying to figure out where I
left off on a project. I purposely ignored information that did not
interest me and then when I needed that information, I did not have it.
I had to learn visually, physically, and mentally to “go” from this to this.
We know that adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum
have strong intellectual abilities and are usually very fluent in language
usage. These young people have great memories and have the ability
to focus intently (especially if they are pursuing their “special
interests.”) “Executive Function” helps them discover that they can
actualize their dreams by learning and using skills to carry them out.
Without this knowledge and proper support, they may feel that they are
drowning in a million different sub-tasks and cannot find their way to the
surface.
“Executive Function” oversees many of the day-to-day situations that
an adolescent or young adult will experience. In “neurotypicals” this
allows a person to oversee the plans and activities of a normal day
such as organizing thoughts, managing time efficiently, and making
decisions. These in turn allow the “neurotypical” person to be in charge
of organization, integration, and production. This has to be taught to
adolescents and young adults on the Spectrum. Here are the basic
interventions for building “Executive Function” ability in connection with
this Skills for Life chapter.
One of the first “Executive Function” abilities students will need to
acquire is mastering the concept of time.

---
## Page 222
MASTERING AND MANAGING THE CONCEPT
OF TIME
To get a good sense of time passing and a sense of time in general,
have students use a clock that has a red block showing time passing.
This will help your students be aware of time in different contexts. To
help them organize their days and routines use:
•
Cell phone reminders.
•
Day planners.
•
Computer email reminders.
•
Watches with alarms.
•
Visual schedules.
•
Checklists that prioritize tasks.
•
Dual alarm clocks – one nearby and one that you have to walk to
across the room.
The following handouts on pages 91–93 can be enlarged and
photocopied for your students or to hang up in the classroom.
Managing Space and Material
•
Create a dedicated work space.
•
Organize work space.
•
Color coded bins.
•
Sections in backpack.
•
Minimize clutter.
•
Schedule cleaning and organizing at least once per week.

---
## Page 223
•
Employ master notebook system.
•
Accordion file or 3-ring file with sections.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Managing Time
•
Segment large tasks into chunks.
•
Use calendars/day planners to keep of long term
assignments, due dates, and deadlines.
•
Reminders on computers and smart phones.
•
Organize – daily, weekly, monthly to-do lists.
•
Time estimation worksheet with estimated vs. actual time
tasks table.
•
Use prompts to guestimate the approximate time and
develop a better internal system for keeping time.
Once the concept of time is mastered and understood, you can
transition ASD/LD adolescents and young adults into managing
personal and shared spaces and materials.
TEACHING INSIGHT: Mary Lawler, a former Program Director of
CIP’s Amherst Center, came up with the following activity to help her
students master the concept of space and then organizing it. She then
took these concepts one step further by asking students to think about
applying this same skill set to their lives. This is a really good example
of integrating “Executive Function” into the skills for life arena.

---
## Page 224
“What Can You Fit in Your Suitcase?”
Students from CIP Amherst engaged in an activity that kicked off
the “Executive Function” theme for the week: balancing priorities
and demands. The task – pack the suitcase with all the items on
the table before them.
With thoughtfulness and diligence to the task, the students gave
it the “old college try” and made efforts to fit everything in. The
problem – there simply was just not enough room no matter how
creative they could be (including efforts to close the suitcase by
sitting on it).
In working through the problem the students realized “on their
own” that everything was not going to fit and they would need to
prioritize what should be included. From here we had a
thoughtful conversation on the importance of striving for balance
in our lives and the importance of making decisions about what
was important, urgent, or just a time-filler.
(Based on Covey 2011)
TO-DO LISTS
“To-do” lists are a great way to organize what needs to be done,
when it needs to be done, and can branch out and reinforce social
skills and help students schedule time to study, eat a healthy meal,
meet up with friends, or to remember things like making an important
phone call or appointment and then using the list to remember to go to
the appointment or make that call.

---
## Page 225
HABITS!
Tell the young men and women your work with that routine habits work
by:
•
Helping each person to remember the little things consistently.
•
Habits help individuals to do the things that seem difficult to do,
that get procrastinated about, or that are mundane/boring, yet
important. Give examples of how the decision gets removed
from the process by forming a habit. To explain this, use this
example: “I should do my homework now … but I don’t want to …
but I should … but I’m tired … but I really need a break …”
•
Ask for a show of hands how many people have engaged in this
kind of thinking in the past 48 hours. Use these results to
generate ideas on how each person might learn to do things
habitually rather than at the last minute or when under great
pressure or stress.
•
Discuss how rouine habits increase efficiency, productivity, and
save time.
•
Talk about how incorporating tasks and things they must do as
a habit increases time awareness and management of time.
Illustrate how time becomes more predictable and how the
ability to estimate how long tasks take improves outcomes and
results.
•
Discuss how routines become habits and that once this
happens tasks take less energy and effort, thus conserving each
person’s valuable time for other life areas. Explain that this might
be more down time, recreation time, socialization time, and
leisure time; all very important for health and quality of life.
Tell students that building habits is easy! Involve each person you work
with so that each individual can learn how to build habits and will be
more invested and motivated to do so if you solicit his or her input in
areas he or she needs to work on. No one has better knowledge about
what needs to become a habit than each individual and the educators,

---
## Page 226
clinicians, mentors, and professionals who are assiting them. Together
you can set goals to help each person form life-long habits that will lead
to success in all life skills areas including school, work, and
independent living. And be sure to tell each one that “It’s better to have
an imperfect routine (habit) that is applied and used than one that is
ignored.”
Plan > Execute > Repair
PLAN:
•
What am I trying to accomplish?
•
What are the necessary steps?
•
What is the sequence of the steps?
EXECUTE:
•
How do I start? When do I start?
•
How will I persevere with the plan?
•
What could go wrong?
REPAIR:
•
Is the plan working?
•
How will I know when I am done?
•
What would I do differently the next time?

---
## Page 227
•
What would I do the same next time?
TEACHING INSIGHT: Section II ends with looking at the model
above: Plan-Execute-Repair. This series of questions will help
students compartmentalize, break down, and accomplish tasks in a
logical, sequencial order. Additional “Executive Function” techniques,
materials, and ideas will be featured in the remaining “skills” chapters
of this book.
TEACHING INSIGHT: To transition Sections I and II to Section III:
Emotional Coping Skills, Holly Brouker, the Wellness Coordinator at
CIP’s Berkshire Center, combines the acquisition of social skills, self-
evaluation, and wellness into what she calls Swellness. Her adapted
story makes a nice bridge between the sections of this chapter. Holly’s
story illustrates many of the key concepts in Section I and II and
portrays the “wellness and emotional coping aspect” that will emerge
in Section III. You’ll see that the young people who participated in
Swellness worked on their social skills (Section l) and key cognitive
skills – such as decision-making, critical thinking, and self-evaluation
(Section II) – while employing a variety of emotional coping skills and
strategies as well.
“Summer ‘Swellness’ at CIP’s Berkshire Center”
“Swellness” is a term that we, at CIP, use to describe activities
that combine working on both social and cognitive skills with
wellness. These three areas go together nicely since group
wellness activities require the use of good social skills and a
variety of cognitive skills in order for the wellness activity to be
successful.

---
## Page 228
The first group of students I worked with using the “Swellness”
idea chose to combine “walking and culture” so we visited three
different historical sites. We visited a historic house and then
walked to an Indian Burial Ground and finally ended up
wandering through a nearby cemetery searching for Norman
Rockwell’s grave. A little help from a “local cemetery visitor” put
us on the right path and it was a special treat for one of our
students who is presently “interning” at the Norman Rockwell
Museum. He was able to tell the group all about Norman
Rockwell, his job at the museum, and how invested he was “in
learning as much as possible” about Mr. Rockwell. I watched his
confidence soar as he talked to his classmates. This
spontaneous incident provided the student speaking with an
opportunity to express his thoughts and feelings appropriately
while providing the others with the opportunity to “listen”
attentively and respectfully to a peer.
Another group I was working with that week toured and explored
a historical mansion. The students had a very enthusiastic tour
guide throughout the tour of the well preserved rooms of the
mansion and they learned all about the family who lived there
during the “Gilded Age.” The students’ questions were
appropriate and instructively answered by the tour guide.
Although it was early in the season, we were amazed at the vast
beauty of the mansion’s gardens. The students were intrigued
by the stories and things they saw that day as they recorded
their experiences in their journals. Having the students “reflect”
on this experience by way of journaling was an important part of
the “Swellness” activity as it gave me insight into what each
person thought and learned that day. More importantly, it gave
the students an opportunity to assess themselves regarding
their behavior and social interactions during the activity.
For the next “Swellness” adventure that week a group of 12
students who were part of CIP’s summer program participated
in “large group” activities with CIP’s year-round (and slightly

---
## Page 229
older) students. We played outdoor games such as kickball,
beach ball, volley ball, went on hikes, and organized an in-town
scavenger hunt. CIP’s year-round students were helpful to the
visiting high school students and made them feel comfortable.
They went out of their way to keep them motivated throughout
the longer sessions and activities. CIP’s year-round students
used all they had been learning to break down the barriers of
anxiety the high school students were experiencing, which
encouraged them to mingle and have fun “together.” These
group activities provided excellent opportunities for all involved
to practice important life skills that included numerous social
interactions, teamwork, and anxiety management (a much-
needed emotional coping skill for many of our students).
Perhaps best of all, the more experienced CIP students
demonstrated their ability to read the body language of the
younger students by monitoring and assessing the situation and
providing encouragement and support (giving to others).
We got in some unexpected potential “career counseling” with
another “Swellness” group that visited a local summer camp. As
students learned all about the operations of the camp and what
it takes to become a camp counselor, a number of the students
expressed interest in becoming a counselor and inquired about
the application process. The tour leaders stressed that the
camp’s focus on sports (this camp has water sports, archery,
tennis, roller hockey, soccer, and baseball), as well as art,
music, and wood shop, required that the counselors have the
ability to work as part of a team. They also stressed that camp
counselors model good sportsmanship at all times while
displaying appropriate leadership.
The last group of students to participate in the “Swellness”
activities of the summer went to the Ashuwillticook Trail (a
rail/trail) in Lanesborough, Massachusetts to bike along the
former rail road line. Many students brought their own bikes and
the others rented them. One of the students – Sam – was able to

---
## Page 230
successfully ride a bike for the first time in his life (though he had
tried many times as a child), and stayed behind to practice
stopping, starting, and keeping his balance in the parking lot. As
the students rode along the trail enjoying the sights around them,
I thought about all that this simple activity offered them: the
chance to be part of a group, have fun, and in the case of Sam,
master a skill that had eluded him for years. Additionally, Sam
was able to cope with a challenging emotional situation in that
he overcame his anxiety around a previous “failure” and the
“embarrassment” of his peers knowing he couldn’t ride a bike.
This required a great deal of risk-taking (an important cognitive
skill) on Sam’s part.
I have come to realize from just this small sample of “Swellness”
activities that students are provided with multiple opportunities
to learn how to work on essential life skills such as interacting
with the public and each other, developing self-assessment
skills, reading nonverbal body cues, being flexible, thinking and
responding socially on their feet, coping with difficult emotions,
and so many more.
Holly Brouker, Wellness Coordinator, CIP Berkshire
Section III: Emotional Coping Skills
Social abilities, wellness, self-evaluation, and monitoring behaviors and
emotions are the areas that give adolescents and young adults the
emotional coping skills they need to function in and be part of life.
Focusing on emotional coping skills is the focus of Section lll – the final
segment of the Skills for Life chapter.
“Social Thinking,” a term coined by Michelle Garcia Winner, includes
the concepts and perspective taking techniques that allow the
adolescents and young adults you work with daily to focus on presence

---
## Page 231
of mind. “Social Thinkers” consider the points of view of others.
“Perspective Takers” consider the perspectives of others as a means
of being able to determine the meanings behind messages
communicated by others sintuitively. “Social Thinking” is second nature
to a “neurotypical” person. However, to adolescents and young adults
on the ASD or LD Spectrum who are often hyper-vigilant, this concept
is difficult.
Therefore, as educators, clinicians, and professionals working with
these young men and women, you will have to teach “Social Thinking”
(individually or in small groups) and then reinforce this teaching through
visuals and illustrative examples, role playing, group outings (such as
Holly Brouker’s “Swellness” program featured at the end of Section ll),
and social activities. “Social Thinking” needs to be taught and
reinforced throughout the entire day and week in all learning
environments and settings by all staff working with these young men
and women.
Allowing adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum to
come to terms and reconcile their own beliefs in order to accept what
others are telling them to do can be frustrating, hard, and rankling.
Rigidity and unwillingness to compromise their own sense of higher
morals and ethics in order to come to terms with what they need to do
to reach their goals is hard.
“Social Thinkers” think about the points of view, emotions, thoughts,
beliefs, prior knowledge, and intentions of others. For most people
being able to determine the meanings behind the messages
communicated by others is intuitive. “Social Thinking” occurs
everywhere, while talking, during walking, and even when reading a
book. “Social Thinking” is second nature to most people.
In order to impart this concept to your students using a variety of
strategies, you need to look at one key and foremost fact. ASD and LD
adolescents and young adults are (in just about all cases) operating
with their best efforts and intentions, and trying as hard as they can to
adapt their behaviors under difficult conditions.

---
## Page 232
You know from everything that this book has stated so far, that these
young men and women have slow processing speeds and lack social
competencies. In order to help each individual you work with acquire
this most essential of all life skills, it is necessary to view the world
through each person’s perspective. It’s really important that teachers,
clinicians, 
and 
mentors 
make 
careful 
observations 
and 
not
assumptions, particularly when judging ability levels and intentionality.
By making observations you’ll soon discover that oftentimes there is
either a lack of social understanding and/or interest in problem solving
on the part of the student; or conversely, excess interests in other areas
that conflict with problem resolution. Pragmatics (language for social
communication purposes) may also be an issue. Because of the
adolescent’s or young adult’s high level of competence and ability in
other areas, it’s easy for educators, clinicians, and professionals to
attribute these young men and women to a higher level of
communicative competence than actually exists. One of the common
errors is mistaking problems in communication/social understanding
for problems in behavior.
For instance, there can be a variety of issues that impact the student
such as:
•
Sensory 
issues 
characterized 
by 
oversensitivity 
or
undersensitivity, producing avoidance behaviors.
•
“Theory of Mind” issues which interfere with a student’s ability
to size up a situation. (This is the ability to learn to track what
others are thinking and feeling by attempting to take their
perspective. It involves learning about social inference, whole
body listening, and using memory to facilitate friendships.)
•
“Executive Function” issues, which create difficulties with:
problem solving, mental planning, organizational skills, self-
regulation, and application of skills.
•
Slow or deficient processing skills that can lead to problems
with retrieval and use of information.

---
## Page 233
It’s easy to see the reactions of students with learning differences as
willful or volitional, and then judge their motives. A student’s significant
and pervasive problems with planning, organization, and social
judgment should not be confused for laziness or non-compliance.
In order to assist those who are imparting emotional coping skills in
this skills for life competency section, here’s a set of guidelines to follow
before delving into or implementing the strategies outlined.
•
Analyze a problem situation with the adolescent or young adult.
•
Find the critical variables which are affecting the situation.
•
Formulate a proactive educational or emotional well-being plan
to assist the individual in understanding and taking action to
alleviate the situation.
THE CORE PRINCIPLES OF EMOTIONAL
WELL-BEING
Flexibility is learning to be open to new ideas regarding the ways of
understanding, and then dealing with real-life situations. Flexibility
allows the ASD or LD adolescent or young adult to consider another
person’s point of view. By learning to interpret what others are thinking
and feeling, and then allowing for differences between what these
(often rigid) young men and women think and what others think and
feel, flexibility is learned. At CIP, we use techniques to help young
adults learn flexibility and so they can look at a situation from another
person’s perspective. We use whole body listening and social
inference to assist them in forming friendships. In my first book, Made
for Good Purpose, I stated that “Many individuals on the Spectrum find
that becoming friends with somebody on the phone is easier than
building a face-to-face relationship because there are fewer social cues
to deal with,” and I advise this as a way to begin to socialize. Young
people today are so savvy with texting and managing their lives on
social-networking sites that this opens up more opportunities for
making friends and finding others who share similar interests than

---
## Page 234
would have been possible even a mere five years ago. They test ideas,
try flexibility, and slowly learn that a relationship between two people
has to be between two people and cannot be one-sided.
Reciprocity means learning to give and take in conversations and
interactions when sharing with others is another vital skill that young
people on the ASD/LD Spectrum can successfully obtain. Have
students practice these in small group sessions or one-on-one with
social or peer mentors. Working as part of a team and being involved
with others during community service learning projects, social activities,
in classes, and during recreational activities gives the young people you
work with opportunities for practicing reciprocity while being fully
supported by staff, peers, and/or social mentors who help them when
this starts getting tricky or uncomfortable. It is important that students
are continuously going into the community to practice in real life their
“Social Thinking” competencies. Class projects out in the community
will make this happen.
Problem solving is the ability to analyze, process, and understand
social situations and the rules that apply in each circumstance, and is
taught using the same methodologies key to the beginning of this
chapter in Section 1 and continuing through Section II. Classroom
work, working with social or peer mentors, and implementation through
initiation or self-motivation are good strategies for problem solving. To
aid your students on a daily basis, use this great acronym that each
person can call upon to analyze social areas where they are not sure
how they should go forward or process. It is called SODA and
encourages students to Stop, Observe, Deliberate (using the “Donkey
Rule)” and then to take Action. This acronym does not replace working
with teachers, social mentors, advisors, therapists, and staff. But … it
does provide a stop-gap method until further advice or input from
others can be obtained.

---
## Page 235
Takeaway
“Staff can work together with students to identify the source of
discomfort in social situations, and together, they can develop
techniques to improve a student’s comfort level. Once students
learn techniques that work for them, (deep breathing, practicing
social skills over and over again, etc.), they will be able to
experience things that they could not before. These strategies
are important for the student to learn so they can make
adjustments independently and live a life not limited by their
social inadequacies.”
Jan Petrides, CIP Berkshire
SODA
The SODA acronym is used by students to help them with decision-
making and emotional regulation. When the young people you teach,
work with, and mentor are stumped, they need to stop and drink a
SODA! This allows them to take a moment to look at what is happening
around them, and then observe the actions of others and themselves.
Students can then deliberate about what they might do in the present
situation to help themselves. It also allows time (while sipping the soda)
to think about what they did in past situations. If possible and if the
adolescent or young adult has the time (especially if it is an important
decision), they can poll three to five of their peers, friends, mentors, or
people they feel use good judgment for input and then take the action
these people deem appropriate. SODA allows adolescents and young
adults to have an improved chance for taking the best possible action in
tricky situations.

---
## Page 236
You can also teach your students to HALT! Rectifying any of the issues
below should help individuals start to feel better and then be able to
assess situations more objectively.
HALT
A large majority of ASD/LD adolescents and young adults you work
with may be dealing with all of the above or with anxiety, panic attacks,
and depression. Teaching calming exercises, encouraging students to
participate in relaxation programs such as meditation and yoga,
engaging in exercise, and using the free app “How2Cope” and the
www.stopbreathethink.org website bring the ASD/LD adolescent or
young adult to the point where the other strategies and coping skills this
section illustrates will be more beneficial.
H – Are you HUNGRY?
A – Are you ANGRY?

---
## Page 237
L – Are you LONELY?
T – Are you TIRED?
ADDRESSING HEALTH AND WELLNESS
The experts tell us and today’s research in this arena confirms that
young adults with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and Learning
Differences (LD) perform at their best when there is a sustained level of
wellness in their lives.
Teaching Insight: Having each student adopt a “meditation ritual or
calming routine,” and a “Personal Fitness Plan” that provides balance
through proper nutrition, exercise, stress-reduction practices, sensory
diet, and sleep regimens must be a priority for each individual who is
on the ASD/LD Spectrum. This plan can then be augmented by yoga,
group workouts, walking/hiking clubs, and other well-being practices
and techniques. If the schools, universities, and transitional support
settings you work at are amiable, students with “Personal Fitness

---
## Page 238
Plans” might meet weekly with a wellness coordinator or peer
mentors, in groups to set fitness and well-being goals.
In dealing with my own issues I have developed fitness, eating, and
stress-relieving routines for flying (which I do a lot of) and for the day-
to-day stress that occurs when long meetings drain my attention span
or aggravate my sensory and/or Asperger’s symptoms. I swim each
morning at 6 AM as this time works for me and I do a lot of physical
activity on the farm that I own. I also engage in self-care by having
massages, eating organically, and practicing yoga and meditation. I
encourage each of you to pass on your own coping strategies as you
work one on one with students so that they know that “at times,” we all
feel on edge, out of sorts, or under pressure from our wide-ranging
everyday emotions.
MAKE IT REAL!
Encourage your schools, colleges, and universities embrace physical
well-being and emulate CIP’s “Make it Real” program for students on
the ASD/LD Spectrum. The “Make it Real” strategy is tangible, has
positive outcomes, and allows young people to succeed and achieve
emotional coping skills through healthy living, making good nutritional
choices, exercising, and creating a climate of overall well-being. Here
is a real-time example of what happens when schools working with
those on the ASD or LD Spectrum embrace the “Make it Real” motto
as a school-wide effort and when working with individuals.

---
## Page 239
A “Make it Real” Health and Wellness
Spotlight
CIP Brevard: The Firecracker 5K
Lance Haskins, CIP’s Brevard Wellness Coordinator, says: “This year
we had our first official CIP Team for Melbourne, Florida’s Firecracker
5K Race, a 4th of July race. This race started with the ringing of a
replica of America’s Liberty Bell and all proceeds from the race
benefited the ‘Honor America’ foundation.
“Although not the fastest, the CIP team did finish and we did have fun.
This event also served as a catalyst for some of our students who
trained for this race by participating throughout the year in some of the
local 5K, 3K, and 2 mile races to prepare for this race. It was a great
motivator for continuing individual fitness programs and embracing
wellness.”
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Once physical well-being has been addressed
students can employ additional emotional coping skills, primarily
learning how to control their emotions through proper nutrition and
eating in a more healthful way. And … seeing is also believing! At
CIP, we post personal student milestones and goals reached, events
young people participated in, and other instances where our students
“Make it Real,” on our blog, in our newsletters, and on bulletin boards.
These opportunities are celebrated and act as inspiration to others.
The student handouts on pages 101–102 gives your students some
basics: ideas and strategies that will help each person cope with a
typical school day and lessen stress, emotional overload, and fatigue
before and after the day as well.
THINK POSITIVE!
PERSONAL INSIGHT: In 2006, I was walking down the street in
Berkeley, California, frustrated and somewhat down. Trying to start a

---
## Page 240
new center, find a building, negotiate a lease, interview staff, order
phone service, and the myriad of other tasks was overwhelming me.
I was getting really down and saying negative things to myself and
finally I said to myself “Michael, you need to Think Positive.” At that
same moment my eyes turned toward the window sill of a store and
perched on it was a stone with “Think Positive” written on it.
I picked up the stone and felt a little guilty taking it, and put it in my
pocket. I felt the stone every time I put my hand in my pocket and at
night put it on the night stand in my hotel. The stone got me through a
“rough time” as I was struggling to accomplish my complicated work
and it was a physical presence and reminder to keep my focus
positive.
I found similar stones near the bay the next day and got a marker and
made “think positive” stones. I placed a stone at the same spot that I
picked up the first stone and proceeded to leave them in many other
places (including bathrooms, on tables, at restaurants, etc.).
I now “repurpose” many found rocks, bringing “Think Positive” stones
to conferences and speaking engagements all over the United States
and internationally. People often tell me that they enjoy keeping “their
stone” on their desk, in their purse, or in their pocket.
Over the years I have given away many “Think Positive” stones. I
recently attended a conference and one of the participants pulled her
“Think Positive” stone out of her purse and asked me if I remembered
the day I gave it to her. She went on to say that it’s been in her purse
for the past three years!
The “Think Positive” stone I had in my pocket that day became (and
still is) my talisman that helps me stay balanced and on track.
After dragging bags of stones to conference presentations all over the
world, my son Dan created stickers, some cards, and even T-shirts
that take the place of my inscribed bags of stones. This reincarnation
of my original stone has now become a part of CIP’s culture. It’s a
prominent logo on all our marketing materials and has become a
“mantra” that staff and students have embraced as they hand out

---
## Page 241
stickers, cards, and T-shirts imprinted with this slogan. You never
know where, an idea, a thought, or in my case, a simple coping
mechanism may lead.
AND … BE HAPPY!
Everything on the “Feeling Fit, Groomed, and Ready to Go” list
(pp.101–2) should contribute to this. Encourage students to laugh often.
Be passionate and courageous. Life is very much a trial-and-error
process. Through this process, find out what is most important, then go
out and do it. Oh, and never, ever take anything too seriously. Life is
way too short for that!
“10 Tips to Help you Stay Focused, as Well as Socially and Emotionally
Present!” created by Sharona Sommer, a former CIP Student Advisor
(who contributed the story “Janie and Her Parents” to Chapter 1) will
also help your students stay focused, stay calm, cope emotionally, and
strive for happiness.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: For adolescents and young adults on the
ASD/LD Spectrum, “skills for life” are the abilities that enable each
individual to effectively deal with the challenges of their lives in a
manner that is appropriate for their age and experience. “Skills for life”
facilitate healthy, positive, and productive personal development and
enable each adolescent or young adult to make meaningful
contributions to society. These “intrapersonal” and “interpersonal
abilities” are interrelated, and collectively they provide a basis for
identifying core skills. These skills can then be applied to the self or to
others. Central to the “skills for life” framework is the need for
adolescents and young adults to demonstrate “cognitive maturity” by
thinking for themselves and taking responsibility for their actions and
their social and emotional development.
I hope this chapter is referred to and used often as you prepare the
young men and women you work with the foundation that will allow

---
## Page 242
each individual to successfully master the next three “skill” chapters:
Skills for School, Work, and Independent Living.
Feeling Fit, Groomed, and Ready to Go!
1. Eat breakfast:
When we skip our first meal of the day, our body has no fuel
to draw on, including energy to get our metabolism started.
We also tend to get hungry later in the evening which means
snacking right before bed, a cardinal sin in the eyes of
nutrition experts. If you are taking medications, and take
them on an empty stomach, this can make you feel unwell
and the medications will not work as they are supposed to,
so breakfast is especially important if you are taking any kind
of medications.
To ensure the day begins on a good note, stock the fridge
with breakfast foods that are easy to prepare like whole-
grain toast and peanut butter, yogurt, fruit, hardboiled eggs,
oatmeal, and low-sugar granola. If cold cereal is your
breakfast of choice, try adding fresh berries to the mix.
2. Engage in low- to moderate-intensity physical activity
every day!
This is much simpler than it sounds. Walk to a friend’s
house. Try biking into town, to an event, to your school, or
out for a ride in the countryside. Take a ten-minute stretch
break while studying. If your roommate is not home – put on
some music and dance in the living room. Is your roommate
home? Invite him or her to join you. Find a way to get the
body moving at least 30 minutes a day.
3. Speak up and let go!

---
## Page 243
A tremendous amount of energy goes into keeping our
feelings hidden and unexpressed. It is also an impossible
task as our feelings will eventually rise to the surface, the
resulting communication often unclear and resentful.
If we are clearly and kindly expressing ourselves when
something bothers us, we don’t have to hold onto it any
more. We feel better, our relationships will likely improve,
and life becomes noticeably easier.
4. Go outside:
The attraction of television, phones, and computer games,
and the intimidation of long cold winters and other
uncomfortable weather, makes staying inside an easy
choice.
Challenge this by going outside and notice how you feel.
Your mood, immune system, and vitamin D receptors will be
very grateful. Give yourself reasons to get up and out, like
offering to walk a friend to school, walking to a cafe or library
to study. And … if you bundle up and dress appropriately in
cold weather this will be more enjoyable too.
5. Eat good-for-you foods:
Our nutritional well-being is not thrown off by eating
unhealthy food. It is thrown off by eating unhealthy food all
the time. Our body responds best to variety – different types
of foods provide different types of nutrients. We need a well-
rounded diet to feel energized and balanced. Start small: use
low-fat cream cheese instead of regular, fat-free milk vs.
whole milk, sugar free sodas instead of calorie laden ones,
or a salad instead of French fries. These small changes will
add up quickly and make you feel better.
If you find yourself cooking meals with similar (or the same)
ingredients, step out of your comfort zone and try out a new

---
## Page 244
recipe. Look in your refrigerator and Google some of the
things in it. A variety of recipes will pop up and give you
many choices for trying something new.
A helpful tip: buy your fruits and vegetables from local
sources. This way, the produce you buy will change with the
seasons. Not only will your food taste great but you will have
a natural change of menu throughout the year.
6. Engage in vigorous exercise three times a week!
Vigorous exercise is defined as getting the heart rate up for
at least 20 minutes. To get motivated to exercise on your
own, have two things prepared: a plan and a partner. Find a
friend with similar fitness goals, choose an activity that you
are both interested in, and help motivate each other to
exercise on a consistent basis.
7. Surround yourself with good people:
Choosing the right friends is one of the best things we can do
for ourselves. Surround yourself with people who inspire you
to be more yourself, and notice if you are around people who
are asking you to be someone you are not. Are your
relationships balanced? Do you receive appreciation and
affection as much as you give it? Let your friends be an
important gift you give to yourself.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
10 Tips to Help you Stay Focused, as Well as
Socially and Emotionally Present!

---
## Page 245
1. Eat Something! To stay focused you need to avoid feeling
those hunger pangs that may distract you from the task at
hand. A healthy snack will give you more energy and help
your mind stay sharp.
2. Turn Off! Shut the door and turn off your phone. Also be
honest with yourself and decide if your MP3 player or
computer will distract you or help your creative juices flow.
Set aside a time block that is electronics free.
3. Have a Mantra! Put up a focus statement in a visible area to
encourage you to stay focused. Examples may be: “Just Do
It!” (Nike) or “It does not matter how slowly you go as long
as you do not stop” (Confucius).
4. Use Timers! Use your computer or watch to set timers for a
predetermined amount of time and concentrate on your task
until the timer goes off. Then enjoy your break and get back
to 
your 
work. 
You 
will 
have 
a 
great 
feeling 
of
accomplishment if you work straight through until that timer
goes off.
5. Stress Ball or Play Dough, Anyone? Keep a stress ball or
a lump of play dough nearby and squeeze it any time your
mind starts to drift. Keeping your fingers busy can help keep
your mind thinking. It also helps when the boredom sets in.
6. Seeing is Believing! Envision what you hope to achieve.
Imagine yourself at your goal and be proud of what a great
job you have accomplished after all the hard work you have
put in. Then get back to work invigorated.
7. Get Inspired! Have someone you can call when your
motivation is waning. Make sure that person is a positive
influence and can get you back to what you need to do.
Think of that person as your personal coach to cheer you on
and get you going.

---
## Page 246
8. What to Do? Create a to-do list every evening before you go
to bed. Cross off your accomplishments throughout the day.
Having a plan for the day keeps you focused and less
overwhelmed than if you just “wing it.”
9. Move It! Take a break from your work and get moving! Take
a walk, do stretching exercises, or do some jumping jacks.
Get your blood flowing your mind will thank you for it.
10.Reward Yourself! Break your projects or tasks into smaller
achievable parts and reward yourself once they have been
completed. Rewards can include renting a favorite movie,
buying yourself a latte, or playing a game with one of your
friends.
Sharona Sommer
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Making It Real
WHAT?
Students will acquire skills for life. These skills are the
abilities that help promote well-being, positive health
outcomes, and productive development in adolescents and
young adults with an ASD (Autism Spectrum Disorder) or LD
(Learning Differences) diagnosis.
Developing skills for life will result in the acquisition of social
and interpersonal skills (including communication, refusal
skills, 
assertiveness, 
and 
empathy), 
cognitive 
skills
(including decision-making, critical thinking, and self-
evaluation), and emotional coping skills (including stress
management).

---
## Page 247
Skills for life help young people on the ASD or LD Spectrum
navigate the challenges of everyday life. Acquiring these
skills through instruction, role playing, role modeling, and
ADL (Activities for Daily Living) allows each individual to deal
effectively with these demands and develop into a healthy,
responsible, and productive adult.
HOW?
Using a structured framework this chapter shows educators,
clinicians, and professionals how to impart skills for life using
a variety of tools, techniques, tasks, and analyses that will
provide measurable outcomes and becomes the fund of
knowledge that each young person needs to develop in
order to work through the next three skill competencies: skills
for school, work, and independent living.
BENEFITS?
Acquiring life skills will empower young people to take steps
to engage in positive social relationships, choose a healthy
lifestyle, and make contributions to society. Skills for life are
important in helping each ASD or LD individual shape his or
her world, not just cope or try to cope with it.
NOTES:

---
## Page 251
“I am indebted to my father for living,
but to my teacher for living well.”
Alexander the Great
In the United States, 35% of students identified with LD
(Learning Differences) drop out of high (secondary) school.
Less than 2% of those who do graduate high (secondary)
school attend a four-year college. 67% students with LD
graduate from high school vs. 74% of students in the general
population. The high school dropout rate for students on the
ASD Spectrum is approximately twice that of general
education students. Only 40% of adolescents or young
adults diagnosed with ASD (Autism Spectrum Disorder) ever
attend college.
Statistics from the National Longitudinal Transition Study
Comprehensive academic support that builds success in school and
moves towards high (secondary) school graduation, a degree, or
career and vocational training is the focus of Chapter 4 – Competency
2: Skills for School. This chapter will assist those working with ASD or
LD adolescents and young adults as they acquire the core elements
needed for successful academic achievement. As your students create
an individual “Person Centered Plan” (PCP) that takes into
consideration each person’s “special interests,” hopes, dreams,
wishes, and potential career paths, each individual develops
appropriate strategies to succeed in school with measurable outcomes
and realistic goals.
This chapter also helps your students achieve additional “Executive
Function” abilities that relate to the school setting. Building on the

---
## Page 252
“Executive Function” aptitudes mastered in Chapter 3, students learn
further techniques and strategies that are needed for effective learning
and skill development. Sections showing students how to manage time,
how to advocate for themselves (disclosure), and how to request
accommodations in the classroom setting are also covered.
Those imparting knowledge to ASD/LD adolescents and young adults
can incorporate programs like “Bookends”™ – a strategy for starting
and ending the school week – and a variety of tips, techniques, and
ideas to help these important skills for school emerge in each
individual. Chapter 4 gives a template to help each student start to build
on his or her academic success by creating a portfolio of school work
and a record of achievements.
This chapter also talks about effective classroom management
techniques and how to recognize the warning signs of bullying and
peer pressure. The latest statistics tell us that 65% of parents report
that their children on the ASD Spectrum have been victimized by peers
in some way within the past year. (National Autism Association)
This chapter ends with a section entitled: “Ready or Not, Here I Come!”
and illustrates various transitioning strategies that can be used as
teachers and support professionals assist each individual as he or she
prepares for college. Especially helpful is a student handout that shows
students the realistic academic differences and expectations that exist
as they transition from high (secondary) school to college.
Building an inclusive, respectful, conducive learning environment (in a
dedicated or mainstreamed classroom) helps to establish workability
with your ASD or LD adolescent and young adult students. Time spent
on implementing and integrating these teaching approaches into each
learning opportunity can produce great results and allow these young
men and women to move forward at a quicker pace.
TEACHING INSIGHT: The beginning of this chapter summarizes the
effective teaching approaches that incorporate “Active Learning” into
any learning environment. These concepts were detailed in Chapters
1 and 2. This refresher at the beginning of Chapter 4 reinforces the

---
## Page 253
“key” and “most essential” concepts that will allow those working with
adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum to adapt,
adopt, tailor, or merge these ideas into the classroom setting. Feel
free to refer back to Chapters 1 and 2 for a more in-depth look at
underlying principles of the methodology overview below.

ever attend college. Nationally 35% of students identified
with Learning Disabilities drop out of high school. Less than
2% of those who do graduate attend a four-year college.”
National Autism Association, United States
“65% of parents report that their children on the ASD
Spectrum have been victimized by peers in some way within
the past year.”
National Autism Association, United States
“Currently 2.4 million students are diagnosed with ASD or LD
and this represents 41% of all students in special education.”

---
## Page 254
SOME BASICS TO INCORPORATE INTO AN
OVERALL TEACHING PLAN
•
Prioritize and zone in on the most important aspects being
taught – first.
•
Use active listening to help students feel that you really
understand their point of view.
•
Teach compromise and negotiation (reinforce successive
approximations).
•
Develop openness and workability.
•
Put emphasis on solving real-world problems.
•
Provide stories, analogies, or practical examples to support
theory.
Remember to:
Ask an individual, group, or class to focus their attention and look at
you.
Give clear instructions.

---
## Page 255
Have each person acknowledge the instruction by replying “ok”, “yes”,
or “I’ll do that.”
Allow enough time to do the task.
Have an individual, group, or class report back to you when the task is
complete.
Give only positive feedback regarding any and all interactions.
Involve Students in Decisions
When a decision is coming up that affects the group, class, or school;
involve students in some way. This might mean discussing issues,
hearing the ideas the class puts forward, and then allowing students to
take action.
Be sure to:
Communicate the big picture. Use visual, oral, and electronic-based
materials to do this.
Update students regularly on how the class is doing, any long-range
plans, about new students and programs, or any new developments,
problems, and opportunities that might be on the horizon. When
students understand the big picture, they make better decisions.
Value student ideas; it’s the key to high morale. Encourage students to
share information, discuss issues, and make decisions with
classmates, groups, or teams.
Help each person see that one-on-one decisions don’t foster a
classroom culture of cooperation, teamwork, or trust.
Use rewards: Recognize and reward desired behaviors with
ceremonies, awards, and putting students in charge of an area or
project. Make recognition very public and personal, so that it is clear to
everyone what behaviors are valued and desired.

---
## Page 256
Show students where they fit. Make sure students understand the
overall program and school and teacher expectations. Each student
should understand how he or she fits in with an overall teaching plan for
the class and how each person’s performance in school will be
measured.
Have students do a SWOT analysis (see examples on page 109) for
subject areas to help them achieve academic, personal, or social goals.
A SWOT will help each person analyze their strengths, weaknesses,
opportunities, and threats in a conducive and helpful way. Let your
students know that Richard Branson, founder of Virgin Airlines (and its
various subsidiaries), developed this tool in the 1970s to help him build
a great workforce and business empire, and that this analysis is in use
all over the world to help individuals (and businesses) set and achieve
goals.
Students can use a SWOT analysis to evaluate goals for academic or
social competency areas, for their own behavior management, or for
acquiring individual “Executive Function” abilities. It can also be used
for planning as they create portfolios, records of achievement, and
Person Centered Plans (PCPs) in this chapter or a resume in Chapter 5
– Skills for Work.
How to Create a SWOT Analysis
The SWOT analysis example on page 109 might be used to help a
student develop self-confidence at school or reach an overall goal of
managing stress in his or her learning environment.
How to Create a SWOT Analysis
