## Page 257
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 258
HELPING STUDENTS TO ACCEPT THEIR
DIAGNOSIS
The classroom setting is an ideal place to work with adolescents and
young adults (individually or in small groups) as each person begins or
continues to understand (factually and emotionally) his or her ASD or
LD diagnosis. As individuals plan for success in school and begin to
develop relationships with teachers, classmates, and peers, having an
understanding of who they are, why they are different, and how to
accept and work with these differences in a high school or college
setting is a critical factor for achieving academic success.
When these young men and women don’t know what makes them
different, they develop negative fantasies about themselves. Because
no one is talking about their differences, they come to the conclusion
that it’s a big (bad) secret. Long-standing self-esteem issues make it
difficult to come to terms with who they really are.

---
## Page 259
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Recently diagnosed adolescents and young
adults have shared that over the years before they knew about their
ASD or LD Spectrum diagnosis, they believed they were crazy,
retarded, psychotic, and numerous other extremely negative
attributions. I drew some of these same conclusions about my own
self before I understood my clinical diagnosis and began to learn how
to accept it.
I can tell you from my own journey into acceptance, that helping each
individual student understand his or her diagnosis enables each
young person to talk about it, read about it, ask questions, do
research, and then identify with a group of similar people. This in turn
leads each one to feeling more hopeful and courageous about getting
help, asking for accommodations, and/or more information in the
various learning environments they will encounter.
Adolescents and young adults with Autism Spectrum Disorders (ASD)
or Learning Differences (LD) have different thinking patterns and brain
processing 
functions 
– 
especially 
in 
the 
area 
of 
social
competencies/social actions. These differences often compromise the
ability to understand individual idiosyncrasies, characters, and
personalities making it hard to have positive social connections with
others in school. The interactions of these emerging adults are often
more negative and/or stressful than rewarding and fulfilling, which
deflates their interest in pursuing self-growth or change. This makes it
very difficult for teachers to integrate individuals into an everyday
classroom setting. Helping individuals understand their differences can
ease this hard and challenging task.
Limitations in self-awareness can make it difficult for ASD or LD
adolescents or young adults to understand their strengths and begin to
overcome their challenges. They feel confusion over why something
works out well some of the time and then falls apart at another time.
They don’t know or may not be able to appreciate how the world
around them affects the events in their lives or how to make things
better.

---
## Page 260
KNOWING VS. NOT KNOWING
Here are some insights into the issue of knowing vs. not knowing about
an individual clinical diagnosis gleaned from students over the years:
•
Knowing enables those on the ASD or LD Spectrum to feel as if
they’re not crazy after all.
•
Knowing helps these young men and women feel as if they
belong to a group of others who are like them.
•
Knowing helps individuals with an ASD or LD diagnosis feel
proud of who they are.
•
Knowing helps adolescents and young adults plan their lives in a
realistic manner.
•
Knowing helps each person move toward greater self-
acceptance and less self-blame.
•
Knowing helps individuals advocate and talk about their
strengths and challenges so others can understand and accept
them.
•
Knowing helps these emerging adults have the opportunity to
look up to their own role models such as Temple Grandin,
Stephen Shore, Jerry Newport, Thomas Jefferson, Albert
Einstein, or other individuals with a known or suspected ASD or
LD diagnosis.
•
Knowing enables adolescents and young adults to find others on
the Spectrum for mentoring and/or friendship.
Generally speaking, all individuals with an ASD or LD diagnosis have a
right to know.
Use the guidelines and suggestions in the Knowing Vs. Not Knowing
section of this chapter to generate lesson plans that will help you
introduce each bulleted point into a classroom, group, or peer
mentoring setting so that students can begin to comprehend,
understand, and accept their individual diagnosis.

---
## Page 261
THE CHARACTERISTICS OF THINKING AND
LEARNING FOR ASD AND LD STUDENTS
Mainstreaming, inclusion, and the effective classroom management
that needs to happen when the first two things occur can only become
a reality when those working with ASD and LD students fully
understand the characteristics of an on the Spectrum diagnosis.
Use this list (drawn from in-depth detail in Chapter 1) to reinforce and
aid in your individual understanding of how and why your students do
what they do in your classroom setting. It’s a comprehensive overview
of how these young men and women approach learning, and what they
are bringing to the table. You can then structure learning and
comprehension strategies within these clinical parameters.
Here are some of the characteristics of thinking and learning students
with an ASD or LD diagnosis might present:
•
Excessive focus on and/or attention to details.
•
Difficulty with concept of meaning and knowing what relevant
(known as “Central Coherence Theory”).
•
Easily distracted.
•
Concrete (set in stone) thinking.
•
Difficulty combining or integrating ideas.
•
Difficulty with organization and sequencing.
•
Difficulty with visual or auditory materials, instructions, and
concepts.
•
Difficulty determining when something is finished.
•
Difficulty in generalizing.
•
Uneven patterns of development.

---
## Page 262
Takeaway
“Smart phones have made life much easier for those ‘on the
Autism Spectrum.’ Adolescents and young adults can track their
budgets and do their banking, navigate directionally through
their communities using map apps, and use voice and text for
help with spelling and filling out forms.
Reminders and calendars are essential for managing time and
the social skills apps are great for helping students figure out
what to do ‘in the moment.’
Access to the Internet and YouTube can help each person
reference ‘how to dos’ for almost everything and anything.
Students can create and store their video self-models for
practicing social interactions and job skills. Utilizing smart
phones make the ASD or LD adolescent or young adult more
independent!”
Jodi Pierce, CIP Brevard
TEACHING INSIGHT: As an overall teaching plan emerges it
becomes clear that teaching students who reside on the Spectrum
requires educators and the professionals who support them in the
classroom setting being open to change and to constantly tailoring the
teaching day as it evolves. As you implement or modify the
suggestions for teaching as outlined in this section, you will be taking
the “Executive Function” concepts and principles learned about in
Chapter 3 – Skills for Life one step further. As you help your students
apply “Executive Function” strategies to the classroom setting for
studying, to retain knowledge, and for organizing their class and home

---
## Page 263
work spaces, students start to begin to look at applying these
principles to other parts of their lives.
“EXECUTIVE FUNCTION” IN THE
CLASSROOM SETTING
“Executive Function,” described in Chapter 3 as a mental process that
governs an adolescent’s or young adult’s ability to manage time, make
plans, complete tasks, and exhibit appropriate behavior, was
approached from a skills for life standpoint in that chapter. Building on
this knowledge, educators, clinicians, and professionals can show
students how to apply this ability in the school setting. For example:
Being able to make it to class on time, being organized with class work,
and participating in social and recreational events allow students to
experience success, reach goals, and gain further self-confidence
regarding school.
“Executive Function” in the classroom helps students to pay attention
and control impulsivity by inhibiting inappropriate or ill-timed
responses. It allows for students to have a smooth transition from one
task or activity to another, as they learn how to manage time, stay
organized, and keep track of their belongings. As students engage in
mental planning (working memory), they learn to self-regulate, exhibit
self-control, and start monitoring their own behavior in relation to
successfully getting through the school day.
“Executive Functions” are cognitive activities of logic, strategy,
planning, problem solving, information processing, and behavior
control. “Executive Function” difficulties can lead to students not being
able to predict what will happen next, which creates anxiety and
transition problems. This often leads to exhibiting inflexibility, getting
stuck, or needing to preserve sameness. Students who can’t
comprehend and implement “Executive Functions” will have difficulty
concentrating and being present, or go to the extreme and exhibit
“blurting” and impulsivity.

---
## Page 264
This lack of cognition leads to difficulty with working memory and
results in:
•
Difficulty managing and allocating time (especially with respect
to long-term assignments; homework).
•
Difficulty keeping track of belongings/materials; forgetting things.
•
Difficulty with multi-step or complex tasks.
•
Disorganization.
•
Difficulty working in groups.
•
Difficulty with planning.
•
Difficulty remembering things.
It’s extremely difficult to work with ASD or LD students who lack
“Executive Function” ability, especially when mainstreamed. Always
keep in mind that the young men and women in your class, group, or
that you work with individually are not doing this (behavior) on purpose
or to drive you crazy! And they are most assuredly not lazy or dumb.
And above all: The student is not being oppositional just to ruin your
day or test your patience.
Here are three common misconceptions that often occur:
1. If a student did well on an assignment or essay yesterday, he or
she should be able to do the same quality or standard of work
today.
2. The best way for all students to focus is when they are still and
the room is quiet.
3. If a student cannot be organized or manage his or her time well,
he or she will never be able to succeed in my class or at school.
Here are some general strategies for implementing “Executive
Function” in any learning environment:
•
Teach step-by-step approaches toward task completion.

---
## Page 265
•
Have students use time organizers, computers, watches with
alarms.
•
Have students use visual schedules.
•
Provide written and oral directions and oral instructions.
•
Plan and structure transition times and shifts in activities.
Help Students Manage Time During the
School Day by:
•
Having students divide large tasks into segments.
•
Making sure everyone is using calendars/day planners to keep
track of long-term assignments, due dates, deadlines.
•
Having students program reminders on computers and smart
phones.
•
Asking individuals to organize their school work, tasks, and
assignments by day, week, and month.
•
Having everyone use checklists, to-do lists, and prepare a time
estimation worksheet or timeline.
Help Students Manage Their School and
Homework Spaces and Materials by:
•
Showing them how to create dedicated and individual work
spaces.
•
Illustrating visually and physically how to organize personal
work and study spaces.
•
Allowing time during school day for locker and backpack
organization.

---
## Page 266
•
Giving students time to organize materials and assignments for
homework.
•
Showing students how to use color coded bins (a place for
everything and everything in its place).
•
Demonstrating how to make sections in backpacks so that
students can easily find what they need in a hurry.
•
Providing examples on how to minimize clutter.
•
Scheduling cleaning and organizing of work spaces at least
once per week.
•
Having each person develop (electronically or physically) and
employ a master notebook system.
•
Showing students how accordion files or a 3-ring file with
sections can help organize subject materials.
•
Limiting the number of folders in use; having students use hole-
punched paper and clearly labeling all binders on spines; and
then monitor student notebooks.

---
## Page 267
Triage for Teachers
“If you notice a student’s attention span waning, try changing
the direction he or she is facing, or posture (if sitting, try
standing). Use different pre-framed listening positions to help
students understand that NOW it is time to pay attention, time to
focus, and that each individual will need to focus, listen, and
then apply what they are learning to what they are doing.”
Lance Haskins, CIP Brevard
“Maintain a calm demeanor at all times. Don’t feed into negative
behaviors. If a confrontation or issue arises, don’t escalate the
situation, END it.”
Ginamarie Iannone, CIP Amherst
“It’s very important to understand and see things from the
student’s perspective. You will have greater results by finding
things you can both relate to ‘as common ground.’
A great teacher never gives up on his/her student but constantly
searches for ways to address needs and challenges.”
Kaeb Menker, CIP Berkeley
Some Additional Academic Interventions That
Really Work Are:
•
Making necessary test accommodations: allow open book
tests, the use of word banks, the use of memory cues, testing in

---
## Page 268
preferred modality, e.g., orally or fill-in-the blank. Try giving
frequent quizzes instead of lengthy, instruction-filled or
complicated tests.
Weekly Sample Schedule
Copyright © College Internship Program (CIP)
•
Allowing various studying and performance strategies to be
used, such as SQ3R, a note- taking assistant, or tutorial support
in class.
•
Having students use mnemonics and flash cards in class to
help memory improve.

---
## Page 269
•
Allowing all classes and lectures to be tape recorded.
•
Encouraging students to use a “Livescribe” smart pen during
class.
•
Supporting learning and comprehension of subject materials
with the use of audio books, Kindles, Nooks, and e-readers.
TEACHING INSIGHT: “Executive Function” also applies to organizing
thoughts and retaining them in memory. None of the suggestions in
this chapter for implementing “Executive Function” strategies in the
classroom will work if students do not have a reliable memory. Being
able to recall facts, processes, and ideas at will requires the use of
working memory.
MEMORY
Working memory is the ability to hold onto information in order to
complete a task or activity. It is a combination of visual-spatial and
auditory memory, and relies on attention skills as well. If adolescents
and young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum have weak working
memory skills, things may slip their minds or be right on the tips of their
tongues, but not retrievable.
Visual-spatial working memory is the ability to use the “mind’s eye”
to hold onto visual information long enough to use it. Tell your students
that visual-spatial memory is like a camera in the brain. It can take
snapshots to help each person do things like: search through laundry to
find a shirt that matches a pair of pants he or she is holding, or a
missing sock that looks like the one he or she is holding. It also helps
each person recall where new things are, and where the person
physically is in relation to him or her, for example: finding the light in the
hallway in the middle of the night at a friend’s house without bumping
into walls, or the classroom, bathrooms, or lunch room in the school he
or she just started attending.
Auditory working memory is ability to hold onto information an
individual hears long enough to use it. It’s what helps each person

---
## Page 270
remember the first sentence of a book he or she just read. It’s also what
helps us remember a phone number long enough to dial it.
ASD and LD adolescents and young adults with working memory
difficulties can hold fewer pieces of discrete information in their mind at
any given moment. They really do need to hear what you said, in order
to successfully complete the task. Once auditory information is lost it is
not likely to be retrieved and it is easy to see how these young men and
women become frustrated and consequently stop listening or paying
attention.
IMPROVING MEMORY
Show your students how to incorporate memory tricks and tools. A
mind map (see the examples below) is a great way to do this. Explain
that memory tricks (such as mind maps) are another set of tools to help

---
## Page 271
each person be successful in his or her studies and in the mastery of
academic requirements.
Explain that a mind map is a great way to help each person remember
and retain information. Use the class or group mind mapping exercise
to illustrate a particular subject area students are having trouble with or
to reinforce concepts that students may be able to take to a higher level
through this activity.
Have students research the many mind mapping websites that are
available on the Internet for inspiration and to see how mind maps can
be used to retain knowledge in memory on any subject or life skill area.
Below is an example of how students might create an individual mind
map.
The example below shows the advantages, diversity and creativity that
can occur during the process of mind mapping! As you can see it can
be used individually or as a whole class or group strategy to retain
knowledge.

---
## Page 272

---
## Page 273
Making Mind Maps Using a Whole
Class Approach
1. Break down the information into categories and
then into individual ideas.
2. Write down all of these ideas on note cards and
then pin or tape them to a large wall. Be sure that
students group ideas by category.
3. Connect similar ideas with string, or color-code
the cards to convey even more information.

---
## Page 274
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
PAYING ATTENTION
Explain to your ASD or LD students that: You can’t remember what
you can’t pay attention to.
Ask if students are over-tasked or have competing stimuli for their
attention.
Help each person deal with any sensory issues that are interfering.
Tell your students that memory is an active process: each person must
learn to manipulate information to get it into his or her head.

---
## Page 275
Explain that to imbed things into memory each person needs to
remember the 3 Rs: Repetition, Repetition, and Repetition! Students
need to be prepared to repeat information over and over again.
Talk about visualization: ask students to see what they are trying to
remember in their minds. Tell students that the mind is like a camera
and they need to form a picture to go with words being said or thoughts
being verbally presented.
Employ and demonstrate the concept of association – sometimes
called scaffolding: grouping and placing like items together. Practice
sorting main ideas and details.
MEMORY BOOSTERS FOR MEMORY
RETRIEVAL
Have students focus on one concept at a time.
Talk about and write down (on a white board) all key steps, so each
person is clear and understands what is needed and required to do the
school work.
Use reading guides, study guides, plot summaries, outlines, and lists.
Teach note-taking skills: let students use a study buddy or teacher-
prepared notes to fill in the gaps. Let students use a computer or tablet
in class for note taking.
Provide opportunities for self-correction.
The student handout on page 119 is for students to use to improve
memory and to develop good study habits. This will give the ASD or LD
learner strategies and techniques to employ, so that each person can
achieve success in the classroom setting and reach predetermined
academic goals.

---
## Page 276
PROGRAMMATIC INTERVENTIONS – THE
“BOOKENDS”™ MODEL
Explicit training modules such as the “Executive Function” group
sessions known at CIP (College Internship Program) as “Bookends”™
are great models for schools to emulate. “Bookends”™ groups meet on
Mondays and Fridays.
During Monday’s “Bookends”™ session, students plan, structure, and
organize their school week around the academic goals, internships, or
part-time work that will happen during this time-frame. They also plan
for extracurricular or social activities that will occur that week and figure
out ways to include wellness, healthy meal planning, and (if living
independently or with a roommate) cleaning, and apartment or room
maintenance.
The “Bookends”™ session on Friday (as the weekend approaches)
allows each person to create or come up with a plan that embraces
leisure time, incorporates wellness and healthy living, and allows time
for studying and preparing for the school week ahead (and their
Monday “Bookends™”session).
Techniques for Improving Your Memory
FIND THINGS TO ENJOY!
You’ll have an easier time remembering the information you are
studying if it’s something that you care about or can get excited
about. Some things in school will be naturally interesting for
you (especially if they in any way, shape, or form touch on one of
your “special interests”) but other things that you need to
remember may seem really boring on the surface. When this

---
## Page 277
happens, you’ll have to find a way to get yourself interested in
the material. There are lots of different ways to go about this.
Try:
•
Finding a reason why the information will be useful to you
later in life.
For example: the math you’re learning can help you calculate
how much money you’ll need to save in order to retire. Be
smart and you might even be able to figure out how to retire
early.
•
Make the information into a story.
For example: if you’re studying history, find a way to adapt
what you’re learning into an episode of your favorite TV show
or computer game. If you’re studying science, think of a way
that the science could be used to make a superhero origin
story.
ACTIVELY LISTEN!
If you pay very close attention during the original lectures, not
only will you have an easier time remembering the information,
but you’ll also be able to study more efficiently because your
brain will have an easier time recalling the information.
Really work hard to listen when you’re in class, and keep
yourself engaged with the conversation of learning by asking
questions and getting involved with the class lesson.
Take notes! Another good way to listen to the lecture is to take
notes. This will help keep you on track for paying attention and
give you great material to study from later. Remember, when you
take notes, the idea isn’t to write down everything that your

---
## Page 278
teacher says. Instead, write down only the important things or
main ideas. Write down the outline of the lecture and fill it in with
facts, and explanations for the tough concepts that you know
you’ll struggle with later on.
For example: If you were taking notes on an article, you’d
probably break your notes down for each section of the article,
and write one or two takeaways for each step.
DO YOUR OWN RESEARCH!
You can help yourself remember what you learn, and also help
yourself get more interested in what you’re studying by taking
ownership of your education and looking for more information
outside of what your teacher talks about. This can help you
better understand the concepts being taught and gives you a
more solid framework on which you can build upon the
information covered in class. You might even find interesting
things that you think are really cool!
For example: Let’s say you’re studying chemistry, and your
teacher is talking about the discovery of all sorts of new
compounds around the late 1800s and early 1900s. You might
stop and think to yourself, “What did people do with all this new
stuff?” If you did some research, you’d find out that all those
new compounds were used to make new, brightly colored
paints. These new colors were responsible for an artistic
revolution which gave us painters like Van Gogh and Monet.
GET SOME CONTEXT!
If you’re having a hard time following along with what your
teacher is saying, try giving yourself a bit more context for the
information. Sometimes, when you can more clearly visualize

---
## Page 279
what’s 
being 
discussed, 
you’ll 
have 
an 
easier 
time
understanding what’s happening and keeping track of new
information as it comes in.
For example: If you’re studying history but you find you just
can’t keep track of everything that’s happening, try going to a
museum, or watching a documentary or video that deals with
that subject. This will give you something to imagine as you
learn, and it might even explain some ideas in a different or
better way than your teacher did.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
On Mondays and Fridays students also evaluate their progress and
share insight about things that worked well and things that did not go as
planned. At CIP, these bi-weekly groups have an instructor and five
students. Students sit in a horseshoe around the instructor and several
“Executive Function” posters are on the wall behind the instructor.
While this structure might not be possible in your particular learning
environment 
– 
integrating 
the 
philosophy 
and 
concepts 
of
“Bookends”™ is.
TEACHING INSIGHT: Feel free to adapt the CIP “Bookends”™
proprietary model and to use the posters below as classroom
examples. Refer to them each day as teachable moments occur
during class time – even if it is just an example or the implementation
of an idea or concept – here or there. Seize any opportunity you can
to impart these skills to the adolescents and young adults you work
with.
The “Executive Function” posters below can be enlarged, reproduced,
and used as each educator or support professional desires. These
posters can also be downloaded from: autismldtoolkit.com. Each one
can serve as a student handout as well.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: To pull the above content of this chapter
together with examples of “real-time and real-place” scenarios I asked

---
## Page 280
Ryan Thierault, the Lead Academic Advisor at CIP Brevard, to
illustrate to readers how “Executive Function,” Social Skills, and the
“Hidden Curriculum” come into play as students work hard to integrate
these concepts in school sometimes with humorous and oftentimes
poignant results. Ryan’s story starts on page 122.

---
## Page 281
“A Day in the Life of an Academic Advisor”
Although a student may be incredibly intelligent and capable of
college work, a simple communication or tracking mishap may

---
## Page 282
get in the way of academic success. As a result, I typically
recommend that students increase their timeline to complete
their high school diploma, degree, or certificate in order to allow
for time to correct mistakes and learn from them. The following
are examples of simple things that could go wrong during
academic study that had nothing to do with course content.
Honesty Is the Best Policy
Molly has been a college student for a long time. She writes well
and completes her assignments independently with only a gentle
reminder to begin or start her task. She is very positive and
spunky. In fact, her language is so positive that I have trouble
figuring out when something is wrong or if she needs help. One
day I looked at her grades and noticed that she was missing
some things that she insisted she completed and I remembered
seeing her do.
At first, I second guessed myself, but Molly assured me that she
did them. However, experience tells me to trust but verify. Molly
had no evidence, but she explained that she must have been
pushing the cancel button rather than the submit button. The
only choice was to start over, regardless of the reason.
Needless to say, I was not sure if I could trust her. I explained
that when there is no evidence of completion and something is
missing, it might look like lying to someone who does not know
her. Molly decided that from then on she would save all of her
work to a second location before submitting. In the meantime, I
advised her to speak to her instructor to let him know that she in
fact, did do the assignment, but made a mistake in the
submission.
The email read something like:

---
## Page 283
“Dear Mr. X,
I am very busy with my internship so I was unable to finish my
assignments on time. Will you please extend the deadline? I
understand if there is a penalty. Please respond quickly. Thank
you.”
I explained that when she makes statements like that, it implies
that her internship is more important than the class. Also, the
statement was not truthful. To my surprise, the instructor gave
her the extra time with no deadline. However, Molly understood
this to mean that she would not have a deadline for any of that
type of her assignments. In addition, she continued to make the
same submission mistake that I once again caught. I advised
Molly to email her teacher with a more accurate and truthful
email this time.
It went like this:
“Dear Mr. X,
I am doing better in my internship and I will have the rest of my
assignments turned in by tomorrow, now that I have time to
catch up.”
Clearly Molly was fearful about telling the truth to her instructor.
We talked about what the worst thing to happen would be.
Eventually we concluded that she should write an email that
stated:
“Dear Mr. X,
I did a really funny thing. I accidently have been hitting the
cancel button instead of the submit button. I feel very silly for

---
## Page 284
doing that. From now on, I will save a copy and will check to
make sure the assignments are submitted on time. I am sorry for
any inconvenience. Thank you.”
The instructor replied: “That is a funny thing. Glad you caught
it.”
Molly received an “A” in the class, but if she had not been
reminded to go back and check her submissions, she may have
been in big trouble and she would not have been able to learn
from her mistakes. In addition, Molly learned that she can earn
more respect and consideration if she is honest.
“Executive Functions” and social skills are learned by these
students “on the Spectrum” if they are taught through explicit
instruction how to take advantage of any learning experiences
that result from mistakes.
In the next vignette you’ll see how Raoul needed to learn and
understand more about the “Hidden Curriculum” in the
classroom setting.
A Very Literal Raoul
Raoul was a college student who took things very literally, as
students who have not mastered the “Hidden Curriculum”
usually do. One day his classmates completed a quiz given in
the classroom. However, Raoul was still working on his quiz and
had a disability accommodation which allowed him to have extra
time to take any quiz he was not able to finish, in the disabilities
office … but the office was closed that late in the evening.
Knowing this, the teacher decided to accommodate Raoul the
best she could and asked him, “Would you like to finish your

---
## Page 285
quiz out in the hall?” To which Raoul replied, “No, I’m fine, thank
you.” At that point, the teacher was stuck. Dumbfounded, she
waited while her precious class time (that she needed to use for
the rest of the lesson) slowly disappeared as Raoul worked on
his quiz.
Later, the instructor, Raoul, and I decided to have a meeting
about how to best accommodate him the next time something
like this occurred, without affecting the rest of the class. My
advice to her was to be explicit when explaining her
expectations, because Raoul had trouble understanding aspects
of the “Hidden Curriculum,” such as indirect language. We all
agreed that from that point on Raoul would take his quiz in the
hall when he was told to do so (and not be given a choice). At
the end of the meeting, Raoul walked over to where the
instructor was sitting behind her desk and looked down at her
silently. She appeared confused and uncomfortable. I gave him
a quiet reminder about “boundaries” and a beckoning gesture.
“Oh,” he replied, and walked out the door.
The instructor learned from this experience that not all students
are created equal. She learned how to be direct without being
rude in order to get the desired response from Raoul. And I think
she also learned that students on the ASD/LD Spectrum will
often forget to stay an arm’s length away from an acquaintance.
Ryan Therriault,
Lead Academic Advisor, CIP Brevard
SETUP A NETWORK OF SUPPORT IN CASE
SOMETHING GOES WRONG

---
## Page 286
RYAN THERRIAULT
LEAD ACADEMIC ADVISOR CIP BREVARD
To increase student success, I recommend that students take the
minimum number of classes each term with mixed difficulty (example: 1
hard, 1 easy). The reduced course load will allow them to attend to the
other areas in their life, while reducing the effect on academics. In
addition, I recommend that they meet with several different people
weekly in order to create a support system with standing check-ins.
They must continue to meet with the people throughout their college
career, even if they are doing well.
The following people will help students to plan, prioritize, and follow
through with assignments in addition to providing emotional support:
•
Tutor: Can provide planning and content related support.
•
Study group: Can provide peer emotional, content and planning
support.
•
Disabilities office: Can provide resources such as planning
courses, extra test time, etc.
•
Therapy: Can assist with maintaining emotional stability overall.
•
Life Coach: Can help reflect upon success and failure to
enhance future experiences.
•
Teacher: Can clarify assignments and provide content and
planning support.
•
Clergy: Can help promote spiritual growth, fellowship support,
and life purpose.
The “Executive Function” student handout with some questions
students can answer and use as a guideline for working through
individual assignments. It can also be applied to the overall school day
or for achieving a specific academic goal.
TEACHING INSIGHT: As students acquire strategies, skills, and the
tools they need to be fully present, participate, get organized, and
experience success in the classroom setting, helping individuals set

---
## Page 287
goals and plan for their future is timely and appropriate. This is done
through “Person Centered Plans” (PCPs). Adolescents and young
adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum set realistic goals that embrace each
person’s expectations, hopes, and desires during their high school
years and beyond. The section below details this process and gives
examples that your students can use to create their individual PCPs.
Get It Done!
•
What am I trying to accomplish?
•
What are the necessary steps?
•
What is the sequence of the steps?
•
How do I start? When do I start?
•
How will I persevere with the plan?
•
What could go wrong?
•
Is the plan working?
•
How will I know when I’m done?
•
What would I do differently next time?
•
What would I do the same next time?

---
## Page 288
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
PERSON CENTERED PLAN (PCP)
A “Person Centered Plan” is a great tool to help students develop self-
understanding. All adolescents and young adults have hopes, dreams,
and ambitions. Individuals with an ASD or LD diagnosis may find it
difficult to form a concept of what their specific wishes and hopes for
the future might entail. “Person Centered Plans” (PCPs) can fill this gap
and serve as a goal setting tool that shows those on the ASD or LD
Spectrum how to develop an appreciation for their unique talents and
capabilities so that they can take control of their own futures.
During elementary and high school, a student’s family, teachers, and
other professionals typically dictate specific goals through an education
plan in which the student is a passive participant. In the later high

---
## Page 289
school years and beginning college or vocational training years, PCPs
empower young people to become actively involved in the process.
A “Person-Centered Plan” (PCP) serves as a unique self-exploration
and assessment tool that provides each student an opportunity to
conceptualize his or her short- and long-term goals in a visual, creative
way. Using multimedia tools, each student creates a personal
PowerPoint presentation. Students begin working on their PCPs
ideally, at the beginning of the school year and continue to refine them
as the semester progresses. Students form alliances with others who
will support them during this process; this might be a teacher, mentor,
peers who have gone through the process before, or his or her
academic advisor.
By understanding that achieving goals requires instruction, practice,
and reinforcement, students begin to enact personal accountability for
each academic or life skill goal. These goals are then broken down into
monthly and yearly segments that they can strive towards with support,
encouragement, and ultimately … success.
PCPs focus on objectives that encompass the school’s curriculum and
what the student envisions. An understanding of each student’s
“special interest” (and this will be wide-ranging, unique, and varied) will
allow educators and the professionals who assist them to help each
person develop realistic goals that can be visually tracked and modified
over time.
The “Person Centered Plan” is one of many tools that you can use to
help your students build competencies and adapt to new environments,
ensuring they will have the skills they need to be successful in school
and as they plan for their futures.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: “Person Centered Planning” allows each
young adult to express what he or she wants, needs, or would like.
Each person gets to declare individual hopes, dreams, and ambitions
and then sketches it out as a road map for future. Help your students
think about things they might include in their PowerPoint presentation,
like music, photographs, and important mementos, videos of people,

---
## Page 290
places, and things they like: art, their pets, favorite places, and even a
job or activities that brought or bring them happiness and joy. One of
my favorite things, as an educator, comes from helping my students
embrace the “Person Centered Planning” process and to know that
many of my former students continue this activity throughout
instrumental or important periods in their lives.
“SPECIAL INTERESTS”
This is a good point in the content of this book to talk about
adolescents and young adults on the ASD Spectrum and their
individual “special interests.” As your ASD and LD students begin to
design their PCPs, the “special interests” of those who reside on the
ASD Spectrum will (without a doubt) invariably surface.
“Special interests” in relation to an official ASD Diagnosis means that
the individual has an intense and obsessive level of interest in one
particular thing. The experts tell us that “special interests” typically
focus on the mechanical (how things work) as opposed to the
psychological (how people work) (Baron-Cohen and Wheelwright
1999).
Sometimes these “special interests” are life-long; in other cases, they
change at unpredictable intervals. In either case, there are normally
only one or two “interests” at any given time. “Special interests” are
often linked in some way that is logical only to the ASD individual
pursuing it.
In this pursuit of his or her “special interest,” adolescents and young
adults with an ASD diagnosis will often manifest extremely
sophisticated reasoning, an almost obsessive focus, and a remarkably
good memory for trivial or meaningless facts (Hippler and Klicpera
2002).
Hans Asperger called his young patients little professors because he
thought his patients had as comprehensive or nuanced understanding

---
## Page 291
of their field of interest as any university professor would have
(Asperger 1944).
Adolescents and young adults with ASD may have little patience for
things outside these narrow interests. In school, they may be perceived
as highly intelligent underachievers or overachievers, clearly capable of
outperforming their peers in their field of the “special interest,” yet
persistently unmotivated to do regular homework assignments
(sometimes even in their areas of interest). Others may be hyper-
motivated to out-perform peers in school.
The combination of social problems and intense “special interests” can
lead to unusual behavior. Autism and Learning Differences (An Active
Learning Teaching Toolkit) gave such an example in Chapter 1. If you’ll
recall – it was greeting a stranger by launching into a lengthy
monologue about a “special interest” (in this case bicycles) rather than
introducing oneself in the socially accepted way.
As you work with students and as this book progresses to Chapter 5,
you’ll learn how to work with students to explore the potential for using
each person’s “special interests” in connection with further education,
looking at vocational training, a career, or helping students realize that
their “special interest” is not viable for these pursuits. And of course, if
appropriate, students should highlight their “special interests” in their
“Person Centered Plans.”
Here are some examples of some common and a few unusual “special
interests.”
•
Aircraft of any type, trains, and bicycles.
•
Pets as they are always around the house and do not talk or
present a socially complex partner.
•
Computer games (these can become very addictive as they
provide visual stimulation minus the need for social interaction).
•
Magical things – fairies, unicorns, and comic book characters.
At CIP – the ComicCon conventions are the most sought after
field trip of the year!

---
## Page 292
•
Cards/sports facts – baseball or football cards and scoreboard
information – become a way to interact with people and their
competitive instincts but not in an intimidating social way (no
discussion required).
•
Unusual things too, like toilets, cement pouring, Communist
dictators, the Holocaust, graffiti, rock stars, and any/many
fictional characters.
HELPING STUDENTS CREATE A PERSON
CENTERED PLAN
The focus for an individual “Person Centered Plan” is to have each one
of your students create colorful, customized PowerPoint slides
accompanied by their own music and graphic design elements. Allow
students to develop their own unique way for doing this and to take full
ownership of the process as they collaborate with others or seek
assistance for their creation. Individual PowerPoints can include
anything that is personally relevant to the student’s mission statement
and goals such as music, video clips, photographs, and art, or anything
that illustrates or incorporates his or her “special interests.”
The visual nature of this approach keeps students on the ASD or LD
Spectrum interested and involved in the PCP process. Each student
sets a time-frame for achieving goals so that they can realistically track
progress.

---
## Page 293
Developing Insight
Many students on the ASD or LD Spectrum have a built-in default
mechanism to go it alone, which can work against them in the adult
world. Many need to be taught to build partnerships and alliances for
accomplishing the goals expressed in their PCPs. Students soon learn
that almost everything they do in life is in relationship to others. Asking
for and receiving help (while maintaining ownership of the process) as
the PCP evolves is important.
Some professionals who work with adolescents and young adults on
the Spectrum claim that insight-centered therapy is unavailable to this
segment, and that they can’t take advantage of this due to their myopic
sense of themselves and the world around them. However, at CIP
(College Internship Program) the PCP process has resulted in much
success with our students. As these young men and women create a
vision for themselves, and identify the personal strategies that they will
need to develop in order to reach their goals, much insight occurs.
Individuals with an ASD or LD diagnosis can and do develop insight

---
## Page 294
into who they are and what they want to be within a framework that
brings forth their own unique talents and gifts.
Reaching Goals
TEACHING INSIGHT: Over the years CIP’s staff has reported that
goals are easier to achieve when they are as specific and detailed as
possible and are written down in the present tense. You might
consider helping your students set really clear time limits for each
goal. This clarifies the expectation and helps each student be realistic
about what they are trying to accomplish.
By doing this students begin to understand that most goals are
accomplished in partnership with others, and that almost everything
they will do in life is in relationship with others. To help your student
understand this process have students view episodes of the “Survivor
Shows” that are on TV. These shows introduce and reinforce the
concepts of creating alliances with others and using input and
advice of others to solve problems and dilemmas (an expansion, more
or less, of “The Donkey Rule”).
The student handout on page 128 is meant to get the “Person
Centered Plan” (PCP) process started with your students.
TEACHING INSIGHT: Your students now have a blueprint for starting
their PCPs. Encourage them to do some Internet research as there
are many examples of PCPs online and some of these may help spark
their creativity while allowing them to see how extensively this method
for setting and attaining goals is used throughout the world.
As your students complete their “Person Centered Plans” it’s time to
look at taking this process one step further. PCPs are a great way to
jumpstart the portfolio and record of achievement process.

---
## Page 295
Creating a Person Centered Plan (PCP)
The Steps to My Dream
By:
___________________________________________
____________
Date: _____________________ Program Year:
_________________
What is a “Person Centered Plan” or
PCP?
This is your Declaration of Independence. You get to
declare for yourself your dream and sketch it out as a
road map for your future. Up until now it has been your
family, teachers, administrators, and professionals
who 
created 
your 
“education 
plan” 
(in 
an
Individualised Education Plan, IEP) and you sat there
and listened. In “Person Centered Planning,” this is
your process, your vision, and your chance to express
yourself.
You will be able to present this to your family, your
teachers, and your peers and mentors in a PowerPoint
format. Build in all the things that are important to you:

---
## Page 296
your music, your pictures, your videos of people, jobs,
art, pets, places, etc. that are important from your past
and that you desire in your future.
Enjoy this process … it will continue the rest of your
life.
My Character

## Page 297
2. ________________________________

4. ________________________________

## Page 298
4. ________________________________
My Personal Mission Statement
 
 
 
 
Below are two examples of a Personal
Mission Statement.
Example 1
I have a very strong desire to succeed at anything that
I try and am very competitive. I am a person who fears
failure and is also enormously motivated by economic
security and wealth. I plan to make a lot of money. I
am motivated by good grades.
I will bring to any situation my very strong work ethic
and sense of ethics. I plan to use my strong leadership
skills and strong problem solving skills.
I have a firm belief in maintaining good physical and
mental health. This includes maintaining good
relationships with my family and my peers. When with
them, I want to work well, enjoy their company, and
communicate my feelings without hiding anything.

---
## Page 299
I want to stay in good physical health, too, without
hurting my body in any way, be it through injury or
alcohol or drugs. I recently found out by injuring my leg
how important sports are to me to maintain my
emotional stability and focus. I am not a quitter and
want to work hard, however.
In summary, my personal mission is to maintain my
mental and physical health through exercise and
meditation and playing the sports that I enjoy. I have
learned to calm myself down and to decrease my
tendency to anger quickly with others. I will continue to
practice meditation. I envision an open, honest, and
happy relationship with both of my parents. In my
career, I will utilize my passion for sports and my
interest in psychology while being successful,
independent, and calm. I will control my anger at work
and have time to be with my family. I will stay relaxed
through 
exercise 
and 
meditation. 
By 
working
independently and having control over my schedule, I
will be able to stay calm and centered through good
self-care.
Example 2
I am someone who has always liked acting and the
arts. I love helping people and I love to laugh and to
feel part of a group. Every summer I have been in
plays and special art projects. For the longest time I
have wanted to help children feel better about
themselves. I know what it is like to feel different and
lonely. Someday I would like to be a therapist, but use
the creative arts to help kids to feel more open about
their feelings and freer and more spontaneous. I know

---
## Page 300
how alive and well I feel when I act and create artistic
collages.
It is my life’s dream to use the creative arts, including
drama and painting, as a therapeutic tool. I would love
to be affiliated someday with a hospital that treats
seriously ill children with this type of therapy. I have an
altruistic love for helping people and like to connect
people with others. I want to help children to be well
and happy with themselves.
I am also someone who has a big need to be my own
person. I have lots of unique ideas and my own
personal style. I enjoy working in my own way, and
want to have my separate niche and place where I can
excel. I like enough creative space for expressing my
uniqueness. I like the time to adjust to change and
want to work with people I like and trust. I need
complete directions to get my work done and I don’t
like conflict and confrontation. I like a lot of
predictability in my environment.
I am definitely someone who wants a small college
with small classes and a very friendly campus. I like
developing good relationships with my teachers. It is
important to me to be part of a team so that I do not
feel like I am alone when I help children. I would like to
work part-time in the beginning, to not feel too
overwhelmed, and to make sure that I do not get too
stressed out. My dream is to have a family of my own
someday and a career that allows for part-time hours
when I have my own children.
My most important goal is to take good care of my
mental and physical health and to not resort to self-

---
## Page 301
destructive behaviors again. I want to stay healthy and
relaxed, and to continue to enjoy drama and the arts.
My Goals
For the next six months:

© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
DOCUMENTING SUCCESS
Encouraging your students to create an ongoing record of achievement
and a portfolio during their high school and college years provides
validation for the process of having each person taking the time to do
so. This documentation provides each individual with a sense of
participation, ownership of academic and personal achievements, as
well as a sense of accomplishment. These documentation tools will
help students identify their strengths and their own unique talents as
they think about transitioning from high school, college, or vocational
programs to the world of work. These records can assist students as
they move on to obtaining skills for work (in the next chapter) and as
they learn to prepare a resume to seek employment or prepare to
interview for a job.

---
## Page 302
Record of Achievement
The purpose of a record of achievement is to allow an opportunity for
your students to document their skills, academic, and personal
successes. Planning and creating a record of achievement is also a
good way of challenging students to create their best work for inclusion
in it. A record of achievement is a tool that allows students to reflect on
what they have accomplished during their adolescent and young adult
years. It can be expanded into a student portfolio.
Portfolios
Portfolios are a visual, written, chronological, and organized way of
chronicling records of achievement in a presentational and more
detailed way.
As students start creating portfolios a folder, binder, or box can be used
to store the items to be organized as they decide how they will use the
data. At this time students will also decide if the portfolio will be paper,
electronic-based, or a multimedia creation or a combination of all three.
Items in a portfolio can include: essays, scrapbooks, artwork,
photographs, group work, research data, newspaper articles, awards
(both academic and in sports or for extra-curricular activities), and
honors. Everything should be dated and can be accompanied by a
short caption or description.
Portfolios should also include any volunteer work, internships, part-time
jobs, job shadowing, or any philanthropically minded activities they
have participated in.
The student handouts on pages 132–133 will help the adolescents and
young adults you work with chronicle their successes.

---
## Page 303
Tips for Creating a Portfolio
A portfolio will be a valuable and visual tool for you to
use to show others what you are capable of doing and
what your interests and special talents are. Your
portfolio includes your record of achievement and is a
record of all of your accomplishments throughout high
school, college, work, and even your life outside these.
Its purpose is to strengthen any application or
presentation that you make to others to help them
understand who you are as an individual person. It
illustrates what skills you possess, what you have
learned in the school setting, and what your interests
and hobbies are outside of the school setting. If you
volunteer or work part-time, this is your chance to tell
people about that as well.
A portfolio is presented in a professional platform of
your choice such as a binder or notebook;
electronically using PowerPoint or another visual
program; or a combination of both using multimedia.
Portfolios can be used to apply for further education, a
full- or part-time employment, internships, or some
type of grant/scholarship.
You need to:
•
Be able to identify and list your interests.
•
Incorporate your record of achievements.

---
## Page 304
•
Prepare to show others your accomplishments in a
verbal and/or visual way.
What goes into a portfolio?
You might have already started to group together
records of your achievement, but in case you are
starting from scratch, it’s a great idea to put all these
documents inside a folder where each piece of paper
is clearly and neatly on display.
•
Your portfolio should include any items that
highlight 
important 
accomplishments 
and
demonstrate your skills, knowledge, creativity, and
personal qualities.
•
Your portfolio is a record of all of your
achievements throughout high school, work, and
even your life outside these. Its purpose is to
strengthen any application or presentation of
yourself you make in a professional platform; this
might be to apply for further education, a full-time
role, or some type of grant/scholarship.
It’s a good idea to:
Arrange your certificates in order of importance,
according to what would best represent you in a
professional environment. The best order to arrange
these documents will depend on your individual
achievements and what type of presentation you are
making or what you will be using your portfolio for.
Here is a list of documents in order to help you get
started:

---
## Page 305
•
Graduation certificate.
•
Higher 
School 
Certificates 
or 
Record 
of
Achievement.
•
Reference letters, and commendations.
•
Anything that has been publically written to you to
highlight your accomplishments or strengths.
•
School awards.
•
Sporting certificates, or proof of any extra-
curricular involvement.
Your portfolio:
Can be used as an addition to your resume and can
be taken with you to work, scholarship, and other
professional interviews. Employers don’t always
expect you to have your achievements, so it’s a great
way to stand out among other applicants while
showing detailed evidence of your skills.
Take advantage of electronic-based
media:
If your school is fueled by Google Apps for Education,
then using Google Sites to create student portfolios, or
“Googlios,” makes perfect sense.
Start with an online template and integrate your work
with other Google apps to create a comprehensive
story of what you have learned. Apps like Slides,
Blogger, and YouTube will help you create an
amazing multimedia portfolio.

---
## Page 306
You can also use your portfolio to build a social media
profile on career networking sites such as LinkedIn. If
you are an artist, check out www.deviantART.com – a
great online artist portfolio website.
Think about using Three Ring. It’s a mobile app with a
desktop version. With Three Ring you can create and
upload content from mobile devices and tag, search,
and share your portfolios.
Regardless of the platform you choose, empowering
and authenticating your accomplishments in print, via
digital media, or using a combination of both, your
portfolio will be your own unique way to showcase
your talents!
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
EFFECTIVE CLASSROOM MANAGEMENT
TEACHING INSIGHT: The next section of the “Skills for School”
chapter looks at effective classroom management. Managing a
classroom that includes or is dedicated to working with adolescents
and young adults on the ASD and LD Spectrum can be a daunting
task. All it takes is one melt-down, one extreme episode of “blurting”,
or an overtired, under-nourished student to throw off and disrupt an
entire classroom day. The ideas, suggestions, and materials in this
subsection of this chapter can help create a learning environment that
enables and empowers teachers to deal with these disruptions as they
impart knowledge to ASD and LD students.
RULES … RULE!

---
## Page 307
As adults, we all know that there are rules for life that may be
negotiable and there are rules that are concrete where there is no
negotiation. They just are, and they come with a firm set of guidelines.
As you begin to construct a classroom environment that fosters
inclusion, learning, tolerance, and respect, talk with your class or
group about these basic tenets:

---
## Page 308
Triage for Teachers
•
Recognize the core issue taking place: who is doing what to
whom, where, when, and what conditions are occurring.
•
Intervene immediately.
•
Have each person explain their point of view.
•
Mediate or have a trained student mediator do this.
•
Agree to disagree if necessary.
•
Remove the student from the classroom if necessary.
•
That we do some things because there is a written or verbal rule
we have to follow.
•
That we do some things just because it is the right thing to do.
•
We do the right thing whether or not there is a verbal or written
rule to follow.
•
Ideally we do the right thing because we want to and not just
because we should.
Explain that in your class, all people are treated with courtesy, respect,
and dignity. Tell your students that your classroom has a set of rules
and standards to help students do this. Talk about these standards and
have the adolescents and young adults you work with discuss the
reasons for these rules, rule! rules.
IN THIS CLASS IN THIS SCHOOL WE:
•
Respect the feelings of other people.

---
## Page 309
•
Refrain from teasing and insulting others.
•
Don’t violate another person’s confidentiality.
•
Don’t talk behind someone’s back.
The ultimate goal in a managed classroom is to have all participants in
the class be open to learning and advocating for themselves while
working within a set of rules that all participants have agreed to adhere
to. With these rules in place teachers can impart knowledge and
students can obtain it.
Practical Classroom Management Tips that
Work
•
Provide rationales (reasons) for what you are requesting your
students to do.
•
Use logic and explain (students with an ASD or LD diagnosis
respect logic).
•
Walk around the classroom to keep a student involved (lightly
touch on the desk or paper to help them focus on the work).
•
Employ eye contact, use a soft voice, and give simple
instructions.
•
Employ peer mentors, tutors, and teaching assistants.
•
Use multimedia in presenting all information (video, audio, flash
cards, handouts, etc).
•
Alternate mental and physical activities.
•
Ask a student to read the assignment or recipe and to explain it.
•
Make assignments/lessons concise and compact.
•
Structure in some de-stim, meditation, or guided day dreaming
time.

---
## Page 310
•
Have five minutes of quiet time during an activity, class, or in the
van to reset the sensory environment.
“12-Step Rule Bound Behavior Toolkit”
1. Help students develop a good support system with solid
mentors, so that they can use “The Donkey Rule” with these
trusted individuals as they make decisions.
2. Help each person gain a better sense of self-awareness:
“Does this rule work for me still?”
3. Look at the beliefs the student has and is adhering to. Talk
about the student’s logic, premise, or underlying motive that
they are based upon and find chinks in the armor.
4. Find the roots of the rule bound premise (e.g., their minister
said that they would “go to hell if they stole something from a
store.”)
5. Ask if a student’s Rule Bound Behavior gets “in the way of
achieving his or her goals.”
6. Encourage these young men and women to have the
willingness to try something different: “Can we try an
experiment for a week?”
7. Ask students to write out what the results would be if their
response to a situation remains the same and ask them to
contrast it by imagining the scenario another way and writing
down a different response.
8. Encourage the young people you work with to be fearless
and courageous in trying new strategies.
9. Create erosion around the foundational principle that the
student is bound to by pointing out examples of positive

---
## Page 311
change that have benefited others they know and any
illogical thought patterns that will undermine their thinking.
10.Give students exposure to alternative ways of thinking (e.g.,
a discussion of race or religious perspectives at “Reframing”
or in group social thinking).
11.Utilize beliefs that a student might have in the past and
successfully changed or had good results with to assist him
or her in trying new strategies.
12.Use “Cognitive Dissonance:” Explain to the student that this
is when a set in stone belief system does not match up with
reality. Point out the pattern(s) to the student and explain that
this is why he or she feels out of sync.
RULE BOUND BEHAVIOR
PERSONAL INSIGHT: In our work with our students at CIP (College
Internship Program) centers we encounter a great deal of rigid
thinking patterns that are tied to erroneous premises that these young
men and women have adopted in their minds regarding the “rules of
life.” Many of these premises are logical in the brain of an ASD or LD
student, but irrational when carried out to the “nth degree.” At CIP we
call this “Rule Bound Behavior.” Some examples that we have heard
over the years are things like:
•
Healthy eating means having a totally and completely fat free
diet.
•
Refusing to go to restaurants that serve other “coke-like”
products out of loyalty to a specific brand.
•
Refusing to associate with people who go to theme parks
because “they are all idiots.”
To counteract this thinking in your ASD or LD students, we have
developed what we call our 12-Step Rule Bound Behavior Toolkit!

---
## Page 312
You can assist students by employing the principles of this mini Toolkit
on page 133 to offset rigidity.
BULLYING AND PEER PRESSURE
PERSONAL 
INSIGHT: 
In 
today’s 
world 
bullying 
(including
cyberbullying) and peer pressure are a reality that all schools are
dealing with. For the ASD or LD students in your classroom, the
following statistics are especially shocking and heartbreaking: 65% of
parents report that their children on the ASD Spectrum have been
victimized by peers in some way within the past year. (National Autism
Association)
I would be extremely remiss if I did not include bullying and peer
pressure in the “effective classroom management section” of Chapter
4. To begin this section and to give a real-time and place example for
readers, I’d like to quote from Adria Nassim’s story: “Living and Loving
Life Outside The Walls.” Adria is a former CIP student and her full-
length story appears in Chapter 1. This is what Adria wrote:
“I was getting ready to begin my senior year of college at Brescia
University, a small, Catholic campus in Owensboro, Kentucky where I
was getting a BA in English and Spanish. While I was doing quite well
academically, I became unintentionally involved in an incident of severe
bullying which later resulted in my parents and I deciding that a
temporary leave of absence and a more supportive environment better
suited to young adults with my specific needs and diagnoses
(Asperger’s Disorder, mild impairment, formerly Asperger’s Syndrome,
also: nonverbal learning disability) would be the best course of action
rather than remaining on a traditional college campus.”
Educators and the clinicians and professionals who assist students
can help the statistics change by knowing what the “warning signs” of
bullying are and then ensuring that all students are aware of them.
Adopting a “zero tolerance policy” and an “open door policy,” where

---
## Page 313
students can approach “you” or “others who guide them” if they are
being bullied, is also part of the solution.
Classroom activities for implementing awareness, ideas for policies,
as well as peer mediation programs, and other strategies will be
presented in more depth in Chapter 7 – Imparting Ethical Values and
Other Kinds of Values. In the meantime, here are the warning signs of
bullying and some information on why students won’t tell others they
are being bullied.
The Warning Signs of Bullying
Aside from physical abuse, bullying can also include verbal abuse
(taunting, teasing, and name-calling), emotional abuse (making
someone the subject of rumors, social exclusion, and intimidation), and
cyberbullying (intimidation or harassment via text messaging or social
media).

---
## Page 314
According to the American Academy of Child and Adolescent
Psychiatry, at least half of all school-aged children will experience some
form of bullying in their lives, with 10% of children being bullied
regularly.
Here are the warning signs of those being bullied and those who do the
bullying. This list has been adapted for this Teaching Toolkit from the
United States Government website: www.stopbullying.gov.
Signs that a Student Is Being Bullied
•
Unexplainable injuries.
•
Lost or destroyed clothing, books, electronics, or jewelry.
•
Frequent headaches or stomach aches, feeling sick, or faking
illness.
•
Changes in eating habits, like suddenly skipping meals or binge
eating.
•
Declining grades, loss of interest in school work, or not wanting
to go or be at school.
•
Sudden loss of friends or avoidance of social situations.
•
Expressed feelings of helplessness or decreased self-esteem.
•
Self-destructive behaviors such as running away from home,
harming themselves, or talking about suicide.
Signs that a Student Is Bullying Others
•
Gets into physical or verbal fights.
•
Has friends who bully others.
•
Is increasingly aggressive.
•
Gets sent to the principal’s office or to detention frequently.

---
## Page 315
•
Has unexplained extra money or new belongings.
•
Blames others for their problems.
•
Doesn’t accept responsibility for their actions.
•
Is extremely competitive and worries about his or her reputation
or popularity.
Both of these lists were created by the United States Government to
build awareness of bullying at school in the United States. The
guidelines were written to apply to the general population and are
relevant for all age ranges. The stopbullying.gov website is a great
website that all students (and those who work with them) should be
aware of.
Why Students Won’t Ask for Help!
Here are some reasons why your students do not seek help:
•
Bullying can make students feel helpless. Students (especially
adolescents and young adults) may want to handle it on their
own to feel in control again. They may fear being seen as weak
or a tattletale.
•
Students may fear backlash from the kid who bullied them.
•
Bullying can be a humiliating experience. Students may not want
adults to know what is being said about them, whether true or
false. They may also fear that adults will judge them or punish
them for being weak.
•
Students who are bullied may already feel socially isolated. They
may feel like no one cares or could understand.
•
Students may fear being rejected by their peers.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Residing on the ASD/LD Spectrum myself, I
was a child who never fit in, who was different from my peers, and I
experienced bullying in various forms during my school years. While I
eventually learned that “bullies always crumble when you stand your

---
## Page 316
ground,” awareness, during these painful years or even to know that
this abuse had a name and a set of criteria, would have been
incredibly helpful. As educators, our role is to put this awareness out
there and to hold students who bully others fully accountable.
The final section on “effective classroom management” ends with a
handout that will be helpful as you continue to teach and prepare
students to transition into the next phase of their lives. The “Problem
Solving Guide” gives educators, and the professionals who work with
them, “help” when things don’t go as planned.
GUIDELINES FOR ASSISTING A STUDENT
TO UNDERSTAND AND ALTER A PROBLEM
SITUATION
Students on the ASD/LD Spectrum really are operating within their best
efforts to adapt in a mainstreamed or dedicated classroom – under
difficult conditions – with slow processing speeds and a lack of social
competencies.
As you work with your students you will notice that there is either a lack
of social understanding and interest in problem solving; or conversely,
excesses in interests in other areas which conflict with problem
resolution. The pragmatics (language for social communication
purposes) may be the issue. Because of a student’s high levels of
competence and ability in other areas, teachers often assume that their
ASD or LD students have a higher degree of communicative
competence than actually exists. One of the common errors is
mistaking problems in communication/social understanding for
problems in behavior.
Here are some reasons why a student may be experiencing or creating
a problem situation.
•
Sensory 
issues 
characterized 
by 
oversensitivity 
or
undersensitivity producing avoidance behaviors.

---
## Page 317
•
“Theory of Mind” issues which interfere with a student’s ability
to size up a situation.
•
Slow or deficient processing which can lead to problems with
retrieval and use of information.
•
“Executive Function” issues which create difficulties with:
problem solving, mental planning, organizational skills, self-
regulation, and application of skills.
It is easy to see the reactions of students on the ASD/LD Spectrum as
willful or volitional and to judge their motives.
The following guide on page 139 is designed to give those working with
ASD/LD adolescents and young adults a teaching tool for:
•
Analyzing a problem situation with a student.
•
Finding the critical variables which are affecting the situation.
•
Formulating a proactive educational plan to assist the student in
understanding and taking action to alleviate the situation.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Effective classroom management allows
students on the ASD/LD Spectrum to receive the structure,
accommodations, and understanding they need in order to cope in
any learning environment. At some point, as students obtain skills for
school, and meet academic goals; time will pass as it usually does.
As the last high school year ends, a good percentage of your ASD or
LD students will want to transition to the next and more adult stage of
their lives. For some this will be post-secondary education, specifically
college.
The concluding section of Chapter 4 addresses looking at the best
ways educators and the support professionals who assist them can
help students and their families realistically prepare for a transition to
college or look at other options.
Helping your students understand the differences between high school
and college can ease this process and ease their anxieties. It can
also be very rewarding for you as an educator or support professional

---
## Page 318
as it gives everyone a chance to reflect on the milestones and
achievements made during the past four years of high school.
In the subsection: “Ready or Not, Here I Come!” these differences are
stated in terms that you, your students, and their parents can easily
understand.
This understanding leads into a short piece written by my son Dan
McManmon. Dan talks about what happens when a student does not
do well in the first year of college, why this occurs, and what the next
steps might be. I feel this is a good way to end this chapter as parents
will often turn back to their son’s or daughter’s high school teacher,
mentor, or support professional for advice, especially when things
don’t go as planned.
Autism and Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit) then moves on to Chapter 5 – Competency 3: Skills for Work.
Teachers, clinicians, and support professionals will help students
merge the skills and concepts learned in the Skills for Life and Skills
for School chapters as these young men and women prepare to look
at the world of work and life beyond high school.

Which of the factors below can be utilized in
developing an action plan to assist the student in
learning how to handle future situations like this
one?
1. General use of visual supports (e.g., transition markers;

2. Visual supplementation vis-à-vis task presentation.
3. Reduce the degree of auditory load.
4. Reduce the presence of environmental stressors.

© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Ready or Not, Here I Come!
The United States freshman college or university dropout rate for students
on the ASD Spectrum is 25–30%. The factors that contribute to these
statistics are as follows (National Autism Association, United States):
Young adults on the ASD Spectrum make many false assumptions about
what college will be and what will be required of them.
Many students believe:

---
## Page 325
•
Their reading skills are adequate.
•
Their classes will be similar to high school in terms of what is
needed to do well.
•
They will have a lot of free time since they only have four or five
classes a day.
•
Faculty will keep track of them and reach out to them if they are
not performing well.
Explain to your students that many of them, lack academic readiness,
especially the reading skills that college requires. Students need to be able
to:
•
Read 15–20 chapters in college textbooks.
•
Do and comprehend readings from handouts.
•
Do research papers for more than one class.
Here’s a great handout (on page 143) to give to your ASD or LD students
who are considering college or attending a university.
Helping students prepare to transition out of high school needs to be a
team effort between teachers, the school’s transitional or support
personnel and services, your students, and their families.
Students can then work with you, and the school, to prepare for academic
supports, accommodations, extra tutoring, or even to take a gap year to
strengthen academic and social skills as each individual prepares to leave
the high school setting.
Here are some ways you might help:
Students with an on the Spectrum diagnosis that are considering college
will need more time, more understanding, and more support. Encourage
students and their families to consider:
•
Taking an extra year after high school to mature and develop
college readiness.
Explain that:

---
## Page 326
•
Because ASD or LD students often lack maturity (an 18-year-old
ASD/LD young adult can be developmentally at the stage of a 13-
or 14-year-old) it will be difficult emotionally not to have a like-
minded social group.
•
Since students often look to this like-minded group of peers for
validation, not having validation can lead to anxiety and low self-
esteem at college.
SELF-AWARENESS AND REALISTIC CHOICES
In order to best help students and their parents with a transitioning
strategy, educators and support professionals need to determine:
How realistic are the student and his or her parents about readiness?
How might you help them see the benefit of other options?
These might be:
•
Volunteer work and internships.
•
Gap year.
•
Art, theatre, or programs with a focus on creativity.
•
Community college or a two-year college.
•
Certificate or Trade programs.
The Differences Between High School and College
HIGH SCHOOL:
COLLEGE:
Six hours/day x
180 days a year
= 1080 hours
12 hours/week x
28 weeks a years
= 336 hours

© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Here’s a thought provoking questionnaire and information sheet to use as
you work with an individual student as he or she makes plans for post-
secondary education.
Questions to Ask Students Regarding Realistic
Expectations about Attending College
1. Do you consider yourself an independent learner? If not, what is
your plan for becoming one?
2. Do you know how to read for college (it’s different than reading
for high school)?
3. Do you have a system for taking class notes? What if the lecture
was three hours long and the instructor talked too fast; could
you keep up?

---
## Page 328
4. Did you know that missing one class in college is often like
missing an entire week in high school?
Talk about the Big Leap!           Talk about
Taking a Gap Year!
Explain that a “gap year” can ease the transition from high school
to college. Compare it to taking a half-step instead of a full step into
adulthood! Talk about how it builds confidence for being able to
handle the adult world, just fine.
Explain the benefits of a gap year:
•
Especially for students who learn differently and/or are young or
immature.
•
That it is especially good for students who might feel completely
depleted and need a break after high school.
•
That it is perfect for students who are challenged by traditional
classroom learning.
•
That it allows for experiential exploration of interests, which will
boost an individual’s excitement about learning through the gap
year experience.
The research presented here about the “gap year” was adapted
from Holly Bull’s website. Please take a look at her amazing
website to explore this option with your students and their families:
www.interimprograms.com.
And lastly, ask parents to consider having their son or daughter
take: College Readiness Assessments.
Here are two excellent resource websites that you, your students,
and their families can explore as you help students transition out of
high school and on to success as they prepare for work and for
independent life and above all, happy and rewarding lives.
