PERSONAL INSIGHT: As mentioned in the beginning of this section of
this chapter, not every student who transitions to college will be
successful. Parents and students who have built good and trusting
relationships with teachers, mentors, and transitional counselors during
the high school years will often turn to them when this situation occurs.
To help you help your former students when this happens, my son Dan
wrote this piece for CIP’s blog to help our staff at CIP assist parents and
students as they assess the situation and go on to the next step. Dan’s
piece is a good way to conclude this chapter.
Helping Students and Their Families After an
Unsuccessful College Experience
Educators, clinicians, and support professionals can help students
and their families when a student has an unsuccessful college
experience in many ways.
For a student, finding out that he or she is failing his or her college
courses, can be a scary thing. The families of these young men
and women often feel there is little to no option after their son or
daughter has had an unsuccessful college experience. Oftentimes,
they will turn to the educators, clinicians, and professionals who
helped their son or daughter in high school or post high school for

---
## Page 330
advice. Here are some things you can do to assist a student and
his or her family when they turn to you for help.
Explain that many students are ill-equipped to manage the
transition to college, even after making great strides during high
school. When students experience failure, it’s important to help
students get back on their feet as soon as possible and turn those
distressful and disheartening times into a tangible and realistic
plan of action for everyone involved in the process.
Acknowledge that the parents asking you for help want their child
to have a real college experience; living with peers, studying in a
field of interest, and learning to manage life’s lessons as they
come. Then go on to explain that there needs to be several layers
of adaptation for an ASD or LD student that need to occur prior to
such a major change in environment. Acknowledge that many
young adults enter the college environment ill-prepared in spite of
the transitional services offered during high school. And that now
is the time to come up with a “Plan B.”
When parents ask you what options are available, the quick
answer is “plenty.” From support programs developed at colleges
to residential or gap year programs, there exist many solutions
depending on who the parents and students talk to. Explain that
the most important part of making a well-planned transition back
into college is to base the transition on realistic goals.
Dan McManmon, CIP President
TEACHING INSIGHT: Helping students on the ASD and LD Spectrum
prepare to graduate from high school gives each one of you an
opportunity to take the knowledge you have gleaned in this chapter one
final step further: exploring the world of work via internships, vocational
training, service learning, volunteer work, and part-time or full-time jobs.
Chapter 5 – Competency 3: Skills for Work looks at the world of work,
and how to help the adolescents and young adults you teach, mentor,

---
## Page 331
and advise acquire the skills and abilities needed for this part of their
lives. Not everyone will choose secondary education. Some will choose
working right out of high school and others will delay it until they have
finished a vocational training program or college. Either way, the Skills for
Work chapter will help the educators, clinicians, and professionals who
work with these young men and women take the knowledge gleaned in
the previous skills chapters (life and school) and apply these concepts,
while obtaining others, to the world of work as they prepare these young
men and women for jobs and careers.
Making It Real
WHAT?
Teachers and support professionals help ASD and LD
adolescents and young adults acquire the core elements
needed for successful academic achievement.
Students learn how to manage time, be organized, and how to
set and reach realistic academic goals.
Students learn to accept their diagnosis and advocate for
themselves (disclosure) and how to request classroom
accommodations.
Teachers and support professionals learn effective classroom
management techniques.
HOW?
ASD and LD students build on their academic success by
creating a portfolio of their work and a record of achievement as
they think about the wider world of post-secondary education or
work. They develop “Person Centered Plans” (PCPs) that take
into consideration “special interests,” talents, and abilities.
Time spent on implementing and integrating these teaching
approaches, principles, ideas, and materials into each learning

---
## Page 332
opportunity can produce great results and allow these young
men and women to move forward at a quicker pace.
BENEFITS?
Skills for school strategies provide comprehensive academic
support that builds success and moves each ASD or LD student
toward high (secondary) school graduation.
Students develop individual approaches to succeed in school
with measurable outcomes and realistic goals.
Teachers and support professionals embrace inclusion in a
managed classroom environment.
Building 
an 
inclusive, 
respectful, 
conducive 
learning
environment (whether dedicated or mainstreamed) helps to
establish workability with ASD or LD adolescent and young
adult students.

---

## Page 334
“There are three types of people in the
world: Those who have to be told what
to do and then grudgingly do it. Those
who have to be told what to do and do
it because they know it is right. And . . .
those who are self-motivated and just
do it! Which one are you?”
Michael P. McManmon
“Whether you believe you can, or
whether you believe you can’t, you’re
absolutely right.”
Henry Ford
“Even if you are on the right track, you
will get run over if you just stand there”.
Will Rogers
“It’s hard to aspire to be something
when you don’t see people in that role
who look like or act like you.”
Justin Edmond, Designer Pinterest Employee, San Francisco

---
## Page 335
Triage for Teachers
“Constantly look for opportunities to increase perseverance, a
necessary skill to be successful at a job. Do not shield students
from all failures or challenges or they will not learn how to
recover when they face them.”
Jennifer Kolarik, CIP Brevard
PERSONAL INSIGHT: When I was in junior high school my mom
helped me get a paper route. She explained that all of my brothers
had paper routes at my age (12) and that I was expected to have one
too. She explained that it would teach me the value of a good work
ethic and allow me to earn some money. As part of the deal, I was to
pay for the family’s daily paper out of the money I earned and
contribute some of my money toward the family expenses. With seven
brothers and sisters, it was clear that I was expected to do my share.
Things have certainly changed since that time and place. Today’s
world of work is challenging and jobs can be hard to come by for
those not “on the Spectrum” let alone for those who are. Therefore, it
is necessary to prepare adolescents and young adults who reside on
the ASD or LD Spectrum with a solid academic, social, and emotional
skill set as they begin to explore volunteering, internships, part-time or
full-time work, a gap year, or further career preparation and training.
Chapter 5 – Competency 3: Skills for Work will allow educators,
clinicians, and support professionals to help these young men and
women acquire skills that will allow each one to enter the world of
work.
With unemployment and underemployment among young adults in the
United States with an ASD (Autism Spectrum Disorder) diagnosis
hovering around 90% and those with an LD (Learning Differences)
diagnosis hovering at 25% to 50%, the importance of imparting skills

---
## Page 336
for work to adolescents and young adults (aged 16–26) cannot be
overstated. (Statistics from the Autism Speaks 2014 Study)
As educators, clinicians, and support professionals you know how
crucial having and keeping a job will be to each individual’s
development of an adult identity. Having meaningful work promotes
self-esteem, supports a growing social circle, and provides financial
opportunities to those on the ASD or LD Spectrum.
Using a logical, step-by-step approach, Chapter 5 of Autism and
Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit) provides
concepts, strategies, ideas, advice, and classroom materials that will
allow the young men and women you work with to make good choices
about careers and identify further job training opportunities. The Skills
for Work chapter will be especially useful to classroom teachers, career
and transition counselors, occupational therapists, job coaches, social
skills instructors, and academic advisors.
This chapter takes the Skills for Life and the Skills for School
competencies that your students acquired in Chapters 3 and 4 and
applies this foundation to obtaining the eight skill sets that employers
value most.
Chapter 5 also looks at the role that “special interests” can or might
play in relationship to career paths and choices. Doing a skills match
then developing employment tactics such as resume creation,
preparing and practicing for the interview process, and showing
students the importance of career counseling (so that each individual
can understand his or her real abilities vs. grandiose beliefs), are also
included in this chapter.
The examples and resources throughout the chapter can be modified or
integrated into an existing curriculum or used in specific group or
individual counseling settings to help small groups or individuals
prepare for work. The activities, handouts, and worksheets are user
friendly and will help you help the young men and women you work with
understand each section of the chapter before moving on to the next.

---
## Page 337
Looking at the value of an entry level job, internships, volunteer, or
community service will allow those working with adolescents and young
adults (ages 16–26) who reside on the ASD or LD Spectrum to show
each individual how vital it is to understand the workplace culture and
its challenges, but how to integrate gradually into this world. This is
referred to as the “Career Continuum.”
Encouraging students to choose volunteer work, part-time work, and
internships as alternatives to a full-time job will allow these young men
and women to master the core competencies they have developed in
the classroom (during high school, college, or vocational training), and
work toward gaining meaningful, paid, full-time employment later on.
Workplace disclosure, helping students obtain accommodations, and
providing examples of how to support students once they are on the job
through job coaching, shadowing, and support groups are also
included.
Stories, vignettes, teaching tips, and real-time examples from CIP
(College Internship Program) staff members round out the Skills for
Work chapter and lend a further helping hand as adolescents and
young adults who reside on the ASD or LD Spectrum prepare to enter
the workplace arena.
Chapter 5 is divided into six subsections: (I) Employability Skills … The
Basics, (II) The Career Continuum, (III) Exploring Career Choices, (IV)
Resume Creation and Preparing for Interviews, (V) Self-Advocacy,
Workplace Disclosure, and Accommodations, and (VI) Supporting
Students While Working.
Imparting the information in this chapter in this order will ensure that the
young men and women you work with obtain the skills needed so that
they can be competent and confident as they enter the workplace
arena. It will also give you easy access to sections that you need to
refer to for additional instruction or reinforcement as problems with
individuals arise.
Dr. Temple Grandin is a professor in animal science at Colorado State
University. She has developed widely used methods for reducing the

---
## Page 338
anxiety of cattle during livestock handling processes. Her advice to
educators and students is timely as you and your students delve into
Chapter 5. Her advice is a good way to preface the “Employability
Skills … The Basics” section of this chapter.
Dr. Grandin’s words come from part of an interview she gave to Daniel
Kelly from the Belleville (Illinois) News-Democrat in February, 2014.
GET A JOB!
BY DANIEL KELLEY
Nationally known autism expert Temple Grandin has a strong
message for Belleville, Illinois students: “Get a job.”
Grandin was diagnosed with autism in 1950. Her success in animal
science and autism education led her life to become the subject of a
documentary film on HBO and a major motion picture with actress
Claire Danes portraying Temple Grandin.
“Too many smart kids on the Autism Spectrum are 21 years old,
playing video games all day, and their parents have them on Social
Security,” Grandin said.
“We’ve got to get these kids working and it has to start with the local
high school. Students diagnosed with Autism and Asperger’s
Syndrome need work skills to compete for jobs,” Grandin said.

---
## Page 339
Grandin also emphasized the importance of such students obtaining
these skills by volunteering or seeking internships and then applying
for and getting a job. She then went on to say: “When I was 15, I
worked on a horse farm. I cleaned out eight stalls every day. I fed
them. If they kicked a hole in the barn, I fixed it. I was very proud of
having this adult responsibility. Kids are not learning enough of these
things.”
Grandin then gave Belleville students further career-centric advice
tailored for those on the ASD Spectrum; for example, how to succeed
during job interviews even though some students with Autism or
Asperger’s struggle with communication skills.
“You have to circumvent the regular interview process,” Grandin said.
“If you are interviewing for programming, show off your programming
skills. If you are interviewing in the sciences, get some photos of your
science experiments. You have to show off what you can do because
your interview skills are most probably going to be lacking.”
“Basic work readiness skills prepare students to tackle the general
duties of any job. Teaching those skills can help students secure work
and keep the job.”

---
## Page 340
(“Get a Job” was adapted from an interview given to the Southern
Illinoisan in March 2014 by Temple Grandin during her speaking
engagement at Belleville High School in Belleville, Illinois.)
Section I: Employability Skills …
The Basics!
Employability skills are the general skills that young adults on the ASD
or LD Spectrum will need to have in order to get most jobs. Practicing,
developing, and then acquiring these skills will help the young men and
women you work with stay in a job and begin to develop skills to
advance in the workplace or explore options in other directions.
While there will always be some job-specific skills that an employer is
looking for, most employers will want all of their employees (not just
those with an ASD or an LD diagnosis) to have learned the basics
before entering the workplace.
Students on the ASD or LD Spectrum need to be taught these skills
(preferably in small groups), and then have the knowledge and the
concepts behind the knowledge reinforced through visuals and
examples, over and over again until students are proficient.
Practicing each employability skill subsection (there are eight of them),
through role play, group outings, and social activities is essential and
will provide your students with opportunities for realistic participatory
instruction that will allow for retention through repetition.
WHAT EMPLOYERS WANT
The eight employability skills that employers look for are:
1. Communication.
2. Teamwork.

---
## Page 341
3. Problem Solving (Using Critical Thinking Skills).
4. Initiative and Leadership.
5. Planning and Organizing.
6. Self-Management.
7. Willingness to Learn.
8. Technology.
WHAT EMPLOYERS WANT: #1 –
COMMUNICATION
The young people you work with will have to learn to be good talkers
and maybe good writers as well (depending on the job they are
applying for). They will need to be confident about speaking to people
face-to-face or over the phone, and they will need to be able to write
well enough to be understood in emails and memos.
This means having good verbal and written skills, as well as the ability
to listen. It’s about being clear, concise, and focused. It’s also about
being able to tailor a message for a specific audience and then being
able to comprehend the views and viewpoints of others. The following
information, handouts, and activities will help the young men and
women you work with obtain these essential communication skills.
Effective Workplace Communication Skills
Explain that in any line of work, effective communication is essential. In
a single work day, an employee may have to communicate with at least
six different people, including employers, co-workers, clients, suppliers,
and the general public.
Ask your students to brainstorm what they think effective
communication in the workplace is and write these on the left side of a
white board. Then write on the right side of the list what are

---
## Page 342
considered by employers to be the most important workplace
communication skills each person will need to acquire. Compare the
lists then go on to explain why the eight things on the right side are the
ones employers are looking for.
1. Being clear and being direct:
•
Explain that in today’s world, language is often veiled in double-
speak, slang, assumptions, and vagaries.
•
Tell students that unless they are clear and direct with the
people they report to or work with – colleagues, co-workers, and
supervisors will not be able to understand them. Therefore, it is
necessary when speaking with others at work to be clear, use
correct grammar, and maintain a friendly, even tone of voice.
2. Getting to the point … quickly!
•
Explain to your students that you know how much they value
their time; therefore, in life and especially in the workplace, it is
only right that they should value the time of others. Explain that
the people they will work with (including their supervisors) will be
more responsive if they are direct and concise.
•
Tell the young men and women you are coaching that if they
continuously say too much at work their listeners may tune out,
let their minds wander, and might miss the most crucial points
they are trying to get across.
3. Being personal:
•
Explain that getting to the point, however, does not mean they
should communicate in a cold, cursory manner. Letting the
person being talked to know that the speaker cares about them
as an individual is very important.
•
Tell your students that an easy way to do this is to let the
communication take the other person’s concerns into account.

---
## Page 343
The person they are speaking to will then know that their input is
important to the person who initiated the conversation.
4. Listening:
•
Explain that communication is not a one-way street and that
each person in a conversation has to be willing to listen as well
as to speak.
5. Thinking before speaking:
•
Explain that thinking before speaking reminds each and every
one of us not to rush into words, but to think about what we say
before we say it. If your students can learn to do this, they can
avoid misunderstandings and … in moments of annoyance, they
can avoid saying something they might later regret.
6. Not being overly negative:
•
Explain that it is necessary to expose and tackle problem
situations in order to solve them. However, when communicating
in the workplace, they need to take the time to accentuate the
positive. By being overly negative, they can destroy the other
person’s desire to help or assist in solving the problem.
•
Tell your students that they can avoid being overly negative by
pointing out one or two positives to balance every negative
statement they make. This will help them reach problem solving
or communication goals faster.

---
## Page 344
The student handout below and on page 152 will help your students
understand how communicating in the workplace differs from
communicating with their families, friends, or teachers.
Triage for Teachers
“Always look at each challenge faced by a student as a learning
opportunity. Brainstorm options quickly and be willing to adapt to
circumstances that arise.”
Jennifer Kolarik, CIP Brevard
Effective Workplace Communication

---
## Page 345
Here are the best methods to use when communicating with
others in any workplace setting. Your supervisor, co-workers,
and work team members will understand what you have to say,
they will be clear about your intent, and they will appreciate your
directness and clarity.
One-on-one:
•
Experts have been able to prove that some people
understand you better when you take them aside and talk to
them on a one-on-one basis. Ensure that you maintain eye
contact with the person you are talking to so that your
words or the message you are trying to convey sinks in.
Emails:
•
In most settings, communicating with co-workers and
supervisors by sending an email is usually the norm. Emails
will enable you to pass messages to people you work with
or report to without having to pull them out of their work
stations or offices.
Presentations:
•
Some people grasp messages more easily when pictures
and sounds are involved. Using presentations like Microsoft
PowerPoint to communicate with your team, your boss, and
your co-workers gives them the opportunity to refer back to
it if they aren’t clear about certain things.
Displaying confidence and seriousness:
•
Display confidence and seriousness to ensure that you will not
be taken for granted. When your boss or co-workers notice any
uncertainty and lack of seriousness on your part when you’re
communicating with them, they are likely to treat the information
with disdain or disregard.
Using simple words:

---
## Page 346
•
The truth is that everybody cannot be on the same page
when it comes to vocabulary. Therefore, to be effective in
your communications with others at work, use words that
can be easily understood.
Listening to your co-workers and team members:
•
Communication is intended to be a two-way street. Don’t
just talk without listening to anyone else. Encourage others
to speak so that you can be well guided when
communicating in the future with your workplace co-workers
and colleagues. Listen more than you speak.
Using appropriate body language:
•
Your body language will help convey and reinforce your
verbal message to help others understand what you are
trying to say.
•
Master the art of using body language when communicating
with your boss, co-workers, and team members. Stand/sit
up straight; use smiles, handshakes, and eye contact.
Practice these skills on your teachers, classmates, families,
and friends.
Using an appropriate tone of voice:
•
One word can mean a different thing when said in a
different tone of voice. It’s important that you use an
appropriate tone of voice to communicate your message to
others so that you won’t be misunderstood or discourage
and de-motivate others.
Avoiding unnecessary repetition:
•
If you want your co-workers or team members to take you
seriously, never sound like a broken record. Tell the people
you work with (clearly and succinctly), what you want them
to know or do and ask them if they are clear about it. If they
are not, then you may repeat what you previously said.

---
## Page 347
Creating a receptive atmosphere:
•
To effectively communicate with others, you need to create
a receptive and positive atmosphere. Avoid a tense
environment at all costs because when you communicate in
an overly intense manner, the message you are trying to
share might not be understood or retained.
Being humorous:
•
If you can, display a sense of humor when communicating
with your co-workers. This will help pass your message
along 
in 
a 
more 
relaxed 
way. 
This 
method 
of
communication has been proven to be a highly effective
way of eliminating or relieving tension. When the
atmosphere is unfriendly and intense, being humorous often
does the trick. If you use jokes – please don’t overdo it – by
remembering that you are not a stand-up comedian.
Being articulate:
•
Communication is a skill that must be learned by all of us
and needs to be practiced. Being articulate when talking
with the people you work with makes it easier for them to
understand your message.
Avoiding mumbling:
•
People need to be able to hear you clearly. When
communicating with them, speak clearly and do not
mumble words. When you mumble words or speak too
quickly, it shows a lack of confidence on your part.
Encouraging feedback:
•
Don’t just talk and walk away. Give room for feedback so
that you can measure the responses of others. This will also
help you to know if your message was understood.
Gesticulating:

---
## Page 348
•
Means using your hands to demonstrate your message.
When you gesticulate you make hand motions and signals
to establish the seriousness of your subject matter when
communicating with co-workers or those you report to. This
too, needs moderation, as you don’t want your hand
motions to become too exaggerated or intense.
Being appreciative:
•
After every communication session, remember to thank your
listeners for their time. It’s respectful and a simple courtesy
that you can extend to others.
And remember …
•
You need to work hard on obtaining and then practicing all
of the workplace communication skills on this worksheet.
Practice on your friends, schoolmates, teachers, and family
members so that you are comfortable portraying all of these
skills in your workplace setting.
Effective communication will make your everyday worklife and
life in general easier. So … be clear, get to the point, be
personal, listen, think before you speak, avoid extreme
negativity, and – most of all – enjoy being a good communicator
at work.
Here are some classroom or small group session activities that
can be used to reinforce the concepts in the “Effective
Workplace Communication” handout.
(A) COMMUNICATION SKILLS PRACTICE
Instructions: Role play and practice oral communication skills
face to face (in groups of two to six), then move on to practicing
reciprocal mock telephone conversations.

---
## Page 349
•
Have participants focus on clear and concise statements
instead of fragments or overly long sentences.
•
Start with simple exercises, such as asking students to
describe a painting in three sentences. Other ideas for this
activity might be describing the weather outside the room
window, how to boil an egg, how to brush your teeth, how to
sit on a chair, the contents of a purse or pocket; anything that
can get the students expressing ideas clearly and efficiently
in short descriptive sentences.
•
Do this simple activity to reinforce these concepts. Divide
students into small groups. Everyone or introduces his or her
self and talks about their likes, dislikes, and their interests
only in three to five sentences. Give students a couple of
minutes to think about this, prepare, and then participate in
the activity.
•
Have students practice talking to each other in a non-
threatening but information gathering way. Ask the group or
class to line up by their birth month and day starting with Jan
1 and ending with Dec 31. This will give students a chance
to seek information (in this case, the month and day of each
person’s birth) from each other without feeling put “on the
spot.”
•
Have students practice having short, succinct, and
meaningful workplace phone conversations such as calling
in sick or letting a supervisor know they will be late. Students
might role play confirming a job interview date, or verifying
days and hours they will be working that week, or asking for
permission to leave work early. If needed, practice telephone
manners and protocol (originally learned in the Skills for Life
chapter), such as introducing themselves first then talking
about the reason for the call.
(B) WRITING SKILLS PRACTICE

---
## Page 350
Talk about business or work related email writing etiquette, as
well as the etiquette used in formal business and work
correspondence.
Stress the importance of proper sentence structure and
paragraph formation for both methods. Focus on formal
business 
communication 
and 
teach 
your 
students 
the
importance of conveying the tone of the message with written
words.
Practice writing an introductory business letter. Students might
use this opportunity to write a description of their interests and
their skills to a prospective or potential employer. The point of
this writing practice is to use the communication skills they have
been learning about to describe their best qualities and what
they have to offer a future employer. This will get them thinking
about what they are good at and what they are interested in for
use later in the chapter when resumes and cover letters are
prepared.
Participants will then take the information in their letters and
compose a short concise email using a condensed version of
the information. Email is a practical and time-saving way of
communicating in the workplace; however, it takes practice to
maintain a professional and formal tone for this type of
correspondence.
Explain that emailing is different from letter writing and that it’s
important to make sentences and paragraphs shorter, use bullet
points, and summarize content efficiently and effectively.
(C) LISTENING SKILLS PRACTICE
Divide students into small groups of four to six people. A
spokesperson is chosen and this person will role play a
supervisor, boss, or manager talking to a small group of

---
## Page 351
employees about various, ongoing “issues” at work. The
scenarios the spokesperson will be describing to the listener are
realistic and portray things that happen in real workplace
settings. The spokesperson is not happy and wants to make
sure each person has heard and understands what she or he
has said and/or is talking about.
At the end of the presentation, the group members will give the
spokesperson a summary of what he or she said. Listeners can
formulate their own opinions, questions, and thoughts on the
topic while listening but will need to keep these to themselves.
The important thing is for each listener to repeat back to what
the supervisor/boss/manager (spokesperson role) is saying so
that this person understands what he or she was saying.
•
The spokesperson (boss/supervisor/manager) will talk to the
group about why it is important to be on time for work each
day and slide in the fact that some people are showing up
late each day and that other employees have noticed. He or
she will offer some suggestions as to how he or she will
handle lateness in the future. The spokesperson does not
want to single anyone out but he or she wants to make sure
that people understand the importance of being on time and
putting in a full day’s work for a full day’s pay.
•
The spokesperson (boss/supervisor/manager) will talk to the
group about why it’s important for employees who work at
his or her business to practice and have good hygiene before
coming to work. He or she will talk about the importance of
wearing clean clothes and be freshly groomed every day.
This spokesperson will mention that some people have been
coming to work looking untidy and that it is clear they are
wearing the same clothes day after day. He or she might
mention that other employees have been seen brushing their
teeth, putting on make-up, and blow-drying wet hair in the
bathroom after clocking in.

---
## Page 352
•
The spokesperson (boss/supervisor/manager) will talk about
the importance of greeting all customers who come into the
store each day, being polite to each one who is shopping in
the store, and making sure employees do everything
possible to be attentive and helpful. He or she will talk about
the fact that the employees on the sales floor or those behind
the counter have been texting while they are on duty and that
this activity goes on in front of customers on a regular basis.
Complaints are coming from customers that employees are
more interested in texting their friends instead of providing
good customer service to them. The spokesperson will talk
about changes he or she plans to make in the workplace
setting that will improve this situation.
I SAY … YOU SAY
Have your students brainstorm and make a list on a white board
of realistic topics or scenarios that might come up in a
workplace setting or use some of the topics listed at the end of
the instructions.
Pair students together; one person is A and the other one is B.
Tell your students that A will choose a topic (from the list on the
white board), and have two timed minutes to talk about or
respond to the topic. At the sound of the timer, roles switch and
B talks or responds to a different topic for two minutes.
Explain that only one partner may speak at a time while the
other one listens and that the listener may not interrupt or butt in.
Reiterate that the listener does not get a chance to respond –
they simply practice listening.
Do the following activity for 10 or 15 minutes then … stop.
Discuss the challenges of being a good listener.

---
## Page 353
•
Upcoming seasonal layoffs and how you hope you are
spared.
•
Having to wear a uniform at work – good thing or bad thing –
state your personal opinion or opinions of co-workers.
•
How hard it is to get from school to work each day and be on
time.
•
What you think of the rule about “no personal emails” or
“surfing the web” during working hours.
•
What you think about having to work on a holiday even
though you get extra pay for the holiday work day. Worth it or
not worth it?
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit

---
## Page 354
NONVERBAL COMMUNICATION SKILLS
Explain to your students that nonverbal communication is the
expression of messages through facial expressions, gestures, and body
language. Nonverbal communication is essential to successful
relationships in the workplace. Examples of this type of communication
include eye contact, posture, and tone of voice.

---
## Page 355
Being able to understand nonverbal communication is important as
65% to 75% of communication (not just in the workplace) is nonverbal.
Tell the young people you work with that nonverbal cues give us
information that helps us make sense of how we feel and what we think
about others.
Explain that people often use nonverbal cues to arrive at judgments
about how they plan to relate to co-workers and even more important;
supervisors may use it to interpret employee performance.
Explain that all nonverbal communication in the workplace is about
appropriately expressing emotions and showing respect.
Examples of Nonverbal Communication
•
Eye contact. In Chapter 3 of this book (the Skills for Life
chapter), 
your 
students 
learned 
how 
to 
communicate
nonverbally in everyday social situations. Review the fact that
this basic life skill is extremely important in the workplace and
comes into play during job interviews, while listening to others at
work, and when being asked to follow instructions.
 
Remind your students that someone who makes eye contact
has open eyes and blinks appropriately. This expresses that he
or she is paying attention.
•
Behaviors such as gesturing dramatically or fidgeting express
nervousness, while speaking loudly and quickly expresses
aggression or excitement.
•
The way we sit, stand, move, how fast or loud we talk, and how
close we stand to another person all send strong signals.
•
Also explain that if they work with others from different countries
or from a different ethnic background, these co-workers may
differ in how loud or softly they speak or may explain that
speaking loudly in their culture is not acceptable.

---
## Page 356
Explain that all of this really does matter. The way we nonverbally
react tells the other person whether or not we are being truthful and
whether or not we are listening. When our nonverbal signals match up
with the words we’re saying, they increase trust, clarity, and rapport.
When they don’t, they generate tension, mistrust, and confusion.
The activity on page 157 will help your students understand how
important body language can be.
But That’s Not What I Meant!
Divide students into pairs. One is A and one is B. Use the
following scenarios – students may also create their own
scenarios from experiences in their own lives – of nonverbal
communication problems. Ask the pairs to talk for a minute
or two among themselves about the scenario they have
chosen and who will play what role.
Have the designated students role play the part of A and B.
Tell students that they might role play the examples in a
casual way, an obvious way, and what might be an
innappropriate way so that they can learn about nuances
and degrees of nonverbal communication.
Stop the activity after five minutes. Ask participants to talk
about their scenario. Have the pairs discuss what the reality
of the situation was, what (if any) misconceptions took place,
and what solution or remedy might occur to resolve the
communication problem. Then gather the class together and
have pairs share with you and the class how things went.
Scenarios:
•
You (role A) think the new person (role B) who started
working in your department at the software
development company does not like you because he

---
## Page 357
doesn’t make eye contact and keeps his distance
from you at all times. You have been avoiding and
purposely ignoring this person and it has become
obvious to your supervisor and others that you are not
making him feel welcome. In fact you avoid him at all
costs.
•
Your boss suggests you talk to the new person and
when you do, you realize the new employee is very
shy, a bit insecure about the new job, and is
uncomfortable talking to new people. This is why he
does not make eye contact and keeps a physical
distance from you. How might both of you work to
change the situation?
•
A co-worker (A) continuously rolls her eyes while
talking to you (B) and your co-workers. It’s a very
deliberate and grandiose expression that is not only
noticed by you – but by everyone who comes in
contact with her. You decide to tell her that this
bothers you. She explains that it is an unconscious
gesture (a bad habit), that she has had since she was
little, that she is a good person, and that she does not
mean anything by it. What might you or both of you do
to help others understand her better? What might
your co-worker do to help herself?
•
You (A) are the supervisor of a dozen people. Each
week you have a staff meeting – and during these
meetings you notice that one person (B) either
fidgets, stares off into space, or slouches so low in his
chair that he looks as though he might fall off it. One
day he even sighs out loud while you are speaking to
your team. While his or her co-workers notice it and
shrug it off, it really bothers you. Discuss various ways
you might handle this. What might you, as the
supervisor, say to the person doing the aggravating

---
## Page 358
behavior? How might you both work together to solve
the problem?
•
You (A) are interviewing for a job that will require you
to greet customers and ask them how you might help
them. During the interview the prospective employer
(B) notices that you do not sit up straight, that you are
not very good at making eye contact, and that when
you stand up – your slouching posture gives him or
her the impression that you do not care about making
a good first impression. When you do not get the job,
you call and ask why. How might the prospective
employer point things out to you that you are not
aware of? How might you learn to help yourself so
that this does not happen again?
Reflection: Tell the class that these were real-life examples
of things that have actually happened in the workplace. Point
out that the purpose of the activity was to become aware of
how nonverbal communication might be interpreted or
misinterpreted in the workplace setting.

---
## Page 359
The second thing employers look for in employees is the ability to work
as part of a group or team. The young men and women you work with
have been learning, in Chapters 1–4 of this book, the importance of
working with others in small groups and being part of a team in the
classroom setting. They have learned to collaborate and have
experienced the various roles that people will take on when they
become part of a group or team, such as the Leader, the Encourager,
the Passive Player, the Team Player (the Cheerleader), or the Hinderer.
These roles also surface in the workplace and the ASD/LD students
you teach, counsel, advise, and mentor will have to be good at working
successfully with each group member, while doing their jobs.
WHAT EMPLOYERS WANT: #2 – TEAMWORK
Tell your students that they’ll need to prove to their employer that they
are a team player and have the ability to do their jobs responsibly and
in harmony with others.
Explain that teamwork is all about building positive working
relationships that help each person meet and achieve goals, as well as
achieve business or company objectives.
Talk about the fact that teamwork also means being able to put things
forward in each person’s own individual way – and being able to
understand where the other people on the team are coming from – so
that they can work with others and feel positive about it.
Use the student handout below to help your students understand what
employers mean when they say, “We are looking for someone who
knows how to work as part of a team!”
Working as Part of a Group or Team!

---
## Page 360
Employers are looking for people who can work as part of a
group or team. They look for people who …
Demonstrate reliability:
•
Employers want to know that they can count on you to
be a reliable team member and a person who does
his or her fair share.
•
Employers need to know you will work hard and meet
your work commitments and if necessary, help others,
so that the team can meet stated goals.
•
Consistency is very important. Your supervisor, or the
people you report to, want to know that they can
count on you to do a good job all the time, not just
some of the time, and that you will not let others
down.
Communicate constructively:
•
Your employer needs to hire people who speak up
and express their thoughts and ideas clearly, directly,
honestly, and with respect to each person on the work
team. That’s what it means to communicate
constructively. Such a team member does not shy
away from making a point but makes it in the best
way possible – in a positive, confident, and respectful
manner.
Actively listen:
•
Good listeners are essential for teams to function
effectively.
•
Teams 
need 
participants 
who 
can 
absorb,
understand, and consider ideas and points of view
from other people without debating or arguing every
point.

---
## Page 361
•
Such a team member can also receive criticism or
feedback without reacting defensively.
Actively participate:
•
Good team players are active participants.
•
They come prepared for team meetings, listen, and
speak up in discussions.
•
They’re fully engaged in the work of the team and do
not sit passively in the meeting and say nothing!
Team members who function as active participants take the
initiative to help make things happen and they volunteer for
assignments. Their whole approach is can-do: “What
contribution can ‘I’ make to help the team achieve success?”
Share openly and willingly:
•
Good team players share. They share information,
knowledge, and experience. They take the initiative to
keep other team members informed.
•
Keep in mind that much of the communication within
teams takes place informally. Beyond discussion at
organized meetings, team members need to feel
comfortable talking with one another and passing
along important news and information on a day-to-day
basis.
•
Good team players keep other team members in the
loop with information and expertise that helps get the
job done and prevents surprises.
Cooperate and pitch in to help:
•
Cooperation is the act of working with others and
acting together to accomplish a job. Effective team
players work this way by second nature.

---
## Page 362
•
Good team players, despite differences they may
have with other team members concerning style and
perspective, figure out ways to work together to solve
problems and get the work done. They respond to
requests for assistance and take the initiative to offer
help.
Exhibit flexibility:
•
Teams often deal with changing conditions – and
often create changes themselves. Good team players
roll with the punches; they adapt to ever-changing
situations. They don’t complain or get stressed out
because something new is being tried or some new
direction is being set.
•
Flexible team members consider different viewpoints
and compromise when needed. They don’t hold
rigidly to their own point of view or argue it to death,
especially when the team needs to move forward to
make a decision or get something done. Strong team
players are firm in their thoughts yet open to what
others have to offer – flexibility at its best.
Show commitment to the team:
•
Strong team players care about the team and the
team’s work. They show up every day with this care
and commitment up front.
Work as problem solvers:
•
Teams, of course, deal with problems. Good team
players are willing to deal with all kinds of problems in
a solutions-oriented manner. They’re problem solvers,
not problem dwellers, problem blamers, or problem
avoiders.

---
## Page 363
•
They don’t put off dealing with issues. Team players
get problems out in the open for discussion and then
collaborate with others to find solutions and form
action plans.
Treat others in a respectful and supportive manner:
•
Team players treat fellow team members with
courtesy and consideration – not just some of the time
– 
but 
consistently. 
In 
addition, 
they 
show
understanding and they appropriate support of other
team members to help get the job done. They don’t
place conditions on when they’ll provide assistance,
when they’ll choose to listen, and when they’ll share
information.
•
Good team players also have a sense of humor and
know how to have fun (and all teams can use a bit of
both), but they don’t have fun at someone else’s
expense.
Did you know?
•
That team players who show commitment to the
people they work for and with don’t come in any
particular style or personality.
•
That team players do not need to be overly outgoing
or demonstrative. In fact, they are often soft-spoken,
but they aren’t passive.
•
That team players care about what the team is doing
and they contribute to its success – without needing
to take and/or receive all the credit or take the ideas
of others.
•
That team players with commitment look beyond their
own personal work and care about the team’s overall
work. In the end, their commitment is about seeing

---
## Page 364
the team succeed and knowing they have contributed
to this success.
•
That winning as “a team” is one of the great
motivators of employee performance. Good team
players have and show this motivation.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
One of the most important things you can do is help students realize the
difference between teamwork at school and teamwork in the
workplace. Being a team player at work means being able to accept
the viewpoints of people they will probably not befriend but will have to
work with, collaborate with, and get along with day-in and day-out.
The following activity can help teach students about effective group
strategies as they practice the cognitive flexibility skills needed to
achieve teamwork and above all, harmony, at work.
Viewpoints
Divide the class into groups of four to six people.
Ask students to form four circles in the four corners of the
classroom or activity setting. Tell the class that each group
will be presented with one of the four scenarios listed below.
They will role play employees in the situation described.
Give a preliminary overview of the problem and let them
know their responses will then play into various viewpoints.
Employee role players should listen to each person’s input
and after everyone has had their say, to reflect on the many
viewpoints that came to light.
Tell the role players that agreeing to disagree is fine, as is
agreeing for the greater good or majority of the group, or they

---
## Page 365
might put the final solution to a vote and majority rules. They
might also employ “The Donkey Rule:” If five people think it’s
a donkey and the person asking the others thinks it’s a
horse, it’s a donkey.
Let them know that in the end, some participants might have
to go with a decision they don’t like by agreeing to disagree
while accepting the majority vote. Explain that all these
options are what being part of a work group or team is all
about.
Focus: It does not matter how stereotyped or superficial the
responses to the scenarios are, it is the cumulative effect and
the awakening of interest that are important in this exercise.
During this exercise, each role player gets to have their say
then a discussion is held that may or may not lead to a
conclusion or a way forward.
Explain that when working as part of a group or team at
work, individuals will be continually challenged by the human
nature of others. This means they will have to work with
people they may have little in common with to make good
decisions for the greater good of the work team.
Scenario A: There is trouble at the cookie factory. Some
employees of the day-shift team feel the factory does not
provide enough training to operate the industrial machinery
and equipment being used to make large batches of cookies,
safely. Others feel that everything is fine as is; they do not
want more training and want the complainers to mind their
own business. A meeting is called and the issue is talked
about.
After listening to each person’s viewpoint – the role players
need to think and discuss how they might reach an
understanding or maybe even a group decision about how to
proceed about this issue. They also need to consider that

---
## Page 366
this problem or concern might be an example of having to
agree to disagree and go with majority rule.
Reflection and discussion: What process was used to
make the final decision? Was it compromise, letting the
majority rule by employing “The Donkey Rule,” or was it
agreeing to disagree and accepting a decision that is not to
the liking of some of the team members? Did the group have
any dissenters who decided to stay out of the plan for
action?
Ask: Could participants (after listening to the viewpoints of
others) be flexible enough to try to understand the viewpoint
of the person they disagreed with?
Scenario B: An older worker and member of your team at
the fast food restaurant you work in has been fired. Some
members of your team think this person was fired because of
his or her age and others think that there must have been
another good reason for the person to have been dismissed.
This topic is all the team on the 5 p.m. to 11 p.m. shift is able
to talk about in between serving customers. Some people
want to go to the boss and demand an explanation, while
others think it is none of their business and trust that their
supervisor had a good reason for letting the person go.
Some people feel they are “owed” an explanation and that
the boss should have talked to everyone before the shift
started.
After listening to each person’s viewpoint – the role players
need to think and discuss how they might reach an
understanding or maybe even a group decision about how to
proceed about this issue. They also need to consider that
this problem or concern might be an example of having to
agree to disagree and go with the majority rule.

---
## Page 367
Reflection and discussion: What process was used to
make the final decision? Was it compromise, letting the
majority rule by employing “The Donkey Rule,” or was it
agreeing to disagree and accepting a decision that is not to
the liking of some of the team members? Did the group have
any dissenters who decided to stay out of the plan for
action?
Ask: Could participants (after listening to the viewpoints of
others) be flexible enough to try to understand the viewpoint
of the person they disagreed with?
Scenario C: Your team works at a car wash. Some
members of the day 8 a.m. to 4 p.m. shift want to go to the
boss and ask for more money. Others are afraid to do this
because the car wash has been losing money and rumors
are going around about lay-offs. It turns out that there is no
majority opinion when the team members meet after work to
talk about it. Half think they should go to the boss and half
are happy with things as they are.
After listening to each person’s viewpoint – the role players
need to think and discuss how they might reach an
understanding or maybe even a group decision about how to
proceed about this issue. They also need to consider that
this problem or concern might be an example of having to
agree to disagree and go with the majority rule.
Reflection and discussion: What process was used to
make the final decision? Was it compromise, letting the
majority rule by employing “The Donkey Rule,” or was it
agreeing to disagree and accepting a decision that is not to
the liking of some of the team members? Did the group have
any dissenters who decided to stay out of the plan for
action?
Ask: Could participants (after listening to the viewpoints of
others) be flexible enough to try to understand the viewpoint

---
## Page 368
of the person they disagreed with?
Scenario D: The staff room at the big box store where you
and your co-workers work is dirty and depressing. The walls
are painted grey and the furniture is falling apart. Sometimes
the microwave works, sometimes it doesn’t. Some people on
your team (you all work in the electronics department) want
to go to the department manager and ask for better
conditions – specifically: a new microwave and newer, nicer
furniture. These people also want to volunteer to buy paint
and to repaint the room after hours in exchange for the
manager’s goodwill in making the necessary changes. Some
people from your team (while agreeing that the staff room is
horrible) feel very under-paid and under-appreciated and
don’t want to give their free time to the store or to the
manager or chip in for the paint. They are adamant that they
will not be part of any redecorating project.
After listening to each person’s viewpoint – the role players
need to think and discuss how they might reach an
understanding or maybe even a group decision about how to
proceed about this issue. They also need to consider that
this problem or concern might be an example of having to
agree to disagree and go with the majority rule.
Reflection and discussion: What process was used to
make the final decision? Was it compromise, letting the
majority rule by employing “The Donkey Rule,” or was it
agreeing to disagree and accepting a decision that is not to
the liking of some of the team members? Did the group have
any dissenters who decided to stay out of the plan for
action?
Ask: Could participants (after listening to the viewpoints of
others) be flexible enough to try to understand the viewpoint
of the person they disagreed with?

---
## Page 369
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
The Concise Oxford Dictionary defines a problem as: “A doubtful or
difficult matter requiring a solution or something hard to understand or
accomplish or deal with.”
Being able to “solve problems” is the third skill on the list of what
employers look for in their employees. Employers want to hire people
who can find solutions when faced with difficulties or setbacks.
Even if an employee can’t think of a solution straight away, his or her
employer will need to know he or she has critical thinking skills and can
come up with a logical process for figuring things out. It is really
important that the adolescents and young adults you work with show
their employers that they can approach problems from different angles.
This section will help you impart these skills to your students.

---
## Page 370
WHAT EMPLOYERS WANT: #3 – PROBLEM
SOLVING
Problem Solving and Critical Thinking in the
Workplace
Explain to the young men and women you work with that critical
thinking refers to the ability to use knowledge, facts, and data to solve
problems effectively. This doesn’t always mean that they need to have
an immediate answer to a problem or a question. It means they have to
be able to think on their feet, assess problems, and find solutions.
Finding answers or solutions may require time and research. Once your
students begin working, they will need to be able to ask for this time so
that they can come up with a solution or an alternative.
Explain to your students that having good strong problem solving skills
can make a huge difference in their career paths. And, that in order to
demonstrate the critical thinking skills that will lead to problem solving,
students will need to be able to use a logical approach when making
decisions.
Explain that problems are at the center of what many people do at
work every day and can be large or small, simple or complex, and easy
or difficult. So, being a confident problem solver is really important for
success in almost any workplace.
Tell students that as employees they might be solving a problem for a
client (internal or external), supporting those who are solving problems,
or discovering new problems to solve. It is really important to realize
that problems come in all sizes and occur during day-to-day, as well as
short- and long-term employment situations.
Explain that much of that confidence comes from having a good
process to use when approaching a problem. A good process will allow
individuals to solve problems quickly and effectively. Without one, their
solutions may be ineffective, sometimes with painful consequences.

---
## Page 371
Here are the four basic steps that result in solving a problem:
•
Defining the problem.
•
Generating alternatives.
•
Evaluating and selecting alternatives.
•
Implementing solutions.
Defining the problem:
Explain that the key to solving problems is ensuring that the employee
can assess and understand the real problem – not the problem’s
symptoms. For example, if the performance of some employees in their
department is substandard, they might jump to the conclusion that the
problem is with the individuals submitting or doing the work. However, if
they look a bit deeper, the real issue might be a lack of training or an
unreasonable workload.
Tell the young men and women you teach, counsel, advise, or mentor
that at this stage, it’s also important to ensure that they (as employees
or supervisors) look at the issue from a variety of perspectives. If they
commit to a conclusion too early, they might end up with a problem
statement that’s really a solution instead.
For example: Ask your students to consider this problem statement:
“We have to find a way of disciplining people who do substandard
work!” This doesn’t allow the employee or the employer the opportunity
of discovering the real reasons for under-performance. Therefore, both
need to look at the fact that there may be many elements that are
contributing to the problem. Both need to define the problem in a better
– fact or evidence-based way – so that it can be solved.
Understanding complexity:
Explain to your students that when the problem at work is simple, the
solution is usually obvious and they don’t need to follow the four steps
that were outlined earlier. Using this line of thinking they can deduce
that they will need to use the four-step method (defining the problem,

---
## Page 372
generating alternatives, evaluating and selecting alternatives, and
implementing solutions), when problems are more complex or difficult to
understand.
The good news is that there are numerous tools your students can
use to make sense of this tangled mess! Many of these will help the
young men and women you work with create a clear visual
representation of the situation so that they can better understand what’s
going on.
Explain that quite often what may seem to be a single problem turns
out to be a whole series of problems. Breaking problems into smaller
parts and solving each one in turn, by using logic and facts, will help.
Using critical thinking skills and the four-step approach to solving
problems will serve your students well.
Remind students that critical thinking in the workplace:
•
Brings new ideas.
•
Fosters teamwork.
•
Promotes options.
•
Uncovers spinoffs, new business models, and solutions to
problems.
Use the student handouts on pages 164–166 to reinforce the key
points for problem solving using critical thinking skills.

---
## Page 373
Helpful Tips for Students
Make sure your resume matches your “LinkedIn” profile or other
online information. Check that contact data on all sites are
correct and up-to-date. “Google” yourself often, checking to see
that your online presence is a positive and accurate one.
Dan McManmon, CIP President
Update your resume monthly. Also keep in touch with the
individuals on your “job references sheet” to ensure the contact
information is updated and also to continue good ties.
Caroline Fortuno, CIP Long Beach
It can be helpful to have some kind of career mantra or psyche-
up routine before starting each work day. This could be
repeating a few words or sentences; listening to certain music;

---
## Page 374
anything that will focus your thoughts and help you get into a
working mindset.
Taylor True, CIP Bloomington
Developing Problem Solving Skills
It is important to show a prospective employer or your
employer (once you land the job) that you have the
right skills to solve problems and that you have the
personal resilience to handle the challenges and
pressures that problem solving may bring.
You need to be able to:
•
Evaluate information or situations.
•
Break each problem down into small parts.
•
Consider various ways of approaching and
resolving them.
•
Decide on the most appropriate way to implement
the solution/s to the problem.
Solving problems involves using critical thinking,
analytical, and creative skills. These skills will help
you:
•
Evaluate the problem and to make decisions.
•
Formulate questions.
•
Gather information systematically.
•
Collate and organize data.

---
## Page 375
•
Condense and collate information.
•
Define the desired objective.
•
Use the information gathered effectively.
•
Break down a problem into smaller, more
manageable, parts.
•
Identify steps that can be taken to achieve the
objective.
•
Decide what action to take or decide on further
information to be gathered before taking action.
•
Decide what resources to use (time, funding, staff,
etc.) to solve the problem.
•
Implement action.
•
Provide the solution or information to others
involved in the process or decision.
•
Monitor the outcome of the action taken.
•
Review the problem and problem solving process
to avoid similar situations in future.
And remember…
That at any stage of this process, it may be necessary
to return to an earlier stage – for example – when
further problems arise or if a solution does not appear
to be working as desired.
Not everyone has these types of skills in equal
measure. For this reason good problem solving
strategies always include:
•
Teamwork and letting others know about the
problem you are trying to solve.

---
## Page 376
•
Communication, persuasion, and negotiation.
The final stage is to put the solution you and your co-
workers have decided on into practice and check the
results.
Developing your critical thinking, analytical, and
problem solving skills:
Most problem solving skills are developed through
everyday life and experience. However, the following
interests and activities will help you further develop
these skills.
•
“Mind games” such as cryptic crosswords,
Sudoku, chess, and bridge.
•
Computer games – the best of these can involve
strategic planning, critical and statistical analysis,
and assessing the pros and cons of different
courses of action.
•
“Practical” 
interests 
such 
as 
programming,
computer repairs, car maintenance.
•
Working with sound or lighting equipment for a
band, event, or show.
•
Academic study: Evaluating different sources of
information for essays, projects, and coursework.
Problems can also be opportunities!
Problems allow you to see things differently and to do
things in a different way. A problem (and helping to
solve it) might even give you a chance to “make a
fresh start.”

---
## Page 377
And remember to “Reframe” obstacles positively and
turn them into opportunities for growth and new
perspectives.
What Would You Do?
(A problem solving classroom activity)
Divide students into four groups and have groups gather at
the four corners of the room. Assign a different scenario to
each group. Allow students 20 to 30 minutes to implement
problem solving strategies and come up with a solution or
solutions that the employee mentioned might use to solve the
scenario problem or problems.
Explain to your students that there is usually no right or
wrong answer to these problems. As each group engages in
the problem solving session encourage students to ask each
other the following questions:
•
Yes, but what if … ?
•
Have you thought about … ?
•
Why would you do that … ?
Here’s an example of what you are asking your students to
do. Read the following scenario and the outcome to the
young men and women in your class and watch the “ah ha”
moment occur. Then have students work on their individual
scenarios.
Sample scenario: A large computer manufacturer has a
problem. Some of the boxes coming off the production lines
are empty. The supervisor for the “production line” isolated
the problem to the “assembly line” that transports the
packaged computer boxes to the “delivery department.” He

---
## Page 378
has determined that these boxes definitely went through
assembly line empty.
The management asked its engineers to solve the problem.
They spent much time and money in devising an X-ray
machine with high-resolution monitors – manned by paid
staff – to watch all the boxes on the line to make sure they
weren’t empty. This solution was costing the company
thousands of dollars a day.
A worker hearing about this problem came up with another
solution. He got a powerful industrial fan and pointed it at the
assembly line. As each box passed the fan, the empty boxes
were blown off the line.
Moral: Sometimes the simplest solution is usually the best!
Problem 1:
•
How would you deal with a staff member who
persistently arrives late for work and takes
regular, unauthorized breaks from work for a
cigarette while you put in a full day’s work for a
full day’s pay?
Problem 2:
•
You are working on the cash register in a retail
store when a customer’s credit card is refused.
The cardholder is a regular customer who is
trying to buy a present for her mother’s birthday
the following day. How would you deal with this
situation?
Problem 3:
•
Your manager regularly leaves you in charge of
your small office in his absence. The other staff
members use the opportunity to complain to you
about the way he runs things and how irritated
they are by his interference in their day-to-day

---
## Page 379
work. How would you handle these interruptions?
What would you do or say about their
complaints?
Problem 4:
•
You work in a company that manufactures frozen
pizza. Sales have been falling for the past two
years and turnover is high. Most employees stay
at the company for only three to six months, then
quit their jobs. You are asked to come up with
ideas to retain employees and revive sales for
your company. Generate some ideas to take to
your boss.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
WHAT EMPLOYERS WANT: #4 – INITIATIVE
AND LEADERSHIP
“Initiative is doing the right thing
without being told.”
Victor Hugo
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Young men and women with an ASD or LD
diagnosis will need to be able to think about the “bigger picture” and
“the future” of the organization they are going to work for. Whether
they are volunteering, interning, or working part or full time – their
employers, supervisors, and co-workers will want to see that each one
can think creatively and to make improvements to the way things are.

---
## Page 380
This requires being able to take the initiative and to display leadership
qualities.
I have personally learned over the years that learning to lead or be a
good leader means “assuming good,” a phrase I came up with and
use often. It means assuming that the person you are working with or
supervising is doing the best he or she can at his or her job. By
assuming that no malice is involved and that the person means well
even though the interaction, result, or scenario is not what you – as
the employer or supervisor – would do or want, the door opens to
guide and truly lead. This might mean more training for the employee
or a clarification of what is expected, or further guidance and
structure.
Acquiring the courage to take the initiative and develop leadership
skills will be a challenge for the ASD and LD students you teach,
mentor, advise, and counsel. Peer pressure, bullying, and other
factors that have led to low self-esteem and feelings of always being
“on the perimeter” and “never in the center of things” can be a factor in
making these skills difficult to impart to your students.
This subsection will be well worth the time and effort taken as it
provides a variety of ideas, materials, and activities to reinforce the
fourth skill set (initiative and leadership) that employers want their
employees to exhibit.
DEFINING INITIATIVE
Tell your students that initiative is defined as displaying behavior at
work that shows others the employee has a self-starting nature, can
use a proactive approach when working with others, and will be
persistent as he or she works to overcome the day-to-day difficulties
that arise when working or pursuing goals at work.

---
## Page 381
Explain that people who show initiative are people who do things
without being told; who find out what they need to do from others to do
their job effectively; who keep going when things get tough; and who
take advantage of opportunities that others might pass by.
Someone who shows initiative and leadership qualities acts instead of
reacts while working. People who have initiative and make things
happen are highly valued in the workplace.
Explain that most of us have seen initiative in action. Maybe some of
your students have experienced internships or part-time jobs and have
observed a friend or co-worker filling his or her boss’s shoes when that
person is sick and the rest of the team is unsure what to do. They might
have watched a colleague or co-worker devise, develop, and then
implement a process to improve customer service – this is leadership
led by an individual’s initiative.
Tell students that initiative is becoming increasingly important in
today’s workplace. Organizations want employees who can think on
their feet and take action without waiting for someone to tell them what
to do. Talk to your students about how this type of flexibility and

---
## Page 382
courage is what pushes their employers and their organizations to
innovate and to overcome competition. By developing initiative the
young men and women you work with will acquire the skills needed to
be good leaders by developing initiative.
The student handout on pages 168–169 will help your students
understand that initiative is a skill they can develop and acquire and
that you will help them do this.
How to Develop Initiative in the Workplace
The good news is that initiative is a skill that you can develop.
There are six steps you can take to develop your own initiative:
1. Develop a career plan.
2. Build self-confidence.
3. Spot opportunities and potential improvements.
4. Sense-check your ideas.
5. Develop rational persistence.
6. Find balance.
1. Develop a career plan:
•
People who have a long-term career plan (based on their
interests and skills they possess or have acquired) are more
likely to take initiative. Once you know what type of volunteer
work, internship program, or career path you want to follow,
you will be far more likely to show initiative at work,
especially when the action or decision will help you further
your personal career goals.

---
## Page 383
•
Once you know what you want to achieve, you will be able to
integrate your career goals with your personal goals so that
you have something to work towards. The key to developing
initiative at work is to set clear personal goals then to work
steadily towards achieving them.
•
You can start to do this by making a career plan based on
your interests listed in the Person Centered Plan (PCP) and
academic portfolio you created or are creating during your
school or career preparation years. Initiative is easier to
develop if you are working at something you really want to
do, that suits your abilities, and allows you to achieve
success.
2. Continue to work on building self-confidence:
•
It takes courage and a strong sense of self to show initiative,
especially if you think that people may disagree with your
actions or suggestions.
•
Some people have a real fear of speaking up, or of taking
any action – that’s not yet authorized by the leadership team
– because they’re afraid of failure or rejection. If this sounds
like you, ask those whom you trust (teachers, academic
advisors, career counselors, peer mentors, your families, or
your friends), to help you work on gaining more self-
confidence and to practice and role play speaking up.
3. Spot opportunities and potential improvements:
•
People who show initiative often do so by spotting and acting
on opportunities their co-workers or supervisors might not
have noticed. They’re curious about their organization and
how it works and they keep their minds open to new ideas
and new possibilities.
•
You should always be on the lookout for areas in your
workplace that could use improvement or for problems you

---
## Page 384
might be able to develop solutions for.
4. Sense-check your ideas. Here is an example of sense-
checking an idea:
•
Imagine that you’ve come up with a creative way of
addressing customer complaints at your job. Before you
approach your boss with your idea … stop and do some
thinking and your homework. Think about the costs involved
or any risks associated with the idea. Ask yourself: Is the
idea reality-based, is it do-able, and most of all is it logical?
•
You will then need to develop a plan and present your idea to
others. Think about any risks involved to your company and
consider stating the case in writing, with your ideas fully
outlined, and then asking for authorization to go ahead and
try out your idea.
•
You’ve already shown initiative by coming up with an idea.
Now it’s time to find a way to implement it. This means
following through by doing your homework and developing
your idea further. The more evidence you have to support
your idea, the higher your chances will be for finding a way to
implement it.
5. Develop rational persistence (also known as “Dogged
Persistence”):
•
Persistence – or “Dogged Persistence” – is the art of moving
forward even when you encounter inertia or difficulty. This
means you will ask questions and do research until an
answer is found.
•
People who show initiative often encounter difficulties and
setbacks along the way, so rational or “Dogged Persistence”
(where you listen to, consider, and appropriately modify your
direction depending on other people’s input) is essential if
you want to achieve what you’ve set out to do.

---
## Page 385
•
When you’re persisting with your idea, you’ll find things much
easier if you learn how to manage change effectively – this
can often make the difference between success and failure
for a project. It’s also helpful to learn how to do this since
people may already have an opinion on a subject before you
even start presenting your idea and they can help you
advance your cause.
6. Find balance:
•
While it’s important to take initiative, it’s just as important to
be wise in the way that you use it. In some situations, it can
be inappropriate to take initiative, because people who
generate extra work for others can upset them. This can lead
to workplace disharmony.
•
For instance, you might have worked with someone who was
ready for action about every idea. A real go-getter or super
high-achiever. He or she was constantly pushing the team
and your boss to implement his or her way of doing things or
to be the one to lead the next project and to implement the
next new idea. However, some of his or her ideas were not
realistic and his or her persistence in taking the initiative
often crossed the line into aggressiveness. You and your co-
workers probably thought this person “rocked the boat” or
“crossed the line.”
Some things to remember:
It’s important to learn good decision-making techniques. The
more you enhance this ability, the better you’ll be at judging
when an idea is good, and when it isn’t. This way, you can
develop a reputation both for initiative and for good judgment –
an invaluable combination!
You’ll also want to develop your emotional intelligence skills. It’s
helpful to know how to read the emotions of others. This

---
## Page 386
sensitivity can help you further decide when to take initiative or
when it’s best to let things be.
Key concepts for developing initiative:
You show initiative when you act without being told what to do,
persist in the face of inertia or difficulty, and see your idea
through to a successful conclusion.
Offering ideas is a great way to show that you are an original
thinker and have acquired the ability to use your initiative.
Leadership requires creativity, resourcefulness, and the
courage to take the initiative.
No matter what kind of job you interview for, your potential
employer will want to know whether you can come up with
original ideas and take the initiative required to put them into
action.
Here are some scenarios, that illustrate key concepts regarding
taking the initiative and several examples that show effective
leadership by acting on the initiative.
Discuss these examples with the young men and women you
work with so that they start to understand what taking the
initiative and showing leadership in the workplace means.
Talk about the fact that most interviewers will ask the young
men and women you are preparing for work to demonstrate a
time that they have taken the initiative or provided leadership in
a school, community, or workplace situation during a job
interview.
Wait Until You Hear What “I” Did!

---
## Page 387
Discuss each example. Ask students if they agree with the
solutions or ideas presented and what they might have done if
put in the situation. Then start a lively discussion (perhaps with
an example from your own life), by asking for volunteers to
present examples from their own lives where they had to take
the initiative (this can be in any situation, not just a workplace
scenario), and illustrate to the class how they either “took the
lead” or “demonstrated leadership.”
Ask students to discuss how their leadership skills have helped
them accomplish goals in the past. They might give specific
examples that used a creative, new, or unusual approach they
took to reaching their goal.
Example 1
Drew’s manager was due to meet with him and his co-workers
to discuss the launch of a new product. Unfortunately, he’s been
snowed in at an airport on the other side of the country, and his
cell phone battery is dead. The deadline is tight and Drew and
