his team can’t afford to waste a day because of his absence.
Drew was the last person to talk to the boss before he left town
and he had outlined clearly who was going to be doing what on
the project. So, Drew decides to take command, and within an
hour, everyone on the team has their preliminary tasks mapped
out.
When Drew’s boss arrived in the office three days later, he’s
impressed and grateful that Drew took responsibility to get the
project moving. He explains to the rest of the team that if Drew
hadn’t assumed this role, several valuable days would have
been lost.

---
## Page 388
Explain to your students that this is a good example of taking
the initiative and showing leadership.
Example 2
Elizabeth is working late one evening and she is the last person
in the office. Just as she’s about to leave, she answers an urgent
telephone call to her supervisor from a sales representative
who’s in the middle of a meeting with a potential client. The
sales rep needs an answer to one important question to close
the sale right then and there. Tomorrow will be too late.
Elizabeth tries to reach her boss on his cell phone but he is not
picking up. Elizabeth feels (and she is 100% sure of this) that
she has the expertise to answer the question, but it’s beyond her
normal level of authority.
After thinking it over, she decides to answer the question based
on her own knowledge. Then she decides to document the
situation so that she can inform her supervisor of everything that
happened via email to her supervisor at home later that evening.
In addition to the email, she asks her supervisor to meet with her
the next day so that she can discuss the matter with him in
further detail. When the meeting occurs, Elizabeth asks her
supervisor if he thinks she made the right decision. He tells
Elizabeth that not only did she make an informed and correct
decision, her initiative enabled the sales rep to make the sale.
Explain to your students that this is a good example of using
logic, personal knowledge, and confidence in ability to solve a
problem using initiative and leadership.
Example 3

---
## Page 389
Sam worked as an intern at a marketing research company. He
noticed that when inquiries would come into the office, they
would be distributed haphazardly among the marketing
assistants. Realizing that there had to be a better, more efficient
way of logging these inquiries, Sam took it upon himself to set
up a better system. He organized the inquiries according to
region and distributed them to the marketing assistants based
on their regions. This approach not only enabled the marketing
team to come up with better and more creative solutions to the
company-wide sales problems, but also addressed the specific
concerns of the company’s pre-existing customers.
When Sam’s supervisor was told of his initiative, he was offered
paid work in the form of a summer job when his first college year
finished.
Explain to your students that Sam’s initiative and willingness to
not only contribute, but implement an idea, led to paid work.
Example 4
For months, the publishing company Erica worked for had been
trying to get an appointment with a particular Fortune 500
company to talk about a possible advertising campaign. After
several sales representatives tried to no avail, Erica volunteered
to take a crack at the task. Rather than contact the vice
president of advertising himself, Erica decided to target his
assistant. She was able to schedule an appointment with the
assistant and give this person her sales pitch instead. Erica
made a very good impression and an excellent, well researched,
and thought out presentation that day. The assistant made an
appointment for her with the VP for that very day. Two weeks
later, Erica’s publishing company got the order, and the deal
was made.

---
## Page 390
Explain to your students that by “thinking outside of the box,”
Erica devised a way to have her presentation heard by someone
she instinctively thought might be able to help her. This is a
great example of using creative thinking, problem solving, and
displaying initiative.
Example 5
This example illustrates what can happen when someone uses
their initiative without gathering information or using logic, and
will help your students understand that initiative cannot happen
without thinking things through, having a plan, and checking that
they have all the information needed for the initiative they wish to
display to be appropriate or effective.
Luke was a student who had recently obtained a position as a
clerical assistant. He was very anxious to please his new boss
and when there was a lag in work he used his own initiative to
seal the envelopes for a mailing that was being completed in the
office. He assumed that he was being proactive and forward
thinking; however, he did this without asking his supervisor.
Luke did not know that the mailing was incomplete. He had no
way of knowing that it was being held up pending one more
paper that needed to be inserted in the mailing before it was to
be sealed and sent out.
By rushing forward and sealing the envelopes, he inadvertently
cost the business many hours of additional work. Luke thought
he was being a “self-starter” but in reality he did not know
enough about office protocol or how to work in a workplace
setting to accomplish this.
Luke set about learning more about how his office worked, about
chain of command, and that if he had free time, asking for

---
## Page 391
additional work was the route he needed to take. He learned that
it was fine to be a self-starter but only if he paid attention to the
tasks at hand, communicated with others, and learned how to
do his job correctly.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit
Exhibiting Leadership Qualities in the Workplace
To be a leader you must have leadership qualities. However,
being a leader does not mean that you have to be in a
leadership (or supervisory) role. What is important is displaying
the characteristics of a leader as these qualities will help you
succeed in the workplace setting regardless of what your job
title is.
Typical leadership characteristics include the following:
•
Dedication to honor, principles, and moral obligations that
you strongly stand for.
•
Ability to inspire and influence.
•
Curiosity to learn and acquire more knowledge.
•
Courage to think differently and imagine the impossible.
•
Drive for seeking what might appear next on the horizon.
•
Ambition and the desire to take initiative.
•
Creativity for thinking “outside the box.”

---
## Page 392
Developing and exhibiting leadership qualities – even if you’re
not identified as a leader in a professional sense – will help you
excel as an employee and as an individual.
By striving to practice leadership qualities, you’ll naturally
become a leader who is, if not directly inspiring and supervising
others, inspiring and supervising yourself to reach your highest
potential.
ACT LIKE A LEADER!
Display integrity:
•
Actions and decisions based on honesty and sincerity create
trust and credibility. People who are trusted and credible are
given opportunities.
Learn to listen and be observant:
•
In silence you can learn a lot; whether you’re silent and
carefully listening to someone else speak or you’re literally in
silence and listening to your own thoughts. If (at the end of
the experience of listening to someone) you have received
nothing beneficial from him or her, you’ll still be perceived as
likeable and reliable.
Say what you mean:
•
Say what you mean and do what you say. Saying something
you think people want to hear, to make people feel happy, or
to put yourself in a good light will have no positive effect on
others. Deliver what you promise. Hold yourself accountable.
Be consistent.
Network and make connections:
•
As you meet people, you’re increasing your network and
there’s always the likelihood that you can help someone or
vice versa. Plus, meeting new people provides you with

---
## Page 393
different perspectives, experiences, and stories that can help
you grow. Be someone who’s confident and whom you’d like
to be around.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
WHAT EMPLOYERS WANT: #5 – PLANNING
AND ORGANIZING
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Planning and organization (the backbone of
“Executive Function”) will be some of the most difficult concepts to
impart to the ASD and LD adolescents and young adults (ages 16–
26) you work with. I know from my own challenges of being “on the
Spectrum” how difficult being able to organize myself, my work space,
and meet deadlines has been.
Being able to exhibit “Executive Function” ability through planning and
organization strategies in the workplace is about having the young
men and women you work with, counsel, advise, and mentor, show
their employers that they can prioritize, work efficiently and
productively, and manage their time well.
Acquiring work oriented “Executive Function” abilities before entering
the world of work as a volunteer, intern, or paid employee will help
your students know how to decide what is important to focus on and
get done (during the course of the work day), and ensure that they
know how to meet deadlines. They will be able to be on time, keep an
organized desk or work space, and follow workplace routines. This
section will build on the “Executive Function” abilities your students
acquired in the “Skills for Life” and “Skills for School” chapters of this
book.
What is planning in the workplace setting?

---
## Page 394
Explain to the young men and women you work with that planning is a
process that will help each person focus his or her ideas and decide on
the steps needed to achieve a particular goal or outcome at work.
Tell your students that planning is an important “Executive Function.”
And that once they start volunteering, interning, or working for pay, their
manager, supervisor, or boss will work with them to plan the tasks and
time required to do a specific job and that these requirements will be
their primary goal while working. Once this has been clarified each
employee will need to know how to plan and organize his or her work
day so that tasks are accomplished and that all individual employees
stay on time and on task.
Tell students that employers need their employees to exhibit the
planning skills that will enable each worker to break down the tasks
they give an employee into manageable chunks so that it gets
accomplished within a time-frame they (the supervisor or boss) have
set for the employee. For example: A task that is supposed to take five
months to complete becomes much more doable if the employee has a
target and a timeline to adhere to. This might mean reporting on
progress to the employer every two weeks until the goal is met.

---
## Page 395
What are planning skills?
Tell your students that planning skills are any skills that allow your ASD
and LD students to look ahead and accomplish goals at work. These
skills let each person make and implement decisions.

---
## Page 396
Explain that planning skills allow individuals to set goals, create
timelines, and accomplish tasks in the workplace setting (internship,
volunteer, or paid work).
Importance:
•
Explain that planning skills are necessary to develop the overall
direction employers want their employees to take so that they
can do their jobs effectively. Planning skills let employees
organize and manage the things they have to do at work at any
given time.
•
Tell your students that they can develop these important skills
with practice and that you will help them do this.
What are organizational skills in the
workplace setting?
Tell your students organizational skills in the workplace include
diagnosing problems and their causes; predicting and forecasting;
communicating; evaluating and comparing courses of action; and
implementing and monitoring actions.
Explain that organizational skills at work are one of the many important
skills that are necessary to become a good employee. Organization is
also used to ensure that the right people with the right skills end up
working on projects that will best suit them.
Explain that each person will need to be able to multitask on different
projects at the same time. This is “Executive Function” at its finest.
Each project will have different goals and separate deadlines. An
unorganized person may get confused among different projects, which
may lead to undesirable consequences, such as missing a deadline, or
doing the wrong task at the wrong time.
Tell the adolescents and young adults you work with that the only
solution to being successful and efficient at work is to become an

---
## Page 397
organized person.
Explain that each individual has to be determined and want to change
old unorganized habits and that you will assist anyone who wants to
make these changes.
The handout on page 175 illustrates all of the above and gives
suggestions and ideas for acquiring the planning, organizational, and
other “Executive Function” abilities they will need to use in the
workplace.
Have the class discuss these strategies, then create a mind map on a
white board, or hold a brainstorming session to come up with concrete
ways they might help themselves and each other to acquire the skills
necessary to implement the strategies portrayed in the handout.
WHAT EMPLOYERS WANT: #6 – SELF-
MANAGEMENT
The ASD and LD students you assist day in and day out will need to
work on developing further self-management skills so that they can feel
more productive when doing daily routines regardless of the working
environment or workplace setting they find themselves in. Well-
developed self-management skills will help the young men and women
you work with, counsel, advise, and mentor to communicate with co-
workers, management and customers, make the right decisions, plan
their working time, and keep their bodies healthy while learning to
effectively manage stress.
This section stresses why this is necessary and will show your students
that by taking the time and putting in the effort to acquire these skills,
both the employer and the employee will benefit. And as an added
bonus, obtaining these abilities will improve their chances of getting,
and then keeping a job. This in turn will pave the way for career
promotion and professional advancement.

---
## Page 398
Self-Management Skills in the Workplace
Explain to the adolescents and young adults whom you work with that
working life presents many challenges (especially to those on the
Spectrum), and that each person will need to show their employer that
he or she is the kind of person who will find a way through, even when
the going gets tough … and remain positive, hopeful, and optimistic.
Tell them that employers want people to have a bit of get-up-and-go,
moxie, and spunk. They also want employees who can keep calm in a
crisis and not become too overwhelmed, emotional, or stressed.
Explain that this also means that each person needs to be able to get
on with his or her work without someone checking up on or monitoring
them every five minutes. Your students will also need to able to stay on
top of deadlines and be able to ask for assistance, delegate tasks, or
work with others to make sure things get done on time.
Tell your students that the first and foremost skill of self-management
(especially in the workplace) is the ability to withstand the day-to-day
stresses that will occur regardless of what their job, internship, or
volunteer work day encompasses.
I’m Ready for Work but Can’t Find My Backpack!
Acquiring “Executive Function” strategies that will help you in
the workplace is important.
“Executive Functions” in the workplace will help you to:
•
Engage in mental planning (working memory).
•
Persist to complete a task or activity.
•
Organize/keep track of your belongings and actions.

---
## Page 399
•
Self-regulate and exhibit self-control.
•
Self-monitor your behavior.
“Executive Functions” are cognitive (brain based) activities of:
•
Logic.
•
Strategy.
•
Planning.
•
Problem solving.
•
Information processing.
•
Behavior control.
“Executive Function” difficulties at work might lead to:
•
Difficulty predicting what will happen next at work.
•
Inflexibility/getting stuck/needing to preserve sameness.
•
Difficulty attending work or concentrating on work.
•
Impulsivity (blurting out).
•
Difficulty managing and allocating time (especially with
respect to work day requirements).
•
Difficulty keeping track of belongings/materials; forgetting
things you need to bring to work or that you need to do at
work.
•
Difficulty doing multi-step or complex tasks.
•
Disorganization.
•
Difficulty with planning.
Here is a list of some strategies that you can implement
(starting today), that will help you at work:
•
Identify and organize tasks or information.

---
## Page 400
•
Use calendars (physical and electronic), “to-do” lists,
checklists, and timelines.
•
Coordinate people, activities, and details.
•
Develop a plan and set objectives.
•
Set up and keep time schedules.
•
Anticipate problems and respond with solutions.
•
Develop realistic goals and action to attain them.
•
Arrange correct sequences of information and actions.
•
Create guidelines for implementing actions.
•
Create efficient systems that keep your desk, your work
space, and your life at work organized.
•
Follow through and insure the completion of a task.
How can you show a prospective employer you have
planning, organizational, and “Executive Function” skills?
You can show your abilities in many ways, including:
•
Planning and then creating an essay or piece of writing that
outlines how you plan to do your job or how you did your job.
•
Explaining how you organized a meeting, task, or event.
•
If you’ve been on a gap year, detail the planning that went
into organizing your year.
•
Explaining how you achieved a goal in a part-time job.
•
Explaining how you managed your volunteer tasks during
your community service project.
•
Creating a visual presentation of how you applied “Executive
Function” during an internship or paid work. You might
create a mini-training manual that will show others how you
did your job.

---
## Page 401
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Managing Stress at Work
Tell the young men and women you work with that by developing self-
management skills they will learn to avoid the many mistakes that
people make when they are “stressed out.”
Explain that stressful situations block our ability to think and make
rational decisions when we feel we can’t cope at work (even with the
simplest tasks), and this makes our productivity go down. The end
result is total frustration.

---
## Page 402
The student handout below and on page 177 with advice, tips, and
strategies is to help your students manage stress at work.
How to Successfully Manage Stress in the
Workplace
First things first!
You can’t control everything in your work environment, but that
doesn’t mean you’re powerless – even when you’re stuck in a
difficult situation. Finding ways to manage workplace stress isn’t
about making huge changes or rethinking career ambitions, but
rather about focusing on the one thing that’s always within your
control: you!
When you feel overwhelmed at work, you lose confidence and
may become irritable or withdrawn. This can make you less
productive and less effective in your job and make the work
seem less rewarding. If you ignore the warning signs of work
stress, they can lead to bigger problems.
DURING THE COURSE OF AN ORDINARY
WORK DAY YOU MIGHT:
•
Feel anxious, irritable, or depressed.
•
Feel apathetic (you experience a loss of interest in your
work).
•
Have problems sleeping.
•
Feel tired all the time.

---
## Page 403
•
Have trouble concentrating.
•
Experience muscle tension or headaches.
•
Have stomach problems.
•
Use alcohol or drugs to cope.
You can help yourself feel better by:
•
Paying attention to your physical and emotional health.
When your own needs are taken care of, you’re stronger and
more resilient to stress. The better you feel, the better
equipped you’ll be to manage stress at work without
becoming overwhelmed.
•
Getting regular exercise! It’s a powerful stress reliever –
even though it may be the last thing you feel like doing. Any
activity that raises your heart rate and makes you sweat is an
effective way to lift your mood, increase energy, sharpen
focus, and relax both the mind and body.
•
Make good food choices that give you energy and
keep you going. Healthy eating can help you get through
stressful work days and help you avoid mood swings. (Sugar
and caffeine zap energy.)
•
Getting enough sleep. Not only can stress and worry
create insomnia, but a lack of sleep can leave you vulnerable
to even more stress. When you’re well rested, it’s much
easier to keep your emotional balance, a key factor in coping
with job and workplace stress. Try to improve the quality of
your sleep by keeping a sleep schedule and aiming for eight
hours of shut-eye each night.
You can reduce job stress by using your prioritizing and
organizing skills:
When job and workplace stress threatens to overwhelm you,
there are simple steps you can take to regain control over

---
## Page 404
yourself and the situation. Maintaining a sense of self-control in
stressful situations will often be well received by co workers,
managers, and subordinates alike, which can lead to better
relationships at work. Here are some suggestions for reducing
job stress by prioritizing and organizing your responsibilities.
•
Ask your supervisor or boss to help you create a
balanced work schedule. Together – analyze your
schedule, responsibilities, and daily tasks – if needed ask for
an accommodation. All work and no play is a recipe for
burnout. Try to find a balance between work and family life,
social activities, solitary pursuits, daily responsibilities, and
just plain downtime.
•
Don’t overcommit yourself. Avoid scheduling things back-
to-back or trying to fit too much into one day. If you’ve got too
much on your plate, distinguish between the “shoulds” and
the “musts.” Drop tasks that aren’t truly necessary to the
bottom of the list or eliminate them entirely.
•
Try to leave earlier in the morning. Even 10–15 minutes
can make the difference between frantically rushing to your
desk and having time to ease into your day. Don’t add to
your stress levels by running late. Set a timer 15 minutes
before you should leave and again five minutes before
departure time, then leave when that five minute timer goes
off.
•
Plan regular breaks. Be sure to take short breaks
throughout the day … to take a walk or sit back and clear
your mind. Also try to get away from your desk or work
station for lunch. Stepping away from work to briefly relax
and recharge will help you be more, not less, productive. Use
this time to stretch, do a bit of yoga, or rest for a few minutes.
Reduce job stress by breaking bad habits:

---
## Page 405
Many of us make job stress worse with negative thoughts and
behavior. If you can turn around these self-defeating habits,
you’ll find employer-imposed stress easier to handle. It’s all
about thinking positive.
•
Resist perfectionism. No project, situation, or decision is ever
perfect, so trying to attain perfection on everything will simply
add unnecessary stress to your day. When you set unrealistic
goals for yourself or try to do too much, you’re setting yourself
up to fall short. Aim to do your best, no one can ask for more
than that. Give yourself permission to sleep, rest, and exercise.
•
Clean up your act. If you’re always running late, set your
clocks and watches ahead and give yourself extra time. If your
desk is a mess, file and throw away the clutter; just knowing
where everything is saves time and cuts stress. Make to-do lists
and cross off items as you accomplish them. Plan your day and
stick to the schedule – you’ll feel less overwhelmed.
•
Flip your negative thinking. If you see the downside of every
situation and interaction, you’ll find yourself drained of energy
and motivation. Try to think positively about your work, avoid
negative thinking about your co-workers, and pat yourself on the
back about small accomplishments, even if no one else does.
•
Don’t try to control the uncontrollable. Many things at work
are beyond your control, particularly the behavior of other
people. Rather than stressing out over things you can’t control,
focus on the things you can control … such as the way you
choose to react to problems.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Making Good Decisions!
Tell your students that one of the most important self-management
skills they can use is their brain. It is the best mechanism they have for

---
## Page 406
making good (and hopefully right) decisions. Even the hardest tasks
can be handled if the mental process in their heads is always in
progress.
Explain that using the problem solving skills they learned earlier in this
chapter will allow each person to get the facts, make the right
assumptions, to analyze the situation, and to find an effective solution.
Tell your students that keeping an open mind, gathering all the facts
before reacting, and remaining calm will allow each of them to make the
right decision even in the toughest situations.
Explain that using all the communication life skills they have learned in
the previous chapters will help them self-manage at work.
Remind your students that this means:
•
Using a moderate tone of voice, no “blurting,” or emotional
outbursts.
•
Thinking before speaking.
•
Taking a short break if they are about to lose control and grab a
SODA (Stop, Observe, Deliberate, take Action).
•
Checking to see if they might be hungry or tired. And remedying
the situation with food, or a break.
•
Using “The Donkey Rule” (asking the advice of five trusted
people to make sure observations are correct). That is, if five
people say it’s a horse and the student says it’s a donkey … it’s
a horse.
•
Being courteous to others even if you disagree with others and
listening to all sides of a story before reacting to it.
•
Asking for help or an accommodation if things seem to be
getting out of control.

---
## Page 407
WHAT EMPLOYERS WANT: #7 –
WILLINGNESS TO LEARN
PERSONAL INSIGHT: When I started my first professional job I
realized how little I had been taught in college. In fact, I felt that I
should probably give all of my salary back to my first employer as I
had learned so much during my first year in my new job – in my
chosen field – that it felt almost unfair to have taken money for this on
the job training.
Employers will look for evidence that their employees not only want to
learn new things but are enthusiastic about doing so. They will also
want to know that their employees can pick up new things quickly.
Helping your students understand this concept by becoming
inquisitive and willing to learn something new will allow these young
men and women to show enthusiasm when tasks change,
responsibilities increase, or an internal structure or workplace
reorganization takes place.
What is a lifelong learner?
Tell your students that a person who is willing to learn new things at
work or at any stage in their lives is a lifelong learner. These types of
people make great employees because they are enthusiastic,
knowledge-hungry, eager to meet challenges, and they quickly
assimilate new concepts and ideas.
Explain that being open to learning new things at work and trying their
best to adapt by asking questions and for advice from their supervisors
or co-workers will allow each person to be successful as changes
occur.
Talk about open-mindedness being critical to job success – and that
this is often a hard, hard concept for those on the ASD or LD Spectrum
to understand. Explain that supervisors will need to know that their
employees have a willingness to learn new things and consider
alternative approaches to problem solving.

---
## Page 408
Explain that learning to be open-minded during a job interview will
instill confidence to the hiring manager that the person being
interviewed is teachable and coachable. Someone who projects a
know-it-all attitude will not get the job. The hiring manager wants to
know that the future employee has a cooperative attitude and will listen
well to supervisors and co-workers when they share their opinions.
Adaptability:
Explain to your students that being open-minded will help them be
more adaptable to a unique work environment or job. Employers
usually want to hire someone they can mold into the type of employee
who fits well in their organizational culture. Open-minded employees
are usually better equipped to cope with jobs and co-workers who are
different than what they are used to. This is especially important as
workplaces become more diverse. Showing acceptance of different
cultures, genders, races, ethnicity, and ages makes it easier for the
hiring manager to take a chance on them.
Being proactive and being willing to learn:
Tell the young men and women you work with that when they first join
an organization, chances are their supervisor will only teach them what
they need to know in order to do their job properly. There are many
things outside their job scope that they will need to learn in order to
familiarize themselves with their organizations. Asking questions or for
advice and then doing Internet research on the company or business is
a good way to show employers you are willing to learn.
There are also things that can’t be taught, such as unofficial or
unwritten shortcuts that supervisors, co-workers, or colleagues might
know of when it comes to dealing with certain things or certain people.
This is an example of the “Hidden Curriculum” at work. Remind your
students that they learned about the “Hidden Curriculum” in the Skills
for Life chapter (all those unwritten life skills rules), and that there is a
definite “Hidden Curriculum” in the workplace as well.

---
## Page 409
Explain that employers will want their employees to find out these
shortcuts by themselves during the course of working and interacting
with co-workers, clients, or customers. That’s where showing a positive
attitude and a willingness to learn will pay off.
Learning things the hard way:
Tell your students that in some cases, there are things they will need to
know that they can only acquire through experience; there’s no way to
go about it other than diving in headlong and learning things.
Therefore, they will need to be prepared and proactive. It will feel
completely against their own nature, but being open to new
experiences, be willing to try out new things, and listen to the advice
from people who have been there and done that at work, will be well
worth the effort, even though it is contrary to their on the Spectrum
nature.
WHAT EMPLOYERS WANT: #8 –
TECHNOLOGY
This is one skill that most of your ASD and LD students will excel at!
Adolescents and young adults, especially on the ASD and LD
Spectrum, are usually the most innovative users of electronics and
technology that you will have in your class.
Explain that almost all jobs these days require employees to use some
form of technology. Tell your students that these abilities will allow them
to have an advantage over others in the workplace and how that
advantage will be attractive to a prospective employer.
Tell them that the good news is that – more than likely – they already
possess a high degree of technological ability!
Several staff and students at CIP’s Berkeley Center have been doing
ongoing research in this arena for quite some time and offer the results
of their research to your students:

---
## Page 410
Employers in the software industry are increasingly hiring young adults
“on the Spectrum.” In some cases young adults and adults with an “on
the Spectrum” diagnosis are much more productive than their peers in
this field.
People with “Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD),” characterized by
social deficits and repetitive behavior, tend to pay great attention to
detail. This makes them well suited as software testers or debuggers.
People with a “Spectrum” diagnosis bring a different perspective to the
workplace that may help with efficiency and creativity. They have a
very structured nature and like non-ambiguous, precise outcomes.
Major corporations and technology centers are looking for those
strengths and realizing that employees with these traits would be of
great value to their organizations.
Conclusion: Students “on the Spectrum” might consider taking on
technical roles in the software industry and then and asking the
employers to assign customer service queries to other members of
their team for troubleshooting.
TEACHING INSIGHT: Section I: Employability Skills … The Basics
ends with an informative piece by Marc Larrivee. Marc is a Social
Skills Coordinator at CIP’s Berkshire Center. He assists CIP students
with their overall program and helps them acquire the skills needed to
be successful at work and when living independently. Marc supports
his students emotionally and socially at each step along the way. His
piece, which is a compilation of two CIP Blogs he wrote, illustrates the
outcomes of imparting, then implementing, the advice and knowledge
that Section I offers. It’s a great way to segue into the next section of
Chapter 5, Section II: The Career Continuum – Exploring Career
Choices.

---
## Page 411
What Employers Tell You They Want and …
What They Don’t Tell You!
It was rewarding to hear a local employer say about a student
interning there, “We really like having Sarah work for us. She’s
pleasant with the customers and she’s able to handle the
register.”
The workplace has interesting and complex social rules and
dynamics. Succeeding in these environments takes the learning,
practice, and mastery of many different social skills and Sarah
has worked hard and has been able to attain these.
I recently took an informal poll of co-workers and asked them
what qualities they like to see the people they work with exhibit.
Their responses included:
•
“Someone who can do their job.”
•
“Someone who is kind to others.”
•
“Someone who has the ability to get along with others.”
•
“Someone who displays a willingness to help each other
out.”
•
“Someone who is considerate and polite.”
A quick survey of “the web” revealed employers are looking for
someone able to do the technical aspects of the job being
advertised and people who have “soft skills.” The “soft skills”
these employers mentioned were having abilities such as
respect for others, getting along, teamwork, communication,

---
## Page 412
listening, and a willingness to learn; all the things my co-workers
listed. They also stated that they are looking for people who:
•
Speak at appropriate times.
•
Use proper manners at lunch and/or breaks.
•
Are problem solvers.
•
Can plan.
•
Are good at time management.
Gail Hawkins, in her book “How to find Work that Works for
People with Asperger’s Syndrome,” (2003) includes a list of
social skills that are also important in the workplace. They are:
•
Presentation skills such as good hygiene and clean clothing.
•
Communication skills such as speaking, listening, and
greeting others.
•
Social skills such as manners and boundaries.
•
Personal characteristics such as attitude and punctuality.
•
Mindful skills such as following steps and taking initiative.
At CIP, students practice many soft and social skills in their
social skills classes, with their social mentors, during advising,
clinical, and wellness sessions, and when interacting with others
in their classes, dorms, and apartments.
Here are some additional tips and strategies gleaned from my
work that will help you assist the young men and women you
work with as they prepare for the world of work.
Help students “plan early” to get relevant internship or volunteer
experience that will give each person a transferable set of skills
that will make them work-ready. Students should have
something lined up for each vacation or school break, or use this

---
## Page 413
time to get their resumes ready and submit internship
applications during these school breaks.
Have students “religiously record” the skills they obtain and any
work experience they acquire so that they can pull out good
examples to use on application forms, in cover letters, and at job
interviews. It’s also a good time to look at and help them assess
their individual strengths (and any weaknesses they need to
work on).
Teach students to network! Talk to your students about using
families, friends, and contacts to get work experience and to find
out more about career areas that interest them.

---
## Page 414
Have student’s visit your school’s careers services office and
find out whether it runs any employability skills sessions. That
way they can sign up for relevant courses or workshops.
Students can also get help from a careers adviser who can help
them write a resume that really showcases their competencies
and abilities.
Encourage students to take advantage of careers fairs and
employer presentations or talk directly to recruiters to find out
what type of employees they are looking for.
Have students do their homework before applying for jobs.
Explain that employer research will help them identify the skills
and competencies a particular organization places the most
emphasis on. In turn, you (or a career advisor) can help students
tailor their application so that it stands out.
Preparing students on the ASD or LD Spectrum is challenging
and from my own point of view extremely rewarding. There are
many Sarahs out there and many employers who will value
people like Sarah … who have prepared, practiced, and are
ready to experience success in the workplace.
Marc Larrivee, MST, Social Skills Coordinator, CIP Berkshire

---
## Page 415
Section II: The Career Continuum
PERSONAL INSIGHT: The strategies I use each day to do my job
were learned through trial and error. I did a lot of reading, self-
education, and experimenting to find systems and ways to make
myself successful in my career and in relationships with others in the
workplace.
Section II of Competency 3: Skills for Work takes the best of what
works for me – and what we know from experience – works for CIP
students as they navigate leaving high school or college to venture out
into the work world. The Career Continuum process offers young
adults a head start on figuring out what their career path might be and
then illustrates how to obtain work that is meaningful and rewarding
through volunteering and internships so that they can learn to

---
## Page 416
implement the skills learned in this chapter “before” applying for paid
work.
As your students gain insight into career paths, this section will help
you help the young men and women you work create resumes and
prepare for job interviews while learning additional skills that will help
them obtain and keep a job.
The Career Continuum
Explain to your students that the “Career Continuum” has four steps:

---
## Page 417
1. Community service/volunteering is unpaid work that
provides character building, service-learning experience, and
social opportunities. This scope of this volunteer work is often
based upon personal interests, social or spiritual beliefs, and
hobbies.
2. Supervised internship is usually unpaid work (or comes with a
small stipend) that complements a skill set learned in the ending
high school years, at college, or in career training. Internships
can lead to job offers and give students the chance to
experience working in a field they might like to pursue as a
career.
3. Internship to work: Internships often lead to an internship to
work agreement for many young adults. Students are
sometimes hired after a specified unpaid internship/training
period to do a paid job.
4. Employment is a commitment to a profession that requires
continued training and learned ability. A career is paid work that
offers a clear path for occupational growth. Some examples of
this would be: Working as a certified nursing assistant, an IT
technician, a chef, a musician, an administrative assistant, or a
customer service representative.
THE BENEFITS OF COMMUNITY SERVICE
AND VOLUNTEERING
Explain to your students that volunteering is a safe place to learn to
practice work skills and to learn to give to others. Tell your students that
“community service introduces you to yourself.” In other words, it
allows each person to learn a lot about themselves in service to others.
Tell students that volunteering or participating in service-learning
activities is a good place to start to learn about who they really are and
what they really enjoy doing.

---
## Page 418
Volunteering/Community Service
Students should look for a community service or volunteer project that
fits their individual interest area/s and that will provide working
experience doing something they like, are passionate about, or that
aligns with future career goals.
Explain that this can be a wide range of things. From volunteering at
the Humane Society – if pets are a “special interest,” or helping out a
food bank or soup kitchen if the student is interested in social issues.
He or she might volunteer to help build a house with Habitat for
Humanity if eliminating homelessness is a passion. It might also be
things like helping out at a day care center, volunteering at a
community garden, or even helping to paint a mural in an inner city
elementary school.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Share the story that follows (adapted from my
first book “Made for Good Purpose … ”) with your students. Remind
them that I – the author of this book – reside on the ASD/LD Spectrum
and that I hope being able to hear the story of what volunteering did
for me personally will help illustrate to each person in your class what
you are talking about.
“THE PARADISE GUILD”
In college I did a lot of community service. One of
the programs I became involved with was called
“The Paradise Guild.” On Saturdays a group of
young men and women would pile into an old station
wagon and head to the “Paradise Boys Orphanage”
just over the state line in Pennsylvania. We played
games and sports with the boys who lived there
every single week that we could. We also put on
holiday parties that made their Christmas and other
holidays special. I remember one year I was

---
## Page 419
nominated to be Santa Claus and this helped my
self-esteem soar.
Volunteering with the Paradise guild allowed me to
give to others and as a bonus, to receive back so
much more that what I gave. To this day, I can
remember the many lessons I personally learned
from the children there and how the giving of my time
and effort was so rewarding. Here are some of the
things I learned that are still key elements in my life
today:
I learned that even though I was earning my English
BA degree, what I really enjoyed was working with
children!
I learned that I was liked and appreciated for myself
– who I truly was – and that I had a lot to offer others
(especially in this type of work environment).
I found out that I was capable of socializing with my
peers (the other students from my school who were
volunteering at the home), and felt accepted by
them.
And … I actually met a young woman while
volunteering and she eventually became my wife.
Sharing my “special interest” with her and giving to
these boys brought us together.
For me, volunteering was a “life changing event” and
taught me humility, humanity, and the value of giving
my 
time 
to 
others. 
Community 
service 
and
volunteering can be a powerful way for those of us
on the ASD or LD Spectrum to gain self-confidence,

---
## Page 420
self-esteem, and the sense of selflessness that are
necessary to be successful in work and in life.
INTERNSHIPS
Tell your students that acquiring an internship will allow for further
development of the essential skills necessary for success in the
workplace. And … that an internship will help them learn to apply the
academic and social knowledge they have learned during their school
years to the real-life workplace.
Talk about how an internship can act as a springboard for students as
they begin to navigate toward a rewarding, part-time or full-time
permanent job.
Explain that internships are usually unpaid; however, the knowledge
and job readiness skills they will develop during an internship will be
invaluable to them later on.
INTERNSHIP TO WORK
TEACHING INSIGHT: Share the following two case studies (from
CIP’s archives) with your students. They are really good examples of
turning an internship into an “internship to work” experience. The first
story charts Natalie’s progression from an entry level position to
attaining a job that she could turn into a career. The second story
talks about how William used “a lifelong dream” to explore and decide
on a career path. Both stories are a great way to show your students
how this next step on the Career Continuum might play out for them
and that an internship to work opportunity can be a realistic outcome
of participating in an unpaid internship program.

---
## Page 421
“Natalie”
When Natalie was learning about the world of work she decided
to follow each of the four steps on the Career Continuum.
Natalie spent one day a week during her first year of college
volunteering at community dinners and helping out in a food
pantry at a local church.
Doing this helped Natalie in so many ways. She learned some
valuable pre-employment skills and was able to acquire
“customer service” ability as she talked with the elderly who
attended the weekly dinners and the less fortunate members of
the community who came to the food bank. Natalie really liked
meeting and greeting people, helping them, and thought she
would be really good at customer service work.
Natalie told the Career Coordinator at her college that she
wanted to work as a cashier at a local drug store and the
Coordinator helped Natalie apply for, then secure, an internship
at a local drug store.
For her initial internship assignment, Natalie was taught how to
do – and then became responsible for – “the store inventory.”
Natalie really wanted to meet, greet, and help customers;
however, her Career Coordinator explained to her that once on
the internship job, she might have to start at the bottom, prove
she was capable, and then would have to work hard to show
she had ability and willingness to advance.
While not entirely happy with having to “do the store inventory,”
Natalie realized (early on) that she would gain experience and

---
## Page 422
show her supervisor she was a team player. In time, Natalie
knew she could earn respect from her supervisor and co-
workers.
Natalie did well during her probationary period. Because of this,
her Career Coordinator was able to approach Natalie’s manager
and set up an “internship to work” agreement. Natalie would
continue her unpaid internship at the store for three additional
months and during that time she would be trained in the
operation of the cash register. If all went well, then Natalie would
be hired as a cashier.
This is a good example of a successful internship. Natalie gave
the employer six months of “free help,” and the employer gave
Natalie a chance to pursue a career that would allow her to do
the customer service work she coveted. Natalie successfully
completed the additional three months required by the internship
to work agreement and was hired as a paid employee.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
TEACHING INSIGHT: Success stories like Natalie’s are built step-by-
step. Natalie’s story serves as a good motivational story for your
students. William’s story will also help. His story is an example of how
an on-the-job coaching and his CIP Career Coordinator helped
William bring his “dream of becoming a sports announcer” into focus,
chart a viable and realistic career course, and set him on a career
path doing something he really loved and was good at.

---
## Page 423
“William”
William’s dream was to become a sports announcer. His Career
Coordinator at CIP’s Long Beach Center arranged for William to
take a broadcasting class at the local college he was attending.
William did really well in the class and the experience allowed
him to define his “dream” career path even further. He clearly
had the passion and the drive as he mastered all of his
broadcasting 
class 
work, 
assignments, 
and 
tutorials.
Encouraged by William’s hard work and determination his
counselor arranged for him to do an internship at a public
access television station.
Because of his almost natural ability in this arena, the staff at the
TV station was willing to let William try to produce and announce
a show about a local sports team in the Long Beach area. While
doing this assignment, William became interested in using the
video editing equipment and everyone agreed that he was very
good at it.
His actual broadcasting debut was not the success William
thought it would be and he initially felt defeated and
disappointed. The staff at the television were not nearly as
discouraged as William was. They knew talent when they saw it
and they helped him improve his skills in the operation of all the
equipment used to broadcast their programs. They also helped
him see how good he was with the technical side of sports
casting.
With the help of on-the-job coaching and his Career
Coordinator, William was able to shift his career goal and focus
into working on the technical side of broadcasting. He found a

---
## Page 424
way to pursue his “special interest” i.e., his love of broadcasting,
become successful at a job he was good at and qualified for,
while acquiring an internship to work role at a local station. This
showed William how to pursue his dream in a realistic (not
grandiose) way.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit
Islands of Competence
Dr. Robert Brooks, from Harvard University talks about islands of
competence in two of his books (Brooks and Goldstein 2001, 2003).
By this he means that each of us has special skills that are unique and
that bring goodness to the world. You can help your students best by
helping each individual uncover his or her special “island of
competence.” For William, it was a love of sports casting. For Natalie, it

---
## Page 425
was customer service. And both outcomes for these young adults were
realized by an internship to work process.
EMPLOYMENT
The last part of the Career Continuum: having your students obtain
paid and meaningful work, using the workplace readiness skills gained
from volunteering and interning, will be the end result of all the work
they have done – with your guidance – in this chapter. Section II: The
Career Continuum provided a natural progression from Section I:
Workplace Skills … The Basics by showing students options for using
these skills.
Section III: Exploring Career Choices will help educators, career
counselors, occupational therapists, and other support professionals
guide students into choosing a career path based on their individual
interests and abilities.
Section III: Exploring Career
Choices
BEGINNING A CAREER SEARCH
Encourage the young men and women you teach, counsel, mentor,
and advise to evoke an image or a vision of something they are
interested in or even passionate about. They might jot down several
things or focus on one key area or “special interest” they have been
thinking about.
Ask each one to envision making his or her vision(s) into a career.
Once the image or images are clear, each person can begin (with your
guidance) to look at the reality of the choice as a career. This will mean
doing Internet and other research on the career option(s) and finding

---
## Page 426
out what skills, further education, career training, and experience are
needed.
This exploration will allow your students to:
•
Gather as much information as possible.
•
Develop a list of transferrable skills they already have.
•
Develop a list of skills they will need to obtain.
•
Look for opportunities for further career exploration by
shadowing mentors who work in the same field or who have
knowledge in their area of interest.
•
Determine if the career path is realistic or grandiose.
“Special Interests” and Career Choices
TEACHING INSIGHT: Adolescents and young adults “on the
Spectrum” usually focus their attention, energy, and free time (and
sometimes all of their spending money) on their “special interest”
areas. You will be approached by students who have (after research
and discussion) determined that their “special interest” might be a
realistic vs. a grandiose career choice or path.
Victoria Biligovskaya, MA, a Career Coordinator at CIP Amherst, wrote
the following story about a young man named Will whose “special
interest” and passion was any kind of dog. It’s a wonderful example to
share with your students and gives a real-time scenario of how Will
and his Career Coordinator “realistically” explored his “special interest”
area and turned his love of dogs into a viable career option.

---
## Page 427
“Stephen”
When Steven started at CIP almost two years ago, he was very
unsure of the career path he wanted to take. Nothing seemed to
click with him. But Steven had a passion and “special interest”
in dogs. He was very interested in all breeds, in their care, and
especially in training dogs. He collected dog memorabilia and
books about dogs; he drew dogs and wore T-shirts with pictures
of dogs on them. He also did extensive research on dogs and
dog behavior and would talk for hours on his “special interest”
subject.
With help of a Career Coordinator Steven researched how his
“special interest” might turn into a career path for him. He found
an online training program, called an Animal Behavior College
(ABC) that would allow him to obtain a Professional Dog Trainer
Certificate upon completion. The program seemed like a perfect
fit, he enrolled, and was eager to begin.
The first stage of the ABC course entailed studying the materials
the program provided and taking tests, which Steven passed
with flying colors. Then … he was on to a second stage: finding
an internship where he could get hands-on training. He asked
his Career Coordinator to help him with this. Together, they
contacted Dogs Days of Buffalo, an animal day care and training
facility. It turned out that the manager of Dog Days was an ABC
certified dog trainer and he was willing to have Steven intern and
gain experience at his facility.
After much discussion and coordination it was determined that
Steven’s internship would consist of three progressive and
ordered phases. The first phase would allow Steven to observe
how Dog Days was run and gain an understanding of what
services they offered to dogs and their owners. The second
phase would be attending an obedience class with a “new”

---
## Page 428
client dog that needed to be trained and the dog’s owner.
Steven would learn the techniques and methods used to train
the dog to obey his or her owner from the Dog Days staff
working with this dog and would practice training the dog (under
supervision), using their techniques. The third and final phase
would be teaching an obedience class. Each phase would be 6
weeks long.
Steven started by observing the obedience class and learning
how he might apply all the information he had been studying as
he watched staff train dogs to obey their owners. He also
observed the techniques the trainers used and what commands
they employed to get the desired responses. This stage proved
to be a very valuable experience for Steven as he was able to
observe and then discuss a variety of dog behaviors and
training techniques with his supervisor and some of the Dog
Days staff members.
For the second phase of his internship, a CIP staff member was
gracious enough to let Steven borrow her dog as a subject to be
trained during the practicum part of his internship. The dog was
an extremely willing participant and excelled in class and Steven
was able to assist the trainers as he started using all that he had
learned during his six week “observation” phase.
Then, Steven was on to the most difficult part: phase three:
teaching an obedience class. This task produced an anxiety
response from Steven and he struggled in the beginning. But
with time, lots of practice, and encouragement from his
internship supervisor, Dog Days staff, and his Career
Coordinator he was able to gain his confidence back. He also
began to understand that he was not just training dogs, but their
owners as well. Once he realized that, everything else fell into
place and Steven was able to successfully complete his
internship.

---
## Page 429
Steven worked on finishing his final exam requirement and
passed his final with flying colors. He is now a Certified Dog
Trainer and happy to be turning his “special interest” into a
rewarding career.
Victoria Biligovskaya,
Career Coordinator, CIP Amherst
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
TEACHING INSIGHT: Steven’s story highlights how a “special
interest” might turn into a viable career path. Over the years I have
worked with students who focused their “special interest” on cars
(makes, models, history of cars, what makes them run), and so on.
Some of these young adults ended up in careers where they restored
cars, became mechanics, body shop employees, or retail car sales
people.
However, for many ASD/LD students, the opposite is often the case.
As your students research their “special interest” areas and look at
their transferrable skills, as well as their strengths and their limitations,
they will find that many “special interest” areas do not translate
realistically as viable options for a career path or future career.
For instance, I worked with a student who wanted to become a sports
announcer because he had the intrinsic ability to memorize all the
player statistics in a specific sport that was his “special interest.”
Acknowledging abilities and giving a “special interest” area its due,
while being realistic, helps our students choose a career path that is
appropriate to their skill set.
This next segment will help you help each individual figure out if his or
her “special interest” area can be turned into a job or career.
TRANSFERABLE SKILLS AND CAREER
CHOICES

---
## Page 430
Tell your students that transferable skills are skills they have developed
during high school, college, or vocational training. They are also skills
they acquired while volunteering, completing internships, and during
entry level part-time jobs. These skills will also be used to identify
appropriate career or training for a career path options as they start to
prepare for meaningful work in an area that interests them. Being able
to tell others that they have transferable skills will help prospective
employers know that the person they are interviewing is qualified.
Explain that these skills are important to write down and organize as
they will be used to help each person figure out what skills he or she
already possesses. This document can help each individual match his
or her skills to a job or be used to develop additional skills he or she will
need as each person pursues a specific career path.
This document will also be used to create a resume, cover letters, and
become a central part of each person’s career and ability portfolio. It
will also help each person prepare for interviews later on in this chapter.
The following student handouts on pages 188–192 explain to your
students what transferable skills are and why they are important.
Transferable Skills
You might be surprised to know that you already have many of
the skills you will need to have in order to do a specific job or
pursue the career of your choice.
Transferable skills are skills you acquired while living your life, in
school, through specific vocational training, or in college. You
also gained transferable skills while volunteering, interning, or
while performing an entry level job. These skills will help you find
your next job, or prepare for a chosen career.

---
## Page 431
Transferable skills will also help you prepare a resume and help
you create career and ability portfolios. When you start to fill out
the transferable skills worksheet, be sure to check any skills you
have acquired that will help you show a prospective employer
that you can do the job!
Why are transferable skills important?
The following example will help you understand why:
•
Two equally qualified people have applied for a job as a
computer programmer. At the job interview, each person is
asked if they think that they would be good at the job. The first
person answers with a simple “yes”, the second one also
answers “yes,” and continues to discuss why he or she thinks
they would be good at the job and gives specific examples that
demonstrate this.
•
Both applicants for the job are well qualified for the job, but the
second candidate has better “communication skills,” because
he or she is able to talk about his or her transferable skills when
asked for the information. The ability to do this gives the
advantage to the second candidate.
Where do I start?
On pages 188–191 are lists of the most important transferable
skills needed to get jobs in the modern workplace. By checking
off the skills you already have, you can see what your strengths
and limitations are. Then, you can look for opportunities to
develop and practice your underdeveloped skills so that
regardless of your academic achievements, you will be able to
show others just how skillful you are. So let’s get started, list
your top five transferable skills here!
1.  ________________________________

---
## Page 432
2.  ________________________________
3.  ________________________________
4.  ________________________________
5.  ________________________________

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. Place a check next to the skills you possess.
2. Circle or underline skills you would like to acquire.
Sensitivity to others:
•
Able to maintain a deep interest in the concerns and feelings
of others.
•
Inclined to find ways to help people.
Insight into others:
•
Have an understanding of what makes people do what they
do.
•
Tolerant of the actions of others.
•
Good at reading the moods of others.
Openness:

---
## Page 433
•
Able to encourage communication with all people at all
levels.
•
Inclined to share personal experiences and trust people.
Respect:
•
Able to take the feelings, needs, thoughts, wishes, and
preferences of others (including other cultures and races)
into consideration, and give them worth and value.
Speaking:
•
Able to present information clearly and confidently to other
individuals and groups.
•
Can maintain good eye contact and can keep the attention of
an audience or individual.
Active listening:
•
Able to devote full attention to what other people are saying.
•
Take the time to understand the points being made, ask
questions as needed, and do not interrupt inappropriately.
Conversation:
•
Able to speak clearly and listen attentively.
•
 Able to attend to another person, not solely to self.
•
Able to seek clarification where necessary.
•
Able to use body language appropriately.
Persuasion:
•
Able to influence people, their beliefs, and actions.

---
## Page 434
•
Able to win cooperation and support for ideas or activities
from others.
Team membership:
•
Able to work easily with groups of people and show loyalty
and commitment to the team’s objectives.
•
Able to accept each member’s viewpoint equally.
Team participation:
•
Able to openly express views and opinions within a group.
•
Can demonstrate willingness to take on tasks and
responsibilities.
Leadership:
•
Have the ability to communicate a vision or goal to others
and lead them towards achieving it.
•
Can push for action and results, and wins the support and
help of others.
Planning:
•
Can create clear goals and identify and find the resources
(e.g., time, people, materials) needed to achieve them.
•
Able to schedule tasks so that work is completed on time.
Time management:
•
Able to take the time to organize events and tasks carefully
so as to use time efficiently.
•
Use calendar/planner to ensure tasks are undertaken;
deadlines met, and to plan ahead.

---
## Page 435
Practical:
•
Able to use equipment, tools, or technology effectively.
•
Can follow instructions and show willingness to use whatever
tools or technology are required.
Computing:
•
Can confidently use a computer to write documents, browse
the Internet, and use email programs.
•
Can save files, locate them efficiently, and print them.
Problem solving:
•
Able to seek answers and find the cause of problems.
•
Can choose effective solutions and take the necessary action
to resolve them.
Learning:
•
Able to seek new opportunities to learn.
•
Able to demonstrate an interest in learning new things and in
personal development.
•
Able to seek feedback to improve understanding.
Adaptability:
•
Able to adapt easily to new challenges and open to new
ways of doing things.
•
Able to change or rearrange plans or actions to deal with
changing situations.
Goal setting:

---
## Page 436
•
Able to make a decision about what is needed or wanted
and able to determine when it is to be achieved.
•
Can stay on task, stay committed to a goal, and deal with
setbacks realistically.
Initiative:
•
Able to take the initiative to improve a situation.
•
Able to seek opportunities to influence events or make
decisions.
Independence:
•
Able to perform tasks effectively with minimum help,
approval, or without direct supervision.
Achievement motivation:
•
Able to exhibit the drive (or determination) to succeed and
excel at tasks.
•
Able to exceed expectations.
•
Able to show confidence in abilities and expect to succeed at
all tasks agreed upon.
Dependability:
•
Can show reliability, responsibility, and dependability in
fulfilling duties.
•
Can carefully check all work to ensure all details have been
considered.
Professionalism:
•
Able to remain calm and self-controlled under stressful
situations.

---
## Page 437
•
Able to stay focused and to deliver the best interests of the
organization at all time.
•
Able to exhibit good hygiene and maintains an appropriate
dress code.
•
Able to keep desk and work area clean and organized.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Career Skills – Summary and Action Plan
Now that you are familiar with some of the most important
transferable skills desired by employers, it is a good idea to
make a list of those you have developed the most. These will be
some of the things you would mention when writing your resume,
along with some examples of how they were developed or used.
You can also use this information when preparing for a job
interview. An employer will be looking to see what skills you
have and for evidence of how you have actually applied them.
And remember … prospective employers won’t accept that you
have these skills just because you say so. Fill in the section
below to summarize your most developed skills.
My Most Developed Skills
Note: Be sure to state how you developed this skill and where
you use or have used it.
1.  ________________________________
2.  ________________________________

---
## Page 438
3.  ________________________________
4.  ________________________________
5.  ________________________________
6.  ________________________________
What about those skills you have not yet been able to develop?
These could be the very things that could go against you when
looking for a job. Remember, most employers want just about all
of the skills mentioned on the transferable skills worksheet. So
why not set targets for developing some of your weaker skills?
Start by listing some of the skills you would like to get more
experience in and then note some of the activities you could
become involved in to help develop them. Ask for suggestions
from friends and advisors if you can’t think of activities yourself.
Skill I Need or Want to Develop
List the skills you want to obtain here along with your ideas of
how to develop them, such as activities you might get involved
in, training you might sign up for, or a course you might enroll in.
1.  ________________________________
