© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 439
CAREER AND ABILITY PORTFOLIOS
Your students are now ready to take their academic portfolios, their
Person Centered Plans (PCPs), and their transferable skills list and
begin creating career/ability portfolios that will show prospective
employers that they have the qualifications and abilities they are looking
for.
Explain that each student will use his or her career/ability portfolio(s) to
highlight their interests, skills, and achievements to date.
Tell the young men and women you teach, counsel, mentor, and advise
that career and ability portfolios will help them showcase their talents
as they begin creating resumes and prepare for job interviews.
This is a good point in time to remind students of Dr. Temple Grandin’s
work in this arena. As mentioned at the beginning of this chapter, Dr.
Temple Grandin often talks to young adults on the ASD and LD
Spectrum about the importance of creating portfolios of their work in
order to “sell” themseles.
In her movie “Temple Grandin” (2010), there is a scene where she
brings her sketches to the meat processing plant managers she is
interviewing with and visually shows them her work. Her detailed
sketches (true works of art) showed the people she was meeting with
what she was capable of. She showed her prospective employers that
she had developed a more humane way of handling animals in cattle
processing plants through her ability portfolio.
Temple knew she was socially awkward and not the best of speakers.
She also knew that if she could convey what she wanted to say visually
the people in charge just might “get it.” They did. As they viewed her
drawings of cattle processing plants and listened to her descriptions of
the cattle procedures she wanted to implement, they realized her ideas
might end up completely changing their industry.
Once she had their attention, she was able to explain how her system
would result in less cattle deaths (at the time, many cows were
panicking in the delousing pits and drowning), and a calm

---
## Page 440
slaughterhouse. Temple Grandin instinctively knew that she had made
her point and managed to get “her foot in the door.”
Creating Career and Ability Portfolios
Explain to your students that the next logical step in preparing for the
world of work is showcasing all they have been learning in this chapter,
highlighting their transferable skills, and utilizing (if applicable) any work
experiences they have gained following the Career Continuum such as
volunteering, internships, as well as any part- or full-time work to date.
Explain that career and ability portfolios become filled with information
that will increase their chances of not only getting an internship or job,
but allow each one to be sure this is the direction they want to pursue.
Tell the young men and women you work with that they need to see the
career and ability portfolio process as one of “self-investment.”
Explain that individual portfolios (and they can develop more than one
if they like) will help each person showcase his or her talents to a
prospective employer.
Tell students that (for decades) artists, photographers, architects,
designers, and writers in search of work have used career and ability
portfolios to showcase their abilities and their talents. And that others –
especially those on the ASD or LD Spectrum – are now discovering
how these types of portfolios can help them tell others about what they
can do.
Explain that many students are discovering that portfolios offer a better
way to demonstrate their learning experiences and abilities and that this
adds value to their potential for specific kinds of work. Career and
ability portfolios are a viable way to communicate to others what an
individual is capable of.
Tell students that each career and ability portfolio should be personal
and contain relevant information. Items that should be included include
(but are not limited to) personal information, evaluations, samples of

---
## Page 441
work, awards, and acknowledgments. Career and ability portfolios are
often kept in a simple three-ring binder or online as an electronic
portfolio and updated often.
The student handout on pages 194–195 will help students create
career and ability portfolios.
How to Create Career and Ability Portfolios
Now that you know what your interests are and what your
transferable skills are, it’s time to put together a career and
ability portfolio (or in some cases, more than one). A career and
ability portfolio does not replace a resume, it “augments” it. It is
usually presented during an interview and not sent out with your
resume. However, your career and ability portfolio can also be
useful as you create your resume as it will remind you of all you
have accomplished!
Career and ability portfolios will help you:
•
Prepare for interviews.
•
Convince others of your skills, abilities, and qualities.
•
Communicate 
clearly 
(by 
focusing 
the 
interview
conversation).
•
Showcase your skills.
•
Demonstrate the results of your work.
•
Document your accomplishments and results.
•
Create a personal database.
•
Assess your own progress in your career development.

---
## Page 442
•
See and evaluate the patterns in your own work preferences
and values.
What goes into a career and ability portfolio?
It’s a collection of artifacts and materials that represent work
related events in your life. Your career and ability portfolio
provides “evidence” of your potential by demonstrating what you
have accomplished in the past.
Artifacts
An artifact is any tangible object or item that can represent your
accomplishments and qualities.
These artifacts work the same way they do in archeology.
Archeologists reconstruct a civilization from the artifacts they
uncover. Your portfolio reconstructs your abilities, skills,
achievements, and accomplishments that have occurred as you
developed your talents and abilities. In both cases, the artifacts
are fragments that represent pieces of the whole.
Artifacts include:
•
Things you have invented, created, or made. These might be
original photographs, artwork, or writing. It might include
examples of academic research as well as reports, computer
print outs, graphics, handouts, published articles, invitations,
programs, brochures, fliers, newsletters, and “before and
after” editing samples of your work.
•
Something you’ve created to summarize or “represent”
things you have done. This might be a summary of
evaluations from a workshop you gave, achievements from a
career specific training course that you took, or a chart

---
## Page 443
showing contributions you made to a project and to the
project’s team.
•
A statement of your philosophy, or you could symbolize your
philosophy by using an image or developing a collage of
images, photographs, and artwork. (This is what Dr. Temple
Grandin did. You can look up examples of her career and
ability portfolios on the Internet. She drew her ideas for
processing cattle in a more humane way because she knew
she would have trouble telling someone about them. She
made a series of sketches to portray what she had in mind.)
•
A photo of you accepting an award or a short video clip of
you making a presentation.
•
Thank you notes, letters of recommendation, photos of you
on the job, and articles about you.
Some things to remember:
•
Your individual skills, qualities, and knowledge can come
from many different places. Dare to be unique, dare to be
different, dare to be as original as you can possibly be.
•
Use your academic portfolio and your Person Centered Plan
(PCP) as a starting point.
•
Do Internet research for examples of the wide and varied
scope or format your ability portfolio might take.
•
Employers are interested in your skills, abilities, experience,
or personal qualities that relate to the specific work they need
to have done. Therefore, you might think about tailoring,
customizing, or even creating more than one career and
ability portfolio to use during interviews and as you apply for
a variety of jobs or positions.
•
Your “purpose” is to demonstrate that you have successfully
accomplished the tasks represented in the portfolio (which

---
## Page 444
should parallel the job description), to support your assertion
that you can do the job.
•
Whenever you make a career and ability portfolio (for any
specific or temporary event), your choice of artifacts from
your collection will depend on your specific audience and
your purpose.
Be sure to:
•
Use a professional binder or notebook that takes clear page
protectors.
•
Use clean copies, rather than originals.
•
Create a table of contents.
•
Organize by categories relevant to the job.
•
Use dividers to separate categories.
•
Insert a summary caption that explains the significance of
each artifact.
•
Use graphics, visuals, and color, if possible.
Here’s an example of a career ability portfolio e-template:

---
## Page 445
Section IV: Creating a Resume and
Preparing for Interviews
TEACHING INSIGHT: Helping young adults on the ASD or LD
Spectrum create resumes and prepare for interviews can be a
daunting, challenging, and difficult task. It’s hard for students “on the
Spectrum” to understand the importance of both processes.
The first and most important thing you can do as your students work
through this section is to give each one a sense of purpose by
explaining how they will benefit from a professional resume (it will be
their primary marketing tool), and how being completely prepared for
the interview process will lessen their anxiety and stress on the day of

---
## Page 446
an interview. Explain that confidence in their own abilities as they note
their accomplishments on paper and become skilled at answering
questions an interviewer might pose will really pay off in the long run.
But most importantly tell your students that this is a good opportunity
to let their creativity “shine.” In the past we’ve had CIP students design
resumes to look like a marketing brochure (for a customer service
job), a fine-dining menu (for a job as an assistant chef), and one who
listed accomplishments, lessons learned, and how she might have
done things differently next to each job she’d done in the past. In all
three cases, the students were hired.
With the Internet it’s easy to just click and create a resume.
Encouraging your students to take the time to tailor theirs individually
will make the task have more meaning. As your students prepare for
job interviews, encourage students to let technology play a starring role.
Allowing students to video tape each other and do all the preparation
involved in doing the taping, preparing to be taped, and watching the
results, will give students the honest feedback they need and help them
improve by leaps and bounds. Our students particularly like watching
the first video that was made of their “mock interview” and comparing it
to one made after practicing and honing their skills.
Resumes
Explain to your students that their resumes will be the way that each
individual makes a first impression on a hiring manager. It’s the key tool
that allows a prospective employer to see – at a glance – that the
person sending in the resume is qualified for the job.
Tell your students that you will help them create resumes that
showcase their achievements and past job experiences so that each
individual can show a potential interviewer why he or she is the best
candidate for the job.
The student handouts on pages 196–203 explain how to create a
resume. They include worksheets, templates, ideas, formats, forms,

---
## Page 447
and suggestions.
Developing and Creating a Resume
Your resume will compete with many others. Developing and
creating a good resume is one of the most important things you
will do as you prepare for the world of work. The following
suggestions, ideas, worksheets, and templates will help you
create a resume and learn how to write a cover letter to go with
your resume.
The purpose of a resume is to get an interview. Potential
employers will ask you to submit a resume so they can
prescreen candidates for the required skills, experience, and
abilities needed for a specific job opening. If you do not have a
resume, you will not get an interview and prospective employers
at job fairs or other recruiting events will not be willing to speak
to you about your skills and work experience.
Potential employers will scan your resume when they receive it
and compare your qualifications with the qualifications for the job
opening. To compete for an interview your resume needs to
include:
•
Your education.
•
Any school activities that focus on your accomplishments.
•
Any relevant career preparation and training.
•
Your transferable skills.
•
Your strengths.
•
Your past work history.

---
## Page 448
•
Statements that emphasize what you can do for the
employer.
Some things to remember:
•
For an interview in a highly competitive industry or higher-
level position, your resume will need to focus on your
accomplishments.
•
For an entry level position you should highlight your
strengths, school work, and activities.
•
You should highlight and showcase your achievements and
relevant work experiences to date.
•
Be truthful! Claiming that you’re able to speak another
language fluently when you do not, referring to yourself as
the world’s greatest (fill in the blank ______________ ), or
making false academic or ability claims is not an option.
•
You should demonstrate through real-time examples (not
dreams or hopes) why you are the best candidate for the job.
•
You should strive to have a professional, tailored document
by keeping the format clean looking, clear, and easy-to-read.
Getting started!
Think about how you want to present yourself. Hiring managers
have many, many resumes to review for each job they have
posted. Because of this, they are always looking for a reason to
put yours in the “no go” file.
When CareerBuilder.com asked hiring managers what would
make 
them 
automatically 
dismiss 
a 
candidate 
from
consideration, here is what they said:

---
## Page 449
•
Typos (an automatic discard).
•
Resumes that copied large amounts of wording from the job
posting.
•
Resumes with an inappropriate email address. Here are
examples they gave: marienut@… mail.com; partyanimal@…
mail.com, and cuteandlegal@…mail.com.
•
Resumes that don’t include a list of skills.
•
Resumes that are more than two pages long.
•
Resumes that are printed on decorative or brightly colored
paper.
However, they did stress that creativity was not completely out
of the question. Here are some examples they gave that made
positive impressions!
•
One candidate sent his resume in the form of an oversized
Rubik’s Cube, where you had to push the tiles around to
align the resume. He was hired.
•
Another job applicant crafted his resume to look like Google
search results for the “perfect candidate.” This candidate
ultimately wasn’t hired, but was considered.
•
Another candidate who had been a “stay-at-home” mom
listed her skill set as nurse, housekeeper, chef, teacher, bio-
hazard clean up crew member, fight referee, taxi driver,
secretary, tailor, personal shopping assistant, and therapist.
She was hired.
The important thing to remember – whether you decide to be
creative with your job application or take a more traditional route
– is that your resume must be flawless. Ask three or four people
to edit your resume, as sometimes you need an objective eye to
notice that some content may be sloppy, inappropriate, or
irrelevant. If you send resumes to employers and are not getting
interviews have your teacher, career counselor, job coach,

---
## Page 450
advisor, or parents look carefully at your resume. If you do not
get the interview, your resume is not doing its job.
Here’s a basic checklist of things “to do”
•
Do include your name, address, phone number, and email
address on your resume.
•
Do make sure your resume is error free – no typographical,
spelling, or grammatical errors.
•
Do select an appropriate format that includes a clean,
professional appearance, and typed in an easy-to-read font.
•
Do make sure your resume is brief, well organized, and
focused on the position that you are applying for.
Here’s a basic list of “do nots”!
•
Do not include a date on your resume; you should put the
date on your cover letter.
•
Do not include personal pronouns such as “I” in your
resume.
•
Do not include abbreviations in the body of your resume.
•
Do not include personal information such as height, weight,
social security number, driver’s license number, race,
religion, marital status, or political affiliation.
•
Do not include miscellaneous details such as salary
requirements, number of children you have or that are in your
family, hobbies, or sports interests.
Key Words

---
## Page 451
There are certain words that are considered “key words” that
are good to use on your resume. These will catch a hiring
manager’s interest. Mention them in your resume and at
interviews but remember not to “go overboard” or you’ll sound
insincere. These words are:
initiative
dynamic
team player
self-motivated
You can also talk in terms of action by using good, strong verbs
on your resume or when filling out job applications and during
the interview process. These words are:
led
achieved
completed
coordinated
delegated
delivered
identified
presented
promoted
reported
resolved
organized
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

that will suit your personality and your personal style.

Here’s an example of an entry level resume that someone with no
formal work experience might send.

---
## Page 455
Creating Cover Letters
When distributing your resume to a potential employer, you
should always include a cover letter. Here are some useful
suggestions for writing a cover letter.

---
## Page 456
“What” is a cover letter?
A cover letter is a brief letter that you write to a potential
employer expressing your interest in an advertised job. The
cover letter states why you are applying for the job. In this letter,
you should “briefly” describe your strengths and skills and how
these match the job you are applying for.
“Why” write a cover letter?
A cover letter serves as an introduction to your resume. Sending
just a resume in an envelope does not look as professional as
sending a cover letter with your resume.
“How” to write a cover letter:
•
Like a resume, a cover letter is a formal document and
needs to look professional.
•
Your cover letter should be no longer than one page.
•
It should be typed in a business-letter format, which contains
the following information in this order:
1. Your name and contact information.
2. The name and address of the person you are writing to.
3. The date (including the month, day, and year).
4. The 
greeting 
or 
salutation 
(Example: 
Dear
____________________,).
5. The body of the letter (two short paragraphs).
6. The closing phrase (Examples: “Sincerely” or “Thank
you”).
7. Your signature.

---
## Page 457
8. Your typed full name.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Here’s an example of a cover letter sent to Mr. Sam White at Sunshine
Publishers from a woman named Sarah Jones.

---
## Page 458
How to Send your Resume and Cover Letter:
Spelling and grammar are important and could make the
difference in whether or not you get a job interview. Make sure
that your resume and cover letter are proofread by another
person and that it is perfect.
1. By mail:
If you are mailing your resume and cover letter, it should be
printed on clean, white paper with no tears or wrinkles.
2. By email:
You’ll be sending your resume as an attachment to your email, so
use a format that most businesses can open. Microsoft Word is
the most common professional word processing program. Name
your resume document appropriately. Using your name as the
document’s name will help an employer find the file on his or her
computer.
If sending a resume by email, you still need to send a cover letter.
You can include your cover letter document as an attachment or
you can write the cover letter in the body of the email. Remember
to use a professional email address.
3. Online:
Some companies require all cover letters and resumes to be sent
to them via their website. If this is the case, provide all the
information they are requesting and be sure to proofread
everything you have written before hitting the “send” button.

---
## Page 459
12 of the Most Outlandish Resume Mistakes
1. Candidate called himself a genius and invited the hiring
manager to interview him at his apartment.
2. Candidate’s cover letter talked about her family being in “the
mob.”
3. Candidate applying for a management job listed “gator hunting”
as a skill.
4. Candidate’s resume included “phishing” as a hobby.
5. Candidate specified that her resume was set up to be sung to
the tune of “The Brady Bunch.”
6. Candidate highlighted the fact that he was “Homecoming Prom
Prince” in 1984.
7. Candidate claimed to be able to speak “Antartican” when
applying for a job to work in Antarctica.
8. Candidate’s resume had a photo of the applicant reclining in a
hammock under the headline “Hi, I’m Sarah and I’m looking for
a job.”
9. Candidate’s resume was decorated with pink rabbits.
10.Candidate listed “to make dough” as the objective on the
resume. He was not applying for a job at a pizza shop!
11.Candidate applying for an accounting job said he was “deetail-
oriented” and spelled the company’s name incorrectly.
12 Candidate’s cover letter contained “LOL.”
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 460
TEACHING INSIGHT: Share with your students the 12 most
outlandish resume mistakes on page 202. Tell your students that
CareerBuilders.com compiled these by polling 2298 hiring managers.
Rosemary 
Haefner, 
vice 
president 
of 
human 
resources 
at
CareerBuilder.com, says “These job seekers were probably trying to
be clever and stand out. It backfired and these resumes went straight
to the ‘no’ pile.” These mistakes should generate a lively discussion as
well as smiles and laughter while serving as a reminder to take the
resume creating process seriously.
Filling Out Applications
Every employer will require you to complete an application. This
is a document that will provide the company with some basic
information about you, such as your name, where they can
contact you, where you went to school, your social security
number (so they can pay you), and other items. It is very
important to fill the application out neatly and accurately.
There are three possible ways to complete a job application:
1. Write on the paper application and then return it to the place
of business.
2. Type your responses onto a downloaded version of the
application, then print it out or email it to the employer.
3. Complete the online application and submit it electronically.
Where do I find applications?
First, you can try finding the company’s job application online. If
you don’t know the company’s website, try searching for it on

---
## Page 461
Google, Bing, or Yahoo. Once you are on the employer’s
homepage, you will have to look carefully to find the jobs link.
Some words to look for are:
•
Careers.
•
Employment opportunities.
•
Job opportunities.
•
Join our team.
•
Open positions.
•
Job openings.
•
Job bank.
Once you find the right page, look for the actual on-line job
application. You might be able to download it and save it to your
computer, then fill it out, or you may have to complete it directly
online.
Sometimes you can obtain a paper application at the actual
place of business you want to work, such as in a store or a
restaurant. If that is what is required, make sure you are dressed
in neat, clean clothes and that you have showered and combed
your hair. You want to try to make a good first impression.
When you walk into the place of business, find an employee and
wait until they are not too busy to talk.
Then walk up to him/her and say something like, “Excuse me, I
would like to pick up a job application. Can you help me with
this? Or … May I please see the manager?”
At this time, you should request two copies because if you make
a mistake on the first copy, you will still have a second one to
use. Make sure you say “thank you” before you leave with the

---
## Page 462
applications. You can fill the application out later when you are in
a place where you can concentrate.
How do I fill out the application?
The application will most likely be a form. Make sure that all
information requested is factually and truthfully stated. Check for
accuracy. Ask your teacher, advisor, career counselor, family, or
friend for help if you do not understand something. Also ask
someone to proofread it for you. Then return the application as
requested either in person, by mail, or via email.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
PREPARING FOR A JOB INTERVIEW
Tell the young men and women you are working with that late teens
and young adults on the ASD or LD Spectrum need extra coaching and
help to be successful at job interviews and that the best way to do this
is to practice through a process called the mock interview.
Explain that being selected for an interview means that an individual’s
resume and cover letter that were submitted for consideration told the
hiring manager that the person applying for an advertised job is
qualified and might be a good choice for the job.
Let your students know that it is normal for most people to feel nervous
before an interview. Being able to practice interview skills through the
mock interviewing process will help your students learn the fine art of
interviewing and interview preparation so that these doubts and
insecurities can be overcome.
Explain that the skills they have been learning throughout this book –
that have helped them become successful in social situations and at
school – will now help them prepare for the interview process.

---
## Page 463
Explain that learning about the interview process and how to prepare
for it will happen through class discussion, handouts, activities, and
practice.
Tell your students they will have many opportunities to role play and
practice their individual mock interview scenarios until they have the
confidence to do this on video tape.
Explain that each taped mock interview process will help your students
refine and hone their skills until each person is ready to go on a real-
time and place interview.
Helping Students Understand the Interview
Process (The Who, What, Where, When, How,
and Why!)
First impressions count:
Explain to your students that their resumes or ability portfolios alone
will not help them secure a job. However, a job interview will. Therefore,
making a good first impression is the most important thing they can do.
Tell the young men and women you work with that a job interview is the
time and place to put their best foot forward.
Ask your students what they think will make a good first impression at
an interview. Write their ideas on a white board.
Then list the five most accepted ways your students can make a great
first impression on an interviewer or interview team. Compare both lists.
1. Extending a hand:
•
Explain that this means being the first person to reach out
for the handshake. If it’s a group interview, shake everyone’s
hand if possible. Remind your students of the guidelines from
the TSA’s Field Marshall training handbook: A handshake
should be firm – not too hard and last only two to three

---
## Page 464
seconds. Maintain eye contact and a relaxed but not sloppy
posture.
2. Learning and using names:
•
Explain that this means trying to use the interviewer’s name
at least three times during the interview. And, using their
appropriate title of Mr. or Ms. (or Dr.) unless invited to do
otherwise. If it’s a group interview, applicants should try to
use at least two names of those present in addition to the
primary interviewer.
3. Listening:
•
Explain that the person being interviewed should listen and
let the interviewer bring up a topic or ask questions first.
4. Asking intelligent questions:
•
Tell students that when asked if they have any questions
they should keep in mind that the employer wants someone
who will get the job done and that this means formulating
questions about the employer’s needs. Preparing questions
in advance based on their research about the company is
the best way to handle this in an interview.
•
Explain that when an employer does ask if they have any
questions it does not mean they should ask about salaries,
hours they will work, or how many days of vacation they will
get. Here are examples of some appropriate questions your
students might ask:
•
What do you believe will be the most pressing needs of your
company in the next year?
•
What are the major issues facing this department and how
might my skills be best used?

---
## Page 465
•
Is the job one that requires a lot of teamwork or working solo
on projects?
5. Remembering to say “thank you!”
•
Tell the young men and women you work with that it can’t
hurt to apply the “rule of thanks … times three,” and that this
means saying “thank you” three times; at the beginning, in
the middle, and the at end of an interview.
•
Explain that thanks might be expressed in the following
ways:
•
Thank you for the chance to speak with you in person about
this position. I appreciate having a chance to learn more
about your company.
•
Thank you for your time and I hope to be hearing from you.
MOCK INTERVIEWS
TEACHING INSIGHT: The video taped mock interview is one of the
best ways to help ASD and LD adolescents and young adults (ages
16–26) prepare for an actual employment interview. It allows the
young men and women you are working with to gain experience and
confidence in their own abilities as they practice by answering the
questions interviewers are likely to pose.
As students watch and review their individual video tapes, it’s a
chance for each person to see how a potential employer or hiring
manager might perceive them, a chance to reflect upon this reality,
and an opportunity to decide what part of the interview process they
want to practice, change, or just get better at.
The Mock Interview Process

---
## Page 466
Explain to your students that you will be role playing the role of a hiring
manager. And, that as the person in this role, you will try to make the
interview as realistic as possible by asking questions that are typical of
those that might be asked for the type of position or job the individual
doing the mock interview is applying for. At some point during the mock
interview the person being video taped will also have the opportunity to
ask the interviewer questions that he or she might ask in a real
interview.
The mock interview takes about 30 minutes; then the mock interviewer
and the student will watch, discuss, and critique it. Explain that the
process is a positive and informative one and repeated until both
parties agree that the individual is ready for a real employment
interview.
Helping Students Prepare for the Mock
Interview:
Explain that students will have plenty of time to prepare for their mock
interviews and that each individual will work with the person who will
role play the interviewer before the taping takes place.
Give the student who will be interviewed a list of questions that you
might ask him or her during the interview (see handout below), so that
he or she can work independently or with you and/or others to prepare
for the interview.
Explain that answering the questions by describing past experiences
on the video tape is helpful and will give the person conducting the
mock interview a better idea of his or her qualifications.
Tell your students that both participants will enjoy the experience, that
it is worthwhile, and that it’s an extremely valuable and important way to
develop the interviewing skills they will need.
The student handout on pages 208–210 will help students prepare for
their individual mock interview video taping session.

---
## Page 467
Mock Interviews: Practice Makes Perfect
What is a mock interview?
•
A mock interview is a 30-minute video taped session of you
in an interview with your teacher, advisor, counselor, or peer
mentor. This person will be role playing a potential employer
who is conducting an interview with you for a job that you
really want to get.
•
A mock interview is a great chance for you to experience
what it would be like to be interviewed for a job. You prepare
for this interview with the same desire and intensity that you
would for a “real” job interview. That way the portrayal on the
video tape is as realistic as you and the role playing
interviewer can make it.
•
A mock interview will not only help you perfect your individual
interviewing technique, it will also allow you to get valuable
feedback and coaching on your performance.
•
The more you practice doing and creating mock video taped
interviews the more adept you will be when you go to your
first, actual job interview.
Preparation
•
Prepare for the session by researching a company or
industry of interest that you and your role playing interviewer
have agreed will be the focus of your video taped interview.
•
Investigate and identify the most common industry or
business traits sought for this job. This might be computer
technology, analytical skills, communication skills, technical

---
## Page 468
skills, business knowledge, customer service, nursing skills,
etc., that the job of your choice requires. Then it’s time for
rehearsal, rehearsal, and more rehearsal!
•
You and your mock interviewer will decide what the script of
your mock interview is going to be and what your lines are.
Then you’ll practice until you feel ready to do a dress
rehearsal.
•
On the day of the dress rehearsal interview you will simulate
the process of an interview from the time you get off the
elevator until you get back in the elevator to leave the
interview.
•
The last step – video taping your mock interview – will
happen when you are confident, know that you are well
prepared, well rehearsed, and are ready to “let the camera
roll.”
Here are some of the things you will be asked to talk about
during your mock interview:
•
What are your educational qualifications?
•
What past work experience do you have?
•
What extra-curricular activities did you take part in during
high school or college?
•
What are your outside interests and activities?
The mock interviewer may also ask you:
•
Do you think you can do the job?
•
Do you think you will be happy doing the job if I hire you?
•
Will you fit in my organization?
And he or she will want to know that you are qualified for the
specific job you are applying for and will ask:

---
## Page 469
•
What qualifications do you have that will make you
successful in the job?
•
How has your education prepared you for a career in this
field?
•
Why should I hire you?
•
Describe your most rewarding educational experience.
•
What are your long-range plans?
•
What motivates you?
•
What two or three accomplishments have given you the most
satisfaction? Why?
•
What do you expect to be earning in five years?
•
Which is more important to you: money or the type of job?
The mock interviewer may also ask:
•
How would you describe yourself?
•
How would a friend or a teacher describe you?
•
Why did you decide to seek a position with us?
•
What do you know about our company?
•
Are you willing to travel?
•
Are you willing to relocate?
•
What rewards do you expect in your career?
•
How do you determine success?
When the interviewer asks you if you have any questions you
might ask:
•
What characteristics does a successful person have at your
company?

---
## Page 470
•
Describe a typical first year assignment for a new employee
in this position.
•
What have been some of the major contributions made by
individuals who have held this job previously?
•
What are the problems I will be expected to solve?
•
What is the career path for this position?
•
Could you tell me about the people with whom I will be
working directly?
•
How is an employee evaluated?
•
When may I expect to hear from you regarding a hiring
decision?
On the day of the video taped mock interview:
•
Do everything you rehearsed.
•
Dress in your interview clothes and do your complete pre-
interview grooming.
•
Put your papers (copy of your resume, career and ability
portfolio) in your briefcase or backpack.
•
Stand outside the door and knock to enter.
•
Greet the interviewer with an enthusiastic handshake and
smile.
•
Listen to all questions asked. Ask for clarification if the
question is not clear.
•
Keep your answers concise and to the point – two to three
minutes long.
•
Make sure you are selling the product: You.
•
Ask the questions you have prepared to ask the interviewer.
•
Shake hands, give the interviewer a smile, and thank them
for their time.

---
## Page 471
Getting feedback:
•
Watch the video tape with the person or persons who helped
you prepare for the mock interview.
•
Observe your feelings while viewing the tape and listening to
your answers.
•
Are you coming across the way you intended?
•
Listen to feedback with an open mind, not defensively.
•
How do you think you would be perceived through the eyes
of someone who does not know you?
•
Learn from your performance.
•
If needed, ask for further assistance, a chance to work on
skills needed, and discuss the possibility of doing another
video taped mock interview at a later date.
Most importantly:
Know that mock interviews can be rehearsed, staged, and re-
video taped until you and your “mock interviewer” feel that you
are ready to take on the challenge of a real-time and place
interview. This is meant to be a positive and rewarding process
that will help you gain the confidence to show a prospective
employer that they will be making a good decision if they hire
you. And they will – because you’ve worked hard, prepared, and
are already showing them this in person!
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 472
TEACHING INSIGHT: There are websites that can help your students
prepare for the interview process and software that has been
developed for this purpose. This particular site (The Molly Porter
Website) also tracks your individual data and provides feedback and
strategies 
for 
improvement. 
The 
website 
is
www.jobinterviewtraining.net/meetmolly.aspx. This particular website
helps students prepare and practice “virtual” mock interviews. Have

---
## Page 473
your students take a look at this site and others to learn more about
what’s available online.
Here are three student handouts for your students to use as they
prepare for an actual interview. It can serve as a reminder or refresher
of all the things that they have learned in this subsection.
“They” Like Your Resume!
The hiring manager has called you and would like to interview
you for the job you applied for. He or she thinks you have the
qualifications to do the job and would like to meet you in person.
You are happy and excited about this. Here is a quick review of
all you’ve learned that will help you as you get ready for your
interview day.
Know that:
•
You’ve worked hard on understanding and learning all about
interviews with your teachers, career counselors, and
advisors.
•
You have practiced and rehearsed and made video tapes of
mock interviews and you know what to do and what to
expect.
•
Being offered an actual job interview will allow you to put all
that you have learned into practice.
Here are a few things to remember on your interview day:
•
Remember to take a deep breath and remind yourself that
this opportunity will give you a chance to hear more about
the job, what it entails, and to see if this is a job you might be
interested in doing.

---
## Page 474
•
Remember that you have prepared for this day and that as
the interview progresses, the hiring manager will get to know
you better and you will get to ask appropriate questions to
the interviewer about the job. You will also be able to find out
more about what the company/business does.
•
Remember that if things go well, this is the first step of a
longer and more detailed hiring process. This first interview
will allow you to determine if the job might be a “good fit” for
you and if you want to take any next steps.
First things first:
•
Find out as much as you can about the company, business,
or job so that you can show an informed interest in it.
Remember, the company is looking for somebody who fits
their needs: it is not concerned about helping you further
your career interests unless you can offer something useful
in return.
•
You will be a more attractive prospect if you can say why you
want to work for this company in particular than if you simply
explain your wish to be a PR executive, a receptionist, or a
software developer, working for whichever company will take
you on.
Ask for directions when you set up your interview
appointment. Why?
•
On the day of the interview, you arrive ten minutes early only
to learn that the manager works out of a satellite office
located thirty miles away. Always ask for the location of the
interview and then get directions before your interview day.
Take a copy of your resume with you. Why?
•
The employer’s briefcase was left at home, the receptionist
is out sick, the personnel department is out to lunch – all of

---
## Page 475
these reasons and more are why you should always carry
your resume with you. Even if the employer doesn’t need an
extra copy, it can be helpful for you to have a copy of your
resume on hand for reference especially if you’ve created
more than one resume when applying for different kinds of
jobs.
Bring your career and ability portfolio. Why?
•
Having samples of your work, copies of project summaries,
or published articles, records of achievements, photographs,
and other artifacts will help an employer gauge the quality of
your work and will show him or her that you have come to the
interview with more than a casual interest in the work to be
done.
Greet the receptionist when you arrive at the interview office.
Why?
•
Your biggest ally is often the person who sits in the front
office. The receptionist or administrative assistant is often
asked – or will volunteer opinions – to the hiring manager.
Follow the protocol you learned in the Skills for Life section of
this book and extend simple courtesies to everyone you
meet.
Have your reference list handy! Why?
•
Hiring managers always check the references of potential
employees. If asked for this and you have this with you on
the day of the interview it will save time in having to mail it in
or call back with the information. It will also demonstrate that
you came prepared to be taken seriously and hired.
Dress according to the setting in which you’ll be working.
Why?

---
## Page 476
•
First impressions count. You want to be physically seen as a
potential and valued new hire. Wild ties with cartoon
characters are best left at home. So are jeans and T-shirts.
Long sleeve shirts for men with ties and if you have one, a
sports jacket with neatly pressed pants and polished shoes
will show the employer you are taking the interview process
seriously. Dresses or skirts with an accompanying
jacket/blazer or pantsuits are appropriate for women.
•
Sometimes a company is known for its employees dressing
casually (Google and Amazon are two examples). Do your
Internet research and find out what the dress code of the
company appears to be. Then, if it is extremely casual, plan
to dress a little more formally than everyone else at the
company so that you are in line with the company’s culture
and not overdressed.
•
If in doubt use “The Donkey Rule” and poll five people you
trust for their opinion on this matter.
Shut off your cell phone. You know why!
•
Take the time to turn your phone off in order to avoid having
the best moment of your interview interrupted by your friend
calling to tell you they are at the mall, coffee shop, hanging
out with friends, etc.
Don’t forget to:
•
Smile occasionally.
•
Sit up straight in your chair and face the interviewer.
•
Try your best to sit still as you talk.
•
Use an upbeat tone of voice as you talk.
•
Answer each question politely.

---
## Page 477
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
The Top Ten Interview Questions
Here are the top ten interview questions that you can prepare
answers for in advance. Rehearse these the night before the
interview – writing out your answers and if you need to,
memorizing these and then having a friend or family member
rehearse them to you.
Top ten interview questions
•
What is your greatest strength?
•
What is your greatest weakness?
•
How do you handle stress and pressure?
•
Describe a difficult work situation/project and how you
overcame it.
•
How do you evaluate success?
•
Why are you leaving or have you left your job?
•
Why do you want this job?
•
Why should we hire you?
•
What are your goals for the future?
•
Tell me about yourself.
Be prepared to ask questions!

---
## Page 478
•
It’s not only the employer who gets to ask questions
during the interview, so you need to be ready with four or
five questions of your own.
•
Why is this a good idea? It shows the potential employer
that you really want this job and have been giving thought
to your decision to work there.
Here are some suggestions:
•
What do you think is the most important thing I need to
know about this job?
•
What kinds of opportunities might there be for me to learn
new skills here?
•
Is there anything else you need to know about me?
•
Will I be trained on the job by you or someone else?
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit
After Your Interview
After your interview be sure to send a “thank you” note.
•
You survived your interview and you are feeling great about
how you handled all the questions. Now it’s time to send a
“thank you” note to the person who interviewed you.
•
You will do this for every interview that you prepare for and
go on. A “thank you note” is not a courtesy, it is a critical part

---
## Page 479
of the interview process.
Here are three reasons for sending that post-interview “thank
you” note the day after the interview.
•
You will demonstrate professionalism.
•
You’ll stand out since most candidates never take the time to
send a “thank you.”
•
You have the opportunity to add a comment or insight that
was missed in the interview.
•
The post-interview thank you note should be written
immediately following the interview and mailed within 24
hours.
What should the note include?
•
First, be sure to let the interviewer know that you appreciate
the time he or she spent in the interview process.
•
Second, add a positive statement about something you
learned about the position or the company.
•
Third, add a comment concerning something that was
discussed during the interview. For example, if the employer
made a passing comment about the company’s need to
upgrade their software then you might add a note about how
you’re ready to help the company employees through the
transition.
•
Fourth, be sure to restate your interest and availability.
Now, it is time for you to wait!
•
You must wait to hear whether or not you got the job. This
can be hard!
•
If you have not heard after one week, it’s fine to give the
prospective employer a call.

---
## Page 480
•
Do this only once; if the hiring manager cannot give you an
answer that day, calling repeatedly will not change this.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit
Section V: Self-Advocacy,
Workplace Disclosure, and
Accommodations
By this point in Chapter 5 – Competency 3: Skills for Work, your
students have acquired the knowledge that will help them choose an
appropriate career path and understand what traits, skills, and abilities
most employers want employees to exhibit.
The adolescents and young adults (aged 16–26) who you have been
assisting have learned how to create resumes using their transferable
skills, as well as career and ability portfolios. They’ve practiced for the
interview process and hopefully by this point, some of your students
have gone on job interviews and acquired volunteer work, an
internship, or paid employment.
This next subsection will help your ASD or LD students learn about
self-advocacy, partial and full disclosure, and how to request and
obtain workplace accommodations. Knowing when and what to
disclose and becoming strong self-advocates will make each individual
student’s experience in the workplace and workplace environment
more successful.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: I am pleased to acknowledge that much of the
content for this subsection was contributed by Dr. Valerie Paradiz and
her colleagues/co-authors J. Richardson Collins, Andrew Nelson, and

---
## Page 481
Anne N. Duquette. Autism and Learning Differences (An Active
Learning Teaching Toolkit) has adapted and used their expertise to
create the content for Section V: Self-Advocacy, Disclosure, and
Accommodations.
Dr. Paradiz is the Director of a consulting agency that designs
curricula and provides technical assistance and strategic development
services to schools, universities, corporations, and agencies that
support individuals with disabilities. She is a curriculum specialist at
First Place Arizona (URL: http://firstplaceaz.org), and the Executive
Director of the Autistic Global Initiative of the Autism Research
Institute. Dr. Paradiz also serves on CIP’s (College Internship
Program)’s Advisory Board.
I want to express my sincere thanks and gratitude to Valerie, Jay,
Andrew, and Anne. Their generosity and ongoing support for this
Teaching Toolkit and their belief that sharing their specialized
knowledge with the readers and users of this book will be for the
greater good is greatly appreciated.

---
## Page 482
Explain to your ASD or LD students that self-advocacy is an integral
part of their daily lives and will be especially so once they start working.
Similar to social skills, self-advocacy skills are essential for all aspects
of day-to-day life and for coping with an ASD or LD diagnosis.
However, becoming a self-advocate doesn’t happen in a vacuum.
Adolescents and young adults on the ASD or LD Spectrum will need to
be taught self-advocacy skills.
Tell the young men and women you are working with that the good
news is that they don’t need to reinvent the wheel to obtain support
and/or accommodations in the workplace.

---
## Page 483
Explain that for decades, adults with disabilities have been hard at
work establishing a network of organizations and peer support groups
devoted to articulating their experiences, sharing advocacy strategies,
practicing self-disclosure, understanding their legal rights, and
developing plans and agendas for change.
Such groups include People First, Advocating Change Together, Self
Advocates Becoming Empowered (SABE), and many others. These
groups have been working toward this goal. Your students should take
the time to view their websites and find out more about each one, either
as a classroom activity, or as individual research.
Explain that while self-advocacy has been discussed throughout this
book in context to the Skills for Life and Skills for School chapters, it will
now be discussed in context to the world of work.
Tell students that this means that at some point in their lives they will
need to disclose (tell) to someone else that they are on the ASD or LD
Spectrum.
Here’s a personal story written by Dr. Valerie Paradiz’s colleague and
co-author J. Richardson (Jay) Collins. It will help your students begin to
understand how important disclosure (either full or partial) can be,
especially as they start working and living independently.
Have your students read the story, “What Is Disclosure” that Jay wrote.
Then start a discussion to talk about the differences between partial or
full disclosure and about when, where, or why doing this might be
appropriate.
Ask how many of the young people in your class can relate to this story
– in any way – and if they would like to give examples from their own
lives where they gave a partial or full disclosure to others and why.
Discuss how Jay was able to ask for and receive accommodations at
work so that he could function in his workplace and do his job
successfully.
Ask students to talk about other accommodations that they might
(personally) need to ask for in order to do their jobs well once they enter

---
## Page 484
the world of work.
Helpful Hints for Students
Employers are – by law – not allowed to discriminate against
those with “differences” no matter what they may be (either
physical or learning). A good way to answer questions that are in
this “gray” area is to say: “I can perform the duties assigned to
me.” End of story. This answer is better than getting defensive
or disclosing more about your “differences” that you would like
to. Remember: the law is on your side about this one!
Jennifer Kolarik, CIP Brevard
What Is Disclosure?
In order to advocate for yourself, you may have to tell someone
that you have a disability (such as being on the ASD or LD
Spectrum), or share something about how your disability
impacts you. This is called disclosure. Telling someone the
name of your disability is called full disclosure. Telling someone
how you are affected in certain situations (but without
mentioning the disability) is called partial disclosure.
I make a disclosure everywhere I go because I have a service
dog. The presence of my service dog indicates that I have a
disability but it is only a partial disclosure. People do not know
what my disability is unless I tell them. They also do not know

---
## Page 485
how the service dog helps me. Often people will ask how my
service dog helps me. I almost never have been asked why I
have a service dog or what my disability is.
I often make other partial disclosures as well. When I travel on a
plane, I require an aisle seat. Otherwise, I feel too closed in. I
call the airline ahead of time. I disclose that I travel with a
service dog and require aisle seating. People with service dogs
are allowed accessible seating and this helps me with my need
for an aisle seat.
As someone with Autism, I have sensory sensitivities. One
example is photosensitivity. This means my eyes do not tolerate
bright or fluorescent lights. You may experience this yourself. I
wear tinted lenses to help but sometimes I have to say
something like, “My eyes are very sensitive to fluorescent lights.
It would help if the lighting could be dimmed or a section of the
lights turned off.” I do not say that I have Autism. In these cases,
it is not important for someone else to know why I am sensitive.
Another sensitivity I have is to all scented products such as
cleaning supplies, detergents, soaps, perfumes, and colognes. I
know that many people with Autism experience this and even
have allergies, as I do. Sometimes, I cannot go in certain stores
or walk down certain aisles in a store. When that happens, I tell
people I am with that the things in the store (or aisle) make my
eyes itch, my throat tickle, and my nose stuffy. When I am going
to a party or other public event, I ask the person planning it to
ask others who will be coming to avoid wearing scented
products. Again, I let people know how I am affected. That is
more important and more helpful in achieving understanding
than saying I have Autism.
As you can see, there are many situations in which a partial
disclosure is all that is needed. However, there are times when it
is necessary to make a full disclosure and tell someone that you

---
## Page 486
have an Autism, ADD, OCD, ASD, or LD diagnosis. This can
feel risky. You may worry that people will treat you differently or
make assumptions about you. Still, it is important to advocate
for yourself. If things do not go well, and you did not disclose,
people will treat you differently or make assumptions anyway.
Although I am an adult, I did not work for a number of years. I
now know that part of the reason was because I did not know
how to advocate for myself in a work setting. I have many needs
that require support. I already have mentioned some, such as
being accompanied by my service dog, requiring comfortable
lighting, and removing scented products. I also need to screen
out or remove background noise, such as other people talking,
and repetitive noises – such as a clock ticking. I need to screen
out or sit with my back to visual distractions, such as movement
in a hallway or cluttered areas. I need clear, direct instructions,
extra time to organize tasks, and definite due dates for getting
things done.
I need co-workers to understand that I am very polite but I am
not good with chitchat or other social niceties. If I did not
disclose these needs, I may be viewed as someone who wants
to control the work space, as someone who is a careless,
inefficient worker, or as someone who is unfriendly. It would take
many and repeated partial disclosures to successfully advocate
for myself. So … when I returned to work after not working for a
number of years, I opted to disclose my disability. I made a full
disclosure to my supervisor and immediate co-workers. This
made it easier for me to self-advocate and easier for them to
understand my need for accommodations.
Nonetheless, full disclosure (even at work) does not mean that
you have to tell everyone or that you have to do it yourself.
Usually, you would not want someone else to disclose your
disability. However, sometimes having someone else disclose
for you can be helpful. In my case, I arranged for my immediate

---
## Page 487
co-worker to disclose to other co-workers in my office before I
started the job. He disclosed my disability and some of the
needs that I had. That way, I did not have to make a decision
about disclosing every time I met a new co-worker. When new
work situations occur, I now make a decision about (full or
partial) disclosure.
No matter what the situation is – disclosure involves three
considerations: whether to disclose, whether it will be full or
partial, and whether you will disclose yourself or ask someone to
do it for or with you.
Practicing disclosure in the classroom setting will help you
understand the process and get used to doing it.
J. Richardson Collins, MTS, MSW
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Here are some role play scenarios developed by Dr. Paradiz’s co-
author, Andrew Nelson, to reinforce the concepts that were discussed
in the personal story above.
Role Playing Disclosure Activity
Divide students into small groups.
Tell the young people you work with that they are
going to role play two disclosure scenarios.
Explain that disclosure (either full or partial) is a
multi-faceted process that takes significant practice
before they will feel comfortable with the various

---
## Page 488
aspects of disclosing to others that they are on the
ASD or LD Spectrum.
Tell students that by preparing for disclosure through
role playing each person will be able to practice the
idea of disclosure in a safe environment, while making
adjustments and discoveries in real-time and real-
place scenarios that will help each person improve
their disclosure skills.
Have groups gather in the four corners of the
classroom so that there is a visual and auditory space
between the groups.
Groups will discuss then role play both scenarios as
realistically as possible, while you and any classroom
assistants you might have circulate with input and
encouragement.
Scenario 1
Each participant will try to think of a time when he or she
disclosed his or her diagnosis and the result did not go the
way he or she would have liked. Set up a role play to re-
create the event. Participants can try using a different
disclosure strategy than they did during that particular past
event. Experiment and adjust until each person in the
group feels like he or she has explored each disclosure
option meaningfully.
Scenario 2
This role play is more specific. Participants (in pairs) set
up a role play to simulate a job interview. They then

---
## Page 489
explore the subject of disclosure in the employment
interview context and in the on-the-job context if possible.
Students then role play interviewer and applicant.
Applicant uses either full or partial disclosure during the
interview. Interviewer responds accordingly – this might
mean offering an accommodation to the prospective
employee or just acknowledging a diagnosis disclosure.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning
Teaching Toolkit
SELF-ADVOCACY PORTFOLIOS
The Self-Advocacy Portfolio extends beyond classroom lessons and
therapeutic activities into everyday experiences. This is where
advocacy learning is translated into practice. It is an ongoing and living
document that students can begin at any time and will use to document,
review, and update their individual and successful advocacy strategies.
Encourage the students in your class to begin the process of creating
this living document that will assist them with disclosure, seeking
accommodations (especially in the workplace), by creating written
scripts to use in advocacy situations, and to record outcomes and
provisions made during their self-advocacy process.
Explain that Self-Advocacy Portfolios will:
•
Provide students with a central location for documenting,
reviewing, and updating their individual and successful
advocacy strategies.
•
Validate an individual’s development in a format that can be
shared 
with 
friends, 
family, 
mentors, 
support 
people,
professionals, therapists, employers, physicians, and teachers.

---
## Page 490
•
Serve as a living document that has the capacity and flexibility to
accompany the individual through a variety of settings and life
changes.
•
Provide individuals with a self-constructed resource they can
use to leverage their advocacy presence in important meetings,
at work, at home, and in the community.
•
Increase each adolescent or young adult’s self-awareness
(knowing that he or she needs to advocate for something and
what it is).
•
Provide tools to advocate with, such as a written advocacy
script.
•
Allow for autonomy (initiating advocacy on his or her own behalf,
independent of prompts or cues).
Self-Advocacy Portfolios start with a section entitled: “My Thoughts
and Ideas.” Students can begin this folder by completing one or more
of the following phrases listed below.
1. My disclosure tendencies are …
2. I would like to consider how disclosure is going to impact …

---
## Page 491
3. Self-advocacy may need to play a bigger role as I …
Participants may write their responses or draw pictures to represent a
personal advocacy thought or idea. Free form art can help a self-
advocate tell his or her story in a unique way. You can introduce
drawing to participants whom you feel may not express themselves fully
in written formats. Comic strips, photographs, or even modified Manga
formats can support individuals as they record and share their stories
and plans for self-advocacy.
In this same folder, students can elaborate or respond to the following
questions.
1. Why is it important for your community to know your perspective?
2. Why is it important for you to begin to develop your own
perspective?
ADVOCACY SCRIPTS

---
## Page 492
Explain to your students that they will create another folder entitled:
“My Advocacy Scripts.” This will contain specific scripts that will
assist them as they advocate for accommodations and gain further self-
advocacy skills.
The two case studies on page 220 written by high school teacher Anne
N. Duquette (Dr. Valerie Paradiz’s co-author) illustrate how two
students used their advocacy scripts to gain accommodations. Share
these stories with your students, and then facilitate a classroom
discussion about creating scripts for inclusion in their Self-Advocacy
Portfolios.
“Case Studies from the Classroom: Advocacy
Scripts”
I am a teacher of high school learners on the Autism Spectrum. I
wish that when I was a learner, I had self-advocacy scripts.
When I was a learner, noises, especially the typing on computer
keyboards, agitated me. I did not even study in the library for this
reason, and when I was in college, I would lie in bed, unable to
sleep due to the noises. I learned as an adult that this sensitivity
that I have to noises can be addressed by simply wearing good
headphones. I carry my headphones everywhere now. These
experiences have allowed me to assist my own learners in
creating their own self-advocacy scripts.
“Howard” was in my class for three years. The naturally
occurring noises of the school irritated Howard, and he
constantly cupped his hands over his ears. I introduced to
Howard the self-advocacy script of requesting to wear the
headphones. As Howard is a visual learner, I introduced this
concept with a social story, using photos of Howard using his

---
## Page 493
hands to cover his ears and then requesting the wearing of
headphones. Howard initially used the script to request
headphones and soon … requested the headphones without the
script in other situations. Interestingly, Howard advocated for
more needs, as he found his “voice” during this process. He
requested to sit alone, for example, when agitated. His father
told us that at home, he would tell his father that he needed to
spend time in his room alone.
“Brandy” is currently my learner. Brandy is sensitive to noises in
the classroom environment, particularly loud classmates. These
noises tend to distract Brandy, to the point that she has difficulty
focusing on her work. I introduced Brandy to making a script in
which she would request her loud classmate to please be
quieter. She also created a script in which she would request
headphones. Brandy now has taken ownership of these scripts
and has them memorized. She is less agitated and more
engaged in her work. She has demonstrated significant progress
in her academic work and will soon be moving to a less
restrictive environment.
Anne N. Duquette
The classroom activity on page 221, created by Andrew Nelson, will
help the young men and women in your class create advocacy scripts.
“Making Advocacy Scripts” is a tool to show students how to script
their current and future needs. Whenever a new advocacy script is
created, it should be added to the individual’s Self-Advocacy Portfolio,
particularly if it is a script a participant finds helpful or successful. The
“Making Advocacy Scripts” worksheet is designed as a guide. Your
students may elaborate and express their needs in any format (written,
visual, or audio) that gets their personal message of self-advocacy
across to others.

---
## Page 494
Making Advocacy Scripts
Explain that each person will think about then create one or
more advocacy scripts to help them make a request or ask for
an accommodation or accommodations by responding to the
following statements and questions listed below.
•
If you could change one thing in this particular setting in
terms of how it affects your comfort level socially, what would
it be and why?
•
If you could change one thing that affects your social comfort
level at school, work, or home, what would it be and why?
How would you change it?

---
## Page 495
Students should transfer their advocacy script(s) to a folder
entitled “My Advocacy Scripts.” This makes them accessible for
use or reference at any time. After participants complete this
activity, they will continue adding additional advocacy scripts
they create, as time goes by and other situations occur, to their
Self-Advocacy Portfolios.
Ask participants to think about a personal preference they
would like others to understand better.
Explain that each individual will use this to prepare a document
that he or she can use for requesting a need or preference.
For example, an individual who is sensitive to fluorescent
lighting can create an advocacy script requesting that the lights
be turned off or that he or she be allowed to sit near a window
where there is more natural light.
Remind your students that scripts may be written, audio-video
recorded, constructed as cartoons (drawn by hand or made
using software), or be an image/series of images (including
drawings and photos), etc.
Share scripts! Have the class work in small groups. Give
participants the time they need to share their scripts and get
feedback from peers. Sharing can also occur by transferring
script illustrations to the white board, scanning script illustrations
for display from a computer, passing around completed
worksheets for others to read, or by role playing their own or
others’ scripts.
Consider inviting a guest speaker with an on the Spectrum
diagnosis to facilitate a follow-up activity and lead the class in a
combination of discussion and role play of the various scripts.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 496
PERSONAL INSIGHT: The spaces and places that young adults work
in should work for them instead of against them. If these young adults
can feel comfortable in their work environment, they will be more
productive and have fewer social, emotional, or work related
problems.
Self-advocacy – especially in the workplace – is empowering and will
allow the individuals you work with to disclose, either partially or fully,
their specific diagnosis to their employers and request necessary
accommodations so that they can be successful at work.
This section ends with a vignette that Dr. Stephen Shore relayed to
me about his first working experience. Dr. Shore is on the ASD
Spectrum and his expertise is featured in a piece he wrote for Chapter
12: The Experts Speak! This vignette is a realistic portrayal of some of
the challenges your students will face. Share Dr. Shore’s experiences
with the young men and women you teach, counsel, and advise.
Stephen talked to me about what he experienced in the workplace
after he graduated from college with an accounting degree. He got a
job at an accounting firm. They agreed he could put the bicycle on
which he pedaled to and from work each day in the furnace room. He
would also use this room to change from his bike riding clothes to his
work attire. Then he would go to his desk and start his work day.
The overhead lights bothered Stephen and so did the surrounding
noises of his colleagues. He felt enclosed and stifled and found the
work boring. After trying an “at a desk” job he decided to study for
another degree instead of staying in an environment that violated all
his sensory issues. The job he had trained for, that of an accountant,
was not a good fit for him even though he liked the analytical side of
“doing numbers.”
Had Stephen had the opportunity to do an internship in accounting at
an actual accounting firm, he might have discovered this earlier. Or he
might have tried disclosing some information about his ASD diagnosis
and requested an accommodation that would have made things
better.

---
## Page 497
Section VI: Supporting Students
While Working
Providing support (emotional and from an advocacy standpoint),
advice, as well as helping students find solutions to problems that occur
while “on the job” is essential to each person’s individual success as he
or she enters the world of work.
Helping students when problems arise usually means assisting them as
they sharpen their “Executive Function” skills so that they can plan and
prepare for the work day, get to work on time, and perform their jobs in
an organized, orderly, timely, and logical way.
When task oriented workplace problems arise, showing students how
to do their jobs more effectively during individual counseling sessions,
through job shadowing or coaching (so that assigned tasks can be
mastered), and helping them devise strategies to communicate
effectively with their employers at work will be an invaluable support to
each and every one of them.
TEACHING INSIGHT: To give readers real-time scenarios, examples,
and solutions of how you might help and/or support your ASD/LD
students in the workplace I’ve turned to CIP’s staff. The following
vignettes, scenarios, teaching tips, and examples – coupled with the
advice, ideas, and strategies staff and students came up with – will be
useful to you when problems arise.
Their real-time examples will make you laugh, cry, and sometimes
(like it does for them), make you want to throw your hands up in the air
and shake your head! Their advice, especially when dealing with their
students’ persistent and ongoing problems, will also give you much
hope and occasionally … a “way out” of a challenging dilemma.
SUPPORT OPTIONS

---
## Page 498
Many of the readers of this book work in support roles with this book’s
target population – ASD and LD adolescents and young adults (ages
16–26). You are the career counselors, social skills instructors, life
skills 
coordinators, 
transitional 
services 
support 
personnel,
occupational therapists, and clinicians who prepare these young men
and women for work and then step in to assist them when problems
arise.
In addition to the classroom instruction that teachers provide, support
service personnel can help students develop a further understanding of
what additional work skills are needed and then help them acquire
these skills. The instruction, strategies, and solutions that will emerge
during individual career counseling sessions will address additional
workplace problems.
Individual Counseling and Job
Coaching/Shadowing
Here’s a real-time example from Taylor True, who is a Career
Coordinator at CIP Bloomington Center that illustrates how individual
counseling and job coaching allowed Joe B. to move toward working
independently with specific work related supports in place.
JOE B.’S PROGRESS: WORKING
INDEPENDENTLY WITHOUT A JOB
COACH
Joe B. is a motivated young man from New York City.
He wants to work in the entertainment industry and be
an actor. His second choice is to work in retail,
preferably men’s wear.

---
## Page 499
Before I started working with him, Joe B. had a
volunteer position at a local boutique/vintage store
called 
Cactus 
Flower. 
His 
previous 
Career
Coordinator expressed that Joe B. had difficulty in
certain areas; namely in accepting feedback from
supervisors and independently asking his supervisor
for more work. When I started working with him, Joe
B. was no longer at Cactus Flower and was looking
for another volunteer job.
I helped Joe B. secure a new position at a local non-
profit store that supports fair trade: Global Gifts, in the
heart of downtown Bloomington and within walking
distance to his school and apartment. He would be
working there every Friday.
After the first two Fridays Joe B. worked, his
supervisor reported to me that this was their busiest
time and it seemed to make Joe B. uncomfortable
and a bit flustered, although he would not express this
to the supervisor.
I talked to Joe about this in our individual counseling
session. Together (on speaker phone), we made an
appointment to talk to his supervisor. With some
minor prompting from me during the meeting, Joe
asked to have his schedule changed to a
Wednesday, a much less busy day at Global Gifts.
The supervisor agreed. This switch allowed Joe B. to
make improvement in his three challenge areas –
being able to accept feedback from his supervisor,
being able to listen fully to instructions, and being able
to ask his supervisor for more work when he had
finished his assigned tasks.

---
## Page 500
I then enlisted the help of Joe B.’s job coach. She
decided to track his behavior at work with a simple
scoring system that would help Joe B. and me
monitor his progress in his problem areas according
to what he had recorded. I would continue to work
with Joe on techniques, role plays, and “Executive
Function” strategies he could use on the job to learn
to accept feedback, listen fully to instructions, and
independently ask for more work from his supervisor.
Joe B. would track his own progress using the
following guidelines:
•
If there is improvement in the behavior we wanted
to see, he gets a 0.
•
If Joe B. continues with the current behavior he
gets a -1.
•
If the behavior gets worse he gets a -2.
•
If the behavior is better than imagined he gets a
+1.
•
If everything is perfect he gets a +2.
This simple scoring system appealed to Joe B’s
nature – and allowed him to set goals for
improvement. He set up a simple spreadsheet that
allowed him to track and assess his own progress
using what he was learning in individual sessions and
then applying at work each day. He loved trying for
“+” goals. Joe’s scores were improving so much that
by the time October came around Joe was able to
hold a two-week, paid, seasonal position at a local –
and very busy – costume store.
I “job shadowed” with Joe B. on his first day at his
new job, and his job coach “shadowed” him for part

---
## Page 501
of the time the next two days. After three days, he
was completely independent.
I stayed in touch with his supervisor as Joe B. worked
the remainder of his two-week stint. His supervisor
reported that Joe B. did an excellent job, especially in
the areas we had been working on. She did not have
any complaints other than he was sometimes “too
helpful” in asking her independently about what he
could be doing.
Taylor True,
Career Coordinator, CIP Bloomington
This was a great example of how one-on-one career counseling and
job coaching can augment classroom instruction.
Getting to work on time!
TEACHING INSIGHT: Jennifer Kolarik, the Lead Career Coordinator
for CIP Brevard, was having ongoing problems with some of her
students being time-challenged. They continuously had trouble getting
to work on time, staying awake at work, and various other “Executive
Function” problems that made them seem disorganized, messy, or
sloppy while at work. Her vignettes, case studies, strategies, and
solutions in the piece she has written below illustrate her ingenuity and
her sense of humor as she helps her students devise solutions to
problems. Jennifer is the first one to admit that on occasion she gets
“Career Coordinator Challenged” by her students.
AWAKE CHALLENGED, PUNCTUALLY
CHALLENGED, AND UNDERGROUND

---
## Page 502
THURSDAY CHALLENGED
Jennifer Kolarik,
Lead career coordinato, CIP brevard
“Awake Challenged”
One of the biggest hurdles that adolescents and young adults on the
ASD or LD Spectrum face is having a reversed sleep schedule where
they stay awake at night (often playing video games or surfing the
Internet), and sleeping during the day. This is especially so if students
are living independently for the first time. No one is around to tell them
to “go to bed.”
This impacts their work lives – whether it’s volunteering, interning, or
doing a paid job. Even if they make it to work on time, they may lack
energy and even nod off because they are tired and/or sleep deprived.
Meet Melanie. Melanie was very bright and enthusiastic about her
internship working with animals. Soon after she started her internship,
her supervisor noticed that when Melanie sat down during slow times,
she would close her eyes for what she thought was a moment and
then fall fast asleep. It was my job to figure out how to best help her
stay awake at work. I came up with some strategies so Melanie and
others like her, who were continually “awake challenged,” could do
their jobs without falling asleep on the job or nodding off.
Suggested strategies for “Awake Challenged” students:
Help your “awake challenged” students realistically decide how many
hours of sleep each one will need each night and what time he or she
needs to wake up by in order to get ready to “make it” to work on time.
Based on that information, decide (together) on a good bedtime and
