get a promise (in writing if you can) that he or she will try to “stick to
it.”

---
## Page 503
Students might ask a roommate or roommates to support the fact that
he or she will be going to bed at this time each night. Students like
Melanie can also use a simple spreadsheet and record how many
hours per night they sleep, as well as the time they went to bed and
got up in the morning. As patterns develop you can further aid
students with positive reinforcement or extra help and encouragement.
Encourage students to develop a wind-down routine. A hot drink,
reading for half an hour, and then turning off the light, having a hot
shower or relaxing bath, or doing some gentle yoga and stretching
right before bed will set the scene for a solid night’s sleep.
Ask the student to set an alarm or multiple alarms to help him or her to
remember when it is time to go to bed and time wake up.
Have the “awake challenged” young person bring healthy snacks and
drinks to work each day and take occasional breaks so he or she can
eat, hydrate, and boost energy levels. The student should not develop
an overdependence on caffeine or energy drinks to keep awake.
Instead encourage him or her to keep a food or energy journal to learn
more about his or her daily patterns.
“Punctually Challenged”
Most of my students have “Executive Function” challenges that impact
them on the job. Ken, one of my favorite students, was severely
“punctually challenged” due to “Executive Function” issues.
Ken had his Associate’s degree and several certifications. He was
very bright and had a great sense of humor. Ken often showed up at
my office 15 minutes before he was supposed to be at work and let
me know he didn’t have enough gas to get to his internship. It turns
out he liked to drive around over the weekend and then realized his
car was on empty just as he was about to leave for work on Monday
morning.
To make matters worse, Ken did not carry his debit card with him and
would have to go to into the nearest bank to withdraw money from his

---
## Page 504
checking account to put gas in his car. He knew by that point that he
would be late for work. Ken would then need to contact his internship
supervisor again and explain that he was going to be late.
I helped Ken by working on his organizational and planning skills. We
made evening and morning checklists to address everything that
needed to be done (including checking the gas tank the night before),
so that he allowed time to buy gas in the morning if he needed it. We
put this in the form of a reproducible checklist that he would fill out
each night.
We decided that each night he needed to:
•
Check the car for gas.
•
Lay out his work clothes.
•
Shower and shave.
•
Make a lunch.
•
Charge his cell phone.
•
Make sure that his keys and backpack were by the front door.
It was a long slow process until Ken finally saw this routine’s value. By
preparing the night before, he could identify if he needed to plan extra
time in the morning to address any glitches or things that would make
him late for work.
Ken is an example of a student who had the education and
experience to succeed but didn’t have the “Executive Function” skills
in place to make sure he had gas or plan ahead for getting gas.
Repetition and routine turned out to be the key in helping Ken. Over
time with repetition, repetition, repetition, and occasional nagging from
me, this young man made wonderful strides in his punctuality. Ken
now has worked nearly full-time for the same company for a number of
years.
“Underground Thursday” Challenged

---
## Page 505
What is “Underground Thursday” Challenged do you ask? It could
really fall on any day of the week … but for Zach, his “underground”
day would always be a Thursday. He used up so much effort and
energy to get through his Monday to Wednesday work days that he
would call in “sick” almost every Thursday or every other Thursday. It
was as predictable as the tides.
Zach and I had many one-on-one chats about this. I finally determined
that Zach lacked the key skill of “perseverance” for the times when he
was feeling overwhelmed, anxious, depressed, or tired. Instead of
hanging in there and getting himself to work on Thursdays, he’d give
up and call in “sick.”
Zach was working toward his AS in Information Technology and was
very bright. When he was at his internship, he did a good job. But as
someone once said, “80% of success is showing up.” Here is how I
helped Zach.
Strategies to help “Underground Thursday” Challenged Zach:
I had him turn in a copy of his internship timesheet to me every week
or every two weeks so we could review work attendance and identify
any reoccurring patterns. This allowed me to show him in “black and
white” that he was missing work on most Thursdays.
In addition to having Zach set multiple alarms to get up on Thursdays
as an audio clue, we looked at his underlying problem of anxiety and
depression and realized that Zach’s stress was being dealt with by
avoidance.
He also told me that he had trouble remembering to take his
medications for anxiety and depression when he was feeling anxious
or depressed. We looked at this pattern as well. Having Zach verify
with me that he had taken his medication daily (via a quick check in,
text, or email) also helped.
We set up a sleep routine (that he would track and chart), and made
getting a good night’s sleep another self-help priority.

---
## Page 506
We asked his supervisor at work if Zach could start an hour later in
the morning or stay an hour later in the afternoon. Zach was one of
those people who just need a little extra time in the mornings. Being
able to start his a job at 10 a.m. instead of 8 a.m. or 9 a.m. allowed
Zach to be on time and the accommodation was motivation to stop
calling in sick halfway through the work week.
At one point we considered having Zach work a part-time job.
Although I would love for every young adult to maintain a full-time job
(whether volunteer, internship, or paid), I would rather see a student
work part-time 20–30 hours per week and be successful than try to
unsuccessfully maintain a full-time job.
I’m Ready for Work and CAN Find My Backpack!
by Jennifer Kolarik, Lead Career Coordinator, CIP Brevard
The night before:
•
Organize your backpack with things you’ll need for work
each evening – including cell phones, keys, laptops, snacks,
pens, and anything else you’ll need “on the job.”
•
Consider showering at night and be sure to lay out the
clothes you’ll need for work the next day. These are great
time savers and will allow you 15 extra minutes of sleep in
the morning.
•
Pack your lunch the night before – so it’s ready to grab and
add to your already packed backpack in the morning.
•
Make sure your car has enough gas in it to get you to work
the next day. Allow time to buy gas if you need it.

---
## Page 507
•
Set one or more alarm clocks to ring and put one on the
other side of the room.
•
Look out the window before going to bed! Or … listen to the
local weather station or channel. Be sure to allow extra time
and set the alarm earlier if it is snowing (and you have to
clean off your car, shovel your driveway, or parking space). If
It is raining hard you will have to allow for slower traffic
conditions or late buses, etc.
Reaching your supervisor or boss:
•
Program your supervisor’s number into your phone. Make
sure to include the name of your workplace, his or her name,
and a work and/or cell number.
•
Ask your supervisor how he or she would prefer for you to
contact him or her if you are ever late or need to miss work
(work phone? cell phone? text? email?). For most
workplaces, supervisors want you to call and make sure to
speak to them or another staff member directly.
Social protocol at work:
•
If your work is having a potluck, make sure to make or buy
something to contribute.
•
Consider holiday cards, birthday cards, thank you cards,
etc., for co-workers and supervisors when appropriate.
Remember both co-workers and supervisors may serve as
job references in the future … so work on building those
relationships.
•
If a staff member will be changing jobs and you think they
would be a good job reference for you, ask for their cell

---
## Page 508
number or a personal email so you can contact them as
needed.
And on a more serious note:
One of the most important lessons you will learn about is the “no
show – no call” dilemma!
This is when you wake up in a cold sweat with sheer panic and
realize that you missed your alarm and accidently overslept …
Make sure to call your workplace right away!
Even if you missed the first half of your work day or maybe even
your entire shift (it does happen) …
Make sure to call your workplace right away!
If you do a “no show – no call,” this is something you can lose
your job over. So …
Make sure to call your workplace right away!
Even if it is uncomfortable in the moment, “facing the music”
early on and “being apologetic” can help save your job. Just
make sure to create a plan so that it doesn’t happen again. And
…
Make sure to call your workplace right away!
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
MY FAVORITE “LEARNABLE” MOMENTS
Jennifer Kolarik, Lead Career Coordinator, CIP Brevard

---
## Page 509
During the six years that I have been the Lead Career Coordinator, I
have had many “teachable” moments with my students. And in return,
I have also had what I call “learnable” moments. Those gems of
wisdom my ASD and LD students impart to me. While perhaps
challenging to address in the moment, each brings a smile to my face
“in hindsight,” of course!
So in honor of David Lettermen’s Top Ten List; here are my Top Five
“Learnable” Moments:
5. From Chad: “Do not leave the door to the armadillo pen open …
they will wander out.”
4. From Dan: “Do not walk into your internship or job carrying your
hairbrush, tie, and shoes.”
3. From Juan: “Do not chase squirrels and lizards while on your
work break (at least within view of your co-workers and
supervisors).”
2. From Sarah: “Try not to drop the “F-bomb” or use other offensive
language while at work … especially when leaving voicemails or
interacting with customers.”
1. From Max: “Do not change the radio station on your co-workers’
radios without asking, or play the radio loudly at work while singing
your favorite Rihanna song … even if it is a good one!”
POSITIVES OUTWEIGH THE NEGATIVES!
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Once pre-working routines are in place and an
accommodation has been made for your students in the workplace
and things are going well at work for most of your students, there is
“additional” good news to be had and then shared with your students
and their employers.
The positive side of ASD and LD is that many of us (I am “on the
Spectrum” too) are always on time. We always do what we say we are
going to do and follow through on our projects. We can bring

---
## Page 510
perfectionism and creative ideas to a project. We remember details
that others forget. We can be put in charge of areas and employers
can rest assured that things will be done exactly the way they have
asked us to do them – over and over again.
As you prepare your students for the world of work, find a way to
praise and highlight these qualities and traits in the students who
display them. This will be very motivational and help these young men
and women feel valued. And … you will be rewarded by the biggest
smiles you’ve ever seen, and possibly an awkward hug or pat on the
back too!
OCCUPATIONAL THERAPISTS
TEACHING INSIGHT: Janell Yonkman is an Occupational Therapist
(OT) at CIP Bloomington. She provides one-on-one support for
students with an ASD or LD diagnosis as they make the transition from
high school to an academic or career path.
Janell is able to work individually with students in their work
environments to develop solutions to the challenges students face
during this important transition time of their lives.
In “Pictures Sometimes Speak Louder than Words,” Janell gives
readers real-time examples of what this means as she works to
support her “on the Spectrum” students when they are “on the job.”
PICTURES SOMETIMES SPEAK LOUDER
THAN WORDS
Janell Yonkman,
Occupational Therapist, CIP Bloomington
Occupation means “engaging” in what you do every day. Occupations
are everyday activities that include self-care, meal preparation,

---
## Page 511
academics, careers, sexual activity, and social interactions. I work
with students in all of these environments.
OTs are trained to skillfully observe an activity, break it down, grade it
up or down, then adapt and develop strategies for students to do the
activity independently. More learning can take place when a student is
actively engaged in an occupation, so this type of intervention is good.
Sometimes it’s about finding “a nuance” in order to elicit change.
Understanding why students on the “ASD or LD Spectrum” do what
they do, and then developing strategies within their individual and
unique thought processes by endeavoring to change the behavior, is
what my job is all about.
Here’s a good example of using my training as an OT to help a
student who was having trouble at work. This student (see photo
below) worked at a horticultural center. His supervisor called me and
explained that this young man was not doing well due to a lack of
thoroughness and distractibility. The plants he was responsible for
taking care of were dying. When he watered them – it was either too
much or not enough. He also would get so wrapped up in watching
others performing their jobs that he would forget to focus on his own
tasks. He was not getting everything he was responsible for “done” on
time.
After talking with this young man and doing some experimenting, we
developed a system to ensure each plant was watered and watered
enough. We ended up with clearly defined chunked sections using the
cement blocks that created a very visible border around the plants he
was to take care of. He would water a plant and count to ten before
moving to another pot. To deal with distractibility we had his plant
section face a stone wall so that he could concentrate solely on his
duties with these plants.
This proved to be a successful way of dealing with both problems he
was having on the job. It also made the student confident as he now
had parameters in place for watering plants he was responsible for
and a reminder (the stone wall) to stay focused on his work.

---
## Page 512
Jacky works at a skateboard shop and his responsibilities include
folding shirts, cleaning up, and restocking items. Jacky and I worked
together to make a binder to provide step-by-step instructions (in large
bolded type and with photos) for folding clothes and other duties. This
helped him accommodate for his low vision problems, while reminding
him to slow down, increase precision when folding shirts, and
implement thoroughness with each task.

---
## Page 513
INTERNSHIP AND WORK SUPPORT
GROUPS

---
## Page 514
Internship and work support groups will allow your students to get other
students’ perspectives on job experiences and challenges. As you help
your students facilitate these bi-weekly or monthly groups explain that
being able to share problems, issues, and concerns about their jobs or
internships with each other is extremely important. It allows for each
one to hear “how it’s going” for each group member and allows for
discussing and brainstorming solutions of what might help solve a
problem. Being able to hear how others reacted or how they came up
with an answer or solution when faced with similar workplace scenarios
is extremely useful. It allows for peer validation of the fact that the
workplace with its tasks, “Hidden Curriculum,” co-workers, and
supervisors can be hard, challenging, difficult, and frustrating for those
on the Spectrum.
Here’s a student handout that the young men and women you work
with can use to get things started when the group gathers. It allows
each person to write down what the problem is and really think about
how he or she might resolve it.
Once the problem is defined clearly, you can support the group as they
work together on strategies, ideas, or accommodations to make things
better.
Feel free to transfer the handout below to a white board or flip chart if
that works better for the support group.
This is What “I” Did – Would It Work for You?
Identify five problems that you have faced at your internship or
job. Then describe the solution you implemented for each.
Share your strategies and tips with fellow students and your
internship or job support group.
Problem Faced at Internship/Job
Solution Used

---
## Page 515
1.
 
 
2.
 
 
3.
 
 
4.
 
 
5.
 
 
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
PERSONAL INSIGHT: The handout on pages 231–232 is adapted
from one we give to prospective CIP employees. We employ hundreds
of people at our six Centers across the United States and strive to
practice what we preach and what we teach our students.
As students seek employment and go through the interview process,
they might inquire to see if some (if not all) of the things on this
handout are in place at the company/business they are looking at for
future employment.
Knowing the values and ethics of a company or business before
agreeing to work as an employee will ensure that the employment
culture your students will be working in is a good fit for their personal
values and ethics.
The handout below looks at two things: Whether or not a company’s
values “fit” a job candidate and also help a candidate determine if the
job “fits” with a candidate’s strengths.
The handout on page 231–232 is given to prospective employees that
CIP is considering for employment and to each employee who is
hired. Both are useful to students as they start looking for a job that
encompasses their values and strengths.

---
## Page 516
The first handout “Investigating a Company’s Values and Employment
Culture” was created by Marcia Scheiner, who is the President and
Founder of the “Asperger Syndrome Training & Employment
Partnership” (ASTEP). ASTEP is an employer-focused organization
working with Fortune 1000 companies to broaden their diversity and
inclusion strategies to include individuals with Asperger’s Syndrome.
I’d like to personally thank Marcia for this valuable contribution to
Autism and Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit).
Investigating a Company’s Values and
Employment Culture
Here are some things to think about to see if a company’s
values “fit” with your needs and to determine if the job would be
a “good fit” for your strengths.
Values

---
## Page 517
Ask:
•
What is important to the company?
•
Do they have non-work related social events? Are they
mandatory?
•
Is there a peer review system in addition to the typical
manager performance review process?
•
Do they offer flexible work schedules?
•
Do they have employee resource groups (ERGs) for
employees with like issues (e.g., disability or need for
accommodations)?
•
Do they encourage and allow time for volunteerism?
•
Are there periodic meetings where senior management
updates all employees about the company?
•
Do they have a “Corporate Social Responsibility” agenda?
What is it?
•
Do they have a mandatory or implicit “up or out” policy?
Strengths
Ask these questions to ensure the job is targeted to your
strengths:
•
How much time will I be working alone vs. in teams
•
How often will I meet with my supervisor for feedback?
•
Will I be working on multiple projects at a time or expected to
finish one project before being assigned another?
•
Do we work under strict deadlines on projects?
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 518
CIP’s (College Internship Program’s) Positive
Employment Culture
Principles that we operate by:
•
Staff are paid a living wage.
•
Staff receive a full benefit package.
•
Staff have great working conditions.
•
Staff receive high quality training for their job.
•
Our company pays its bills on time (not monthly).
•
We resolve issues quickly.
•
We do employee evaluations on time.
•
We invest back into our program.
•
We always have something to give.
•
We have bright, cheery and well furnished environments.
•
Staff are rewarded for their achievements in many ways.
•
We don’t micro-manage our employees.
•
We operate with efficiency.
•
We communicate and explain things thoroughly.
•
We do and provide what we say we will.
•
We want staff longevity.
•
We do not penny pinch with parents of students, our
students, or our employees.

---
## Page 519
•
We offer the best services possible to our students and their
parents.
•
We follow through with student goals.
•
We follow through with parent goals.
•
We provide the most tuition assistance we can.
•
We follow our students and track their success.
•
We show care and concern for our graduates.
•
We build community and consensus with our staff and
student body.
•
We mentor community service.
CIP’s feedback loop:
The following feedback mechanism presumes that we are hiring
intelligent, educated staff and that we pay them to use their
skills, intuition, and abilities to come up with solutions on the job.
We are not going to do the thinking for our employees and we
depend on them (as they are on the front line) to tell us what the
problems are and what the best possible solutions might be.
Therefore:
•
We will not accept staff dumping their dissatisfactions at
meetings on other staff or administrators. If you bring up a
problem, you have to be part of the solution. (“He who has
the mind to criticize, should have the heart to help,” Pope
John the 23rd.)
•
Staff should have the commitment to continue to bring up
important problems and continue to advocate, ask
questions, and seek solutions until the problem is solved
(“Dogged Persistence.)”
•
The presumption needs to be that everyone on every level of
CIP wants the best for our students and staff and that all are

---
## Page 520
working for the same goals.
Feedback mechanism:
The following is CIP’s feedback mechanism employees use to
make working with each other productive and efficient.
When a problem arises (or you think of a change or innovation to
create):
•
Explain the problem.
•
State at least two possible solutions and the rationale for how
they would work.
•
State your thinking regarding which answer you think would
be best and why.
Accountability:
Creating CIP’s positive open culture is about accountability and
getting things done, whether the problem is a broken doorknob,
a difficult student problem, or an internal business practice.
The best solutions arise out of “ideas and proposals” initiated by
staff and students. Putting rationale in writing and explaining why
the solution benefits all parties is the best way to approach
problem solving when changes or new ways of doing things are
needed.
CIP is always looking to improve and use our resources in the
best possible ways to benefit our students, parents, and staff.
We need staff input to do this.
It’s the little things that matter:
•
Maintain professional dress.

---
## Page 521
•
Maintain a professional physical environment (advocate for
what is needed). For example: If the carpets are dirty –
speak up until they are cleaned. Don’t just blame or accept it.
•
Double check all reports to make sure they are written
professionally with substantive content and are fact based
and do not contain personal content that is unnecessary.
•
Make sure you write down all the work you are doing with
students and explain the curriculum to families. People will
not know your efforts unless you explain them.
•
Work on making each student’s experience special. Extend
yourself to make sure goals are accomplished. (Remember
the teacher who mentored you and made sure your project
was complete and assisted you just enough to get it done.)
•
Greet all guests and talk with them, make them feel
welcome, and give a short explanation of what you do and
are working on presently. A firm handshake, clear eye
contact, and a greeting: this is expected behavior of every
staff member for anyone new you see during your working
day at any time.
•
Uphold each other and challenge each other (support each
other’s efforts but do not put up with poor performance).
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
PERSONAL INSIGHT: The “Skills for Work” chapter ends with a story
written by Stephen Volan who (like me) is on the “Autism Spectrum.”
Stephen lives in Bloomington, Indiana (pop. 82,000), where he has
served as a member of the city council, with a constituency of 13,000,
for 12 years. He also teaches a popular undergraduate course at
Indiana University called “Introduction to Chess,” and is also pursuing
a Master’s degree in the IU Department of Geography.
As if he were not already busy enough, Stephen also enjoys
volunteering his time as a host: as programmer of a weekly music

---
## Page 522
show on a community radio station, as an emcee at an annual world-
music festival, and as the moderator of an annual local quiz show. His
talk at TEDxBloomington 2011, “Approaching Autism Theatrically,”
has had more than 15,000 views.
Stephen Volan’s story is absolutely the “best example” I can
personally think of that illustrates what understanding and then
learning to accept an “Autism Spectrum” diagnosis is all about while
seeking meaningful and fulfilling work. His story illustrates the concept
of “Dogged Persistence” (questioning and seeking answers until the
correct solutions and answers are found), as he perserveres until a
career path emerges that is fulfilling, personally satisfying, and in
Stephen’s case, one that gives back to his community in so many vital
and important ways.
Share Stephen’s story with your students. Have them read it in small
groups and then ask:
1. How many students can relate to his story or have felt like Stephen
has?
2. Ask students what was the most relevant part of the story for each
person in the reading group:
•
 Acceptance of his diagnosis?
•
Using “Dogged Persistence” to find right career path?
•
Stephen’s gradual self-understanding and further acceptance of
his diagnosis as his career path emerged?
•
Or … how Stephen figured out how to find meaningful work
(and “a good fit”) that makes him feel happy, content, and
fulfilled while giving to others?
A follow-up activity might be viewing Stephen Volan’s “Ted Talk” as a
class and discussing the poignant messages that his inspiring
presentation imparts.
I want to personally thank Stephen Volan for sharing his story and
speaking so honestly about his life to the readers and users of this

---
## Page 523
book, and … for being an outstanding role model to all of us (myself
included) who reside on the “ASD Spectrum.”
“My Careering Life on the Autism Spectrum”
I’ve always fantasized about working nine to five. I imagine
getting up in the morning, commuting to work, accomplishing
important things, leaving at five (without taking work home with
me!), enjoying an evening with my family, going to bed at a
reasonable hour … and repeating that pattern every day, like
normal people.
I day dream like this because I think of myself as normal, as just
like everyone else. But I’m not normal in a couple of different
ways.
For one, I forget that I’m not of average height. The average
man in America is 5’10”. I don’t usually think about my height
until I stand up … and hit my head on something, and I
remember, oh that’s right, I’m actually extremely tall. At 6’8”, I
have to take precautions that other people never contemplate.
(Two words: ceiling fan.) Even though reminders of my height
assail me daily, I forget that I’m unusual up until I’m reminded of
it.
In the same way that I think I’m normal height, I also think I’m of
normal mind. But I’m on the Autism Spectrum. Besides many
other modest social challenges, I have trouble remembering
what I was just doing, remembering what I still need to do, and
getting what I need to do done without being distracted.
I stay up all night because I can’t stop doing other things. I have
trouble waking up before noon. (I like to say that I live in Indiana,

---
## Page 524
but I’m living on Hawaii-time.) In short, I’m high-functioning
enough to seem like I should be able to hold a regular job, but
not quite high-functioning enough to hold on to it for long. My
nine to five fantasy has proven to be all but unsustainable.
“My Careering Life on the Autism Spectrum”
I gravitated to a technical career early on – not surprising for an
“Aspie.” My first job was as a professional typesetter, on a
computer system dedicated to typesetting. After that I was a
consultant in the personal computer support center of my
college campus. Then, I became the production manager for a
start-up weekly newspaper. Eventually I set type for a professor
who was developing a course to teach English speakers an
Asian language that had never been taught in America before.
With one exception, I was fired from every one of those jobs
after less than a year, for my inability to focus or get to work on
time. Even with the exception – the campus computer
consultant, which lasted for the better part of four years – I
almost got fired once. Here’s what happened: The boss who
hired me, a laid-back guy who just cared about the quality of the
work his employees did, got a well-deserved promotion after a
few years.
His replacement was much more authoritarian, very by-the-
book. Suddenly I could never be even a little bit late; my
performance declined drastically as she found fault with traits
that I don’t fully control – what I now attribute to being on the
Spectrum. Fortunately, fate intervened: most of my colleagues
were fellow nerds who chafed at her strictures and began to

---
## Page 525
revolt. Just before she could fire me, she was transferred, and
our old boss was placed in charge of us again.
The environment of the computer support center on campus
was the best I ever worked in, a perfect fit for me. Having a boss
who understood me was crucial. (This was before anyone knew
what Asperger’s was; he was just tolerant of nerds.) Attending
the front desk for walk-in customers removed the mystery of
“What Was I Supposed to Do Next?” (The answer was obvious:
If a customer came through the door, help them; otherwise, learn
more about your computing specialty.) My colleagues were
friendly, funny, and nerdy like me. It was a very flexible and
welcoming environment. But … I had gotten this job as a student;
it wasn’t a long-term career.
Unable to sustain working for others, I turned eventually to
working for myself. I happened to be around at the right time in
history to be on the vanguard of the commercial Internet. I also
happened to be able to persuade someone to loan me money;
allowing me to launch the first company to provide Internet dial-
up access and services in my city.
The late nineties was a heady time to be in the business of being
online. The Internet was growing by leaps and bounds – it was
all we could do to keep up with the demand. The constant inflow
of customers masked my managerial shortcomings long enough
for me to grow the company to a size that was appreciable
enough to be sold to a larger firm. This was just before the first
Internet bubble burst at the turn of the century.
After that I started a book-publishing company, which didn’t last
too long. Then, long after the heyday of such places, I started a
video-rental store. This, into which I put all my money and some
of other people’s money, was the most expensive mistake of my
entrepreneurial career. I kept the store open too long – long after
it was clear to others that it wasn’t working.

---
## Page 526
In retrospect, I kept at the store, and in general trying to start my
own businesses, for longer than I should have because I had
come to believe that I could not work for anyone else – that there
was no nine to five work environment that would have me, or
where I felt I would last. This despair came from not knowing
that there were alternative career paths … even for someone like
me.
I remember the day before my first full day of work as a campus
computer consultant. I had just moved out of my first-year dorm
and into the dorm for summer students (a high-rise). To move
my possessions, I had rented a car and needed to return it that
first day of work on my lunch hour. So I drove it to work.
The rental car was new – it had nine miles on it. It was a picture-
perfect spring day. I had dressed up for my first day, in a short-
sleeve button-down shirt and dress slacks. As I drove to work
through the tree-lined, sun-dappled campus, I vividly remember
thinking, “This must be what it’s like to make $50,000 a year.”
That moment, ironically, may have been my career high-water
mark; decades later, I have yet to make that much money in a
year. It isn’t that I’ve longed to have money. I’ve wanted to make
money: to be self-supporting, and to be responsible for myself
without outside assistance.
It turns out that many people “on the spectrum” have a similar
issue. In my case, I’ve managed somehow to scrape by, but with
nothing left over to take a vacation with or to save for retirement.
If I had known back in the late 20th century that there was a
“spectrum” – and one that everyone was on, not just children – I
would have made different choices earlier and not beaten myself
up for shortcomings that weren’t my fault, that were in reality, not
moral failings.

---
## Page 527
I also remember thinking as a kid how the world would be when
I finally got to it as an adult. I imagined that I would move to the
big city and live in a high-rise apartment building. I assumed I
would soon make a passel of fast friends, they’d be really smart
and accepting and diverse, and we’d have many entertaining
adventures together. All of this, along with my self-liquidating
career, would somehow drop into my lap. At least, that’s what
prime-time television led me to believe. How it would all happen
exactly was hazy, but it couldn’t be that complicated. (By the
way, I do not recommend TV as the primary source of guidance
for one’s future.)
So I waited for the magic future I wanted to just happen to me. I
never wound up moving to the big city, never landed the cushy
job. I dropped out, stayed in my college town, and scraped by
while I tried to figure out what went wrong.
One day it occurred to me that I actually had “old” friends and
“close” friends, but not as tight-knit a posse as I’d hoped, and
about 15 or 20 years later than I expected. To reach this
epiphany, I had to learn that Autism wasn’t just a childhood
condition – that it was lifelong, pervasive, and had applied to me
all along.
The simple idea of being in the world with everyone else – the
act of being social – is thrilling to me. Being social is also, for
me, a great deal of work in and of itself. I have to think about
how to behave in virtually every social interaction I find myself in.
I think about being social in the same way that people who don’t
like school think about math: It’s a lot of work, it’s hard, it
doesn’t come naturally to me, and I hate having to do it, even as
I want the rewards that come from doing it. When I can do the
social math – when I can pull off being a mundane part of the
social environment and not an unintentional center of attention –
it is as thrilling as a heist caper or a spy mission. I feel like I’m
getting away with something.

---
## Page 528
So, I’ve whittled down my fantasies of living a “normal” life. I’ve
given up hope of working nine to five. More importantly, I’ve also
stopped beating myself up about it, and have found work that
I’m good at that doesn’t require a normal work schedule. I’ve
stopped expecting the world to happen to me, and am trying
instead (however feeble my efforts) to make the world I want
happen.
There’s one fantasy I still indulge in: To have a full-time personal
assistant, who keeps my calendar and to-do lists, dogs me, and
makes sure I’m staying on task. I look for ways to generate
enough revenue from my work to justify an assistant, while
recognizing that I might need that assistant to generate that
revenue in the first place. Perhaps I should ask someone to
invest in me so as to eliminate that Catch-22.
More important for me though, is to realize that, even if such
financial investment is unavailable, each of us is worth being
invested-in in the first place, me included. Until I believed that I
wasn’t simply a screw-up, I could not improve my lot in life. My
diagnosis of Asperger’s Syndrome was also the first part of my
cure.
Stephen Volan
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Making It Real
WHAT?
Looking at employability skills and the eight key abilities
employers want their employees to exhibit.

---
## Page 529
Exploring the Career Continuum: Volunteering/Service
Learning → Supervised Internship → Internship to Work →
Employment.
Helping ASD and LD students explore career paths. Looking
at “special interests” and transferable skills as a way to a
viable and realistic career path.
Showing students how to prepare resumes, develop
interviewing skills, and prepare to enter the world of work.
Helping students become self-advocates by learning about
workplace disclosure (when and how to disclose), and how
to ask for and seek workplace accommodations.
HOW?
By using a logical, step-by-step approach, educators,
clinicians, and support professionals will impart this
knowledge via concepts, strategies, ideas, advice, and
classroom materials that will allow students to identify a
career path, look at their transferable skills, and look at job
training opportunities.
Using the materials in this book, students will create
resumes, practice interviewing skills, and create video taped
mock interviews. They will also prepare career and ability
portfolios and a Self-Advocacy Portfolio as they prepare to
enter the world of work.
BENEFITS?
Students realistically prepare for a career path that suits their
talents, abilities, skills, and interests.

---
## Page 530
Students on the ASD and LD Spectrum (ages 16–26) enter
the world of work with a set of workplace readiness skills that
will equip them for success.

## Page 531

SUPPORTING STUDENTS WHEN PROBLEMS ARISE
CHECKLIST AND NOTES

---
## Page 532
Competency 4: Skills for
Independent Living
“You have to learn the rules of the
game. And then you have to play better
than anyone else.”
Albert Einstein
“Every great dream begins with a
dreamer. Always remember, you have
within you the strength, the patience,
and the passion to reach for the stars .
. . to change the world.”
Harriet Tubman
Helpful Hints for Students
The checklist! Have a checklist each evening before you go to
bed for the next day. The first two things on it should be the ones
you are most excited about doing. Only add one thing you are
not excited about doing. After two weeks of doing this, have
three exciting things to do and two not so exciting. Keep doing
this and before you know it you will have tackled many things.

---
## Page 533
Remember, if you can do the task today, why wait until
tomorrow? Never borrow time from tomorrow for a task that can
be done today.
Kaeb Menker, CIP Berkeley
The national average of US college graduates with an ASD (Autism
Spectrum Disorder) diagnosis who are planning to live independently
and separate from their parents is currently 15% (2015). The
importance of imparting and then reinforcing the content of this chapter
while enlisting support personnel in your school or learning environment
cannot be overstated. In order for these numbers to change, students
will need to obtain practical and everyday life skills in school from
classroom instruction and while working individually with transitional
service counselors, life skill and social skill coaches, and tutors.
Chapter 6 will focus on the skills and abilities that students will need to
obtain so that they can be successful when living independently alone
or with others (roommates, friends, or in a relationship). It will also
focus on living independently as part of a larger group or community,
such as a university/college dormitory or an apartment complex.
The Skills for Independent Living chapter will cover the need for
concrete residential strategies and the value of detailed instruction, as
well as social and peer mentoring in this context. Roommate rules and
ways to live in harmony and settle disputes will help educators and
support professionals show students how to share an apartment or
dorm room with others.
Self-care (including managing anxiety and stress and taking
medications), handling finances by budgeting and being fiscally
responsible, menu planning, grocery shopping, cooking, and
housekeeping are included. The “Hidden Curriculum” that comes into
play for your students with this newly found independence and other
strategies for being successful in a neurotypically wired world are also
part of this chapter.

---
## Page 534
PERSONAL INSIGHT: I know that the young people you have been
working with have been working hard on the first three competency
chapters of this book: Skills for Life, Skills for School, and Skills for
Work. The last component – Competency 4: Skills for Independent
Living takes all that they have been learning and looks at applying
these abilities to living independently.
Living independently can cause anxiety, forgetfulness, and at times
disarray and disorder in their lives. In order to be successful and
independent young adults on the ASD (Autism Spectrum Disorder) or
LD (Learning Differences) Spectrum need to – once again – be taught
specific skills so that they can live “on their own.” These skills do not
come naturally, but with practice and implementation, and a lot of
“how to’s” and “checklists” your students will acquire what they need to
live independently and become part of their wider communities.
This chapter, like the previous skill set chapters, breaks down each
ability area into a logical sequence for imparting knowledge to your
students. This sequence can be merged into an existing curriculum,
used in a dedicated classroom, or be used to provide one-on-one
instruction.
Chapter 6 rounds out the “Competency Chapters” and sets the scene
for taking the skills for life, school, work, and independent living
students have acquired out into the wider world that exists around
them.
I commend readers for their perseverance, diligence, and adherence
to the methodology of Autism and Learning Differences (An Active
Learning Teaching Toolkit), as you head into Chapter 6 – Competency
4: Skills for Independent Living.
INDEPENDENT LIVING
Independent living requires a set of skills, actions, and thought
processes that engage all of us (whether we are neurotypically wired or
not) in the daily activities that happen routinely and sometimes

---
## Page 535
automatically within our places of residence, outdoor environments, or
both.
Things like feeding ourselves, bathing, dressing, grooming, working,
cooking, and/or social and leisure activities give us quality of life and
allow us to be part of larger and wider groups and communities.
Without these basics we cannot live independently.
Adolescents and young adults (aged 16–26) who reside on the ASD or
LD Spectrum require routines, prompts, and strategies to help them do
what most of us take for granted. Helping the young men and women
you work with to acquire these skills through the materials, activities,
handouts, and advice in this chapter will go a long way to making the
journey from dependence to independence easier.
Some of your students will require many supports, much repetition, and
a lot of visual cues and prompting as they begin this journey; others will
get things the first time around. Having a fund of knowledge of tried and
true ideas, processes, and techniques will assist you in helping
students implement independent living skills at all levels.
This chapter is divided into four subsections that can act as templates
for imparting independent living skills.
Section I: Self-Care includes tasks such as hygiene, grooming,
manners, and health routines. These are things that are mostly non-
negotiable in terms of living independently. Since acquiring these basic
self-care abilities and other skills for independent living can be
stressful, this section also looks at the anxiety and stress that often
surfaces during the independent living process and provides strategies
and concrete ways for coping with this.
Section II: Daily Living includes the acronym SHIFT (shopping,
housekeeping, independent finances, food preparation, transportation),
and allows the ASD or LD student to take care of buying food and basic
necessities, managing his or her living environment, handling finances,
eating properly, and getting where he or she needs to go.
Section III: Community Integration looks at building relationships and
caring for others, as well as using the independent living skills that are

---
## Page 536
performed in cooperation or in co-existing/occupation with others.
Section IV: Supporting Students When Problems Arise gives each
reader a further helping hand. The strategies, tips, suggestions, ideas,
and solutions that contributing CIP staff members have found useful
when problems arise, as well as the trial and error that sometimes
occurs in the process, are uplifting, heartwarming, and inspiring.
As Chapter 6 concludes, it will allow readers and those who have been
acquiring the knowledge in the book (the wonderful young men and
women you work with) to breathe a huge sigh of relief as it completes
the four competency areas that make up the “skills” part of the book.
The remaining chapters of this book help students take this acquired
fund of knowledge forward, as students apply their obtained skills for
life, school, work, and independent living toward becoming valued
members of their communities.
Section I: Self-Care
This section starts with the basic of all basics … hygiene! Because of
the way an ASD or LD brain is wired, daily hygiene is often the hardest
thing for students to remember to do each day. Showing students how
to create hygiene routines – and then turn these routines into habits –
will help each person conquer his or her individual hygiene challenges
and lead toward feeling fit, groomed, and ready to go every day.
HYGIENE
Tell your students that having good personal hygiene is non-negotiable
at school, when working, and while performing any and all phases or
aspects of daily living.
Explain that this means having good grooming routines (like showering
and brushing teeth), that are practiced daily. It also means wearing

---
## Page 537
clean and unwrinkled clothing at all times.
Explain that it’s important to dress appropriately for social occasions
and the current season (e.g., no cutoffs in 10 degree weather). It also
means making sure that shoes are in good repair and that socks are
clean, matching, and without holes. And lastly, that shirts, blouses and
pants, dresses, and skirts are not wrinkled, are tear and stain free, and
are color coordinated.
Helpful Hints for Students
1. The Quick Ten! When arriving or just before leaving your
apartment, dorm, or house – take ten minutes (set a timer), and
race against the clock. See how much you can spot-clean, tidy
up, organize, or de-clutter. By doing this every day – you’ll have
put in over an hour a week of household maintenance. And… it’s
a good mental and physical workout too, especially if you are a
“wiz” with the vacuum cleaner.
2. Set two alarms, one at your bedside and one across the room.
Once you are up DO NOT GO BACK TO BED! If this does not
get you up and moving set three – and put the third one in
another nearby room like the bathroom, the kitchen, or next to
the coffee pot!
Michael P. McManmon
Explain that “people really do think about how others smell, even
when they are not talking to them!” And, that there is a “Hidden
Curriculum” rule about this. The rule is that “we do not tell people
when we have weird thoughts about how they smell.” What usually
happens is this person will tell ten other people, friends, or peers that

---
## Page 538
another person stinks or smells funny but will not (for whatever reason)
tell the offending person.
Tell students they can avoid this “Hidden Curriculum” scenario by
keeping their bodies, teeth, hair, nails, and clothing clean and well
cared for – not only for their own health – but to avoid other people’s
unkind thoughts about them.
Here are several student handouts that spell out exactly what good
hygiene and wearing clean clothes means. The hygiene handout is
accompanied by a somewhat humorous story that a CIP staff member
wrote (about a bad smell), as well as some tips that students might
employ to remember “self-care” every day.
Something Smells in Here!

---
## Page 539
And … it might be “you” if you forget to practice good personal
hygiene.
Having good personal hygiene means:
•
Taking a shower or bath every day and then checking during
the day that your hands, face, and nails are clean.
•
It also means making sure the bathroom is stocked with
plenty of soap, shampoo, and other toiletries.
•
And … it means making sure you are free of body odor and
bad breath. This means using a deodorant and brushing
teeth (and using toothpaste), as well as showering or taking
a bath.
Here are two “true” stories about this:
A residential counselor in a student dorm noticed that there was
never any soap in the soap dish in “Sam’s” bathroom. He also
noticed there was never any shampoo in his shower and tub.
This was odd as he knew that the school provided these things
to students (as part of their residential fee structure), and that
cleaning staff regularly checked then restocked these items on a
weekly basis.
One day the residential counselor asked “Sam” why he never
had any soap or shampoo in his bathroom. “Sam” replied –
“Well, I just don’t know; I haven’t had any for months.” Together
they approached one of the cleaning staff members on duty that
day. She smiled and led “Sam” to his bathroom, opened up the
double doors under the sink to show him that he had plenty of
soap and shampoo in his bathroom. “Sam” had never thought –
not once in the six months he’d been at school – to open up the
cabinet under the sink. It never occurred to him that this cabinet

---
## Page 540
might be where the soap and shampoo could be found. This
also explained why sometimes “Sam’s” lack of using soap and
shampoo made him hard to be around.
Here’s another poignant and slightly humorous story. This one
was submitted by Ryan Therriault who tutors and helps students
on the ASD and LD Spectrum in Melbourne, Florida. It’s about a
strange odor that was coming from a student she was helping.
This … is what happened!
Students say the Darn-dest Things
I was in a tutoring appointment with a young man named
Joseph in the computer lab. We were sitting side by side and
reviewing his Humanities Class paper. Across from the room
from us, a staff member was shooting a video of another
student. I squatted down on the floor next to Joseph so that I
would not be in the scene, when I caught an “odor.” This student
was one who showered daily, so I was surprised to catch a whiff
of a very bad smell (which we learned later to be coming from
the adhesive on his “soon to be recalled” new sneakers).
I mentioned to him that I smelled something. To which he, with a
flat affect, replied, “Maybe I didn’t wipe very well this morning,
smell my patootee.” Trying to be as serious as I could, I popped
up and said, “I don’t think that will be necessary, Joseph, let’s
continue with your paper.”
Nonetheless, my reaction led to a second take for the student
being filmed, who was doing quite well until I popped up like a
“jack in the box” right behind her to make my comment. A
promising note was that we had it on video, which helped to
support a discussion with Joseph about age appropriate
language. We then tracked down the “real” source of the smell.
Those darn sneakers!”

---
## Page 541
Ryan Therriault
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
TEACHING INSIGHT: Ok! So now your students have the basics of
hygiene down pat but still forget to do these things every day because
they can’t find or locate the things they need to do them! Here are
some ideas that were created by Janell Yonkman who works with “on
the Spectrum” students (ages 16–26) at CIP Bloomington. Janell is an
OT (Occupational Therapist) and her techniques are referred to as
“visual management systems.”
Visual Management Systems
Painter’s Tape: Use this “blue” tape to section off the area around
your sink. Place items that you will need in the taped sections where
you can easily see them. This example shows you that the student has
all the things to brush his teeth in the upper right hand corner. His
deodorant and aftershave are in the lower right hand corner and his
contact lens supplies to the left of the sink along with other things he
needs daily (reflected in mirror), in the upper and lower left hand
corners. This student has also written (with a black marker) on the tape
what goes in each section. Everything is right where he needs it each
morning and night.

---
## Page 542
I had another student who kept forgetting to brush his teeth no matter
how many verbal reminders I gave him. Even a photo on the bathroom
mirror of a person brushing his teeth didn’t work.
We came up with this plan:
We decided to line up a toothbrush in a glass for each day of the week
(see the photo), and by process of elimination – this shows Thursday to
Sunday as four cups are left. This young man would brush his teeth
twice a day; he would use the toothbrush in one of the glasses in the
morning and evening and then put the glass and brush under the sink.
By the time Sunday came around, the bathroom counter would be
empty. He would then wash the glasses and reset them up on the sink
every Sunday night. It worked, and tooth brushing eventually became a
habit. By the end of the semester, this young man felt confident enough
to just have one cup and one brush on the sink.

---
## Page 543
Janell Yonkman, Occupational Therapist, CIP Bloomington
I’m Ready for Life but “Can’t” Find My Pants!
(Or shoes, coat, belt, shirt, blouse, skirt, dress, underwear,
etc.)
Does this sound familiar? And … if you can find and locate your
clothes, are they clean?
Who cares, you say? You “should” and this is why:
•
According to the Center for Disease Control (CDC), in
Atlanta, Georgia, dirty clothes can harbor microorganisms,
and wearing clothes with these microorganisms on them can
lead to rashes and even skin infections.
•
Body odor can also occur when you wear clothes that are
dirty and this too is because of the bacteria and fungi that
are growing on unclean clothes. Clothes capture the
bacteria, making them risky to wear again without washing
them.

---
## Page 544
And now for something truly disgusting!
•
Dirty underwear has traces of many germs, e-coli, and other
bacteria from body fluids and solids that end up in your
underwear, which makes infection more likely if worn again
without washing. Yuk!
What to do?
•
The only way to prevent the spread of germs and bacteria
found on clothes from normal wear is to wash them and it’s
also important to wash your hands after contact with dirty
laundry.
•
The Center for Disease Control (CDC) also describes high-
risk dirty clothing as items containing blood from an injury,
sweat, or contaminated foods or clothes that were in contact
with animals. These clothes must be washed at 140 degrees
Fahrenheit with a bleach-based product.
•
In order to clean clothing completely, clothes must be dried
immediately upon washing. Mold can occur on clothing left
wet for a prolonged period of time, which can also cause
odor and skin infections.
Keeping clean clothes in order:
•
Once clothes are washed and dried, the next thing is to keep
them organized so that they don’t end up on the floor, or in
the back of your closet, or under your bed. Having a system
for organizing clothes, shoes, underwear, socks, and
outerwear will ensure that when you pull something out of a
closet, drawer, or bin, it is clean and ready to go.
•
This will also save you time on a busy morning and decrease
stress as you will not get frustrated having to hunt or tear
things apart in order to find a clothing item that you need.

---
## Page 545
You need to go from this:
To this:

---
## Page 546

---
## Page 547
Here are some things that will help you:
Some people use clean color-coded or flip-flap bins to put
shoes, clothing, and other items in to keep their closets
organized. Others use a combination of labeled color-coded
bins, dresser drawers, shelves, and a traditional closet system.
The following checklist will get you started on organizing all of
your clothes and things you’ll need so that you can find and put
on clean clothes and be ready to start your day.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
I’m Ready for Life and “Can” Find My Pants!
(Or shoes, coat, belt, shirt, blouse, skirt, dress, underwear,
etc.)
Organize hanging garments by type and color. Blouses,
for example, can be sorted first by sleeve length and then
by shade. By keeping like with like, options for a given outfit
are clear at a glance.
Choose the right hanger. Your closet will look neater if
you use just one type of hanger – wood, wire, or plastic.
Set up zones. A low rod holds tops and skirts; an eye-level
pole, dresses; and a high bar, shirts and suits. A high shelf
works fine for out-of-season shoes and sweaters.
Arrange folded items. Any clothing that will stretch out of
shape should be folded over hangers. When organizing the
folded clothing you’ll store on shelves, place heavier items
at the bottom of the pile, and lighter ones at the top. After
folding, arrange garments by function (workout tops or shirts

---
## Page 548
together, work tops or shirts together, going out on a date
shirts or tops, etc.) and color (white to pastels to bright
colors to black). If your closet doesn’t have shelves,
consider using part of your clothing rod for hanging canvas
ones.
Manage odds and ends. Store bags, belts, ties, scarves,
and other accessories in plain sight on hooks or racks,
which can be attached to the inside of your closet door.
Contain what doesn’t hang. Use color coded containers
or consider a matching set of baskets–to hold accessories
and clothing that can’t be hung, such as socks and
undergarments. Smaller baskets or boxes can store a single
type of accessory, such as scarves or hats.
Pick a system for storing shoes. Choose between a
shoe rack on the floor, a hanging shoe organizer, see-
through plastic boxes, or original shoe boxes with
photographs stapled to them to identify the boxes’ contents.
Put shoes you don’t wear frequently in labeled plastic boxes
on a high shelf, in another closet, or under your bed.
MANNERS
Tell your students that having, using, and displaying manners is about
treating people with respect and dignity. This means that they need to
display good manners to all the people that they will come into contact
with every day. Therefore, the word manners is more than just sitting
down and eating politely (although that is important too).
Explain that whether it is greeting the people they interact with cordially
and with politeness, making a conscious effort not to be rude or
inappropriate in a public setting, offering to do a favor for a friend, or
apologizing if they have offended someone, learning manners (in this
context) will help your students understand how important simple

---
## Page 549
courtesies like being polite and even having good table manners will
be as they integrate into their communities.
Explain that you will help them understand various types of manners
and that having good manners when eating is actually a good place to
start talking about manners in general, so that’s what your class will
talk about first.
Ask students to think about the fact that they eat three meals a day.
And that some of these meals take place in public places like
restaurants, school or work cafeterias, in public settings (like a potluck
at work, a picnic in a park, or a late night meal in their dormitories).
They will also have meals with their families, roommates, and from time
to time, their co-workers.
Ask students to think about how unpleasant any meal with another
person would be if the person they were dining with did not know how
use appropriate table manners.
Explain that table manners are something that many people need to be
taught, not just those on the ASD or LD Spectrum, and the student
handout on page 246 is something they can throw away, if they are
100% sure that they know how to do all the things listed. However, if
they are unsure about anything on the list, they can use the handout to
learn something that will help them blend in with peers and not draw
attention to themselves by exhibiting bad manners in public. Having
and using appropriate table manners will give each person the
confidence to enjoy dining (whether it is breakfast, lunch, or dinner), in
the company of others.
Using Appropriate Table Manners
Stretch your imagination if you will, and picture yourself living in
your first apartment or dorm room. You and your roommates
decide to have a potluck dinner where everyone brings

---
## Page 550
something they have cooked and you all sit down and share the
meal together.
Now … imagine getting stuck sitting across the table from
someone who puts his head down to his plate and gobbles down
all his food within seconds. The person on your right is shoving
large pieces of food in her mouth and chewing with her mouth
open. The person on your left decides it’s ok to eat mashed
potatoes with his fingers. Three people decide to eat and run,
while another wipes his mouth on his sleeve and lets out a big
belch. Are you having fun yet?
Believe it or not, these are some of the most common mistakes
people make when in the company of others. You can avoid
making one or more of these mistakes by following the “golden
rules” for displaying good table manners; that way you’ll never
be like any of the people in the above scenario and more
importantly – people will want to be in your company when
eating. And … you’ll be invited to a lot of potlucks!
Having good table manners means:
•
Taking small helpings.
•
Using “please” when asking for something and saying
“thank you” when you receive it.
•
Taking reasonable sized bites.
•
Chewing with your mouth closed.
•
Using the correct utensils.
•
Not using your fingers to eat food except when appropriate
(chicken, ribs, French fries, etc.).
•
Keeping your mouth clean by using a napkin.
•
Eating at a reasonable speed.

---
## Page 551
•
Refraining from belching.
•
Sitting up straight.
•
Refraining from resting elbows on the table.
•
Conversing with others and not rushing through the meal to
leave.
•
Asking permission to leave the table.
If you are not displaying any of the above behaviors, now is the
time to stop and change your habits. If you want to be taken
seriously and treated with respect as you begin working,
making new friends, and engaging in social activities, you will
need to have good table manners. If you already have them –
display them often. This is called peer mentoring.
RELATIONSHIPS AND MANNERS
Explain to your students that manners play a role in all relationships
they will form with others and especially with their peers. It’s about
displaying courtesy, respect, and treating others with dignity.
Tell students that this means thinking about the people they are
interacting with and also about how peers and others might be thinking
and feeling.
Explain to your students that it’s about being aware of their physical
presence and the physical presence of others. And, using their eyes to
think about others and watch what they are thinking about.
Explain that it’s also about using language to relate to others and that
this is called interacting verbally with another person.
Tell students that this means including others in their conversations
and when appropriate, complimenting others.

---
## Page 552
Explain that using manners when relating to others also means that
from time to time they will need to express apologies to a person they
have inadvertently offended or upset.
Tell students that this means verbally apologizing by saying “I’m sorry.”
It also means offering to make up for their actions when appropriate.
Explain that apologies should be made face to face, with sincerity, and
most importantly by not making excuses.
RESPECTING THE POSSESSIONS OF
OTHERS
Explain that respecting the possessions of others is another way that
your students can exhibit and show others that they have good
manners.
Tell students that this means:
•
Always knocking before entering someone’s private room.
•
Asking permission before borrowing another person’s property.
•
Returning what is borrowed in a timely manner.
•
Returning what is borrowed in the same condition as when they
first received it.
•
Replacing items you may have accidentally damaged.

---
## Page 553
VOLUNTEERING TO HELP OTHERS AND
ASKING FOR HELP FROM OTHERS
Explain that volunteering to help others and asking for help from
others is also an area where students can show others they know how
to exhibit good manners.
Tell students that when they volunteer to help others by helping with a
task, project, or job or by giving assistance in other ways (emotional or
physical), they should:
•
Check to make sure the person wants help.
•
Ask how they can help the person:
º
This might be offering personal assistance such as helping
an elderly person cross the street, or carrying grocery bags
to the car of a mom with three kids.
º
It might mean helping with a task or job –perhaps helping a
friend move to a new apartment.

---
## Page 554
º
Or … it might mean giving another person emotional help:
listening to a friend when they are upset or experiencing a
stressful situation.
•
Respect the other person’s right to confidentiality if asked to give
it.
•
Maintain a positive attitude.
•
Follow through with the help immediately.
•
Check back after the help has been given to see if there is
anything else that you might help the person with.
Explain to your students that having manners also means requesting
help politely and at the other person’s convenience.
Tell students that this means:
•
Describing the help needed and this might be a ride to school,
help with a problem like a flat tire, or emotional help and support
when they are upset.
•
Asking for assistance; an example of this might be:
º
Asking someone to listen or support you emotionally after a
fight with your roommate, when you receive bad news, or
during a stressful period in your life.
•
Thanking the person for helping you.
ESTABLISHING HEALTH AND WELLNESS
ROUTINES
The last segment of Section I: Self-Care is about showing your
students how to establish health routines that will keep them socially,
emotionally, and physically healthy. It’s also about being positive and
learning how to handle anxiety and stress as students deal with living
independently while interacting with others.

---
## Page 555
Tell students that setting wellness goals when they are living
independently will literally “save their lives.” And that they have already
learned a lot about getting and staying physically fit during their high
school and college years and while working through the chapters in this
book.
Explain that maintaining a healthier diet, eating well, getting some form
of exercise daily, and getting enough sleep will lead to stress reduction,
feeling well rested, and becoming emotionally balanced. And that in
order to do these they will need to establish wellness routines.
Here are some student handouts, that will allow students to plan for all
of the above, and have checklists and reminders for doing so.
Explain that they will be creating some sample wellness routines in
class (using the forms on pages 249–252) so that everyone
understands the concept and why it’s important (initially) when living
independently to have these (written) routines.
Tell students that at some point, these routines will become habits and
they will no longer need visual and written cues.
Morning Routine
You know from the “Executive Function” work that you have
been doing in class that this means being organized, planning,
and following the same pattern each and every day until your
morning (and other routines) become habits.
Your morning routine includes getting out of bed at a specific
time, taking care of your hygiene and getting dressed, eating
breakfast, getting everything ready to leave the house, and
planning enough time to get to school or work. It might also
include scheduling time for exercise, meditation, or yoga.

---
## Page 556
Whatever your individual routine might be – know that it will help
to get your brain organized and this will help you “have a good
and successful day.”
Before you create your own routine, here’s a list of some
things that a lot of people do during their morning
routines:
•
Wake up at the same time each and every day (including
weekends).
•
Pray, meditate, or do yoga to spiritually center yourself for
the day.
•
Head for the gym to exercise then return home.
•
Take a shower, do general hygiene routine, and put on
the clean clothes you’ve laid out the night before.
•
Eat a good breakfast (it can be fast and easy), and do the
dishes.
•
Take any morning medications and/or vitamins and
supplements.
•
Consult a “to-do” list for the day and get things
organized.
•
Make sure backpack or briefcase contain all that’s
needed for the day.
•
Pack a lunch for school or work.
•
Do a “quick ten” pickup before leaving for the day –
taking ten minutes to wiz through the apartment or dorm
room and tidy as much as possible in that time-frame.
Now, create your own morning routine (what will “for sure” work
for you), and hang it up on a bulletin board or your bedroom,
bathroom, or your refrigerator door.

---
## Page 557
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
My Morning Routine
FEELING FIT, GROOMED, AND READY TO
GO!
Write out the steps for your personal and individualized
morning routine:
1. ______________________________
2. ______________________________
3. ______________________________
4. ______________________________
5. ______________________________
6. ______________________________
Now you’ll create a similar list for the evening that will help
you get ready for bed.
This checklist will help you prepare your brain and body for a
good night’s sleep. It will allow you to get organized for the next
day, then settle and calm down as you prepare to sleep.

---
## Page 558
Be sure to include things like:
•
Planning the night before for the next day.
•
Packing up your backpack the evening before class and
putting it and your keys by the front door.
•
Having multiple backpacks for different days of the week.
•
Taking all night time medications.
•
Doing stretching, yoga, or meditation, or anything that will
help you “unwind.”
•
Preparing a nice warm drink like hot cocoa or heated milk.
•
Doing nightly hygiene routine, and plan to get at least six to
eight hours of sleep each night.
•
Turning off all electronics – putting your cell phone and iPad
or anything else you might be tempted to turn on in the
middle of the night in a night stand or desk drawer.
•
Setting two or three alarm clocks (at least one should be
away from the bed), if necessary.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Evening and Bedtime Routine
Here’s a handout that you can put on your bulletin board or tape to your
bedroom door to help you remember your “getting ready for bed”
routine.
Brains like routines. Getting ready for bed using the same
routine every night allows your brain to settle and calm down
and allows for a peaceful and restful night’s sleep.

---
## Page 559
Write out the steps for your personal and individualized
evening and bedtime routine:
1. _________________________________________
2. _________________________________________
3. _________________________________________
4. _________________________________________
5. _________________________________________
6. _________________________________________
TAKING MEDICATIONS
The last thing you need to have a very important routine for is
taking your medications. This will help you remember why you
take your medications, when to take them, and how much to
take. If you don’t take any you can throw this handout away or
give it to someone who might need it.
And remember … Each of us has a brain that has trouble
remembering things. Sometimes, depending on how your brain
works, you might feel anxious, worried, sad, or engage in
hyperactivities. Doctors have developed medications to help you
find balance.
However, they will not work unless you take them at the same
time each and every day of the week. It’s good to have a routine
that you can write out and refer to each day so that you can help
your brain by taking your medications on time.

---
## Page 560
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
My Daily Medication Routine
Medication
Reason I Take This
Time of Day I Take It
Amount I Take
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The medication chart can help your students to remember to take their
medications. Explain that they can also program their smart phones and
use apps that will help them remember this.
ANXIETY
Helping the young men and women you work with understand that
stress and anxiety in adolescents and young adults on the ASD or LD
Spectrum can be debilitating or excessive at times as they undertake
independent living is important.
Explain that stress and anxiety levels are often exacerbated by school
or work commitments, having to interact with peers and other people in
their lives, and by trying to establish a social life that might include a
long-term relationship as well.
Tell students that figuring out ways to manage this anxiety and/or
stress is the back-bone of self-care from a wellness angle and the most
important thing they can do for themselves.
Explain that awareness of anxiety and its symptoms is essential. It’s
the only way your students will be able to tell others that they are

---
## Page 561
experiencing it.
The student handout on page 253 can help your students gain
awareness of how anxiety might be playing out in their lives.
Tell students that you will be talking about anxiety and stress in general
ways and that you and other “school” support personnel are available
to talk to them on a one-to-one basis at any time.
Explain that by sharing the following stories, tips, and techniques of
how to manage stress in the following student handouts you are
showing students ways of handling their stress that have worked for
others on the ASD and LD Spectrum. And that these are suggestions.
Remind students that they should let others know if they are still in
distress after trying the suggestions, ideas, and strategies. Sometimes
a different type of intervention is needed and that might mean seeking
the assistance of a trained professional who specifically helps people
deal with anxiety or stress.
Anxiety and Stress Warning Signs
You might be experiencing anxiety and stress as you undertake
living independently either on your own or with roommates and
others (like a girlfriend or boyfriend). If the things listed below
are happening to you, know that telling someone, then working
with that person to develop and devise techniques and
strategies to help you, will give you much relief. Awareness is
the first step.
Here are some things to be aware of:
1. People may not tell you directly that you are experiencing stress.
However, if people use words when describing you such as:
“irritable,” “difficult to get along with,” or “gets upset about things

---
## Page 562
easily,” these may be indicators that you are under stress and
that your stress is affecting your behavior.
2. If you are spending an excessive amount of time sleeping, you
may be experiencing stress and anxiety and may need to see a
counselor.
3. If you are worried about little things, are easily distracted, or
can’t focus and concentrate on day-to-day tasks, you are
probably experiencing anxiety or stress.
4. If you stop going out with friends, socializing or going to the gym,
because of inward or outward fears, you are probably
experiencing anxiety or stress.
5. If you have changed your routines, such as sleeping or eating
cycles, and are missing or skipping meals or classes, these
things may be due to anxiety or stress.
6. If you are always worrying about what others think of you or your
actions, you might be under stress or experiencing anxiety.
Remember to: Use the HALT scale when you start
experiencing stress or others are telling you that you are
becoming “intense.”
The HALT Scale
On a scale of 1 to 10 indicate where you are right now. H is for
Hungry: You might say you are about a 9 if you didn’t eat
breakfast. A is for Angry: You might be about a 1 if you were
five minutes late for school today – more of a slight irritation. L is
for Lonely: You might be a 2 if you don’t feel lonely because you
are doing things with friends today. T is for Tired: You might be
a 10 if you stayed up on your computer until 2 a.m. and had to
get up at 6 a.m.

---
## Page 563
What you might do to cope: For the H (9) – go get something
nutritious to eat and take your vitamins. For the T (10) you might
take a rest between your classes that day, and get to bed at a
reasonable time that night.
Also know that: It is typical for young adults with an ASD or LD
diagnosis who are going to college, working at a job, or living
independently for the first time, to experience anxiety or stress. It
is also typical to seek assistance from a counselor, a mentor, a
teacher, or an advisor, so don’t hesitate to take advantage of
these services. If you have checked one or more of these things,
it’s important to tell someone … together you can work on
strategies to make things easier for you.
And … you are not alone. More than 27% or one in four adults
in the United States received treatments such as medication,
therapy, or medication and therapy in the last two years. (Harris
poll, Psychology Today Magazine 2004)
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
PERSONAL INSIGHT: As you discuss anxiety and stress with the
young adults you work with, explain that they are going to create a
special notebook entitled “Anxiety: the Nemesis of All Awesomeness!!”

---
## Page 564
And that they are going to “Meet Jennifer!” through her terrific writing,
coping strategies, and student handouts that she’s created for this
book.
Explain that Jennifer Cook O’Toole is the winner of the Autism Society
of America’s 2014 Outstanding Literary Work of the Year, the 2012
Temple Grandin Award, a recipient of GRASP’s 2012 Distinguished
Spectrumite Medal, 2012 AuKids Speaker of the Year, a nominee for
the 2012 Autism Society Book Award, and Godiva’s Woman of the
Year. Most recently, she’s been invited to speak to the President’s
Council on Fitness at the White House Summit on Americans with
Disabilities. Jennifer Cook O’Toole is one fantastic and inspirational
lady!
Jennifer has graciously allowed Autism and Learning Differences (An
Active Learning Teaching Toolkit), to excerpt and adapt “Anxiety: the
Nemesis of all Awesomeness!!” and her classroom materials, ideas,
and materials for this book from her book, Sisterhood of the Spectrum.
Jennifer talks about herself (she, like me, is on the Autism Spectrum),
and then proceeds to give the young men and women you work with
