her story and her tips for dealing with anxiety. She is also the mom of
two children on the ASD Spectrum.
Explain to your students that I have used these tips myself and so
have my co-workers. And I know that once they read the materials that
they (and that maybe some of their teachers and professionals using
this Teaching Toolkit) will too.
And lastly, I hope you and your students are motivated by Jennifer and
to know that people like her are doing everything they can to raise
awareness about the issues people on the ASD/LD Spectrum
experience.
Jennifer Cook O’Toole has also contributed a story to Chapter 12: The
Experts Speak!, entitled: Bottoms Up! Thinking and Learning
Alternatives. I thank Jennifer for both of these contributions and for her
generosity in allowing me to use her work in this book.

---
## Page 565
Ideas for using Jennifer O’Toole’s “Anxiety: The Nemesis of All
Awesomeness!!” student handouts.
•
Read each and every word to or with your students. Hearing
Jennifer’s words “out loud” will be a powerful experience for
your students.
•
Have each student create a notebook or folder that he or she
can take home, keep handy, and refer to often. Have students
put the materials that follow in this notebook.
•
Take the sections of her essay apart – then ask students for
examples from their own lives.
•
Divide students into small groups and do some role playing with
the scenarios portrayed and have students talk about their own
anxiety coping tools and ideas.
•
Ask students to get creative when reading through the student
materials and make suggestions of how others might use some
of Jennifer’s ideas.
Helpful Hints for Students
How to avoid a melt-down:
Ask yourself – have you had your medications? Have you had
breakfast or any food at all today? Did you shower and put on a
clean set of clothes? If not – start by seeing if these things will
turn the situation around. An energy bar, something to drink,
your meds, or a warm relaxing shower may be what’s needed.
Michael P. McManmon

---
## Page 566
Anxiety: The Nemesis of All Awesomeness!!
by Jennifer Cook O’Toole (Aspie, Author, and Mom of Asperkids!)
adapted from Sisterhood of the Spectrum
You Need to Know (And Believe):
•
Everything about fear is primal. There’s no logic involved.
•
If we focus all of our energy on curbing tantrums or melt-
downs without addressing the cause of our anxiety, nothing
will change.
My Story:
The year was 1982. Ronald Reagan was in office. “Don’t You
Want Me, Baby?” was on the radio, and the smiley face
emoticon was born. But, like, what I most clearly remember
about, like, that year isn’t valley girl speech or, like, Ms.PacMan.
It is E.T., the Reeses-pieces eating alien who I was
absolutely sure was hiding in my closet.
Try as they might to counter my certainty that there was not, in
fact, an extraterrestrial lurking in my bedroom, my parents
couldn’t convince me otherwise. So I clutched my teddy bear for
reassurance, and sat there in my bed – scared. Now, I’m not
talking nervous or “trying to sneak into bed with Mom and Dad”
scared. No, I mean to the pit of stomach, cold sweat, freak- out
if you touch me terrified.

---
## Page 567
I’d venture that almost everyone reading this has felt that kind of
fear at some point in his or her life. But try this for me: allow your
body, not just your mind, to remember that feeling – from when
you were little, or even now, that you are older, of your heart
thudding, mind racing, stomach lurching, your little self ready-to-
run-or-fight against any shadow. That’s what fear actually is, you
see. It’s not a concept or idea; it’s not a topic to be discussed
rationally. Everything about fear is primal – irrational … and
bodily. There’s no logic involved.
Anxiety is a little bit different. Imagine the volume of that fear is
turned down just a bit so that it’s not so immediate a threat or so
acute a danger. Instead, it’s replaced by a gnawing, jittery, ever-
present sensation of waiting for the threat…waiting for the fear.
It’s like living with the “Jaws” music playing. You don’t see the
danger. But you surely know there’s something “out there.”
That’s anxiety!
We “spectrumites,” whose bodies and minds are wired a little
differently, live with varying levels and intensities of almost
perpetual anxiety.
That may sound paranoid – but it’s not. Paranoia is irrational
fear. Most of us have been bullied, are constantly assaulted by
sensory input, must fend our way through daily social situations
which seem random and chaotic – and often, just as we think we
are at the top of our games, the rug gets pulled out from under
us. In other words, our anxiety is an absolutely rational reaction
to the experiences we have had.
If we focus all of our energy on curbing melt-downs, obsessions,
or rigidity, we’re only tearing at the leaves. Nothing will change –
either in our behavior or in our hearts. It’s like yanking at a weed
and just tearing off the leaves. The weed disappears from view,
but it isn’t gone. In time, it just grows back. But grab that weed
near the base – dig at the roots, and pull – gently. What

---
## Page 568
happens? Yes, another weed may grow elsewhere, but this one
is gone. Anxiety is that root. It is the seed from which our topical
fixations and “overly sensitive,” routine-driven, black/white,
obsessive behaviors arise.
We are trying to catch the rain. We are trying to create
predictable order in a chaotic, often random world … by asking a
million questions, by challenging exceptions to rules, by scripting
dialogue we know was funny (once), or dictating play. It’s not
that we want to be unlikeable or difficult or dominate the
conversation with topics other people don’t enjoy. We just want
to feel secure, safe – and to be able to stop the endless waiting
for unwelcome surprises.
We’ve Got This!
As a child, you learned to recognize (and now, hopefully, as
young adults you respond) to the physical symptoms of hunger,
thirst, and fatigue. That’s a start toward stress-busting ninja
status. But there’s more … Our minds are wired differently. The
result is that we’re more sensitive to some of what our bodies
take in from the world around us (maybe sounds or smells), yet
we crave other kinds of sensory input (pressure, extreme
flavors/temperatures). And, just like mixed-up signals in the
inner ear can cause carsickness (which seems pretty
disconnected), if the sensory input we get isn’t what we need,
it’s a good bet that anxiety spikes (nervousness, stress,
snappiness, confusion, etc.) will be along for the ride, too.
Independence means more than knowing how to make yourself
dinner or pay your bills. It means learning to listen to our bodies
and minds, manage stress and anxiety, and spend our energy
on LIVING.

---
## Page 569
Every person on the HUMAN spectrum must
LEARN to:
•
RECOGNIZE your needs.
•
TALK to yourself.
•
ASK FOR what you need.
•
IDENTIFY and CHOOSE a solution.
•
ACCESS that solution independently.
When I was little, I remember being taken shoe shopping by my
fashion-conscious (rather stern) grandmother. Two hours later,
we left – without any shoes. I was in tears and my grandma was
mortified. Every pair I’d tried had wrought a different complaint
from me, and while I was merely being honest and trying not to
waste her generosity on shoes that I wouldn’t wear, she and the
salesman perceived my “oversensitivity” as a bratty lack of
gratitude.
My mom reports that Halloween wasn’t much better – every
year. Itchy costumes made me miserable (her too, I’m sure).
Trips to the beach were itchy and sandy. And heaven forbid
there was an un-cut (or poorly cut out) tag in my shirt or shorts,
there was nothing else on earth I could think about. But back
then, there was no “SPD” or “Asperger’s” label for bright,
articulate children – everyone simply decided I was a rude,
overdramatic, hypochondriac who couldn’t play well with others.
In truth, I was a child in real pain with no one to believe me and
largely without friends…but desperately in want of them. The
truth is that, often, our biggest hurdles come from the
neurotypical adults who just don’t “get how real our reality is.”
To put it bluntly – their “normalcy” is getting in the way! After all,
even the best teacher or most loving parent would never think to
teach us to “scratch” an “itch” that she’s never felt. No one has
to teach parents to understand our hunger or weariness or thirst.

---
## Page 570
When a baby cries, those are the natural go-tos. Parents expect
those problems, asking, “Are you hungry?” teaching both
sensation recognition and vocabulary as they do.
But no typical mom says, “Aw, sweetie, are you feeling
dysregulated?” Instead, neurotypical adults just see raw, “bad”
behavior – the symptoms of sensory/emotional dysregulation,
not of bad parenting or a bad child.
On the other hand, as an Aspie adult, the idea of
“overstimulation” is utterly natural. I never could make sense of
people who would jangle things at my children or toss them
around to try to stop their crying. If I were a baby, I’d be terrified!
And, more often than not, my kids were only made more upset
by the additional sensory distress well-meaning adults put “in
their faces.” Instead of lights and keys and gizmos, my
Asperkids needed deep pressure, rhythmic motion, and white
noise. As an (albeit undiagnosed) Aspie myself, I sensed that.
So – Here’s How “I” (Aspie Mom and Mom
of Asperkids) Can Help You!
Step 1: RECOGNIZE What Your Body is Saying!
I can’t overstate this: when we feel either understimulated or
overstimulated, we physically CANNOT reason, listen, or think about
anything else. We can’t learn. We can’t rationalize well. We can’t
hear others’ needs. It’s like trying to see your own reflection in a pot
of boiling water. Nothing is clear. So … first things first.
You have to learn to recognize the signs of your own sensory
dysregulation, (which changes from day to day and situation to
situation). Remember that we can’t expect others to do for us what
we aren’t willing to try to do for ourselves.

---
## Page 571
When sensory input is a problem, try tuning out by turning your other
senses into a solution. Consider: Do I want something to TOUCH
(stress ball, emery board, plush animal), something to HEAR
(meditation podcast, wind-chimes, sound machine), something to
SEE (snow globe, lava lamp), something to TASTE (minty gum, sour
candy, or even a lemon, fruit leather), something to SMELL (candles,
perfume, fresh laundry)?
Step 2: TALK TO YOURSELF. Seriously!
Breathe slowly. Smell the roses (inhale, through your nose); blow out
the candles (exhale completely out of your mouth). Again. Breathe.
You’re going to be ok. You’ve felt this before, these feelings you have
right now.
Uncomfortable. Anxious. Embarrassed. Afraid. You’ve felt them,
and you have survived. Breathe, and know you will survive this, too,
wiser and stronger. The feelings can’t break you. They can hurt.
They can sting. They can way-lay your plans. But if you’ll sit with the
feelings – notice them, stay with them without trying to run – if you do
sit with them and really feel them – you’ll discover they have a
bottom. They have an end. They aren’t all-consuming. You won’t
drown in them. They will become shallower … eventually; shallow
enough that you will begin to walk again.
Keep breathing. Keep moving. Soon, you’ll emerge from the hurt.
You’ll be able to turn around and see the far shore where you started.
You’ll know that you made it through and that you are powerful and
resilient enough to float above anything. Right now, that sounds
impossible. It feels unbearable. I know. So for now, just breathe. You
will get there. One breath at a time. This moment will pass. It will be
over. And you won’t be.
Step 3: ASK FOR WHAT YOU NEED (People Can’t Read Our
Minds or Bodies)!

---
## Page 572
I know – I mean really, I know – how hard it is to experience strong
emotions and explain whatever it is that you’re feeling. It’s a bit, I tell
neurotypicals, like being famished and having to listen to a lesson on
fractions. Or having desperately to use the restroom and explain
Shakespeare. Yeah, right. That’s going to go well. And really, it’s
awfully tough to even realize that whatever we’re experiencing is
different from what other people feel. Your body’s normal is your
normal. Until you use this simple process, your particular “brand” of
sensory needs are going to feel invisible – obvious – to you AND to
everyone else. To you, they are always been, always-known physical
experiences … so find a CALM moment and try this:
•
Trace this outline:

---
## Page 573
•
Circle or color or describe what it FEELS LIKE inside your
body when others see those “outside the body” behaviors
listed above – use crayons, magazine clippings, or even a
thesaurus.
•
Need some suggestions? Visually, black circles around your
eyes could indicate tunnel vision. Squiggly lines in the legs
might represent anxiety. A green swirl in the belly might be

---
## Page 574
nausea. Or use words like sweaty, tight, claustrophobic, and
shaky.
This is BIG, important stuff, my friends. With information – with
awareness – comes POWER. Years ago, you were taught that a
rumbling, empty sensation meant you were hungry – next, you
learned that just because you felt that tummy growl, relief wasn’t
simply going to appear. Nope. You needed to ask for or go get food.
Well, now you are both teacher and student. You are teaching
yourself to pay attention to and identify your own biological
experiences … and that’s the first step toward independence.
Fidgeting, squirming, thrashing – many of the negative behaviors the
world associates with people on the ASD or LD Spectrum are really
just “bigger” versions of a “grumbly tummy.” The body is
communicating loudly – not asking for food, but for protection from or
more of the sensory input the brain needs. Once you can discern
your body’s messages, you can also learn to express discomfort and
request whatever you’ll need to feel better.
Step 4: IDENTIFY AND CHOOSE A SOLUTION
Katniss Everdeen wouldn’t want to enter the arena with one arrow in
her quiver. And Harry Potter needed to know more than “Reducto!”
to survive. No one sensory solution will always work for you or
always be available – so like Katniss and Harry, you need options
to give your body what it needs. Here are some great ones:
•
Chewing gum (two sticks at a time).
•
“Fidgets,” “kooshes,” and stress balls.
•
Salty pretzel rods.
•
Sugar-free hard candies.
•
Drinks (especially thick, cold smoothies) through a straw.
•
Long walks or runs (repetitive motion).
•
Swimming.

---
## Page 575
•
Lava lamps.
•
Noise-canceling headphones.
•
Swings – hammock, gliders (include back and forth AND in
circles going both directions).
•
Rocking chairs.
•
Bean bag chairs.
•
A calming room where you can be alone.
•
Irlen colored lenses for glasses (reduce visual fatigue).
•
Climbing (trees or indoor wall/mountain climbing).
•
Pounding – clay, play dough, hammer/nails.
•
Spinning (twisted swing, office chair).
•
Playing catch with a weighted ball or heavy pillow.
•
Human burrito (swaddle yourself in a blanket and roll back
and forth).
•
Hot showers or baths.
•
Classical music.
•
Meditation podcasts.
•
Locks, keys, and switches.
•
Tug-of-War with a table leg and a stretchy exercise band.
•
Yoga.
•
Scented candles.
•
Massage.
Step 5: ACCESS A SOLUTION
•
Having Asperger’s, Autism, ADD/ADHD, and/or Sensory
Processing 
Disorder 
are 
real 
MEDICAL 
diagnoses.
Therefore, you have the right to expect easy, independent

---
## Page 576
access to whatever tools you need – regardless of what rules
apply to other students at school or in your workplace. You
are not asking for favors. By occupying your nerves as your
body needs it, you create a sense of balance – that means
better 
focus, 
more 
emotional 
calm, 
and 
deeper
concentration. You’re stating what you need. (After all, is it
special treatment that someone who is nearsighted gets to
wear contacts to school or on the job?)
•
Experiment with various sensory strategies and assemble
your favorites into a toolkit; keep kits in your desk (either
school or workplace), locker, your counselor’s or therapist’s
office, in your car, and in your backpack or briefcase.
•
Share your needs (privately) with teachers and supervisors
at work BEFORE you have a tough time, so they can work
with and empower you.
•
Create a plan for “escape” to a quiet place (the library, the
work break room, outside, etc.) when emotions get too
overwhelming. You should be able to excuse yourself without
making your needs public knowledge.
Doing It Anyway!
There was no alien in my bedroom back in 1982, of course. But
in my 30-odd years of life, I’ve met very real danger in places
and with people that should have been as familiar and safe as a
childhood haven.
Those of us on the Spectrum want to be liked; we want to
please – even to impress. We certainly don’t want to be
the problem. If you can remember that in the hardest moments,
if you can remember what the feeling of true fear is, then you
can give yourself a great gift: respect, understanding, and a
plan.

---
## Page 577
After all, courage is feeling fear doing the “scary stuff”
anyway. Those of us on the spectrum have to choose to be
courageous almost every day … like that little girl in the bedroom
back in 1982, when we are afraid (even if that fear is
unnecessary), we certainly don’t want to feel condescended to
or be punished.
We need understanding, respect, and patience. Like I did years
ago (and still do now), we all need allies – stuffed or otherwise –
to cling to until we can steady ourselves … until we can see,
peeking through the fear, the safety and calm of an unsullied
tomorrow.
Special Stuff: The Box on the Shelf!
Your mind is racing. And now, no matter how hard you try, no
matter how many people tell you to lay off or tone it down …no
matter how much you hear “Don’t worry about it,” you just can’t
stop thinking.
That thing that might happen. Or that might not happen!
There are other things you have to do. Other things you want to
do. But dang it, you’re stuck in endless loops of “yeah but, what
if …”
“Just don’t think about it,” they say. And all you can think is,
“Oh. Ok. Thanks. There’s one I hadn’t tried.”
“Let it go,” as the song says. But you shake your head. And pick
at your nails. If only letting go were as easy as Disney makes it
sound.
I know. I get it. You’re exhausted. You’re alone in your head.
And you need some help getting out.

---
## Page 578
Here’s how we’re going to make that happen.
Imagine that you are holding a box. It has a lid, which you take
off and put aside.
Now imagine that thing you’re worried about. Maybe you see a
photograph of it. Maybe you picture words on scraps of paper.
Maybe you see a movie clip. It doesn’t matter how you envision
the worry. It’s your idea and your box. So however you do it,
you’re doing it right.
Next, see yourself placing the worry into the box, and put the lid
back on. Although the sides are opaque (you can’t see through),
you know what’s in there, and you know it’s safe.
Next, imagine that you write one word on one of the side of the
box. “BREATHE.” Because it’s really enough if that’s as much
as you can do. Really.
Now envision an empty closet. There’s no door – you can see
the shelves clearly. Place the box on one of those shelves, with
the “BREATHE” side facing out. Step away.
Your worry is there. It’s safe. No one can take it away from you,
and if you need to go back and check on it at some point, you
can. It’ll still be there. You don’t have to let it go. But you do get
to take a break.
What will you do now? Well, you can also walk away for a little
bit. The length of time – a minute or a month – isn’t important.
What matters is that you are in charge now.
So, take it down and look when you need to. Leave it there if you
feel like you can. Or just glance up, read … and BREATHE.
Safely. Steadily.
You’ve got it. Anxiety just no longer has you!

---
## Page 579
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Jennifer is an inspiration to me not only
because she is on the “ASD Spectrum” like I am and has “Asperkids”
(as she calls them), but because she is upfront, personal, and making
sure that the world starts to not only understand people on the
“ASD/LD Spectrum,” but that people who reside “on the Spectrum”
have tools, ideas, and resources to cope with the day to day. And …
most importantly, she shows “us” that we can become “self-change
agents.” Her visual imagery of the opaque box with your fear inside it,
and putting into the mental closet where you can feel free to come
back to it when you want, is a great visualization that I will always
remember.
After reading and working through Jennifer’s personal story,
discussing her ideas, and making sure your students understand her
concepts and ideas have the class do a little research on the web
about Jennifer Cook O’Toole. Along with all the good advice Jennifer
has graciously given readers, she has made YouTube videos, written
many books, and has many stories she has written online about her
work.

---
## Page 580
Jennifer and her family
Section II: Daily Living
For this section you and those you teach, counsel, advise, and mentor
will be working with the acronym SHIFT (shopping, housekeeping,
individual finances, food preparation, and transportation). It’s an easy
way for your students to remember the important daily living skills they
will be learning as they learn to shop, clean and maintain their
apartments or dorm rooms, handle their finances, plan and prepare
nutritious meals, and figure out how to get to where they are going by
car or public transportation (if they do not drive), and to ask for help or
directions.
You’ll be starting with the S in SHIFT: shopping which should elicit
attention from your students as many of them do this really well in
connection with malls, things they want, their “special interests,” and
things they covet. They’ll be slightly or even majorly disappointed to

---
## Page 581
know that what you’ll be talking about is the shopping they need to do
in order to survive on their own: grocery shopping and shopping for
necessities such as clothing, shoes, outerwear, etc. And, that most
importantly, they will be learning how to do this in a logical and cost
effective way.
THE “S” IN SHIFT – SHOPPING
Explain to your students that the objective of the “shopping section” is
not to purchase the latest cell phone, DVD, or video game, but to teach
each of them to prepare a shopping list then purchase groceries,
household products, self-care products, and other necessary items
such as shoes, a coat, underwear, and other essential clothing.
Tell students that doing their own grocery shopping and shopping for
necessities is the way that others (their parents and those helping them
with independent living skills) will hold them accountable.
Tell students that grocery shopping and shopping for necessities must
be done in order to cope with independent living in a healthy and
necessary way.
Explain that initially they may ask others to assist them until they are
comfortable on their own.
Tell students that later on in this chapter they will learn about nutritious
meal planning that will add many ingredients to their basic shopping
list, but for now navigating stores, the cash register, and making
general lists will suffice.
The Grocery Shopping List
Tell students that using a list when shopping will ensure they have what
they need to prepare meals and feed themselves until the next trip to
the store is required. Having a list will also help them avoid impulse
buying and it will help them stick to their budget.

---
## Page 582
Explain that once all items on the list have been obtained, then, and
only then, can they splurge on things not on the list.
Tell students that grocery shopping using a list takes practice and that
one thing that will help them maintain control is to “never shop when
they are hungry.”
Explain that students will practice making a list of things that should be
on a weekly list and that they will add seasonal or specialty items to
their lists. They can, if they like, create a “wish list” at the end of it. If
they have money left from their budgets after buying the needed items
they can purchase “wish list” items.
Remind students to take a calculator along (or use an electronic one
on their phone or tablet) so they can add up items as they put them in
the cart.
The student handout on page 263 will get your students started. They
should practice making a list, taking a budgeted amount of money with
them, and then either alone or with peers, go shopping. This will be
their hands-on experiential “homework.”
Students will then report back (divide class into small groups) as to how
it went.
If available at your school, a social or life skills coach, or other support
professional can accompany small groups to the store for the
“homework” assignment. Or … you might consider making the
excursion a “class trip.”
Grocery Shopping and the Well Stocked “Fridge”
Benefit: You will never be hungry or out of laundry detergent
again!

---
## Page 583
Create a shopping list and keep this list on the refrigerator door
so that you can add to it as you and/or your roommates use up
the last of an item that’s needed (if you take the last of the milk,
eggs, yogurt, or bread; write it down!).
In addition to milk, eggs, bread, butter, yogurt, snacks, and
veggies (called staples) – be sure to include any ingredients that
you do not have in your pantry (the area where you store non-
perishable foods) that you will need to complete any recipes you
have pre-planned to cook during the week.
Write down any sundry items like cleaning products or personal
products (shampoo, soap, toiletries) that you need as well.
Here’s a sample list you can enlarge and photocopy. Do a little
research and find a list and format that you like and reproduce or
“create your own list” using a template from Microsoft.
Use whatever will work best for you and/or your housemates,
and remember that a list isn’t any good to you if it’s left on the
refrigerator or in your car. You’ll want to make sure your list is in
your hand when you enter the store.

---
## Page 584
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Grocery Shop Like a Pro!

---
## Page 585
Money
•
Take only the budgeted amount of money that you have
allotted for food each week with you to the store.
•
Withdraw funds using your debit card.
•
Use a calculator while shopping and buy only what is on your
list.
After you’ve gathered what you truly need – if you are below
budget – then and only then – do you add extra or non-essential
items. That way when you come to the end of the month you will
not be out of money for food during that last week.
By sticking to your shopping list, you will have what you need to
prepare planned meals and also have a variety of things to grab
from a shelf or the refrigerator when you are hungry.
Doing all these things will help you save on your weekly total and
you can use the saved money toward things not on your list or to
save for something else you might want to buy that is not food
related.
Shopping for other necessary items:
•
Make a list of what you need.
•
Look at your budget (the amount you can spend this week or
month), on necessary items like clothing, shoes, makeup,
underwear, etc., and comparison shop first.
•
Look for sales or the best value.
•
Withdraw budgeted funds from your debit card before
shopping and purchase only what is on your list.
If you come in under budget, you can put these funds toward
something that is not on your list or save it for buying another

---
## Page 586
item later on or … simply “save it.”
The “Grocery Shop like a Pro” handout has a lot of great advice,
strategies, facts, and tips about grocery shopping. Use this
handout to grocery shop with your classmates when you do your
sample “grocery shopping” assignment.
Restaurant vs. making own food:
Food purchases are one of the few budget items that are within
your control. Eating out has largely been accepted and
encouraged by society in the United States, but consider the
alternatives:
•
Pasta might cost $2–$3 vs. a mid-range Italian restaurant
where you might expect to pay $10–$15.
•
Sharing a meal at home with others is much more personal
and cost effective than dining out with friends.
•
Restaurants often maximize their profits by using lower
quality ingredients, whereas at home you know exactly what
you’re eating.
Here are Some Additional Things to
Consider When Shopping!
Nutritional value:
•
100% juices – higher vitamin and mineral content, but lack
fiber, and contain high amounts of natural sugar.
•
Shopping the perimeter – avoiding the middle, this is where
the soda aisle, chips, junk and snack food live!
•
Avoid excess sugars and artificial flavors and fillers.
Generally, the shorter the ingredient lists on the product the

---
## Page 587
healthier it is.
•
Opt for whole-grains and fiber when possible.
•
Buy “local” from farmers’ markets or directly from local farms
or farm stands. The food is fresher when in season and
costs less.
Preparation methods:
•
Processed vs. whole foods – opt for fresh foods whenever
possible. Processed foods can contain lots of preservatives,
sugars, fats, excess sodium, and are typically higher in
calories.
•
Shop specialty stores for gluten-free/casin-free/Kosher
items.
•
Vegetarian and vegan – available almost everywhere. Eating
a vegetarian diet can be fine; just make sure you are getting
the right amounts of proteins and fats from your alternatives.
Deals and specials:
•
Coupons – pick up a weekly store flyer on your way in. In-
store coupons can help you save substantially.
•
Generic vs. brand name? Generic versions of products often
contain the same tasting ingredients at a much more
affordable price.
•
Buying in bulk – ten cartons of yogurt for $3.99? What a
deal! What you didn’t know: Usually you don’t have to buy all
ten to get the sale price.
•
Buy one get one free. Things like loaves of bread and large
packages of meats can be frozen, so take advantage of
those buy one get one deals and stock your freezer!
Food labels:

---
## Page 588
•
Price per pound/oz. shelve sticker? This is an easy way to
determine the best price vs. quantity on nearly all items when
comparison shopping. This rounds the price up to the
nearest pound or oz. for easier comparisons.
•
Organic vs. conventional? Some items are more important to
buy organically than others. Try doing a web search for “the
dirty dozen organic foods” to see which items contain higher
amounts of dangerous pesticides.
•
GMO: There is a lot of disagreement about genetically
modified foods (GMO) and the potential health effects. Do
some research to determine if boycotting these products is
something you want to do.
Cost effective meals to make at home:
•
Oatmeal (add banana).
•
Spaghetti and marinara.
•
PB & J.
•
Grilled cheese and tomato soup.
•
Rice and beans.
•
Pancakes with fruit.
•
English muffin pizzas.
•
Burritos.
Here’s an enlightening activity:
Take one week and write down how much you spend eating out!
Keep a food log for your purchases you make while eating out.
Include all snacks, coffees, drinks, vending machine items, as
well as your meals at restaurants and all those expensive
popular latte and specialty drinks.

---
## Page 589
Are you shocked?
Feel free to track your “eating out expenses” on a free service
from www.mint.com if it is easier for you than writing it down on
this form!
Now, do the math:
•
Multiply the weekly total by 4 to get a monthly cost.
•
Multiply the weekly total by 52 to get a yearly cost.
Ask yourself:
•
How much can be saved or spent on other more important
items by not eating out or buying expensive coffee drinks?
•
How much would it cost you to make what you ate out in a
restaurant or cafe at home (such as a cup of coffee)?

---
## Page 590
Compare prices of generic vs. name brand
Use the following form to compare the prices of three common
products at your local grocer such as laundry detergent,
breakfast cereal, or facial tissues. Compare products that are
somewhat similar in size. In most major supermarkets you can
find an orange colored price label listing the price per ounce
(oz.). Use this as a quick reference for the best price in relation
to the quantity you are buying.

---
## Page 591
Are you surprised?
Discussion questions:
•
What did you learn?
•
How would regular purchases of brand name vs. generic
affect your budget?
•
What items do you feel compelled to buy brand name
products on?
•
Can you change your mind about this in order to save money
for something else you want?
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 592
THE “H” IN SHIFT: HOUSEKEEPING
Explain to students that keeping their living spaces clean is no different
from keeping their bodies clean. If they let dishes go, never dust or
vacuum, never wash a floor or clean a bathroom – their living
environment will be full of germs and bacteria and be unhealthy to live
in (especially if they are allergic to dust or have asthma). It will also
probably smell bad, look messy, uninviting, and/or appear disgusting to
others.
Tell students that this is the main reason that people who live
independently do basic housekeeping every week and deep cleaning
(refrigerators, stoves, carpets) on a regular basis. Keeping a living
space clean is part of becoming a responsible adult and a big part of
showing others that he or she can live independently.
Explain that when young adults take care of and take pride in their
living spaces, they take pride in themselves.
Explain that you’ll be helping them learn how to do this by giving each
person materials, ideas, strategies, and advice on how to create
cleaning routines and schedules, as well as basic instructions on how
to clean, how to do laundry, and how to keep their apartments or dorm
rooms organized and tidy.
Tell your students that this is yet another one of those non-negotiable
life skills (like hygiene and having good manners) that come with
independent living. And … that if they want to live free of parents and
others controlling them, they will need to exhibit this by having clean
and habitable living spaces.
Here’s a student handout to help your ASD or LD students start
acquiring the abilities they will need to “clean house.” This handout, like
Jennifer Cook O’Toole’s handout on anxiety, can be made into a folder
or notebook that students can keep handy and refer to as they set up
their first apartments, dorm rooms, or shared living space
environments.

---
## Page 593
I Don’t Want To … But I Will Anyway
Before getting your first apartment or dorm room you probably
lived with your parents. Your mother probably took care of a
large portion of the housekeeping and your laundry. If you
needed a clean towel, the linen closet was full of them. If you left
laundry on the floor, it was magically picked up and returned to
you clean and folded. You rarely washed dishes, used a vacuum
cleaner, mopped a floor, or worse … deep cleaned anything, like
a refrigerator, bathroom, or bedroom.
Living on your own can be stressful in and of itself and making
sure your apartment, shared living spaces, or dorm room is
clean and habitable should be one less thing you have to worry
about. Setting up a schedule for keeping your living spaces
clean and tidy and the dreaded “deep cleaning” will solve this
problem. And … if your mom, teacher, tutor, or anyone who is
supporting your independence pays a surprise visit … you’re
covered!
Why clean?

---
## Page 594
The reason you will want to keep your living environment clean
is very basic and easy to understand. A clean house is
necessary if you want to stay healthy. If your living spaces are
clean (especially kitchens and bathrooms) you will not get sick
or contract and spread diseases to others.
Living independently on your own or with others (roommates,
girl or boyfriends, or spouses) requires you to maintain certain
hygienic standards. A clean environment, besides being a
pleasure to live in, is a healthy environment. Your home will also
stay free of roaches, rodents, ants, flies, and unsanitary
conditions (dirt and grime everywhere) which can make you and
anyone who comes into your home sick.
Keeping your living environment clean is just like your daily
hygiene routines; it is non-negotiable. So let’s get started on how
you (and any roommates you have) will establish a plan, then a
routine, and learn how to “clean house.”
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit
Keeping Living Spaces Clean – Housekeeping
101
Always Have a Cleaning Checklist and a Plan
of Attack!

---
## Page 595
•
Know which rooms you plan to start and finish cleaning in
(generally back door to front door works best). This will help
expedite the process and ensure you are not duplicating your
efforts, especially if more than one person is involved in the
cleaning.
•
Attempt to delegate tasks. If you live with other people,
cleaning the house should not be exclusively your
responsibility! You may have to take on a leadership role by
setting up a rotational cleaning schedule, but it’s far better
than having to do this backbreaking work alone. It should
also be equal – cleaning the toilet is hardly on par with
organizing the coffee table.
•
Make a plan so that you can vacuum, sweep, and mop all
rooms together (and so that your hot water doesn’t get cold)
and you don’t have to go from one task to another. For
example broom sweep all the floors in the apartment, then
vacuum rugs, and then mop all the floors.
•
And remember: Keeping your house clean is about as easy
as making it messy. As you and those you live with walk
around the house, apartment, or dorm room … if you step
over, trip over, or pass something out of place, pick it up and
put it where it belongs. Every time.
HOW TO CLEAN AND WHAT TO CLEAN
FIRST – A TASK LIST
This list of tasks can serve as a general all-purpose checklist for
weekly cleaning. It does not have to be completed in this order
but this list puts the most unpleasant tasks first.
The Bathroom

---
## Page 596
Clean the toilet. Ugh! Cleaning the toilet is one of the worst
tasks, so it’s best to get it over with first – this is how you do this.
•
Throw on a pair of rubber gloves (not the ones for the
dishes) to keep your hands away from the grime and
bacteria, and give the toilet a gentle rub down with a sponge
and really hot water to loosen everything up. Let it soak in
the hot water while you move onto the toilet bowl.
•
Squirt toilet cleaner into the inside of the bowl and around
the rim. Let it soak for a minute and then scrub it with a toilet
brush. When you’re finished, give it a flush.
•
When you’re done with the toilet bowl, it’s time to wipe down
all the outside surfaces. Spray them down with a disinfectant
spray first and wipe dry with a cloth or a paper towel.
Clean the shower or tub. Showers are notorious for getting
grimy quickly.
•
A shower cleaner and bristled brush (and some muscle
power) should work great. Use a spray or foam type of
antibacterial cleaner.
•
Use car wax on showers to make it stay cleaner longer (not
the floor; you might slip).
•
For cleaning glass shower doors, use a half cup of ammonia
and 8 drops of dish soap in a gallon of water. Dry with a
paper towel.
Clean the sink. Most sinks are pretty heavy duty, but before
you use any household cleaner, make sure it’s appropriate for
your surface. Once you’re sure:
•
Spray the sink with cleaner. Leave it for a minute to start
eating away the bacteria and mildew and then scrub it down
with some elbow grease and a sturdy sponge.
•
When it seems to be sparkly and fresh, rinse it out with warm
water and wipe dry with a clean cloth or paper towel.

---
## Page 597
•
If you have some stubborn stains, you may need to use a
brush to dig into them and loosen them. A wire-bristled brush
(like one you use to clean your shower) is good.
Clean the glass/mirrors. It is commonly thought that glass
cleaner does the cleaning. In fact, it’s meant just to make it
sparkly, not to get it clean. It’s actually no substitute for soapy
water, especially if your mirror is quite dirty. Here’s how you
should clean your windows and mirrors:
•
First, wash your glass with a solution of warm or hot water
and dish soap with a rag, sponge, or squeegee. A powdered
no-scratch cleaner works fantastically for cleaning mirrors,
glass, ceramics, and metals because it scrubs off hard water
residue without scratching the surface.
•
Then wipe down the surface with a dry, lint-free cloth, or lint-
free paper towels.
•
If you want to clean glass and be environmentally friendly,
clean the glass with vinegar and water, dry with a lint-free
cloth, and rub the glass with newspaper. No streaks! Make
sure you use some elbow grease – it requires pressure to
properly clean glass.
•
Alternatively, spray glass cleaner on a paper towel and clean
the glass surface. The glass cleaner acts as a shield to help
keep spots away and make the dust easy to remove. When
misused, glass “cleaner” will leave streaks. You can also use
old newspapers to wipe the glass surface after washing; the
glass will be streak free and this is a great way to reuse old
newspaper.
The Kitchen
Using the dishwasher:

---
## Page 598
Your dishwasher works best when you load it completely and
run it right after you load in your dishes.
•
Big things like pots and pans are usually best hand-washed
because they don’t fit well in the dishwasher.
•
Dishes wear out more quickly when washed by machine than
by hand because the dishwasher’s soap is abrasive; china,
delicate wine glasses, and other really fragile things should
be carefully hand-washed instead.
Hand-washing dishes: It’s much easier to clean your dishes if
you wash them right after you use them. You will rarely need to
soak anything or scrub hard because the food and food juices
won’t have a chance to dry and harden.
•
Wet your sponge or scrubber brush with hot water, squirt on
some dish soap, wipe each dish (both sides), and rinse
thoroughly with hot water.
•
If you need to soak a casserole that has baked-on gunk for
10 or 15 minutes, that’s ok, but when you can, it’s usually
better to just wash the dishes promptly and avoid soaking.
•
Be sure to let your brush, sponge, and dish towel dry
between uses to keep them from accumulating germs, too.
Cleaning the microwave and the oven: Clean these at least
once a month or more often if you use them daily.
•
For the oven, check if there’s a self-cleaning setting. That
could do the brunt of the work for you. If you self-clean your
oven you’ll just need to wipe it down with a damp sponge
afterward.
•
If it doesn’t have a self-cleaning option, remove the racks to
soak in soapy water, spray down the insides of the oven with
a cleaning solution, let it sit, and then get to work with a
sponge and a scraper.

---
## Page 599
•
As for the microwave, you can use a bowl of vinegar, lemon
and water, dish soap, or window cleaner. Just place it in the
microwave, turn the microwave on for a few minutes, and
then wipe it down with a rag. All that crusted on gunk will
come off easily, leaving your microwave looking like new.
•
Microwave tip: Place a paper towel over your food to avoid
spattering – this will keep your microwave cleaner and save
you time!
Cleaning the kitchen sink: Just for the record, these
instructions can be found in the bathroom section. Sinks are
sinks, after all.
Organizing the kitchen cabinets:
•
This part is actually up to you and what makes sense to you;
just make sure you can come up with a system that is fairly
intuitive and sensible.
•
Sometimes it’s just easier to take everything back out and
then put everything back in, rather than trying to work around
everything and see what you can make work.
•
Put your bowls with your bowls, your nice glasses with your
nice glasses, and your baking supplies with your baking
supplies – you get the idea.
Organize all the clutter in all the other rooms!
•
Again, we’re starting from the top to down here. The first
step to organizing any room is to get rid of or organize all the
small bits. This means those papers you have thrown about,
the clothes on the floor, the wrappers next to your bed, and
the empty drink bottles. Get rid of ’em. Then you can really
start to get to work.
Another great tip!

---
## Page 600
•
As you are cleaning, walk around the house with a garbage
bag and a laundry bag. This way you can pick up as you go
and won’t have to walk back and forth.
Then … dust, vacuum or mop, and spray a nice scent into
the room. All those shelves and nooks and crannies (not to
mention under and behind the bed) are paradise for dust and
dander. To get at them:
•
Using a dust mitt or dust cloth works wonders, but you could
also use dusting spray or a disinfecting wipe to get the job
done. Once everything is dusted, corners of the floor
included, you can vacuum or mop accordingly.
•
Be gentle with certain pieces, like your lampshades and
curtains. For these, you can take a blow dryer to them and
gently blast away the dust.
•
When you’re done, top off all your hard work with a nice,
fresh scent, like lemon or lavender. Either a room spray or
scented candle will give your room a nice smell and be a
soothing touch.
Be sure to clean all the floors. This all depends on what kind
of floors you have: wood, ceramic, linoleum, and carpet all
require different methods of cleaning. Determine what method is
appropriate for your floor.
Vacuuming is the most effective way to clean up all the dust and
other loose junk that accumulates on your floor when you have
carpeting or throw/area rugs (and it’s practically a daily
necessity when you have shedding pets).
Alternatively, try using a dry dust mop (the microfiber kind works
well), if you have tile/wood floors. You can use a carpet sweeper
(a non-electric machine you push that has brushes underneath),
if you have carpet. Both involve less fuss than getting out the

---
## Page 601
vacuum and will let you go longer between more thorough
vacuuming sessions.
Mopping floors. Some of the new and innovative substitutes
for mopping can be helpful, but they are still no substitute for
a good rag mop for getting rid of glued-on grime. In your
kitchen and other areas, this is a necessity for getting your
floors looking sparkling clean. When it comes to tile or
textured floors, nothing else will get the dirt out of the cracks
and depressions.
Dust the furniture. Teeny invisible dust mites are everywhere,
and if we could see them, we would be dusting 24/7. They can
be found just about anywhere in the home and can even cause
sneezing, coughing, and asthma.In addition to dusting,
vacuuming and mopping help, too. To dust your furniture:
•
Take a damp cloth or dusting mitt and carry it over your
surfaces. Go in a continual motion on every surface so you
don’t miss any spots, and work in one direction around the
room. You may want to use a scented furniture spray like
Pledge to help you out.
•
Polish the wood furniture. Like glass cleaner, furniture polish
isn’t meant to be a cleaner. However, it can be used for
cleaning. If you’d like to use furniture polish, make sure to
carefully read the labels and find one made specifically for
what you need.
•
Some furniture can handle water, and those that can should
be used with the soapy solution from step two. Be sure to
quickly dry off these surfaces.
•
Next, apply the suggested amount of furniture polish and
wipe away according to the directions. This will keep dust
from magnetizing to your furniture.

---
## Page 602
Wrapping Up Your Cleaning Session
Do the laundry using your washing machine and washing
delicate things by hand. That pile of dirty clothes on your
bedroom floor has to go somewhere, and that place is the
washing machine. The following are generic instructions for
using a washing machine:
•
First, begin the water at the needed temperature and level.
•
Next, pour in the needed amount of laundry detergent where
the water is pouring.
•
If you use fabric softener, a great thing to use is the fabric
softener balls that you pour your softener into and just throw
in at the beginning. (This saves you from having to wait for
the rinse cycle.) If you use one, do this with the detergent. If
you put your clothes in first, you risk staining your clothes
with the coloring in these liquids.
Dry your laundry. The way you transfer your clothes into your
dryer can affect the way they come out.
•
Once the entire washing cycle is complete, shake to remove
the twisting of fabric and the heavy wrinkles from the clothes
and then toss them into your dryer. This process can help
prevent wrinkling and helps your clothes dry more efficiently.
•
It also helps to empty the dryer while your clothes are still
warm. Not to mention the awesome feeling you get from
holding a pile of still warm, extra clean clothes.
Walk through your house and complete any remaining
room-specific cleaning necessary. Though this was a pretty
big list, it was by no means exhaustive. Other tasks include:
•
Emptying trash bins and taking out the trash.
•
Cleaning the kitchen table.

---
## Page 603
•
Changing bed sheets, pillowcases, and duvet covers.
•
Cleaning walls (as needed).
•
Cleaning the refrigerator (as needed).
And … Some Final Suggestions!
•
Have cleanliness in mind from the second you start setting
up your own independent living space.
•
When unpacking and putting things away, make it so
everything has a designated spot – a place for everything
and everything in its place. This will help you keep organized
if everything you bring into your new apartment, home, or
dorm room belongs somewhere.
•
Make your bed every day. It doesn’t have to be perfect, but it
will go a long way towards making your bedroom look
cleaner and gives you a nice morning routine. As long as the
sheets and the blankets cover up a major portion of the bed,
you’ll be fine.
•
Make sure you have enough storage bins and shelves for all
your “stuff.”
•
Spend ten minutes a day tidying up. Every … Single … Day!
It’s difficult to make it into a routine, but once you do, it’s SO
much easier than spending a couple of hours or more, once
a week. Honestly, the best thing is to just get into a routine of
putting stuff back in a set period during the day. It doesn’t
take more than a couple minutes to un-clutter the past 24
hours, but it takes a lot longer if you wait a month or year or
whatever. Ten minutes. Every day.

---
## Page 604
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
THE “I” IN SHIFT: INDEPENDENT FINANCES
Skills to Pay the Bills Handling Finances
Most of the young men and women you work with have had some
experience with managing their own money. However, when they go off
to school or leave home for a job and then move into their own
apartment, it may be the first time that they are handling money on a
daily basis. Before financial independence can happen, teachers,
support professionals, counselors, or advisors will need to sit down with
their students and show them how to create a realistic budget.
Being able to handle money is necessary. In order for your students to
become self-reliant with day-to-day expenses and to have the self-
confidence necessary to make appropriate money decisions, they need
to understand the role that money plays in each and every day.

---
## Page 605
The I (independent finances) in SHIFT will help you teach your ASD
and LD students how to budget, manage money, and retain funds.
Being fiscally aware will lead your emerging adult students on a path
that leads to making wise financial decisions and that in turn leads to
real-life skills for living independently and managing money responsibly.
There seems to be an ATM machine on every corner. Young adults on
the ASD or LD Spectrum often have difficulty being accountable for
their money. The easy access to cash that ATMs provide, means that
cash is available 24 hours a day. Some students, when faced with a
financial problem, will say, “It’s alright, I will just go to the ATM
machine!” For students with an on the Spectrum diagnosis, this
temptation will be exacerbated by financial naïveté.
Explain something as simple as taking out a fixed amount of money at
the beginning of each week and allotting that money wisely, until the
following weekly withdrawal allows them to use the ATM with discretion.
By learning to budget, your young students learn to be realistic about
meeting their daily needs and expenses in a proactive way.
The traits that accompany an ASD or LD diagnosis often lead to
repetitive 
and 
rigid 
thought 
patterns. 
These 
patterns 
are
counterproductive when it comes to money management. The
challenge will be teaching the young adults you work with to become
flexible and open to mastering the skills needed for learning to handle
money.
Learning to Budget
Tell your students that learning to budget and handle their day-to-day
expenses is a lot like learning any other subject in school. They start
out with small amounts of information (usually given by their teacher)
then when they have absorbed that knowledge, they are given a bit
more, then a bit more, until they have overcome the learning challenge
and absorbed the thought process of what is being taught.

---
## Page 606
Tell your students that you will be using this same formula to teach
each person how to handle his or her finances.
Explain that learning to create a budget will be done on a step-by-step
basis and that you will be working with the class until each person
understands the concept and how to create a basic budget.
Tell your students that they will be working on a realistic budget using
their actual expenses if they know them, or by using a realistic amount
of “pretend” money, if they do not.
Tell students they’ll also be learning about wants and needs and about
the common problem that often surfaces when young adults on the
ASD or LD Spectrum start living independently: financing their “special
interests.”
Explain that sometimes students living on their own for the first time will
become obsessed with buying things that are related to their area of
“special interest” and that you will be showing them how to deal with
this issue as well.
Explain that other financial difficulties occur when students are not able
to utilize common sense. Because of the way ASD and LD brains work,
sometimes using common sense is an abstract concept and one that is
difficult to understand.
When amounts spent exceed amounts budgeted, it’s usually because
students are buying things that are beyond their spending means. And
they may or may not understand exactly what this means. But the good
news is you’ll be helping them with this as well.
Explain that some students on the Spectrum will take saving to an
extreme. These young men and women will save all their money and
refuse to spend any of it on themselves. This might mean they wear the
same clothes until they fall apart; they also might forgo activities with
classmates or classmates, or stay home when others are eating out,
because they do not want to spend any money.
Explain that other students will hoard money and that you will be
helping each person in the class to find a balance between over

---
## Page 607
spending and under spending and how to avoid spending extremes and
saving extremes.
The student handout below contains a story written by Matthew
Kosiorek from CIP’s Berkshire Center. In this particular story Matt was
working with a young man who was 17 years old and attending a two-
week summer program designed to help teenagers in 10th, 11th, and
12th grade learn independent living skills and meet specific goals set
by each student before coming to the program.
Matt’s story is one that some (if not all) of your students might identify
with. It will also show the young men and women you work with that
budgeting can be a hard concept to master (especially if they are prone
to impulse buying).
But the best part of the story is that it illustrates how making real-time
and real-place mistakes and errors while handling money can be part
of the solution in helping an individual understand the value of learning
to handle his or her finances.
Divide students into small groups and hand out copies of the story.
Have one person read the story to the group. Have groups talk about
the scenario. Circulate among the groups, listen in, and ask your
students if they can: (a) relate to the story, (b) what other solutions
might help a student like the one in the story, (c) how do they think their
parents would react if their son or daughter called and related this same
story to them, and (d) was it a good idea for Matt to approach the whole
situation as teachable moment instead of the failure the student
thought it was? How was Matt’s reaction good and/or valuable for the
student?

---
## Page 608
“My Mom Is Going to Kill Me!”
It was the first day of our summer program for adolescents and
young adults on the ASD and LD Spectrum. Students and
parents had been anticipating this day for many weeks and I
knew the group of students I was about to meet were anxious
but also excited about adventures to come.
This particular group had adolescents and young adults who
would be entering 10th, 11th, and 12th grade from “all over the
world” in it. Most of these young men and women were leaving
home for the very first time to spend two weeks learning
independent living skills. I myself was traveling 2875 miles to
work with these young adults and just as nervous about the
“unknown ahead.”
After arriving and settling into their dorm rooms … it was time for
goodbyes. As students started to relax and get to know each
other and I started to get to know them a bit better as well, it
looked like everything was going to fall into place and be a
rewarding two-week experience. The group was connecting and
everything looked great.
I asked the students if anyone forgot anything as a shopping trip
is always planned for the first night at each summer session
since many students forget needed items, including me.
Although we all followed the provided packing list of
recommended things, I knew from experience that it’s human
nature to forget at least one thing. I then asked if anyone needed
to go to the store and three students raised their hands. A young
man named Alex was one of them.

---
## Page 609
I remembered Alex from the earlier admissions process for this
summer camp program and I even remembered the three goals
he wrote down on his application as he anticipated participating
in the program. One of his goals was that he wanted to work on
“money management” and “impulse buying.”
Before we left the dorms we sat down and made lists of the
items that needed to be purchased. With debit cards in hand, we
were off on our very first summer adventure. As an educator, I
look at every activity as
an adventure with the added (and usually realistic) possibility of
a beneficial learning experience thrown in to boot, as “you never
know what to expect or can predict what will happen.”
At the summer program we do the buddy system, meaning you
have to be with another student or staff member at all times.
When we arrived at the store, we picked our “hot spot” (the
place we’d all meet after shopping) then rushed off (in pairs) to
find the items on our lists.
Once my group completed their purchases we went over to the
“hot spot” and waited for the rest. A few minutes later I saw Alex
struggling with his purchases as he was attempting to complete
his transaction at the register. He became anxious as the card
he was using was not working.
By then the line he was in was starting to grow, and I knew by
looking at Alex that he was beginning to “lose control” over the
situation. I decided to go over and see what was happening. He
was trying a second card just as I reached him.
Alex’s bill was $140 and I knew that this couldn’t be right as I
had looked at his list before we left for the store. I asked (in a
non-threatening way) about the $100 gift card to iTunes that he
was trying to purchase. He said he needed it.

---
## Page 610
I chose not to say anything at the time as he was already upset
and waited patiently for Alex (whose second card was also
declined) to complete his purchase. Reaching into his wallet he
proceeded to use all the cash that his parents had given him as
“extra money” in case he needed it, to pay for his purchases.
Alex grabbed his bags in distress and rushed over to the group.
Remembering that money management and impulse buying was
a problem for Alex I decided to sit with him later on and talk
about this experience.
In the car ride back to his dorm room, Alex seemed very
overwhelmed and I could tell he was upset. I myself was actually
feeling “a little” excited about the whole situation. Why? I knew,
just knew, deep down inside of me, that this was going to end up
being a very good, memorable, and poignant “teachable
moment” for Alex.
I went up to his room a few minutes after arriving back to the
campus. It was clear that Alex was disappointed in himself and
this was also indicated by the look on his face and his dejected
posture.
I sat down with Alex and we talked about the chain of events
that led to what had happened at the store. I asked Alex if there
was something else (besides a shopping list) that we could have
done to avoid his impulse buy.
Alex agreed that creating a list was a good idea; however, he did
not stick to it. We then talked about making an agreement
wherein Alex would verbally (and in writing) state to me that he
would “stick to his shopping list” for the next two weeks and only
buy what he had written on the list. And that the things on the list
would be only what he really needed to have during the two-
week camp session.

---
## Page 611
Having the agreement and the list would serve as a dual visual
reminder. And, I felt that with practice, prompting, and
reminders, Alex would be able to comply. We then came up with
a plan on how Alex would fix the immediate problem, since he
was now “tapped out” on day one.
After a lot of discussion, we both agreed that it would be best to
return the gift card. Looking at the activity schedule we were
able to choose an appropriate time to bring the gift card back to
the store. Alex then asked if I was going to tell his mom about his
mistake.
I told him that I thought this was a great learning experience for
him. I also told him that my “gut instinct” was that his mom
would actually be very proud of the fact that he took the
necessary steps to fix the problem with the guidance of staff.
Alex was still of the opinion that his mom would “kill him.”
Alex was able to return the gift card a few days later and with
some prompting from me, he was able to tell his mom what had
happened. She did not “kill” Alex. His mom realized as I had,
that it was actually a good thing that Alex was able to “fail” early
in the program and then have the support needed to come up
with a plan on to fix the problem.
I was then able to work with Alex during other shopping trips “on
his problem” and was pleased that he made positive progress
towards achieving one of his summer goals: learning to control
impulse buying!
Matthew Kosiorek, CIP Berkshire
Understanding What Budgeting Means
(Needs vs. Wants)

---
## Page 612
Explain to your students that it’s now time to get started on mastering
the concepts of budgeting then creating one.
The student handout on page 277 can be used as a lesson plan to help
students to further understand the concept of budgeting “and living
within their means.” The “Top Five Financial Tips” handout will be very
useful to the young men and women you work with as you explain the
why, what, when, and how of budgeting.
Now it’s time to have students take a realistic look at what their
individual (and realistic) needs and (possibly unrealistic) wants are. The
student handout on page 278 will help you help your students get
started.
List the “Needs” and “Wants” headings on a white board. Have
students brainstorm things that they think should go in each category.
Then have students fill out their own lists in as realistic and reality
based a way as possible.

---
## Page 613
Explain that the things they write down will help them determine what
items will go into their own individual budgets and that this is the first
step in creating one.
Creating a Budget
Explain to students that it’s now time to try to create a realistic monthly
budget.
Explain that budgets can be forecasted in weekly, monthly, and yearly
increments. However, for the purposes of this unit, students will be
creating a realistic monthly budget either based on facts that they know
or facts that are supplied to them.
Explain to students that if they are living on their own, they will use
“real time and place” amounts. If not, they will the suggested amounts
that are listed on the worksheet.
Tell students that there are hundreds of templates online that they can
use once they are budgeting on their own. There are also many
spreadsheets and websites that they can consult to develop one that
will work for them. However, for your class, they will be using the one
on the handout for this purpose.
Top Five Financial Tips
1. Be realistic!
•
“Time” spent developing a budget is “time” well spent.
•
A common error people make when they’re planning their
budget is to list unrealistic dollar amounts. If you spend $500
at the grocery store each month, then it isn’t reasonable to
list $300 in your budget.

---
## Page 614
•
Keep a spending journal for at least two weeks prior to
creating a budget for yourself. This will help you establish
realistic numbers. A comprehensive budget will not only tell
you where the money is going, it can give you a “road map”
to tightening expenses. It will also allow you to put more
money away (save) for your short-term and long-term goals.
2. Know the difference between luxuries and necessities
(needs and wants).
•
Knowing the difference between a “want” and a “need” can
help you save money.
•
Many of the items we spend money on are things we want. If
you don’t have to “have it in order to survive,” then it is a
“want.” If the item doesn’t fit comfortably into your budget,
you need to set it aside until your budget is ready to handle
the purchase.
3. Don’t count on your next bonus or your next pay raise!
•
Until the money is in your account, don’t spend it. It’s not
wise to budget money that “may be coming to you.”
•
Focusing on your present financial state will help you reach
your financial goals in a more realistic fashion.
4. Keep control of your money.
•
You earned it, so you should know how it is being spent.
When you let someone else control your money, you are
putting yourself at risk.
•
If you are single, and you have employed someone to help
you manage your money, you should know what the
accountant or financial consultant is doing with your money.
Your involvement will help negate any questionable activity
that could have a negative impact on your future finances.

---
## Page 615
5. Think before acting!
•
Make wise buying decisions. The experts tell us that a
consumer’s spending decisions are made “5% by looking at
what an item costs and determining if the purchase is
necessary or needed and 95% by his or her emotions.”
•
It is critical to make decisions based upon “need” and not
just by “what you want.” Here’s an example: When you sign
a loan or a lease, you are locked into a payment that may not
give you the room needed for financial emergencies like
illnesses, auto repairs, or other unexpected expenses.
•
To avoid over-extending yourself, think before you act.
Before you “sign on the dotted line” for a large ticket item,
such as a car, you should examine your budget to see if this
purchase will be “a good fit.”
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Wants vs. Needs Worksheet
Knowing the difference between “wants” and “needs” is an
important part of learning to manage money, especially in tough
economic times.
It’s easy to spend money. What’s not so easy is spending
money wisely. One way to help you spend wisely is to separate
your “needs” from your “wants,” and then learn to budget your
money primarily on your “needs.”
“Wants” are things that you would like to have but are not
essential, such as eating out, going to movies, or getting the
newest cell phone and ring tones.

---
## Page 616
“Needs” are the essentials; the basics of life that you must have
in order to survive: Food, housing, clothing. Some expenses that
relate to your job (that is, your ability to pay for the basics) also
are “needs,” such as transportation to and from work and health
care coverage to keep you well enough to go to work.
Before you buy something, ask yourself, “Do I ‘need’ this item,
or do I just ‘want’ it?” You will probably be surprised at how
many things are actually “wants.”
List some of your “needs” in the spaces below. Try to think of
“needs” that are immediate, as well as things you’ll “need” in the
next several months.
“MY WANTS”
“MY NEEDS”
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
_____________________
Are you starting to see the difference? Are there “wants” that
you could do without?
Now take your two lists and:

---
## Page 617
1. Decide if you really “need” or just “want” everything on the
list. Are some “needs” actually “wants?” Cross off the
“wants” that are the least important to you.
2. Put stars next to the “needs” that are particularly important to
you.
3. Decide if each item makes sense. You may “need” a car to
get to and from school or work, but you may “want” a brand-
new sports car. You can meet this “need” by buying a fuel-
efficient used car rather than a sports car.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
My Monthly Budget
Here’s a step-by-step guide to preparing a
budget.
Getting Started!
•
Use this worksheet to forecast how much money you will
spend in a month’s time.
•
Some bills are monthly and some come less often.
•
If you have an expense that does not occur every month, put
it in the “Other expenses this month” category.
Here is a sample monthly budget with some amounts you can
work with if you are not yet living on your own! If you are living
on your own take the blank budget form and fill it in as
realistically as you can, then use it (for real) next month and see

how you do. Make minor adjustments if needed, and then use
this form (or any online template) to track your money every
month!

---
## Page 619
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Being Generous and Being Careful!
Now that your students have a realistic idea of how budgeting and
accounting for their money works, it’s time to broach a subject that
affects many adolescents and young adults on the ASD and LD
Spectrum: Being generous, and the second part of the equation …
being careful.

---
## Page 620
Explain to your students that sometimes people on the ASD or LD
Spectrum want to fit in so badly with their peers that they will often try to
win friends by giving gifts or buying things for others that they cannot
afford.
Tell students that people on the ASD or LD Spectrum can sometimes
be perceived as vulnerable or an easy target by those not on the
Spectrum and on occasion, will be asked to lend others money.
Explain that because of the way an ASD or LD brain is wired it’s also
easy for an individual on the Spectrum to misread a situation and use
gifts, or the lending of money, to win and influence friends.
Talk about this in several contexts with your students, so that they
understand that they can be generous but need to be careful.
Explain that they also need to able to “read” the social cues of others,
so that gifts they give to others are warranted (for a specific) occasion,
appropriate, and above all, wanted.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: The story on page 282, “Misunderstandings –
Jeff,” adapted from my first book “Made for Good Purpose …”
illustrates how “not reading a situation correctly” and “desperately
wanting to be liked” led a student named “Jeff” to totally misread
social cues and give inappropriate and unwanted gifts.
You can help your students learn from Jeff’s story by discussing the
scenario from the story in class and explaining that this is an example
of a situation where not “reading” a situation in a realistic way caused
confusion and misunderstandings for everyone involved.
Financing “Special Interests”
Explain to your students that finding the funds to support their “special
interests” is another common problem that comes up for ASD and LD
young adults as they start living independently.
Explain that some young people on the Spectrum become obsessed
with buying things that are related to their area of “special interest.”

---
## Page 621
Spending money that was allotted for food or rent on funding a “special
interest” can wreak havoc with a budget.
Tell students that awareness is the first step in gaining control of any
excessive spending in this area and that oftentimes financial difficulties
occur when students are not able to utilize common sense.
Share Chris’s story with your students. His story (also adapted from
Made for Good Purpose…) is a good example that illustrates how Chris
conquered spending nearly all of his allotted and budgeted money on
his particular area of “special interest.”
Have students read the story on page 283 in small groups and discuss
it. Ask students if they can relate to Chris’s and Jeff’s problems and
how they might use the strategies and advice in the story if “special
interest” spending is an issue in their lives.
“Misunderstandings – Jeff”
Jeff attended a two-year college in Buffalo, New York. At the
start of the spring semester, he became friendly with two of the
young women who were in his English class. Jeff would talk to
them briefly each day saying hello or occasionally talking about
assignments after class.
When February rolled around Jeff knew that Valentine’s Day
would soon be here and he thought that it would be a good idea
if he bought two large heart shaped boxes of candy for them and
spent quite a bit of money in doing so.
He took these Valentine’s Day presents to his English class.
After class, he walked up to the two unsuspecting girls who had
been somewhat friendly to him, and gave them the candy boxes.
Jeff had totally misconstrued the relationship he thought he had

---
## Page 622
formed with them. In his head, he had imagined they were his
girlfriends and because they paid attention to him during and
after class, he felt close to them. Both young women were
surprised and did not know how to respond to these lavish,
inappropriate gifts. They hardly knew who he was. They were
very kind to Jeff and then tried to give the boxes back to him, but
he insisted they take them. It was awkward for the girls and Jeff
simply did not understand what had taken place.
Jeff had totally misunderstood the social cues and was ascribing
feelings to the situation that simply did not exist except in his
mind. He misinterpreted their nonverbal behavior toward him
when they smiled and were kind to him.
Luckily for Jeff, another student observed this exchange and
mentioned to Jeff’s social skills instructor that Jeff seemed
perplexed by the girls not wanting his gifts and that his feelings
were hurt. He talked with Jeff later that day about why giving
such lavish gifts to casual acquaintances was an inappropriate
gesture.
Jeff had wanted so much to win their approval and did not
understand why his expensive and lavish gifts were rejected. To
his way of thinking, he had built a relationship with them when in
reality he hardly knew them.
The social skills instructor helped Jeff understand that his
perceptions of the situation that led to buying the gifts were
incorrect. And that he needed to work on learning to differentiate
fact and reality from imagination.
Knowing that this would be hard for him, the social skills
instructor asked the staff at Jeff’s school to become supportive
of his efforts, and little by little, Jeff started to “get it.” He learned
through practice how to take perspective and “check in” with
others about his thoughts and feelings before acting upon them.

---
## Page 623
With support, Jeff learned how to read situations better to avoid
misunderstandings.

---
## Page 624
“Chris”
Chris, a student from Toronto, Canada, was obsessed with
“video gaming.” He owned the latest version of every video
game and game player on the market. Chris was so involved in
his “special interest” that he monitored websites for new
releases and would purchase these products the minute they hit
the market. He had several bookcases filled with his prized
possessions.
This behavior in a 14-year-old would be understandable.
Parents would monitor a child of this age and set limits on
spending. Chris’s problem was that he was 22 years old and
trying to master the skills necessary for independent living,
including money management.
Chris used gaming and buying video games as a substitute for
“taking action” and moving forward in the academic, career, and
social areas of his life. The people who were helping Chris (his
tutor, his Career Coordinator, and his therapist) knew that Chris
would need to acknowledge this and then be willing to look at
alternatives and take offered suggestions to curb his behavior.
When approached by staff Chris was initially unwilling to put
gaming into balance with the other areas of his life. When
Chris’s therapist talked to his parents, they told her that they
wanted to support Chris in what seemed to be the one and only
area of his life that he enjoyed. Their view was that if you took
gaming away from Chris, he would not have anything left in his
life.

---
## Page 625
This was not the intention of Chris’s therapist, or the tutor and
Career Coordinator who were working with him. They explained
to Chris’s parents that they were looking to help Chris achieve a
balance that would put his finances in order, allow for gaming,
and also open up opportunities to move in new directions.
Achieving this balance with Chris took some time. His therapist
worked really hard with him to come up with a compromise that
Chris, his parents, and the rest of the school’s staff would
approve.
With input from staff, Chris’s parents put a limit on his spending.
Chris knew that he could not make a new purchase until all his
monthly bills were paid. Having this new structure allowed Chris
the time to work on other areas of his life.
In time, he gained the emotional balance needed to start
socializing with others. Once he did this, other interests came
into his life. He started forging friendships with his peers (some
were interested in “gaming” and others were outside the
“gaming world”).
This was a delicate, well planned, and well thought out process
for the staff members who were involved with Chris. They did not
want to become surrogate parents and Chris’s parents did not
want to seem punitive or controlling. Chris needed to see for
himself that he needed to make changes and then he had to
want to change.
With compassion and full staff support Chris was able to align
his spending and his “special interest” with making friends and
to his surprise, discovering he was interested in hiking,
skateboarding, and going out on Saturday nights for a movie
and a pizza.

---
