## Page 626
Credit Cards
Tell students that another tricky area in managing their money is using
a credit card or cards.
Explain that until they are staying within budgets and handling money
responsibly, young adults on the ASD or LD Spectrum should start off
with credit card limits.
Tell students that they should think about starting off with a maximum
$500 spending limit and learning how to spend this money responsibly
(by paying off the balance each month). This will ensure that they are
not taking on more debt than they can handle.
Explain that it’s really important to ignore and not respond to any
credit card offers that appear in their apartment complex or college
campus mailboxes. Credit card companies target students and young
people entering the workforce.
Tell students about credit card hygiene and that this means:
•
Not lending credit cards to roommates or friends.
•
Shredding credit card statements that have their credit card
numbers on them.
•
Being vigilant about where the card is (i.e., in a wallet, desk, or
backpack) at all times.
•
Not going over your limit … ever!
Explain that the purpose of a credit card is to build credit and that there
is danger in obtaining a card that has a high interest rate. High interest
rates can make repayment really difficult. Credit scores can be lowered
or plummet if payments are late or skipped.
The handout on page 84 is a tip sheet that your students can keep
handy and summarizes what this unit has detailed. This handout can
serve as a good go-to guide to remind students what banking,
budgeting, and money management is all about.

---
## Page 627
Checking Account vs. Savings Account
Savings account:
•
The main function is to save money.
•
The number of withdrawals per month may be limited.
•
The account might require a minimum balance to avoid fees.
Checking account:
•
The primary purpose is for everyday money transactions
such as paying bills and making purchases.
•
There are few or no restrictions on the number of
withdrawals or checks per month.
•
You should pay close attention to fees that will affect you
most, including ATM fees, debit card fees, overdraft fees,
and bounced check fees. These fees add up and can wreck
your monthly budget.
Protect your personal information:
•
Don’t give anyone your social security number, credit card,
or bank account numbers unless you know why the
individual or organization needs them and are sure of the
identity of the person requesting them.
•
Ignore and delete emails requesting personal information.
•
Shred anything with your name, address, credit card
information, or bank account numbers before putting it in the
trash or recycle bin.

---
## Page 628
Separate your needs from wants:
When spending your money think about what’s really important
to you and what has lasting value. Can you:
•
Rent movies and make snacks at home rather than going to
the movies?
•
Wait to buy clothes until they are on sale at your favorite
stores?
•
Pack your lunch or snacks instead of buying food every day?
•
Use your cell phone during off-peak hours?
•
Avoid taking money out of ATM machines that have
surcharges?
Build good credit!
•
Pay basic expenses on time.
•
Pay credit card and loans on time.
•
Don’t bounce checks.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
THE “F” IN SHIFT: FOOD PREPARATION
Tell your students that learning how to plan for and then cook nutritious
meals while staying within a budget is what the F in SHIFT is all about.
Explain that living independently also means being able to plan
nutritious meals, cook them (by following recipes), and then cleaning up
after cooking.
Explain that cooking and preparing food is about following directions
and being able to master the basic skills that will allow students to
safely operate a stove, oven, microwave, and other kitchen appliances.

---
## Page 629
Tell students that in this part of the Skills for Independent Living
chapter you will be taking a lot of the mystery and/or concerns they may
have about learning to cook and do basic prep work out of food
preparation.
Explain that you’ll be focusing on basic cooking skills but will not help
them with any clean up. Clean up is something they will have to do on a
daily basis, and requires more work than skill.
Explain that they learned clean up skills in the H of SHIFT:
Housekeeping. And … that you’ll expect them to complete all kitchen
chores and that includes sweeping, taking out the trash, and wiping
down the counters after each “practice” session.
The student handout on pages 287–288 is a general nutrition guide to
help students begin to understand some of the basics involved in
healthy eating.
Explain that a healthy meal always tastes great and that you are going
to talk about an easy way to control portions – the plate method.
Directions: On a white board draw an oversized plate shape.
Draw a line through the middle and divide the lower half into two
sections.
Explain that the top (and largest) half is for vegetables, the lower left
section for protein (like chicken or fish), and the lower right for a starch.
General Nutrition Guidelines
In order to look and feel your best, eating a variety of foods and
mixing in vegetables and fruits with proteins will help you to stay
alert and keep you from becoming over-weight. Here are some
guidelines that will help you as you plan your menus and gather

---
## Page 630
the ingredients you will need to make nutritious and great tasting
meals.
BREAKFAST
•
Cereals: Forgo anything with “chocolate” or “sugar-coated”
in the title and choose cereals that are high in fiber and low in
sugar. You can add fresh or dried fruit, frozen berries, nuts,
milk, etc. Oatmeal is a good choice too. These types of
cereal will not only fill you up – nutritionally they are super
good for you.
•
Other easy options: Eggs (add in some veggies and cheese)
with toast. Yogurt with fruits and nuts is quick and easy and
high in protein and fiber.
LUNCH
•
Quick and easy: Leftovers from dinner!
•
Sandwich options:
•
Whole-grain breads and wraps.
•
Fillings: ham, turkey, roast beef, cheese, hummus, canned
tuna or salmon with mayo, tomato, cucumbers, sprouts,
greens, sliced apples with almond butter, peanut butter, or
grilled cheddar (or any kind of cheese) sandwich.
•
Canned soups: Lentil, bean, and split pea soups are great.
•
Salads: Leafy greens and lots of veggies, chickpeas, dried
fruit and nuts, hummus, diced cold cuts, sliced hardboiled
eggs, sliced fruit, tuna, cheese, cold leftovers, and the list
goes on.
•
Sides: Fruit, pita chips, nuts, raw veggies.

---
## Page 631
SNACK
•
Fruits – great when paired with nut butter, and crackers or
bread.
•
Nuts, seeds, and of course raw veggies.
•
Pretzels or whole-grain crackers with mustard, cheese,
and/or hummus.
•
Light microwave popcorn flavored with garlic sale, salt and
pepper, or even cinnamon.
DINNER
•
Veggie – anything – the greener the better. Use these in stir
fries or steam and add some olive oil or butter, salt, and
pepper – delish!
•
Have a protein: Chicken, fish, or meat.
•
Use only extra-virgin olive oil and real butter when cooking.
•
Add: a grain (brown rice, quinoa, couscous) or pasta, root
vegetable (sweet potato or yams instead of white potatoes),
and a bread (if you like). That’s dinner!

---
## Page 632

---
## Page 633
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Food and Meal Preparation
Explain to your students that they will pick one day a week as their
“plan and prep” day. This will also be the day they grocery shop.
Saturday or Sunday is a good day to do this.
Here’s a student handout that they can use to accomplish this task.
Have students create a folder or binder to hold all this information so
that they can have it all in one place and refer to it often or until
planning, prepping, and cooking become routine.

---
## Page 634
Explain that you will go over everything in great detail and that their
“homework” will be to do this task either on their own or with a
roommate’s help or the help of a tutor, mentor, or one-on-one support
staff person to help them the first few times.
Use whatever resources are available to assist students at your
learning facility – including – the culinary arts department kitchen. If this
is not possible, assign prepping and cooking tasks like you would any
other homework. In this day of cell phone cameras, students can make
a photo essay to present each other in small groups during class time
of “how things went.”
Plan, Prep, Cook, and Enjoy!
PLAN
Plan your dinner meals for the week out using the following
guideline: Protein + Starch + Vegetable (make sure to get your
healthy fats in!). Write out with pen and paper or enter into a
“tablet” or your computer all the meals that you or you and your
roommate will prepare during the week.
Here are some suggestions:
•
Chicken breasts – boneless, skinless.
•
Chicken thighs are okay for specific recipes with other
ingredients.
•
Ground beef – 85% lean or higher.
•
Ground turkey – 93% lean or higher.
•
Fish – salmon, tilapia, shrimp.

---
## Page 635
•
Eggs (hardboil a bunch for the week!).
•
Steak, pork, or sausage are all options too
Vegetarian or vegan?
Try plant based proteins: Beans (black beans, garbanzo beans,
kidney beans, etc.), edamame, tofu, and tempeh.
Starch options: opt for whole-grain, complex carbohydrates as
your fuel!
•
Quinoa (you can add whatever flavors/spices of dish to
quinoa to boost flavor).
•
Sweet potato.
•
Brown rice.
•
Pasta.
•
Potato.
•
Rice varieties: Jasmine, Basmati, Wild Grain, or Black
•
Buckwheat/Amaranth
Vegetable options:
•
Spinach.
•
Green beans.
•
Mushrooms.
•
Asparagus.
•
Broccoli.
•
Cucumber.
•
Red, yellow, orange peppers.
•
Zucchini.

---
## Page 636
•
Kale.
•
Bok choy.
•
Spaghetti Squash.
•
Carrots.
•
Tomatoes.
•
Sugar snap peas.
PREP
Grocery shop for the week! Check your recipes to make sure
you have all the ingredients for all recipes beforehand! Add
necessary items to the shopping list.
When you get home:
Peel, chop, cut all raw veggies for the week to put in lunch or to
have as a snack.
Repeat the process for any fruit that requires cutting to be eaten.
COOK
Now, you can take “prepping” one step further if you want to!
You can:
Cook enough protein, veggies, and starches to eat for your
meal that night or cook two meals to generate three or four
meals during the week (using leftovers).
Ways to cook your protein:

---
## Page 637
•
Grill chicken with a variety of seasonings to switch up the
flavor during the week.
•
Bake chicken with roasted root veggies, or potatoes, or just
bake it by itself.
•
Use your crock-pot! Makes enough of a dish to have for the
week!
•
Cook chicken in chicken broth in the crock-pot and use as
shredded chicken to add to recipes for the week (think
chicken salad, chicken tacos, and to put on top of salad).
•
Sauté ground beef/ground turkey with a little bit of garlic
powder (eat as is throughout the week or create dishes with
it such as tacos, beef and peppers, etc.).
•
Hardboil enough eggs for the week as a lunch option or go-to
protein based snack.
Tip #1
Do all your cutting up of vegetables and fruits on the day that
you grocery shop, and cook one of your meals, ensuring you
have enough to eat for that night, and the next day!
Tip #2
Make your lunch the night before, or even multiple nights in
advance!
Remember, do what works best for you!
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 638
Following Recipes
Tell students that reading a recipe is just that: Reading and following
each step in the instructions.
Here are two super “beginner” recipes you and your students can
prepare together or if this is not possible, assign it as homework and
have students report back to the class how they did in following the
recipe.
If working independently they might consider enlisting a roommate or
friend to help out. When reporting back: Encourage video tapes,
pictures, and anything else that will let you know how they did.
RECIPE FOR TACOS
Shopping list:
Taco shells (2 or 3 per person)
Can of refried beans
Can of corn
1 pound of ground beef or turkey
Packet of taco seasoning
Jar of salsa
Bag of taco cheese
Sour cream

---
## Page 639
Head of lettuce
1 onion
1 tomato
Directions:
Brown the meat with the taco seasoning.
Heat the beans in a sauce pan on low.
Finely chop the onion and tomato.
Place each ingredient in a bowl.
Set up a buffet so each person can make their own
taco.
RECIPE FOR HOMEMADE PIZZA
Shopping List:
2 bags of wheat pizza dough from the dairy case
1 jar of pizza sauce (near the spaghetti sauce)
2 small bags or 1 large bag of mozzarella cheese
1 green pepper
1 bag of pepperoni (deli case)
1 onion
1 small can of mushrooms

---
## Page 640
Directions:
Pre-heat the oven to 400 degrees.
Chop the onion and pepper.
Open and drain the mushrooms.
Lightly oil your pizza pan or cookie sheet with olive oil.
Press the pizza dough out onto your cookie sheet or
pizza pan.
Cover with a layer of sauce.
Sprinkle liberally with cheese.
Place your toppings on the cheese.
Pop into the oven at 400 degrees.
Check it every ten minutes until it’s done (crust brown
– cheese bubbly).
Cut and serve!
Being able to prepare basic yet nutritious meals is a big milestone for
young adults on the ASD or LD Spectrum. This coupled with taking
care of their basic hygiene, housekeeping, grocery shopping, and being
able to budget their money sets each student up for success.
The last letter in the acronym SHIFT is T – Transportation. If your
students can’t get to where they need to go by asking for and following
directions and then driving or using public transportation they won’t be
able to get to where they need to go (jobs, school, social events,
shopping, etc.).

---
## Page 641
This next section will help you prepare your students for the last hurdle
in acquiring the SHIFT skills, Transportation.
THE “T” IN SHIFT: TRANSPORTATION
Tell students that transportation – how we get to and from the events
that make up our lives – is a primary consideration for everyone,
including people with an ASD or LD diagnosis.
Explain that the T in SHIFT – Transportation – will focus on the
independent living skills needed to travel from one location to the other.
And, that transportation is critical to being able to get to their jobs and
classes. They also can’t meet up with people for social occasions, or
get to an appointment on time if they don’t know how to drive or use
public transportation.
Explain that travel skills are critical to the successful use of
transportation and that you will be showing them a lot of good examples
of how to do this.
Tell your students who are learning to drive or those with driver’s
licenses that they (or their parents/older siblings/friends) learned to
drive by training and hands-on experience to learn to safely and
independently drive a vehicle.
Explain that (in most cases) the same attention is not necessarily given
to the skills needed to safely and independently use public
transportation.
Explain that in some instances (because of the way an ASD or LD
brain is wired) driving will not be a viable option for many people on the
Spectrum until they are much older. Remind students of the “1/3 Rule”
and that typically those on the ASD or LD Spectrum are two to three
years behind their peers when it comes to emotional and social
development/maturity).
Therefore … using public transportation is essential to enhancing
quality of life and advancing independent living.

---
## Page 642
Explain that learning to travel via public transportation, safely and
independently, will be the focus of your classes on transportation and
that the benefits to each student will be measurable and immediate.
Tell your students that the basic skills for using public transportation
include 
orientation 
to 
the 
immediate 
environment, 
identifying
landmarks, learning a route, street-crossing skills, and safety while
moving in close proximity to traffic.
Explain that additional skills are needed – such as understanding the
concept of fixed-route schedules, identifying the public transit vehicles,
locating transit stops, paying fares, recognizing when to disembark,
and learning the route from the transit stop to the points of origin and
destination.
Tell students that problem solving skills are also needed, such as how
to interact with the vehicle operator and other passengers and what to
do when unexpected changes occur such as delays, detours, or
inclement weather.
The student handout on pages 293–294 will get students talking about
the concepts described above and then practicing what you have gone
over in class in real-time and real-place settings. This practice can be
done as a homework assignment in small groups, or with peer mentors,
or any support personnel (tutors, social skills instructors, advisors, or
counselors) that your school provides.
Using Public Transportation
Public transportation (how we get to and from the events that
make up our lives) has many benefits. It will help you experience
freedom of movement, independence, community inclusion, and
increase your confidence in your ability to get where you need to
go or want to go each and every day.

---
## Page 643
Since transportation is “necessary” for a fulfilling and happy life,
good independent travel skills are “critical” to the successful use
of transportation.
Riding public transportation is a great way to get to where you
need to go. But that doesn’t mean public transportation doesn’t
come with its own unique set of problems.
Public transportation, whether it’s on a bus, light rail, or subway,
can get a little overwhelming even for seasoned veterans. Here
are some strategies, ideas, and “tricks” to make using public
transportation a good experience.
Preparation: Know Your Schedules and
Routes!
The days of fold-out, impossible to follow bus schedules are
long gone. Most major cities have public transit system apps for
both iPhone and Android. If yours doesn’t, apps like Embark,
and Google Maps will fill the void.
All you need to do is type in your current location and destination
and you’ll be on your way. If you do have questions about
transfers, don’t be afraid to ask the bus driver or fellow
passengers. Chances are someone is heading the same way as
you.
Getting to Your Bus or Train on Time!
Leave your apartment, dorm, or house on time … so that you get
to your bus or train station or stop on time. No bus system is
perfectly timed, so it’s always a good idea to show up a couple
of minutes early.

---
## Page 644
It’s also good to know how long it takes to get from your home
(or office) to the station or stop. Then overestimate this. For
instance: If it takes you two minutes to walk there allow five.
Be Investigative!
If this is your first time on a transit system in the city or town
where you live (or that you are visiting) it’s good to do a little
research beforehand. Hop a bus or train (as a “trial run” – or as
the “homework” your teacher, life skills advisor, social skills
instructor or tutor has assigned you or a small group of
students).
Pay close attention to each stop. A lot of buses and subways
don’t announce each stop, making it easy to miss yours when
you’re not carefully watching each cross street.
If you don’t trust your eyes to tell you when to get off the bus or
subway, you can check out an app like Travalert (Android/iOS)
that alerts you when you’re getting close to your destination.
Get Your Wallet in Order: Correct Change and
Passes
Buses (at least in the United States) don’t give change. While
light rails and subway systems often have ticketing systems
outside the stations, buses are almost always exact change only.
However, if you are going to use the bus, subway, or train every
day, it’s worth picking up a week-long or month long transit pass
just to ensure you’re always covered. It will also save you
money.

---
## Page 645
To save even more money on your commute, yearly passes are
also available and completely unlimited and allow you to ride
seven days a week. This is a great way to explore your city, and
plan adventures and day trips with your friends on weekends or
during your days off from work or school.
On the Ride: Keep Yourself Entertained
Depending on your disposition, public transportation can either
be a great place to chat with strangers, or a horrifying social
situation you have to navigate.
For those who like to chat, public transportation is a good place
to spark up a random conversation with a stranger (who can’t
really walk away). This means asking relevant questions and
starting off with neutral topics (like the weather). Just remember,
not everyone is interested in talking.
If keeping to yourself is more your thing, headphones, reading
an e-book, or watching a video on your “tablet” are the easiest
ways to avoid a social interaction.
Digging into whatever you enjoy, whether it’s crossword puzzles,
games, podcasts, or books, is a great way to pass the time, and
in most cases it helps insulate you from any passengers who
might be a little chatty when you’re not interested.
Staying Safe and Sound!
Use 
your 
instincts. 
Sometimes 
a 
situation 
will 
feel
uncomfortable. Always (100% of the time) go with your instincts
and if you feel uncomfortable (feel is the important word here; it
does not matter whether there is real danger or not), take action.
Here is what to do when “any” feeling of discomfort occurs:

---
## Page 646
•
Avoid eye contact.
•
Lean against the window and stretch out your legs.
•
Look out the window with a blank stare.
•
Without drawing attention to yourself, make sure all your
possessions are close to you and that you are physically
holding your backpack, purse, briefcase, or computer case.
Be Courteous, Polite, and Kind!
It’s easy to disappear into your phone when you’re on public
transportation, but that’s no excuse for not using or displaying
common sense and exhibiting good manners. For instance:
•
If you see someone who could use your seat more than you
(they’re sick, elderly, holding a ton of bags, etc.), offer yours
if you’re able.
•
Slide over to the window seat when the bus is full, and if
someone needs a hand, help them out.
Remember that people don’t want to hear your loud
conversation on your cell phone. If you have to make a call, keep
it brief and as quiet as you can. If you’re lucky, everyone else on
the bus will do the same (ok, at the very least you won’t be the
loud annoying person).
Exiting Your Bus, Subway, or Train
When you’re getting off your bus, subway, or train … look for
signs that mark the actual exit. Most buses have an exit in the
middle of the bus, and trains will open the doors on the opposite
side of people entering. If you can … get off without getting in the
way of people getting on; everything will be quicker for everyone
involved.

---
## Page 647
Off the Bus
Don’t forget your hand sanitizer! You’ve just been touching
surfaces that hundreds if not thousands of other people have
been touching. Use a hand sanitizer, especially during cold and
flu seasons!
Summary
Use apps to help you navigate the public transportation system,
keep yourself entertained, and be polite. It’s really as simple as
that.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
PERSONAL INSIGHT: This section ends with a story I wrote about
navigating my life by learning to use the SHIFT skills portrayed in this
chapter. It’s been edited and adapted from my first book: “Made for
Good Purpose …” and illustrates how independent living skills helped
me navigate through a variety of situations that ended up with me
being able to ask for directions in Australia. And … being able to get
where I needed to go, without melting down! Please share my story
with your students – I have a feeling many of them will relate to it and
may even take some of my suggestions and implement them in their
own lives.
Asking for Help, Directions, or Advice
Students need to develop the navigation skills necessary to be able to
make it in the world of independent living. When writing this story, I
realized how much this statement applied to me personally. I have an
ASD diagnosis, and have strong sensory challenges as well.

---
## Page 648
This means (to my way of thinking), being able to navigate not only
social environments but physical and directional navigating as well.
When I work with students, one of the first things I tell them is that they
are going to encounter many roadblocks and that they will have to
gather up every ounce of courage they possess and ask for help. For
instance: I remember driving back and forth over a long bridge in
Jacksonville, Florida and not stopping to ask for directions to where I
was going, just kept making the same mistake over and over until
somehow I found my way. It was a huge waste of time and gas money!
I also tell them (from my own perspective) how empowering these
roadblocks can be: that it opens up everyday environments and that
this often leads to opening up the world and developing a sense of
adventure.
I further explain that it’s all about asking questions, seeking answers,
obtaining solutions, and then either mentally or physically finding their
way to wherever they are going or whatever answer they are seeking.
Last spring I got off an airplane in Sydney, Australia and got into my
rental car. I had to contend with driving on the left side of the road AND
following my printed MapQuest directions to my hotel in Newtown at the
same time.
I realized almost immediately that the directions were wrong. No matter
how hard I looked I could not find the road I needed. I was on an
interstate highway heading toward a tunnel and I had to make myself
accept the fact that I was going to get off the first exit after the tunnel
and stop and ask for help and directions.
I know following driving directions is not a strength I possess so in
preparation for this trip I had (on the QT) given myself permission to
screw up the directions part of the trip. While giving myself this
permission I took the time and established a couple of rules for myself.
When driving in Australia:
•
I would take my time and not be rushed into any turns or any
driving decisions I instinctively thought were wrong. Instead, I
would simply pull over if I was “stressing out” or becoming

---
## Page 649
“anxious.” I would do my breathing routine to calm down and not
let the physical or mental distress I was suffering take over.
•
That I would always just stay “left” (instead of the customary
“right” on United States roads), on any road I ended up on. In
fact, I made “two labels” to stick on the steering wheel and
dashboard that said “STAY LEFT” on them.
•
I also arranged for my flight to arrive early in the day so I could
drive in the light of day and take my time to find my way.
In the end I had to stop three times to ask for help and get directions.
But I got to where I needed to go with as little physical and emotional
distress as possible.
The techniques I use each day to navigate my job, my life, and regulate
my emotions were learned through trial and error. I did a lot of reading,
self-education, and made many mistakes until I was able to find
systems and ways to make myself successful in my career, when living
on my own, and in interpersonal relationships when dealing with people
I would come into contact with in the course of a normal day.
I encourage all young people on the ASD or LD Spectrum to do this
too. As they learn the skills needed to navigate independent living, they
should be very brave and never, ever, fear asking for directions,
advice, or help. And, if necessary, go out and buy a lifetime supply of
sticky notes to remind them of this!
Section III: Community Integration
This section looks at co-existing in harmony (with roommates, friends,
co-workers, and acquaintances) and learning to care about the feelings
of others while beginning to understand diversity. It’s also about
understanding how different each one of us is in our thinking, beliefs,
and tolerances. The independent living skills illustrated in this chapter
are skills that are performed in cooperation while co-existing with
others.

---
## Page 650
These skills will allow your students to be interested in other people, to
expand their social circles, and to figure out strategies for dealing with
people (roommates, co-workers, and others) whom they may, in reality,
have very little in common with.
It’s about trying to figure things out about others, how they are thinking,
feeling, and if they are people your students might want to get to know
better, or cultivate as friends. It’s also about self-regulation and using
appropriate emotions to express how they are feeling. The hope is that
these young men and women will learn how wonderful it is to interact
with others and embrace the support they will feel by developing their
own cadre of mentors and supporters.
This section also sets the scene for Chapter 7 that further explores the
values, ethics, and community integration of each individual into his or
her community, and for Chapter 8 that takes a more in-depth look at
conflict resolution and peer mediation, as well as a deeper look at
dealing with bullying.
Perspective Taking

---
## Page 651
Explain to your students that all people have little thoughts about the
people around them, even if they have no plan or intention of
communicating with them.
Tell students that all people try to figure out “why are you near me,”
and/or “why are you saying this to me?” And that this statement leads
to the next: “Since I know you are thinking about me, I’m going to try
and figure out what you are thinking about me.” This of course is done
without asking the other person directly.
Explain that this type of thinking usually leads to the person modifying
their behavior to keep the other person thinking about him or her in the
way he or she wants to be thought of.
Tell students that they will be learning strategies for “being
themselves,” while regulating emotions and using their social skills to
put the right message across at the right time instead of guessing how
the other person wants each individual to behave.
Explain that this involves self-regulation and filtering.
The student handout below is about self-regulation and about using
emotional regulation strategies when dealing with stressful situations.
Use it as a lesson plan to get your students thinking about how they
“present” to others when things don’t always go as planned. Have
students role play sending “big box messages” and “little box
messages.”
Big Box Messages
Do you find yourself thinking about what someone else is
thinking … about you, about others, or about their life or your life
in general, or even a specific situation? Congratulations! Every
day all kinds of people think about all these things as they try to
determine what each other is thinking or feeling.

---
## Page 652
You learned all about verbal language and nonverbal body
language, and that you are expected to observe another
person’s body, eyes, and listen to their words. Well guess what?
Other people are thinking the same thing about you! They are
trying to figure you out (who you are, where you are coming
from, if you are a nice person, etc.), just as you are trying to
figure them out.
You also learned about being polite and treating people with
respect and dignity. But you did not learn about “big boxes” yet.
This means that if you act in a way that makes people have
strong (usually negative) thoughts about you, you are sending
your message in a big box! Instead of in a carefully wrapped
smaller, nicer (and a more easy to handle) looking box.
You have to be very careful about how you send a message to
others. If you are frustrated and you stomp your feet, yell, cross
your arms in a hostile way, or make angry faces, you are
sending a “big box message.”
Other people don’t always like to get “big boxes,” and may not
want to be around you if this is the image you portray on a
regular basis. And … they will feel uncomfortable when you are
around them and may not be very helpful or friendly to you.
Here are some good tips to deal with “big box messages” so
that you message comes out in a “smaller or even a little box”
instead. When you are frustrated, angry, or unhappy people
might know you are irritated or unhappy with a situation because
you “look” a little frustrated. You might say in a moderate tone of
voice that you are upset. Or … that you are unhappy or that you
would like to discuss something with them.
This is called self-regulation and controlling your emotions. Or,
just simply … “staying under control.”

---
## Page 653
“This” is how you deal with difficult people or people who
have upset you!
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
The story on this page and the story on page 299 talk about filtering
and cognitive rigidity.
Tell students that Michael McManmon (the author of this book – who
has an ASD diagnosis), wrote them to help other people who have the
same problems he does.
Explain that Michael often turns these two stories into workshops and
conference presentations that he delivers to people all over the world.
Filtering and Do I Want to be Right … activity instructions: Divide
students into small groups. One student reads the first short story to the
others and students start a discussion. Ask students to relate any
personal stories they might have along these lines or any or “aha”
moments they might have had when reading or listening to Michael’s
story about filtering.
Then have students (still in small groups) read the second story,
entitled “Michael’s Quest: Do I Want to Be Right or Do I Want to Be
Happy?” This story will also facilitate a provocative discussion.
Do I Want to Be Right or Do I Want to Be
Happy?
“Michael’s Experience”

---
## Page 654
“Filtering”
When I speak nationally at conferences, seminars, and to my
students or staff, I always say that my ASD (Autism Spectrum
Disorder) diagnosis has made me an “equal opportunity
offender.” Over the years, no one I know has been immune to
my “accidental insults.” I know that I must consciously work
every single day on developing a filter between my brain and my
mouth – an emotional modulator of sorts.
As a child, then an adolescent, and now well into adulthood, my
emotional regulation skills were (and sometimes still are)
lacking. I also had (I should really say “sometimes still have”) an
extreme “self-righteous streak” that fed (and still sometimes
feeds) my belief that people needed (and still need) to hear what
I have to say. In my opinion, I was (and am) justified in saying
what I have to say, because it was (is) the TRUTH!
My ex-father in law used to say to me, “Mike, you are such a
smart guy and talented in many ways, so why can’t you control
your mouth?” I had no answer for this question. I would just
stammer and walk away feeling very embarrassed. It seemed
like I had only two choices: Be an idiot and speak my mind or
shut up. Throughout adulthood, I vacillated between the two with
(depending on the situation) very mixed results. Sometimes my
ASD and LD students, due to lack of being able to filter
perceptions and actions of others, will misunderstand and
misinterpret a social situation (as I often do).
Over the years I have had to develop a flexible brain about
“always thinking I am right,” or that “my way is the best” (or
most truthful, or only way) of doing something.
I try really hard to “think” before I speak and when I do I make
an effort to listen first, to the opinions, ideas, and thoughts of

---
## Page 655
others. This … by the way … is really hard for me to do. But the
more I acknowledge my lack of a “filter” the more I am able to
help change my behavior. I consider myself “a work in
progress.” And, I hope that my story helps those who have this
same issue, develop a filter too.
Michael P. McManmon
“Michael’s Quest”
As a young adult, I was frustrated in my dealings with others, I
could not agree with them on many issues, but at the same time,
I desperately wanted to be friends with them. I felt isolated and
alone and clung to my beliefs that, in reality, separated me from
others. I would outwardly respect others in public, but secretly
not accept them as having a “valid existence” in private. If they
did not believe in my religion or my way of being in the world, I
became even more intolerant.
As an adult, I had to learn to suspend my judgments of others. I
learned, over time, to listen to others and ask them questions
about who they are, where they came from, and what was
important to them. I came across this saying while practicing my
newfound diversity, “Do you want to be right or be happy?”
These words helped me break through the cognitive rigidity that
seemed to always be engulfing my brain.
I learned that I do not have to agree with everyone and they do
not have to agree with me. I can still be friends with people of all
different religions and political, racial, and national origins and
still be me.

---
## Page 656
Michael P. McManmon
Relationships
Now that your students have had a chance to look at “how they present
themselves to others,” and learned a bit about filtering and
understanding the diversity that exists among people, it’s time to look at
the stages of friendship (five of them) and how the young adults you
work with move from stage to stage. It’s also appropriate to spend
some time looking at how your students can live in harmony with
roommates.
Explain to your students that they will be working with you and each
other on understanding the five stages of friendship.
Tell students this will help them understand how friendships are built
using a formula that talks about each one of the five stages in a realistic
and factual way. That way, they will be able to take things in order, and
be sure of one stage (reality vs. a grandiose belief) before moving on to
the next.

---
## Page 657
Explain that the handout on pages 300–301 shows these stages as
concentric circles that start with #5 (strangers) and work through the
stages of relationships to #1 (self-intimacy).
Divide the class into small groups. Each group will create a role play for
each of the five stages. Depending on the class, groups may choose to
present their role plays to each other or work with each stage (in their
individual small groups) until the concepts are understood. Students
can feel free to use real-time examples from their own lives as well.
Feel free to transfer the concentric circle design to a white board for
further illustration and discussion with the entire class.
The Five Stages of Friendship

---
## Page 658
#5 Strangers
Strangers are people you begin to share information with on a
superficial level. This might be someone you greet in the
“hallway” at work or school. A stranger might be someone who
“makes” eye contact with you on the bus, or someone who has
been introduced to you for the first time by another person. You
would begin to speak to a stranger in general terms that reveals
very little, if any personal information. Topics might be the
weather, current events, or what town or city they live in. If you
both respond positively to the “initial” conversation, you might
consider participating in some activities that stranger might do. If
things go well, you would advance to the next circle: Casual
acquaintances.
Things strangers might do:
Attend activities and events together such as volunteer work;
agree to meet up at the gym or movie; meet at a coffee shop to
get to know each other better; meet to engage in a sport like
running, kayaking, or skateboarding. They might meet at the
mall to do some shopping together. It can be any activity that
takes place in a public setting that will allow you to get to know
the person better.
#4 Casual Acquaintances
You still do not know each other well enough to share personal
information. If it feels comfortable or “right,” you might ease into
sharing some feelings and thoughts but only “positive” ones
about non-controversial topics. You’re there to have fun, share a
task or coffee together and you should keep conversation light
and friendly. This stage is all about getting to know one another
better, but not divulging a lot of personal information or intimate

---
## Page 659
details. If things continue to go well, you would advance to the
next circle: Friends.
Things casual acquaintances might do:
Attend some of the activities you would if the person were a
stranger. At this point it is ok to take some risks to see if you
both have things in common such as likes and dislikes, all while
using caution, as you still do not know the person very well. You
want to make sure they are genuine, and that you have enough
in common to consider making this casual acquaintance a
friend.
#3 Friends
As you move into this circle, you begin to trust one another
more. You feel secure enough when you are in the other
person’s company to be more spontaneous, laugh, giggle,
tease, and share stories and life experiences with each other.
It’s ok to discuss negative things as long as you spend more
time talking about positive ones and leave on an upbeat,
emotionally supportive role. If all goes well (give it a couple of
months or even a year or more, if needed), you would advance
to the next circle: Deep friendship.
Things friends might do:
Spend some face-to-face time with the person regularly (at least
once every two weeks). Be willing to share personal information
and gain personal information about the other person. Be willing
to support each other’s needs while really getting to know their
likes, dislikes, philosophies, and character traits.

---
## Page 660
Note: There are two other important “friend” categories:
Romantic friends and romantic lovers.
Romantic friends:
As above, BUT … you both need to be willing and able to give
and receive physical gestures (e.g., hold hands, hug, or kiss).
Romantic lovers:
Ensure that you have discussed sex with your romantic friend
and that this is a choice both of you want to make.
#2 Deep Friendship
It takes much longer to reach this stage. By this point both of you
have experienced each other closely for so long that you trust
each other deeply. You’re best friends and intimate friends. Your
relationship has been tested and you have seen each other
through good times and bad times. You have also seen each
other at your worst and survived.
Things deep friends might do:
Moving to this step takes time but also a commitment from both
people to continue working on the relationship. If you are
struggling with this stage, you may want to read some books
that explain more about maintaining relationships (called self-
help books). In addition, it might be helpful to seek the
assistance of a therapist, or trusted advisor, or counselor.
#1 Self-Intimacy

---
## Page 661
This innermost circle consists of you and only you! It’s healthy to
keep some thoughts, ideas, hopes, wishes, dreams, and
feelings totally and completely to yourself. On the other hand,
staying too much in this circle can prevent others from getting
close to you.
And remember:
Healthy friendships move gradually from #5 to #2. It is not
healthy to jump from circle to circle without experiencing each
one. In other words you would not jump from #5 to #3 after a first
meeting. If you do this, you’ll be skipping too many boundaries.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Roommates
Your students have come a long way by beginning to understand the
stages of friendship. This is a good and logical point in time for talking
about relationships with roommates.
Tell your students that a roommate can be a person that they have
voluntarily chosen to live with (like a friend), someone that a school or
college may have assigned to them, or even a stranger who answered
an advertisement for a roommate from a newspaper or online
publication.
Explain to your students that this person may become a friend (over
time), or may stay in the acquaintance category and simply share an
apartment or living space.
Tell students they do not have to “like” every single thing about the
person they live with but they do have to make an effort to share tasks
like chores and shopping for groceries and try to find enough in
common with each other so that everyone lives in an atmosphere of
harmony, dignity, and respect.

---
## Page 662
The student handout on pages 303–304 is one that students can refer
to often. After discussing the “Roommate Rules” handout ask students
to relate their personal experiences or those of people they know who
have had good or not-so-good roommate experiences to the class. If
it’s a negative experience, discuss ways it might have been handled. If
it’s a positive experience, have students point out the factors,
strategies, and ideas that made the relationship successful to the rest
of the class.
Roommate Rules!
There are a variety of reasons why people choose to share their
living space with another person. Whether you’re a college
student or a young adult who needs a roommate for economic
reasons, you’ll have a much better experience if you follow these
guidelines.

---
## Page 663
1. Establish some basic rules. The first thing you should do
with your new roommate is to sit down and establish a short
list of rules. This is a give-and-take conversation, so list the
most important things that each of you expects. Include how
to divide the bills, i.e., who pays for what, policies regarding
guests, cleaning schedules, grocery shopping, and anything
else you can think of.
2. Don’t borrow anything without asking. Your roommate
might have the cutest top that hasn’t been worn in ages, but
that doesn’t give you the right to wear it without asking. She
might be saving it for a special occasion. Or he may have the
best leather coat you’ve ever seen, but that doesn’t give you
the right to wear it without asking. Ask permission first, and
always honor his or her wishes. If he or she gives you
permission, and you spill something on it, pay to have it
professionally cleaned. If that doesn’t work, replace the item.
3. Respect the other person’s space. Everyone has a need
for some personal space and privacy, regardless of how
outgoing he or she is, so avoiding intruding on the other
person’s space is very important for both of you.
4. Follow the Golden Rule. Before you take any questionable
action regarding your roommate, stop and think about how
you would feel if the tables were turned. Following the
Golden Rule (do unto others as you would have them do
unto you) can save your relationship.
5. Be respectful when inviting guests. Whether you are
having someone over for a dinner party or an overnight stay,
it’s always a good idea to discuss this with your roommate.
Unless you are co-hosting a party or other event, never
expect your roommate to share in the entertainment or
expenses.
6. Clean up your own messes. If you make a mess, clean it
up. Don’t expect your roommate to help clean up after a

---
## Page 664
party, unless the two of you co-hosted it. You also shouldn’t
leave the mess for the next day because the other person
shouldn’t have to face dirty dishes and crumbs when he or
she gets up in the morning. If you share a bathroom, keep
your personal belongings neat and in order.
7. Keep your hands off the other person’s food. When a
carton of your favorite ice cream suddenly appears in the
freezer, don’t assume that it’s half yours. Don’t eat even one
bite of it until you have permission from your roommate. He
or she might be saving it for a special occasion, or he or she
may have purchased it with the last of what was left in his or
her food budget.
8. Respect each other’s need for quiet time. If you see
your roommate reading a book, don’t constantly interrupt him
or her with conversation. If you know that he or she has to be
at work early the next morning, keep the noise level low. One
way for roommates to let each other know when they’re
sleeping is to have a small “I’m Sleeping” or “Do Not
Disturb” sign made that can hang from their bedroom
doorknob.
9. Take care of your own pets. If you and your roommate
have agreed that pets are allowed, don’t expect the other
person to care for yours. Feed your own cat, walk your own
dog, scoop your cat’s litter pan, and vacuum the birdseed
from beneath the cage. If your roommate feels extra
generous and does it for you, show gratitude and consider
doing something for him or her in return.
10.Don’t gossip about your roommate. Living in close
quarters, you’re bound to discover something that would
make excellent fodder for gossip. Don’t do it. Remember that
he or she has the “goods” on you as well, and think about
how you’d feel if he or she broadcast your bad habits to the
world.

---
## Page 665
11.Don’t get your feelings hurt. Being roommates doesn’t
mean you have to be best friends. If your roommate makes
plans without including you, accept that he or she has other
friends he or she enjoys hanging out with. Besides, if you
have a positive attitude about his or her other friends, you
are likely to be included more often. Most people resent
someone who clings and smothers.
Deal with problems quickly. As soon as you notice a problem,
let your roommate know that it’s time to have a talk. Go into the
discussion with your point in a matter-of-fact way and avoid
flinging accusations, or constantly trying to correct him or her.
Never try to deal with the situation with the “silent treatment,”
passive-aggressive behavior, or yelling. Most problems can be
worked through, but if it’s something you can’t agree on, you
might want to consider having a teacher, tutor, mentor, advisor,
or friend act as a mediator. This person (the third party) has to
agree to remain impartial and unbiased and help the roommates
resolve the problem.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Section IV: Supporting Students
When Problems Arise
TEACHING INSIGHT: Section IV: Supporting Students When
Problems Arise gives each educator, advisor, mentor, or support
person working with students on the “ASD or LD Spectrum” a further
helping hand. These are the tips, suggestions, ideas, and solutions
that CIP’s (College Internship Program’s) contributing staff members
have found useful when problems arise. As each staff member imparts
“skills for independent living” to the adolescents and young adults he

---
## Page 666
or she works with they talk about the day-to-day, the obvious, the
unexpected, the challenging, the humorous, and the “I just simply
don’t believe it” situations that each one faces as he or she does his
or her job each and every day. Their experiences offer a “been there,
done that” snapshot that, I hope, will be invaluable to readers.
DON’T ASSUME ANYTHING!
Oriane Robison, Residential Coordinator, CIP Bloomington
One of the first things I tell incoming staff is, “don’t assume anything!”
Just because something would occur to you, or the solution to a
problem might seem really obvious to you, it does not mean (in any
way) that this will be the case for the individual you are working with.
I find that a large portion of my day is spent running on auto-pilot; I don’t
really think about getting dressed, taking a shower, feeding the cat,
driving to work, and so on. I react to what I encounter in my
environment almost instinctively, nailed down by habit, and granted …
all of it seems pretty easy and straightforward.
However … it wasn’t until I started working with adolescents and young
adults on the ASD and LD Spectrum who experience “Executive
Function” difficulties that I realized this isn’t the case for everyone.
Working with my students forced me to break tasks down into their
most basic parts, and make myself explain things with the most precise
explanations I could come up with. Against my will, it made me take an
introspective look into my own way of doing things to figure out how
exactly that all comes together. I don’t have to think about how to make
a peanut butter and jelly sandwich. But … if I were to explain it to
someone who had never made one (and yes, this is a true story!), this
is what I would say:
1. Find a store that has the ingredients you need.
2. Find a way to get there.
3. Purchase peanut butter, jelly, and bread.

---
## Page 667
4. Bring the items back to your home.
5. Clear a space to work on making the sandwich in your kitchen.
6. Clean the space if dirty.
7. Open the bread.
8. Take two slices out.
9. Tie up the bread so it doesn’t go stale and put away.
10.Open the peanut butter.
11.Remove the protective seal on the peanut butter with your
fingernails.
12.Take your knife and put peanut butter on it.
13.Spread the peanut butter on one side of one piece of bread.
14.Repeat steps 12 and 13 until peanut butter is completely
covering the bread, though not too close to the edge.
15.Put the lid back on the peanut butter.
16.Put the peanut butter away.
17.Clean off your knife with a paper towel.
18.Dispose of the paper towel in the trash can.
19.Open the jelly.
20.Take your knife and put jelly on it.
21.Spread the jelly on one side of one piece of bread.
22.Repeat steps 20 and 21 until jelly is completely covering the
bread, though not too close to the edge.
23.Put the lid on the jelly.
24.Put the jelly away.
25.Rinse your knife.
26.Put the knife in the dishwasher.

---
## Page 668
27.Take the slice with jelly and turn over onto the piece with peanut
butter so that the jelly and peanut butter are touching.
Oh! And one more thing … eat the sandwich and enjoy!
Broken down, I can count 27 steps to ending up with a peanut butter
and jelly sandwich, with some cleaning up in between, from start to
finish.
I decided early on in my job that this is how I need to see everyday
living tasks, as I rarely have a student who does not have trouble
somewhere along the way.
I’ve witnessed very bright students:
•
Unable to locate items in a store.
•
Unable to open a jar.
•
Wiping up a mess with their T-shirt.
•
Disposing of garbage on the floor instead of a trash can.
•
Eat moldy bread or undercooked meat.
•
Pour laundry detergent in the dishwasher.
•
Cleaning sinks with a toilet brush.
•
Spray themselves with air freshener in place of taking a shower.
•
Using bleach-type cleaning wipes as toilet paper.
I can think of a hundred times where I might have said to myself,
“surely, Tom, Sarah, Michael, Matthew, Todd, Lisa, and Annie etc.,
know how to do that properly.” And often they don’t, so I intervene,
break the task down, and tell them the missing steps that no one has
told them or shown them before (or maybe just not enough times).
And then I try to understand their thinking. And believe it or not, a lot of
times, it sort of makes sense. “If disinfectant wipes are good for
cleaning countertops, they’re probably especially good for cleaning my
body!” Or, “I’m out of dish soap, but these laundry pods look pretty

---
## Page 669
similar and clean my clothes well, so I’m sure they’ll do the dishes in the
dishwasher just fine!”
Well, what I think, is that when it comes down to it, there is a really
lengthy “Hidden Curriculum” when it comes to life skills. And it’s chock
full of unspoken rules and subtleties that can be difficult for students on
the Spectrum to navigate.
Some people are probably reading this story and thinking that the
person who can’t make a peanut butter and jelly sandwich is just being
lazy, or not trying hard enough. However, I cannot stress enough times
to all the readers of this book … how wrong this is.
If making a sandwich from start to finish is 27 steps, imagine how many
steps they have to go through in a day (thousands? tens of
thousands?), to do other tasks that we (who are not on the Spectrum),
do mostly on “auto pilot.”
For myself, many (although not all) of them are done without a second
thought, but for students struggling with “Executive Function” difficulties
each one of these steps might as well be a trial, judge, and jury. And I
watch daily, how frustrating doing simple daily living tasks can be for
these young men and women.
They may be challenged with something that, to all outward
appearances, seems easy and common-sense to most people. But
what a lot of us (as educators and support personnel) fail to realize is
that having to struggle with routine, rote, or simple daily living skills
often creates feelings of shame and great failure to those trying to live
on their own.
So … for these amazing young men and women, I will continue to fill in
the missing gaps, help them practice the small things, and remind them
endlessly of what they need to do each day, and I will do this with
compassion on my side. I will help them as constructively as I can, and
I will celebrate their small victories, and remind them of my own
struggles and successes.
I may even tell them that doing laundry isn’t my favorite thing, and this
is how I make it bearable: I play music and dance in between. I also

---
## Page 670
throw garbage in the trash like it’s a basketball hoop. I tenderize my
chicken with a mallet and pretend to be a super villain. The stain in my
toilet bowl is an enemy and I must conquer it with a vengeance!
I will tell them (if they can) to keep things light-hearted. And then, I will
give them clear directions and feedback, and cheer when the job is
done.
To you … it may only look like, smell, and taste like a peanut butter and
jelly sandwich, but to me … it looks, smells, and tastes like victory.

---
## Page 671
TEACHING INSIGHT: The following classroom activity on 307–308
“What Would You Do?” is used at all CIP Centers. Support staff will
use the cards to generate discussions about various independent
living problems that might come up and how students might handle
them. Students role play the various problems or emergencies in class
and also talk about preventive measures they might take or do to
avoid some of the scenario situations.
What Would You Do?
Directions: Divide students into small groups. Give each
group a set of cards. Have students brainstorm answers and
if they are “up for it,” role play some of the scenarios as they
come up with solutions.

---
## Page 672

---
## Page 673
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning
Teaching Toolkit
TEACHING INSIGHT: Marc Larrivee is a Social Skills Instructor at
CIP’s Berkshire Center. His poignant vignettes that make up a “Day in
the Life of a Social Skills Instructor” end this chapter and prove once
again, as Oriane Robison did with the first story in this section, that
you “will get there” with your students; they “will” learn skills for life,
school, work, and independent living, it just may take much, much
longer than you ever, ever, imagined, but that’s ok … it will be worth
every minute you have invested.

---
## Page 674
“Social Skills Stories: A Day in the Life of a Social
Skills Instructor”
“Tara”
I met with Tara at 9:00. She and I had planned on taking the bus
today. Tara is 19 years old and loves fashion. She likes
shopping for new clothing on her own, trying on different outfits,
and looking presentable for her appointments and internships.
She has a goal of learning the local bus transportation system so
that she can get to her internships and to the local community
college by herself. To make things interesting for her today, we
planned an outing to a nearby mall, where she could browse for
an outfit that she needed for work. In prior sessions we had
reviewed transportation safety, studied the schedule, and
bought a bus pass. Tara asked to change this plan.
Tara decided that she wanted to take the bus on her own. I
thought this was a great idea. We met at the center a few
minutes before the bus would arrive, and walked to the bus stop.
There, we reviewed what she would do, what safety measures
to remember, what my phone number was, and the times to call.
Tara called after she successfully got on the bus, as it pulled
from the curb. She called when she reached her destination, and
then later on while she awaited the return bus. She called again
when she was returning. Tara’s first independent transportation
journey was a success. What a happy smile she had as she
stepped from the bus. She had done it on her own!

---
## Page 675
Next week she wants to go by bus to the nearby village, a bit
further away.
“Jimmy”
Next I met with Jimmy, a young man of 24 with ASD who prefers
to isolate instead of socialize.
Jimmy has a Bachelor’s degree in communications and loves
film. He loves researching about actors, producers, and special
effects in films. Jimmy loves going to films and will frequently
watch movies, on his own, in his apartment.
Jimmy’s goal was to make friends. This is a challenge for Jimmy
because he prefers to be alone and because he prefers to listen
in silence. When asked a direct question, he generally answers
with a generic phrase, appropriate to the topic.
Over the past couple of sessions, he had identified a movie in
the theaters that looked interesting and that he thought would be
a popular film. He then made a list of people who might be
interested in the film and with whom he got along. He had even
asked a couple of them if they would sign up to go on a planned
weekend activity to the movies and one person had said yes.
Today we would plan out the outing a bit more and hopefully
Jimmy and a friend would go to see the movie this weekend.
During our session, Jimmy created a “mind file,” a listing of the
interests of the other person. He spent a few minutes learning
more about the movie, its actors, and its production. He then
drew up a list of tentative topics that they might talk about
afterward. We then practiced a conversation with some of the
questions. After doing these things, Jimmy appeared a bit more
relaxed.

---
## Page 676
Next time, we’ll review the outing, how it went, and what worked
well for him. We’ll also consider what movies he might invite
people to watch with him at his apartment.
“Sondra”
Sondra was having trouble with acquaintances. She liked the
fact that others liked her but felt frustrated when others didn’t
seem to like her. She wasn’t sure why or what to do about it.
Sondra is a young lady of 19 with a shy demeanor. She likes
acting, dancing, and singing.
She also likes watching the soaps on TV and doing weekend
activities with others.
Her goal was to get along better with acquaintances. Especially
with people she did know well at her workplace. Sondra also
knew that in order to keep a job, she would have to learn to get
along with others that she worked with.
Today, we would talk about “The Donkey Rule.” “The Donkey
Rule” states that if five people call an animal a donkey, it’s
probably a donkey, and not something else, like a chimpanzee.
If five people we trust are telling us the same thing, it benefits us
to believe that.
Likewise, if many people are annoyed with us, it benefits us to
consider our part in the problem.
We started the session listing her many positive qualities.
Sondra then listed the many things that her friends liked about
her. It was a bit difficult for her to identify some, but she was able
to think positively and do it.

---
## Page 677
She then identified instances when she had some trouble with
her friends. She explored this a bit further and drew pictures of a
couple of the instances instead of writing them down.
We discussed what had happened and why she thought that it
had happened. And we talked about the role others played in
each instance. We also talked about what her role may have
been in the outcome. We looked at all the roles people played …
good and bad.
We discussed “The Donkey Rule,” and she reflected on what
her friends were telling her. Sondra considered her part, and in
particular, the common circumstance of spreading of stories
about her friends, and how she felt when others told stories
about her. She then suggested something that she might do
differently. She would only talk positively about her friends when
discussing them with others to see if things would change.
At the end of the session, she felt a bit more aware of her role in
spreading stories and more resolved to be mindful in speaking
about others.
Next time, we’ll take this further, and talk more about the effects
of speaking about others negatively and broach the subject of
gossiping.
“Kevin”
Kevin is 24 and has been diagnosed with Autism Spectrum
Disorder (ASD). One of his main goals this semester is to learn
how to transition from one area of his life to the next. He is
moving and going to another school. In order to transition and
“move on” he would like to learn how to say goodbye
appropriately. He would also like to learn how to pack up his
belongings independently with a plan and in some kind of order.

---
## Page 678
In our session Kevin and I discussed ways in which he would
like to say goodbye. He decided he would like to have a small
get together for his closest friends at a local restaurant.
We discussed who he might like to invite and what restaurant
would be pleasing to each person. Additionally, we discussed
the need for a reservation – as it is a busy season and dining
with larger groups and no reservation might be problematic.
I offered to show him how to begin organizing and packing up
his kitchen. We discussed what items he could pack now and
that he would not need for the next week. He pulled several
glass items from the cabinet and I modeled how to appropriately
pack them in a box (wrap in newspaper, gently place in box, and
then label the box). Kevin was able to finish packing this box
independently.
Kevin and I then looked at what food items he would not need in
the upcoming week and discarded those items and emptied the
trash. At the end of the session, we placed the completed boxes
that Kevin had packed independently on the shelf in a corner so
they were out of the way.
In the next session I will follow up with Kevin about the dinner
get together and discuss any last minute items he may need to
pack, as well as any cleaning that may need to take place. We’ll
go over his guest list for his going away dinner and check to
make sure the reservation is in place and that Kevin is ready to
say goodbye appropriately to his friends.
“Scott”
Scott is a student who has a very hard time with social
encounters. His interactions with other people cause him great
anxiety, which results in it being very difficult for him to interact

---
## Page 679
with employees at his workplace and makes everyday tasks
such as buying food at the grocery store, going to the bank, or
ordering food at a restaurant very taxing as well.
Knowing that he has a love for animals, I would often take Scott
to the local pet store to browse on a regular basis as it always
opened up avenues for “small talk.”
Each time we would walk in the store clerk would always greet
us; a greeting that for our first trip to the store, Scott would
ignore and walk straight back to the section of books about dog
training.
The second week, this happened again – despite our discussion
about the importance of responding when greeted. He ignored
the clerk and headed straight to the animal section where he
was comfortable and could avoid the anxiety producing thought
of saying “hello.”
I persevered. We would walk to the store and on our walk there
we would practice how to respond to a greeting. I would always
remind him that even though responding to the clerk seemed
scary, as soon as it was over he would get to look around the
store and browse the things, and visit with the animals – all the
things that he enjoys.
As time progressed and Scott became more comfortable with
the practice exercise, his level of communication increased with
the clerk. Instead of ignoring her, he would wave. After a few
weeks, instead of waving, he would muster up a quiet “hi.” After
another month of easing into further communication, Scott was
able to have a quick reciprocal conversation; after the typical
hellos, the clerk asked Scott how he was doing, to which he was
able to reply with a shy, “good.”
So that’s a day in the life of a Social Skills Instructor. I
encourage readers to remember that there are many Scotts,

---
## Page 680
Taras, Kevins, Jimmys, and Sondras in the world. And that they
are truly doing the best they can. These young people do, in
most cases, “get there” in the end. And it is up to us, who have
chosen to work with those on the ASD or LD Spectrum (the
teachers, the social and life skills coaches, the OTs, the tutors,
and others) to continue to have patience with each person we
work with and to “simply” or at times, “not so simply” just hang
in there.
To keep offering support, even if it is the hundreth time in one
week you have asked someone to do something. To offer
encouragement even when it feels like “you are not getting
through to your student and don’t feel as if you ever will.” To put
the frustrations, the sameness, the routine, and the day-to-day
challenges aside and remember to (above all else) express
“gratitude and sheer joy” for the smile as “a Tara” gets off the
bus, or for the “high five” you get from “a Kevin” who can pack a
moving carton, and for the visible sigh of relief you will see on “a
Scott’s” face as he looks a stranger “in the eye” and says “hi.”
Making It Real
WHAT?
Helping adolescents and young adults (aged 16–26) who reside
on the ASD or LD Spectrum obtain the skills needed to live
independently. Students will learn:
Self-care that includes tasks such as hygiene, grooming,
manners, and health routines: things that are, for the most
part, non-negotiable for independent living.
How to grocery shop, keep their houses clean, manage their
finances, prepare nutritious meals, and use transportation to

---
## Page 681
get to where they need to go (jobs, school, and leisure
activities). Students will learn how to integrate into their
communities, develop healthy relationships, and care for
others.
How to develop relationships with others and co-habit with
roommates. Students will learn the skills needed so that they
can work in harmony using cooperation, fairness, mediation,
and learning to treat people with dignity and respect.
HOW?
Through active learning in classroom or individual one-on-
one settings that will allow students as much repetition,
practice, visual or verbal cueing, and prompting as they
need.
Through a step-by-step process using classroom materials,
activities, and instruction that presents each skill set in a
logical order so that students build upon each skill mastered
before taking on the next set of independent living
challenges.
BENEFITS?
Students will able to live independently and without
supervision.
They will be able to perform daily living tasks and manage
their emotions and stress levels as they set up apartments,
manage their finances, and learn to eat in a healthy way.
Students will begin to form relationships, make friends, and
co-exist with others as they start contributing to and
participating in the living, working, and social environments
of their surrounding communities.

---
## Page 682
Imparting Ethical Values and
Other Kinds of Values
CHAPTER 7 OVERVIEW:
CREATING A COMMUNITY OF LIKE-MINDED EDUCATORS
CHARACTER STRENGTHS
UNIVERSAL ETHICAL VALUES
RULES AND LAWS
RIGHTS AND RESPONSIBILITIES
CHECKLIST AND NOTES

---
## Page 683
Imparting Ethical Values and Other
Kinds of Values
“Circumstances are beyond human
control, but our conduct is in our own
power.”
Benjamin Disraeli
“Not everything that can be counted
counts and not everything that counts
can be counted.”
Albert Einstein
“When we work in harmony and in
relationship with others, it may take
longer to accomplish a goal, but the
outcome is usually much better and
everyone is on board to carry it out.”
Michael P. McManmon
“If you cannot find the truth right where
you are, where else do you expect to
find it?”
Dogen

---
## Page 684
“Success is doing each small action
well, and doing the right thing over and
over again.”
Michael P. McManmon
“The greatest good you can do for
another is not just share your riches,
but to reveal to him his own.”
Benjamin Disraeli
“We learn to build houses by building
houses; to the harp, by playing the
harp; to be ‘just’ by doing ‘just’ acts.”
Aristotle
The focus of Chapter 7 is being able to have the young men and
women you work with identify the values and ethics that are important
to each one of them while learning to be tolerant of differences in
others. Values in life, in a classroom, in the workplace, and that reside
in each individual, will only work if they are implemented through
development rather than change.
Working with those on the ASD/LD Spectrum to cultivate shared values
is key to having each person understand how people treat each other,
how they perceive each other’s needs, and how they behave
sympathetically towards their parents, peers, and those in the wider
community. Shared values allow people to communicate effectively with
each other, exercise objectivity, judgment, and respect truth.
This chapter is also about teaching acceptance and fostering diversity.
You know from working with young people on the wide and varied ASD
and LD Spectrum that your students are often judgmental, analytical,
disapproving, and rigid in their thinking about other people. These are
all traits that correlate with an Autism Spectrum Disorder or Learning

---
## Page 685
Differences diagnosis. This lack of tolerance toward others can be
exacerbated by their own depression or lack of self-esteem.
The purpose of this chapter is to get the young men and women you
work with to explore the values they feel are important and that they
would like to adopt by asking them to look at:
•
Character: What qualities do they possess or want to develop in
themselves?
•
Values: What are the important values that will guide their lives?
•
Ethics: Why doing the right thing and making good choices is
important.
In summary, this chapter becomes the glue or the icing on the cake
that binds the skills your students have acquired for life, work, school,
and independent living in the four competency chapters of this book
together. Merging these competencies with the character traits, values,
and ethics that each of your students will explore and endeavor to
adopt will allow each individual to become self-reliant, self-respecting,
self-confident, and self-disciplined.
Creating a Community of Like-
Minded Educators
PERSONAL INSIGHT: This chapter is going to begin differently. It will
start, not with classroom instruction, materials, and activities for your
students, but with a “handout” for all of you: the educators,
counselors, advisors, mentors, and support professionals who have
been reading this book from the beginning.
As I was putting this chapter together, I realized that everything that I
was writing about: character traits, values, ethics, tolerance, and
diversity would have to begin by looking at these qualities in myself. I

---
## Page 686
then took my thinking one step further and applied this logic to the
readers of this book.
This was my “aha” moment. I realized that giving readers a template
(that could be modified or added to), for “creating a community of like-
minded 
educators” 
within 
their 
own 
teaching 
and 
learning
environments, would allow those imparting this knowledge to explore
and find their own sense of what is important when working with their
students and what is not.
Using this template to explore and support the vast and varied
reasons for working with adolescents and young adults on the “ASD or
LD Spectrum,” while sharing ideas, strategies, and materials with
each other, is also what this chapter and this book are about.
At about the same time this “aha” moment came, I received an outline
that was submitted by Karen Thomas, the Program Director from CIP’s
Bloomington Center, for creating just such a “community.” I liked it so
much that I decided to expand upon the topic headings of her outline
and use these categories to explore ways that educators might
embrace, then share, these ideals, strategies, and suggestions with
each other. And … from this sharing process (hopefully) “a community
of like-minded educators” would emerge.

---
## Page 687
I realized that in the end, teaching shared values and ethics to “ASD
and LD” students is all about working for the “greater good.” Creating
a “community of like-minded educators” will serve this work well.
GETTING STARTED
This handout on page 316–318 is meant to be used individually to
challenge yourself and to acknowledge all that you bring to your
profession. And … on a particularly hard or frustrating day, you can
even use it to ground yourself and return to the basics of why you do
what you do each and every day. Then … it’s meant to be passed on to

---
## Page 688
others, so that they can also use the information in it to re-energize,
gain support, and then continue the process of passing it on to help
others. This is how a community of like-minded educators will develop
within your schools and even within your communities.
Creating a Community of Like-Minded
Educators
“Begin with a growth mindset.”
Believe in your students:
Believe that they were “made for good purpose” and
that they are “inherently valuable.”
Believe that they were made this way (with brains that
are wired a bit differently) for a very good reason: to
teach us to have tolerance and respect for those with
unique and distinct differences.
Believe 
that 
they 
are 
not 
defective, 
faulty,
substandard, or flawed in any way, but that they are
special, extraordinary, distinctive, and exceptional.
Believe in yourselves:
You have to “exhibit” then “mentor” through your own
thoughts, actions, and behaviors what you are
teaching your students or it will have little value.

---
## Page 689
If you model kindness, tolerance, respect, dignity, and
a genuine concern for your fellow man, your students
will too.
How you behave during the process of teaching is
equally as important (if not more so) than what you are
teaching.
How a “positive stance” and the
“ethical assumptions” we have can
guide us:
When you come from a space of acceptance and
understanding regarding yourself, you become open
to the world and all it has to offer.
By espousing these values, you set the stage for
growth internally by demonstrating and displaying it
with your students.
If you have the willingness to try new skills and
behaviors, as well as foster growth within yourself, and
then display these to your students, they will imitate
you.
Character strengths:
Focus on your own character and character strengths;
they will ground you, guide you, and help you look at
your personal life, your work, and your world from a
“strength-based” perspective.

---
## Page 690
It’s important to remember that you have strong
assets and strengths to build upon and (because you
are human) that you also have areas that can be
improved upon. Displaying strong character strengths
will allow you to make a plan to move forward in both
areas.
Creativity:
Requires an open mind and flexibility. Students on the
Spectrum are naturally very creative – in many
idiosyncratic ways – as are their teachers, mentors,
and advisors.
As like-minded educators, your job is to make the
connections from the idiosyncrasies of creative
thinking to openness in other areas that are not as
interesting to your students. Pair these skill sets
together.
Albert Einstein (who was on the ASD Spectrum
himself) said “creativity is seeing what everyone else
has seen, and thinking what no one else has thought.”
Find this trait in yourself and then allow your students
to embrace it as you teach the mundane, the boring,
the inspiring, and the challenging.
Develop a lifelong love of learning:
You and your students live in exciting times. Neuro-
imaging and brain science experts are making great
