efforts and strides as they put forth new research and
ideas that you can use in your work with students on
the Spectrum.

---
## Page 691
Become a “lover of lifetime learning,” with a special
emphasis on deepening your own knowledge of
working with ASD and LD students in every area.
Why? Because this will improve your ability to impart
knowledge in the most effective way possible using
the latest advances in these research areas. And, this
knowledge will benefit your students.
The importance of building community
connections:
Take every opportunity you can to educate the
community regarding the needs and abilities of those
on the Spectrum.
Invite 
the 
community 
in 
– 
build 
sustainable
partnerships 
with 
colleges 
and 
community
organizations, so that they know about the work you
are doing, how they might assist and support your
students by offering opportunities for your students to
volunteer or participate in an internship program.
Transition:
Like parents, the best thing you can do for your
students is to let them go. Help the young men and
women you work with prepare for the next steps in
their lives. This might be college, volunteering, a gap
year, an internship, or paid employment.
Help your students go from external to internal self-
regulation 
so 
that 
they 
can 
work 
towards

---
## Page 692
independence with less structure from you, their
parents, and others who work with them.
Always remain student centered and student directed.
Zest:
It’s important for you to seek renewal and reset
opportunities. This might be continued professional
development, support groups (within your like-minded
community of educators), and taking care of your own
well-being through exercise and relaxation outlets.
You can only give away what you have for yourself;
and it is vital for you to continue learning and growing
in your own life – not only to set the example – but for
refueling your creative energies and personal, social,
and emotional growth as well.
Humor:
Humor CAN help you hold it all together:
Individuals on the Spectrum exhibit various kinds of
humor that can be very specific in nature. Learning
what that is can open up a new avenue of acceptance
as you work with them. For example: a mathematician
may think a play-on-words regarding dates or figures
is very funny, while many of us would not think it very
humorous. Many individuals “on the Spectrum” have
to be taught to understand the humor in certain
situations, and teachers need to look at humor from
their ASD or LD student’s point of view.

---
## Page 693
Humor can take the edge off a serious situation and
set the stage for flexibility and open the door of
willingness to try something new.
And lastly: Reach out to each other and remember
that by “building a community of like-minded
educators” you will not only have a “shoulder to lean
on,” you will be surrounded by people who will
become your biggest fans as you work day in and day
out to not only teach, but influence, and impact the
lives of your students.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Character Strengths
Each adolescent and young adult whom you work with is a unique
combination of character strengths.

---
## Page 694
Explain to your students that character strengths are the things that
bring out “the best in all of us!” Character strengths serve as pathways
for developing a life that is rewarding, good, and meaningful.
Explain to students that they will be looking at what particular
character strengths they have or would like to cultivate and develop
(there are 24 of them), and developing a list of five that are most
important to each individual.
Tell students they will be using this list later on in the chapter, and that
it will become something that they can use to guide them through all
aspects of life, work, school, family, friends, and community.
Have students create a “What’s Important to Me!” notebook or folder.
Students will need to use all the worksheets and handouts throughout
the chapter for various projects and when working in small groups. This
is a good way to ensure that everyone has what they need to use in
class and that the information will be handy.
The student handout on page 320 will help students understand the
concept of character strengths and begin the process of developing
their own list of character traits that will be used to create individual
character strength profiles later on. Do the reflection below after
students have had a chance to make their lists.
Reflection: After students have completed their “Identifying Your
Character Strengths” handout, do the following class activity. On a
white board – brainstorm all the things your students have listed on
their worksheets and try to come up with a top ten list. Generate a
discussion on why these things (the top ten), were chosen over some of
the others.
This reflection will lead right into the “Looking at Ethical Values and
Other Kinds of Values” section of this chapter and will make a nice
transition for you and your students as you start talking about values.

---
## Page 695
LOOKING AT ETHICAL VALUES AND OTHER
KINDS OF VALUES
Developing ethical values involves asking each adolescent or young
adult that you work with to take ownership of the process by creating a
set of individual and personal standards to live by.
Explain that they have already begun looking at doing this by
determining what character strengths they possess.
Tell students that being able to put an individual ego aside and develop
a strong moral fiber (or backbone) builds character and allows each
young person to focus on the wider world that exists around them.
Explain that the skills for life, school, work, and independent living that
were acquired in the preceding chapters will be more meaningful to
each of them when they are structured around a base of values and
ethics.
Tell students that the word ethical means: “having to do with standards
and knowing right from wrong.”
Explain further that having ethics means “people do the right thing.”
And that an ethical decision is the kind of decision that means making
a good choice.
Tell students that when people talk about values they are often
referring to ethical values, the type of values that mean being good and
doing the right thing. Ethical values are values that are good for
everyone.
Explain that there are also other types of values that have nothing to
do with ethics. And that it is important to be able to tell the difference
between ethical values and non-ethical values if they want to
understand goodness or doing the right thing.
The student handout on pages 321–323 that will help your student
begin to explore the differences between ethical and non-ethical values
and how these values along with their character strength profiles will

---
## Page 696
help each person determine how they wish to act or interact with
others.
Identifying Your Character Strengths
Character strengths are the positive components
that make up who you are, in other words, what’s best
in you! Your personal character strengths (the
things you display to others) will help you “do the right
thing” at work, at school, when interacting with your
family, friends, and with people in your community.
Character strengths (there are 24 of them) fall under
six broad categories:
Wisdom, 
Courage, 
Humanity, 
Justice,
Temperance, Transcendence
Here’s a list of the 24 universal character strengths:
Creativity
Perspective
Judgment
Curiosity
Honesty
Bravery
Fairness

---
## Page 697
Humor
Zest
Perseverance
Love
Teamwork
Kindness
Leadership
Social Intelligence
Love of Learning
Forgiveness
Hope
Prudence
Appreciation of Beauty and Excellence
Humility
Spirituality
Self-Regulation
Gratitude
These character strengths are valued by all people,
in all countries, all over the world. Character
strengths are the common thread that binds us
together and they transcend religion and culture.
Character strengths are universally valued and
encompass our capacities for helping ourselves and
others and produce positive effects when we express
them. Having “character” means you will live and
have an authentic life.

---
## Page 698
Knowing your character strengths and endeavoring
to cultivate and develop others is the first step towards
living a happier and more personally satisfying life.
Use the character strengths list above to check the
ones that you possess now or that you would like to
develop as you grow into your adulthood. Then go
back and circle your top five!
Looking at Values and Ethical Values
Did you know?
•
That the notion of community, in its widest
sense, is rooted in the values of each and
every one of its members and this includes
you!
•
That these developed sets of values are
reflected in the way people treat each other
and how they perceive each others’ needs.
•
Values allow us to act sympathetically towards
another person and show kindness and
compassion.
•
Values allow us to be tolerant of another
person’s religious or moral values and his or
her way of life or point of view.
•
Shared values allow us to communicate
effectively with other people while exercising

---
## Page 699
objectivity, good judgment, and respecting
truth.
•
Values are things that that just “jump out and
call to you,” and make you say: “Yes, this value
is part of me!”
•
Values are things like:
Accomplishments
Adaptability
Anticipation
Appreciation
Approachability
Art
Artistry
Balance
Boldness
Bravery
Calmness
Cleanliness
Commitment
Community
Compassion

What things are important to you?
•
List the things that are important to you – you can
write them down here or on a separate sheet of
paper. Take your time and put down everything
that you can think of.
 
 
 
Which of these things are not physical objects?
•
List those here:
 
 
 
The things on this second list are called values.
Values are personal expressions of what’s important
to you. Values are generally long-term standards or
principles that are used to judge the worth of an idea
or action. They do not work for rightness or wrongness

---
## Page 705
in society. Values are your preferred personal
principles.
Values are also attitudes:
•
This means how you state your values to others.
•
Attitudes are generally the things that you believe
in and tell others (what you say and do).
•
These attitudes (good or bad) lead us to respond
in particular ways to people and events.
Values and attitudes:
•
The values and attitudes we live by affect how
we relate to other people and they affect all of our
daily activities. That’s why it is so important to
figure out what is important to you personally and
how you will relate (by your attitude) your values
to others.
Looking at ethical values:
•
The word ethical means: “having to do with
standards and knowing right from wrong.” In
other words: Having ethics means people do right
thing. And that an ethical decision is the kind of
decision that means making a good choice.
•
When people talk about ethical values, they
mean the value (the thing that is important to
them) is also about being good and doing the
right thing. And that this also means that the
ethical values they are talking about are good for
everyone. That is why these types of values are
called ethical values.
Putting it all together:

---
## Page 706
•
If you feel some confusion about the difference
between values and ethical values, try this test.
Ask yourself: Is this value about something that
is really important to me (a non-ethical value) or
about doing the right thing (an ethical value)?
Sometimes the value is both things.
•
An attitude is how you relate to another person …
in this case, concerning their values or their
ethical values. Are you tolerant or judgmental?
*Keep this worksheet in your “What’s Important to Me” folder
or notebook; you will be using it later on!*
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Here’s a classroom activity that uses the “Looking at Values and
Ethical Values” handout called “What’s Important to You?”
Purpose: To encourage students to think about the differences
between things that are important to them in terms of values and things
that are not material things. To introduce the idea that things that are
important can also be considered ethical values.

---
## Page 707
Divide the class into small groups. Have students find out what is
important to the group as they share what is important to each one of
them as an individual. Students should follow the instructions on the
handout.
Universal Ethical Values
Tell students that the things that are important to them are important to
people all over the world. And that many of these people got together
and created a list of universal ethical values.
Explain that you’ll be going over the list together then working in small
groups on an activity using the things on the list.
The handout on pages 325–326 includes the universal ethical values
for this activity. After a general discussion about these values, divide
students into six groups. Assign each group one of the six universal
values. Have students discuss what would happen if no one practiced
their particular core or universal value. After the discussion have the
groups come together and talk about the consequences of living in a
world without trustworthiness, respect, responsibility, fairness, caring,
or citizenship.
Tell your students that they will now take their folders or notebooks and
create their very own character strengths profile and values
statement(s) or stories.
Encourage students to use multimedia and spend some time (you
might give them a week or two weeks to complete the project) thinking
about who they are and what is important to them.
Explain that this is not as scary as it sounds and that there are several
examples of how they might do this in the handout below the character
strength profiles.

---
## Page 708
What’s Important to You?
Who else shares your values? (Have your personal values list
handy!)
•
Start a discussion with each other and see if you can find
similarities in your lists of things that are important to you that
are not objects.
•
List some values you and your small working group have in
common.
•
Prepare to tell your teacher and the other small working
groups about the values your group has in common.
•
Listen carefully to the other presentations and see if all the
groups can come up with a list of four or five things that you
all have in common in terms of your personal values. Things
that you all believe are important.
•
List these top four or five things on a white board and then
compare this list with your personal list. If there are changes
or additions you want to put on your own list, feel free to do
so.
Universal Ethical Values
The six universal ethical values are:

---
## Page 709
Trustworthiness
Respect
Responsibility
Fairness
Caring
Citizenship
Using these six universal ethical values as the basis
for ethical thinking can help us detect situations
where we focus hard on upholding one value that is
important to us. An example of this might be: “We are
loyal to friends and so do not always tell the truth
about their actions.”
1. Trustworthiness:
Trustworthiness concerns a variety of behavioral
qualities that include honesty, integrity, reliability, and
loyalty.
Honesty:
•
The most fundamental ethical value is honesty.
We associate honesty with people of honor and we
admire and trust those who are honest.
•
This means we are truthful, we do not lie, and we
represent ourselves factually.
•
Honesty in conduct prohibits stealing, cheating,
fraud, and trickery.

---
## Page 710
Integrity:
•
This means that there are no differences in the way
an ethical person makes decisions from situation
to situation and no difference in the way they act at
work, at home, in public, and when alone.
•
A person of integrity takes time for self-reflection
so that the events, crises, and the things that
happen in the day-to-day do not determine the
course of their moral life. They stay in control.
Reliability:
•
When we make promises or commitments to
people this means making all reasonable efforts to
fulfill our commitments.
Loyalty:
•
Loyalty is about promoting and protecting the
interests of certain people, organizations, or
affiliations. Some relationships – husband-wife,
employer-employee, citizen-country – create an
expectation of loyalty.
•
Loyalty 
is 
about 
safeguarding 
confidential
information. Loyalty requires us to keep secrets or
information learned in confidence.
2. Respect:
Respect is about honoring the essential worth and
dignity of all people, including yourself.
•
We are morally obligated to treat everyone with
respect, regardless of who they are and what they
have done.

---
## Page 711
•
We have a responsibility to be the best we can be
in all situations, even when dealing with unpleasant
people.
•
A respectful person is a good listener and treats
others with consideration.
3. Responsibility:
Responsibility means being in charge of our choices
and therefore our lives. It means being accountable for
what we do and who we are. It also means
recognizing that what we do, and what we don’t do,
matters.
4. Fairness:
Fairness is a tricky concept. Disagreeing parties tend
to maintain that there is only one fair position – their
own. But while some situations and decisions are
clearly unfair, fairness usually refers to a range of
morally justifiable outcomes rather than discovery of
one fair answer.
•
A fair person uses open and unbiased processes
for gathering and evaluating information necessary
to make decisions.
•
Fair people do not wait for the truth to come to
them; they seek out relevant information and
conflicting perspectives before making important
decisions.
5. Caring:

---
## Page 712
Caring is the “heart” of ethics. It is not possible to be
truly ethical and not be genuinely concerned with the
welfare of others. That is because ethics is ultimately
about our responsibilities toward other people.
6. Citizenship:
The concept of citizenship includes how we ought to
behave as part of a community. The good citizen
knows the laws and obeys them – and they stay
informed on the issues of the day.
•
Citizens do more than their “fair” share to make
society work, now … and for future generations.
•
Citizenship can have many expressions, such as
conserving resources, recycling, using public
transportation, and cleaning up litter.
•
Citizenship can also mean volunteering to make
the world a better place through activities and
actions.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

creations with each other. This can be presented to the whole class or
to small groups.
Tell your students that by sharing their individual character strengths
and values with each other, they have the opportunity to shape how
others perceive them from that day forward.

wrote values and visions statements and/or stories about what is

important to them. Share these examples with your students as they
create their own character profiles.

statements/stories are as the students wrote them and
were not edited or changed in any way.*
“Meet Sawyer”
Sawyer felt his photo summed up who he is and what he values
most!

---
## Page 714
“Meet Patrick”
What Values I Want My Life to Serve
Mind
“It should be fairly obvious that I care about the mind quite a bit.
While I’m not entirely sure how much “knowledge, truth, or
clarity” I can spread, and in what areas (obviously, language,
and more particularly, how language is used in real life), but I do,
in fact, have a lot of other interests, possibly to the point of
detriment, but, provided I do in fact manage to obtain the
necessary degrees in order to become an academic, my life
would 
essentially 
be 
devoted 
largely 
to 
disseminating
knowledge.”
The Will or Conscience

---
## Page 715
“This one is possibly less obvious. I care quite a bit about
morals and justice, but am rather reserved when talking about
them, partly due to self-consciousness, and partly due to the fact
that I feel like I shouldn’t try to articulate something I don’t fully
understand (it’s a wonderful way never to make a fool out of
oneself; as I admittedly don’t know enough about ethics and
political philosophy to really talk about them on more than a
superficial level, I don’t articulate too many ethical or political
propositions around others, primarily because I am terrified that
someone who knows more than me might argue with me after
making such a proposition).”
“What might I contribute? I’m not sure, as I’m afraid of the
inherent dangers of activism (like getting arrested for
protesting), which perhaps says a lot about me (that apparently I
care about myself more than my supposed commitment towards
a more equal society, making me an enormous hypocrite – this
honestly makes me feel terrible quite often).”
Eyes and Other Senses
“I am a writer, a musician, an artist, and an aesthete. While my
aesthetic preferences are sometimes at odds with conventional
norms, I would, in fact, like there to be more beauty in the world.
That said I’m not sure how much of it I would personally
contribute.”
The Earth
“I am deeply concerned with the way that capitalism, with its
ethos of constant growth and its entirely profit-driven motives,
effects the environment. It is no coincidence, I believe, that
global warming, deforestation, the endangerment of numerous
species, and pollution only became issues after the Industrial
Revolution, and began to get exponentially worse after 1980, a
point generally regarded to be when capitalism became

---
## Page 716
globalized. The problem has only gotten exponentially worse.
We are at the point where the UN is saying that we have
essentially missed the boat in preventing the long-term effects of
global warming, and that the only hope we have to prevent what
would essentially be an ecological catastrophe is to take
immediate action.
“There will still be long-lasting damage, however, and I am
highly skeptical that corporations would do any more than the
bare minimum (enough for it not to cut into their bottom line). I
am also skeptical that today’s governments would do more than
the bare minimum.”
Body
“I am also concerned with public health. There are still millions
of Americans without health insurance (personally, I am a strong
advocate for a single-payer system), obesity rates continue to
rise (due largely to a lack of food that is both affordable and
healthy for many people, and stressful work environments that
both make people too exhausted to work out, and often afford
people very little time to exercise, even if they aren’t exhausted
five or more days a week), our mental health system is an
absolute disgrace, the over prescription of antibiotics (caused at
least in part by both the health care-as-business model and a
work culture that doesn’t allow people to take anywhere near
enough sick days, thus making many doctors desperate to
please their patient-customers, and as such will prescribe
antibiotics even if a patient-customer doesn’t need them) has
put us at an enormous risk for extremely deadly (and antibiotic-
resistant) bacteria to evolve, and the pharmaceutical industry is
primarily motivated by its bottom line, causing profits to dictate
which drugs get researched and made. The scary thing is that,
absent radical change, none of these issues are likely to get
much better.”

---
## Page 717
“Meet Cody”
My Life’s Purpose!
I want my life to serve many different values. The most important
value for me would be knowledge. The lack of knowledge is a
common companion of discrimination and human suffering. It is
because of this that Knowledge is my most valued value.
I also want my life to serve the values of beauty, love, and
compassion, and entertainment. I aspire to create video games,
which are quickly becoming a new form of art. I believe this is
because many video games in the past few years have created
worlds and characters that inspire emotions like movies, and
people look at the world a little differently after playing them.

---
## Page 718
Additionally, video games are fun and a great way to bond with
friends and family.
Your Personal Character Strengths Profile and
Values Statement/Story
Now it’s time to do something with all you have been learning
about character strengths, values, ethical values, and attitudes
about values. Something that will reflect who you are and what
is important to you.
This project will take you some time as you’ll want to think about
it and plan the format (visual, written, electronic) that you’d like
to use to create your own character strengths and values
statement(s). It’s all about what you wish to bring to yourself as
well as others (such as dreams, hopes, or awareness).
Your character strengths and values statement(s) will give
others a clear picture of your goals in life. Your personal
statement will also help guide you towards achieving goals and
objectives. It will help you stay focused and give purpose to your
individual and personal actions.
Your values statement or story can be as short or as long as you
would like to make it. As you’ll see from the examples, these
stories project a lot of the 24 character strengths that you
learned about and also display many of the universal ethical
values and non-ethical values that you have been learning
about.

---
## Page 719
Your students are making tremendous progress in this chapter. They
have explored their own character strengths, values, and ethical values.
They are now ready to start talking about the values and ethics of
others and about various Codes of Ethics.
CODES OF ETHICS
Tell your students that the universal ethical values that your class
learned about are often used to determine if a group of people that
come together for a specific purpose, such as practicing a religion,
being part of an organization, a business, a club, or another purpose,
share the same values.
When they do – they often create a Code of Ethics that the group
agrees to abide by, honor, or obey.

---
## Page 720
Explain that there are many examples of Codes of Ethics and that the
student handout on page 331, that you are going to give them has
some good examples on it. After discussing these with your students
ask them if they have any examples that they’d like to add to the list. Or
you might have students use the Internet to research additional codes
of ethics, and then bring in three or four examples each for a class or
small group discussion.
CREATING A CLASS CODE OF ETHICS
Tell students that they will now have a chance to create a class Code
of Ethics and that this is very important as it will give each student a
chance to re-examine their own values and ethics, those of their
classmates, and create a document by consensus (majority rule), that
the class agrees to implement when they are in your class.
Tell students that “Our class needs a Code of Ethics – a few shared
values that we can use to let people know what we stand for.”
Students should start by working with a partner, then in small groups
before coming to a whole class decision. Partners will present their
ideas to groups, and groups will then present to the class. Then the
class will decide on what statements will make up the actual class
Code of Ethics by majority vote.
You can help your students with this project by using a white board and
process of elimination. Allow time to brainstorm more values that the
class feels are important that are not on the combined list.
Here are three things for the class to keep in mind. A Code of Ethics
should:
1. Start off with a mission statement.
2. Be brief.
3. Be general, not detailed.

---
## Page 721
4. Be stated in positive language (we should, we believe, or we
will).
Allow students to post the class Code of Ethics in the classroom. They
might want to create a poster, illustrate it, and make it very visible!
Use the student handout on pages 332–333 to get your students
started with a sample Code of Ethics that a group of 11th graders
created for their class.
Codes of Ethics and Mission
Statements from Around the World
Codes of Ethics usually start off with a mission
statement. Here are some examples of mission
statements from various religions:
Buddhism: “I will act towards others exactly as I
would act towards myself.” Udana-Varga c. 500 BCE
Hinduism: “This is the sum of duty: Do naught to
others which if done to thee, could cause thee pain.”
From the Mahabharata c. 150 BCE
Christianity: “Treat others as you would like them to
treat you.” From Luke: Revised Standard Version of
the New Testament c. 90 CE
Judaism: “What is harmful to yourself do not do to
your fellow man. That is the whole of the law and the
remainder is but commentary.” From Hillel: The
Talmud c. 100 CE

---
## Page 722
Islam: “None of you ‘truly’ believe until he wishes for
his brothers what he wishes for himself.” A saying of
Prophet Muhammad recorded by accepted narrator
al-Bukari, c. 7 CE
*Discuss what all of these Mission Statements have
in common!*
THE TEN COMMANDMENTS OF
COMPUTER ETHICS
Thou shalt not use a computer to harm other people.
Thou shalt not interfere with other people’s computer
work.
Thou shalt not snoop around in other people’s
computer files.
Thou shalt not use a computer to steal.
Thou shalt not copy or use proprietary software for
which you have not paid.
Thou shalt not use other people’s computer resources
without authorization or proper compensation.
Thou shalt not appropriate other people’s intellectual
property or output.
Thou shalt think about the social consequences of the
program you are writing or the system you are
designing.
Thou shalt always use a computer in ways that ensure
consideration and respect for your fellow humans.

---
## Page 723
(Computer Ethics Institute, Washington D.C., 1998)
THE BOY SCOUT LAW
A scout is to be trusted.
A scout is loyal.
A scout is friendly and considerate.
A scout is a brother to all scouts.
A scout has courage in all difficulties.
A scout makes good use of his time and of his possessions.
A scout has respect for himself and others.
THE 4-WAY TEST
(The Code of Ethics for Rotary International)
Of the things we think, say, or do:
1. Is it truth?
2. Is it fair to all concerned?
3. Will it build goodwill and better friendships?
4. Will it be beneficial to all concerned?
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 724
Creating a Code of Ethics
Your class has narrowed down the values that
everyone feels are important to include in your Code
of Ethics. Now it’s time to create a class Code of
Ethics starting with your class mission statement.
Mission Statements
The first step is writing a mission statement.
A mission statement gives you and your classmates
a clear picture of your goal. It helps to guide you and
your classmates towards achieving goals and
objectives. It will help all of you stay focused and give
purpose to your individual and personal actions. Your
class mission statement should be one line.
Many businesses and organizations have clear,
concise mission statements. Here are some examples
from:
•
The Institute for Global Ethics in Camden, Maine:
“Respect for self; Respect for truth; Respect
for community; Respect for environment;
Respect for the future.”
•
The Smithsonian: “The increase and diffusion
of knowledge.”

---
## Page 725
•
The Humane Society: “Celebrating Animals,
Confronting Cruelty.”
•
The Wounded Warrior Project: “To honor and
empower wounded warriors.”
•
The Nature Conservancy: “To conserve the
lands and waters on which all life depends.”
•
The National Wildlife Federation: “Inspiring
Americans 
to 
protect 
wildlife 
for 
our
children’s future.”
•
The New York Public Library: “To inspire
lifelong learning, advance knowledge, and
strengthen our communities.”
Here’s a form to help you and your classmates create
a mission statement:
Our class/group will work together to create change by
 
(insert statement of action)
which will
 
(insert desired result)
and help others to
 
(insert desired outcome of mission statement)
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning
Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 726
Creating Your Class Code of Ethics
Keep in mind that your Code of Ethics is:
•
Several concise statements of shared and basic
values.
•
Succinct.
•
General and not detailed.
•
Stated positively (“we should,” “we will,” or “we
believe”).
•
We will always try to have respect for each other.
•
We will always try to be sympathetic towards each
other.
•
We will always try to be responsible people.
•
We will always try to be friendly towards other
people.
•
We will always try to do our best.
•
We will always try to learn to trust each other.
•
We will always try to speak to each other nicely.
•
We will always try to be helpful to each other.
•
We will always try to encourage each other.
•
We will always try to have a good attitude towards
each other.
Your class will now create a Code of Ethics!
Remember that Codes of Ethics are:
•
Statements of shared, basic values.
•
Succinct and realistic.
•
General and not detailed.

---
## Page 727
•
Stated positively (“we will,” “we should …”).
After your Code of Ethics is the way the class wants
it – there are two more steps before you all “make it
real.”
Divide into small groups.
1. Discuss what you really think of this Code of Ethics
with each other.
2. Do you think you could personally live by it?
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Rules and Laws

---
## Page 728
Now that your students have a basic understanding of values, attitudes
about values, and ethical values, it’s time to acquaint your students
with the idea that laws and rules are usually created because people
lack a sense of personal responsibility or lack values, attitudes about
values, and ethical values.
Explain that a person’s values and their ethical values usually include
obeying laws and following rules.
Explain that following rules and obeying laws means doing the right
thing. It means making a choice and then putting forth the effort to obey
others in authority. This might mean legal laws, workplace rules, school
rules, and even the rules that govern the states and countries they live
in.
The student handout below will help your students understand this
concept. Use it to start a class discussion.
Here are some classroom activities and handouts to get students
thinking about laws and rules and how these mandates impact just
about every situation your students will find themselves in from the time
they get up in the morning until the time they go to bed at night.
The first activity on page 335 is called Thinking about Rules and Laws!
This is followed by an activity entitled “Which Is It?”
Talking About Rules and Laws!
Why Do We Need the Law?
•
Almost everything we do is governed by some set of rules.
There are rules for games, for social clubs, for sports, for
children, for teenagers, and for adults in the workplace.

---
## Page 729
•
There are also rules imposed by morality and custom that
play an important role in telling us what we should and
should not do.
•
Some rules – those made by the government, state, or the
courts – are called laws. Laws resemble rules of morality
because they are designed to control or change our
behavior. Unlike rules of morality, laws are enforced by the
courts; if you break a law, whether you like that law or not,
you may be forced to pay a fine, pay damages, do court
mandated community service, or go to prison.
Why Are Some Rules So Special that They
Are Made into Laws?
Because some rules have a consequence if they are not
observed or obeyed that makes them a law instead of a rule!
If we did not live in a structured society with other people, laws
would not be necessary. We would simply do as we please, with
little regard for others.
•
Ever since individuals began to associate with other people –
to live in society – laws have been the glue that has kept
society together.
•
We need laws to ensure a safe and peaceful society in which
individuals’ rights are respected.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

---
## Page 730
Thinking about Rules and Laws!
Now that you have learned some key concepts about
rules and laws it is time to think about the following
questions:
•
Why do we have rules and laws?
•
What do you think would happen if we didn’t have
rules and laws any more?
•
Do you think there were as many rules and laws
long ago as there are now?
Which is it?
We do some things because we know there is a rule
we have to follow. There are other things we do just
because it’s right, even if there is not a rule or law
written down about it.
In small working groups read each example below
and discuss then decide whether it’s a real rule or
something you do just because you should.
Sometimes it’s both!
In some of the examples the word “should” might be
replaced with the words “want to.”
If we make the choice to do something right, ideally it
is because we want to, not just because we should.

---
## Page 731
The school principal announces that all the unclaimed
lost and found items for the year are in the gym and up
for grabs. When you are looking over the items, you
spot a jacket that you really like. However, you realize
the jacket belongs to a boy you know from another
school. He must have left it in the gym when he visited
your school to watch a basketball game. You could
keep the jacket, but you decide to return it to him.
Why?
•
It’s a rule
•
You think you “should”
•
Both
You have permission to use the class computer for
another 15 minutes. However you know another
classmate needs extra time on the computer in order
to finish an assignment. You decide to let your
classmate use the rest of your computer time. Why?
•
It’s a rule
•
You think you “should”
•
Both
You are heading for the school cafeteria and you are
really hungry. You feel the urge to run down the hall
but you decide to walk instead. Why?
•
It’s a rule
•
You think you “should”
•
Both
You are playing a game of volley ball in the park when
you notice a shy classmate who is standing nearby
watching you and your friends play. You know this

---
## Page 732
classmate doesn’t have many friends and would like to
be included more. You decide to ask her if she wants
to play. Why?
•
It’s a rule
•
You think you “should”
•
Both
You’re meeting some friends at a local coffee shop.
When the bill comes you notice that you have been
charged for a regular coffee and not the fancy latte
with mocha that you ordered. You decide to point it out
to the person at the cash register even though your
friends think you should only pay for what is written
down on your bill. Why?
•
It’s a rule
•
You think you “should”
•
Both
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
RULES, LAWS, THE POLICE, AND THOSE ON
THE AUTISM SPECTRUM!
Explain to your students that young adults with an ASD or LD
diagnosis aged 14–30 are seven times more likely to have an
encounter with the police than someone who is not on the Autism
Spectrum. Therefore, it is extremely important that your students know
how to respond to the police, their authority, and to their requests or
demands.
This means:

---
## Page 733
•
Keeping his or her hands visible at all times.
•
Doing exactly what the officer(s) tells him or her to do (turn
around, stand up, face the wall, etc.).
•
Producing ID or allowing the officer to reach into his or her
pocket to get it.
•
Remaining calm and doing exactly what is being requested,
even if this means going to the police station or getting into a
police vehicle.
Tell students that police are being trained, all over the United States
and in other countries, to understand how to approach and work with
young adults on the Autism Spectrum, aged 14 and up when they need
to stop them, or ask them questions, or even arrest them.
Explain further that someone on the ASD or LD Spectrum might be
tempted to:
•
Run.
•
Fight.
•
Shut down.
•
Melt down.
•
Talk to the police like they have seen others (like actors or an
animated character) do in a movie.
Tell students that each person in the class will create a safety plan
(using their own research and the handout below), and that they can
make several copies of this plan and refer to it should the police or any
law enforcement official, first responder, or other person in authority
need to interact with them in any way.
Explain that students will also break into small groups and role play
scenarios during which they would need to use the information they
have documented.

---
## Page 734
Sample disclosure handout (left) and disclosure card (right).
My Safety Plan for Dealing with the
Police or Others in Authority
When you are out in your community you might find
yourself in a situation that requires you to interact with,
talk to, and/or be questioned by law enforcement
professionals or others in authority. These officers or
officials may not have knowledge or prior training on
how to deal effectively with individuals or people with
Autism Spectrum Disorders (ASD) or on the Learning
Differences (LD) Spectrum.
The following materials will help you develop a
personal handout and a disclosure card. You will also
learn the skills necessary and the resiliency needed to

---
## Page 735
appropriately 
disclose 
your 
need 
for 
an
accommodation or accommodations.
This information can be used to help you interact with
anyone in authority such as: the police or other law
enforcement 
officials, 
security 
guards, 
campus
security, customs and immigration employees, and
first responders (such as fire or rescue personnel and
paramedics). This information will also be useful if you
need to go to a hospital emergency room or find
yourself being detained by an authority official for any
reason.
The first thing you need to do is tell someone you are
on the ASD or LD Spectrum and show them your
handout (disclosure) card or information materials.
Then explain (calmly) that you need to have someone
notified who can explain your diagnosis and your
needs to them.
Disclosure Tools and Options
What’s the best tool to use when you decide to
disclose your diagnosis to a police officer and any
other type of authority official? A handout card!
Develop a handout card (perhaps your class or group
can do this together) that can be easily copied and
laminated. Remember that the handout card is
replaceable. You can give it away to any officer or
official who is on the scene. Carry several at all times.
The handout card will be specific to you. This
card will have:

---
## Page 736
•
Your name, your age, and your diagnosis on it
and if possible, your photo.
•
The names of people the officer or official can
call.
•
The name of your doctor, therapist, or
someone “official” who can tell the officer or
official that the information on the card is true
and verify your diagnosis.
•
Instructions that “one or all” of the people on
the card should be called immediately.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
My Safety Plan for Dealing with the
Police or Others in Authority
What’s the best way to tell the officer that you
have a handout?
•
Avoid making sudden movements to reach for the
handout card.
•
Obtain permission or signal your intention of
handing the officer your “disclosure card” before
reaching into your coat or pants pockets,
backpack, handbag, or into glove compartments of
vehicles to obtain it.

---
## Page 737
•
If you can, verbally let the officer know that you
have Autism, Asperger’s Syndrome or state your
specific diagnosis.
•
Tell the officer or official that you have an
information (disclosure/handout) card for him or
her to read and would like to give it to him or her.
•
If nonverbal, or if sudden interactions render you
nonverbal or mute, consider using a medical alert
bracelet for an officer or official to read that alerts
the person detaining you to your condition.
Disclosure to a police officer, authority figure, or
official:
•
The decision to disclose your diagnosis to a police
officer or other type of official will always be yours
to make. If you have learned through experience
that disclosure would be helpful in the particular
situation, you may decide to disclose to a police
officer or someone in authority.
•
Law enforcement officers, first responders, and
other officials report that they make their best
decisions when they have the best information. By
giving them your handout (disclosure) card you will
be giving them the information that will help them …
help you.
•
A good, strong diagnosis disclosure that includes
the use of an information card and contact
information for an objective professional to talk to
them about you is one of the best ways to let
others know you need an accommodation because
of your diagnosis.
Plan and practice disclosure techniques:

---
## Page 738
Plan and then role play with another person (at home
or in school) “telling someone” (a person in authority)
that you are “on the Spectrum,” what your diagnosis
is, and that you want to give him or her your
disclosure/information/advocacy card.
Further suggestions to consider during “sudden
interactions” with the police or officials in
authority:
•
Do not attempt to run away or flee.
•
Do not make sudden movements.
•
Try to remain calm.
•
Ask permission to give them your advocacy and
disclosure/information card or handout.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
WHAT DOES IT MEAN TO BE
ACCOUNTABLE?
Explain to your students that all of the skills for life, school, work, and
independent living they have been obtaining will not help them become
successful at their jobs, in relationships, and in their lives in general, if
they do not hold themselves accountable for their actions.
Tell them that it’s all well and good to have these life skills, but it is the
actions – that accompany using the skills that they have obtained – that
will hold them accountable.
The student handout that follows will help you explain this concept. Tell
your students that it might be looked at as sort of an “Accountability
Code ofEthics.”

---
## Page 739
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Your students will now delve into “Rights and
Responsibilities.” This section leads quite naturally into the next
concept: Looking at what makes an “Ideal Citizen,” and from there, to
discovering the vital elements of the student’s surrounding community
and how the citizens of these communities contribute to the
individual’s surrounding environment.
It is my hope that you will encourage your students to take the
concepts in the “Ideal Citizen” activity and add art, theatre, drama,
poetry, and creative writing to create something they might share with
others. This might be presenting it to other classes, taking it into an
elementary school or nearby middle or high school, creating an after
school program, or even a presentation for parents and families.
This, coupled with presentations of their “character strength profiles”
and their values statements and/or stories, is a powerful message they
might deliver to others in their surrounding communities.
You can use the many examples in this chapter to illustrate this
concept. Explain that Michael, Ellen, Patrick, and Rachel had their
profiles and stories published in this Teaching Toolkit. Explain that
their thoughts, words, and beliefs will now be read by thousands if not
tens of thousands of people, and that anything is possible.
This is a great opportunity for your students to think about using the
“Ideal Citizen” project and other activities from this chapter to help and
inform others.
Accountability
An “accountable” person is not a victim and doesn’t shift
blame or claim credit for the work of others. People “who hold
themselves accountable” have the following qualities and
traits.

---
## Page 740
They pursue excellence:
•
The pursuit of excellence has an ethical dimension when
others rely upon our knowledge, ability, or willingness to
perform tasks safely and effectively. They hold us
accountable for our actions.
They are responsible:
•
Responsible people are reliable, careful, prepared, and
informed.
•
Responsible people finish what they start – overcoming
rather than surrendering – to obstacles and excuses.
•
Responsible people look for ways to do their work better.
•
Responsible people use self-restraint.
•
Responsible 
people 
exercise 
self-control, 
restraining
passions and appetites such as lust, hatred, gluttony, greed,
and fear. They delay gratification and never feel it’s
necessary to “win at any cost.”
This is what it means to hold yourself “accountable for
your actions.”
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
RIGHTS AND RESPONSIBILITIES
Explain to your students that as citizens of a democratic society, we all
have certain rights as well as responsibilities.
Divide students into small groups and have them look at the two lists
below then work with their groups to decide:
Which two rights does your group value most?

---
## Page 741
Why?
Which two rights does your group take for granted more than
others?
Why?
Then ask the groups to select two responsibilities. Have the groups
discuss and prepare their answers to this question:
In what way do you fulfill these responsibilities?
The last part of the activity is asking students to add at least two
things (they may add more if they like) to the rights list and to the
responsibilities list.
Each group will present the results of their group discussion and their
additions to the two lists to the rest of the class. It should make for a
lively discussion!

---
## Page 742
Rights and Responsibilities
Rights
Responsibilities
Life.
To respect human life.
Liberty.
To respect the rights of others.
Dignity.
To be tolerant.
Security.
To be honest.
Equality of opportunity.
To be compassionate and
to demonstrate self-control.
Justice.
To participate in the democratic process.
Privacy.
To work for the common good.
Private ownership of property.
To respect the property of others.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
THE IDEAL CITIZEN ACTIVITY
Explain to students that they are going to take large sheets of paper
(either rolls of brown wrapping paper or flip chart paper taped together)
and trace each other’s bodies. They will use this drawing to create an
“Ideal Citizen.”
Explain that they are drawing a cursory outline of a “he/she” – that
gender is not important – it is what is inside the person that counts.
Ask students to think about:
What might give their “he/she” its humanity – its sense of self-worth?
Ask students to illustrate their “he/she” in a way that they think makes
up an “Ideal Citizen.” Here are some examples to list on a white
board:

---
## Page 743
•
Drawing a heart to show a person cares.
•
Hands to show compassion by giving a pat on the back or a hug.
•
A brain that thinks logically about what is right and what is
wrong.
•
A “shoulder to lean on.”
And so on … anything students can come up with will be correct. There
are no right or wrong answers or right or wrong way of creating an
“Ideal Citizen.”

---
## Page 744
CIP staff members creating an Ideal Citizen sample.
A few more suggestions:
Students might cut words and images from magazines, add
photographs, or parts of photographs, use news article headlines and
words from newspapers to individualize their “Ideal Citizens” so that
their “he/she” represents all that they value and hold dear if this is a
direction they want their project to take.
Cultures can be represented by using the “he/she” to look at what an
“Ideal Citizen” might look like in another country.
Students can use the “he/she” to think about what an “Ideal Citizen”
living in an inner city might look like vs. one who lives in a rural area.
They can then discuss commonalities and/or differences and if they
think the perceptions revealed are realistic or true or just that a
perception.
They might split the “he/she” down the middle to portray an inner city
“he/she” and a rural “he/she.” Or use the split person to compare
themselves to someone living on the other side of the world (after
researching realistically what the differences might be).

---
## Page 745
Now What Happens?
•
Students can hang their creations in the classroom, around the
school, or wherever people can see them.
•
They might line a hallway that people pass by often or decorate
the walls of the school cafeteria.
•
They can adapt the activity and draw their “he/she” outlines on a
painted wall and make a mural of all the things that make up an
ideal citizen.
•
They might line up a row of “he/she”’s to liven up a wall on the
school campus or in a community center or even in an
elementary school to get younger students thinking about “Ideal
Citizens.”
•
They can think about taking the activity to an “assisted living
home” and have residents create “Ideal Citizens” based on their
accumulated wisdom. It would be interesting to have the
students bring their “citizen” or one or two examples and
compare them to what the older folks have created.
ADAPTATIONS FOR THE IDEAL CITIZEN
ACTIVITY
Here are some adaptations for “The Ideal Citizen Activity” if making
life-sized versions is not possible:

---
## Page 746
•
Students might consider drawing each other from the waist up
or make “mini versions” using a template of a body outline that
they’ve drawn on a smaller scale.
•
This activity can easily be adapted to portray altered states. For
instance they might take an “Ideal Citizen” and next to it draw
and illustrate what happens after taking drugs or too much
alcohol.
•
Dramatic presentations about “labeling people,” “lack of self-
respect,” “lack of self-esteem,” etc., make good assemblies,
video and play productions, and these modalities can be used to
expand on values and ethics discussions.
•
Students can accompany their “Ideal Citizen” with a poem, short
story, and/or additional artwork – perhaps putting their life-size
or template version on a world map or a drawing of a country or
world.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: This is a powerful activity and it will serve as
an introduction for getting your students to really think about their
wider communities and the role they will play as “Ideal Citizens” now
and in the future.
This activity is also a natural “lead in” for beginning to look at then
creating a class community service-learning project. Community
service-learning projects are a good way to help students on the
“ASD/LD Spectrum” enter their communities and provide a solution to
a problem the class has decided to work on. Service-learning
provides a tangible means of helping students integrate into their
wider communities with support from their teachers, advisors,
mentors, counselors, and their peers. It allows students to contribute
in a real and authentic way, while gaining the confidence and ability to
do so.
MAKING A DIFFERENCE IN THE WORLD

---
## Page 747
Write on a white board: Do you think you can make a “real” difference
in the world?
Divide students into small groups and let them brainstorm – on large
pieces of flip chart paper – how they feel about this statement. Have
groups share their conclusions with the class.
Interact with students as much as you can while they are doing this
activity. Take the role of the encourager and be supportive of what they
are trying to accomplish. You might give examples from your own life
about how you felt about this statement when you were in high school.
Explain to students that it can be scary to become part of their wider
community and get involved in projects that may fall outside their own
comfort zones.
Explain that your class is going to try to figure out a way to explore the
various elements in their communities in a way that is comfortable and
that will allow each person in the class to make a “real” difference.
Explain that sometimes these fears encompass these factors:
•
We are too young.
•
We won’t be taken seriously.
•
It will be too hard.
•
We won’t know what to do.
•
The problem is too big for us to make a difference.
Divide students into small groups. Ask students to write a positive
response to each fear or concern from the list. Write these on a white
board or flip chart.
Then bring the groups together and list all of the positive responses
next to each fear. It should be an “aha” moment for most of them. An
affirmation that they are not too young, that they will be taken seriously,
it will not be as hard as they think it will be.
Explain that a good place to start making a “real” difference is by
creating a service-learning project that you can work on together as

---
## Page 748
a class. A project that will have an impact on their school, their town, or
their wider community.
MAKING A DIFFERENCE/SERVICE-LEARNING
Explain that the word service-learning means just that. The service
part is a project – a solution to a problem, or providing a specific
service that will be used to help others. The learning part – is working
in cooperation with peers and supportive adults to complete or deliver
the project.
Explain that what usually happens is each student on the project team
learns a lot of new skills and how to communicate effectively with others
on the project and … they add to their own fund of knowledge while
helping others.
Tell students that this is how adolescents and young adults start to
make a “real” difference in school, their town, their local communities.
By meeting a need, they get a chance to exercise and implement their
own values, ethical values, and character strengths to help others.
The student handout on page 344 will help students understand
what service-learning is all about. This handout is followed by additional
materials and “how to’s.” These handouts and worksheets should be
kept in a separate folder entitled “Service-Learning Community
Project.” Students will also keep their personal writing journaling, and
documentation of their project in this folder or notebook.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: It’s time to let your students take the lead, form
their own working groups, investigate their local communities,
brainstorm, argue, and finally reach consensus for a service-learning
project. They have worked hard on their skills for life, school, work,
and independent living and are ready for this challenge.
In the beginning you will feel like you are walking behind “toddlers on
wobbly legs.” However, as you facilitate (rather than teach) each
phase of the process, that leads to the creation and implementation of
a community service-learning project, I promise that those toddlers

---
## Page 749
will become confident young adults who will have made a “real”
difference, in a student led and meaningful way. Let the service-
learning begin!
What Is Service-Learning?
It’s a great way for you and your classmates to make a “real”
difference in your communities.
By participating in a service-learning project you will have the
opportunity to decide upon and act on something that needs
doing to “improve life or lives” in your school or your local
community.
Service-learning integrates meaningful community service with
instruction and reflection to enrich the learning experience, teach
civic responsibility, and strengthen communities. You and your
classmates will get to be part of this.
Through service-learning you will be joining thousands of other
young people who perform service-learning projects in their
communities every day.
You’ll develop new ways of working in teams and fresh
approaches to gathering information and making decisions. And
… you will grow and develop personally through interactions with
others and by learning new skills.
Service-learning is a process of involving students in
community service activities combined with facilitated means for
applying the experience to their academic and personal
development. It is a form of experiential education aimed at
enhancing and enriching student learning in course.

---
## Page 750
And the best part is … it will make all you have been learning
throughout your high school or college years relevant!
All service-learning projects have five elements:
1. Preflection – thinking about what you might do.
2. Planning and preparation.
3. Action.
4. Documentation/reflection (telling others and then recording
your own thoughts about the project).
5. Celebration.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
How Can My Class Get Started?

---
## Page 751
You start by looking at vital elements of your
community. Here’s an activity to do as you begin
thinking about how you might do this:
•
In small groups of five or six people, brainstorm
this question:
What do you think the word community means?
•
On large pieces of flip chart paper write or draw
out your group’s thoughts and answers then
present all your cumulative ideas to the rest of your
class. Be sure to listen carefully as the other
groups present their thoughts.
Now, as a whole class, discuss the different types of
communities and describe them! Write a summary
statement here to remind you of what the class came
up with.
 
 
 
 
You know from this activity, and the ensuing
discussions, that a community can vary from a school,
to a neighborhood, a town, a city, a country, or
encompass the world.
As a class, use “majority rule or democratic process”
to decide on a geographical area of the community
you will be looking at for a potential service-learning
project.
Write your decision here:
“Our class has decided that the community we will be
working in will include:

---
## Page 752
  .”
Now it’s time to investigate this named community.
There are many ways to find out information about the
community that will be the focus of your service-
learning project.
Think about the community you are going to develop a
project for and what you know about it through the
media, TV, newspapers, and/or the Internet.
Now it’s time to: Take a walk (with your class or your
small working group) around your school, your
neighborhood, or your town and look … really look at
the community you will serve.
Read local newspapers, watch local television news
reports, listen to the radio, and surf the Internet. Cut
out articles of things that apply to all the topics your
class is interested in looking at. This can be done in-
class or on your own as homework.
Write summaries of things you think the class might be
interested in pursuing. Or … do a visual representation
using art concepts or electronic media.
You’ll be using this information to classify the needs
and assets (good things) of the community you’ll be
working in.
Then … come together with your class and work in
groups of four or five students to list needs and assets
that your research and the research of your
classmates have uncovered. You can use the form
below, write on flip chart paper, or a white board to
record your ideas. Whatever method works for you or
your group is fine.

---
## Page 753
Now it’s time to start investigating potential service-
learning projects. Your class can do this by:
•
Talking to your friends, parents, teachers, and
other people you come into contact with.
•
By going out with your working group of five or six
students and mapping your area. For instance if
there is an elementary school and it looks run
down – you might create a project to landscape it,
plant a community garden, or paint murals to
brighten things up.
•
If there is a park nearby – is it filled with litter, do
people use it, how might your group make it
better?
•
Is there a senior citizens’ home or assisted living
facilities near your school? You might create a
program to bring art, music, or a specific project –
like creating a shared garden (maybe a butterfly
garden), or helping residents with household
repairs. You might build something together that
the facility could use: like picnic tables or a fire pit
to make a spot where people could be outside all
year round.
•
You might visit local agencies and find out if day
care centers, homeless shelters, or soup kitchens

---
## Page 754
and food banks could use your group’s help.
•
Find out when your town’s “make a difference day”
is and develop a project to be completed on this
day. Research “make a difference day” on the
Internet and you will see thousands of projects and
ways you and your group can “make a difference.”
•
Set up a meeting with town officials, a newspaper
editor, or local business owners and find out what
their roles are in the community. These people are
valuable resources and can become partners with
you when you do your project. For instance: If you
are painting a mural at a local elementary school or
community building and there is a pizza or
hamburger shop nearby, you might partner with
them to provide drinks and lunch. If there is a paint
store nearby – you might ask for donations, and so
on.
Ok … Now What?
Now you will decide what your team (the five or six
people you’ve been working with) will investigate and
write that down here:
My team’s issue or need to investigate is:
 
 
My team members are:
 
 
Now do the research so your team can decide on what
your agreed upon service-learning project will be!

Here’s a form to help you record your personal

for the class to address and write it down here!
“Our class service-learning project will be:

Your mission statement will help guide you and your

## Page 759

Bringing your service-learning project to a close:
Celebration! You’ve worked hard and your efforts
should be recognized. As a group or class, you will
now plan a way of celebrating the service you have
provided to meet a community need.
You might think about ways to include or involve the
recipients of your service-learning project and your
community partners.
Record your ideas and be ready to share them with
your teachers, advisors, mentors, counselors, and
anyone who helped make your class community
service-learning project a reality.
Most importantly … have fun! You and your
classmates deserve it.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Reflection!
Now that you have completed your service-learning project you need to
do a final reflection. This is your chance to think about the fact that you
and your classmates made a “real” difference in your community. That
you were not too young and that many people took you seriously. They
heard “your” voice and allowed “you” to prove to others that the

---
## Page 760
character strengths you possess, the values you hold important, and
what you personally bring to the world are valid and important.
Your final reflection will be a visual image, a story, or a poem about
your personal service-learning experience. Feel free to use pictures,
symbols, words, or dialogue.
It’s up to you whether or not you want to share this with others. The
purpose is for you to be able to end your project by reflecting on your
personal journey and then experiencing closure. Some people will
share this with the class, others will choose to keep their final reflection
private. It is completely your choice.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: This chapter answers the question “Can I
really make a difference in the world?” Students on the “ASD and LD
Spectrum” have an especially hard time believing this. This chapter
will help your students realize that they can not only make a difference
but how to go about it and to do so with a firm belief in their own
values and ethics, as well as their “life skills.”

“show us in very certain terms” that the answer to the question is a
resounding “Yes, I can make a real difference in the world.”
Share these profiles and messages from these young men and women

NOTES:
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Making It Real
WHAT?
Teaching and showing adolescents and young adults on the
ASD or LD Spectrum how to identify the values and ethics
that are important to each individual while learning tolerance
for differences in others.
Showing 
students 
how 
to 
cultivate 
shared 
values,
communicate effectively with each other, exercise objectivity,
judgment, and respect truth.

---
## Page 766
This chapter is the glue that binds the abilities each
individual has acquired in the four competency chapters:
Skills for life, work, school, and independent living …
together.
HOW?
The purpose of this chapter is to get the young men and
women you work with to explore the values they feel are
important and that they would like to adopt by asking them to
look at:
1. Character: What qualities do they possess or want to
develop in themselves?
2. Values: What are the important values that will guide their
lives?
3. Ethics: Why is doing the right thing and making good choices
important?
BENEFITS:
Shared values are key to having each person understand
how people treat each other, how they perceive each others’
needs, and how they behave sympathetically towards their
parents, peers, and those in the wider community. Shared
values allow people to communicate effectively with each
other, exercise objectivity, judgment, and respect truth.
Merging these competencies with the character traits,
values, and ethics that each student will explore and
endeavor to adopt, will allow each individual to become self-
reliant, self-respecting, self-confident, and self-disciplined.
Values allow each student to begin to integrate into his or her
wider community and show others that he or she can make a
difference in a positive, meaningful, and worthwhile way.

---
## Page 767
Awareness, Peer Support, and
Conflict Resolution

GETTING STARTED: AWARENESS
SUPPORTING EACH OTHER
GETTING STARTED: CREATING A PEER MEDIATION PROGRAM
