BECOMING A “CHANGE MASTER”

---
## Page 768
Awareness, Peer Support, and
Conflict Resolution
“Life is constantly changing and each
moment we are transitioning to the
next. When we accept that and get into
the flow of it, life is so much easier.”
“There are no mistakes, only
approximations to the right answer. So
keep trying new things and keep
refining your skills.”
“Who you are and how you are being
with people is more important than
what you are saying.”
“Your time on earth is limited, so use it
wisely. Be in your own life fully and set
your own path and follow it. Don’t let
the rigid thinking and dogma of other
people over-ride your dream. Be true to
your vision and what you truly are;
everything else is there to support you.”

---
## Page 769
Michael P. McManmon
Chapter 8 gives educators, clinicians, and the support professionals
who work with adolescents and young adults on the ASD or LD
Spectrum information, teaching tools, techniques, and materials to
raise awareness and then address conflict such as bullying,
harassment, teasing, discrimination, and verbal or physical assault.
It has been proven throughout the United States and the world, that
oftentimes, the best way to raise awareness, provide assistance, and
then resolution to conflict at school and in the workplace in general, is
through peer listening, peer support groups, and an actual peer
mediation process.
Having young adults address these issues directly with peers (who are
trained and supported by their teachers, support professionals, and
school or workplace administrators) often turns out to be the most
effective way to lessen conflict at school or at work.
It does not matter whether the conflict originates in school, workplace,
at home, or in local communities. Providing a structure (in the learning
and working environment) where young adults, especially those on the
Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and Learning Differences Spectrum
(LD), can (a) know the signs of conflict; (b) be able to tell someone; (c)
have an action taken to stop the conflict; and (d) find a peaceful
resolution to the conflict is extremely important.
Encouraging young people to be part of the awareness campaign,
solution, and resolution is what Chapter 8: Awareness, Peer Support,
and Conflict Resolution is all about.
This chapter begins with awareness of conflict then moves on to
address conflict and lastly, offers options and ways for resolving
conflict through peer support.
It then defines the various ways young people might seek assistance
from peers (if these programs are in existence), or help their schools

---
## Page 770
create programs that address conflict through peer listening, peer
support groups, and peer mediation.
In some cases, if the conflict at school between two young people is
direct and resulted in a specific incident, the victim and the perpetrator
can be required by school administrators to undergo the peer mediation
process (they can also request mediation), to resolve the problem. This
process allows a peer mediator (who has been trained) to moderate the
meeting, listen to all parties involved, discuss, and ultimately, help
participants find a peaceful solution that all parties sign off and agree to
uphold.
Schools that have implemented peer support or mediation programs
have found that this is a viable solution to conflict that is not violent, or
physically or emotionally dangerous to the parties involved. Peer
support reduces bullying, discrimination, harassment, arguing, and
violations of the right to attend school in an atmosphere of dignity and
respect.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: This chapter will focus on showing you (as
teachers and professional support staff) and the young men and
women you work with how peer support can help schools create
safe, harmonious, and peaceful environments for learning. This is
achieved when young people help each other solve and resolve
conflict.
Experience and research has shown us that young people who have
been enabled to take a responsible role in partnership with each other
regarding rules and behavior deal with problems more effectively than
if adults were mandating these expected, requested, or required
behaviors.
Who better to understand bullying, peer pressure, sexual pressure,
harassment, discrimination, and aggressive behavior than the young
people themselves? This chapter will illustrate that young people
“can” and “do” offer the best solutions to conflict resolution by giving
each other “a shoulder to lean on.”

---
## Page 771
If your school has a program like this in place, use the activities and
exercises in the book to revisit the causes of conflict in school by
having young people work through the materials and activities in the
chapter. These activities will be a good lead in for talking about the
specific things your school’s peer support programs offer.
If your students are volunteering, doing an internship, working or
attending college – have them do some research to see what peer
mediation programs or other avenues of mediation are offered at work
or on campus. Knowing what is available and utilizing these services
when needed is all part of “becoming aware.”
I was recently flying to Melbourne, Florida and while waiting in a line
at the airport I got to talking to a young couple (in their early twenties).
Believe it or not, when I talked about the Toolkit (much to my
surprise), the man asked me if I had any advice for him as he was
being “bullied” at work by a co-worker. He was pretty sure his
workplace used a mediation process that had a trained Human
Resource (HR) person (not necessarily a peer) conducting it, but he
was also very fearful about speaking up about the matter.
We talked about some options and he decided that he would
approach the HR with a co-worker who had been a supportive
listener. He would also ask two other peers, who were also being
harassed by this same employee, to accompany him and his
supportive peer/co-worker. The young man looked relieved and said
that it was good to talk about this to someone who was writing about
“these things.” He thanked me for listening and for helping him come
up with a viable plan that might work and went to catch his plane.
AWARENESS
It’s important for you (as educators, mentors, therapists, advisors, and
support professionals) to recognize the importance of teaching your
students skills that are not necessarily met through traditional
academic studies. One of the best ways to teach the adolescents and

---
## Page 772
emerging adults you work with how to recognize bullying, taunting,
teasing, or anything that causes them fear, or physical and emotional
pain is to let them know that by law, and by the UN Rights of the Child,
they have a right to live without these fears.
Talking about rights in your class and then talking about strategies for
dealing with some rights violations via peer listening, peer support
groups, and peer mediation is an effective way to raise awareness of
these issues at school. It will also stress the importance of having these
young men and women seek assistance in a safe and confidential way.
Young people who are reluctant to “tell” a teacher or an administrator
about bullying, harassment, and personal or interpersonal problems
may find it more comfortable and viable to “tell” an identified peer
supporter.
Peer mediation and peer support groups have been instrumental in
changing bullying statistics and creating schools where dignity, respect,
and students’ rights are taken seriously. This is because it starts with
the problem – the students themselves. These same statistics show
that many bullies have turned into effective peer mediators through this
process. And it all started with … awareness.
As stated earlier in this book – parents of adolescents and young adults
on the ASD and LD Spectrum report that 65% of their children have
been bullied, taunted, harassed, ostracized, or “made to live in fear” at
school. Teaching awareness about these statistics then showing
students various avenues for seeking assistance and resolution to this
population is just as important as teaching academics and skills for life,
school, work and independent living.
GETTING STARTED: AWARENESS
Explain to the young men and women whom you work with that they
will be learning how to communicate effectively with others when
conflict arises and how to be assertive (within acceptable guidelines
and norms of society), and that they will be learning the importance of

---
## Page 773
respect for oneself and for others, as well as the importance of
cooperation.
Explain that you will be doing activities in class to raise awareness of
problems that can arise at school and talking about programs that exist
in your school (if they do), or that might be proposed to school
administrators and then implemented, that will show students how to
help each other when problems arise.
Tell your students that many times when problems occur such as
bullying, friendship issues, squabbling, arguing, and interpersonal or
personal problems, it is easier or more comfortable to “tell” a trained
peer support person than it is to “tell” a teacher or administrator.
Explain that this is why these programs were created.
Explain that some of the students in your class or group will be glad to
know how to recognize bullying, harassment, taunting, teasing, and
other forms of fear and have this awareness.
Explain that:
•
Some students will use the information you are giving them to
report their personal experiences of conflict to others and some
will take this knowledge to help others.
•
Some students will want to become part of the solution while
others in the class will experience an “aha” moment and be
greatly relieved that they have received information about ways
to handle a situation they might be in.
•
And lastly, that some students in the class will now use this
information to take advantage of any peer support programs
offered at your school and benefit from it.
•
Some may want to work with you (if programs like this are not
offered at your school) to see if this is something your school can
implement.
Tell students that the overall and far-reaching outcome of the
information you will be giving them is to understand that they have

---
## Page 774
rights by law and are entitled to live in an atmosphere of dignity and
respect.
Discuss the “rights” described in the handout on page 359 and let
students know that they are important to know … and that they will be
learning about things they can do if they feel their rights are violated.
CREATING SAFER SPACES
The poem on page 360 starts a subsection that will focus on creating
safer spaces in school, at home, and in the communities the students
live in.
Divide the young people you work with into small groups of 4-6 people.
Give each group a copy of the poem to be used after setting the scene
(verbally) for the activity.
Ask the small groups to discuss the poem and form their own opinions.
Bring everyone who participated in the activity together. This activity is
powerful and emotional and especially effective when it is used as a
springboard to discuss homophobic, immigrant, and racist bullying and
what causes it after the poem has been discussed in small groups.
Did You Know?
•
Did you know that on November 20, 1989 a group of
people from all over the world came together for the
sole purpose of creating a document that would inform
anyone under the age of 18 of their rights? It is called:
The “UN Rights of the Child.” This protects children

---
## Page 775
from the time they are born until they reach age 18.
Then … other laws take over and protect adults.
•
You can look this document up on the Internet; it has
42 children’s rights in it – but for the purposes of the
work you are doing with your class, this handout will
list the ones that might affect you or someone you
know, before, during, or after school.
You have a right to:
•
Express your views (even if they are very
different from your peers or the adults around
you), and have that opinion taken seriously.
•
To attend school, engage in leisure activities,
and live your daily life “without fear, harassment,
or someone hurting you (physically or verbally).”
•
Be 
treated 
with 
dignity 
throughout 
your
childhood (ages 0–18) so that you can become
self-reliant, self-assured, and self-confident while
participating in a full and active life in society.
•
An education that prepares you for an active,
responsible, life as an adult.
•
To be informed of these rights.
So now you know your rights! “Knowing” is also called or
referred to as “awareness.” You will be learning what to do,
who might help you, and how to tell someone if your rights are
being violated. You will also learn ways to solve problems at
school, At some point, you may want to volunteer to learn
how to be trained to help your classmates resolve conflicts
that occur at school or that happen to them in other places,
but affect them at school. This might be something like:
•
Being bullied while walking to or from school.
•
Being taunted or teased on the school bus.

---
## Page 776
•
Being threatened during lunch in the school
cafeteria.
•
Fighting, squabbling, arguing, or being yelled at
before, during, or after school.
These laws and “rights” apply to you when you are in school,
in your home, in your community, and if you have a job,
internship, are doing volunteer work, and/or working. They
apply to you wherever you are – you have a right to live your
life, at all times, in an atmosphere of dignity, respect, and
without fear.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
“They Came”
First they came for the Jews
And I did not speak out –
Because I was not a Jew.
Then they came for the Communists
And I did not speak out –
Because I was not a Communist.
Next they came for the Trade Unionists
And I did not speak out –
Because I was not a Trade Unionist
Then they came for me.
And there was no one left –

---
## Page 777
To speak out for me.
NOTE: This poem was written anonymously; however, it was
based on a statement made by Pastor Friedrich Gustav Emil
Martin Niemöller who said: “First they came for the socialists
– but I was not a socialist,” in 1937.
Discuss the following questions with your group:
•
What do you think the poem is about?
•
Who are the “they” mentioned in the poem?
•
Describe the kind of situation you imagine when “they
come to take the person away.”
•
What do you think the central message of the poem
is?
•
What does the poem have to do with your life at school
or the lives of your classmates?
•
Who might speak out for you? Who might speak out
for your classmates?
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

## Page 778
THE “SNIDE COMMENTS” CLASSROOM
ACTIVITY
Explain to the adolescents and young adults you work with that it is
human nature to “gossip.” That at some point in time they will hear or
spread “gossip.” Sometimes the things that are said are harmless and
sometimes the words can hurt. Sometimes “gossip” gets so vicious, it
becomes bullying. And, sometimes people will get together and really
“go after” another person, hurt their feelings, their pride, or hurt them
physically.
Tell students that they are going to divide into groups of seven, to work
on an activity called “Snide Comments.” Each group will present their
reactions and results to the class after their role play. They should all
“do” or “role play” exactly what their card says and “try not to be
swayed” by what another student might ask them to do. Students will
role play their “role” at the same time as the other members of the
group are doing theirs.
Explain that the people who are acting out a specific role may be taken
from it (by request or command) by another student and involved in
another situation. When this happens individuals should still try to do
what their card says. For instance: If a student is talking to a “friend”
(because this is what their card tells them to do), and someone else
pulls them away and wants to start bad mouthing the friend – the
person should try to stay in his or her role (that is written on his or her
card), as “friend of the person being gossiped about” and try not to be
swayed. If the student is swayed he or she might say something like,
“hey, he/she is a friend of mine; please stop saying bad things about
he/she.” If their card says “go along with what everyone says,” they
should do this and join in on the “bad mouthing.”
When the activity is over – have each group tell the class how their
“Snide Comments Scenarios” went.
Preparation: Make the cards ahead of time. Then, hand out seven
blue, red, orange, light green, purple, white, and dark green

---
## Page 779
construction paper cards to each person in the group of seven – with
the following words printed on each card. Make sure students
understand that they are to read their card privately and not show it to
the others and play their role in their group of seven.
The blue card says: Your best friend is purple. Talk to purple and
support him or her. Listen and respond positively to what he or she is
saying.
The red card says: You are best friends with orange, dark green, and
white. You don’t like purple. Take your color friends and without purple
hearing (you might walk away from the group) make “sly or unkind”
comments about purple, purple’s hair and clothes, purple’s shyness,
purple’s new car (an old used one), and purple’s part-time job (working
at a local pizza shop) to see if you can get your friends to agree with
you.
The orange card says: You are friends with purple, blue, and light
green. Talk to them and support them. Talk about things you have in
common, plan a get together after school, or ask one to recommend
you for a part-time after school job where he or she works.
The light green card says: You are friends with red. Talk to red and
agree with everything red says. If red says you are all going to go
skateboarding after school – agree. If red says you are all going to
vandalize a building that night, agree. Agree with red no matter what
red says.
The purple card says: You are friends with orange and blue. Talk to
them and support them. While being supportive you might talk about
things you might do together such as going to the movies, or playing an
interactive video game later on in the day, or going out to a local coffee
shop after school.
The white card says: You want to pick on someone today. You don’t
know who yet, but if anybody else starts to have a go at somebody –
you will listen in then join in and pick on them as well. You will be nasty,
unkind, and engage in name-calling.

---
## Page 780
The dark green card says: You are good friends with red. Red is your
best friend, but you get on “really well” with everyone else. Be friendly
to all but try to hold a one-on-one conversation with red.
*Check that students understand what their card says and what they
are to do. Explain one more time that they must not show their cards to
anyone and they need to try to stay in their role within their group of
seven people.*
After they’ve had time to “act out” their roles, bring the class together
and talk about what happened, how students felt, and what, if any,
alternatives or peaceful solutions might have been implemented if there
was conflict in their particular group. Who might help the trouble
makers? What do they think should be done about the trouble makers?
How might the non-trouble making people become peer supporters
instead of joining the trouble making people?
Now that things have been stirred up a bit in your class you can do the
next activity (either in the same lesson or on a different day).
“THE BULLY”
Divide students into groups of four or five.
Distribute large sheets of newsprint or flip chart paper to students.
Ask the members of each group to take a black marker pen and draw
a big black rectangle on the paper.
Explain that this image is the “feared and dreaded bully” that has been
making lives miserable.
Explain that each group will label their “bully” with as many types of
“bullying” they have seen, heard about, or experienced as possible.
Tell participants they will not be censored and that you will be allowing
complete freedom of language. However, it’s important to portray
things that are factual or that have been “told” to them about “bullying.”

---
## Page 781
And, that this means things that they have seen, heard about, or
watched happen in real life.
When the “bullies” are completed the groups should share their
“bullies” with the whole class. They should talk about “bullying,” its
effects on the student being “bullied,” why it might be taking place, and
solutions for combating “bullying.” As students offer their opinions write
them on a white board.
Help students determine whether or not they think their class or their
school has a problem with “bullying.” If the answer is yes, ask your
class what they might do to change this at your school.
Give the young people you work with some examples of things others
have done with this exercise if your class or group is having trouble
coming up with ideas or possible solutions to “bullying.”
Explain that some students who decided to address “bullying” in their
schools have:
•
Created a class or school-wide service-learning project to
“combat bullying” in their school.
•
Written “anti-bullying policies” and asked school officials to
make it a rule with consequences such as zero tolerance.
•
Made “anti-bullying” posters in art class – to get the message of
the “anti-bullying policy” out this way. They have made posters
that said: “If you are being bullied tell someone” and listed the
names, email addresses, and phone numbers of who students
might go to.
•
Created a play or assembly presentation about the effects of
“bullying” and “solutions.”
•
Prepared and then conducted a survey (confidential and
anonymous) to find out more about the “bullying” going on at
school, then presented the results to school administrators.
•
Asked school administrators to train other students to help
support those “being bullied.”

---
## Page 782
•
Had peer listening, peer support groups, and peer mediation
implemented in their schools to “reduce bullying.”
If a “bullying” problem is identified and your students want to do
something about it, they will do this by the same method they used for
their 
service-learning 
project: 
Preflection, 
planning, 
action,
reflection/documentation, and celebration of the fact that they created
awareness and potential solutions for victims.
IMAGES
This is a subject that many of the young men and women you work with
will assume means: “how they look that day” in terms of hair, clothes, or
the latest gadgetry they have on display: iPhones, tablets, eBooks, etc.
However, you need to let them know that this is not what you’re talking
about.
Explain that the “image” you are talking about is about means of
finding a way – no matter how long, or stressful, or difficult, or
challenging their day may be – to project an “image” that is positive in
language, body language, and actions.
Explain that projecting a positive image is vital for being a person
that others want to be around.
Ask how many people in the class would want to be friends with
someone who:
•
Looks, feels, talks, and acts negative all the time.
•
Is grouchy.
•
Is mean and uses unkind words.
•
Has an unhappy look on their face, frowns or grimaces a lot.
•
Insults people.
ACTIVITY: Write this list on a white board and ask the members in your
group or class to add to it!

---
## Page 783
Then ask them to write out a list of what “being positive” around others
means.
You might start them off with things like: a positive or happy person:
•
Looks, acts, and sounds happy.
•
Smiles.
•
Uses kind words.
•
Gives compliments.
•
Uses uplifting and positive statements when conversing with
others.
Ask students to continue the list.
Discuss the fact that positive people are fun to be around. They attract
people with similar qualities and traits.
Tell the young men and women you work with that this is someone that
people will say something positive about such as: “I really like being
around him or her and want to spend more time with him or her.”
Explain that when people are negative they will feel uncomfortable
around the unhappy person. They will go out of their way to avoid this
person. They might label the person as being grumpy or a complainer
or worse: A whiner. Others will not want to be with this person.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Recently, my colleague Maggie and I were
taking a shuttle service to the airport to catch our different flights
home. Maggie had just read and proofread this chapter the evening
before our flight. We were talking about leaving the nice Florida
sunshine (it was February) when the “talk radio” announcer on the
van’s radio did a piece on this very subject. He told listeners that the
latest scientific research has proven beyond a shadow of a doubt that
people who smile, do not slouch, who have a pleasant look on their
faces, and who do not act grouchy or look unpleasant are happier. He
went on to say that the research proved that people who project a
positive image suffer less depression and have more success,
happiness, and joy in life and work.

---
## Page 784
Maggie and I just looked incredulously at each other. The radio
announcer then ordered all listeners to “sit up straight and smile” until
they got to where they were going! Of course by this point, Maggie
and I were telling all the passengers about the book and this section
of this chapter!
Here’s a student handout to remind students to pay attention to the
image they project.
Read the story on page 365 out loud to your students. When the story
says “STOP” ask your students to answer the question or comment
by contributing their thoughts, feelings, and words to the story.
If You’re Happy and You Want Others to Know It
…
•
Smile, stand up straight, project a friendly image.
•
Listen to others, look interested in what they have to say (be
genuine about this), and make eye contact.
•
If you are in a group, keep your body and shoulders facing the
group.
•
Use a steady, pleasant tone of voice. Avoid drama or dramatic
statements.
•
If you are genuinely upset about something, it’s ok to tell others
you are having a “bad day;” it is not ok to elaborate endlessly on
why – unless you are asked to do so.
•
When gesturing, keep your movements small and friendly.
•
Try not to use threatening or bad language. This can put others
on the defensive.

---
## Page 785
•
Be amenable; everything does not always have to “be your own
way.”
•
Repeat the first thing on this list often! Smile, stand up straight,
project a friendly image!
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
“BEHAVING BADLY”
“We can all think of an example of young people behaving badly. This
might be a friend using really bad language all the time, or reports of
young adults vandalizing or stealing cars. It can even be something as
simple as letting elderly people stand on a bus, or train, while a younger
person enjoys a seat. Most people (especially parents and other adults)
also agree that it’s high time something was done about it. “STOP.”
“What are some of these things that might be done?”
Then … explain that some of these “things that might be done” are
called consequences.
“Now that we understand that consequences hold people accountable
for their actions, think about how behaving badly affects the people
around you. For instance: The young adults who are planning to steal a
car have little empathy with the person who worked really hard to save
up and buy the car. They may not even think about the fact that this
might have been the person’s only way to get to work the next day.
They may think “who cares” if an elderly person stands while they sit,
not knowing that this person is in severe pain from arthritis, has just
come from a doctor’s appointment where they received bad news, or
may have been unable to sleep and is really tired.” “STOP.”
“What might be some ‘effects’ on others for these scenarios:
using bad language all the time, spray painting graffiti on a
church or synagogue wall, not giving your seat up to someone

---
## Page 786
who is on crutches, or being rude to a waitress in the restaurant
then not leaving her a tip?”
Explain that not taking into consideration the “effects” of behavior
results in being disrespectful to others.
TEACHING INSIGHT: The handouts on page 365–366 will lead your
students to a discussion about the subject of respect – of self and
others. Remind students that the second most important universal
ethical value is respect and that they will be reading the story in
“small groups,” talking about it, and then bringing their thoughts about
the story back to the rest of the class.
Charles Houff, a clinical psychologist at CIP’s Berkshire Center, wrote
the story entitled: R-E-S-P-E-C-T. It’s a good starting point for
revisiting what respect means in context to this unit on awareness,
solutions, and resolutions to conflict.
CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: After reading and discussing the story that
Charles Houff wrote, ask students if they think “lack of respect towards
others” is a problem at their school. The answer will probably be a
resounding yes.
Discuss ways the class might implement R-E-S-P-E-C-T for each
other. This might be revisiting the class Code of Ethics and adding
respect to it if it is not already on it. Or your class might do a project to
bring this message into clearer focus to each other as well as sharing
the message with peers, or even other classes.
The “Respect Builds Us Up” project on page 366 might be something
the young people you work with would like to duplicate, modify, or use
simply as an example or guide for thinking up ways your class might
get Charles Houff’s message across to others.
Divide the class into small groups. Have the group select a reader. After
reading about the project have your students discuss what they think
about the story and what the students did and report back to the other
groups.

---
## Page 787
Ask the class if they’d like to take the project forward as a service-
learning project or build a school awareness campaign around some of
the ideas in the story.
“R-E-S-P-E-C-T!”
“R-E-S-P-E-C-T … Tell you what it means to me.” Forty
years ago, Aretha Franklin had a huge hit singing about R-E-S-
P-E-C-T. Everybody’s heard the song. “All I need … All I’m
askin’ is for a little respect …” Aretha knew how important
respect is.
Of course, respect from others doesn’t really work unless you
respect yourself. Self-respect is an essential ingredient in any
kind of success – personal, social, academic, or professional.
The good news – self-respect is something you’re in charge of.
Everyone starts out with a healthy dose of self–respect. We all
know how insistent a baby can be when it’s crying out for
attention. And little kids “do their thing” like they have every
“right” to do whatever they want. They are who they are with no
apologies.
But it isn’t long before self-respect starts to shrink. Wanting to
perform as well as possible, kids start comparing themselves to
their siblings and schoolmates and their media idols and they
find all the ways they don’t measure up. Worse yet, many kids
are teased and taunted in school – even bullied – as they make
their way through the grades.
So by high school self-respect has often been replaced by self-
consciousness, defensiveness, even self-loathing for many of

---
## Page 788
us. So what can you do? Well, you just have to reclaim that
self-respect of yours.
Basically, self-respect means that you like yourself. Many of us
are better at thinking of things that we don’t like about
ourselves than things we do like.
As a therapist, I believe this is one of the strong supports that
therapy or talking to others about self-respect provides. Talking
to others can help us find – and strengthen – things to like
about ourselves. This means liking ourselves for who we are,
not for what we can or cannot do.
Who we are has to do with how we live – our principles and
character – and how we treat others – with consideration and
kindness, for example. When we develop these qualities, we
start to like and respect ourselves. And when we respect
ourselves, we are more in control of our own well-being and
less dependent on what others think.
This may sound like a major makeover for some of us, but the
sooner we get started, the sooner we’ll start to feel different. So
ask whoever supports you, whether it’s your parent, your
teacher, your advisor, or your therapist, to help you learn how
to R-E-S-P-E-C-T yourself.
Charles Houff, LMHC,
Head Clinical Therapist, CIP Berkshire

---
## Page 789
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Respect Builds Us Up!
Some young adults at an inner-city high school in
Philadelphia were tired of feeling disrespected. They decided
to take the message of using respect to build self-esteem and
showing respect to others out into their community.
Specifically, to acquaint the younger children in their local
community with the idea that they have to respect themselves
first in order to be able to show respect to others.
They thought up many ideas, but in the end, they decided to
take one of the walls on the playground of a local elementary

---
## Page 790
school (after getting permission for the project), and paint it
white, then create a mural entitled “Respect Builds Us Up!”
They then decided that the mural would have an outline of a
“he/she” (your students will remember “he/she’s” from doing
the “Ideal Citizen” activity earlier in this book) for each class
member. Using their creativity, they would paint faces and
clothes on each one and personalize “their he/she” in any
way that they wanted to, using the theme of respect
throughout their creation.
Each figure would have a caption about respect that students
researched using the Internet. They could write these words
as thought bubbles or on any part of the mural that was white
or even on the “he/she” (one ADD person created a logo and
wrote the respect saying on the “he/she’s” T-shirt).
The project turned out really well and involved a lot of
preflection, planning, and action. Students wrote a letter to a
local paint store to ask for paint donations. The store liked
what the students were doing and supplied paints in all colors
(mostly mistakes that people had returned), and paint
brushes.
A group of students went down to the local fast food
restaurant. After hearing about the project from the students,
they donated lunch on the day the students would paint the
mural.
Some students who were good at writing created “press
releases” and one newspaper and one local radio station
turned up to write and talk about this project to the public.
For celebration: The students asked the community to come
to an “unveiling” and “dedication” of the mural to the school.
On the day of the unveiling everyone in the elementary school
turned out to celebrate the project. One of the teachers and
some students sang Aretha Franklin’s song and danced;
another student sang (accompanied by another student on

---
## Page 791
the guitar) “We all need Somebody to Lean On.” Students did
a short dramatic presentation on bullying.
Parents, students, and teachers made refreshments and
everyone celebrated this important message that students
would be reminded of every day.
After reading about this project in your small groups – start a
class discussion. Would you, the members of your reading
group, or some of the people in your class, be willing to
create a service-learning project like this one, or think of
other ideas or concepts that could teach others in your school
or local community about R-E-S-P-E-C-T?
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Your students have learned a lot about awareness and bringing
awareness to others. They understand their rights and that they are
entitled to live their lives in dignity, with respect, and free from fear.
They’ve investigated and perhaps even acted upon ways to impart this
awareness to others.
Explain to your students that it’s now time to learn about the various
ways they can “tell someone” if they are being bullied, harassed or
living in fear, to “tell someone” if their rights are being violated and they
have been hurt physically or emotionally, or to “tell someone” if they
see this happening to others.
Explain that there are people in the school who will listen to whatever
they need to talk about, and that these people are their teachers,
advisors, school counselors, and other school administrators. And …
that it is important to know that these people want to know when they
are being mistreated and that these people will always be willing to
hear what they have to say and believe them.
Explain to your students that at some schools another way of being
able to “tell” someone they are being mistreated is by talking to a peer

---
## Page 792
listener, a peer group leader, or asking for peer mediators to help them.
Tell students they are going to learn about peer support – and that if
this is already being implemented in your school – you will tell them how
to access it. If it is not, that together, you’ll look at these programs and
see if it might be something they could help develop to help others at
their school.
Supporting Each Other
Explain to the young people you work with that sometimes telling
another student who has been trained to listen, offer advice, and help
his or her classmates deal with an issue or any type of conflict they may
be dealing with at school, is easier for a student in distress, than telling
a teacher, counselor, or school administrator.
Explain that in many schools (this is only if your school does not offer
this), students have been trained to help their peers in three different
ways: They are trained as:

---
## Page 793
•
One-on-one peer listeners.
•
Facilitators (or leaders) of peer support groups.
•
Peer mediators (they help solve conflicts with others peacefully).
Tell the adolescents and young adults you work with that you’ll be
talking about these different types of peer support in detail and then
looking at possibilities (together) for implementing one or more of these
services at your school. This might be deciding this is not the right time
or place to do this at your school. It might be that you and your students
might want to do more research to see if it might be something your
school could implement or at least start talking about. Or … the class

---
## Page 794
might decide to start working on having these services provided by
your school immediately. The important thing remains awareness and
to keep talking about solutions to conflict at your school.
The student handouts on pages 368–370 outline what peer support
means and what peer mediation means.
“A Shoulder to Lean On”
Peer support occurs when students provide knowledge,
experience, emotional support, and practical advice to you
and your classmates to help you find solutions and solve
“conflict” problems.
Why?
•
Because sometimes, a peer is in a position to offer you
support by virtue of relevant experience: he or she has
“been there, done that” and can relate to others who
are now in a similar situation.
•
Because the peer supporter knows there are problems
at his or her school and genuinely wants to help you
solve your problem or help you find others (teachers,
parents, counselors, school administrators, etc.), who
will help you resolve it.
What is peer support?
Peer supporters are people who have been trained to help
you deal with “conflict.” “Conflict” means things like bullying,
teasing, harassing, fighting, arguing, and making others

---
## Page 795
fearful of being on the school premises. It also applies to
these situations if they occur before or after school.
Peer support has three components: Peer listening, peer
support groups, and peer mediation.
Peer supporters assist and lead by personal example and
help you seek additional answers and problem solve until a
solution can be found to your “conflict” issue.
Peer supporters cannot help you if the
situation involves:
•
Assault: physical or sexual.
•
You trying to harm yourself or commit suicide.
•
You bring guns, drugs, or any illegal substance or
weapon to school, like a knife or illegal prescription
drugs.
•
You wanting to hurt yourself or others using violence.
These situations will be handled by school administrators,
staff, and officials.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
The Three Types of Peer Support

---
## Page 796
Peer listening:
•
This form of peer support is widely used in schools in
the United States and around the world. Peer listeners
are trained, normally in their schools or universities, to
listen to an issue and help you determine what to do
next.
•
Peer listeners support you and your classmates and
help you until you find a solution to your problem.
•
For instance: If you are being bullied, this person will
accompany you and help you “tell” a teacher or
someone in charge about the issue.
•
Whatever the problem: personal, interpersonal, or
unique, the peer listener will listen to you and give you
advice.
•
 Within schools, peer supporters are normally
available at break and lunchtimes.
Peer support groups:
•
The facilitator of this group is also trained and meets
with you and a group of students (usually 6 to 8
people), to address any issue that the group wants to
talk about.
•
This can be related to school studies, relationships in
the school setting, or personal or interpersonal issues.
•
These groups usually meet once every two weeks or
once a month. In this way positive relationships are
built among all the participants.
•
Anything students say in these groups remains
confidential unless it is a situation that will physically or
emotionally harm the person being listened to.

---
## Page 797
Peer mediation:
•
Instills the values of tolerance and peacemaking in
students as they become empowered to resolve
conflicts between two people who are having an issue
such as bullying, fighting, or engaging in behaviors
that will hurt each other physically or emotionally.
•
Peer mediation is a means of handling these incidents
by bringing the perpetrator and the victim together
under mediation by one of their peers. The peer
mediator acts as an impartial third party who listens
and helps participants devise a peaceful solution to
the problem.
•
If you are participating in peer mediation, you will have
your “side of the story” heard as you tell it to an
impartial listener (the mediator), and the other person
will have the same opportunity. You will follow rules
that state things like not interrupting, and waiting for
your turn. Together the three of you will try your best to
come up with a solution to the problem.
•
Once an outcome for the “conflict” has been agreed
upon, all three people in the mediation will sign a
contract wherein you and the person you are “in
conflict with” agree to settle the problem once and for
all using peaceful means.
•
This contract is given to school administrators to keep.
Should the contract be broken these people will take
over and determine what will happen next.
Why should my school offer this?
•
The purpose of the peer support program is to foster
the physical and emotional well-being of you and your
classmates.

---
## Page 798
•
Sometimes it’s easier to talk to someone your own
age. Peer supporters develop skills, attitudes, and
understandings needed to help you make safe and
healthy decisions about your own well-being at school
and to help you find solutions if you are experiencing
“conflict.”
•
It provides an alternative to traditional forms of school
discipline such as detention, being suspended, and in
some cases being expelled from school.
What are the benefits?
•
Students play a more active role in their welfare by
working with peer supporters and this builds
confidence and shows students how to solve their
problems in a proactive way.
•
Peer supporters help students accept responsibility
and ownership for their own behavior.
•
Peer supporters introduce solutions in a way and
manner that their fellow students can understand and
accept.
•
It has been proven that peer support works and
reduces incidences of bullying, teasing, taunting,
fighting, arguing, and harassing students.
The benefits of such a program for the school include:
•
The building of positive relationships among students
and between students, teachers, administrators, and
others.
•
A 
harmonious, 
safe, 
tolerant 
student/school
community and environment.
In summary:
•
Peer supporters teach you and your classmates
healthy communication skills and provide a safe

---
## Page 799
setting for reporting incidents.
•
They facilitate classroom or school-wide programs in
which they can model these skills to their peers.
•
Peer supporters instill the values of peacemaking in
you and your classmates, and they work to help you
become empowered to resolve conflicts without
teacher, principal, or adult disciplinary intervention.
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Triage for Teachers
The following materials are meant to be used as a starting point
for development of a peer support program in your school.
Students who want to be trained as peer listeners, peer group
leaders, or peer mediations should undergo full training using a
vetted method of instruction. Many of these training programs
are available locally (just search the Internet) and there are also
many resources and books devoted to creating peer support
programs. It just takes a little research to find a good fit for your
school.
The materials in this book will be useful as you plan and develop
your school’s program with others. The forms and worksheets
can be used as examples to clarify how peer support works and
once your program is up and running, can be used to support
your school’s peer program.
It’s a good point in the book to reiterate that peer mediation does
not replace traditional disciplinary measures for incidents where

---
## Page 800
bodily harm is involved or a law has been broken, or the severity
of the incident is beyond the realm or scope of what the peer
mediator has been trained to do. Peer mediators do not
intervene in school fights; their role is to recognize conflict in its
early stages and refer disputants to mediation through this
awareness.
The true value and scope of peer mediation is for all participants
(mediator, victim, and perpetrator) to come together to resolve
personal and interpersonal conflicts in a healthy and productive
way.
The real successes of implementing a peer support program in
your school will be reflected by a decrease in bullying, taunting,
teasing, fighting, arguing, and squabbling. It can also be a first
step intervention in an incident that without peer mediation
would escalate into a situation that physically or mentally harmed
a student at your school. Here is some information that you
might share with your class if a program like this is not already in
place at your school.
Getting Started: Creating a Peer
Mediation Program
Explain that peer mediation is both a program and a process
where:
•
Students of the same age group facilitate resolving disputes
between two people or small groups.
•
This process has proven effective in schools around the United
States, changing the way students understand and resolve
conflict in their lives.

---
## Page 801
•
Changes include improved self-esteem, listening and critical
thinking skills, a better school climate for learning, as well as
reduced disciplinary actions and fewer fights.
•
These skills are transferable outside of the classroom settings.
Explain that the process is voluntary for both sides.
•
Peer mediators do not “make decisions” but rather work
towards a win-win resolution for both sides in order to avoid
further trouble. Administrators in charge of discipline incorporate
this conflict resolution process into their strategies as well.
Explain that peer mediators handle the types of problems that
include:
•
Social media improprieties.
•
Relationship difficulties/harassment.
•
Rumor and gossip.
•
Cheating and stealing.
•
Racial and cultural confrontations.
•
Vandalism.
•
Classroom or extracurricular disputes.
•
Bullying, minor assaults, and fighting.
Explain that more serious problems require professional referral
and are not appropriate for peer mediation. These include:
•
Sexual abuse.
•
Assault.
•
Suicide.
•
Drug use.
•
Weapon possession.
•
Those that involve breaking a law.

---
## Page 802
Explain that in setting up a peer mediation program, there are
costs 
involved 
for 
the 
school, 
therefore 
the 
school
administration must approve the plan and agree to pay for the
costs before a program can be implemented. These include:
•
Materials.
•
A dedicated location/facility for mediation.
•
Training, staff support and office space, rewards.
Once a plan is in place to create or begin further discussions to create
a program, the following section will be helpful.
BEGINNING A SCHOOL MEDIATION
PROGRAM AND TRAINING MEDIATORS
A period of planning is critical:
•
Peer mediation has as its foundation … student empowerment.
Students are critical to all stages of development and
implementation of the program.
A coordinator (teacher or community member) is selected or
chosen. This person is responsible for:
•
Developing a core group of adults within school community to
train mediators as they role play and model the mediation
program for students being trained.
•
Overseeing the selection, training, motivation, and debriefing of
peer mediators.
•
Serving as the facilitator as the program is implemented.
•
Establishing protocol for intake and referrals.
•
Selecting and scheduling mediators.
•
Keeping 
records 
and 
report 
regularly 
to 
all
communities/stakeholders whether via newsletters or web sites.

---
## Page 803
•
Keeping current with literature and research.
•
Working aggressively to overcome attitudinal and structural
resistance with their schools by developing and maintaining
support from strategic groups, including parents.
Students form a leadership team:
•
Facilitated by the nominated coordinator, as well as teachers
and/or other professionals who might want to be part of the
program. The team can be appointed or self-developed.
The leadership team:
•
Researches the basics of peer mediation.
•
Develops and publishes a clear vision of its conflict resolution
program. This vision includes an overview of publicity, training,
practice, modeling, and evaluation.
•
Educates the various communities of the school on peer
mediation, its expectations and limits toward building a
commitment 
toward 
its 
implementation 
into 
a 
more
comprehensive peace-based curriculum.
The team secures commitment and support from the school’s
administration, including agreements from all major school groups to
follow this practice (including teachers and administrators).
The team commits to long-term buy-in: preparation meetings,
practicing mediation skills, and continued research about violence, its
causes, and prevention.
The coordinator acts as liaison between major groups,
including: the leadership team, student body, administrators, parent
groups, and outside trainers/professionals.
Peer mediator selection:
•
Mediators should reflect the school’s diversity including cultures,
gender, behavior, academic, social, and race.

---
## Page 804
•
Selection procedure and process should be publicized and
include recommendations and self-referral.
•
Commitments include continued skills development, willingness
to co-facilitate sessions, and mentor new trainers.
•
Rejection or deferral should be sensitively explained so as not to
alienate the student.
Initially, a system of “experience” must be developed:
Peer mediators must be trained and monitored since they often lack
maturity and experience, both in conflict management and negotiation
skills. Strategies include:
•
Role playing.
•
Problem-based learning and active learning.
If possible, workshops should be conducted away from school to
minimize distraction.
Peer mediation goal:
•
The goal is to move from mutual blame toward a solution
acceptable to all parties.
Disputants then:
•
Fill out a pre-session questionnaire establishing ground rules:
committing to solve the problem, tell the truth, and listen
respectfully and without interrupting.
•
Disputants meet with mediators to see if chemistry is right and
ensure there are no conflicts of interest.
Mediators:
•
Meet with disputants and explain exceptions to confidentiality at
the beginning of the mediation and ask if they wish to continue.
•
Explain their role: That they will be using listening and
communication skills to help students resolve conflict and

---
## Page 805
disagreements before they escalate and lose power over the
situation.
•
Solicit questions and clarifications on the process before
beginning.
The session disputants:
•
Introduce themselves.
•
Each in turn tells their story to the mediator focusing on issues,
not on who did what, while the other concentrates on listening
without interrupting.
•
Parties change roles: Each repeats the other’s story to their
satisfaction to demonstrate they understand the other’s position
(not that they necessarily agree with it).
The session co-mediators:
•
Summarize the facts and feelings of both sides for verification
and agreement on the issues; lead a discussion of the issues
and acknowledge the difficulty in dealing with any emotional
baggage.
•
Ask both parties if any solutions come to mind, or begins a
brainstorming session without judgment. All suggestions are
noted and acknowledged.
•
Lead a discussion of the solutions checking off only the
solution(s) that both parties can agree to.
Disputants:
•
Determine implications of solutions in selecting the best possible
outcome.
•
Select the best alternative and agree to this verbally.
Co-mediators:
•
Verify the verbal agreement with all parties, ensuring that no one
is reluctant or afraid to speak out or dissent.

---
## Page 806
•
Write a memo of understanding/contract in parties’ own words.
Co-mediators and disputants:
•
Sign the contract.
•
Develop a process for follow up.
•
Verify that all will be committed and monitor this process.
Co-mediators thank each person for their contribution to the process,
and for letting the mediation service help them.
The following section (pages 374–378) contains handouts and forms
that will be useful in constructing your program.
Mediating a Dispute
Before mediation:
•
Take time to center yourself and ensure that you have
everything you need to bring to the meeting with you.
Helpful hints:
•
When disputants enter the room, introduce yourself.
•
Remember, everyone will be nervous, including you. Try to
set disputants at easy by saying: “I’m really glad you are
here today.”
•
Have disputants read a hand out of the “Ground Rules” out
loud. Make sure all agree to conditions, including you and
any other mediators present.
•
Let the disputants talk – be a good listener! Remember
silence on your part forces the disputants to talk. Don’t be
afraid to say to the disputants:

---
## Page 807
•
Can you tell me more?
•
I’m not sure I understand, try again.
•
In other words, I hear you saying …
•
Do you have anything else to add?
Make sure disputants state how the conflict made them
feel!
•
If a disputant says: “I feel like …” or “I feel that …” they
are not sharing their feelings!
•
They need to say: “I feel angry or mad or sad!”
This is a detective process:
•
Ask questions if you are not sure what is going on.
Ending the session:
•
Remember to get students to sign the Commitment to Action
form and to read their solutions out loud.
•
Give compliments to the disputants throughout the mediation
and especially at the end.
Remember that you have been trained and you will do
well!
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit

2. I agree to respect the leadership of the mediators.

without interrupting or name-calling.

## Page 809
Peer Mediator Contract
As a peer mediator I understand my role is to help students
resolve conflicts peacefully.
As a peer mediator I will do my best to respect the
participants of mediation, remain neutral, and keep the
mediation confidential.
As a peer mediator I agree to the following terms:
•
To complete all training and practice sessions.
•
To maintain confidentiality in all mediations.
•
To perform all duties required of a peer mediator,
including conducting mediations, completing all
necessary forms, and promoting the program.
•
To maintain satisfactory school conduct (this includes
requesting mediation before taking other actions if I
become involved in a conflict).
•
To maintain satisfactory grades in all classes and
make up any work missed during training and/or
mediation sessions.
•
To serve as a peer mediator until I leave school.
Possible actions if these responsibilities are not met:
•
First time: Warning.
•
Second time: Loss of peer mediation status for a set
period of time.
•
Third time: Suspension (no longer a peer mediator).
