Helpful Hints for Peer Mediators
FIXING WORDS:
When you interrupt it makes it hard to work towards resolving
this conflict.
You’ll get a chance to tell what happened in just a minute.
Do you remember what you were thinking when you did that?
How were you feeling when this was going on?
I’m confused. Can you help me understand this better?
I get confused when I hear two different versions of what
happened?
I can understand how you might see the situation differently.
I’m a little confused about what you just said.

---
## Page 813
Do you have any idea what could have happened to his
jacket?
Can you put yourself in her place for just a a moment?
What are you willing to do now?
I’m not sure, but it sounds like you want to be free to go out
with other people?
I’m aware that you’re concerned. I can see there’s a problem.
You’re right, this is a real problem.
It sounds like what happened is a “really big deal” to you – is
that correct?
FIGHTING WORDS:
Stop interrupting!
Can’t you just listen to him first!
What in the world were you thinking!
I can’t believe you DID that!
Somebody is lying!
It’s obvious who is in the wrong here!
Are you sure you’re telling the truth?
It seems there’s been a misunderstanding.
Why did you take his jacket?
Can’t you understand what she’s trying to say?
Well, it’s too late now!
You 
mean 
you 
want 
to 
go 
out 
with 
somebody
else?!?!?!?!?!?!?!?
It’s ridiculous to feel that way!

---
## Page 814
This isn’t anything to get so upset about!
You shouldn’t think like that!
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
PERSONAL INSIGHT: This chapter ends with a piece that has been
adapted from my first book, “Made for Good Purpose …” It’s a piece
that I wrote to inspire students to take action and become their own
Change Masters. To create a place for themselves in this world that
allows them to not only survive, but thrive.
Please share this story with your students and keep encouraging them
daily to become their “own” Change Master. To seek solutions to their
problems and when “conflict” arises, to be bold enough to find a
“shoulder to lean on.” “Conflict” is part of life, and being equipped to
seek help and keep searching for help until an answer is found is
critical to being successful in school, at work, and in life.
Being on the Spectrum myself, I tend to get stressed easily, not focus,
and act impulsively. But I have learned from peers, from getting to
know and trust myself better, and from realizing that “conflict,” and
things going wrong and irritations with other people or situations, is
often part of daily life. So in becoming a Change Master when
“conflicts” of any kind arise I “slam-dunk” every situation even if it
takes me several days and numerous requests for help, intervention,
and mediation until I reach that hoop!
“Becoming a Change Master”
As I work with students I always ask them if they are willing “to
grow.” This is more palatable than asking them “to change.” It
has been my experience that young adults on the ASD and LD

---
## Page 815
Spectrum are “stuck” in the process of making changes and this
sometimes surfaces as anxiety or procrastination.
In AA (Alcoholics Anonymous), they say, “move a muscle,
change a thought.” This quote certainly has credence. Getting
into action, any action whether this is a plan, thinking creatively,
or trying something new, is better than doing nothing at all.
I often tell my students that we simply do not learn without some
unease and at times, conflict. This can be conflict with others or
conflict within themselves.
It takes courage to address conflict from internal or external
factors. I sometimes tell students: “If there is no wind in the sail –
you are not going anywhere!” So I encourage them to set sail, in
a dinghy if need be, the sailboat can come later.
Instead of dreaming vaguely or being a victim of your random
thoughts, or someone else’s actions, listen to your own mental
music and change any negative “self-talk.” Be brave and seek
assistance, let others help you, and you will (in the end) greatly
help yourself.
I experienced a lot of conflict recently, none that would harm me
physically, but conflict caused by situations that stressed me
greatly!
On a recent trip to Athens, my flight took me from Hartford to
Dallas. I would then take a flight from Dallas to Madrid and then
one to Athens. When I got to the airport the flight to Madrid from
Dallas was showing a half-hour delay. I knew that my flight in
Madrid was with a different carrier and that I had to get my bags
then recheck in at a different terminal upon arrival. At this point, I
knew that I had four hours between flights, so I felt confident that
I had enough time to get to my new airline in Madrid. I decided
that I did not need to worry or think about this as it seemed
under control.

---
## Page 816
Back in Dallas, the flight delay was extended to 45 minutes
because of maintenance problems with the plane, and then to
an hour and finally to an hour and a half. I started to get
somewhat anxious and found myself constantly calculating the
time difference with the delay and then using that to figure out
the remaining time I would have left to make the other flight. The
plane was finally towed into the gate and we were ready to
board.
No sooner did this happen, when it was announced that they
needed to run the engines for a while to get them ready. I then
waited another 20 minutes. Finally, they started to load the plane
at (what I was anxiously thinking was) a “snail’s pace.” When
everyone was seated, the flight crew announced they had to run
the engines again, then turn them off and then have the
maintenance department verify that the original problem was
fixed. This took another half hour. As the plane taxied (this took
forever) to the take-off runway, I was convinced we had driven
halfway to Madrid.
I recalculated that I now had about an hour between flights to
get to the other airline, check bags, and board the other flight.
Then I realized I had to go through customs and immigration,
then get my luggage and proceed to another terminal, check in,
go through security, and get to the new airline’s gate. I then
made the correct assumption that this would not be possible
unless the other plane to Athens was late in taking off. And …
then I began to relax (something the “old” Michael would not
have been able to do).
When we approached Madrid the pilot announced that we were
being put in a delay pattern and would land at the airport in
about 15 or 20 minutes. Having accepted the fact that I would
miss my flight earlier in the flight, I figured I would just rebook on
a later flight in the afternoon or early evening and that it would
not be a problem.

---
## Page 817
Upon arrival in Madrid, I went through customs, baggage, took
the bus to the next terminal and went to the airline booth for
Aegean Airlines only to find out that it was closed. The person in
the next booth said that Aegean only ran one flight to Athens
each day and that the next flight would be at the same time the
following day.
I had tried to get help and it simply was not possible. Instead of
acting inappropriately, I decided to get back on the bus, return
to the terminal where my flight came in, and see if American
Airlines could help me. Of course, they could, but they would
charge me an additional $1600. I called another airline
(Lufthansa) and was told the same thing – they could help me,
but it would cost about the same amount of money.
Surprisingly and despite having little sleep throughout this whole
process, I remained calm. I knew that one way or the other this
situation would work out and that sooner or later, if I asked
enough people, someone would help me and this personal
conflict would end.
Putting all my internal and external calming techniques into
place, I was able to come up with another idea and plan. I
decided to call Aegean Airlines in Athens, Greece. After stating
my case, Aegean gave me credit for the flight and booked me
out on the first flight for the next day.
Buoyed by my luck in telephoning Athens, Greece directly, I then
contacted the hotel in Athens to tell them I would not be arriving.
They kindly did not charge me for the missed evening in their
hotel. On a roll now, I called the taxi service and changed the
pick up to the next day and finally (being my own superior
customer service agent by this point), I went up to the hotel
counter and booked a room in central Madrid.

---
## Page 818
Within a half hour, I was in my Madrid hotel room, showered,
and about to have a nice meal at a sidewalk café. I spent the
evening at the Prado Museum looking at the El Grecos and
walking through the gardens of the Arboretum. I stopped to
listen to some music at the festival Isadora and did some
window-shopping in the warm evening breeze. I then returned to
my room and I painted a picture from the balcony.
I realized how quickly my learned and much practiced cognitive
flexibility skills came into play when I was in distress and in a
personal “conflict.” I was able to make a wonderful day out of a
nerve wracking and distressing situation. And, I knew where this
newfound ability had come from. From gleaning knowledge
about my ASD, practicing techniques to deal with my personal
stressors, and then analyzing the changes in my behavior that I
needed to make from within while seeking and asking for help
from others.
I was my own “Change Master.” I had turned a negative thinking
pattern to an optimistic and explorative one and then using a
positive outlook (instead of projecting a negative image) and
was able to cope. From many, many years of experience
traveling and from having to deal with similar situations, I knew
what did not work for me and I consciously made myself
physically and mentally curb my anxiety.
I kept reminding myself throughout the process that “everything
was for good purpose” and that problems often present
opportunities. I convinced myself to remain calm and to just “go
with the flow.”
When I ran into frustration after landing and wanted to “pick a
fight” and “be grouchy,” I made myself be nice to all the people I
dealt with at each airline. And … I thanked them for their help. I
showed and was given respect. This was not “Michael Melt-
down,” guy of the past. The one no one (not even me) wanted to

---
## Page 819
be around. The Michael who exuded anxiety, fear, and anger
and a negative image to everyone else!
Instead, I took on the challenge, controlled my actions, and
demonstrated commitment to the process by staying calm and in
control. I had every reason to blame the airlines and to be angry
and miserable. Instead, I had a wonderful evening in Madrid
filled with art, music, and food.
This outcome is the culmination of the last eight years of working
to understand, accept, and learn to live with my ASD. This is the
example I set for my students. I am living proof that I practice
what I preach and teach.
My students can change their lives through action and being
willing to grow and seek assistance when problems arise, at an
airport, in school, or when dealing with their peers and others. I
always say “Have a great day, a day in which you deal with
everyday stresses, and any personal or interpersonal conflicts
with the assistance of others. Slam-dunk!”

---
## Page 820
Making It Real
WHAT?
Chapter 8 gives educators, clinicians, and the support
professionals who work with adolescents and young adults
on the ASD or LD Spectrum information, teaching tools,
techniques, and materials to raise awareness and then
address conflict such as: bullying, harassment, teasing,
discrimination, and verbal or physical assault.
Giving young adults, especially those on the Autism
Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and Learning Differences
Spectrum (LD), awareness and a structure that they can use
to:
1. Identify the signs of conflict.
2. Be able to tell someone.
3. Have an action taken to stop the conflict.
4. Find a peaceful resolution to the conflict.
HOW?
By raising awareness, providing assistance, and offering
solutions and resolutions to conflict at school and in the
workplace through peer listening, peer support groups, and
an actual peer mediation process.
BENEFITS?
Having young adults address these issues directly with peers
(who are trained and supported by their teachers, support

---
## Page 821
professionals, and school or workplace administrators) is the
most effective way to lessen conflict at school or at work.
NOTES:
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

---
## Page 822
Curriculum Integration
CHAPTER 9 OVERVIEW:
DEVELOPING A DEDICATED CURRICULUM MODEL
SAMPLE SCHEDULE
CREATING A DISPERSED INTEGRATION MODEL
Curriculum Integration
This chapter will focus on how to integrate the teaching of skills for life,
school, work, and independent living into a dedicated curriculum model
or fully dispersed into an already established curriculum model. It will

---
## Page 823
also look at integrating the principles of the skill chapters into specific
academic subject areas.
Various models are possible: From 100% use of dedicated time in a
specialized learning environment or in a transitional services program
devoted exclusively to assisting adolescents and young adults, to a fully
dispersed model that integrates this book in a variety of contexts.
In the fully dispersed model, the content from this book or chapters
from this book are integrated into the regular academic classes or
lessons that take place during a regular high school or college day. This
might also mean that schools develop specific classes to augment
academic studies that focus on particular life skill areas. Many high
schools, colleges, universities, and ASD/LD support programs will, in
practice, opt for a mixed model to meet the needs of this growing and
mainstreamed population.
The idea of curriculum integration may appear daunting; however, it is
worth remembering and reminding readers that most teachers,
clinicians, and support personnel have chosen to work with ASD and
LD adolescents and young adults because they want to help individuals
develop into happy and productive adults. Remembering that your
students will get there – it just may take longer than anyone could have
possibly imagined – is the key to making curriculum integration work.
This chapter supports using the materials contained in Autism and
Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit) in
whatever way best suits the needs of the educator or support
professional in his or her specific learning environment.
Students with ASD and LD can succeed. The goal – whether
integration is completely dedicated or dispersed – is to graduate (from
high school, college, or vocational training programs), young men and
women of confidence, ability, character, and integrity who are capable
of making contributions to society as they claim their places as citizens
of the world.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: The first part of this chapter looks at using this
Teaching Toolkit as a 100% dedicated model. As most readers know

---
## Page 824
by this point in the book, I am the founder of the College Internship
Program (CIP), a program designed to help young people on the “ASD
and LD Spectrum” make a successful transition from adolescence to
young adulthood. The materials in this book are used daily at our six
centers across the United States. to help these young men and
women become productive members of society.
We use this Teaching Toolkit model with high school graduates who
have not yet reached the age of 27 and during our summer programs
for 10th, 11th, and 12th grade high school students. Most of our full-
time students attend college or take part in career development
classes or vocational training alongside our comprehensive social,
academic, and life skills dedicated curriculum model.
As students are introduced to the comprehensive curriculum detailed in
the chapters of this book, they develop skills for life, school, work, and
independent living. Individuals acquire critical thinking and social skills,
as well as a variety of transferable abilities, such as “Executive
Functions” and the ability to “Reframe” thoughts and actions. As
students begin to choose career paths and prepare to live
independently each individual learns to manage finances, do their own
grocery shopping, cooking, laundry, and housekeeping. Each person
also develops a plan for wellness and for his or her individual emotional
well-being needs (managing anxiety, stress, and anger).
“Developing a Dedicated Curriculum Model” shows the readers and
users of this book how CIP (College Internship Program) does what it
does as we work with our students each and every day. This section of
the chapter also explains how educators and support professionals can
apply this formula to the already crowded and overbooked lives of their
students as they attend college, work at internships or jobs, or advance
a career path.

---
## Page 825
Developing a Dedicated
Curriculum Model
Research shows that students with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)
and Learning Differences (LD) achieve the greatest levels of success
when enrolled in individualized programs that address their unique
strengths and challenges. A dedicated program for young adults that is
focused on concrete goals such as completing high school, college, or
vocational training, exploring a career path, developing a social
network, and learning independent living skills is essential for this
success.
GETTING STARTED
Establishing a Student Advising Department
Student advisors work individually with students on goal setting, taking
self-assessments (see Chapter 10), building self-advocacy skills, and
dealing with day-to-day issues while they attend school, participate in
vocational training, perform during an internship program, or work at a
job. Strong relationships are created as advisors build a foundation of
trust with students and their families so that the student’s needs are
fully met.
Advisors oversee the development of each student’s individual skill
development plan. This plan includes obtaining support and guidance
for each individual as he or she begins to take classes, reaches
academic or career path goals, and integrates into his or her
surrounding community. The advisor monitors and assesses (on a
regular basis) the individual’s success in acquiring “Executive
Function” abilities, social competencies, critical thinking skills, cognitive
flexibility, and independent living skills as the student begins a
dedicated program of study to meet these goals.

---
## Page 826
Student advisors also serve as case managers and liaise with families,
educators, clinicians, and support professionals – keeping them up to
date – regarding each individual’s progress.
Individual Person Centered Plans (PCPs)
Student advisors oversee the development of each student’s individual
Person Centered Plan (PCP) and are responsible for coordinating
progress reports and maintaining communication with staff in other
departments regarding progress.
The Person-Centered Plan (see Chapter 4 – Competency 2: Skills for
School) is a unique self-exploration and assessment tool that provides
an opportunity for each student to conceptualize his or her short- and
long-term goals using a visual, creative process. Using multimedia
tools, each student creates a personal presentation of academic and
personal aspirations that encompass individual interests, wishes, and
hopes while outlining a plan and setting realistic goals to meet each
component. Students begin working on their PCPs at the beginning of
the school year and continually refine them throughout the year, while
understanding that achieving goals requires instruction, practice,
repetition, and reinforcement.
PCPs focus on objectives that encompass acquiring skills for life,
school, work, and independent living. An understanding of each
student’s special interests allows staff and especially the student’s
advisor to help each individual set realistic goals that can be assessed,
monitored, and visually tracked over time.
A PCP is also used to help students adapt to new environments,
develop cognitive flexibility, and engage in “Reframing,” ensuring they
will have the skills they need to be successful independent adults.
DEVELOPING A DEDICATED CURRICULUM

---
## Page 827
Social Skills Classes
Developing social skills and social thinking is an important part of each
of the competency components of this book: skills for life, school, work,
and independent living. A student’s social and emotional growth
precedes all other growth. Classes dedicated to working on social
competencies in multiple areas include creating, developing, and
implementing classes for individual and small group sessions. These
classes discuss topics and practice real-life social situations until the
social skill is acquired. Instructors work on key areas such as
perspective taking, reciprocal conversation, body language, eye
contact, and social awareness.
Creating a Social Mentoring Program
The experts tell us that modeling behavior by peer mentors in real-life
situations carries the highest degree of learning success. Students
receive a carefully selected social mentor who serves as a peer and
generational (social) translator. This mentor works hard to engage the
student he or she is working with socially in areas of individual or
mutual interest, as well as encouraging the young person to try new
things and acquire new skills and behaviors. Mentors accompany
students on outings to museums, computer stores, and engage in
activities that connect a student’s special interests with opportunities to
practice social competencies in a variety of relaxed and real-world
settings.
Social mentors are young men and women who are hired to perform
this task. They are within a ten-year age range of the student whom
they will be working with. Social mentors have a background of working
with the ASD/LD population, or are being trained to do so. This is a
great opportunity for schools to work with community colleges and local
colleges to recruit interns or to advertise for paid employees to serve
this role.

---
## Page 828
“Executive Function (EF)” Classes and
Support Groups
“Executive Function (EF)” is a mental process that governs a student’s
ability to manage time, make plans, as well as complete tasks, and is at
the core of any cutting-edge life, school, work, or independent living
skills curriculum. These classes focus on the abilities needed for each
key life skill area and dovetail with the Autism and Learning Differences
(An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit) materials provided in each
competency skill set chapter.
“Executive Functions” occur during daily activities such as making it to
class on time, being organized with class work, and when engaging in
social and recreational events. “Executive Function” classes provide
students with essential supports to help them experience success.
Because adolescents and young adults with an ASD or LD diagnosis
are often overwhelmed by a typical high school or college experience,
they need to learn “EF” skills which include planning, goal setting, and
scheduling, along with strategies for school, work, and independent
living.
Students also need to prioritize tasks and communicate more
effectively. Each student needs to develop various organizational skills
and follow through strategies for his or her academic schedule and for
his or her social and (if applicable) work life after classes.
By working in classes and in small support groups using visual
prompts, adolescents and young adults can learn how to carry “EF”
skills into their high school/college classes, the workplace, and into
their daily lives.
“Executive Function” support groups meet at the beginning and end of
each week and serve as bookends as one school week ends and
preparation for the next one begins. Students meet in small groups to
develop personal organizational strategies, learn techniques to manage
their time, and develop a plan to organize their class work with a

---
## Page 829
timeline. They evaluate their progress and share insight and strategies
together, as a group.
Advice for creating a similar program like CIP’s “Bookends”™ support
group program is detailed in Chapter 4 – Competency 2: Skills for
School.
“Reframing” Classes
“Reframing” is a term used to describe the process in which a person
converts the thoughts and feelings about a negative situation into a
positive pathway for change. A “Reframing” class (see Chapter 4 –
Skills for School in this book) identifies the strengths of a behavior
being exhibited and readjusts the thinking process so the individual is
able to recognize a positive outcome from the situation.
“Reframing” classes also include an emotional regulation process to
help students identify their thoughts and feelings. Weekly and daily
themes 
help 
students 
with 
self-understanding 
and 
structure.
“Reframing” helps students connect the dots between behavior and
emotion.
A gathering once a day, usually in the morning, provides a consistent
schedule where students can evaluate their feelings and plan out their
day. This may seem mundane, but students with ASD and Learning
Differences crave consistency, so a daily practice session for
“Reframing” aids in the alteration of behavioral patterns.
“Theory of Mind” Classes
Social Cognitive Learning Disability is among the most abstract of all
Learning Differences. In “Theory of Mind” classes, students learn to
track what others are thinking and feeling by attempting to take their
perspective. Students learn about social inference, whole body
listening, and using memory to facilitate friendships. (See Chapter 6 –
Competency 4: Skills for Independent Living, for more information on

---
## Page 830
how to develop “Theory of Mind” classes to impart this skill set and
knowledge.)
CREATING AN ACADEMIC SUPPORT TEAM
Academic Coordinators
Academic coordinators provide a comprehensive support system that
will enable students with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and
Learning Differences (LD) to achieve academic success while finishing
their high school years, pursuing studies at a local college, or at a
vocational training center.
Academic coordinators, who are trained to work with this population,
understand the unique strengths and challenges that individuals on the
Autism and Learning Differences Spectrum possess.
Academic coordinators provide valuable guidance and assistance to
each student as he or she identifies a field of study that is both
challenging and obtainable.
Academic coordinators also work with high schools, colleges,
universities, and vocational training programs in order to assist
students with self-advocacy and self-disclosure. Academic support staff
can recommend specific classes and teachers or professors that will
meet each student’s unique needs.

---
## Page 831
CREATIVE ARTS PROGRAMMING
Adolescents and young adults on the ASD or LD Spectrum are
extremely creative and talented. Helping young adults embrace and
develop their performing and visual arts talents will help these young
men and women achieve skills for life, school, work, and independent
living by embracing, developing, and sharing their talents.
Including or developing a creative arts program when designing a
dedicated curriculum model enhances the core values that underpin
teaching social competencies and “Executive Function” techniques and
allow students to build self-confidence and character. A creative arts
program also has the added value of allowing these young men and
women to explore potential career paths and utilize their amazing array
of talents.
INDIVIDUAL TUTORING AND STUDY
GROUPS

---
## Page 832
Tutoring and Study Groups
Individual tutoring sessions are scheduled throughout the week and are
designed to improve academic skill levels. During these sessions,
students also receive valuable assistance and training with time
management, organization, and study skills. Students who may be
struggling with specific academic areas will benefit from regularly
scheduled and supervised study halls.
Tutors provide individual and small group tutoring sessions that meet
throughout the week and help keep students on track. These sessions
are designed to help students in specific areas where they are having
difficulties, as well as help students improve study and/or basic
academic skills.
Tutors can also help students obtain classroom or coursework
accommodations provided by the disabilities support department or
center at their school, on campus, or while attending vocational training.
Study groups can also help students with social or cognitive learning
differences. Students need to interpret what others are thinking and
feeling by assuming another’s perspective. Students need to learn
whole body listening, social inference, and use memory to facilitate
friendships. They need to learn to interpret facial expressions and take
perspective on what others are feeling. Working one-on-one or in a very
small group with a tutor can be extremely beneficial.
CREATING A HEALTH AND WELLNESS
DEPARTMENT
Health and Wellness Coordinators
Young adults with an ASD or LD diagnosis perform at their best – in all
key life skill areas – when there is a sustained level of wellness in their
lives.

---
## Page 833
Health and wellness coordinators work with each student to create a
personal fitness plan that will provide balance through proper nutrition,
exercise, stress-reduction practices, a sensory diet (if needed), and
sleep regimens. This plan is augmented by yoga, group workouts,
walking clubs, and other fitness or sport centered regimes. Students
meet weekly with wellness coordinators either individually or in groups
to plan and set goals.
Sensory Integration Classes
Students benefit from having a holistic understanding of their sensory
issues. This helps them improve attention, decrease anxiety, and
increase environmental comfort. Classes or individual sessions that
focus on sensory integration – and the importance it has in everyday
tasks and life – help to give an individual insight as he or she develops
coping strategies and requests accommodations.
Sensory integration sessions include work on gross and fine motor
control and help students understand the effects of the individual
senses (tactile, vestibular, auditory, visual, and olfactory). Calming

---
## Page 834
strategies are taught and a sensory diet is developed. Daily activities
that help calm and relax are established for each student.
Wellness Classes
A healthy lifestyle can help each individual reduce stress and elevate
his or her level of healthy functioning. Exercise and good diet increases
energy, promotes positive social behaviors, and strengthens the
immune system. It can also improve self-esteem as well as perceptions
of others. Starting with individual assessments, students can then focus
on the areas of nutrition, hygiene, sensory diets, weight control, and
physical fitness that they need to address.
Dedicated Program Coordinated Activities
An active social life is an important component of a dedicated
curriculum. Students have the opportunity to develop lasting friendships
through planned weekly recreational activities that are coordinated by
health 
and 
wellness 
coordinators. 
These 
include 
musical
performances, sporting events, museums, and outdoor activities.
Individual Therapy
Many students on the Spectrum need support with social, anxiety, and
sensory issues. Every student with an ASD or LD diagnosis arrives with
a unique set of challenges. Most attend their high school classes,
college, or vocational training programs without being able to ask a
teacher or advisor for help. They have trouble working in groups and
developing typical college friendships. Individual therapy utilizing a
cognitive behavior therapy approach is very effective in assisting
students as they learn to deal with their emotions and solve problems.
As the number of students being diagnosed with ASD and LD
increases dramatically, high schools, colleges, and vocational

---
## Page 835
programs need to develop curricula and supports that provide students
with individualized services when needed.
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)
Individual therapy sessions help an individual shape and manage his or
her life while improving the quality of interpersonal relationships with
others. Students also receive guidance and validation as they learn to
deal with day-to-day challenges, frustrations, stress, and anxiety.
Emphasis is placed on acceptance of self and others while establishing
a solid foundation for a fulfilling, productive life. CBT is an important
part of any individual therapy program a school with a dedicated
program of support should offer.
Equine-Assisted Therapy
Licensed psychologists often utilize equine-assisted therapy with
students on the ASD or LD Spectrum enabling them to develop
successful interactions and relationships that can be translated and
generalized into social opportunities. This form of therapy can be useful
for 
understanding 
perspective 
taking, 
social 
cues, 
nonverbal
communication, and body language while helping to alleviate anxiety. If
at all possible, implementing or forming an alliance with an organization
offering these services, is a worthwhile exploration.

---
## Page 836
Art Therapy
Art therapy provides students participating in a dedicated support
program opportunities for individual self-expression, both verbal and
nonverbal. During group art therapy sessions students experiment with
various art mediums and engage in conversations focused on making
art. These classes also result in students being able to make
connections with each other.
Art therapy teaches students frustration tolerance, planning, and
organization skills, as well as limitations. These classes allow students
to explore the possibilities of imagination and individual artistic
expression. These are skills that are useful in everyday life. Students
involved in art therapy feel successful, regardless of their skill level.
Medication Support

---
## Page 837
Students who have been prescribed medication and who meet with a
psychiatrist on a regular basis would coordinate prescribed medication
from a doctor with a medication coordinator. The medication
coordinator helps the student keep his or her medications organized,
ensures prescriptions are filled when needed, and develops a schedule
or check in system to ensure that each person taking prescribed
medications is self-medicating while attending school, classes, living
independently on campus, or in an apartment.
Career Coordinators
Career Coordinators counsel individuals and provide the following
educational and vocational guidance services, such as:
•
Organizing, maintaining, and providing a wide variety of career
information and materials.
•
Communicating with students, faculty, parents, and community
representatives regarding career planning, volunteer work,
internships, and full or part time jobs for students on the ASD/LD
Spectrum.
•
Administering assessments to determine abilities, transferable
skills, interests, and viable career paths for each individual.
•
Planning, organizing, developing, and implementing career
events.
•
Attending career fairs with students, and working within the local
and surrounding communities to help students find opportunities
for volunteer work, internships, and job placement.
•
Contacting, scheduling, and arranging guest speakers from the
local business community to present information regarding
specific occupations.
•
Providing job coaching or job shadowing, as well as training in
specific job skills and giving assistance, guidance, and direction

---
## Page 838
to student volunteers, interns, or those working at full-or part-
time jobs.
Internships and Community Service
Community service (volunteer work) and internship placement is
facilitated by the school’s career coordinator. ASD and LD students
who apply their academic and social knowledge directly to real-life
workplace experiences through volunteering or an internship program
will be more successful once they enter the paid-for-work job market.
Career Coordinators assess each student’s interests and ability level
and then help him or her find an appropriate volunteering opportunity or
internship program.
Career Coordinators facilitate community service and internship group
meetings where students openly discuss personal experiences,
performance, advocacy, challenges, and what they have learned about
themselves during the internship or volunteering opportunity. These
meetings are very beneficial and are usually scheduled and led by the
school’s Career Coordinator.
CREATING AN INDEPENDENT LIVING
RESIDENTIAL SUPPORT PROGRAM
Relationship Development Classes
Students need to explore attitudes and values regarding healthy
relationship development with special consideration given to issues
related to those on the ASD or LD Spectrum. Topics such as friendship
building, communication skills, relationship dynamics, and sexuality
education are essential when building this component into a dedicated
curriculum. Students need instruction and then reinforcement in
relationship development, as well as further instruction in common

---
## Page 839
strategies from initiating friendships and conversations to learning how
and when to be intimate.
Attitudes and values regarding healthy relationship development as well
as the five stages of friendship (Chapter 6 – Skills for Independent
Living) are explored during these classes.
Residential Advisors
In a combined effort with student advisors, Career Coordinators,
wellness instructors, and other support staff, residential advisors work
with students individually on a variety of “Executive Function” tasks
including grocery shopping, cooking, personal organization strategies,
cleaning, laundry, and health and wellness regimes. Students learn to
develop these skills in groups and individually with staff.
Residential advisors can also help students with practical money
management skills such as banking and budgeting and paying their
monthly bills. Residential advisors assist each individual with balancing
needs v.s wants and learning how to save for future purchases.
CREATING A TRANSITIONING PROGRAM
As the number of young adults diagnosed with ASD or LD explodes to
nearly 1 in 100, it is important to address the type of support each
individual will need as they transition to college, vocational programs, or
to full- or part-time jobs.
Transition Counseling
Young adults on the ASD and LD Spectrum often take the same
challenges they had during their high school years in areas of social,
organizational, and “Executive Function” skills with them as they
transition into college, vocational training, or into a full- or part-time job.

---
## Page 840
Many go off to a traditional college or follow a career path but aren’t
successful because their school or workplace does not provide the day-
to-day supports they require. Even though these bright young adults
have great potential, many will return home from college, quit a
vocational program or job, and isolate themselves, and lose motivation.
Transition counseling assists students with planning and preparing for
their next pivotal step after attending a dedicated ASD/LD support
program. Whether it’s living independently, employment, or transferring
to a college or career program, a transition counselor or advisor works
closely with students and their families to create a plan for future
success (ideally), before the student leaves high school or when he or
she attempts to re-enter a college or vocational program or rejoin the
workforce after an unsuccessful experience.
SAMPLE SCHEDULE OF A DEDICATED
AUTISM AND LEARNING DIFFERENCES
CURRICULUM
The following student schedule is provided as an example and sample
of how a dedicated curriculum based on skills for life, school, work,
and independent living might be structured within a first year college
student’s average day and includes the student’s external internship
program as well.

---
## Page 841
Sample student schedule for a dedicated Autism and Learning Differences curriculum
This is meant to show readers that various scheduling options are
possible and will depend on the level of support that an individual
needs, what skills need developing, the student’s career or academic
pathway, and/or other areas of interest or study.
Specific classes that encompass all of the above are built around
academic requirements and will work well for those creating a 100%
dedicated program for helping young people on the ASD/LD Spectrum
that is similar to that of the programming offered at the College
Internship Program (CIP).
Partial models using some of the above classes and services are also
possible. It is for schools, educators, and support professionals to
determine what parts of the above dedicated curriculum model will best
suit the needs of the school and the needs of the population the school
will serve.
Creating a Dispersed Integration
Model

---
## Page 842
TEACHING INSIGHT: This next section acts as a springboard for
determining what parts of the dedicated model (detailed on previous
pages) can be realistically merged into an existing curriculum. Adding
specific classes and/or specific advising, academic, and career
supports, as well as classes in social thinking and mentoring are
possible.
In many instances schools will integrate the teaching of skills for life,
school, work, and independent living to students on the “ASD or LD
Spectrum” into regular academic coursework. Some schools will
choose to develop – based on the percentages of ASD and LD
students enrolled at the school – specific classes that augment what
is being imparted during the teaching of standard academic subjects.
The concessions teachers will make as they integrate the teaching of
“key life skills” into their classes will benefit all students in the class
and will (as the experts and research tells us) improve academic
outcomes for the subject being taught for everyone, not just the
students in the class with an “ASD or LD” diagnosis.
GETTING STARTED: ACADEMIC
INTEGRATION
An academic curriculum (in any mandated subject area) that imparts
skills for life, school, work, and independent living to students on the
ASD/LD Spectrum incorporates training and supports the continuing
education of the staff members who will be integrating the components
outlined into their specific subject area.
EMBEDDING SKILLS FOR LIFE, SCHOOL,
WORK, AND INDEPENDENT LIVING
Embedding key life skills into a specific subject or class is done by
seizing each and every teachable moment to model and demonstrate
the behaviors that will help students improve their skills for life, school,

---
## Page 843
work, and eventual independent living. Embedding these skills should
not hinder the primary subject but underpin it. Key life skills can come
from specific learning outcomes in their own right or contribute to the
main subject course outcomes. Here are three examples:
1. In an English class, students who want to improve their confidence
and ability to talk to other people interested in the subject they have
chosen to write an essay about, can work in small groups or with a
partner on specific tasks. As essays are developed and written,
students would ask each other to edit, give poignant feedback, or
assistance with grammar and punctuation basics if needed.
Students would then practice reading their essays within their small
groups. As they practice and refine what they have written, they
gain the confidence needed to present an oral version of their work
to the class. These small incremental steps taken to write an
interesting and effective essay, lead to the learning outcomes of the
academic coursework.
The group work done during the English classes allows each
individual to learn appropriate communication skills and offer peer-
to-peer feedback as they fully participate, interact with, and help
each other. Students also learn to use accurate formatting, spelling,
punctuation, and grammar, as well as how to stay on topic while
making the content of their essays appealing and interesting.
2. In a physical education class where wellness, exercise, and nutrition
are key learning outcomes, students could work in small groups to
talk about what they have learned in class and discuss their own
needs with others. As they develop individual plans with input from
teachers and peers they also get to hear some of the health benefits
or challenges their classmates have experienced. As the educator
imparts knowledge, the small group work becomes a speaking and
listening outcome and a learning reflection that is applied to each
individual as they create wellness plans with peer input.
3. In a math class, students could practice fractions, proportion, and
scale through learning the art of origami. (Do not be afraid to think

---
## Page 844
outside of the box when creating ways to impart key life skills.) In
small groups, students experiment, talk to each other, and conclude
why paper can only be folded a fixed number of times.
They can also learn fractions by baking a real pie instead of
watching a teacher draw a circle on a board, or to learn how to
accurately work with ingredients, time, and measuring by baking a
loaf of bread.
These examples could be expanded exponentially to encompass a
service-learning project wherein students start a soup kitchen, teach
nutrition to young children, teach origami at a retirement community
center, or run an essay contest. The opportunities to merge academic
content with experiential active learning where key life skills are
developed are endless.
Embedding skills for life, school, work and independent living into an
academic curriculum is no different than content teaching in many
ways. The classroom instructor assesses each student, then makes
sure he or she is taught something new, only this time it is not just
content based, it includes key life skills.
Educators can then gauge where the student is when they start the
class, design outcomes that reflect what he or she can achieve while
participating in the class, monitor these outcomes along the way, revise
if necessary, and then reassess what the student has achieved at the
end of the course.
Assessing each student’s ability in terms of key life skills (see Chapter
10) will help each educator identify specific learning difficulties within an
ASD or LD diagnosis such as dyslexia, sensory issues, hyperactivity,
inability to focus, or other learning challenges. As with any assessment
activity, this will enable the educator to differentiate and alter teaching
approaches according to the different learner levels in the class.
Engaging students with the real skills to cope with life, school, work,
and independent living in the future requires embedding these skills
across content areas in a competency-based learning environment
during all formative school years. Students learn best and retain

---
## Page 845
knowledge when actively engaged and fully participating in their
learning.
USING A FULLY DISPERSED MODEL
•
Enables innovative learning methods that integrate the use of
supportive technologies, inquiry- and problem-based approaches,
and higher order thinking skills.
•
Highlights ways teachers can seize opportunities for integrating
skills, tools, and teaching strategies into their classroom practice –
and helps them identify what traditional teaching activities, such as
lecturing, they might replace or de-emphasize.
•
Figures out ways to allow students to work in small groups and to
impart knowledge to each other.
•
Balances direct instruction with project-oriented teaching methods.
•
Illustrates how a deeper understanding of subject matter can
actually enhance problem solving, critical thinking, and develop
skills for life, school, work, and future independent living.
•
Encourages the integration of community resources beyond school
walls and service/active learning activities.
•
Enables the development of professional and collaborative teaching
communities for educators that model the kinds of classroom
instruction that best promotes the active learning of key life skills.
•
Cultivates an educator’s ability to identify an individual’s particular
learning style and his or her strengths and weaknesses.
•
Helps teachers develop their own abilities for using various
strategies (such as formative assessments), to reach ASD and LD
students and create environments that support differentiated
teaching and learning.
•
Encourages 
knowledge 
sharing 
among 
communities 
of
practitioners, 
using 
face-to-face, 
virtual, 
and 
blended

---
## Page 846
communications.
CLASSROOM INTEGRATION
Successful classroom and subject area integration for teaching skills
for life, school, work, and future independent living to those on the ASD
and LD Spectrum involves using a variety of techniques and strategies,
as listed below.
Thinking Creatively
Using a wide range of creativity techniques (such as brainstorming,
mind mapping, SWOT analyses, and small group work), students come
up with new and worthwhile ideas for retaining knowledge.
Students learn to elaborate upon, refine, analyze, and evaluate ideas in
order to improve and retain knowledge. This allows each individual to
maximize his or her creative efforts for thinking and learning in a way
that best suits his or her needs.
Learning and Studying with Others
Students develop, implement, and communicate new ideas to others in
small groups that enable each person to contribute ideas to the learning
process.
Students are given opportunities to be open and responsive to new and
diverse perspectives that incorporate the input and feedback of the
group into the work of each classroom session.
Students are provided the opportunity for each person in the group or
learning cell to understand that acquiring knowledge is a long-term,
cyclical process of small successes and frequent mistakes, or as this
book frequently states: approximations to a right or correct answer.

---
## Page 847
Critical Thinking and Problem Solving
Students learn how to use various types of reasoning (inductive,
deductive, etc.) to solve problems that are appropriate to the
knowledge and course content being taught.
Students learn to make individual judgments and decisions as they
analyze and evaluate evidence, arguments, claims and beliefs, and ask
questions based on what they are hearing or seeing in class.
Students learn to make connections between information and
arguments, interpret the information being taught, and then draw
conclusions based on the best analysis.
Students become active participants in the acquisition of information.
Knowledge is retained as they ask significant questions that clarify
various points of view and further enhance their understanding of the
subject material.
Communicating Clearly
Students learn valuable communication skills in all subject areas as
they articulate thoughts and ideas effectively using oral, written, and
nonverbal forms and contexts in each academic class setting.
Students learn to listen effectively and decipher meaning, knowledge,
values, and attitudes.
Collaboration with Others/Teamwork
Students demonstrate the ability to work effectively and respectfully
with diverse teams.
Students exercise flexibility and willingness to be helpful in making
necessary compromises to accomplish a common goal.

---
## Page 848
Students learn to assume shared responsibility for collaborative work,
and value the individual contributions made by each team member.
CIP Berkshire students and teachers celebrating the end of a dedicated curriculum model
year
Imparting Life and Career Skills
Today’s life, school, and work environments require far more key life
and thinking skills, as well as content knowledge of specific academic
subjects, than ever before. Teaching in a way that allows each student
to acquire the ability to navigate complex life and work environments
requires each student (on the ASD/LD Spectrum or not) to develop key
life skills and content knowledge in each academic class they take.
Students need to be able to:
•
Be adaptable and flexible. They need to acquire the ability to
change course in mid-stream (if necessary), through various
varied roles, responsibilities, schedules, and contexts.
•
Incorporate feedback effectively and to deal positively with
praise, setbacks, and criticism.
•
Understand, negotiate, and balance diverse views and beliefs to
reach workable solutions in a variety of classroom settings.

---
## Page 849
•
Manage goals and time by monitoring, defining, prioritizing, and
completing tasks without direct oversight.
•
Demonstrate a commitment to learning as a lifelong process.
•
Reflect critically on past experiences in order to inform future
progress.
•
Interact effectively with others by knowing when it is appropriate
to listen and when to speak.
•
Behave in a respectable and professional manner as they work
with others to embrace inclusion.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: There is no right or wrong way to help
adolescents and young adults gain skills for life, school, work, and
independent living. The advice given in this chapter is based on
things that have worked for CIP (College Internship Program) at our
six centers in the United States. We also know from experience that
these strategies, ideas, and methodology have worked in other or
similar support programs that dedicate 100% of their time to working
with adolescents and young adults on the ASD or LD Spectrum.
As stated throughout the chapter, the various models presented can
be adapted into any learning environment. The end result of
implementing a dedicated or a dispersed model will be graduating
young adults with an ASD or LD diagnosis who have the skills needed
to become productive and contributing members of society.
I hope that these materials are useful as you take the content of this
book and apply it to the needs of your particular learning environment
and that your goal becomes that of CIP’s: Continuing to embrace
inclusion and prepare these amazing young men and women for life
outside the classroom setting.

---
## Page 850
Assessment, Evaluation, and
Measuring Outcomes
CHAPTER 10 OVERVIEW:
ASSESSING SKILLS FOR LIFE
ASSESSING SKILLS FOR SCHOOL
ASSESSING SKILLS FOR WORK
ASSESSING SKILLS FOR INDEPENDENT LIVING
ASSESSING “EXECUTIVE FUNCTION (EF)” SKILLS

---
## Page 851
Assessment, Evaluation, and
Measuring Outcomes
Assessment is an important part of monitoring the progress of
adolescents and young adults as they work toward achieving goals. A
good way of doing this is through regularly scheduled assessment and
review. This process should involve the young people themselves and
there are many ideas and suggestions for doing this, as well as forms
and worksheets, included in this chapter. Documentation of
improvement, achieved goals, and other successes should be included
in each individual’s record of achievement and in his or her portfolio/s.
Assessment in connection with Autism and Learning Differences (An
Active Learning Teaching Toolkit) does not, in any way imply, that
adolescents or young adults are failing a competency level or have
failed to achieve or obtain a skill set.
This chapter is not about judgment of the worth, personality, or the
value of an individual person. It is about finding areas of strength and
building upon those strengths while developing strategies to address
limitations. This is particularly important to keep in mind when
assessing progress of adolescents and young adults on the ASD/LD
Spectrum. It is also equally important to remember when using the
assessment strategies and models in this book, that progress can
come from:
•
The adolescents and young adults themselves through self-
assessment and peer-aided assessment that provides evidence
of progress towards preset goals and objectives.
•
Teachers', clinicians', professionals', and parents' assessment
of progress and results obtained or areas that still need to be
worked on or mastered.

---
## Page 852
Chapter 10 promotes active participation by encouraging students to
review and record their own progress and develop other skills such as
managing their own learning through various record keeping methods.
This includes creating a personal progress file or an achievements
journal.
The focus of the chapter is to help educators and support professionals
utilize tried and true assessment strategies that will assess the four
competency areas: skills for life, school, work, and independent living
fairly and effectively.
PERSONAL INSIGHT: Students and the educators and support
professionals who work with them should value, praise, and
acknowledge any improvement (however slight), in all the competency
areas. As students recognize and identify their own learning goals and
aspirations they begin to understand that any progress attained allows
for the integral development of each individual at his or her own pace.
This is also a great opportunity for you and the young men and
women you work with to practice reflection. Students can ask
themselves: How have my ideas about what I am learning, doing, or
are capable of changed now that I’ve evaluated my progress? Am I
satisfied with the evaluation or can I “Reframe” or change the way I
am working to attain goals and outcomes?
Assessment will give everyone a clear picture of goals and progress to
date. Various assessment strategies will guide each individual and
give purpose to the action of assessment. As everyone stays focused
and works together to create positive change, growth, further ability,
and achievement through assessment, the end result is progress and
that aids in each individual’s growth, maturity, and further self-
assessment.
I always encourage each student I work with to: “Set your sights high,
the higher the better. Expect the most wonderful things to happen, not
in the future but now.” This is the ultimate goal of assessing progress.
This chapter is divided into five key segments:
•
Section I: Assessing Skills for Life.

---
## Page 853
•
Section II: Assessing Skills for School.
•
Section III: Assessing Skills for Work.
•
Section IV: Assessing Skills for Independent Living.
•
Section V: Assessing “Executive Function (EF)” Skills.
This last section assesses “EF” ability across the four competency
areas: skills for life, school, work, and independent living.
GUIDELINES FOR ASSESSING PROGRESS:
SETTING THE SCENE
Assessing progress in skills for life, school, work, and independent
living, whether educator, clinician, or support professional led, needs to
be based on a solid foundation that is secured by trust, fairness, and
mutual respect. Here are some guidelines for creating an atmosphere
that will invite this to happen.
Build relationships:
Good relationships are the foundation for better communication,
improved decision-making, and increased performance as you and the
young men and women you work with set goals, assess progress, and
work on reaching goals. The most direct way to build relationships is by
getting to know students personally, through a confidential dialog, and
then listening – really listening – to what each individual has to say.
Involve students in decisions:
When a decision is coming up that affects an individual, involve him or
her in the decision-making process … in some way. This doesn’t mean
you hand over the store, but it does mean you discuss issues and ask
the student to help you generate ideas or solutions.
Communicate the big picture:
Update students regularly on the big picture, how he or she is doing,
long-range plans, new programs, new developments, problems, and

---
## Page 854
opportunities. When students understand the big picture, they make
better progress.
Use rewards:
Recognize and reward progress, goals met, or desired behavior
changes with ceremonies, awards, and putting students in charge of
specific things or by giving individuals leadership roles. Make
recognition very public and personal so that it is clear to everyone what
outcomes and achievements are valued and desired.
Tell students where they fit in:
Make sure each individual understands the overall program/school
process for evaluating progress. Each one should understand how he
or she fits in, and how the school/facility will be measuring his or her
performance.
PROCESS EVALUATIONS AND OUTCOME
EVALUATIONS

---
## Page 855
The term process evaluation documents what has been done and by
whom. The term outcome evaluation helps those who work with
ASD/LD adolescents and young adults evaluate the content of each
skill set that has been delivered and helps to chart the degree of
success imparting the knowledge has achieved.
An outcome evaluation is used to determine and record progress and a
process evaluation is used primarily in self-assessments that are then
factored into an end result of achievement with the outcome
evaluation. Examples are given throughout this chapter for both
process and outcome evaluations in Sections I to V.
Outcome evaluations show teachers, support professionals, and
students whether what has been taught has made a difference. Both
process indicators and outcome indicators are necessary for a
thorough evaluation.

---
## Page 856
Here are some examples of how process and outcome
evaluations work:
•
Was the skill set taught as intended? (Process)
•
Did students’ knowledge, attitude, and skill level for the
competency area change as intended? (Outcome)
For educators/facilitators:
•
Was the teaching methodology enhanced? If so, how?
(Process)
•
Did educator/facilitator knowledge, attitudes, and skills change
as intended? (Outcome)

---
## Page 857
For participants:
•
Did students actively participate in design, delivery, and
evaluation of the program? (Process)
•
Did learning outcomes improve as a result of the program?
(Outcome)
For each skill set evaluated:
•
Was the methodology implemented as intended? (Process)
•
Did students’ ability level change as intended? (Outcome)
Indicators that process and outcome evaluations are effective:
•
Knowledge, attitudes, and skills are achieved by the student at
the time of or very soon after the educational activities are
completed.
•
Educators and support professionals feel confident that each
skill set they have taught (skills for life, school, work, and
independent living) has a measurable outcome and can be
applied by each individual student to real-life situations or
toward reaching new goals set in order to do so.
OVERVIEW
All of the assessments in the Sections 1 to V dovetail with the content
of each Skill Competency Chapter: Skills for Life, School, Work, and
Independent Living in this book. These assessments are a good way of
measuring progress made during the course of instruction as outlined
in those specific chapters. The assessments can also be used as
needed when integrating the content from the book in specific classes,
curricula, or during one-on-one tutoring sessions that augment
classroom instruction.
Initially, assessments should be done at the beginning of the course of
instruction and then at intervals (i.e., every two or three months at the
teacher’s discretion). This will help students and their teachers or

---
## Page 858
support professionals determine where progress is being made and
where skills need improvement.
As goals are set and reached, assessment continues until each skill or
skill set is acquired. A final assessment is done at the end of the
coursework and this goes in the student’s personal record file or record
of achievement.
Section I: Assessing Skills for Life

Instructions: Take this self-assessment when you first begin your wellness
classes or are working toward developing the wellness skills listed below. Then
self-assess every two or three months (at your teacher’s discretion), to help you
figure out the areas that you need to improve upon. Discuss the results with the
educators, clinicians, or support professionals who are helping you acquire these
skills. Together you can plan a course of action, set goals, and plan for the next
reassessment date.

© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
“Using Sensory Assessments Effectively”
TEACHING INSIGHT: If the young men and women you are
working with are facing significant sensory issues a further
profile is usually warranted. There are many specific profiles
that 
can 
be 
used 
for 
this 
purpose 
such 
as 
the
Adolescent/Adult 
Sensory 
Profile®, 
see:
www./pearsonclinical.com/therapy/products/100000566/senso

---
## Page 876
ry-profile.html, that can be administered by an OT or support
professional trained to do so. Janell Yonkman, CIP
Bloomington’s Occupational Therapist (OT), offers a good
case study as an example that illustrates the strategies and
solutions she used to address her student’s issues. This case
study shows the benefits achieved from using an in depth
sensory assessment to deal with sensory issues so that her
student could acquire the skills needed to cope with her
issues.
Background:
Prior to joining CIP I worked ten years at a large children’s
hospital in Indiana providing occupational therapy to pediatric
patients diagnosed with autism. The patients I treated were
typically between the ages of three and ten and many were
diagnosed with sensory processing issues. Outside of my
clinical practice I took the opportunity to attend as many
sensory integration courses as I could to increase my
knowledge.
Children with sensory processing issues may be over or
undersensitive to information, and this can affect how they
interact with their environment. Based on the evaluation of the
child I was working with and their sensory needs I would
develop a treatment plan that might include the use of sensory
equipment followed by a functional task. For example, if a
young student had difficulty hanging up his or her backpack
and coat at school I might have the student engage in an
obstacle course on his or her hands and knees to help increase
body awareness. This awareness would eventually allow him or
her to hang up the backpack and coat on the hook. The
sensory input helps to get the child ready for functional
engagement in activity.
After completing my first assessments of the students at the
College Internship Program (CIP), I knew that my treatment

---
## Page 877
plans would need to be adapted to treat the unique challenges
faced by students in their late teens and early twenties. I was
excited by the various aspects of applying the concepts of
sensory integration to their challenges to see if we could identify
techniques to help CIP students in their daily lives.
A sensory story:
In 2014 I met a student at CIP named Rachel. Rachel is 22 and
she had been at CIP for approximately one year. She has an
amazing ability to articulate her needs and we worked closely
together to identify a treatment plan for her.
Rachel exhibits many of the typical sensory seeking behaviors.
For example she would frequently spin/twirl, put pressure on
her chin using the topside of her hands, and smell items
inappropriately. These behaviors have made it difficult for
Rachel to reach her potential independent living, at college, at
work, and in making friends.
Rachel is a very bright woman and she expressed a desire to
develop an understanding of why she used sensory behaviors
inappropriately and what need they were fulfilling. Once she
began to understand why she did what she was doing, we
worked together to identify alternative methods to meet her
sensory needs that would be more appropriate and less socially
awkward.
Assessment:
At the beginning of our treatment sessions Rachel completed a
printed version of an online Adolescent/Adult Sensory Profile
purchased for this purpose. This required her to fill out a
questionnaire. As she performed daily living tasks in the
assessment, I observed her actions and noted any sensory
behaviors as well. Upon completion of the assessment we

---
## Page 878
reviewed each of her behaviors and developed an alternative
activity to meet her sensory needs.
I am including a few examples from Rachel’s self-assessment
of her current behaviors and some recommended replacement
behaviors. While these are not a complete picture of her
sensory therapy, they are good examples of the process and
how sensory integration therapy can be successfully used to
help students like Rachel.
RACHEL’S SENSORY SELF-
ASSESSMENT
PROPRIOCEPTION: awareness of the position of one’s
own body.
Behavior: pressure under chin/on head.
Why do you do that?
1. “It feels good.” 2. “Helps with anxiety.”
When do you need to do it?
1. “When excited.” 2. “When happy.”
When I explained that the pressure on her head/chin area could
be seen as an odd behavior, she said, “I shouldn’t do it at a job
interview then?”
Suggested replacement behaviors:
Squeezing hands together.
Interlace fingers and bend fingers back.
Sitting on hands.
Squeezing forearms.

---
## Page 879
Rachel’s reaction to the replacement behaviors:
“The pressure on my arms and hands felt good and the
replacement behavior for chin pressure, of squeezing my hands
together gave me a similar sensation.”
OLFACTORY: sense of smell.
Behavior: smelling items inappropriately.
Rachel and I discussed that people may think it is odd if she
frequently smells objects.
Replacement behavior:
I provided Rachel with a lavender air freshener to have on hand
when she felt the urge to smell an object.
Rachel’s reaction: “It smelled good and getting that good
smell and processing that in my brain helped me stop sniffing
other things.”
VESTIBULAR: information related to movement and head
position.
Behavior: spinning/twirling.
Why do you do that?
1. “It feels good.” 2. “It helps with stress.”
When do you do it?
1. “When happy.” 2. “When stressed.”
When is it appropriate?
1. “Bedroom.”
When is it not appropriate?
1. “Classroom.” 2. “Lab.”
Replacement behaviors:

---
## Page 880
Jumping on mini-trampoline or bouncing on ball.
Occasionally twirling in window room.
Sitting in a swivel chair for tutoring/academics to allow
intermittent vestibular input.
Rachel’s feedback: “Jumping on the trampoline felt good, and
the motion of jumping was similar to the motion of twirling;
because of this, the trampoline helped me stop twirling around
other people.”
These simple techniques offered Rachel a more socially
appropriate set of behaviors to meet her sensory needs.
Interestingly she had already been engaged in an activity prior
to her time at CIP that helped address her sensory processing
challenges. She is an experienced horseback rider and has
won several gold medals in the Special Olympics.
HORSEBACK RIDING:
Why do you like horseback riding and how does it meet
any of your sensory needs?
“I like it because I like horses, and I get sensory input for things
such as smell, bouncing, and from the feel of the horse.”
What do you like about the smell?
“I like the way the horses and barn smell and this gives me
sensory input.”
What do you like about the bouncing?
“I like bouncing because when I am bouncing while trotting, it
feels a little like twirling or jumping on the trampoline. Because
of this, it gives me sensory input.”
What do you like about the feel of the horse?
“The horse feels very soft and I like this because I like to touch
animals and feel them. Because of this I get some tactile input.”

---
## Page 881
Conclusion:
There are many, many activities and techniques that can be
adapted to help students with sensory issues. For instance:
Developing a plan that utilizes activities that students already
enjoy to help them interact more appropriately in social,
educational, and work settings.
Rachel training for the Special Olympics.
Near the end of our sessions I asked Rachel if she liked
working on her sensory issues with an occupational therapist.
Her response is why I love my job:
“I like occupational therapy because it helps me with finding
appropriate ways to fulfill my sensory needs, and I feel that this
is sometimes fun. Occupational therapy is important because
you learn appropriate sensory stimulation, so you don’t do
inappropriate things that people think is weird or odd.”

---
## Page 882
Janell Yonkman, Occupational
Therapist, CIP Bloomington
Section II: Assessing Skills for
School
As students create an individual “Person Centered Plan” (PCP) that
takes into consideration their “special interests”, hopes, dreams,
wishes, and potential career paths they develop individual, academic
strategies to succeed in school with measurable outcomes and realistic
goals.
This section helps teachers and students determine if the student has
acquired the skills and strategies that are needed for effective learning
and development in the school setting.
Researchers have proven that non-cognitive skills and attributes are
just as important as cognitive skills in determining academic success.
The assessments in this chapter will focus on whether or not students
have achieved the following skills for school: teamwork, creativity,
ethics, resilience, a curiosity for learning, and time management.

---

Discuss the results with the educators, clinicians, or support professionals who are
helping you acquire skills for school. Together you can plan a course of action, set
goals, and plan for the next reassessment date.

## Page 898
Section III: Assessing Skills for
Work
Assessing work skills involves looking at the student’s interests,
transferable skills, and qualifications as he or she looks for an
internship, internship to work program, and full- or part-time work.
Students also need to have the ability to create a resume, fill out

---
## Page 899
applications, prepare for a job interview, and follow up after the
interview has taken place. This assessment also looks at the abilities
and skills needed to perform a job well and then looks at punctuality,
work speed, ability to work as part of a team, and to work with attention
to detail. Assessing the student’s ability to pay attention to detail, take
initiative, and accept feedback are critical to mastering skills for work.
Looking at endurance, work schedules, disclosure, and obtaining
workplace accommodations round out the assessment.
TEACHING INSIGHT: The form on page 432 will be very useful to
those providing all aspects of career services to young adults on the
ASD or LD Spectrum. It was created by Jennifer Kolarik, the Lead
Career Coordinator at CIP Brevard. It can be used by employers,
supervisors, and job coaches to assess how volunteer work, an
internship, or a part- or full-time job is going. This feedback is
valuable. As the student works on self-assessment, this tool can help
those working with the student by adding the employer’s or
supervisor’s feedback for comparison and then to formulate a plan for
improvement or action. These two assessments together will allow
teachers, clinicians, and support professionals providing career
services to work with students so that they can be as successful as
possible in the workplace arena.
SKILLS FOR WORK: A Student Self-
Assessment
FOCUS: WORKPLACE READINESS AND WORKPLACE
SKILLS
Name: __________       Date: ________

Instructions: Take this self-assessment when you begin
working with your career advisor, teacher, or the support
professionals who are helping you prepare to begin working.
Work on developing the skills listed below then self-assess
every two or three months (at your teacher’s or advisor’s
discretion), to help you figure out the areas that you need
improvement on. Discuss the results with the people who are
helping you acquire skills for work. Together you can plan a
course of action, set goals, and plan for the next
reassessment date.

## Page 904
© Autism and Learning Differences – An Active Learning Teaching Toolkit
Section IV: Assessing Skills for
Independent Living
This section will assess the skills and abilities that students will need to
acquire so that they can be successful when living independently alone
or with others (roommates, friends, or in a relationship). The
assessments cover residential strategies, as well as daily living and
include self-care (including managing anxiety and stress and taking
medications), personal hygiene, handling finances, and being fiscally
responsible, menu planning, grocery shopping, cooking, housekeeping,
and integrating into the community.

---

---

## Page 911
Demonstration Assessment:
INDEPENDENT LIVING SKILLS
Name: ___________       Date: _____
Assessor’s Name: _____________
Instructions: This is a demonstration assessment – the young adult will show you
by demonstration that he or she can do each skill. It should be done around the
same time that the Student Self-Assessment for Independent Living Skills is
completed. Educators, clinicians, and support professionals can use both to get an
accurate reading of where the individual is making progress and where he or she
needs to set goals for improvement. Discussing the progress and reality of both
assessments together will allow for a dialogue that leads to praising the areas the
ASD or LD adolescent or young adult is doing well in, or assisting the student with
further instruction and/or behavior modification strategies for areas that need
improvement or strengthening. The assessment should be done at the beginning of
the course of study or skill acquisition curriculum and then every two or three
months, as well as at the end of the coursework term or semester.
Circle or mark one answer that fits best: (Yes, Sometimes,
or No)

No

## Page 917
Section V: Assessing “Executive
Function (EF)” Skills
The “prefrontal cortex” is responsible for all cognitive functioning and
processing and is the last component to develop and mature in
adolescents and young adults on the ASD and LD Spectrum.
With neurotypicals, maturation occurs by age 25; this is three to five
years later with an ASD or LD young adult. This later maturation
process is often an explanation for unpredictable teen behavior.
“Executive Function” ability in adolescents and young adults on the
Spectrum will continue to develop well into adulthood.
“Executive Function” difficulties can lead to:
•
Difficulty predicting what will happen next (creates anxiety and
transition problems).

---
## Page 918
•
Inflexibility/getting stuck/needing to preserve sameness.
•
Difficulty attending/concentrating.
•
Impulsivity (blurting out).
•
Difficulty with working memory so that it is not available when
needed.
•
Difficulty managing and allocating time (especially with respect
to long-term assignments; homework).
•
Difficulty keeping track of belongings/materials; forgetting things.
•
Difficulty with multi-step or complex tasks.
•
Disorganization.
•
Difficulty working in groups.
•
Difficulty with planning.
“Executive Functions” impact all areas of development, in particular:
•
Academic studies.
•
Money management.
•
Intrapersonal relationships.
•
Interpersonal relationships.
•
Health and fitness.
•
Occupational and organizational skill development.
Intervention and direct instruction, explicit feedback, and assessment of “Executive Function”
skills will help students build “EF” ability.
“EXECUTIVE FUNCTION” SKILLS: A
Student Self-Assessment

---
