Name: ________________       Date: __________
Instructions: Take this self-assessment when you begin working with your
teacher, advisor, or the support professionals who are helping you acquire
“Executive Function (EF)” skills for life, school, work, and independent living.
Work on developing the skills listed below then self-assess every two or three
months (at your teacher’s or advisor’s discretion), to help you figure out the areas
that you need improvement on. Discuss the results with the people who are helping
you acquire these skills. Together you can plan a course of action, set goals, and
plan for the next reassessment date.

## Page 929
In Their Own Words: Stories from
CIP
As stated throughout this Teaching Toolkit, the College Internship
Program (CIP) is the result of more than 30 plus years of research and
development leading to the creation of cutting edge curriculum
strategies, as well as teaching and assessment materials. This
innovative way of working with young adults on the ASD and LD
Spectrum has led to each one developing skills for life, school, work,
and independent living.
The Autism and Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit) model of working with young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum
has been successfully implemented throughout CIP’s six centers in the
United States with thousands of students aged 16–26.
Each CIP Center has a Program Director, as well as staff members and
support personnel who come from a diverse background of psychology,
counseling, teaching, administration, mentoring, and other human
service fields. Many of CIP’s staff have had or now have personal
connections with those on the Autism and/or Learning Differences
Spectrum.
CIP’s Program Directors and their staff members are passionate about
working with ASD and LD students. These incredibly talented
individuals understand the unique strengths and challenges that each
student possesses and together they prepare CIP’s students for
meaningful lives, positive school experiences, workplace readiness,
and successful independent living.
In this chapter readers will experience the pedagogy and methodology
of this book through the eyes of the Program Directors, their staff
members, and CIP’s students and alumni. As you read their personal

---
## Page 930
stories and essays … the first ten chapters of this book come to life and
become reality based for each reader.
The creative writing, poetry, photographs, and artwork that accompany
some of the stories from individual students detailing their participatory
experiences at CIP and those of the staff members who work with them
every day are genuine and heartfelt.
The goal of this very personal chapter is to inspire educators, clinicians,
and professionals to venture forward with confidence and a realistic
viewpoint of how and why this methodology works. It is also meant to
be a celebration of the individual journey that each person who has
contributed to this chapter has taken.
By sharing these insights and words with the readers of this book, the
voices of CIP’s Program Directors, their staff members, and the young
men and women who have benefited from using the core principals
outlined in this book are validated, heard, and honored.
WITH THANKS: I want to personally thank Jay Mikush and the editor
of Autism/Asperger’s Digest for allowing the story Jay wrote for their
magazine to be used in this chapter. Jay’s goals, hopes, wishes,
dreams, and aspirations are clear and he is a very motivated young

---
## Page 931
man. This real-time and real-place example shows how obtaining
skills for life, school, work, and independent living brought forth Jay’s
abundance of talents and skills within a framework designed to assist
him in acquiring essential life skills.
MY JOURNEY TO INDEPENDENCE
by Jay Mikush, CIP Brevard Alumnus
My name is Jay Mikush and I am 23 years old. When I was first
diagnosed with Asperger’s Syndrome (AS) at the age of six, I had many
challenges interacting with other children. Over the years, I have
learned how to be more social with all sorts of people.
I am proud to have been born and raised in wonderful Winston-Salem,
North Carolina. After I graduated from high school, I enrolled in the
College Internship Program’s (CIP’s) Brevard Center, a post high
school program for young adults with ASD and other Learning
Differences. During this time I also attended Brevard Community

---
## Page 932
College in Melbourne, Florida, where I learned how to live
independently.
After I graduated from high school, I found out about CIP and its
services from my parents. At first, I was not terribly interested in going
because I could not imagine being away from home. However, when I
arrived at CIP and got used to being away from home and knowing
everyone and how the program worked, I got accustomed to it and
realized it was a helpful environment to live in. I had many friends at
Brevard, and I participated in a variety of activities, including bowling
and going to the beach.
Social Skills:
If I were to choose one of the most critical skills that I improved upon
within the last few years, it would be social skills. I had the biggest
issue with not being able to say “no” to peer pressure. This occurred
mostly in high school where it caused me to get in a lot of trouble with
my friends – who I now identify for what they really are – enemies. Now
I am able to say “no” in potentially dangerous situations.
I 
have 
made 
a 
lot 
of 
friends 
through 
the 
iCan 
House
(www.icanhouse.org), a place for young adults with ASD and social
challenges in Winston-Salem, where I do many things with friends in
my spare time such as visiting amusement parks, going to movies,
biking, swimming, attending the local fair, bowling, mini golfing, and
much more. I also keep in touch with peers from CIP in the Melbourne
area through social media such as Skype and Facebook.
Independent Living Skills:
After attending CIP, I moved back to North Carolina and I now live in
my own apartment with a roommate. I am working with a life coach
from the iCan House who is helping me with budgeting, cooking, and
other skills. I feel that I have improved in the financial area because I do
not spend as much on things that I want, such as a $100 iTunes gift
card. I generally focus on things that I need such as groceries or gas.
Now I am much more cautious with spending my money.
Job Skills:

---
## Page 933
I have a job coach from a service called Vocational Rehabilitation who
is assisting me in seeking a full-time career in the aviation industry once
I complete my Associate’s degree in aviation management.
DJ: I own and operate a professional mobile DJ (disc jockey) business
and do many types of parties and events throughout the city, county,
and state. My long-term goal in life is to be one of the best and well-
known DJs in my area.
Customer Service/Retail: I recently started working part-time at a
local independent movie theater that shows films such as
documentaries and independent films. I have been working at this job
for about two months now, and I am really enjoying it. My job duties
consist of cashiering, ticket handling, and working in the concession
area popping popcorn, pouring drinks, and assisting customers’ needs.
Aviation: I have a private pilot certificate and fly regularly in my spare
time. I am currently studying in the aviation management program at
Guilford Technical Community College in Greensboro, North Carolina. I
have an internship at the local branch of Landmark Aviation, which is a
nationwide aircraft charter company. Besides helping out at Landmark,
I also wash and wax aircraft at the local flight school to make their
airplanes look spic-and-span for customers. With my internship at
Landmark Aviation and work at the local flight school, I hope to build
upon my experiences to gain more knowledge of the aviation industry.
My goal is to own and manage my own airport.
I feel like CIP helped me in many ways because I am now able to live
without my parents or other assistance in various areas. I’ve improved
greatly in a variety of ways: cooking, cleaning, social, and financial.
CIP’s services have helped me become independent, live happily, and
become the person who I am today.
BIOGRAPHY: Jay Mikush has two siblings and a dog named Banjo.
He loves skiing, riding roller coasters, flying airplanes, mobile DJing,
biking, singing, karaoke, swimming, hanging out with friends, and
listening to and writing music.

---
## Page 934
This article is reprinted with permission from Autism/Asperger’s
Digest, January/February 2014 and with permission from the author,
Jay Mikush.
*
WITH THANKS: This next story is written by Jennifer Kupiec, Social
Skills Coordinator, from CIP’s Amherst Center and details how
Jennifer worked with Lindsay on specific life skills that needed to be
acquired and achieved so that Lindsay could develop friendships with
peers and others who shared her interests. It’s an excellent example of
how awareness, then practice of essential life and social skills, can
lead to success. Thank you, Jennifer.
Lindsay and her great smile!
LINDSAY: A SUCCESS STORY!
by Jennifer Kupiec Social Skills Coordinator,
CIP Amherst

---
## Page 935
What I’ve Learned Working With This
Population …
My name is Jennifer Kupiec and I am the Social Skills Coordinator at
CIP Amherst. I have my NYS Certification in School Counseling and
have experience working with students in both a school and community
setting. Social skills is a huge part of CIP’s program as many students
on the Spectrum have challenges in this area. I work with students
individually on their specific goals and skills as well as in a group
setting where they can practice those skills in a safe environment.
One of the first things I do in individual social skills sessions is have the
students take a Social Skills Checklist assessment to help them
determine “how self-aware they are,” and “how they evaluate
themselves” in terms of their strengths and what they still need
improvement on.
One of the items asks if they have at least two or three close friends.
On average, nine out of ten of my students check the box that says
“needs improvement.” When I ask students about this in more detail,
most couldn’t name more than one close friend, if that. It was sad to
hear how so many of these young men and women struggle in terms of
social deficits and how essential it is for these emerging adults to learn
social skills.
One student in particular, Lindsay, came into CIP’s program extremely
talkative and not afraid to share her thoughts or opinions. However, she
had difficulty with interrupting, being able to stay on topic, or recognize
social cues from others (such as peers not being interested about that
topic or what she was talking on and on about). She also had difficulty
making small talk, listening to others, and being socially aware.
Lindsay had no problem interacting with others but that didn’t mean it
was always a two-way conversation. Working on these specific skills in
a one-on-one setting was the first step. We were able to go through
each problem area, step-by-step, determine what skills she needed to

---
## Page 936
learn, and then discuss why they are important. We would also talk
about expected vs. unexpected behaviors.
During our group session with other students, Lindsay was able to
practice all of these skills and I was able to observe her. We brought
this feedback into our individual sessions. Lindsay was very eager to
improve and was able to reflect positively from the feedback.
Although most of my students do not like groups, this was a case where
it was particularly beneficial for the challenges Lindsay faced. Not only
did she have a chance to practice and be observed but other students
learned how to help her by correcting her inappropriate behaviors in
verbal and/or nonverbal ways.
For example, if Lindsay would start to interrupt another student, the
student would give a cue (usually just a glance in her direction), and
she was able to recognize that she was interrupting, apologize, and
wait her turn. Eventually it became automatic and the amount of
interrupting decreased significantly.
Lindsay was able to apply the skills that she learned when she went
home on breaks with her family. Her family noticed right away the
difference and they were happy to let me know this! In turn, Lindsay
gained more confidence from these experiences.
Skill practice and exposure is the most helpful way to get feedback and
improve on a student’s social skills. In Lindsay’s case, she was able to
have more positive interactions which led to better roommate
communications, finding a friend in class who shared a common
interest with her and with whom she could make plans with. After these
first steps, Lindsay gained the confidence to enter into any type of
social situation that presented itself to her with ease.
*
WITH THANKS: This is Oriane Robison’s second story for this book.
She wrote “Don’t Assume Anything” for Chapter 6 and detailed the 27
steps and instructions she needed to create for one of her students so
that he could “make a peanut butter and jelly sandwich.” She also
contributed various teaching tips and vignettes throughout the book.

---
## Page 937
Her further insight, as she talks about her work and own personal
journey in this story, are human, heartfelt, and a great contribution to
this chapter. Thank you, Oriane!
Oriane with her student Jacky
MY STORY AND MY TIPS
by Oriane Robison Life Skills Coordinator, CIP
Bloomington
My Story …
When I first signed on to work at the College Internship Program, I was
looking for summer work in between college semesters. An
acquaintance told me that she thought a job at CIP (College Internship

---
## Page 938
Program), working with young adults on the ASD and LD Spectrum,
would be perfect for me.
I didn’t know very much about Autism and wasn’t sure what to expect,
but I had feelings of doubt with regard to my ability to help students on
the Spectrum in any meaningful way. After all, I was no expert.
However, I decided to give this opportunity a try when I was offered the
job.
My supervisor at CIP wanted me to work with students with whom I had
a common interest, so I was paired with one young woman who
enjoyed photography, a young man who wanted to study philosophy,
and another young man interested in singing.
I made it a point to take my students “out” as much as possible to do
the things that they loved. I have vivid and joyful memories of setting up
photo shoots with Rachel and getting a Starbucks coffee afterwards. Of
going to a playground with Johnny to muse over theories of ethics and
existentialism, and with Mark (who was quite shy), of finding a secluded
cave where his voice of pure gold could echo notes along side of me in
the darkness where it felt and was most safe and secure.
My students always seemed happy to see me and to allow me to come
into their space – even through the more dull parts of my work –
because they knew I was not there to judge them and that I considered
myself an equal, if not a friend.
My first and foremost priority was to know them for who they were and
listen to what they had to say, even if it meant some time “off the clock.”
How privileged I was as they let me into their hearts. This relationship
building allowed them to better hear my input when challenges were
placed in front of them.
Now, years later, it is gratifying to note that the three students I worked
with that summer are in apartments or houses of their own, living their
lives, working at jobs, and also working through ups and downs. When
I see how far these young men and women have come, it makes my
heart swell with happiness.

---
## Page 939
For so many of the students I work with in my full-time role as
Residential Coordinator at CIP, the reality is: They don’t have a lot of
friends. And in some cases, their only friends might be those who they
communicate with during online gaming. Being able to have these
young men and women get into a car with me that summer, wander
around in the woods chatting, then get a burger and some fries after,
was … for some of them, the closest thing to a friend they had ever had.
Looking back at my summer job with CIP, I think the experiences I had
with my students meant the world to them. I know it did for me and
deeply shaped how I think of loneliness in connection with an ASD or
LD diagnosis and the negative effects these two things can have on an
individual.
In my present work as CIP’s Residential Coordinator, I don’t get to do a
lot of the fun stuff anymore … but my staff does! I encourage anyone
working with students in this capacity to savor the down time, the
outings, the parties, and the active learning, in short … the fun each and
every day!
I tell them from my experiences that these are the moments that mean
the most to students I’ve worked with; the times that both they and I
remember best: connecting to someone on that level is a more precious
tool than any lesson any book could have delivered to me.
Although I have less time on my hands to run around parks doing photo
shoots or giving voice lessons, I always make sure to secure time in
each busy day to try to learn what a student has to teach me: about
themselves, or their worldview, what they hold dear, what they
absolutely hate, or what they are afraid of. Learning about them, feeling
blessed enough to have them share their lives with me, will always be
my favorite and most enriching aspect of my job.
My Tips …
Treat each person you work with, with respect and show them
openness. Get to know each one and don’t be afraid to share a little of

---
## Page 940
yourself along the way.
No one likes to be talked down to or shamed for something they have
done. Caring for each person you work with and showing it with your
actions can be the first step in the right direction.
Use your own perspective taking skills to see life from “your student’s
side.” Use your best judgment to understand why something is
happening and how he or she feels about it.
And remember … what works for you may not work for the young men
and women you work with, so be tolerant when things don’t go as “you”
planned.
Let each student take the lead in his or her life. Give each young man
or woman a sense of control. Ask questions, rather than give directions,
and let each one find answers that make sense. Giving each person
control of how he or she would like to face a challenge is empowering;
dictating how one should approach a problem can be discouraging,
especially if there are guilty feelings involved.
Have faith in the people you are working with because most of it is in
their hands anyway.
Be clear and prompt when something goes wrong. Act without
judgment and always offer a solution to the challenge, situation, or
problem. Your students will appreciate clear, focused, and constructive
feedback.
Give these emerging adults a break! Everyone needs time to recharge;
I know I do. Some days will be more productive than others. A student
who is overly stressed and worn out will only push you away more. So
… if you can … take time to watch a show, play a game together, or
maybe just allow time to lie on the couch a bit. We’re all human! So let
each person know that this type of regrouping or recharging is ok.
Try to relate with compassion when a student struggles. This doesn’t
work for everyone, but it shows that you’re on his or her side and that
you’re not coming from a place of judgment. For example: If a student
says I hate doing dishes. I say: you hate doing dishes? Well let me tell

---
## Page 941
you how much I hate doing laundry! Misery loves company, and
sometimes opening up about your own challenges will get you a lot
farther than a reprimand or an order would.
Better yet, make them laugh! Recently I said to a student “This fridge is
pretty bad, Zach. But not as bad as mine was once. I let a mushroom
stroganoff go so long in the fridge I thought about calling in scientists to
study a new life form!” It’s ok to exaggerate for a laugh and being silly
is underrated in my opinion.
Be creative in your thinking on how to approach problems, and if
something isn’t working, then it’s worth moving on to another option or
suggestion. In my years of working with students on the Spectrum, I’ve
come to realize there is no fix-all solution. Strategies often need to be
developed with the individual in mind and then adjusted, and adjusted
again. I liken it to science or engineering, where coming up with
solutions often has to take a trial and error approach.
Break tasks down into their most simple parts; don’t assume that a
student knows how to do something because in most cases, they really
and truly do not. And once you’ve taught them (whatever it may be),
repeat and repeat the activity or advice until you are absolutely sure
they’ve got it!
Don’t make things too complicated. The simpler and less complicated a
plan … the better. You can increase complexity as the student gets the
“fundamentals” down pat. This goes for communication as well. There
have been times I’ve been explaining things to students and I could see
their eyes starting to glaze over as I started to sound more and more
like the teacher from a “Charlie Brown” cartoon. Better to keep things
direct, brief, and one thing at a time.
Remember that progress with an ASD or LD adolescent or young adult
takes time (actually more time than you could ever imagine), and you
cannot expect results overnight. Track progress but don’t be
disheartened if things are going slowly. There is a story of a Buddhist
spiritualist, who was frustrated by the rate of his progress. He found
comfort in the fact that a drop of water, over the course of many years,

---
## Page 942
was able to wear a hole into the hard rock it was dripping onto.
Sometimes my work seems a lot like that; progress is best seen over
the course of years, not days.
*
WITH THANKS: Lucy Allarie-Gosselin, Program Director at CIP’s
Berkshire Center, is a clear minded and direct leader who is “no
nonsense” in her approach. She has been able to clearly
communicate to the families of students what their changing role is as
their young adults move forward and through CIP’s program.
Lucy understands the importance of community inclusion and has
been instrumental in the development of classes and curriculum, as
well as student programs, internships, and internship to work
programs at CIP’s Good Purpose Art Gallery, the Spectrum Playhouse
and Theatre Arts, the Starving Artist Café (all located on our Berkshire
Campus), as well as developing an internship program at CIP’s
affiliated organic farm: Bradley Farm in Lanesborough, Massachusetts.
As her students develop real skills for life and work, she has played
the role of guide, mentor, advisor, and sometimes substitute “mom” for
the hundreds of students who have passed through the Berkshire
Center during the past nine years. Thank you, Lucy for your work, your
work ethic, and for your story.

---
## Page 943
PREPARING STUDENTS FOR THE WORLD
OF WORK
by Lucy Allarie-Gosselin, Program Director, CIP
Berkshire

---
## Page 944
Our philosophy at the College Internship Program (CIP) is that
everyone you encounter is working and living with differences. Having a
difference, whether it is a learning difference or something else, does
not make you unique. What makes you unique is how you work with
that difference in a positive adult way so that it becomes a part of you,
rather than a controlling factor in how you live your life.  Learning how to
take on that challenge and turning it into an asset to gain personal
success is the key to your present and your future.  
When CIP accepts a student for enrollment into our program, it is
because we see potential in each individual and can visualize each
emerging adult taking the next steps in their lives.  We see, in these
young men and women, the desire to grow and learn as they leave their
secure family supported “nest.”  This leads to our biggest challenge:
The crippling fear and anxiety that students feel in relation to their
parents’ expectations.  
Parents, due to years of being the primary care givers, problem solvers,
decision-makers, and advocates for their adolescent or young adult
child on the ASD or LD Spectrum, often struggle (even more so than
the student), with letting go as their young adult child acquires life skills
and explores the world of work. While common goals from families of
our students include independent living, going to college, and/or gainful
employment, these very things are sometimes (and at the same time)
commonly hindered by parental involvement and expectations.   
Many times, parents are unaware of the fact that the deep involvement
they have had in their child’s life has resulted in crippling dependence –
the very thing they state they want their child to avoid in CIP’s various
programs of study.   Students sometimes parrot this parental advice
back to us:  “I spoke to my mother/father last night and they said I
should …” without learning what it is that they themselves would like to
achieve. If students are not given the personal space to make their own
mistakes, they will never achieve their goals. They need to be trusted to
grow, learn, and develop. 
A number of years ago, CIP established a program to teach students
about the world of work by understanding how necessary it is for each

---
## Page 945
student to participate in a successful internship in a workplace setting
before actually obtaining a full- or part-time job. Included in the
preparation for an internship was training in the skills needed (by the
student) and training in the expectations desired (by the employer or
supervisor) in order to perform “on the job” competently.
When I started at CIP (initially as the Career Coordinator) and started
spending time with the students I immediately knew what I had to do to
help students move forward in their lives. Creating a curriculum to
support success during an internship, impart job skills and ethics, and
then help students turn an internship into paid employment has been
one of my primary contributions to CIP’s program.
Parents often take the stance that “my child can do this and he or she
should pursue a career in that” because their son “knows everything
about basketball.” This particular parent said to me: “I can see him
someday becoming a coach or a broadcaster for basketball games.”
At CIP, my staff and I work on reality and what a student can actually
be successful at for future employment and then help the student seek
internships or job opportunities based on their interests and what their
actual transferable skills are. This may, in some instances turn out that
their emerging adult child becomes a broadcaster for national
basketball games, but most often this is not the case.
As we assess and look at each student from this realistic standpoint we
encourage families to “Keep It Real,” a phrase that not only appears
throughout this book, but is repeated often during each and every day
on our campus, as we deal with students and their parents. My job and
the job of those who work under my direction is to help our students
separate what is considered a “hobby” or “special interest” into what
might be a “real” possibility for an internship or a job and then help
each individual look at workforce expectations from employers in this
regard.
As we “prepare” students for the world of work each individual
“advances” to a level of understanding so they can “succeed” and be
happy, as well as successful in an internship and then in a job. A multi-

---
## Page 946
fold challenge and balance is achieved … fun, friends, then hobby and
knowledge, and lastly, skills and a job.
Over my nine plus year tenure here at CIP, I have witnessed many
student success stories. The students who achieve empowerment
through daily independent living skills and success in employment and
academic situations are those who are given the most space to fly.
They go forth into the world to live productive, active, and independent
lives, while being accepting of their differences and needs.  In this way,
they transition into the world with support from families, friends, and
CIP staff, while embracing independence and all of its potential for the
future.     
I am very proud of CIP’s Berkshire Center. Our students are
spectacular and they thrive on being able to explore various career
possibilities. The Good Purpose Art Gallery, the Spectrum Playhouse
and Theater (in an old converted church), the Starving Artist Café, and
CIP’s organic farm (in Lanesborough, Massachusetts), all lend
opportunities to our students as they explore various career options.
It is wonderful to watch our students take advantage of internships and
internship to work opportunities that reside within CIP and in our
surrounding communities. As students pursue art, theatre, a culinary
curriculum, or opportunities for organic farming – talents, special
interests, and hobbies are pursued in a realistic way. Many of our
students have sold their artwork in the gallery, interned or worked in the
café, or participated in all aspects of four staged productions at the
theatre.
As students leave CIP and prepare to finish college or explore career
paths on their own, they begin living independently and are able to use
what they learned during their time with us to create lives that make
them happy and embrace inclusion. They become part of their wider
communities and the world that surrounds each one of them.
*
WITH THANKS: Ellen Orell (now a third year student at CIP) created
the art piece entitled “A Girl with a Past” as a representation of how

---
## Page 947
she felt when she was bullied during most of her high school years by
nine other girls. The poem “From Chaotic to Peaceful” that
accompanies her artwork puts the emotion she painted into words. As
readers will recall – 65% of parents who have children “on the
Spectrum” report that their child was bullied during their elementary,
middle school, or high school years. This shocking statistics are
brought to light in Ellen’s faceless painting and her poignant poem.
Thank you, Ellen, for sharing your art, your poem, and the speech you
wrote and delivered as a CIP student ambassador during the 2013
“parents’ weekend at CIP.” These three contributions show the
readers of this book how you have come to know yourself and accept
your challenges as you embrace your strengths and then find the time
to give back to others who are just starting our program. The valuable
life skills and maturity you exhibit daily at CIP provide insight not only
into your young life but insight into the exciting future that lies in store
for you. I feel that seeing all three of your contributions together can
give those who read this book great hope as they work with young
adults like yourself on the “ASD or LD Spectrum.”

---
## Page 948
“A Girl with A Past” painted by Ellen Orell
FROM CHAOTIC TO PEACEFUL
by Ellen Orell, CIP Berkshire Student
The forest stretches far and the trees stretch tall.
The ravine trickles and the wind whispers.
To the unknowing, the trails are hard going.
But to her, habit leads the way forward.
As she walks on, she thinks and she remembers.
Nine. The number that used to overwhelm.
Excluded. For she was one and they were nine.
“That was the past and this is the present.”
Is what she says now – a conscious reminder.
The winds are now coaxing her confidence.
Leading her lovingly to her location.
The trees, weeding out all the bad feelings
as if they were stitching up old wounds in need.
Once there, the ravine commands her to sit
and listen to the sounds that nature surrounds.
Then the wide woodlands ask her: Are you glad?
PARENTS’ WEEKEND: SEPTEMBER 20, 2013
A Speech Written and Delivered by Ellen Orell

---
## Page 949
Hello families, friends, staff, and students. For those of you who don’t
know me, my name is Ellen Orell and I have chosen to accept the
opportunity to give this year’s student speech. I am a third year student
at CIP. I am a Student Ambassador and have more than likely seen
many of you at various times and various places on campus. I am also
a Student Residential Advisor. This means I choose to live with first
year students in order to assist them with having a better transition into
life at CIP. I can remember my first year; entering a new place, knowing
pretty much no one, and being both eager and nervous to start the next
stage of my life. I hope that my giving this speech gives all of you some
perspective into what the College Internship Program can help you to
achieve and look forward to while you attend.
I entered CIP straight out of high school at the age of 18. For me high
school was a completely different world. I attended a high school out of
district and it totaled no more than 30 students. All my friends lived a
minimum of half an hour away. I relied on my parents for any sort of
transportation. It was rare when there wasn’t tension between my family
and me. My dad and I were usually at odds with our opinions,
especially in regards to chores. My sister and I never really had defined
roles of “older sister” and “younger sister” despite the fact that I was
born two years prior. This may have given us more cause for having the
classic case of sibling rivalry to the point of sometimes not being able to
even have a conversation with each other. My mom and I were closer
but when we’d argue, it was never quiet. I never fully understood some
of the rules my parents would enforce, or ways of trying to teach me
certain values, or how to do things. I’m sure many of you can relate to
this situation.
My family discovered CIP in the fall of 2010 – right at the start of my
senior year in high school – and I can remember really wanting to
attend. I toured at an open house that October, interviewed over my
February vacation, and was accepted less than a week later. I started
my orientation in August 2011.
During my first year, I was a level one, meaning I had the most supports
which also meant I had the most classes and appointments to attend.

---
## Page 950
Many people struggle with a transition to a place where they are
incredibly busy, especially if, like me, they were not used to busy
schedules. However, I embraced all the challenges by attending
classes and appointments; getting involved in extra-curriculars; and
taking this place (and myself) seriously; and because of this I have
learned much.
Over the past three years I have grown immensely. My friends here
describe me as an over-achiever. However, I prefer the terms
motivated and determined. I started out taking only one class at
Berkshire Community College but now can balance an average of three
each semester. I have even earned my Introductory Certificate in Early
Childhood Education (which is my major), and hopefully by May 2014
will have my Intermediate Certificate in the same field.
I used to have two individual “Executive Functioning” appointments
every week with a staff from the residential department. I knew how to
clean, it was just a matter of keeping up on it. But now I no longer need
that support. Now I find cluttered areas and having a messy room
annoying.
I also used to have advising sessions twice a week but now I hold my
own check book and keep track of my expenses independently.
Through the Career Department I have gained valuable knowledge
about interviewing skills that alone … I never would have known. I
currently intern at St. Paul’s Children’s Center and I have been working
there for a little over one year. I am able to get there and back using
public transportation independently.
Socially I used to have difficulty problem solving on the spot and would
often realize what would have been a better choice, at a later time. Now
my ability to recognize and process difficult situations has gotten better
and I am capable of figuring out a solution in a much smoother and
quicker manner. I also used to be extremely cautious when talking to
new people, especially those in my peer group, but now I find myself
initiating conversations with all sorts of people (at the college, around
the town of Lee, and on the bus, are a few examples).

---
## Page 951
My journey during the past three years has also helped me grow in my
family life. I actually see more of my parents’ reasons for them doing the
things they did. Being a Residential Advisor (RA) has definitely helped
me realize that and probably accept it in a much quicker manner. I
often find myself enforcing rules they taught me on myself. Values as
simple as “if you don’t go to school today, you can’t go outside later,”
which they taught me, are still ingrained in my head and I still follow
them.
When I started attending classes and sessions here I began wanting to
fix my relationship with my sister. I tried very hard to communicate with
her in order to start mending past issues. At first she was not receptive,
but with some guidance from CIP’s clinical department on my problem,
gradually things improved. Actually just this past Sunday I called her up
and we had a peaceful conversation that only ended because one of
our cell phones was getting a bad connection.
The past three years have been more “than just a school to attend.”
CIP to me is an idea … and a good one at that. It’s helped me in such a
way that I have become a better person and I feel more well rounded in
more areas of my life than just at school. I am someone who believes
that things happen for a reason. If a Genie suddenly appeared in front
of me offering a free chance to erase all of the hardships of my past,
he’d find himself on the receiving end of a very cold shoulder because if
I accepted his offer, I don’t think I’d be the person I am today. I like who
I am. I am glad that CIP; its staff, its students and its concept are in my
life and I will always feel blessed to have been able to come here. And
no … they didn’t pay me to say that! Anyway, I hope you enjoy the rest
of parents’ weekend and that you look forward to the rest of this school
year. Thank you.
*
WITH THANKS: Ryan Therriault, the Academic Coordinator at our
Brevard, Florida Center, created the title and topic of this story using a
phrase I coined many years ago when a student marched into my
office and said “Sh** Happens!” I asked him if he might consider the
alternative: “Shift Happens.” The phrase caught on and appeared on

---
## Page 952
T-shirts, mugs, and bumper stickers at all CIP campuses. Ryan’s “Shift
Can Happen” story is a good example of how acquiring “Executive
Functions” and other essential life skills can – with time, patience, and
practice – cause “Shift” to “Happen.”
Ryan has graciously contributed teaching vignettes, stories, and
teaching tips to other chapters in this book (in particular, Chapter 4),
where she details a day in the life of an Academic Coordinator, and in
Chapter 6 with her piece entitled “Students say the Darn-dest Things.”
Thanks for contributing throughout the book, Ryan!
Ryan with her students!
“SHIFT CAN HAPPEN”

---
## Page 953
by Ryan Therriault, Center Academic
Coordinator, CIP Brevard
When students begin an academic track at the Brevard Center they
take on a lot of responsibility. Assuming they are ready … they work
hard to juggle college classes, CIP classes and appointments, new
surroundings, and new routines. They essentially begin a new life,
which can be overwhelming. However, with each individual’s hard work
and determination in combination with dedicated staff, “Shift can
Happen.”
One student in particular, Holden, displayed this significant “Shift.” He
has mastered – in the few short years he has been in our program –
skills for life, school, work, and independent living. He has made “Shift
Happen.”
Holden is a very dedicated student. He works ahead, he completes all
his work, and he always asks for help when he needs it. As a result of
his academic dedication, Holden has acquired his general Associate of
Arts degree from Brevard Community College (BCC) while at CIP, and
he plans to pursue a Bachelor’s degree in business from the University
of Central Florida.
Holden has accomplished so much in the time that he has attended
CIP’s Brevard Center. He is more confident, self-assured, and is better
able to manage stress. Managing stress has allowed Holden to lower
his test anxiety and helped him maintain better verbal self-control. He
was able to accomplish these things by remaining open to feedback
from staff and students.
He also learned “Executive Function” skills for managing time and
space, and as a result, became punctual to appointments and
remained consistently engaged during CIP and BCC classes.
Socially, Holden became very involved in alternate activities on the
weekends, but was able (by mastering all life skill areas) to maintain a
balance between school and leisure activities. Holden developed
valuable friendships during his time at CIP and he plans to foster and
maintain these when he leaves the program at the end of this semester.

---
## Page 954
Holden’s participation in counseling and social mentoring helped him
gain vital social skills and helped him in so many other ways. Holden is
about to finish his time at CIP. He has done much “Shifting” during his
time at CIP and he will be missed. I look forward to hearing updates on
Holden’s continued progress post CIP.
*
WITH THANKS: Margaret Markham has worked with CIP for over 22
years and in many ways is responsible for this book being
published, as she first approached me about seeking an official
diagnosis, then supported me as I learned to accept, then manage,
and live with my “ASD” diagnosis. She has been intricately connected
with her students in both academics and the performing arts. Her
students have grown and benefited greatly by performing for young
children in the communities that surround CIP’s Berkshire campus.
Margaret’s years of hard work at the Center have had a cumulative
effect on the development of the positive programming that exists at
CIP. Her story details just how life-changing stepping outside oneself
and giving to others can be for those of us who reside on the “ASD or
LD Spectrum.” It is difficult to say who benefits more, our students or
the community that receives the benefit of CIP’s Puppet Theatre.
Margaret’s work with her students is just about the best example of
“Service Learning” that I can think of to share with readers. Thank
you, Margaret, for all your dedication over the years and for your
contribution to this book.

---
## Page 955
THE ANIMAL CRACKERS PUPPET THEATER
(ASD and LD Students Making a Community-
Wide Difference)
by Margaret Markham, Special Educator, CIP
Berkshire
I have been teaching and tutoring at CIP for over 22 years. My MS
degree is in Special Education, and I started out as a part-time
tutor/teacher. Within a year or so I became the Academic Coordinator
for CIP Berkshire and remained in that position for 16 years. When my
first granddaughter was born I left that full-time position, so that I could
be involved with helping to care for her, but I continued to work for CIP
as a part-time teacher/tutor doing what I love best … working directly
with the students.

---
## Page 956
My background includes an AS degree in Early Childhood Education,
an area that I’m very enthusiastic about, so I tutor most of our students
who are in the Early Childhood classes at Berkshire Community
College. I tutor other subjects such as Psychology or English as well,
but I truly love tutoring in the Early Childhood field and helping students
put together magnet board stories and finger plays to use with the
young children at the centers where they are interning, or coming up
with interesting science or art projects to try out.
I guess you might say that I am passionate about Early Childhood. I get
as much enjoyment out of working with and being around young
children as I get from my work with young adults on the ASD and LD
Spectrum. This is one of the reasons I decided to form a puppet troupe
with CIP students called: The Animal Crackers Puppet Theater.
The idea for this came about many years ago when our founder,
Michael McManmon, decided that the students in the CIP program
could benefit by getting involved in doing some form of community
service. He taught staff and students that “community service
introduces you to yourself.” I soon discovered how right he was as I set
out to learn which of our CIP students would be interested in forming a
puppet troupe that would put on puppet shows (initially) at the local
library to entertain young children in the community.
The library was in possession of a lovely puppet theater which could be
set up for us to use for rehearsals and to put on shows in the library’s
community room. The first group of students I worked with decided to
put on a show based on the book, “The True Story of the Three Little
Pigs” by Jon Scieszka, which for those of you who are unfamiliar with
the tale, tells the story of “The Three Little pigs” fairy tale from the
maligned wolf’s point of view. A script was written up based on Jon
Scieszka’s children’s book, and we used backdrops and props that
had already been made by a group of mothers several years prior to
our debut.
In preparation, the students met weekly in a classroom at CIP. They
read through the scripts, trying out for different parts, practiced using
different voices, and got comfortable operating their puppets in front of

---
## Page 957
each other. To my surprise and delight, I discovered that many of the
students presented themselves in a completely different light than when
they were sitting beside me at a desk during a tutoring session or in a
more formal classroom setting. I shared my passion for finding ways to
get young children enthusiastic about books and that enthusiasm was
catching. Soon my students were getting excited about finding ways to
make their puppet characters and dialogues more appealing to younger
children. As they were looking for ways to help others, they were
learning about themselves and their capabilities. They wanted, very
much, to create a humorous show that would be exciting for young
children to watch and this is exactly what they did.
One of the puppeteers, a young man named Rich, often presented
himself as being quite awkward socially with the other students. He
would often be too loud and would frequently get in another student’s
space which caused some students to avoid being around him. Of
course ground rules about being courteous and respecting others were
established right from the beginning during our work on the script, in
rehearsals, and of course, social thinking was a very important aspect
of our weekly meetings.
Rich, who could be very amusing at times, poked his little pig puppet
through the theater’s peep hole and jokingly asked, “Got Milk?” after
the wolf ate up one of the little pigs. Rich was getting attention by being
the “class clown” in a way, but in a small informal setting of fellow
puppeteers it wasn’t as disruptive as it might have been in a larger
classroom setting. In fact, as we all laughed at and with Rich, everyone
loosened up a bit and let their own creative juices flow. The other
students thought that he was hilarious, and the “Got Milk?” line became
a part of the script. Moments like this helped the students appreciate
each other in new ways.
Dave was another student in the cast of “The True Story of the Three
Little Pigs” who had the part of one of the little pigs. Dave was an
extremely quiet, shy student on the Autism Spectrum. He often did not
speak distinctly enough for others to understand him. At about the
fourth rehearsal I began to encourage all of the students to really speak

---
## Page 958
out when they were doing their parts, because when you are sitting on
small chairs behind a puppet theater, your voice projects into the wall of
the theater in front of you and gets swallowed up very easily. It turns out
that Dave actually listened to this advice. He began reading his lines,
“Get out of here wolf. Don’t bother me again or I’ll call the cops and
they’ll throw you in the pen.” His voice was so loud and so emphatic
that the other students’ jaws dropped to the floor along with mine.
Everyone gave Dave the kudos he deserved, and after that we all had a
new appreciation for Dave’s abilities, as did Dave himself. As I worked
with my students in these early days, I kept thinking that Michael
McManmon was right. Community service does indeed introduce you
to yourself.
Several years later, I worked with a student named Eddie. Eddie was a
very bright young man on the Autism Spectrum who was very much
into theater and acting of all sorts. Although he was talented, he had
difficulty being a team player and could be quite argumentative with the
other students at times, especially when someone came up with an
idea to use in the show that he did not agree with. Eddie had had
various roles in several puppet shows over the years, and even though
he was quite talented, he had never played a main character chiefly

---
## Page 959
because he could not be counted on to show up on time for rehearsals
or shows. When it was decided that CIP’s puppet show for that year
was to be a show we created called “The Reluctant Dragon”, a tale of
St. George, Eddie decided that he was going to be St. George. He took
a script home and practiced for the try-outs, and when the “try-out” day
came his portrayal of St. George was amazing. Needless to say he got
the part with the proviso that he would always be on time and always
show up for rehearsals.
During one of our practices I noticed Eddie in a corner by himself
staring into his puppet’s eyes. He appeared to be “communing” with St.
George. Whatever it was that he was doing, when his scene came up
and he took St. George into the puppet theater, extraordinary emotion
came into the speech and movements of that puppet.
After the show’s first performance at CIP’s Spectrum Playhouse (we
were putting it on for a group of summer camp youngsters from a
nearby school), a young camper approached me. He said: “Excuse me;
I would like to meet St. George.”
I knew that the puppeteers had already mingled with the campers after
the performance allowing them to try out the puppets if they desired to,
but apparently this boy had not gotten a chance to operate the St.
George puppet. Eddie came out speaking like St. George and
manipulating the puppet so that he bowed and brandished his sword. It
was extremely heartwarming to see Eddie and the young boy having a
dialogue together, and the look of admiration on the boy’s face as he
spoke with “St. George” was priceless. When my students see how
much the children in their audiences admire the puppeteers, their self-
esteem grows before my very eye as they bask in this gratification. It is
a win-win situation.

---
## Page 960
The CIP Puppet Troupe (in later years) obtained their own puppet
theater which was constructed by a friendly volunteer. It could be
disassembled and folded up so that it could fit in our van or in a pick-up
truck. This allowed us to offer performances throughout the Berkshires.
Each year the puppeteers practice a show, and when they are ready,
they literally take the show on the road going to area preschools, day
cares, after school programs, and elementary schools to put the current
show on for small groups of children. We are always in demand, always
booked way in advance, and always asked back.
Every college semester CIP generally has a few students who are
taking the Early Childhood curriculum at our local community college.
These students participate in internships at various sites in the area.
Often times an internship is done both through CIP and also as a
practicum for college credit through the community college. The troupe
usually goes to those schools and centers first as a way of giving
something back to the young students of teachers who have been kind
enough to mentor one of CIP’s students as an intern.

---
## Page 961
We recently tried something new. The puppeteers performed one of
their shows, “Lobsters in Love,” at an area nursing home. This show
was based on the book of the same name written by Richard and
Lindsey Kidd. One of our students was doing an internship at the
nursing home and mentioned the puppet theatre to the activities
director. She invited us to perform partly because she knew that the
student, Vincent was very well liked at the nursing home and many of
the elderly people who resided there were anxious to see him, as well
as the rest of the puppet troupe perform. We put the show on in the
dining area while the residents were having lunch. We had added quite
a few musical selections to the story, and the diners applauded after
each musical number was over. They seemed to be really enjoying
sharing the experience with the CIP students. And, the students got a
chance to perform for a completely different demographic.
As each new puppet show is being created, the students have many
opportunities to share their special talents with the group. I remember in
particular a student named Donald. Donald was a young man who
loved to make up rap songs. He was in one of the groups that put on
“The True Story of the Three Little Pigs,” and he asked if he could
make up a rap song to teach the puppeteers and use it to introduce the

---
## Page 962
show to the young viewers. The song was a big hit both with the young
children and with the other members of the puppet troupe.
We use various sound effects during our shows such as water
splashing or rocks falling, for example. One year a young woman who
played the cello was in the troupe. She suggested using her instrument
to create the moods and sounds that were needed for that year’s show,
and it worked out very nicely.
That same year Paul (a young man who attended the University of
Connecticut after leaving CIP) was able to film the show and assist the
producer at Community Television for Southern Berkshire (CTSB) in
editing the show which was then put on television. They producer said
that it was their first programming for children. This is yet another
example of how something as simple as putting on a puppet show in a
library can grow exponentially. The puppeteers actually went to the
studio to do the filming. It was a highlight of our “show season” that
year.
This year the show is an adaptation of “Peter Pan.” The young woman
who is playing the part of Peter is a phenomenal and gifted singer. She
is gaining the confidence she needs by rehearsing her solos and
perfecting them with feedback from the rest of the cast. The young men
who are playing the pirate parts are going to be singing together also as
a quartet and they are also excited about their “live” performances. The
rest of the songs will be sung by puppeteers with the aid of recorded
music.
The students who are involved in CIP’s visual arts program often help
out with the creation of backdrops for the shows. It has also been
recently suggested that we look into having a new theater made out of
PVC pipe, that would be easy to assemble and take down. The
concepts, ideas, teamwork, and ideas for presentations are limitless.
The future is exciting. It is great to be a part of CIP’s recently formed
“official” Creative Arts Program and to watch the talents of our students
continue to unfold.
*

---
## Page 963
WITH THANKS: When I first read student Parker Smith’s story that he
created for an assignment in his Career Skills I class at CIP
Bloomington, I immediately asked his Career Coordinator, Taylor True
if Parker would allow me to put his essay in this chapter of this book.
Parker’s words portray not only what he values, but his vision for the
future he is about to embrace as he claims his place as a citizen in
the world. I am pleased that he was happy to allow us to print his
story.
The students in Taylor True’s class were asked to write about the
values they wanted their lives to serve. The depth of maturity,
emotion, and hope that Parker wrote about is inspiring, profound, and
encompasses a wide range of emotions as he begins to think about
and then reflect on what he hopes will be become reality in the future:
Making a difference in the world starting with himself. I am so pleased
to share his story and hope that readers will get to know this young
man through his words.
When I asked Parker for a short biography he told me that he is 20
years old and moved to Bloomington (he attends CIP’s center there),
from Houston, Texas. He said he’s only been at CIP six months and
that he is learning a lot about himself, setting important goals that he’d
like to achieve, and gaining even further insight into his diagnosis. He
plays basketball in the Ivy Tech Community College basketball
league, sings, is active in his new Bloomington church group, and is
studying art. He told me that eventually he wants to become a chef.
Thank you, Parker, for sharing your story and I hope that all your
dreams, hopes, wishes for yourself, and for our world come true.

---
## Page 964
THESE ARE THE VALUES I WANT MY LIFE
TO SERVE
by Parker Smith, First Year Student, CIP
Bloomington
I want there to be enough food to feed the world’s hungry children. I am
disgusted by how many poor children die from famine and malnutrition.
I cannot comprehend how selfish we are. We cannot look beyond
ourselves and our own needs.
I want there to be more fitness, health, and wholeness in the world. We
need to provide education, food, water, shelter, and medical care to
prevent or at least reduce the spread of disease outside the United
States.
I want there to be more love and compassion for others. We do not live
in a perfect world. We need to do a much better job of accepting people
and not judging them. Why do we address people by their race,
ethnicity, color, gender, sexuality, or well-being? Everybody is different
and no two people think the same way.

---
## Page 965
I feel we are called by God to love and serve. Regardless of our
religion, we can all agree that judging and discriminating against people
is a huge problem that has been part of our society for at least 400
years. Not to mention that slavery still exists in certain countries around
the world.
I want there to be more honesty, justice, and righteousness in the
world. Why do people scrutinize, terrorize, and violate our society as
we know it? We live in an epidemic of violence and injustice. There is
sex trafficking, drug dealing, shootings, rapes, and domestic violence,
just to name a few. Imagine living in a perfect society without crime.
Unfortunately this is unrealistic since there are seven billion people on
the face of the earth.
I know that we have great people in our society but also cold-blooded
monsters who continuously wreck and destroy our communities.
Sometimes, entire nations and this impacts the world as a whole.
Luckily most of us go the extra mile to make our society better. And of
course, today’s sophisticated technology has helped us feel a little bit
safer, but there is much improvement to be done.
Our world is full of diversity. We know that religion and politics are very
touchy subjects. There are so many different religions and within each
religious group are an abundance of different branches. We have a
hard time forgiving each other. We are so quick to resort to coercion
over religious and political beliefs. There is no particular age group that
this affects and to say that would be delusional because political and
religious discrimination affects everybody.
We need to have more love, more love for whatever God we believe in.
Even if you are atheist or a non-Christian you would agree that we are
all called to serve and help one another. Some people also have
falsified beliefs regarding what others believe. Not all of us have
common interests, but we all have human spirit, the most important
thing to have.
We do not care enough about one another and some of us refuse to
think of anything else but ourselves. We need to treat each other as

---
## Page 966
equals, or the human spirit that surrounds us will dissolve into a cloud
of hatred.
Finally, I think we are all so caught up in materialism that we lose our
true selves. We always want more and more. Whether it be money,
clothes, a nicer car, a bigger house, more income, or whatever drives
our need to make us feel pampered and privileged. We are very
blessed and fortunate but some of us – I think, are spoiled rotten. What
if we gave to charity more? In my opinion, materialism is a human
nightmare because our need to have more disables us. We can’t see
the major challenges and problems that we face in our community, our
nation, and our world.
My heart is telling me to lend a hand and reach out. Reach out and help
others and to be a Good Samaritan. I would also tell others not to feel
the need to be recognized for your efforts. Do it because it is important
to you. I see people idolizing certain assets they have rather than
seeing what others need or how they might help them.
Let us all, starting with me, make small changes in our lifestyles one
step at a time that will positively impact everybody … everywhere.
*
WITH THANKS: The next two pieces (one story and one poem) were
written by staff members who have immediate family members on the
“ASD or LD Spectrum.” The first piece, a story, was written by Sarah
Blackwelder Williams, CIP’s Communications Coordinator. Sarah is
also the parent of a son with “Asperger’s.”
Sarah details the difficulties young men and women “on the Spectrum”
face as they begin working. She details the experiences of several
young men and women as they endeavor to become financially
independent while pursuing a career path that will lead to independent
living.
In the second piece, a poem, Maggie Dillon Katz (who is the
Presentation Coordinator for CIP and presents at national and
international conferences on Autism and Learning Differences on CIP’s
behalf) reflects on her personal connection to “Learning Differences

---
## Page 967
and Autism,” as it runs in her family. She shares with readers her
personal journey as she endeavors to make intimate connections with
her two brothers who reside “on the Spectrum.”
Both submissions are poignant and allow readers to reflect on the fact
that many of us who are working in this field have encountered family,
relatives, friends, and co-workers who reside “on the Spectrum.” A
willingness to know more, understand more, and ultimately pass on
solutions, strategies, and ideas to others is what draws a lot of us to
working with this population. I would like to express my gratitude to
Sarah and Maggie for sharing their personal and special connection to
“ASD and LD” within their families, to readers.
AUTISM IN THE WORKPLACE … SPEED
BUMPS INCLUDED
by Sarah Blackwelder Williams,
Communications Coordinator for CIP
The road to employment for young adults with autism can be bumpy.
Educators, parents, and professionals need to recognize the unique
challenges faced by these young adults as they transition to adulthood
and enter the workplace. During their high school years and well into
their 
twenties, 
research 
shows 
these 
students 
benefit 
from
comprehensive social and life skills support, along with employment
readiness training. A college degree does not guarantee a job. In fact, if
a student has not reached an appropriate level of social competency,
the degree will have little value in the workplace.
When establishing employment goals for young adults with Autism
Spectrum Disorders (ASD) it’s important to understand their unique
struggles and strengths. It may take them much longer than their
neurotypical peers to find employment. Once employed, they may
benefit initially from an on-site job coach who can help resolve any
potential issues or answer questions that may arise.

---
## Page 968
Many young adults with ASD are underemployed due to their lack of
social cognitive skills. They may do well in a post-secondary program,
but still need ongoing support in several key areas. However, despite
the hurdles they may encounter, many young adults can experience
success on their own terms in the workplace and beyond. Here are
some excellent case studies that illustrate what I’m talking about.
Adam
Adam recently graduated from college with a major in sound
engineering. Although he has a degree and a solid resume, Adam has
not been able to find a full-time job in his desired field. Instead, he is
working part time at a music studio and living with his parents. While he
has not achieved his career and independent living goals, Adam is in a
relationship with his first girlfriend. He has dreams for the future, but he
is currently satisfied with his life.
Adam has Asperger’s and is among the roughly 90% of adults on the
Autism Spectrum who are not fully employed or living independently.
Given their social skills deficits and quirky behaviors, many have
difficulty landing a career job and are not successful beyond the first
round of competitive job interviews.
Chris
Chris also has Asperger’s. He studied geology and received an AA
degree at a community college. He now works for his uncle at a family-
owned deli. He attends a weekly meet-up designed for young adults
with ASD where he has made two friends he sees outside the group
setting. Chris is passionate about geology and takes continuing
education classes at a local college after work. He hopes the skills he
learns in his classes will help him gain the technical skills that could
someday land him a job at a science research center. The classes give
him a renewed sense of purpose.

---
## Page 969
Josh
Josh attended a three-year program at the College Internship Program
(CIP) for young adults with Learning Differences. He took many classes
offered by CIP and also enrolled in a local community college. Josh has
High Functioning Autism and struggles with motivation issues. Although
he did not graduate, Josh earned a certificate in Communication Arts.
Josh, now 24, has just started attending to his personal hygiene. His
family is thrilled to see that he showers regularly and no longer wears
dirty clothes. He has the social skills of someone several years
younger, but he is slowly starting to mature. Two years after leaving the
program, Josh is beginning to gain a perspective about the value of the
life skills support he received at CIP. Josh enjoys his part-time job at a
paintball recreation center and hopes to be a DJ someday.
Facing Challenges
By the time they reach adulthood, many young people on the spectrum
have faced countless challenges and hurdles. Poor time management
and anxiety may have caused them to fail a class, or not turn projects in
on time. Most have had difficulty making friends and don’t understand
why having a group of friends is so hard, when it comes so naturally to
others. These types of issues may carry through into the workplace
where time management and social awareness are important skills.
Additional support from a social mentor may be helpful in order to
establish and maintain good relationships with co-workers and
supervisors at work.
New Employment Programs
With the tidal wave of young adults aging out of their K-12 support
systems, a few organizations are starting to recognize the value of
hiring young adults with ASD. For instance, the technology company
SAP has recently established an employment initiative and is dedicated
to hiring and training young adults on the Spectrum.
In Australia, the Australian government has just launched the Dandelion
Project and is working to provide skilled employment for a significant

---
## Page 970
number of people with Autism.
After prioritizing the needs of children for years, “Autism Speaks,” the
nation’s largest Autism advocacy organization, is turning its attention to
expanding housing and employment options for adults. ASTEP, a non-
profit organization based in New York City, offers a lecture series where
young adults with Asperger’s can meet staff at Fortune 500 companies
in a professional setting. The program provides a unique opportunity for
employers to meet qualified young adults on the Spectrum and
consider them for competitive employment.
Many of the efforts by companies developing these new projects are
spearheaded internally by a senior level employee who is a parent or
relative of someone with Autism whom they have watched struggle to
find meaningful employment.
The number of young adults with ASD who are benefitting from
programs like these is still very small, but it’s a start. It will take years
for employers to learn how to properly support and accommodate this
population 
in 
the 
workforce 
and 
unfortunately, 
careers 
and
independence will not be an option for everyone. The Autism Spectrum
is broad, and the number of young adults who need various types of
support is growing at an unprecedented rate.
Setting Realistic Goals
Goals for this unique group of young people should be measured and
defined differently. Realistic goals may not include a career at Google
or an upscale apartment with a wide circle or group of friends. But
these young men and women may experience other forms of success
along the way that can be just as valuable; a girlfriend, an interesting
part-time job, or a renewed sense of purpose.
Despite challenges and a lack of career job opportunities, some young
adults on the Spectrum will experience success in the workplace, and
others will experience success in different areas, on their own terms.
With the right mix of comprehensive support and self-determination,
many of their goals can be achieved, one step at a time.
*

---
## Page 971
BEAUTIFUL BROTHERS, I LOVE YOU
by Maggie Dillon Katz Presentation Coordinator
for CIP
Beautiful brothers, I love you.
It’s been years since you’ve seen me,
looked my way, years since you’ve noticed, asked me a question.
“How are you?  How’d it go? Tell me more.
Years since you’ve looked back to see if I’m coming.
I miss you. I miss your teachings, your guidance, even your grumpy
moods. Your silent companionship. I need you and you need me. We
can hold hands to soften the blows to our hearts, our silent agonies, our
disappointments. We need each other – to remember what once was …
and is yet to begin again.
Oh, sweet brothers, don’t you remember?
Before puberty how we shared secrets, endured our parents’ silences
and squabbles.
Have you forgotten we are from the same flesh – our sinews the same?
Can we not laugh once again as we once did?
Touch hearts?
Touch eyes?
Touch souls?
Promise to stand beside one another – united – but separate.
One team, one family.
One!
*

---
## Page 972
WITH THANKS: Joan Williamson, Program Director at CIP Brevard,
has been the vanguard in developing CIP’s curriculum over the past
decade. She has put together, supported – then trained and
developed – a strong team of highly skilled staff members who have
become experts in assisting students as they develop skills for life,
school, work, and independent living.
I want to thank Joan for being the inspiration and the guiding light from
the day CIP Brevard opened its doors in Florida 11 years ago. Joan
retires in June of 2015 after compassionately serving hundreds of
students during her tenure at CIP. Thank you, Joan, for all you have
done for CIP and for contributing the heartfelt words portrayed in your
story. Readers will be able to draw strength, knowledge, and wisdom
from your words.
TAKING IN THE GOOD
by Joan Williamson, Program Director, CIP
Brevard
I began working as the Business Manager for the Berkshire Center in
December of 2000. At that time, the Berkshire Center was the only CIP
(College Internship Program) center and we had 23 students. Shortly
after I began, the students left for the holiday break and I remember
sitting amongst the piles of paperwork trying to sort it all out and make
sense of it. I knew right then and there, that this job would be different
from any other job I had ever had. Now 14 years later, I realize just how
true that was.
During that time, The Berkshire Center began accepting students with
a diagnosis of Asperger’s Syndrome. About a year later, a member of
Michael McManmon’s staff suggested he might also have Asperger’s
and/or reside on the Autism Spectrum. With a solid diagnosis behind
him, his vision began. Michael dreamed of opening another center
dedicated to Asperger students. During a trip to Orlando to attend an

---
## Page 973
IECA conference, Michael, another colleague, and I decided to explore
nearby Melbourne, Florida as a possible site for this new center.
It was perfect and the more we visited Melbourne the more I became
convinced I wanted to be a part of this endeavor. I moved to Melbourne
and became the Administrative Director of the Brevard Center. The
Brevard Center opened in August of 2004. The first year was a difficult
one. We weathered four hurricanes and two Program Directors. My role
of Administrative Director had grown naturally into the role of Program
Director as the center evolved. Since then I’ve learned that our students
are “special” in many ways. And I’ve learned that letting each one
“experience success” is key. Through the positive experience of
success, gaining confidence, and becoming optimistic about his or her
future, as each student achieves and creates a balance in his or her
life.
There have been many success stories over the years at the Brevard
Center. I am delighted to share these with you by starting off with an
overview of what some of our students have been able to accomplish
as they achieved skills for life, school, work, and independent living. I
am also going to talk about not being able to help each and every
single person that walks through the door. And lastly, to tell you about
one of our more challenging students and how he “did get there” in the
end. It’s as realistic a portrayal as I can give you about what it is like to
work day in and day out with young adults on the wide, varied, and
challenging Autism and Learning Differences Spectrum.
An Overview of Accomplishments
Chris graduated with his Bachelor’s degree in Clinical Psychology.
Peter, David, Kira, Holden, Lawrence, Zach, Andrew, Dane, and Ben
all received their AA degrees in General Studies. Charlie received his
AS degree in Drafting and Design. Sara and Daniel received their
certificate as Office Specialists. Sarah, Ross, Andrew, Zach, Carl, and
Thomas transferred to four-year universities to complete their degrees.
Taylor, Tim, Colin, and Lawrence have jobs and live on their own in

---
## Page 974
Melbourne. Jarrett, Val, Dane, and Dan moved back to their home
towns and are successfully working. Dave moved to New York City and
has a job.
I believe the Brevard Center’s success lies in its team approach. The
Brevard staff’s seasoned experience and training provide an
exceptionally strong foundation in recognizing and coordinating the
most effective supports for its students. Many of the Brevard Center
staff have worked at the Center for more than six years’ giving them the
opportunity to establish an excellent working relationship with one
another. The Brevard Center’s greatest strength lies in the ability to
combine ideas, implement strategies, and have its staff work as a team.
New Spaces and New Directions
The Brevard Center is celebrating its 11th successful year in
Melbourne, Florida. In anticipation of the growing need for our program,
as well as the expansion of the local Community College, CIP
purchased five acres directly across from Eastern Florida State College
three years ago. The renovations to the buildings were completed in
May of 2014. This new location has a 2000 square foot fitness room
and outdoor space for volleyball and other sports. That large outdoor
space will also give our students the opportunity to plant, nurture, and
learn how to grow an organic garden. We anticipate an on-site weekly
“farmer’s market” in the near future.
Not Everyone Will Be Successful
While we have great success stories we also, sadly, encounter
students who – for whatever reasons – we cannot reach or get through
to. It is important to acknowledge this too, especially to those reading
and using this book. For instance, I remember a student who was
particularly rigid. So rigid, that he refused to enter any restaurant that
did not serve Coca Cola™ products. When our Life Skills staff
members would knock on his bedroom door to tell him it was time for

---
## Page 975
cooking instruction, he would yell and curse at them through the door. I
met with him and asked if there might be a better way to advocate for
not wanting to participate in cooking instruction. He nodded his head
and muttered yes. I thought this is good; maybe we are getting a slight
“shift” in this thinking. I naively asked what that would be. He said he
could have opened the door and spat on them. Sadly, though not
surprisingly, that student went home after the first term at CIP.
Challenges and Solutions
While being the Program Director for students with Learning
Differences has been extremely rewarding, it has also been most
difficult at times. There are days I leave with a heavy heart, asking
myself was there something I could have done or said differently to
achieve a better outcome in a particularly difficult situation. I then
remind myself of the successes mentioned in the first part of this story.
Just recently, I interviewed a very meek young woman who graduated
from college with a four-year degree and was living at home with her
parents for the last two years – staying up all night on the Internet and
sleeping all day. She asked me, “If I come to your Center, what will I do
after that?” She was fearful that she would return to her parents’ home
and do the same thing again.
The Career Department and our career curriculum are an integral part
of the Brevard program. Strong internships and practical experiences
assist in the long-term success of these students.
Our transition assistance program also is a key to successfully
transferring to life “back home” or venturing out into independent living.
To facilitate transition, our advisors develop an individual transition plan
for each student leaving the program. Whether a student is planning to
return home, to the workforce, or is transferring to a four-year college to
complete their degree, there is a plan in place to assist them. I
explained this process to the young woman mentioned above.
Thankfully, today she is a student at the Brevard Center in our Career

---
## Page 976
Skills Training and Employment Program. I am confident that I will add
her name to the list of successes mentioned above.
A Unique Feature of the Brevard Center: The
Equine Social Skills Group
Many students at the Brevard Center have the opportunity to
participate in the Equine Social Skills Group located in Forever, Florida
where they learn about nonverbal communication and situational
awareness. Led by Dr. Sandra Wise, licensed psychologist, Dean Van
Camp, equine trainer and specialist, and Brevard Center’s Social Skills
Coordinator, Jodi Pierce, sessions in equine assisted therapy involve
no riding, as interactive activities with horses are done on the ground
and provide a wider variety of experiences that address issues such as
impulse control, anger management, problem solving, and social
functioning.
Students are taught the fundamentals of “operant conditioning” (a
process by which humans and animals learn to behave in such a way
as to obtain rewards), and are putting their skills into practice teaching
the horses at the ranch various practical and novel behaviors. This
social skills group is well liked by the students and this term we will
have two groups of four students going out to the ranch weekly as well
two students participating in an internship there.
