Alex
Lastly, I’d like to share a story about a student named Alex. When I first
met Alex at his interview, he was scowling. He clearly was a very
unhappy and angry young man. I asked him my series of “usual
interview questions.” He answered many of them with a shrug and
some of them, not at all. His parents became distraught as he was
introduced to staff and they watched his interactions with them. They
felt helpless and hopeless. The CIP Brevard staff agreed unanimously

---
## Page 977
that we would accept Alex as a student and try to help him. He entered
CIP as a student in our Career Program. Gradually, he let go of his
anger and began smiling. He learned to cook, clean, grocery shop, and
manage his budget. That was two years ago. Tomorrow, he will attend
his first class at Eastern Florida State College. His parents are amazed
and proud of Alex’s transformation and so are my staff and I. Alex still
has a way to go, but he is well on his way.
There are many “Alex”s out there and many of the readers of this book
are working with them. If given the chance, inspiration, motivation, and
encouragement by those working with all the “Alex”s – the list of
student successes portrayed in this story will continue on.
*
WITH THANKS: In Chapter 1, Adria Nassim, a CIP Alumna, spoke so
beautifully and from her heart in her piece “Living and Loving Life
Outside the Walls,” and about how self-acceptance of her diagnosis
has led to personal growth and becoming a “Autism/Special Needs
Advocate and Speaker.”
In the story below, she talks about a chance encounter with an
Autistic young man at a bus station and shows us all (especially those
of us residing on the Spectrum) that compassion, kindness, and most
of all understanding, go such a long way. Her encounter is beautifully
described as she yearns to speak up and out and say “I have Autism
too,” but chooses to focus on helping the young man get on and off
the bus with kindness and compassion. Thank you again, Adria for
this second contribution. Your words will go a long way toward helping
others understand why you and I sometimes do the things we do or
act the way we act.
THE BOY AT THE BUS STATION
by Adria Nassim, CIP Alumna Autism/Special
Needs Advocate and Speaker

---
## Page 978
My suspicions were first aroused by the DVD in his hands: Disney
Pixar’s “Shrek.” He looked to be in his mid to late teens and most
teenagers I knew didn’t watch Disney movies. Then, his blatantly
forward question, the way he just looked at me, and then looked at my
dog resting on the ground, and said: “Are you blind?” with such
childlike innocence and curiosity.
“No,” I said.
“Well, why do you have the dog then? Doesn’t he help you?”
“Yes,” I replied. “She finds the way home when I get lost.”
“How come you get lost?” His syntax, I thought … so inquisitive,
somewhat juvenile, surely it doesn’t match his age?
“It’s because of a Learning Disability,” I answered.
“I see,” he said, but I could almost guarantee he didn’t. “When is the
bus coming?” he asked, “When is it going to get here? The #3 bus,” he
pressed, the panic rising in his voice, “I don’t want to miss it!”
By then I knew I wasn’t sitting beside a young man. I was sitting on a
bench waiting for the bus next to a child in a young man’s body.
“It will be here in about ten minutes,” I said.
“Oh … good! I can’t miss it!”
Suddenly, a fire engine surged out of the fire station nearby. The boy
immediately covered his ears. I desperately wanted to cover mine too,
but I knew he was more afraid than me, so I didn’t. I also wanted to tell
him we were more alike than he could ever know. I recalled the
thousands of times as a child I had gone to play outside and would
immediately cover my ears and cower at the sound of sirens, which I
loathe to this day. I recalled how I too, was preoccupied with Winnie the
Pooh and the Disney Channel long after these things were considered
socially acceptable interests and especially how, to this day, my life is
governed by schedule and routine. How changes and surprise make
me terribly anxious and that this anxiety makes my voice sound just like
the boy’s fearful one. And, that I begin to cry and quote the lines of

---
## Page 979
certain films over and over again to calm down. I’m sure he does as
well. I thought: “I have Autism too,” I wanted to tell him.
“It’s alright,” I said when the truck had driven away. “It’s gone now.”
“What’s gone? Is the bus gone?” he asked, the panic coming back
again.
“No,” I said, “it isn’t even here yet.” I paused. “Oh … here it is now.”
He immediately got up and stood in line to board without saying
goodbye or thank you. I wasn’t offended. It is something we “Autistics”
sometimes do. We don’t mean to be rude, but sometimes we are just
too focused on the task at hand to think of anything else, even if it’s
something like saying goodbye or thank you.
Before we both got off at the mall later, I shouted toward the front for the
driver to please open the door. “Excuse me, sir, could you open the
door please?” Nothing. I tried again. Nothing.
“Hey Mister!” the boy yelled, “Open the door! I’m getting off too!”
The door slid open. Scowls and surly remarks followed us out the door.
The driver almost yelled at us, but we departed too quickly. If they
“only knew,” I thought. If both the driver and the young boy only
knew.
*
WITH THANKS: In my first book, “Made for Good Purpose …” I said
that “Happiness comes from within you; what you eat and how you
take care of yourself determine how you show up in life.” I have
learned the hard way; how maintaining a healthy lifestyle, getting
enough exercise, and eating a good diet increases my energy,
promotes positive social behaviors, and strengthens my immune
system. A healthy lifestyle helps those of us on the “ASD or LD
Spectrum” improve our self-esteem and how we perceive and view
others. Holly Brouker’s work with our students exemplifies this as she
helps our students figure out diet (including sensory diets), exercise,
nutrition, weight control, and physical fitness regimes that will serve

---
## Page 980
them best. Thank you, Holly, for bringing the work you do at our
Berkshire Center into such clear focus for the readers of this book.
REFLECTIONS OF A WELLNESS
COORDINATOR
by Holly Brouker, Wellness Coordinator, CIP
Berkshire
I am the Wellness Coordinator at the College Internship Program (CIP),
in Lee, Massachusetts.
After spending many years as a Physical Education/Health teacher for
the public school system in the State of Massachusetts, and instructing
many outdoor sports, including programming special outdoor
educational events, I was now overflowing with ideas that would assist
my learning-different students enrolled in the Wellness Program at CIP.

---
## Page 981
We have a user friendly, inviting, colorful area that encourages students
to come and work out. The space has full length mirrors, tall windows,
shelving for equipment, and a lot of open space. The look and feel of a
“membership fitness center” with all its exercise equipment and free
weights has allowed the Wellness Program to blossom.
Once we were up and running, it wasn’t long before many students
were attending wellness on a regular basis and actually enjoying the
benefits they felt from their achievements. As the practice of exercising

---
## Page 982
became a routine for the students they became empowered to show up
and begin their warm-ups entirely on their own initiative. It is always a
proud moment for me – as the person who is constantly encouraging
my students to “get moving” – to walk into the wellness room and find
many had arrived early and already begun to stretch and exercise.
Last week I got a wonderful response from an overweight student who
never exercised much in the past. After asking how he was feeling that
day he responded: “Much better now that I am moving on the
treadmill.” Ah, such music to my ears! I immediately proclaimed how
wonderful that was to hear.
Providing a variety of offerings is the key to keeping this population
interested. Even though repetition is important in developing a routine,
many students can get bored and lose motivation if they are not
stimulated. Music helps and many students are willing to bring in the
music they like to share with the class while exercising. Fitting exercise
or movements to the music adds to the fun of working the various
upper, lower and core muscle groups, as well as coordinating the gross
motor, range of motion actions, to the rhythms.
There are many processing skills going on before the movement even
takes place. Visual recognition, auditory understanding of cues,
coordinating body parts when more than one is being used, spatial
awareness (where the body is and amount of space utilized), and self-
confidence all play a major role.
Some students are fearful of making a mistake, mixing up lefts or
rights, moving slower, of the inability to perform some motions and will
just stop all attempts to try – thus limiting their possibilities for
improvement at all.
To counteract this, I tell my students that there is no right or wrong in
any of my classes, but to just try their very best and continue moving –
catching up when they can and never giving up completely. They are
encouraged and welcome to move their arms and feet in any fashion
they like rather than standing motionless. Many students enjoy making
up their own movements. This is a great opportunity for them to build

---
## Page 983
their confidence and I always praise them for their independent
creativity. I will often add their movements into the flow of the workout.
Working in this manner with students can serve as the beginning of
leadership skills – as often students who are asked to lead an exercise
of their choice and creation for the group happily take on the challenge.
Another important thing that students appreciate and that I remind them
of daily: The wellness room is a judgment-free zone. Students are
encouraged to assist and help each other when the appropriate
situation arises. Many times the session will pause in order for a
“teachable moment” to take place and this allows students to learn
from each other.
Many students on the spectrum have missed out on physical activities
when they were young, due to never being taught basic gross motor
skills. Now, having the chance to develop these skills at this time of
their lives, proves beneficial to many life-skill procedures. Through
practice and the basic breaking down of skills such as: throwing,
catching, footwork, hand-eye coordination, etc. will improve as will the
student’s gross and fine motor skills. These simple skills will lead
students to more independence in being able to grasp objects, open
jars, cans, windows, drive cars, and perceive daily tasks with less
difficulty and more competency.
I also explain that once they get moving, blood circulation improves,
resulting in a heightened sense of happiness and brain stimulus. Our
brains are made of cells and the increased blood flow to the brain from
exercise works just like a muscle being used over and over, getting
stronger and larger: The brain grows and performs better from the
stimulation.
Stretching is necessary for every student to practice. Assisted
stretching can also be beneficial when students are ok with someone
touching them and helping to gently push the leg or upper back a tiny
bit further. Most students opt out of this situation due to feeling
uncomfortable. It is challenging for many students on the ASD or LD
Spectrum to demonstrate proper posture due to their undeveloped

---
## Page 984
musculature that can often result in an unconventional stature.
Encouraging them to keep working at the stretches is beneficial, yet
difficult: 20-some years of not stretching does not immediately improve
without dedicated work … over time.
Praise, praise, and more praise for what they are doing during class. I
tell my students they are doing great during their workouts as long as
they are trying and doing their best. We make corrections to their form
once they understand the concept of improper form will not get the best
results for their efforts.
Many student benefits and positive changes are achieved through
exercise, diet, nutrition, and wellness classes. The most obvious is that
stress and anxiety levels decrease, and outbursts and emotional ups
and down begin to stabilize. Additionally, students have lost weight,
changed eating habits, learned African drumming, Zumba, Frisbee,
group games, weight training, Yoga, circuit training, breath work,
Pilates, rode ten miles on bikes, gone kayaking, swimming, played
Adult Community Volleyball, joined tennis leagues, hiked, learned Geo-
Caching and Letter Box finding. They have learned how to enjoy
physical activities, like never before.
One memory I will always cherish is a student who was on his return
half of a ten-mile bike ride and said he was having the best time he
EVER had at CIP while riding along and enjoying the sights along the
way as he pedaled along with a smile on his face. I knew exactly how
he felt. It’s a pleasure to assist my students towards a healthier, happier
way of life as their Wellness Coordinator.
*
WITH THANKS: Jennifer Kolarik has contributed throughout this book,
from teaching tips, to vignettes, to case studies, and through her story
“Awake Challenged, Punctually Challenged, and Underground
Thursday Challenged” in Chapter 5. She gives us yet another heartfelt
and inspiring snapshot or the work she does in her story: “A Day in the
Life of a CIP Career Coordinator: No Two Days Are Quite the Same.”

---
## Page 985
At CIP’s Brevard Center, Jennifer leads by example, and infuses her
creativity, sense of humor, excellent rapport with students, and her
teaching talents and gifts to all the students she works with. Jennifer is
also an incredible team player and I thank her for sharing her gift of
storytelling and writing with the readers of this Teaching Toolkit.
Thank you, Jennifer.
A DAY IN THE LIFE OF A CIP CAREER
COORDINATOR: NO TWO DAYS ARE QUITE
THE SAME
by Jennifer Kolarik, Career Coordinator, CIP
Brevard
Stephen Shore has said “if you’ve met one person with autism, you’ve
met one person with autism.” No two individuals with autism are the
same. And … I’ve also found that, often, no two days are the quite the
same at CIP.
Although my weekly Career Coordinator schedule typically “appears”
the same on paper, in reality no two days are alike. There is always
something new and different just around the corner. My typical
schedule is filled with career counseling appointments, classes that I
teach, job coaching at student internships, team meetings, and time for
documentation and communication.
In career counseling, I might help students with resumes, references,
cover letters, disclosure letters, interviewing … and the list goes on.
Also, during some weeks, I might present at workshops or webinars …
or meet with CIP families at a Parent Weekend event or awards
ceremony … or network to set up new internships … or bring students to
job fairs or career workshops.

---
## Page 986
Jennifer Kolarik doing what she does best: Helping and encouraging
one of her students
I always relish opportunities to observe students at their internships or
jobs so I can assess how they are performing and give feedback on
how they can improve. Deciding how best to share feedback so that it
will be received well by a student can sometimes feel like a tight-rope
balancing act. Some days go according to plan, and others involve a
high degree of flexibility and quick problem solving to adapt to
situations that arise and meet students’ needs.

---
## Page 987
For example, take Adam: Adam missed his ride to his internship last
week. Once he arrived at our center, we had him call his internship
right away. Initially, I thought that Adam would miss his internship that
day. But after some reflection, I believed that Adam would be capable
of taking the bus to his internship so we researched bus schedules.
Adam would have to take two buses with a changeover at the local
mall. This is something he had never done before but this situation was
the perfect learning opportunity to do this.
Triage for Teachers
Always look at each challenge faced by a student as a learning
opportunity. Brainstorm options quickly and be willing to adapt to
circumstances that arise.
Constantly look for opportunities to increase perseverance, a
necessary skill to be successful at a job. Do not shield your student
from failures or challenges or they will not learn how to recover when
they face them.
We printed out the information, reviewed the bus schedule carefully,
discussed where to meet the first and second buses, and made sure he
had the necessary money. I had Adam call me once he got on each bus
and when he arrived. He did it all beautifully. Although I wouldn’t do this
for all of our students, Adam was ready for the challenge. Staff at his
internship were counting on him and it is better to be late than not to
show up at all. And the last thing we wanted was to reward him for
being late with four extra hours of video game time … sound familiar?
As Career Coordinator, my first priority on any given day is to make
sure that all students make it to their internships (if possible). Many
times a student will call in “sick” and I try my best to evaluate what
“sick” means. Of course, all parents have faced these same situations

---
## Page 988
before. For example, “My child says he is ‘sick’ and doesn’t want to go
to school. He is not running a fever and felt fine last night … is he sick
enough to miss school … or is he avoiding something?”
For our students, I have to ask myself, does “sick” mean, “I overslept
and I won’t be ready in time?” Or “I’m just tired and don’t feel like going
to work today?” Or does it mean … “I didn’t do my college homework
which is due this afternoon (or I have an exam I need to study for)?” Or
maybe it means a student is legitimately sick and they need to stay
home to rest or visit a doctor.
If a student has exhibited a pattern of calling in sick to their internship, I
will explain how it will look to his or her supervisor and highly encourage
the student to work through the moment. On the other hand, if a student
has a pattern of being late, sometimes it is better to have them miss
their ride and learn from that natural consequence than to bail them out
of it by providing late transportation. And sometimes it is best to have
them take the bus late (like in Adam’s case).
Meet Heather. Heather had a job coach in place to help her at her
internship. She was doing very well and we both felt it would be good
for her to try one day a week without coach. However, when her first
day without a coach came, she was extremely anxious and did not want
to go to her internship program that day despite good intentions and
phone calls from staff. So I drove to her house to help her work through
that anxious moment and reminded her that another student would be
there to help her (as well as the staff at her internship site). She was
able to work through a difficult morning and stay the full shift on her
own. Excellent! Giving praise after a student works through a
challenging situation is definitely an important part of my job. Each
successful moment will lead to increased perseverance for a student
and in my experiences, perseverance when times get tough is one of
the biggest challenges for young adults on the spectrum.
Another student I was working with missed his internship work day.
This was a student who was having difficulty getting into a routine with
his internship (lots of “Executive Function” challenges), and had also
missed a day the week before. So I showed up at his house and

---
## Page 989
“offered” to take him to his internship. He got ready quickly and off we
went! Would this work if you had to do it all the time for a student? Of
course not, but sometimes it might take a step like this to get a student
back on track after they have derailed a bit. I just happened to be
heading to a meeting in the same direction, so it worked out well to take
him to his internship program that day.
Tips for Students
•
Put your health first in order to be an effective employee (take
medications consistently, get sleep, eat well, implement OT
recommendations for sensory issues, and see a therapist to
manage anxiety, depression, etc.).
•
It is usually better to do something “imperfectly” than to not do at
all (unless it involves safety of course).
•
Don’t be afraid to “ask, ask, ask!” for feedback. The key is to
practice asking in an appropriate way and at appropriate times.
Perhaps ask your supervisor to schedule a regular time every
other week to receive feedback.
Like most neurotypical people, our students seem to benefit the most
from a predictable routine. While some can adapt to slight variations in
their routine, many of our students struggle if they start at a different
time each day. For those students, I will try to schedule appointments
with consistent morning start times (for example: 8 a.m. or 9 a.m.). Staff
will often see a much higher success rate in dependability with a
consistent routine.
Yes, there are days that we all do not want to get out of bed … however,
with a consistent routine it is easier to fight through those tough
mornings knowing that your co-workers or clients are depending on

---
## Page 990
you. The irony is that while a consistent routine benefits young adults
with an ASD or LD diagnosis, it is also important to demonstrate
flexibility within that routine. Remember, consistency is good … but not
rigidity. As we all know, things don’t always go according to plan at
work and work procedures can and will change over time.
When selecting internships for students, I typically will start small and
build gradually. Too many young adults on the Spectrum have
experienced too many failures. If a student has never participated in a
job, internship, or community service, I would rather have him or her
start with just two to three hours per week. He or she can then learn the
tasks gradually and not be overwhelmed. And if a mistake occurs, it will
typically not be as “costly” to an organization. If a student can gradually
learn about his or her work strengths and challenges and then work to
improve in areas needed, things will go more smoothly, especially in the
beginning.
Triage for Teachers
•
Provide routine while still encouraging flexibility.
•
Start small and build gradually upon success (with time and
intensity).
•
Keep a good sense of humor.
•
Problem solve creatively and remember that each student is
unique.
•
Cherish the successes, big and small.
If a student is being successful during an internship or internship to
work program, build upon that success by gradually increasing hours
and seeing if he or she can continue to maintain his or her

---
## Page 991
performance. And … encourage each student to ask for feedback.
Some of our students would rather “duck and cover” and hope for the
best. But many times in life, that approach is likely to bite you when you
least expect it. Have the student ask his or her supervisor “how am I
doing?” Have each person ask what he or she might do to improve.
Co-workers can be a great resource for feedback. Explain the
conundrum of “assuming that just because nothing is said, that
everything is all right.” When I ask students how often they think
performance evaluations are conducted, many will say once a month.
But of course, once a year is the norm within the workplace … and
much can go wrong within a matter of weeks. I encourage my students
NOT to wait a year to get feedback … as they might not make it through
the year!
I have also found that most students appreciate a good sense of humor
whether it occurs when I’m teaching classes or working together during
career counseling or job coaching. Of course, it is important not to over
use sarcasm which can be difficult for someone on the ASD or LD
Spectrum to understand, but enjoying a good laugh helps keep
students engaged. And it teaches a good lesson to students: That it can
be helpful not to take yourself too seriously when at work. I also explain
that everyone makes mistakes and it is often good to have a good
laugh and learn from a mistake than get upset when something doesn’t
go as expected. I think having a great sense of humor is one
characteristic that the Brevard CIP teaching and advising team has in
common, which has made our team strong.
In my nine years at CIP, I have learned that above all else, it is so
important to be creative when problem solving. There is no perfect or
straightforward approach to working with people in general … let alone
working with young adults with an ASD or LD diagnosis. Each student
is different so each approach must be different. Some students respond
well to strict rules and boundaries … and for others, an indirect
approach might be needed (the heavy handed approach might drive
some students further away).

---
## Page 992
When faced with a student challenge, I like to reflect upon approaches
that have worked and not worked with other students. And … I listen to
my students … really listen. So much can be learned from them, not just
taught to them. I then bounce ideas off other staff (or a student’s
parents) before implementing a solution. I ask for feedback and try to
be flexible so I can adapt my thinking if an approach isn’t working.
My concluding words to readers: Cherish the successes, both big and
small. They will help you get through the challenging moments or days.
Remember that, while you may not always see an immediate impact, I
strongly believe that your efforts will pay off down the road. I keep in
touch with many of my former students, even years after they have left
our program. While my main focus with students during their time at
CIP is employment readiness, my end goal is always their ability to get
and maintain jobs. And above all, keep your good sense of humor … it
will help to cushion the bumps faced day to day in your journey!

---
## Page 993
The Experts Speak!
CHAPTER 12 OVERVIEW:
STEPHEN SHORE
BRENDA SMITH MYLES, JILL HUDSON, AND HYO JUNG LEE
JENNIFER COOK O’TOOLE
DAN MCMANMON
MICHAEL JOHN CARLEY
BARBARA BISSONNETTE
LIANE HOLLIDAY WILLEY
MICHAEL P. MCMANMON
DEBRA MUZIKAR
JOANNE LARA

---
## Page 994
The Experts Speak!
This chapter is devoted to hearing from the experts across the United
States and around the world who focus their work, their expertise, and
their professional writing on a particular aspect of the Autism or
Learning Differences Spectrum. By including the expertise and life’s
work of these distinguished educators, clinicians, and professionals in
Autism and Learning Differences (An Active Learning Teaching
Toolkit), the reader is offered an additional evidence-based fund of
knowledge on the best ways to work with and teach adolescents and
young adults on the ASD/LD Spectrum. Readers will find these
contributions encouraging, heartening, hopeful, and extremely valuable.
The knowledge portrayed in these individually written essays, articles,
and stories adds up to decades of clinical research, ongoing
implementation, documented assessment, and measurable outcomes.
This in turn has led to acceptance in today’s clinical world of the
ASD/LD research and writings of each contributor. These experts are
the professional men and women who have set standards, provided
evidence-based outcomes, and offered their expertise to Dr. Michael
McManmon and to CIP (College Internship Program) over the past 30
plus years.
Hearing about the lives, workplace experiences, clinical research,
development, and conclusions of others in the ASD/LD field is relevant
and adds tangible backing and support to the content of this Teaching
Toolkit.
WITH THANKS: I want to convey my thanks and gratitude to each
and every one of you – my colleagues working tirelessly in this field –
who have contributed your expertise, research, work, and personal
stories to this chapter.
I appreciate that each one of you took time out of your busy lives in
order to give this book an additional fund of knowledge: an array of

---
## Page 995
timely, insightful, well written pieces that lend enormous credibility to
this book.
Your writing, your research, your work, as well as the personal stories
from your lives will augment and reinforce the content of the first ten
chapters of this book in a very positive way. The educators, clinicians,
and support professionals, using Autism and Learning Differences (An
Active Learning Teaching Toolkit), will gain added insight, advice,
ideas, motivation, and inspiration for working with adolescents and
young adults who receive an ASD or LD diagnosis.
Some of you, like me, reside on the Autism Spectrum, or have
immediate family and friends who carry this diagnosis. Reading the
stories you wrote from this unique perspective and being able to refer
your experiences back to chapters or content in the book is a true gift
to our readers.
Please know how much I appreciate your support, encouragement,
faith, belief in me, in CIP, and in what this book is trying to
accomplish: Imparting skills for life, school, work, and independent
living to the amazing young people we all are privileged to know and
work with.
It is with heartfelt appreciation that I say one last “thank you” to you all
for contributing to my book.
OBSERVATIONS FROM THIS SIDE OF THE
TEACHER’S DESK
by Stephen Shore, EdD
“One day I had a stack of astronomy books on my desk. I was
busy taking notes and copying diagrams and a teacher told me I
never learned how to do math. However, I had learned just
enough math to teach statistics at the university level.”
Stephen M. Shore, EdD

---
## Page 996
“These sure are talkative students!” I thought as I began teaching my
first semester of courses at Adelphi University. After presenting
internationally for over a decade I secured a full-time faculty position at
Adelphi University teaching courses in autism and special education.
Conference organizers and the audiences seemed to like my
presentations – often inviting me to return to give additional talks –
suggesting that I had presenting information to groups of people in the
proverbial bag. Perhaps I did. However, things turned out to be very
different when teaching in higher education.
Takeaway
While there may be similarities between grade school or work
and higher education there are also going to be some larger
differences. It’s important to be open to these changes in
education, social interaction, and other aspects of being a
college student.
From the first day of class my students seemed to engage in what
seemed to be gratuitous social interaction. For example, my students
were so loud and garrulous that it almost hurt my ears! However, it was
always very easy to get the students to quiet down and launch into
about two hours of colorful power points, questions, and discussion.
Sometimes I felt a need to derail discussion between students in order
to finish all the material I had for the class.
Part of being a professor at Adelphi University and most other
institutions of higher education involves obtaining and giving feedback
to colleagues in a continuous quest to improve our teaching abilities. In
a “previous life” as professor of music, people seemed to find every
nugget of difficulty as a means for destructive criticism making teaching
very difficult and it was even impossible to remain at that school. A bit

---
## Page 997
shell shocked from that experience, I initially played it safe and asked
colleagues who I knew would give me friendly reviews before taking
risks.
Takeaway
The adult world in higher education tends to be very different
and more mature than in public school. Therefore it is easier to
find people willing to help. Although bullying may be present it is
usually drastically reduced or just not present.
Ask for help from your professor, fellow students, a tutoring
center, or disabilities office. Make it your business to know
where assistance can be obtained.
The written evaluations and following discussions with my colleagues
were good and I learned some valuable teaching tips regarding
presentation of material and interchange of information between
educator and student. However, the “gratuitous social interaction” I
described earlier that exists between my students before, sometimes
during, and after class continued to mystify me.
Reaching out of my comfort zone I asked a colleague who had 30 plus
years of experience as a special education teacher and administrator.
A straight shooter, she looked to me like someone who might tear you
limb from limb if given the chance. Fortunately, I found that assumption
to be completely untrue.
My colleague’s volley to my request for an observation was her wanting
to know my objectives for her time in my classroom – to which I replied
“I want to improve my teaching.” The colleague assented and
mentioned that it would be impossible for her to be silent in my class
and she would join my students in any discussion or class activity. With

---
## Page 998
the expert in my next session we began. Her strategy of blending in as
one of the students helped me quickly forget that she was evaluating
my teaching and the session went well.
My colleague’s feedback was fascinating. Her compliments on content
knowledge, clear delivery, and beautiful PowerPoint slides were
followed with an emphatic “why do you chop off the discussions?”
“Conversation between students is like pearls to be polished,” she
exclaimed. “It’s clear you know how to present material. Now find out
how the students feel about the material discussed. Find out about their
impressions. Even ask them what they learned and if they found the
session helpful.”
Takeaway
You will learn a lot more going out of your comfort zone.
That may mean asking a question that you think everyone else
knows the answer to. As I tell my students … the only stupid
question … is the one you didn’t ask because you thought it was
a dumb thing to say. It’s very likely that whatever question you
have, others will have the same query – and be secretly relieved
that you asked before they had to do so.
With a fresh set of proverbial ears I learned that inter-student
conversation before, during, and after class was usually focused on the
course matter at hand. The focus on the material suggested to me that
instead of presenting the material for the session, perhaps we could
cover the same ground through more interactive discussion. Gradually,
my teaching style morphed from doing most of the talking with
PowerPoint as an outline to guiding student conversation to a majority
of the content being covered through discussion rather than didactic

---
## Page 999
lecture. Doing so made teaching easier, more rewarding, and even
greater fun than before!
Takeaway
Be open to feedback as constructive criticism. You will learn a
lot, things will make more sense, and life will become easier.
My changes in teaching style have morphed over into my presentations
as well. While my talks – especially to larger groups – are more
didactic, I enjoy working in discussion where possible. Small groups
often become workshop-like with more interaction between participants
and with me serving as a coordinator of the flow of information,
concepts, and feelings then being the main focus of the event. Working
with and collaboration with the students as a facilitator rather than
didactic presentations are exactly what my college students needed.
Takeaway
My experiences as a professor in higher education were
enhanced when I approached teaching course material in a
collaborative fashion as a facilitator of acquiring knowledge.
Similarly, it is the responsibility of the student to take an active
role in their learning. Your return in higher education directly
correlates to the effort you put into it. That goes for students and
their professors.

---
## Page 1000
As a person on the Autism Spectrum the lessons I have learned have
important implications for individuals with autism making a successful
transition into higher education and staying there.
•
Just as you have preferred learning modalities, your professors
are going to have particular teaching styles which are likely
aligned with how they learn best. Your instructors may not even
be aware that they are visual, auditory, kinesthetic, or other type
of learner.
•
Adaptability is important. Whereas I began as a highly visually
based instructor, I realized that I needed to work on my auditory
based interaction with my students to supplement the visually
based teaching I did. Similarly, where possible do your best to
engage with how the instructor is teaching. Many times as a
college student I would have to convert concepts presented
orally into graphic form before I began to make sense of it.
•
Listen to your mentors and others supporting you through
college. Although it may be scary at times to seek and hear
feedback, these people want to see you succeed and will do
what they can to make it happen.
BIOGRAPHY: Diagnosed with “Atypical Development and strong
autistic tendencies” and “too sick” for outpatient treatment Dr. Shore

---
## Page 1001
was recommended for institutionalization. Nonverbal until four, and
with much support from his parents, teachers, wife, and others,
Stephen is now a professor at Adelphi University where his research
focuses on matching best practice to the needs of people with autism.
In addition to working with children and talking about life on the
Autism Spectrum, Stephen presents and consults internationally on
adult issues pertinent to education, relationships, employment,
advocacy, and disclosure as discussed in his books “Beyond the
Wall: Personal Experiences with Autism and Asperger Syndrome;”
“Ask and Tell: Self-advocacy and Disclosure for People on the Autism
Spectrum,” the critically acclaimed “Understanding Autism for
Dummies;” and the newly released DVD “Living Along the Autism
Spectrum: What It Means to Have Autism or Asperger Syndrome”.
President emeritus of the Asperger’s Association of New England and
former board member of the Autism Society, Dr. Shore serves in the
Interagency Autism Coordinating Committee, Asperger Syndrome and
High Functioning Autism Association, United States Autism and
Asperger Association, and other autism related organizations.
THE HIDDEN CURRICULUM IN COLLEGE
by Brenda Smith Myles, PhD, Jill Hudson, MS,
and Hyo Jung Lee, PhD
“Understanding the hidden curriculum is the most important
aspect of life.”
Judy Endow, autistic person
The college years can be exciting and fulfilling for many students,
including those with high functioning autism spectrum disorder
(HFASD). In fact, young adults with HFASD possess many
characteristics that foster college success:
•
Some people with HFASD have unusual memory capacity
and/or a natural affinity for computers – both of these can give a

---
## Page 1002
student a head start.
•
The formal style required for academic essay writing is usually a
lot easier to master than casual social conversation.
•
Hans Asperger suggested that academia might be the natural
environment for more academic people with HFASD. Other
researchers have even suspected that the stereotype of the
“absent minded professor” might be based on people with this
condition.
•
Students with HFASD are often original and creative in their
thought patterns.
•
Students with HFASD can have good attention to detail – and
precision.
•
They can be very independent in their study.
•
They usually try extremely hard at what they are doing.
•
Being single minded and determined are useful learning traits
when something has to be studied in depth. (Blamires and Gee
2001, pp.10 – 11)
Despite these assets, however, many students with HFASD experience
challenges in the college environment. In many cases, the difficulties
involve social understanding or misunderstanding (Golan, Baron-
Cohen, and Hill 2006; Lawson, Baron-Cohen, and Wheelwright 2004;
Schultz et al. 2003), such as understanding words with multiple
meanings, recognizing sarcasm, interpreting facial expressions, or
attempting to interpret others’ thoughts. One area of social
misunderstanding that is often neglected, despite its lifelong
importance, is the “Hidden Curriculum,”
Definition of the Hidden Curriculum
The “Hidden Curriculum” is the set of rules or guidelines that are often
not directly taught but are assumed knowledge (Bieber 1994; Garnett

---
## Page 1003
1984; Hemmings 2000; Jackson 1968; Kanpol 1989; Myles Smith et
al. 2013). The “Hidden Curriculum” contains items that impact social
interactions, school performance, and safety. The hidden curriculum
also includes idioms, metaphors, and slang – items that most people
learn through observation or through subtle cues, but that elude most
individuals on the Autism Spectrum.
Because limited lists of “Hidden Curriculum” items exist (it is doubtful
that a comprehensive list can ever be created), it is difficult for HFASD
students to recognize “Hidden Curriculum” items. One of the primary
means of identifying a “Hidden Curriculum” item is when a “Hidden
Curriculum” error occurs.
Helpful Hints for Students
If a professor or fellow student has said one of the following
phrases to you, it is probably a sign that you have committed a
“Hidden Curriculum” error:
•
I shouldn’t have to tell you, but …
•
It should be obvious that …
•
Everyone knows that …
•
Common sense tells us …
•
No one ever …
The “Hidden Curriculum” is complex and elusive. Despite this, breaking
a “Hidden Curriculum” rule can make a person a social outcast. For
example, there is a “Hidden Curriculum” for nose picking. It is not
“Don’t pick your nose.” Rather, it is “Pick your nose in the bathroom
and use a tissue.” Not following this “Hidden Curriculum” can cause a
person to be shunned.

---
## Page 1004
For example:
Professor Marks asked Lauren, a student with HFASD, if he had the
time. Lauren replied, “Yes,” and went back to reading his textbook. Dr.
Marks’ quizzical expression revealed that he thought Lauren was being
curt, rude, or antisocial. Lauren, reading his book, did not notice his
expression. Lauren committed several “Hidden Curriculum” errors.
Helpful Hints for Students
•
When someone asks, “Do you have the time?” he or she
does not want a yes or no response but the actual time at the
moment.
•
When someone provides a response to a question, it is
usually best to look at that person to ensure that the person
understands the speaker’s remarks. A facial expression can
usually provide a cue.
•
When someone responds to a question, it is typical to
maintain eye contact or orientation to allow the other person
the opportunity to respond (i.e., thank you or a nod – which
can also mean thank you).
•
Students generally should attempt to be perceived as
respectful to their professors.
Further examples:
1. In the cafeteria over lunch, Mike and Jim were talking about whom
they would ask to the dance Friday night. Mike said he would
eventually ask Sally, but only after he had been able to talk to her a
little more and become more comfortable with the idea.

---
## Page 1005
Jim, who has HFASD, did not realize that Mike needed time to
warm up with Sally before asking the big question. When she came
over to their table to say hello, Jim exclaimed in front of Sally,
“Mike, now you can ask Sally to go to the dance Friday night. Sally,
Mike wants to take you to go, ok?” Mike was embarrassed and just
smiled.
2. While hanging out in the student union building, Zach wonders why
he has to continue to get the attention of his peers by tapping them
on the shoulder as they constantly turn away. He is trying to tell
them about the newest developments in chemical engineering. He
does not understand why they keep changing the subject even
though they commented that chemical engineering was “real
interesting.” Zach did not pick up on the sarcastic facial expression
or tone of voice used.
Every situation has a unique “Hidden Curriculum”, which is assumed
and absorbed by almost everyone in the environment. The “Hidden
Curriculum” constitutes those implied messages given and received
over time that eventually set the boundaries for successful interactions.
Without the ability to perceive those messages, students with HFASD
often fail to communicate effectively, ending up confused, frustrated,
and misunderstood.
Common “Hidden Curriculum” Areas that
Present Problems within College Settings
College students with HFASD encounter countless situations that have
a “Hidden Curriculum.” These go beyond interactions with professors
and academic requirements and include:
•
Time management.
•
Materials management.
•
Procrastinating perfection.
•
Financial management.

---
## Page 1006
•
Stress and anxiety.
•
Finding a niche.
Professors and academic requirements:
Significant differences exist between high school teachers and colleges
professors. While high school teachers’ priorities generally focus on
instruction and, to a great extent, helping students master content,
many college professors expend their resources on their research and
consider teaching a secondary or tertiary concern. In addition,
traditionally they rarely focus on helping students organize and follow
through on assignments.
Helpful Hints for Students
The following “Hidden Curriculum” items may relate to the
professors in your college.
•
Most professors will not remind you that assignments are
due or tests are coming up.
•
Many professors will not focus on whether or not you
understand their lectures. They assume that you understand
unless you ask for assistance.
•
Some professors encourage students to ask questions;
others do not. Also, it is important to know how many
questions a student may appropriately ask during class.
•
If you wish to see a professor about his or her class, visit
during office hours or make an appointment. Most professors
post their preferences for student meetings on their websites
or on their office doors.

---
## Page 1007
Time management:
In college, students often have more unstructured free time than
scheduled time. This is a major adjustment for students with HFASD
who are used to following routines and who thrive within a structure that
is typically planned by others. Time management in college is essential.
College students must select classes, determine the amount of time
needed to prepare for class, determine how long to spend on one
homework assignment before moving on to the next, and so on.
Students with HFASD often focus on one task at a time and feel
compelled to complete it before moving on to the next. However,
because of class demands, typically many tasks must be balanced at
the same time.
For example:
Sam had three tests scheduled in the next week. Thursday through
Sunday prior to the examinations, he studied for his first test and did
little else. On Monday morning, he was satisfied with his preparation for
the test and performed well. However, when he discovered that he had
only Monday evening to study for his two tests on Tuesday, he
panicked. He was overwhelmed and had trouble focusing on the
material because he was preoccupied with the limited time left to study.
Helpful Hints for Students
The following “Hidden Curriculum” items might assist in time
management.
•
Establish a schedule for studying and stick to it. You are
solely responsible for ensuring that you study.
•
Plan to study throughout the semester for each class. Even if
the class only requires a mid-term and a final, it is best to
study some each week.

---
## Page 1008
•
Realize that it is likely to be impossible to complete a paper
or other task in one sitting. You may need to leave an
assignment incomplete during a study session if it is not due
immediately.
Materials management:
Dorm life can be exciting, but it can also be an organizational challenge
… cramming important possessions such as clothing, computers,
television, and schools supplies into a small space. Understanding
boundary lines of personal space, arranging belongings in a way that
makes them easy to store, easy to locate, and functional for use are all
challenges that need to be met.
For example:
Justin was late for class again because he could not find the right book
and folder for his particular class. He tripped over his dirty clothes in the
middle of the floor as he shuffled around searching for his supplies. He
checked his desk, but it was overflowing with papers and books, and
he could not sort out which materials went together. Finally, he saw an
assignment sitting on the printer. He grabbed it, hoping it was the
correct one.
Helpful Hints for Students
Materials management can be problematic unless the “Hidden
Curriculum” is understood, as suggested in the following:
•
If you have a roommate in a dorm or an apartment, identify
which specific areas belong to you, which belong to your
roommate, and which are communal.

---
## Page 1009
•
If you tend to be messy and your roommate is not, you will
probably have to change your habits.
•
Pack up your backpack the evening before class.
•
Consider having multiple backpacks for different days of the
week.
•
Consider consulting an organizational magazine, such as
Real Simple, for ideas on how to organize your desk and
space. If you have adequate financial resources, talking with
someone at a store that caters to organizational needs may
be helpful.
•
Leaving towels and dirty clothes on the floor or out in the
open almost always will upset your roommate.
•
Decide with your roommate which of his or her belongings
you may use without permission and what requires prior
permission.
Procrastinating perfection:
Individuals with procrastinating perfectionist tendencies need every
detail to be exact, just as originally thought out, regardless of any time
or material constraints. This often results in no product at all. This
tendency can affect all areas of a person’s life from class projects to
social encounters. Procrastinating perfectionists invest too much time
on details because they have a preconceived idea that must be carried
through. It is their way or no way. This tendency is often a barrier – a
task must be completed perfectly or not at all. Many people have a
tendency to procrastinate or to be a perfectionist. While that can cause
a problem, it is the combination of these two characteristics that can be
truly problematic.
For example:
1. Shannon anticipated Clare’s birthday for weeks. She had finally
decided on the perfect gift, how she would wrap it, and where she

---
## Page 1010
would give it to her. As Shannon began her hunt for the gift so
specifically detailed in her mind, she discovered that it was nowhere
to be found. In addition, she could not find the right color of
wrapping paper to match the ribbon that she had picked out. She
was unable to change the idea in her mind, so she spent all day
searching for the present and wrapping paper. At the end of the
day, Shannon was so frustrated that all she could do was cry.
2. James’ geology professor assigned a paper to be completed within
two weeks. James almost immediately defined his topic and even
decided how he would complete it. However, because he could not
conceptualize an introduction, he did not write down one word for
over a week. Once he sat down and began to work he started over
five times, becoming increasingly frustrated. Each time he stopped
it was because the assignment did not appear exactly as he
envisioned it in his mind.
3. As Becca began decorating her dorm room, she looked through
magazines for ideas. However, because she did not know how to
make her room a replica of the one in the magazine, she did not
decorate at all. When it was suggested to her that decorating could
be a process and items could continuously be added until the
desired look was achieved, Becca responded that she did not want
to have her room half done and “look bad,” so instead, she did
nothing.
Helpful Hints for Students
•
If you find yourself being a procrastinating perfectionist, seek
help from a college counselor.
•
If you are stuck when doing an assignment, it is best to get a
few words down on paper to begin the task.

---
## Page 1011
•
Sometimes working with a study partner to prepare an
outline for an assignment or develop an introduction is an
excellent way to stop procrastination.
Financial management:
Budgeting is a common challenge for college students. It can be
particularly difficult for students with HFASD because money
management is often an abstract concept.
For example:
Mary thought as long as she had checks in her checkbook she had
money in her account. In addition, she did not feel responsible for
paying bills that she had not opened because she had not actually seen
them.
For many students, this is an area where they have had little prior
responsibility. Without understanding that a caretaker was always
paying for the comforts of home, Mary simply enjoyed television, lights,
and water, expecting them to work without considering who paid for the
convenience.
In addition, with the ease of gaining and using credit cards these days,
students can accrue unexpected debt quickly without understanding
the consequence of swiping their ATM or credit card.
Here’s another example:
As long as the card was accepted at stores, Steven continued to use it.
Unaware that his charges continued to pile up, he was shocked to
receive his statement showing his charges had reached the maximum
limit. In addition, he did not understand why extra fees for being over
his credit limit were charged to his account, when in the store he paid
the amount just for the purchase. He was also puzzled by the 23%
finance charge.

---
## Page 1012
Helpful Hints for Students
•
Establish up-front with your roommate who is responsible for
expenses. If you order pizza without discussing it with your
roommate, even if she eats a piece or two, she might think
that you are paying for the pizza because you did not discuss
this with her before ordering the pizza.
•
Have a budget. Decide in advance how much you should
spend each month and try to stick to it.
•
Use debit cards and credit cards only in a planned manner.
•
Remember that all credit card debt and student loans must
be repaid.
•
Consult an adult you trust to help you establish budget if this
is the first time you have created one.
Stress and anxiety:
The impact of stress and anxiety underlies and complicates many of the
problematic interactions and situations encountered by students with
HFASD. In every example throughout this story, social skills deficits and
elevated stress/anxiety complicate already problematic situations.
Whether in the dorm, in the classroom, hanging out with peers, or
studying in a group, each demands an element of appropriate social
behavior and stress management.
For example:
1. Mark became so overwhelmed with his schedule of homework and
his upcoming chess tournament that he could not figure out how to
budget his time. As a result, he became so anxious that he locked
himself in his dorm room and did not come out for three days.

---
## Page 1013
2. When Mary was working on a group project with four class
members, she became increasingly frustrated with what she
perceived as their level of stupidity. She did not know how to initiate
her ideas in an effective manner, nor did she understand that the
group needed to work collaboratively, while allowing everyone to
make suggestions and compromise each idea into one effective
tactic. Because she was unable to voice her needs, Mary’s stress
level hit its maximum, and she ended up screaming out in frustration
that she would do all of the assignment herself.
Helpful Hints for Students
Understanding that stress and anxiety can be debilitating and
excessive is key.
•
People may not tell you that you are experiencing stress.
However, if people use words such as “irritable,” “difficult to
get along with,” or “gets upset about you,” these may be
indicators that you are under stress and that your stress is
affecting your behavior.
If you are spending excessive time sleeping, you may be
experiencing stress and anxiety and may need to see a
counselor.
It is typical for college students to become stressed, and it is
typical for college students to seek assistance from a counselor
so don’t hesitate to take advantage of these services.
Finding a niche:
College campuses attract diverse individuals with unique perspectives
and genuine interests. You may find that students with HFASD are not

---
## Page 1014
the only individuals with a special or genuine interest in a particular
topic. The college years are often times in which students, including
those with HFASD, meet others who share their genuine interests.
Helpful Hints for Students
Genuine interests will help you make friends – some may
become lifelong friends; others may be friends for a semester or
year. Additionally, exploring and sharing a genuine interest with
others may provide an introduction to a potential career option
later on. Seek out clubs and activities that interest you.
•
Don’t be afraid to approach others who you think may have
similar interests.
•
If you reach out to another person twice and he or she does
not respond favorably, that generally means that it is time to
find another potential friend.
•
If the same type of social mistakes occurs with you and your
friends over and over again, you may need to develop a new
repertoire of social skills. A college counselor may be able to
help. It may also mean that you may need to find a new
friend who is more compatible with you.
This may be the time in your life when your genuine interest
becomes a vocation. Work with a career counselor to identify
coursework that can help you pursue a degree that matches
your genuine interest.
Summary

---
## Page 1015
College is a time of independence for young people. No longer living
under the direct supervision of their parents, college students must
independently structure every segment of their lives from brushing teeth
to studying to budgeting money and time. In addition, they must
balance social opportunities and academic requirements, allocating
appropriate time and resources to each. For even the most socially
competent college student, these tasks can be daunting.
Understanding the “Hidden Curriculum” that surrounds the multiple
environments and activities in college can help make college an
exciting time of growth and independence.
BIOGRAPHY: Brenda Smith Myles, PhD is the recipient of the Autism
Society of America’s Outstanding Professional and Princeton
Fellowship Awards. She has made over 500 presentations and written
over 
150 
publications. 
Brenda 
Smith 
Myles 
was 
recently
acknowledged as the second most productive applied researcher in
ASD in the world. She is a consultant with the Ziggurat Group and
Chief of Programs and Development for the Autism Society. Dr. Myles
is also a consultant with the Ohio Center for Autism and Low
Incidence in Columbus, Ohio.
BIOGRAPHY: Jill Hudson, MS is a certified child life specialist trained
at Johns Hopkins University and has a graduate degree in
autism/Asperger Syndrome from the University of Kansas. Hudson is a
private consultant working with families and children of all ages
internationally. She is author of two books and several articles and is
consultant with the Ohio Center for Autism and Low Incidence in
Columbus, Ohio.

---
## Page 1016
BIOGRAPHY: Hyo Jung Lee, PhD is part of the Department of
Education at Dongguk University in Seoul, South Korea. He is the
author of many articles and research papers on ASD.
BOTTOMS UP!
by Jennifer Cook O’Toole adapted from
Sisterhood of the Spectrum

---
## Page 1017
In the United States, every Christmas Day brings the annual television
network airing of The Sound of Music. While the house still smells of
eggnog and edelweiss (OK, balsam fir) Julie Andrews sings “Let’s start
at the very beginning/A very good place to start …” And that makes
sense – whenever learning anything new, you start at the beginning,
obviously, and then you move on from there.
The challenge when teaching or raising children with Asperger’s
(Asperkids) isn’t whether to start at the beginning, it’s in determining
precisely where that beginning might be.
For example: the Von Trap children start their singing lesson by
echoing and then naming notes (musical building blocks): “When you
know the notes to sing, you can sing most anything,” Maria (Andrews)
instructs gaily. And sing they do. They assemble the notes into phrases
and the phrases into melodies, then they add lyrics and lo and behold,
they have music. That’s one way to learn – adding up specific examples
to create a larger whole, like assembling a mosaic of bits of tile. It’s a
bottom-up approach.
Traditional education and authoritarian views of parenting, on the other
hand, tend to favor a top-down, “because I said so,” concept-first,
details-later form of instruction. It’s how I learned to play the violin, via
“the Suzuki Method.” We were taught almost immediately to play
melodies by ear, with great emphasis on memory. Much later, we were
introduced to written notes and sheet music, but even at that point,
every piece was reviewed and memorized without relying upon a piece
of paper.
These different approaches to music lessons embody the “where’s the
beginning” dilemma: Is the beginning of learning at the foundation, as in
Maria’s “Doe-a-deer” building blocks? Or, as in the “Suzuki Method,”
is the beginning a complete whole, broken down only after we’ve
learned to love the tune? The answer, really, has more to do with who is
learning and how they need to learn.
Teach How They Learn

---
## Page 1018
Temple Grandin has noted that most individuals on the Spectrum are
usually bottom-up learners. And I thoroughly agree. I would have
wanted to learn to make music using Maria’s building blocks, just as I
began dance with basic positions and steps before pirouettes and
leaps.
Most neurotypical folks, however, are top-down learners; forget the
notes and sharps and things, you all want to start with melody. Of
course, neither way of learning is right or wrong, unless, that is, we
mismatch student and style. Then, boredom, frustration, and self-doubt
take over the show.
Asperkids depend upon us to teach them in the way they need to learn.
I should know. I’m an Aspie, I have three Asperkids, and I was a
teacher. So this is a topic I understand intimately. Like most people on
the Spectrum, I absorb information best by engaging with it directly,
noting specific, concrete experiences, facts, and examples. Then, I
spot trends, notice patterns and develop a perception.
Truth be told, I was powerfully fortunate in school; everything academic
came easily to me. Mind you, I was also the consummate Aspie
perfectionist, so “excellent” wasn’t good enough for me – only “perfect”
(in the most challenging subjects) would do.
This is why, at age 13, I completely freaked out. As a high school
freshman, I had elected advanced geometry for my math course of the
year, a bold placement that had to be staff-approved. I wasn’t being
arrogant; I knew I could tackle this challenge, I wanted the transcript
that went along with it and I enjoyed pushing myself to learn new things
(I still do). However, this class threw me completely.
Suddenly, we were memorizing seemingly random theorems about
abstract rules which seemed unimportant and disconnected. For
example, we read that a trapezoid is isosceles if and only if the base
angles are congruent. Ok, well, I understood the words, and the little
picture in the textbook looked right, so, if they said so … But there was
no real learning happening.

---
## Page 1019
Then, suddenly, we were expected to apply these random theorems in
real time when solving problems that didn’t look anything like the
original instructional illustrations. I was lost and, worst of all; I truly
didn’t understand what I didn’t understand. And how do you ask for
help when you don’t even know what’s confusing you?
Keep It Practical
Skip ahead about 20 years. For one year, I found myself
homeschooling my Asperkids, filling in holes where their genius-level
IQs didn’t match with their confidence or comprehension. By this point,
I had already fallen in love with the “Montessori” method of learning
because of its basis in sensorial, first-hand exploration of concrete
materials. Children first encounter ideas by seeing, touching, and
hearing three-dimensional expressions of sophisticated concepts. After
doing so, they independently and organically begin to make high-level
connections, analyze ideas, and question possible patterns.
One day, I brought out the geometry stick box, which contained flat
wooden bars, color coded according to their lengths. Each stick also
had holes where drawing pins could affix it to a cork tile.
On this particular day, my Asperkid was building trapezoids. I told her
she might use any color (length) bars she liked and to experiment, to
make every kind of trapezoid she could. Knowing from previous lessons
that two opposite sides of a trapezoid had to be parallel, she began by
using a “spacer” bar to keep the distance between tops and bottoms
equidistant (no following lines or tracing with rulers, she was building it
to be parallel). Then she filled in the sides.
After she decided she was done, I asked my daughter to find any
trapezoids where the non-parallel sides were the same color (length).
Easy. Then, I handed her a protractor and made my final request:
Would she please measure the different interior angles in each
trapezoid and jot them down in a notebook?

---
## Page 1020
“Huh,” I heard her mutter a little bit later. “Mom, take a look at this.
Every single time, the top angles are congruent and the base angles
are congruent too.” She’d made a discovery from trapezoids, not from
a textbook. “What about the others, the ones where the nonparallel
sides were different lengths?” I pushed, and she turned back to check.
“Nope!” she decided. “Not in these. The angle pairs don’t match
anymore.”
After just a few questions, she was able to determine for herself that
trapezoids are only isosceles if the base angles are congruent, and vice
versa. Twenty years before, I didn’t understand the same concept
because it was taught top-down from a book, and I couldn’t generalize
that information. But my Asperkid got concrete, hands-on, bottom-up
instruction and discovered the reality of geometry for herself.
Keep It Logical
Those of us on the Autistic Spectrum rely upon specific facts,
examples, and experiences to logically construct absolutes. We start
with fundamentals – grammar, numerical patterns, music notation – and
build up to bigger pictures. Asperkids learn that way, so we adults need
to teach that way. Because Asperkids aren’t typical, neither are the
patterns they observe nor the theories they propose. That’s a good
thing. If we lived in a world where everyone arrived at the same
conclusions, we would be without creativity, problem solving, or
curiosity.
Our bottom-up learning is also part of the reason for Asperkids’
amazing ability to retain tomes of facts, but it is also why they struggle
with general (seemingly obvious) principles. It is why Asperkids need to
concentrate on something until they have mastered it, rather than
dabble casually at this and that. It is why Asperkids need detailed rules
and plans of action rather than general suggestions, timetables, or
guidelines.

---
## Page 1021
And, it’s why if particular experiences, facts, or examples don’t add up
to the exact big picture you want to teach, there will be a locking of
horns and a gnashing of teeth. Any Asperkid is going to be frustrated,
confused, upset, and utterly lost when she feels as though 2+2
suddenly has to equal 5, and she just knows that that’s not true.
Keep It Real
Asperkids don’t easily generalize from one subject or experience to
another; being told to clean off the counter in the bathroom before
washing it down doesn’t mean they will do the same in the kitchen,
unless you say so. Our minds simply do not operate as yours do. So,
for the sake of your students, clients, or kids, you have to operate
differently too. Encourage first-hand encounters with the world through
any means possible; more experiences means more data to assemble
into those concept file folders.
For example, forget lectures, worksheets, and tedious textbooks.
Teach history through field trips to battlegrounds, by reading scans of
handwritten letters, listening to popular music of the day online,
experimenting with authentic recipes, playing historically accurate
games, reading real advertisements, newspapers and etiquette guides,
and by exploring portraiture and photography. Interact with real things.
Then discuss patterns in the resources (behavioral expectations,
cultural norms, popular trends), and finally, watch your Asperkid
assemble perceptions, mosaics, concepts.
Think of Einstein and e=mc2. Generalizing to great degrees of
abstraction is entirely doable for people on the Spectrum, if they have
the right start. Parents and teachers have to present numerous
concrete examples of a concept in multiple settings to set imaginations
whirring.
Given enough concrete examples (how many depends on the
individual, the age, and the concept), the child will assemble a broader
– and possibly brilliant – takeaway understanding.

---
## Page 1022
Remember those music lessons we discussed? Well, now imagine that
a symphony is about to get its debut performance on stage. When the
musicians first read the new piece, each one learned her part. Then in
rehearsal, the conductor probably pointed out where common melodic
themes would emerge. Only the conductor, who held the entire
orchestra’s score, could see the big picture. Everyone else could see
only what was directly in front of his or her eyes.
As an adult in the life of an Asperkid, you are a lot like that conductor.
You will have to point out some themes and connections that Asperkids
cannot yet see. First, though, allow them to learn their own melodies.
Give them the chance to experience and explore the world directly, to
hear the music and to absorb rather than simply memorize. Let them
create, develop, and invent tunes that aren’t just stuck in their heads,
but which begin in their wonderfully precocious hearts.
BIOGRAPHY: Jennifer Cook O’Toole is the creator of The
Awesomeness Foundation & Asperkids LLC, and is author of the
internationally-bestselling Asperkids book series (2012–2015). These
books are all Amazon bestsellers, including several “#1”s and the
ASA’s 2014 Outstanding Literary Work of the Year. Jennifer has been
recognized as one of the “World’s Top Aspie Mentors,” is the winner of
the Temple Grandin Global Contribution Award, has advised the
President’s Council at the White House, addressed Her Royal

---
## Page 1023
Highness the Countess of Wessex, in England, and was named one of
the 50 Most Influential Women in North Carolina. Jennifer keynotes
around the globe and is the mom of three (awesome) Asperkids, and
proudly, is an Aspie, herself.
“GENERATION AUTISM”
by Dan McManmon, President, College
Internship Program (CIP)
My father has Asperger’s. Now in his mid-sixties, he was diagnosed in
his early fifties and happens to be the founder of one of the most
successful post-secondary programs supporting young adults with
Autism and Learning Differences in the world. Since my start in the
family business ten years ago, I’ve worked my way up through the
ranks to become CEO.
During my personal and professional journey, I’ve faced a unique set of
challenges, but one that is not all that different than many of you (who
are) reading this Toolkit. Most of the staff members at our six centers
nationwide have someone in their immediate family, or are close and/or
connected to someone who is on the Autism Spectrum.
It is certainly common for parents to have autistic kids in this day-and-
age. However, it is not so common for kids to have autistic parents. As
time marches on, this phenomenon will be more common during the
course of this next generation. With somewhere between 40,000 to
50,000 officially diagnosed individuals with Autism turning 18 this year
alone, and a projected 500,000 over the next decade, there will
certainly be an increase in adults with Autism and other Neurological
Differences.
The big question that continues to linger is: What do we – as educators,
clinicians, support professionals, parents, and siblings – do with the
wave of autistic people who are becoming adults? And … how do we
prepare for this evolving population?

---
## Page 1024
Unfortunately most of the focus is on last minute interventions – not
future supports. My aim here is provide insight into my experience of
having grown up with a father with Autism. In this sense and context, I
already have a lifetime of experience in what will be one of the next big
emerging sectors of people in society. Who knows, maybe the next
demographic cohort entering adulthood should be called Generation
Autism.
I am number four out of five siblings. My three older sisters had the
unfortunate experience of having a “helicopter parent” with Asperger’s
(in typical helicopter parenting, a mother or father swoops in at any sign
of challenge or discomfort, so called because such a parent “hovers”
like a helicopter). This was very emotionally challenging for them and
we’ve reminisced on a myriad of both humorous and tragic stories;
however, I believe it has actually brought us closer as a family.
Some of the key things that I’ve learned from having an autistic
(Asperger’s) parent:
•
Parent hierarchy rules fly out the window. My father often
brings up the fact that his children’s social and emotional
development surpassed his own a long time ago. If you think
that parenting a child with Autism is hard, imagine parenting
your parent with Autism.
•
Logic beats emotion. As a kid, appealing with emotion didn’t
get me anywhere!
•
Adults on the Spectrum can become more rigid over time.
We know from our work with the young adults in the CIP
program that the earlier we can work with each individual, the
better. The transition from high school to college is major, but it
just multiplies from that point on. Instilling cognitive flexibility and
promoting openness from an early age is critical and I work hard
on this factor with my father and so does he.

---
## Page 1025
Forthright and Rule-Bound Plus … Hyper-
Vigilance
Many mothers, fathers, sisters, and brothers of someone on the Autism
Spectrum also play a dual role as teacher, mentor, counselor, and
activity director. We’re often bumping up against this “fuzzy gray area”
as we attempt to balance our work-day lives with our personal
relationships, especially if we are working in the ASD or LD field and
dealing with family members on the Spectrum.
I know from working in this field that within special education there is a
higher level of inter-family involvement, bringing with it a higher level of
emotion and connectedness. In fact, in education in general, the
millennial generation seemed to trigger more intense emotional
involvement in the education of one’s child; often referred to (as
referenced above) as “helicopter parenting.”
Considering that the current research indicates that strong relationships
are very effective and that it is not only extremely rewarding to be
involved in the accomplishment of milestones with your loved ones, but
in reality, a necessity – during certain years – we embrace this
involvement heartily. I, as the son of an autistic parent, experience this
same parent/child dynamic, except that it is flipped.
My Story
Growing up with a father on the Autism Spectrum, I always knew our
family was different, but I didn’t have the knowledge or experiences as
a kid to fully understand those differences. My parents did not have
many social connections outside of our family and their immediate
families.
I would notice that at school events, sporting events, or other social
activities, many other parents seemed to be closer with each other. My
mother could navigate those waters a bit better, but there was still –

---
## Page 1026
understandably – some co-dependence (and I would assume some
embarrassment) factoring in.
I fully understood that there was a community that existed outside of our
familial world. My father recognized this as well, and when he
attempted to infiltrate that social world, it often felt a bit uncomfortable.
I would be the one to notice some of the subtle disinterest, facial cues,
and not so subtle social cues from others. I let myself take on the
shame and embarrassment for the both of us since my father did not
seem to be phased by it.
When you are a child, you tend to specifically remember certain life
events that now would seem very minor but that for some reason … and
at the time, had a large part on your development. I went through my
childhood experiencing my father’s strengths and challenges first-hand.
One particular incident that was repeated almost every Sunday
morning was his hyper-vigilance about being on time to church. At 8:45
a.m. he would be in the car waiting, while the rest of us were still inside
getting ready. At 8:50 a.m. he would beep the horn, 8:55 a.m., beep the
horn a lot more … becoming more irate. By the time all five children and
our mother were ready and seated, he was silent; already having
imploded. As a small child, I could clearly see the disparity in this; we
were our way to church to practice patience and loving care for others
but the process of getting there was quite the opposite.
On the strengths side, a highly regimented schedule, appreciation for
the arts, wildly inappropriate fun – such as massive water fights
throughout the entire house – were memorable experiences as I was
growing up. My father was always a teacher at heart and the time we
spent together was very active and rewarding.
People did not have an understanding of Autism/Asperger’s when I was
growing up, nor did they attempt to hide their actions or under-the-
breath remarks from the little kid named Dan, who was standing by,
intensely interested in these interactions like it was a real-life social
studies class.

---
## Page 1027
When I was young I was more shy and introspective than a more
typical child. I rarely spoke in formal settings like school. I had not
developed much self-confidence, although I knew inside that people
recognized me for my strengths (an athlete, good at drawing, and
working with computers).
It took a long time to build my sense of self and a huge part of that
happened when my work began at CIP. As I worked directly with
students on the ASD and LD Spectrum, I began to recognize my own
talents and the traits, scenarios, and familiarity of what I had lived with
as a child. I was good at this job (I had trained for it all my life), and
good at working with our students. Having others recognize my talents
at the same time created a positive cycle that helped me build and gain
further self-esteem.
Helping Yourself, Helping Parents, and
Helping “Generation Autism”
Educators, clinicians, and support professionals working in the field will
need to be prepared to deal with students and in some cases, with
parents (sometimes “helicopter”), who are on the Autism Spectrum.
Realizing, as I mentioned above, that hierarchy does not exist and that
adults become more rigid over time … will be challenging.
My father, Michael McManmon, put four words together a long time
ago, sort of a mission statement for CIP, and a personal credo for him
to achieve. They are: Tolerance, Diversity, Respect, and Love. I believe
he is right and that these words are the key to handling Generation
Autism.
Creating Boundaries with “Generation
Autism”
Establish strong relationships

---
