## Page 1028
In all interactions with students and families, clarify your boundaries up
front by making clear your availability and/or your role or your primary
purpose for each interaction with students and parents. It’s good to
remember (always) that you cannot be all things to everyone, and
stepping outside your area of expertise (or job description) can be
dangerous and unproductive.
Mentors … not friends
At CIP, mentors provide guidance and advice. We have found over the
years that keeping the role of mentor firmly in place is hard, as students
and mentors drift naturally toward friendship. This is sometimes a
difficult boundary to keep but it is an important one to mention here.
Family and friends can offer emotional support and shared interests;
mentors can offer guidance and advice. If friends and family are not
accessible, a mentor, a therapist, or a trusted advisor is a good option.
Keep in mind that as a mentor, it is fine to relate your personal
experiences to help your students gain perspective. My advice would
be to ask yourself what will have the best long-term benefits for the
student? In nine out of ten answers to this question, you will usually find
the right path.
Establish milestones
Work with each individual student to break down their larger goals into
a small number of clear and concrete milestones that fall within your
domain of expertise. Share these with everyone who is involved in the
student’s interventions or education. Milestones are more flexible than
goals, and once reached, give the student and teacher a new vantage
point in which educated decisions can be made.
Let the student fail
Failing is one of the best learning experiences there is and a student
with a safety net of support and trusted mentors will bounce back
stronger. Students with Learning Differences have often experienced
many more failures than neurotypical people their age. When guiding a
student toward a goal or milestone, start very small with one or two

---
## Page 1029
achievable goals to build up self-confidence and help the student invest
in the process. Pre-teach and consider the types of obstacles that may
be faced in order to build a stronger prefrontal cortex and work toward
self-determination.
The assets of living with an autistic father
I am grateful for the fact that I had the opportunity to polish my own gift
for working with young adults with ASD and LD (and thus advance my
career), and that in doing so, my parents (especially my Aspie dad),
continually supported me while I pursued my own interests. Even in the
absence of some more typical childhood experiences, I was taught the
value of lists and routines, asking questions, a strong work ethic, and
doing things for the right reasons.
Another huge asset for me was that my father enlisted himself (and by
proxy, his children) in more self-help strategies than many know are
possible, such as Catholicism (for a period of time), daily affirmation
books, 12-step programs, being a group home parent, and eventually …
a licensed psychologist. Over his 40 plus years as a special educator
and father, he has given back so much and to so many people. These
are certainly the ideals and professions that attract those with their own
intense interest in the exploration of the mind and self that influenced
me and my work.
Some final thoughts
If you’re in this category (having a parent, spouse, child, or loved one
on the Autism Spectrum), and your life’s work is in this field you have
several options. I’ve come up with a short list of tips and strategies to
help you:
•
Leave 
your 
profession 
(educator, 
clinician, 
or 
support
professional) at the door. Your intentions to learn more to assist
your family member are fantastic, but consider what your family
member needs most. Usually this is the brother, sister, father,
mother option. I’ve learned from my own four sisters that – try as
I might – they don’t want my advice. But over time, by focusing
on my own growth, they’ve come to respect and admire certain

---
## Page 1030
traits that I possess just by me being a better version of myself.
The changing of relationships tends to have this slow, round-
about approach.
•
Be the best family member you can be first then, offer your
support as a professional in limited amounts and in the
background. Define what it means for you to be a good family
member and realistically analyze your own intentions. What are
your fears or concerns? How do they affect your relationships
personally? Professionally? Where do your work life and
personal life intersect and what are the common themes that
emerge? Be open with each other and yourself and you will be
surprised.
BIOGRAPHY: Dan is President of the College Internship Program, a
national post-secondary program helping young adults on the Autism
Spectrum and with Learning Differences find success in college,
employment, and independent living. Dan began working at CIP as a
residential instructor and National Marketing Coordinator in 2005 and
aslo teaching independent living skills at the CIP Berkshire Center in
Lee, Massachusetts. In 2007, he assisted with the opening of the CIP
Berkeley Center in California. Three years later Dan and his family
relocated back to Western Massachusetts where Dan served as CIP’s
Director of Admissions and Marketing. Dan’s father, Michael

---
## Page 1031
McManmon, founded the College Internship Program in 1984 and was
diagnosed with Asperger’s syndrome in his late fifties. The experience
and relationship growing up with his father have had a profound effect
on Dan’s understanding and compassion for those with an ASD and
Learning Differences diagnosis.
WHEN WE ARE OVERWHELMED
by Michael John Carley
A long, long time ago – before my diagnosis, my son’s diagnosis, and
whatever work I’ve done over the last 14 years in the Autism/Asperger
field – I had a different career …
As a Non-Government Organization Representative (“NGO Rep,” a
very, very, low-level diplomat) through the United Nations, I got to travel
to some out-of-the-way places. I did some work in Cuba, some in
Bosnia, but I spent more of my time than anywhere in the Middle East
as the Project Director for an endeavor that repaired water treatment
facilities in Iraq. Because of the economic sanctions back then, the
chemicals and coagulants required to purify water could not be
imported; and, according to a 1998 UNICEF report, over 5000 Iraqi
children were dying every month as a direct result of sanctions – mostly
due to waterborne illnesses. Eventually, by late 2001, The Iraq Water
Project would provide clean water to 81,000 people through four
restored water plants in the hardest-hit Basra region. If I were to “rate”
my accomplishments, I think it is this feat that has the most measurable
value of anything I’ll ever do, even if I achieved greater notoriety in the
Autism/Asperger field.
But before The Iraq Water Project’s success, as before any endeavor,
there existed only uncertainty, doubt, and hope that we could pull it off.
It was early 2000, and not only was it in question that I’d be able to
secure an agreement with Saddam Hussein’s angry government; there
was also no certainty that I would keep my emotions together in the
process. Though no fan whatsoever of United States policy towards

---
## Page 1032
Iraq, I also (no surprise) despised their regime. Yet it was I who was
tasked to choose the facilities to be repaired, to negotiate, and to get
permission from them without the ability (strategic or moral) to utter one
kind word about their government either in the contractual language, or
in face-to-face meetings. We discussed the unnecessary suffering, and
only the unnecessary suffering, and as a result the permissions were
obtained. But it was before this ambiguous period that I and my
colleagues paid the first of four visits to special children’s wards at Iraqi
hospitals. We were to visit the victims.
Knowing ahead of time that the visit would be rough didn’t help much,
and our anxiety levels were high. Apprehensively, we entered a room
filled with disappearing, tiny life systems too weak to cry much;
dispersed in a cube roughly 30 by 40 feet in a health care facility that
rarely enjoyed electricity. No matter, though, as a bright desert sun lit
up the room through the window, and electrical equipment herein
served no one. Overwhelmed staff tried in vain to keep things clean,
working around the bedside mothers, who looked handcuffed to the
metal-frames of glorified cots. But the day-to-day trauma and difficulties
outside that room made the lack of cleaning materials a smaller
tragedy. It was no secret to them, or anyone else, that no child would
survive their stay. And were a bed to empty, it would be filled almost
immediately by the same human dilemma.
But I was to discover a surprising solace – useless to them but not to
me – as I spent time with each child. In focusing on the individual, the
political angers disappeared. The big picture frustrations were forgotten
within the revelatory context that in that moment, they were not within
my control. To my relief, right in front of me, was something that I could
control, even if it was a human speck as small as a moment of
reassurance (one that I might still be fooling myself in remembering as
important). Fellow delegates followed suit. We did not renounce the
politics; we in fact suffused our belief systems by shelving them
temporarily, by shutting them out so we could stop remembering that
which we already knew, clear the brain, and therein learn more. Those
kids did not share the grownups’ offended sense of justice – that was
our baggage – and most of these children were well past the point of

---
## Page 1033
fear. They just wanted to be held, and told that everything would be all
right, until it was over.
When our delegation departed, overwhelmed colleagues tearfully
vented about the indisputable global injustices that had influenced this
day. They were not articulate, did not make good points, and they
raged against the heavens. Yet the human need to speak in this clumsy
manner epitomized necessity itself.
But when they next approached a microphone, back in the States,
testifying about what they saw or did, they spoke differently, sensing the
responsibility they had in a very different kind of moment. People
needed them to clarify and enlighten, they sensed this, and they rose to
the opportunity to both be, and do good. To spew would have had a
counter-effect. So instead, they led.
Imagine my shift then, as I switched careers into a culture where having
a child who couldn’t talk was often treated as the worst injustice
possible, where in the anti-vaccination camp having measles was
preferable to having Autism, and where supposed leaders spat out
alarmist rhetoric upon their overwhelmed constituencies – consciously
and strategically deciding not to guide them to some place where they
could constructively process the forces that made them feel
overwhelmed … As one prominent leader in the Spectrum world
confessed to me, “This is war.”
*
Oddly enough, there are very humanist origins for all these comic/tragic
staples of “life on the Spectrum” and its politics. The English-speaking
world, for one, still reigns as the most financially solvent on earth. And
as a result, our standards for income, life expectancy, sex lives, “my
rights” this and “my rights” that etc. are higher than elsewhere in the
world. When surprised by a child who might not be able to take
advantage of our surrounding materialistic wealth, we feel a higher
degree of an injustice done to us than might exist elsewhere.
Yet when I first started listening to these parents, to their commitment
to tragic interpretation – and noticing my inner disgust – I quickly

---
## Page 1034
surmised that they were responding in the same manner as I: Both
sides were simply reacting to the conditioning that our separate
experiences had taught us.
Furthermore, it’s theoretically logical that in the richest nation on earth
(the United States) there should be the funds to provide the services
that these families and grownups need. Instead, the chasm between
need and delivery is catastrophically wide. It is simply a different face of
injustice.
The next aspect I had to digest was how hard Autism’s scope is on our
systems. Unlike other disabilities it casts a wide net for those who
qualify. Swallowing that this diagnosis can reflect Albert Einstein, Alan
Turing, Thomas Jefferson, Emily Dickinson, and Beethoven, etc., and
yet also an adult who can’t use spoken language or clean themselves
independently? We still struggle with this idea. Such unheard of
disparity offends our very human need to compartmentalize, yes, but
the mistake herein is that it is not Autism that has to change, or our
definition of it; it is that human need that must. Not all of our instincts,
despite their humanity, are worth keeping.
As a result of Autism’s complexity, the Spectrum world is not the world
of Down Syndrome or Cystic Fibrosis. In the Autism/Asperger world, we
do not have one, unifying non-profit that everyone is happy to raise
money for, and rallies around in a consensus-filled atmosphere of
shared goals. Instead, we have thousands of organizations – often
holding polar opposite viewpoints from one another. In the
Autism/Asperger world, we do not seek unity, or an acceptance of the
other side of the Spectrum’s feelings. We fight over language (ex:
“cure”), which behavioral or educational strategies work best, aversives
(i.e., what are we allowed to do to restrain a nonverbal, 400-pound
individual in some Kafkaesque facility). We fight over research dollars,
the DSM revisions …
And despite the mountains of evidence, we fight over the very origins of
Autism – do we get this from genetics (primarily, yes), vaccines, used
iPod batteries, or staring too close to the TV? Well, as insane as we
now know it to be, the anti-vaccine movement too, has origins that lie in

---
## Page 1035
very humanist explanations (if not justifications). It could easily be
considered a very negative offshoot of a larger, very positive movement
– the environmental movement. Additionally, all those parents reporting
changes in their children right after “the combo shot” at the time of the
millennial … they weren’t lying.
But the ill-informed pediatricians and autism organizations of the time
were failing to explain that (a) that same age in which that shot is
delivered coincides with the age in which we developmentally start to
show our Autism, and (b) that nobody doubts that the vaccines might
have exacerbated already-existing autism (much like a sensory
reaction would). And their efforts have been assisted by a variety of
factors, such as the revolting marketing tactics of the pharmaceutical
industry whom they still need to blame, as well the pandering nature of
the Centers for Disease Control (CDC), who for so long refused to
figuratively hit back at the anti-vaccine crowd, despite having more than
enough evidence that vaccines do not cause Autism. For this, I think
the CDC has to equally share culpability, alongside the anti-vaccination
crowd, for the recent measles outbreak.
In a comic/tragic way, their inability to let go of an idea long since
disproved (often a trait of autism) is proof of the genetic nature. They do
not see the humor in an ex-Playboy bunny (Jenny McCarthy) leading
their movement, which they say is rooted in science. And they do not
see that one of the criteria that often accompany a diagnosis is the
inability to emotionally regulate, or to trust. They are a volatile crowd.
Not all of them may condone those in their camp who issue death
threats, but I’ve certainly never heard one of them say “That person
issuing death threats does not speak for me.”
Some can embrace the diagnosis and others can’t, but Autism’s
complexity still baffles all sides. Of course this is sad, as there are
beatific teaching moments that lie as possibilities in embracing such a
continuum; opportunities that would make us all better people as we
attempt to control less of that which we cannot control. But we
collectively reject such openings for growth. The Spectrum is simply
bigger than most want it to be. So we fight.

---
## Page 1036
Is the answer not to fight, to not get involved? To just stick to getting the
services we, or our children need? I’d answer no. Despite my
diplomatic history and all that I’ve thus far said, I believe in
confrontation 100%, and even privately believe that those who are
afraid of confrontation actually do engage – in a very negative and
subtle way. But in how we confront lies why the Autism/Asperger world
is such an emotionally dysfunctional universe, and this starts with our
so-called leadership.
For some reason, those in charge of the larger organizations disgorge
the alarmist rhetoric – not to mention misinformation – that theoretically
should be coming from only their most overwhelmed members.
Furthermore, these Presidents or Executive Directors intentionally
provide figurative or literal microphones into the hands of their angriest
constituents, not their healthiest. And though these average families will
pull at our emotional heartstrings for legitimate reasons as they spill
their stories of woe; when people are overwhelmed they simply say
stupid and angry things.
In another comic/tragic twist of anthropological study, we fail to see that
while both extreme ends of autism politics have polar opposite views,
when we analyze their behaviors they are revealed to be unbelievably
similar. Both use the war talk, both feel it necessary to find an entity to
blame (the diagnosis, “big Pharm,” or each other), and both
occasionally put forth astonishing misinformation. The pro-cure crowd
puts forth unmercifully demonizing videos (“I Am Autism”), fictitious
“facts” (that parents with Autism have an 80% divorce rate), or that
none of the three million of us Spectrum folks in the States can use the
toilet. And on the other end, there lie the militant spectrumites; my
brothers and sisters in shared goals; who in feats of wishful thinking go
after fellow spectrumites (especially yours truly) not for disagreeing on
the world we seek, but in pushing a “light skin vs. dark skin” agenda
over disagreements on how we’ll get there. They think you can’t be
fired for being an asshole on the job, and use words like “murderer,”
and “genocide,” as they attack those on the other side of the ideological
fence …

---
## Page 1037
And in what might be the most comic/tragic similarity of them all, both
sides invalidate the seriousness of the others’ experiences, and yet
have the audacity to use the word “community,” as they engage in a
blatant competition of suffering.
Diplomacy worked for me for a very long time with GRASP, and the
hate mail I got from both extremist ends of all the debates I engaged in
had me in an ideal position. The hurt (i.e., “the crazies”) were a loud
minority – especially on the Internet – but they were still a minority.
But then the day came when diplomacy wasn’t working anymore; when
our press (and subsequent funding) started shifting away to those
organizations who engaged in the rhetoric. It took me two years (too
many) to figure it out, but what I had to digest was that the extremists,
and not I, had accurately calculated that “the masses” now wanted
them to behave in this way. The press and funders merely obliged.
The constituents of these leaders are often overwhelmed, and they look
up to their leaders for guidance. They need their leaders to help them
get out of the awful mindset they’re in, and all it would take for these
leaders to shift away from the blame game is to face one overwhelmed
person that they have the ability to help. Human contact can do that for
you. And the fact that their response has been to pour gasoline over the
fires that consume those who trust them, rather than assisting them to
find emotionally healthy or constructive roads leads me to believe they
don’t interact with their members in meaningful ways very much.
Though they have not beaten each other; together, both extremist sides
have won in a way. But I still feel that they can kick diplomacy’s ass,
and yet also honor the responsibilities that have been legitimately
placed on them.
For myself, I don’t have to be as much of a diplomat as I used to be. It’s
kind of frustrating (I hate losing) but it’s also very liberating. And so I
would conclude with calls to both sides that are based only on my
personal experience …
To the spectrumites who are overwhelmed, your unmet needs are
legitimate. But listen to the stories of potential, to the strategies towards

---
## Page 1038
a better life, and hope. Self-pity and victimization, as encouraged by
supposed leaders, will not improve your life – even when you truly are
being victimized. For when you self-pity, you do not love yourself. It’s
impossible. And nobody in their right mind wants to date or hire
someone who doesn’t believe in themselves. Be a pest to the world
only in securing the best life possible for yourself, for herein others have
a duty to get out of your way. You are alive, so love yourself, if only
because you really have no other choice.
To the parents who are overwhelmed, your unmet needs are legitimate.
But your child is alive. Please shut up, and love it – as is. The old “I love
my child but hate his Autism” script has long been shown to be
nonsense. Those who uttered these words actually didn’t love their
children. They just wished they did. You too, really have no other
choice.
No matter how inadequate my impact on them, having the smarts to
love those Iraqi kids in the moment as if they were my own may be the
most valuable epiphany I’ll ever experience. We may even have
succeeded in our mission because of it. Fearful of being traumatized by
such repeated experiences over the years, I instead emerged with a
commitment to gratitude itself. Oh, and love too. My family, and not the
Autism/Asperger world, is the chief beneficiary.
Diplomacy may be dead for a while, but other elements are alive. Ok …
Empty platitudes about “love” may have desecrated our belief in it, our
understanding of what it is, or how its power allows us to move
figurative mountains. But let’s still ask our leaders to love, instead of
use their members. The Spectrum world is full of humans in real crisis,
especially on an emotional level. Despite whatever comic/tragic
hysterics have transpired people still need those leaders. The
opportunity for them to change still exists, and always will, so long as
there are people in need.

---
## Page 1039
Iraqi child. Basra Pediatric Hospital 2001. Photo © Michael John
Carley.
BIOGRAPHY: Michael John Carley is the Founder of GRASP, a
School Consultant in Wisconsin, and the author of “Asperger’s From
the Inside-Out” (Penguin/Perigee, 2008), “The Last Memoir of
Asperger Syndrome” (unpublished), and numerous articles. He is also
at work on “Unemployed on the Autism Spectrum,” and “‘Why Am I
Afraid of Sex?’ Building Sexual Confidence in the Autism Spectrum …

---
## Page 1040
and Beyond!” (both JKP, due 2016). In 2000, he and one of his two
sons were diagnosed with Asperger’s Syndrome. Re-evaluated in
2014, he was diagnosed with ASD. More information about John can
be found at www.michaeljohncarley.com.
MAKING OCCUPATIONAL CHOICES:
INTERESTS ARE NOT ENOUGH
by Barbara Bissonnette
Many individuals with Asperger’s Syndrome have at least one “special
interest” that is pursued with unusual intensity. The individual develops
encyclopedic knowledge of whatever topic has captured his or her
interest.
Luke was passionate about World War II weaponry. He was not at all
versed in the politics of the war, the gross magnitude of human
suffering, or how it shaped history. However, he could rattle off all sorts
of facts about Thompson submachine guns, Winchester shotguns, and
various bazookas, flame throwers, hand grenades, and bayonets.
Special interests can lead to fulfilling careers for individuals on the
autism spectrum. Temple Grandin is a well known example. A
childhood visit to her aunt’s Arizona ranch sparked an interest in cattle
and cattle chutes. She went on to earn a doctorate in animal science
and developed a very successful career designing humane livestock
facilities. Almost half of the cattle in North America are handled in
systems she designed.
However, it is a mistake to assume that interests, special or otherwise,
will always lead to gainful employment.
Several years ago, I participated in training for vocational rehabilitation
specialists. One of the counselors was working with a client whose goal
was to be an airline pilot. It was the only career that this man envisioned
for himself. Since this client had no aviation background, the vocational

---
## Page 1041
counselor questioned him to learn more about his interest. The client
wanted to be an airline pilot so that he could wear a uniform!
I often repeat this story because it is an excellent example of how
restricted interests in Autism can negatively impact a job search. This
man focused on a single – and in this case, irrelevant – detail about
commercial aviation. He had no concept of what it means to be an
airline pilot, or understanding that it was a completely unrealistic career
choice.
(This story does have a happy ending. The counselor found his client a
job as a doorman at an upscale hotel. The man was completely
satisfied because each day he wore a uniform.)
Sometimes I work with coaching clients who have limited interests, too
many interests, or interests that are unrealistic given their abilities,
education, or geographic region. One man wanted to be a computer
technician, but only to fix certain models of computers. A woman with
aspirations in the entertainment field didn’t want to move from her small
town in the Midwest. A young sports enthusiast set his sights on a
broadcasting career, despite having a serious speech impediment.
It is critical to probe interests when assisting individuals on the Autism
spectrum. I say to clients, “It’s great that you want to be [insert
occupation]. What do you see yourself doing once you are working in
the field?”
The answer I receive most often is: “I don’t know; I haven’t thought
about it.”
This is a troubling response because it sets an individual up for a lot of
potential frustration. It is true that many people do not work in jobs
related to their post-secondary schooling. However, Aspergians are
less adaptable than neurotypicals. They begin with a smaller pool of
suitable jobs to choose from.
Alec is typical of many history majors I’ve coached. After discovering
how very few job opportunities there were at museums, he decided to
pursue archivism. He pictured himself spending his days absorbed in
examining and categorizing historic photographs and documents.

---
## Page 1042
A bit of research revealed that archivists spend a lot of their time
creating and maintaining electronic databases. Alec did not have strong
computer or database skills, and was not particularly interested in
learning them.
There are also practical concerns, such as whether the individual can
complete the required education, and compete for jobs in more
competitive fields. Steven earned a Bachelor’s degree in political
science with the goal of finding a job in public policy. He figured that his
knowledge of government, interest in research, and 3.8 GPA would
make it easy to find an entry-level position.
Instead, Steven found himself in a field that attracted many applicants
and offered a small number of jobs. Employers expected job applicants
to have internships or related volunteer experience. Steven had neither.
During the school year, all his energy was devoted to keeping up with
class work. He had not been able to find any internships during summer
breaks. Most of the job openings were in the Washington, DC area, and
Steven did not want to move.
Steven continued to visit job boards, send out resumes, and hope for
interviews. But now, 11 months after graduation, he wasn’t thinking
about shaping public policy. His priority, and that of his parents, was
any job that would provide steady income.
When I am working with a client to determine manageable occupations,
we look at four areas: Interests; talents and skills; right work
environment; and how Asperger’s Syndrome impacts the particular
individual. I consider each of these to be important pieces of a puzzle.
All of them must fit together in order to identify occupations with the
greatest likelihood of success.
An interest inventory is a helpful tool for individuals who are confused
about their direction, too limited in their thinking, or who have too many
interests. These assessments ask a person to rate his or her interest in
various subjects or activities. Based on his or her responses, a list of
possible jobs is generated. An interest inventory does not measure

---
## Page 1043
whether a person has the aptitude or skill to succeed in an occupation.
It suggests vocational possibilities based on interests.
Interest inventories are widely available. Some can only be
administered by a qualified career counselor. Others are available to
individuals via the Internet at low or no cost. The quality of interest
inventories varies. I suggest using assessments that have been
scientifically validated to measure what they claim to.
There are caveats when administering an interest inventory to persons
with Asperger’s Syndrome.
They have trouble imagining what they haven’t actually experienced.
An individual may not know how to rate items that are novel or too
abstract. One young man asked, “What exactly does it mean to ‘care
for others?’” Differentiating “strongly liking” an activity from “liking” or
“somewhat liking” can also be challenging.
Such confusion can skew results. For a while, I used an interest
inventory that asked individuals to rate over 150 items. Some clients
evaluated nearly every item as “neutral” (neither liking nor disliking the
subject or activity), or as something they disliked. One or two items
were given very high interest ratings. The assessment indicated strong
interest in a certain career cluster based on only one or two responses.
I have experienced similar problems with assessments that ask
individuals to indicate whether they would enjoy performing a task,
regardless of whether they know how to do it. I knew I was in trouble
when one young person asked, “What is a blueprint?” A package of
those assessments is collecting dust in one of my filing cabinets. The
questions are too abstract for my clients.
The terms talents and skills are often used interchangeably; however, I
make the distinction with my clients. I want them to understand the
difference between their natural abilities, and proficiencies that they can
learn and develop over time. For example, an Aspergian may dismiss
an occupation because he or she does not currently possess the
needed skills or experience. Additionally, job search skills such as
resume writing, networking, and interviewing can seem overwhelming.

---
## Page 1044
When I explain that these are skills that everyone has to learn and
practice, the process becomes less intimidating.
I give clients a list of common talents and skills and ask them to
indicate which they possess. Not everyone is able to complete this
independently.
When an individual’s abilities are not clear, I will suggest an aptitude
assessment. Typically, an individual takes a series of timed tests that
measure various abilities such as verbal reasoning, mechanical or
numeric ability, or spatial relations. Based on the results, occupational
areas where there is the greatest likelihood of success are suggested.
These assessments cannot predict the right job, but they can provide
guidance. It is probable, for instance, that someone with poor numeric
ability will not do well in accounting.
Brian enthusiastically described his love of numbers. He had many
challenges, and worked extremely hard for four years to earn an
Associate’s degree. According to an aptitude assessment, his numeric
ability was low. He could not recall which mathematics classes he took
in college. He was puzzled when I asked whether he enjoyed statistics.
Finally, I asked him to add six plus five. He had to write down the
equation to solve it.
Brian did indeed love numbers. He loved to look at them, draw them,
and find interesting patterns in them. “In a few weeks,” he told me
excitedly, “it’s going to be 10–11–12!” He was referring to the date:
October 11, 2012.
In contrast, Eric showed a very strong aptitude in spatial relationships.
When I explained what that meant his eyes lit up. “I’ve always enjoyed
taking things apart, seeing how they work, and putting them back
together!”
This was surprising since Eric was researching administrative jobs.
When I asked him about the discrepancy he said, “I didn’t know what
kind of job I could do that involves taking things apart, but I know that I
can sit behind a computer.”

---
## Page 1045
Although it is not a career tool, an up-to-date neuropsychological
evaluation can provide valuable information related to occupational
choices. Clinical neuropsychologists conduct interviews and various
tests to understand an individual’s brain functioning. They evaluate
cognitive ability in areas such as attention, memory, language, visual-
spatial ability, and Executive Functioning. The evaluation is used to
diagnose a variety of disorders, including autism.
Clinicians summarize test results and their observations in patient
reports that are written in lay person’s language. Evaluations of adults
include vocational recommendations.
This information is helpful for understanding how a person’s strengths
and weaknesses could impact employment. Someone with poor
working memory, for example, will have difficulty in jobs that require
multitasking.
Sarah was a client who had been diagnosed with NLD. Despite having
a college degree, she had been fired from six jobs. The problem was
usually that she couldn’t learn jobs quickly enough. She had many
challenges, including marked impairment in visual discrimination and
visual memory.
To earn some money, she took a job handing out food samples in a
grocery store. This would seem to be an easy job for a college
graduate. However, it took Sarah an inordinate amount of time to set up
her display table each morning. One reason was that she couldn’t
easily locate products on the store shelves. She had to painstakingly
scan rows of items three or four times to find the product she needed to
hand out that day. It was a slow and painful process of matching a
product name and logo to a photograph on her instruction sheet. She
also had problems figuring out how to set up and use the toasters,
blenders, and warmers that were used to prepare the samples.
Each morning, Sarah wondered anxiously whether her supervisor
would check in during her set up, and realize how long it took. By
understanding Sarah’s cognitive strengths and challenges, we were

---
## Page 1046
able to explore better options and, in a few months, she found a less
stressful job.
Neuropsychological 
testing 
does 
have 
limitations. 
Tests 
are
administered in a controlled setting, free of the distractions of the
workplace. Clients sometimes tell me that the pressure of reacting in
“real time” at work causes them to have trouble performing tasks that
according to their evaluation should be “easy.”
I find that the work environment can be as important, or even more
important, than job tasks for individuals on the autism spectrum.
Factors to consider include aspects of the physical surroundings, the
demand for interpersonal interaction, and productivity requirements.
Problems with the physical work space are largely sensory-based. An
individual might find certain occupations to be off limits because of
sensitivity to:
•
Odors: printing plant, restaurant, cosmetics department.
•
Noises: construction site, cubicle, and factory.
•
Visual distractions: fluorescent lighting, flashing lights.
•
Tactile sensations: tight-fitting uniforms, hats, formal attire (e.g.,
neck tie).
He or she might become quite anxious when surrounded by people,
such as at a call center or event stadium. Noisy environments or those
that require working in groups might best be avoided, if a person has
trouble processing auditory information.
The performance of certain tasks might be impacted by poor hand-eye
coordination or fine motor control. Examples are assembly work or
stuffing envelopes at a mailing facility.
Requirements for interpersonal communication can be difficult to
evaluate, yet they factor heavily into employment success. It is useful to
think in terms of how sophisticated a level of interaction is required.
Some individuals manage quite well when interaction is limited to
persons within their department or work group. Jobs that are tightly

---
## Page 1047
scripted, like some telemarketing positions, are also possibilities.
Others can handle more complex exchanges, such as those involving
customers.
Aspergians tend to be slow, methodical workers, but this is not always
the case. Jim fixes recycled cell phones. He works so quickly that his
employer has trouble keeping him busy. However, it is not uncommon
for individuals to have trouble meeting productivity standards.
The challenge is to know the abilities of the individual, and match them
to the pace of job; amount of structure; level of independence required;
complexity of decision-making and problem solving; and the amount of
multitasking.
Finally, I believe that the more an individual understands about how
Asperger’s Syndrome impacts him or her, the easier it will be to find a
manageable job.
When discussing personal challenges I stress that every human being
has limitations of one kind or another. A client should not interpret this
exercise to be a review of deficiencies. I state clearly that the
information will be used to identify occupations that emphasize the
person’s strengths, and minimize his or her areas of weakness.
I place limitations into two categories. The first category is limitations
that can be mitigated by learning new skills, utilizing assistive
technology, or with job accommodations. The second category is
limitations that an individual cannot do much about. Knowledge of these
enables a person to avoid occupations that would be frustrating or
impossible to manage.
Adapted from Helping Adults with Asperger’s Syndrome Get & Stay
Hired, Career Coaching Strategies for Professionals and Parents of
Adults on the Autism Spectrum, © 2015, Barbara Bissonnette; Jessica
Kingsley Publishers.

---
## Page 1048
BIOGRAPHY: Barbara Bissonnette is a certified coach and the
principal of Forward Motion Coaching. She specializes in career
development coaching and workplace advocacy for individuals with
Asperger’s Syndrome (and similar autism spectrum profiles) and
Nonverbal Learning Disorder. She also offers consultation and training
for organizations on how to utilize the skills of employees with
Asperger’s Syndrome and other social, communication, and executive
function challenges.
Barbara is the author of “The Complete Guide to Getting a Job for
People with Asperger’s Syndrome”; “The Asperger’s Syndrome
Workplace Survival Guide: A Neurotypical’s Secrets for Success”, and
“Getting & Staying Hired with Asperger’s Syndrome (Autism Spectrum
Disorder): Coaching Strategies for Professionals and Parents”.
LIVING SAFELY ON THE SPECTRUM
by Liane Holliday Willey, EdD
Stay Safe. Where did your thoughts go when you read those simple
two words? Did you picture a pocket filled with pepper spray or a bunch
of firewalls protecting your computer passwords? Did you think back on
a tragedy that befell you? Did you see a short montage of high-risk

---
## Page 1049
safety guards from lifeboats to a parachute? Did you get the chills
merely thinking about what might hide in the half lit hallways of life?
Whatever your thoughts or picture there is no right or wrong to this
question, no distinct way to succinctly grasp the myriad perplexities of
safety. Its variables are as fluid as a slope of water over slippery rocks.
It took me decades to figure out safety is not one-dimensional. No
surprise, as I am a person on the Spectrum who spent many years
without benefit of a strongly developed central coherence, in other
words, little ability to see the big picture behind a multifaceted concept
like “safety.” I have suffered through sexual abuse, an emotionally
manipulative relationship, an eating disorder, theft, and a financially
exploitative business partner. I’ve found myself in harrowing
circumstances I happened upon quite by accident because I roamed
too far from my home. The hard road has taught me life is never
completely kind and only occasionally gentle. The long road has taught
me just how wide the concept of safety can reach. It took decades of
raising daughters, riding horses, traveling solo, and seeing my father
take his last breath after a simple fall left him with a brain bleed, before
I came to understand the scope and breath of safety.
I share what I know about staying safe. I have written loads of articles
and a book titled Safety Skills for Females with Asperger Syndrome. I
consult with people on the Spectrum and provide educational and
motivational presentations to our communities worldwide. But new
frights find me. I think they always will, because frankly, I’m not sure
there can be advice for staying safe in every single situation. But I am
sure a good offense is the best defense. Learning as much as we can
about what makes life so precarious for people on the Spectrum, then
combining that knowledge with good safety structures will go a very
long way toward keeping safety as secure as possible.
Why are people with an Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) typically at a
vast disadvantage when it comes to matters of safety? The academic
research behind that reality can get technical fast, but suffice it to say,
the ASD brain has a particular set of neuropathic nuances that leave us
vulnerable and ripe for the picking. For example, we tend to send our

---
## Page 1050
attention toward the shiny, moving, glowing, attractive, odd, nifty
objects in our consciousness, rather than the path holes, predators,
unsafe highway lanes, closed doors, and other such warning signs. In
other words, we can become too preoccupied and single minded in our
lookouts, forgetting about looking out for those things that would cause
us pain or suffering. The ASD brain also makes it difficult to correctly
conceive and predict nonverbal communication.
That’s a real problem because more than 80% of human
communication is nonverbal in nature. Imagine how easy it would be to
misread a person’s intentions. Let’s say someone smiles, for instance.
The first hypothesis would lead most people to think that was a friendly
signal, a good starting place for further conversation, or at least a sign
not to go into self-protective mode. Makes sense, but what if you don’t
know how to read more subtle signals? You’d probably stop at the
smile and not see or recognize the person’s nefarious signals. Nothing
good can come from those miscues.
The inability to gather good perspective taking skills in general, can
also lead people on the Spectrum to danger. A person could directly
say he was intoxicated by the idea of raping a woman and a person on
the Spectrum could absolutely ignore this horrible confession, thinking
it to be some kind of joke or an unimportant bit of information, because
the idea would never enter her mind. The confession passes by the
individual’s safety walls because it just does not make any sense or
because it has no place in the spectrumite’s reality.
Sometimes desperation to have a friend or to fit in can push safety
thoughts to the back door. If a person on the Spectrum has a strong
desire to have friends or be like others, she could convince herself she
is mistaken should any notion that something is amiss happen. This is
at least part of the reason why so many people with an ASD end up
spending time in disciplinary institutions. Despite a need to follow rules,
people in our ASD community are also keen to copy their peers, follow
the rules their peers created, and do favors for anyone who will just
include them in the club.

---
## Page 1051
Unsavory environments and situations can come on as if out of
nowhere, fast and quick. Think tornado, fire, assault, a hit and run. No
matter how much practice one puts in to place to prepare for these
situations, a fast processing speed is essential to deal well in an
emergency. People on the Spectrum tend to have a slower than optimal
processing speed. By the time someone with an ASD realizes they
need to move and move fast, it may be too late to find the best
protection or escape route.
Anxiety and other psychological struggles like depression or insecurity
can bubble up and overshadow sound safety reasoning and cause
erratic, suspicious, and vulnerable behaviors that send unwanted
messages to predators looking for easy prey. First responders report
assault happens more to people who look lost, scared, unconfident, or
distracted. That’s a scary fact for Spectrum people who are notorious
for acting in non-typical ways.
A good, solid, and direct list of support strategies is a must for anyone
on the Spectrum. In the best-case scenario, a few people will work with
the person with an ASD to design a personalized list that fits the
individual in need. When the list is made, everyone should go through it
and 
check 
for, 
and 
fix, 
possible 
misunderstandings 
or
misinterpretations. Then plenty of role playing should happen both to
explore the variables that may or may not occur during safety scenarios
and to be certain the strategies are easy enough to follow. Every list
should be unique, but I suspect there will be lots of commonalities.
Here’s a quick look at my personal basic safety must do list.
I carry a noisemaker that will signal distress.
I never look like a tourist or lost traveler. I learn the morays of the
community or area I am traveling to, and do everything I can to act as
inconspicuous as I can.
I try not to travel or go out to new places or areas, alone. If that is
impossible, I carry emergency contacts and an ID card that will let first
responders know how my ASD affects my behavior and thoughts.

---
## Page 1052
I learn and practice a relaxation technique that I can employ even if I
am in public. Deep breathing to a slow song I hum in my mind is a
favorite trick.
I learn how to follow the expected etiquette in high security areas like
airports, banks, public transportation areas, etc.
I carry and know how to use a GPS, as well as a paper map, of the
area I am exploring.
I never go anywhere without someone knowing where I am or without
leaving a note in case I do go missing.
I do not hesitate to leave any place if my instincts tell me to.
I find an exit to every place I am in and find out where the fire escape is,
the back doors, etc.
I know my limits. I may want to go to New Year’s Eve in Times Square,
but I’m not going unless I’m with people who can help me navigate the
potential dangers.
Remember, safety can be taught, but it may never be truly mastered.
Help the person on the Spectrum come up with solid back-up plans
and should the need arise … help them find the counseling or health
care that will bring them back to a safe state of mind and healthy self.

---
## Page 1053
BIOGRAPHY: Professor Liane Holliday Willey, who holds a doctorate
in psycholinguistics, was diagnosed with Asperger Syndrome when
she was 35 years old. Since her diagnosis Liane has focused her
academic research on females with Asperger Syndrome and
communication skills for people on the spectrum.
Liane is the author of the new book “Safety Skills for Asperger
Women: How to Save a Perfectly Good Female Life,” and the author
of “Pretending to be Normal: Living with Asperger’s Syndrome,”
“Asperger Syndrome in Adolescence: Living with the Ups, the Downs
and Things in Between,” and Asperger Syndrome in the Family:
Redefining Normal.”
Liane has been featured in USA Today, The Associated Press, The
New York Times, The LA Times, The Washington Post, Autism One
Radio, Oxygen TV, several NPR stations, and many other media
outlets.
Though Professor Willey loves helping others understand Asperger
Syndrome, her professional employment happens at Kirkshire Farm,
an equestrian facility she owns and operates.

---
## Page 1054
ANXIETY AND DEPRESSION IN STUDENTS
WITH LEARNING DIFFERENCES AND ASD
by Michael P. McManmon, Founder, CIP
Years of failure at social interaction can lead to strong feelings of
isolation and a lack of self-esteem. The majority of adolescents and
young adults with an Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) or Learning
Differences (LD) often develop anxiety and other difficulties. The
experts tell us that anxiety in this population is two times more prevalent
in women than in men. Prolonged states of anxiety can lead to a state
of depression. Family problems and complications are often a source of
stress for all involved and especially for a student with a Learning
Difference.
In my own case, I have spent a lifetime attempting to overcome some of
the emotional trauma I experienced in my family of origin. Being
diagnosed at age 54 with Asperger’s has helped me to understand and
to cope with these past events. I have experienced symptoms of
anxiety and depression and have had to use most of the prevention
strategies that will be outlined later in this essay.
Contributory factors
There are many factors that can increase anxiety and depression and
developing an understanding of them helps us to be able to implement
strategies for prevention. I have experienced empathic attunement –an
extreme sensitivity – to the suffering of others. When I view any
documentaries regarding the Holocaust or cruelty to animals I get upset
and overwhelmed.
Many students become intellectually overloaded from observing and
absorbing life’s absurdities and tragedies that they perceive and that
others fail to see. There seems to be no separation from what is
occurring in the news and many students seem to be taking on the pain
of the world without any filter or defense mechanism.

---
## Page 1055
An extreme instance of this conundrum is my brother Bob. Bob was a
social loner, but very convicted about politics and was so upset about
the election of George Bush senior that he committed suicide the day
after the election.
During my own divorce (more than 20 years ago), and before my
diagnosis, I was living in the basement apartment in our home and
playing the record by Sinead O’Connor with the words “nothing
compares to you” over and over again and getting more and more
depressed. I felt like I was sleeping in my coffin and had no clue what to
do about it. I was festering and getting worse and worse and pushing
everyone out of my life. Fear and depression took over. For some
reason I went to an Alcoholic Anonymous meeting (as I had alcoholism
in my family growing up), and people listened to me. I returned over and
over again to these meetings and things slowly got better. I also went to
therapy and things got even better. If left in that basement to fester,
instead of seeking help, I may have taken some extreme action as my
thoughts were often obsessing about this.
Having had a sister with ADD and mental illness, a brother who was a
psychiatrist who had Asperger’s Syndrome and another sister who had
High Functioning Autism (all three committed suicide), I saw what
doesn’t work. Each one of them was isolated due to circumstances or
choice and had no way to communicate what they were feeling. No
mentors or friends to reach out to. No therapy, no support groups.
I know – from the work I have done with myself and the advice I have
given to others over the years – that perfectionism in all its forms is a
root cause of anxiety and depression. This leads to procrastination and
an inability to finish projects that are started. Those (especially young
adults) on the ASD and LD Spectrum have unrealistic thought patterns
about the way life is supposed to be and how others live in the world.
These young men and women can feel that everyone knows how to be
with each other and that they are like an android floating in space,
unconnected to the planet.
When this occurs, an overwhelming sense of separation and
disconnection can develop. Often a student’s inability to self-regulate

---
## Page 1056
emotions and behavior can lead to a sense of failure in relationships
and employment. I have a sign over my desk that says “Always make
new mistakes.” This helps me to accept the fact that I am sometimes,
imperfect. Another saying I have is “All relationships are imperfect,” to
remind myself that there will always be disagreements even between
spouses and family members. The world is full of problems, but it is
also full of overcoming problems.
Loneliness – that terrible sense of isolation – can push an on the
Spectrum student inside him or herself. Feelings that he or she doesn’t
belong can well up inside a student and stay there for quite some time.
When I was in high school I would look out my bedroom window and
watch my best friend across the street get into a car and go off with his
new friends. I did not know how to call someone up and have a
conversation or ask someone to go somewhere. I never thought to ask
him if I could tag along. I just sat in my room getting more and more
depressed. I remember feeling suicidal and not knowing why, playing
Simon and Garfunkel’s song “I am a rock, I am an island” over and
over again and feeling depressed.
With a sense of intellectual superiority many students can believe that
they can think their way out of any situation on their own. This coupled
with the belief that asking for help is a sign of weakness can be
crippling. I was clueless in even knowing that I needed help.
As the external evidence of social incompetence accumulates,
adolescents and young adults with an ASD or LD diagnosis become
caught in “analysis paralysis.” This coupled with mental exhaustion can
contribute to sensory overload. They can become hyper-focused on the
negative. These young men and women often shut down and escape
into their computer or fantasy. Other contributing factors to these
sensory implosions can include a lack of a proper nutrition; sleep
deprivation; lack of a sensory diet; or a lack of a self-care regimen.
Environmental factors also can contribute to melt-downs. A limitation of
sunlight can contribute to a lack of vitamin D, just as being in enclosed
spaces for long periods of time can contribute to a sense of alienation.
Build up of clutter and unfinished tasks from lack of “Executive

---
## Page 1057
Function” skills can overwhelm students. They feel a profound
separation from nature and an inability to see the beauty in the small
things around them.
Students can be tormented by their seeming failure to adhere to over-
scrupulous, rigid, and unattainable religious doctrines and practices.
My sister Mary Jo, with High Functioning Autism, at age 73, could
never accept forgiveness for sinning when she was a nun in her
twenties.
All of these contributing factors lead some students to such a lack of
self-acceptance and feelings that they cannot be themselves.
In the last few years we have had several high profile incidents around
the country with young people who either were diagnosed with or have
many of the characteristics of individuals with Learning Differences
and/or those on the Autism Spectrum.
These extreme situations that have occurred in school shootings and
social situations are definitely not par for the course. In most cases the
young person on the Spectrum or with the Learning Difference is the
victim of social or physical violence and/or suicidal tendencies from the
abuse he or she has suffered.
In many cases the parents and professionals around these students do
not understand the repercussions of letting them “stew” in their
situations or circumstances. They are devoted to these young people
(especially if they are their children), and seem to accidentally
mishandle their treatment or fail to get appropriate treatment due to the
young person’s rigidity and lack of insight or willingness to take action.
Parents often have the financial resources to help their child, and are
even given feedback that is clear from professionals, but are afraid to
rock the boat or push their child, adolescent, or young adult into getting
the help they need. These young people then wallow in depression and
because they are so intelligent in their areas of special interest, and are
strident about “not needing any help,” those around them let them sit in
this really deleterious and dangerous set of circumstances and

---
## Page 1058
helplessly watch as their son or daughter slips further and further into
depression and delusional thinking.
Here are some of the areas that cause deep depression with
young people on the ASD or LD Spectrum:
•
Loneliness (a terrible sense of isolation) and feeling they don’t
belong.
•
Mental exhaustion.
•
Sensory overload (lack of a proper nutrition, sleep, and sensory
diets).
•
Lack of self-care regimen.
•
Lack of sunlight and vitamin D.
•
Being in enclosed spaces and lack of appreciation and
connection with nature and fear of leaving a space of comfort.
•
Inability to self-soothe.
•
Inability to self-regulate.
•
Lack of self-acceptance: Feeling that you cannot be yourself.
•
Unrealistic thought patterns about the way life is supposed to be
and how others live in the world.
•
Failure to live up to over-scrupulous and rigid adherence to
unattainable religious and other doctrines and practices.
•
Hyper-focused on the negative: Inability to see the beauty in the
small things around you.
•
Build up of clutter and unfinished tasks from lack of “Executive
Function” skills.
•
Inability to finish projects that are started.
•
Overwhelming sense of separation and disconnection.
•
Intellectual superiority – believing that you can think your way
out of any situation on your own.

---
## Page 1059
•
The belief that asking for help is a sign of weakness.
The problem is when parents allow their own lives to collapse around a
child like this and feed the special interests but do not require any
socialization, therapy, or other activity out of the home; the situation
festers and the individual continues to implode. At an early age, parents
often learn to give in rather than force issues with their children and let
them have their way. Because these children, adolescents, and
emerging young adults have a combination of hyper-intelligence with
hypersensitivity, if they move in a negative direction they can plan and
execute with huge consequences. They are also capable of amazing
positive gains if channeled in the right direction.
Parents often try to be “self-reliant,” “independent,” and “take care of
their own.” While it is very noble to be very protective and hands on, it
feeds the problem. Not only does it deprive the parent of a real life
outside the home (calm mom, calm child), but it also can feed into the
social dysfunction of the young person.
People on the Spectrum often obsess on special interests and some of
those are not healthy ones to have (such as Adam Lanza’s obsession
with guns). Having some kind of thought disorder and mental illness
combined with this extreme focusing ability can produce horrendous
results.
So, pulling a student out of high school and letting him or her take up
residence in the basement can heighten his or her sense of alienation
and thought disorder. In adolescence these kids either implode or
explode. I say that: “all imploders eventually become exploders.” Who
knows what kind of bullying these young people have gone through (in
the hallways, bathrooms, or online) or what effect this has had on them
at an early age. The vast majority of the time children, adolescents,
and young adults on the Spectrum are the victims and not the
perpetrators.
It is easy to judge what some of these parents and professionals should
have done. Individuals with Aspergers/Learning Difference traits often
push away those trying to get close to them. Boys, in particular, need

---
## Page 1060
authority figures in their lives. They need a father or brother figure who
will make the effort to take them out of their self-constructed lairs and
expose them to safe activities where they could experience life.
The first steps in finding a solution are:
•
Knowledge and understanding of what to do in situations when
you are dealing with socially reclusive children, adolescents, and
young adults like this early on.
•
Open access to the important services that young adults (like
Adam Lanza needed), for families who cannot afford them.
What are the services that could alleviate a severe situation like
Adam Lanza, and possibly prevent this type of tragedy?
Given the depth that some of these situations build up to, removing the
child, adolescent, or young adult by ambulance to a hospital where they
could be evaluated and stabilized on medication is the first intervention.
If there are incidents of violent behavior directed toward parents in the
home/or at school, an immediate psychiatric evaluation is warranted.
But let’s step back and talk about prevention before situations like this
build up into a crisis. What are the proactive services that could help
prevent this in the first place? Here are my suggestions for prevention:
Social strategies and building a support network such as:
Social Thinking sessions (in a group or individually at first) that can help
individuals understand the nonverbal behavior of others and learn how
to have reciprocal conversation skills; how to respond to others in
various situations and “take the edge” off his or her lack of socialization.
Social mentors (college or graduate students trained to work with
young students with Learning Differences) can help individuals learn to
enjoy healthy and appropriate special interests while having someone
to confide in and to learn the “social graces” from. This type of “social
translator” can be a bridge from isolation to the world outside their
home environments.
Getting involved in a special interest:

---
## Page 1061
Joining a social group or church group.
Doing community service to help others. For example: In college I was
very active in community service, going to orphanages to play with the
kids on weekends and visiting nursing homes to sing to the patients. I
was involved in teaching religious education to seventh graders and
went to all the athletic events. I realize that I was “parallel playing” most
of the time and not really engaging with my peers, but at the same time
able to talk one on one with these children – younger than me and the
older people in the nursing home. Volunteering really helped me
conquer my social awkwardness. This is a very common and positive
outcome for individuals on the Spectrum.
Initiating strategies:
Solitary self-appraisal does not work; this only allows those on the
Spectrum to self-justify actions or lack of action. The default mode of
most young people on the Spectrum is isolation. These individuals
must have the courage and a willingness to implement new strategies
such as:
Giving oneself permission to ask for help.
Having social mentors and friends that they can share their real
thoughts with. For example: Seeking people, situations, and
circumstances that are more positive or optimistic and avoiding toxic
negative people or places. Once I realized that people really wanted to
help me and that I had deprived myself of that during my youth –
thinking that I should just tough it out or go it alone – things really began
to change for me. I had a couple of mentors I could bounce things off. I
trusted older people, in particular, my father’s best friend. I could
debate without judgment. He would listen to me and then give me some
advice. I respected his opinion.
Flexibility strategies:
Being committed to listening to feedback and having the willingness to
implement others’ feedback.

---
## Page 1062
Utilizing “The Donkey Rule” (asking five people their opinions and
using the majority rule as an answer).
Cognitive behavior therapy (CBT) with a good therapist or psychologist
(this could even be done by Skype initially if the individual is too shy to
come to the office but that is not preferential). CBT helps the ASD or LD
individual erode the underlying rigid belief systems based on fallacious
reasoning that keep him or her from trying new strategies. This is a
really useful therapy for helping each person understand the erroneous
premises and foundations upon which his or her irrational thoughts
were built and helps each person have a clearer understanding of how
his or her actions affect him or herself and others.
For example: I started to use “The Donkey Rule” after my diagnosis (if
five people call it a donkey and I still think it is a horse, then I shouldn’t
be a “jackass” and I should follow their advice). I was extremely rigid in
my religious observance and took the rules literally. I thought that if
others did not follow my beliefs they were all “going to hell.” After many
years of meeting people from different faiths and even atheists who
were wonderful caring people, this gradually eroded for me.
Wellness strategies:
Learning that our emotional well-being is our responsibility is a valuable
lesson as is learning to care for ourselves and our bodies. All are
essential for our mental outlook.
The importance of learning to self-soothe.
A wellness coach who could help an individual do some sensory and/or
physical activities that would relieve the pressure on the brain of his or
her obsessive thoughts and sort of “cool down the nuclear power plant
core.” Physical exercise can relieve a lot of anxiety and depression;
most of us benefit from getting out into the fresh air and sunlight. A
wellness coach can help each person develop a plan for eating good
foods and a sleep or sensory diet.
Ask individuals to try and commit (by exhibiting cognitive flexibility), to
trying new activities, reading new or different books and materials, and
trying new foods, films, etc.

---
## Page 1063
I became distraught after my third sibling died and I thought I might
need to have medication or go to a hospital. I asked myself what would
make me feel better and I thought I would get a pedicure. I had had one
before and they also massage your feet and legs while you are sitting in
one of those massage chairs. This helped me a lot. I also got the
opportunity to talk to some of the older ladies in town while doing this.
Afterwards, I had lunch with my son and grandson at an outside café
and sat in the sun. I felt somewhat better. My anniversary was the next
day, so I thought to make a reservation that evening for a couples’
massage and then eat at the restaurant where we first met. By the end
of the day I had spent about $350, but the bottom line was … I took care
of myself and felt so much better.
I never would have done this for myself in the past. I would have
endured an emotionally painful state and not taken any action to help
myself. This particular day stood in direct contrast to my sister who had
hoarded her money and would not go to the beautiful beaches and
cafés near her apartment in Florida, who died with $80,000 in the bank
(and from suicide).
Gratitude strategies:
When life crises occur in multiples, anyone can be thrown into a state of
anxiety and/or depression. Getting perspective about ALL that is in
one’s life is an antidote. When I work with young adults, “don’t sweat
the small-stuff, it’s all small-stuff” is a saying I use often.
I encourage the young men and women I work with to be in the present
and appreciate all the gifts they have.
I advise each one to keep a daily gratitude journal or box as a good
way of “acknowledging the good.”
And above all: Each person should be taking stock of his or her special
gifts and contributions and being grateful for each gift and each
contribution he or she made in the past.
I also tell students to seek out and read books that promote well-being,
self-help, and helping others.

---
## Page 1064
I have read several books over and over again (with passages
underlined), that remind me to be present and enjoy what is right in
front of me, to not let the “dead past” or the “imagined future” dictate
my happiness in the present.
Physical strategies:
Create the kind of atmosphere and energy that invites calm and
naturally lowers anxiety. Use colors and music to reduce stress.
Avoid alcohol and other depressants.
Take vitamins (especially D).
When I wake up in the morning, I light candles in the bathroom, dining
room, and kitchen instead of turning on the lights. I sit at the table and
sip hot tea, putting my hands around the glass to feel the warmth of the
cup on them. I read a couple of segments from meditation books and sit
quietly. I eat simply and I put myself into the present where everything
is calm and quiet.
Spiritual strategies:
Uncover individual spiritual values and find ways to express them.
Be in nature: Nature lowers anxiety – have photos of horses or animals,
plants are relaxing, fish tank, even a dog if it’s acceptable to others or a
landlord, to lower stress.
Employ meditation and Yoga.
Animals are beautiful and I have always loved dogs. I often take my two
Labrador retrievers out in the fields behind the farm I live at, to run.
There are mountains and hills and I sometimes sit on a rocking chair
and look at the sunset over the pond and the mountains while the dogs
find sticks to chew and play with. It is very grounding and hard to be
anxious in the presence of such beauty.
In conclusion:
Having a set of strategies for handling anxiety and depression does not
replace the need for therapy. Adolescents and especially young adults

---
## Page 1065
on the Spectrum need to process unhealthy relationships or patterns of
interacting with a professional.
I have used therapy to help me with my social understanding and to
process all the feelings from the tragedies in my family. Therapists are
a great support in helping individuals with Learning Differences to
understand problems, analyze their options for problem solving, and
encouraging students to take action to help themselves.
I also learned after my diagnosis that self-care is the most important
thing those of us who work with this population can impart to our
students. Cognitive flexibility is important but you cannot achieve it
unless each individual has developed a good sleep diet, is eating
healthy meals, has some form of sensory diet to release their physical
energy, and is at the same time learning the rules of social engagement
while receiving the counseling to help them make good decisions and
work through any childhood trauma or bullying.
I hope that all those working in this field will continue to focus on
identifying issues and problems as early as possible, then implement
preventive strategies that will help individuals and their families be
proactive and lower the statistics of further tragedies from occurring.
Dr. Michael P. McManmon is the author of this book and of “Made for
Good Purpose: What Every Parent Needs to Know to Help Their
Adolescent with Asperger’s, High Functioning Autism or a Learning
Difference Become an Independent Adult.”
THE ART OF AUTISM
by Debra Muzikar
Kevin’s Story: Painting the World with Colors
When my son Kevin was in Kindergarten, out of all the bright colors in
the crayon box, he always picked the black one. Instead of holding the

---
## Page 1066
crayon between his forefinger and thumb like the other kids, he grasped
the crayon tightly in his right fist and scribbled dark circles in the center
of the paper. “A black spider?” I guessed. “No, a train,” he said in his
signature low and flat voice. “That’s the smoke?” I asked, pointing to
black swirls on the paper. “A train,” he insisted. At that time, I assumed
art was not his forte. Boy … was I wrong!
Kevin doing what he loves … painting
From the time Kevin was diagnosed with autism, a revolving door of
therapists came and went, each offering a skill or a part of themselves
that Kevin seemed to integrate into his own repertoire. A Frisbee
football player taught Kevin how to hold and throw the plastic disc, a
chess champion taught Kevin sophisticated chess moves, and there
was Keith, who brought his dog Bella, whom we affectionately called
Bella, the Autistic Dog, as she ran around in circles, oblivious to what
others thought. While they all were helpful and nice, it was Colin, the
artist, who became a part of our extended family and forever changed
Kevin’s life.

---
## Page 1067
“Draw me a picture,” Kevin demanded upon learning that Colin was
artistic. “You draw me a picture,” Colin replied back. Kevin wasn’t
pleased with Colin’s response; however, he picked up a marker and
drew something that resembled a dog, and this time it wasn’t black.
The next week Colin brought watercolors, the week after that they
painted with acrylics, the third week it was oils, and so on. Colin’s
tutelage included going to the library with Kevin in search of art books.
They’d come home with books on Van Gogh, Kadinsky, Hopper, and
Gauguin. Kevin would pore over the pictures as Colin talked to him
about the artists. During this phase, Kevin created paintings, which we
called Kevin Gauguins, Kevin Van Goghs, and Kevin Kadinskys.
Kevin Hosseini Smithsonian VSA Exhibit “Bus or Cycle” in Acrylic
Colin used art to enhance Kevin’s social communication. “What color
comes from yellow and blue?” Colin would ask while dabbing each
color on Kevin’s palette. Kevin mixed them and replied, “Green.” Kevin
was learning the art of conversation with ART through practical and

---
## Page 1068
visual experiences in a way that, I have come to find out, many autistics
seem to learn.
Colin the art therapist may have started out showing us how to
communicate with our boy better, but he taught us a great deal more.
He showed us how to follow our child’s lead and taught us that a child’s
obsessions can become an art form – LITERALLY!
Kevin, from a young age, was obsessed with trains, so we encouraged
him to put the trains on canvas. As with many things, you never know
where creativity can lead. One of Kevin’s train paintings ended up at
Union Station in Washington DC for an art exhibit, and last year his
painting “Bus or Cycle” travelled the world as part of a VSA art exhibit.
We were even flown to Washington DC for the unveiling at the
Smithsonian.
Kevin’s experience inspired me to find other autistic young people, and
in so doing, I compiled the book The Art of Autism: Shifting
Perceptions. I was introduced to so many talented artists, all of whom
are just as compelling as the art they have created. Here are just a few:
Grant’s Story: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle,
CREATE
Grant Manier had a compulsive habit of tearing paper. His mom, Julie,
instead of trying to extinguish his habit, channeled Grant’s compulsion
into beautiful collages. Many of Grant’s collages take months to
complete. Grant incorporates thousands of pieces of paper, puzzle
pieces, and recycled cardboard into his cool-ages. He is now referred
to as an eco-artist and sells his creations for thousands of dollars. In his
words, “I have autism, and I have daily challenges and struggles, but
my disability has become my ABILITY to contribute to help changing
the earth’s environment and raise the awareness of special talents
among those with different abilities.”

---
## Page 1069
Grant Manier working on an Owl Collage
Wil’s Story: The Paper Cutting Picasso
Wil Kerner, autistic and mostly nonverbal, received a pair of scissors
and some construction paper for his tenth birthday and his obsessive
cut-outs quickly began to fill his grandma’s home. Wil’s grandma Susan
noticed that his creations resembled characters that he was watching
on TV. They were actually compositions! Wil and his grandma pasted
his cut-out compositions onto paper and voila, an artist was created!
Now Wil, referred to as the “cutout kid,” has artwork, which sells for
thousands of dollars, on display in galleries on both coasts. But even
more than financial gain, art has helped Wil to connect with the outside
world. It’s given him confidence, which we all know is priceless!

---
## Page 1070

---
## Page 1071
“The Little Prince” and Wil Kerner in his studio
James’ Story: Composer of the Canvas
James Frye, who is known as the “Futuristic Throwback” artist, says he
can see the colors when he listens to music. Although James is 20,
Wendy, his mom, only recently discovered that he has this capability.
He creates original, bright, hand-sketched, electronic, music-related
images that are then transferred to high-quality canvas, which he sells.
James says that his art allows him to move beyond his communication
limitations, and the pieces he creates challenge those who view them to
push the limits of their imagination!

---
## Page 1072

---
## Page 1073
James Frye and “Electro King”
Introduce Your Child to Art
Try different mediums. Maybe your child won’t enjoy using crayons but
will like to make collages or cut-outs.
Make art fun. Go on a scavenger hunt for materials in your own
neighborhood. Incorporate recycled material into art. Consider your
child’s interests. For example, if your child likes trains, have him or her
paint trains or train stations or photograph trains. Turn on music. Have
your child paint what he or she hears.
This article was adapted and reprinted with permission from the winter
2015 issue of Zoom Autism Magazine and with permission from Debra
Muzikar.

---
## Page 1074
BIOGRAPHY: Debra Muzikar is co-founder of The Art of Autism
collaborative, which showcases the gifts of autism. She has compiled
two books on art and autism: “The Art of Autism: Shattering Myths”
and “The Art of Autism: Shifting Perceptions.” She blogs at The Art of
Autism and writes regularly about creativity and the human spirit.
Debra posts art of those on the spectrum on The Art of Autism
Facebook page.
AUTISM MOVEMENT THERAPY®
Aut-erobics® Movement & Music Sensory
Integration to “Wake Up the Brain”
by Joanne Lara, MA
The brain is an information processing wonder. We process and store
information in either long-term or short-term areas of the brain. When
we need that information again, we retrieve it via a pathway (white brain
matter) in the mapping area (gray matter) of the brain. Brain mapping is
an interesting process. In a nutshell, it’s like this. Say you move into a

---
## Page 1075
new home. You don’t know where to find the grocery store, the dry
cleaner, the gas station, or any other neighborhood establishments you
need on a daily basis. But within a short period of time you’ve found
these places and you no longer have to think about the specific
directions to get to any of these places. You get in your car and drive
right to the spot, almost as though you’re on “automatic pilot.”
Daily we map hundreds of pieces of information by placing them in a file
in our brain. Without even having to think about it, when we need the
information we retrieve the file by going straight to it, via cognitive maps
or highway pathways.
Individuals with Autism have difficulty accessing and retrieving
information in both long-and/or short -term memory banks. Either the
pathway does not exist or the transmitters are impaired. This makes
learning especially difficult for them. The analogy is that our kids’ brains
function like a library where none of the information is stored in any
organized, categorized way. Think of the confusion this would cause!
The good news is that scientists now know we can often jumpstart
